topics.title: Definitions - Perception
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definitions - Perception

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Perception** in the context of **Robot Motion Planning** is formally defined as the *computational process by which a robotic system acquires, interprets, organizes, and maintains an internal representation of its own state and the state of its surrounding environment from raw sensor measurements, in order to support decision-making, action selection, and trajectory generation.*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, perception is treated as the **upstream cognitive stage** of the classical *Sense → Plan → Act* paradigm. It transforms noisy, incomplete, and high-dimensional raw sensor data into a structured **world model** (a map, occupancy grid, object list, pose estimate, etc.) that the motion planner can consume.

**Standard KTU Sub-Definitions:**

- **Exteroception:** Perception of the *external* environment (obstacles, landmarks, other agents).
- **Proprioception:** Perception of the robot's *own* internal state (joint angles, wheel velocities, IMU readings).
- **World Model (Internal State):** $\xi_t$ — a complete or partial symbolic/numeric representation of the world at time $t$.
- **Sensor Measurement:** $z_t$ — the raw observation vector at time $t$.
- **Observation Model:** $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ — the probabilistic likelihood of receiving measurement $z_t$ given the true robot pose $x_t$ and map $m$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — driving a car in fog at night.**
>
> Imagine you are driving in dense fog. You cannot see far ahead, but you rely on:
> - **Headlights** (active exteroception) to actively probe the road,
> - **Side mirrors** (passive exteroception) to perceive other vehicles,
> - The **speedometer and steering wheel feedback** (proprioception) to know your own motion,
> - And your **mental map** built from all this data to decide *where* to steer next.
>
> **Perception is exactly this — the car's "senses."** It is the *bridge* between the physical world (which the robot cannot directly access) and the symbolic world (which the planner reasons over). Without perception, a robot is blind; without motion planning, perception is purposeless.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \;\mathbf{m/s}$ (governs time-of-flight for LiDAR/radar).
- **Speed of sound:** $v_s \approx 343 \;\mathbf{m/s}$ (governs ultrasonic sensing).
- **Standard perception latency bounds for KTU problems:** $f_{sense} \in [10, 100] \;\mathbf{Hz}$.
- **Spatial resolution of typical sensors:** Camera (mm), LiDAR (cm), Radar (m), GPS (m).

## 1.4 The Role of Perception in the Motion Planning Pipeline

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Motion planning without perception is *offline* (a priori). Motion planning **with** perception is *online / reactive*. KTU questions frequently test whether students can distinguish these two regimes.

| Regime | Perception Role | Planner Type |
|---|---|---|
| Offline planning | None / prior map | Deterministic, complete |
| Online planning | Continuous updates | Reactive, sensor-based |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Perception Process

The perception pipeline can be decomposed into **four sequential logical stages**, each of which the KTU examiner may isolate as a sub-question.

1. **Signal Acquisition (Sensing Stage)**
   - *Why:* Robots cannot directly read the world — they must interrogate it using physical phenomena (light, sound, electromagnetism, mechanical contact).
   - *How:* Sensors convert physical energy (photons, acoustic waves, force) into measurable electrical signals.

2. **Pre-processing & Feature Extraction**
   - *Why:* Raw sensor data is noisy, redundant, and high-dimensional; planners cannot ingest pixels or point clouds directly.
   - *How:* Apply filtering (Kalman, low-pass), edge detection, RANSAC plane fitting, normal estimation, segmentation.

3. **Interpretation & State Estimation**
   - *Why:* Convert low-level features into semantically meaningful entities (obstacles, free space, robot pose).
   - *How:* Probabilistic inference using Bayesian filtering, SLAM, object detection neural networks.

4. **World Model Maintenance**
   - *Why:* Maintain a persistent, queryable representation that the planner can access in $O(\log n)$ or $O(1)$ time.
   - *How:* Update occupancy grid, topological graph, signed distance field, or semantic voxel map.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Perception-Action Loop:** In modern reactive systems, perception is *not* a one-shot pre-processing step. It forms a closed feedback loop: *Sense → Plan → Act → Sense → …*. This is the foundation of **reactive / behavior-based motion planning**.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Perception (KTU High-Yield)

### 2.2.1 By Modality

| Modality | Active/Passive | Measured Quantity | Typical Use in Planning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Camera (monocular/stereo) | Passive | Light intensity | Visual servoing, lane detection |
| LiDAR | Active | Time-of-flight range | 2D/3D obstacle maps |
| Radar | Active | Doppler shift / range | Outdoor dynamic obstacle tracking |
| Ultrasonic | Active | Acoustic echo | Short-range indoor obstacle detection |
| IMU | Passive | Acceleration, angular velocity | Dead reckoning, pose prediction |
| GPS | Passive | Satellite ranging | Outdoor global localization |
| Tactile / Force | Passive | Contact force | Compliance control, manipulation |
| Encoders (joint/wheel) | Passive | Position / velocity | Proprioceptive odometry |

### 2.2.2 By Direction of Energy Flow

- **Active sensors:** Emit energy into the environment and measure the response (LiDAR, radar, sonar). They *illuminate* the world.
- **Passive sensors:** Merely receive ambient energy (cameras, microphones, encoders). They *observe* the world.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *active/passive* with *exteroceptive/proprioceptive*. These are **orthogonal** axes — a joint encoder is passive + proprioceptive; a sonar is active + exteroceptive.

### 2.2.3 By Spatial Reference

- **Ego-centric (body-frame) perception:** Coordinates relative to the robot.
- **Allocentric (world-frame) perception:** Coordinates in a global reference frame, requiring localization.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are examinable in **Part A (3 marks)** derivations or as components of a **Part B (14 marks)** problem.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Time-of-Flight range | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | m | $c = 3 \times 10^8 \;m/s$ for LiDAR |
| 2 | Ultrasonic range | $d = \dfrac{v_s \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | m | $v_s \approx 343 \;m/s$ |
| 3 | Pinhole camera projection | $u = f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z}$ | pixels | $f$ = focal length |
| 4 | Observation model | $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ | — | Probabilistic likelihood |
| 5 | Bayes filter posterior | $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \propto p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_{t-1}) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid \cdot) \, dx_{t-1}$ | — | Foundation of SLAM |
| 6 | Odometry update | $x_t = x_{t-1} + \Delta x_{odom}$ | m, rad | Proprioceptive integration |
| 7 | Sensor noise (Gaussian) | $z = h(x) + \mathcal{N}(0, R)$ | — | $R$ = covariance matrix |
| 8 | Occupancy probability | $p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}) = \dfrac{p(m_i) \, p(z_t \mid m_i)}{p(m_i) \, p(z_t \mid m_i) + (1 - p(m_i)) \, p(z_t \mid \neg m_i)}$ | — | Log-odds form preferred |
| 9 | Coordinate transform | $p^{world} = T_{robot}^{world} \cdot p^{robot}$ | m | SE(2) / SE(3) |
| 10 | Field of view | $FOV = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{w}{2f}\right)$ | rad | $w$ = sensor width |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Perception is the enabling technology behind every modern autonomous system:

- **Autonomous vehicles:** LiDAR + camera fusion enables centimeter-level obstacle detection at 100 m range.
- **Warehouse robots (AMR):** 2D LiDAR SLAM for dynamic obstacle avoidance.
- **Surgical robots:** Force/tactile perception for safe tissue interaction.
- **Drones:** Visual-inertial odometry (VIO) for GPS-denied flight.
- **Humanoid robots:** Multi-modal perception (vision + tactile + proprioception) for dexterous manipulation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides the *exhaustive mathematical background* for the formulas listed in §2.3. Since the topic is primarily definitional, derivations are presented at the depth needed to support KTU Part-B numerical sub-questions.

## 3.1 Derivation: Time-of-Flight Range Measurement

The most fundamental active ranging principle. The sensor emits a pulse, which travels to a target, reflects, and returns.

**Step 1 — Define the round-trip propagation equation.**
A pulse emitted at time $t_e$ travels at speed $c$ (or $v_s$ for acoustic). If the target is at distance $d$, the round-trip distance traversed is $2d$.

$$
\Delta t = t_r - t_e
$$

**Step 2 — Express total distance traveled in terms of speed and time.**

$$
2d = c \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $d$.**

$$
\boxed{\,d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check with units.**
$[c] = m/s$, $[\Delta t] = s$, so $[c \cdot \Delta t] = m$. Dividing by the dimensionless factor 2 yields meters. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **For ultrasonic sensors**, replace $c$ with $v_s \approx 343 \;m/s$. The factor of 2 accounts for the *out-and-back* path; students frequently forget this and lose marks.

## 3.2 Derivation: Pinhole Camera Model (Monocular Projection)

The pinhole camera model is the canonical geometric projection used in visual perception.

**Step 1 — Define the coordinate system.**
A 3D world point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ in the camera frame projects to a 2D image point $p = (x, y)$ on the image plane located at distance $f$ (focal length) in front of the pinhole.

**Step 2 — Apply similar triangles from the pinhole geometry.**
The ray from $(0,0,0)$ to $(X,Y,Z)$ intersects the image plane at depth $f$ at a point $(x, y, f)$ where the ratios of corresponding coordinates are preserved:

$$
\dfrac{x}{f} = \dfrac{X}{Z}, \qquad \dfrac{y}{f} = \dfrac{Y}{Z}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the image coordinates.**

$$
\boxed{\,x = f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z}, \qquad y = f \cdot \dfrac{Y}{Z}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to pixel coordinates $(u, v)$** using principal point $(c_x, c_y)$ and pixel size $(s_x, s_y)$:

$$
u = c_x + s_x \cdot f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z}, \qquad v = c_y + s_y \cdot f \cdot \dfrac{Y}{Z}
$$

**Step 5 — Implication for depth ambiguity.**
A monocular camera cannot recover $Z$ from a single projection — division by zero is avoided only when $Z \neq 0$. This is why monocular depth *perception* requires either stereo, motion (structure from motion), or learned priors.

## 3.3 Derivation: Occupancy Grid Bayes Update

The occupancy grid is the most common KTU-tested world model.

**Step 1 — Define notation.** Let $m_i \in \{\text{free}, \text{occupied}\}$ denote the state of grid cell $i$. We want the posterior $p(m_i \mid z_{1:t})$.

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' rule.**

$$
p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}) = \dfrac{p(z_t \mid m_i) \cdot p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1})}
$$

**Step 3 — Avoid underflow using log-odds form.**
Define $l_t = \log \dfrac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t})}$. The recursive update becomes **additive**, which is computationally cheap and numerically stable:

$$
\boxed{\,l_t = l_{t-1} + \log \dfrac{p(m_i \mid z_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t)} - l_0\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert back to probability when queried by the planner:**

$$
p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}) = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{l_t}}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Active vs Passive Sensing

| Property | Active Sensors | Passive Sensors |
|---|---|---|
| Energy emission | Yes | No |
| Power consumption | Higher | Lower |
| Susceptibility to interference | Self-induced | Ambient noise |
| Range resolution | High (LiDAR: mm) | Lower (camera: pixel-limited) |
| Environmental dependency | Works in darkness | Requires illumination |
| Examples | LiDAR, Radar, Sonar | Camera, Microphone, IMU |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| KTU weightage | Frequent in Part B | Frequent in Part A |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Perception vs Motion Planning Coupling

| Coupling Level | Description | Algorithm Example | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Decoupled** | Perception produces full map → planner runs offline | A*, RRT on prior map | Common in early modules |
| **Loosely coupled** | Perception updates map asynchronously, planner re-plans periodically | D* Lite | Mention for Part A |
| **Tightly coupled** | Perception and planning share state, joint optimization | Perception-aware RRT*, belief-space planning | KTU 2024 trend — high weightage |
| **Reactive / sensor-based** | No explicit map; control law directly maps sensors to actions | Bug algorithms, VFH, Potential Fields | Often a 7-mark sub-question |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Perception-Action Closed Loop (KTU Most-Frequently-Drawn Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENV[World Environment] -- "raw energy" --> SEN[Sensors Signal Acquisition]
    SEN --> PRE[Preprocessing and Feature Extraction]
    PRE --> INT[Interpretation and State Estimation]
    INT --> WM[World Model Maintenance]
    WM --> PL[Planner Motion Planning]
    PL --> ACT[Actuators and Controllers]
    ACT -- "physical motion" --> ENV
    OBS[Observation Model p of zt given xt m] -.-> INT
    PRIOR[Prior Map and Prior Belief] -.-> WM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The solid arrows trace the *forward* information flow; the dashed arrows represent *probabilistic priors* that bias inference. The loop closes when the actuators act on the environment and the environment's new state is sensed again.

## 4.2 Sensor Taxonomy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PER[Robot Perception]
    PER --> EXO[Exteroceptive]
    PER --> PRO[Proprioceptive]
    EXO --> ACT1[Active]
    EXO --> PAS1[Passive]
    PRO --> ACT2[Active only in special lab setups]
    PRO --> PAS2[Passive]
    ACT1 --> LID[LiDAR]
    ACT1 --> RAD[Radar]
    ACT1 --> SON[Sonar Ultrasonic]
    PAS1 --> CAM[Camera RGB Depth]
    PAS1 --> MIC[Microphone]
    PAS2 --> ENC[Joint Encoders]
    PAS2 --> IMU[Inertial Measurement Unit]
    PAS2 --> GPS[Global Positioning System]
    PAS2 --> TAC[Tactile and Force Sensors]
```

## 4.3 Perception-to-Planning Information Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Raw Sensor Data zt] --> S2[Filtering Kalman EKF Particle]
    S2 --> S3[Feature Extraction Edges Planes Landmarks]
    S3 --> S4[State Estimation Localization Mapping SLAM]
    S4 --> S5[World Model Occupancy Grid Topological SDF]
    S5 --> P1[Global Planner A Star RRT]
    P1 --> P2[Local Planner DWA MPC]
    P2 --> C1[Controller PID]
    C1 --> R1[Robot Actuator Commands]
    R1 --> W1[World]
    W1 -.-> S1
```

## 4.4 Functional Mapping for "Definitions — Perception"

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[What is perception] --> A1[Process acquiring internal world representation]
    Q2[Why is it needed] --> A2[Motion planner requires state estimate and map]
    Q3[What are the types] --> A3[Exteroceptive vs Proprioceptive and Active vs Passive]
    Q4[What are key sensors] --> A4[Camera LiDAR Radar Sonar IMU GPS Encoder Tactile]
    Q5[How does it integrate] --> A5[Sense to Plan to Act closed loop with feedback]
    Q6[What is the output] --> A6[World model occupancy grid SDF topological graph]
    Q7[What are the uncertainties] --> A7[Sensor noise model bias and partial observability]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**
**Define perception in the context of robot motion planning. Distinguish between exteroception and proprioception with one example of a corresponding sensor for each.**

**Model Answer (≈ 130 words):**

Perception is the process by which a robot acquires and interprets sensor measurements to build an internal representation of itself and its environment, enabling informed motion-planning decisions.

- **Exteroception** perceives the *external* environment. *Example:* a 2D LiDAR measuring the distance to nearby walls.
- **Proprioception** perceives the robot's *own* internal state. *Example:* a wheel encoder measuring the rotation of the left drive wheel.

These two modalities are complementary: exteroception answers *"Where are the obstacles?"* while proprioception answers *"Where am I and how am I moving?"* — together they constitute the complete perceptual state required by a motion planner. **(3 marks)**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between active and passive sensors. Give two examples of each and state one advantage and one limitation of active sensing.**

**Model Answer (≈ 130 words):**

| Type | Definition | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Active** | Emits energy into the environment and measures the response | LiDAR, Ultrasonic sonar, Radar |
| **Passive** | Merely receives ambient energy | Camera, Microphone, IMU, GPS |

- **Advantage of active sensing:** Works in complete darkness and provides direct metric range information.
- **Limitation of active sensing:** Higher power consumption and potential interference when multiple robots operate in proximity (cross-talk). **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B offers **internal choice**. Both alternatives below are independent and at the same cognitive level.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the perception pipeline in a mobile robot. List the four functional stages and describe the role of each stage in supporting motion planning.

**Model Solution:**

The perception pipeline transforms raw sensor data into a planner-consumable world model. It consists of four functional stages:

**Stage 1 — Signal Acquisition.**
Physical sensors (LiDAR, cameras, encoders, IMU) convert environmental energy into electrical signals. Each sensor has an *observation model* $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ relating the measurement to the true state. Role: provides the raw, time-stamped measurement vector $z_t$ to subsequent stages.

**Stage 2 — Pre-processing & Feature Extraction.**
Raw data is filtered to remove noise (Kalman, low-pass, morphological), and salient features are extracted (edges, corners, planes, RANSAC-fitted lines). Role: compresses the high-bandwidth data stream into a compact, noise-reduced feature set that is interpretable by the estimator.

**Stage 3 — Interpretation & State Estimation.**
Features are fused with the prior estimate to compute the posterior belief $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ over robot pose $x_t$ and map $m$, typically via Bayes filtering, EKF, particle filters, or graph-SLAM. Role: produces a statistically consistent estimate of *where the robot is* and *what the world looks like*.

**Stage 4 — World Model Maintenance.**
The estimate is committed to a persistent spatial representation — occupancy grid, signed distance field (SDF), topological graph, or semantic voxel map. Role: provides the planner with a queryable structure (e.g., *is cell $(i,j)$ free?* in $O(1)$) on which graph search, sampling, or potential-field planning can be executed.

A **block diagram** (see §4.3) showing these four stages feeding the planner completes the answer. **(7 marks)**

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** A 2D LiDAR on an autonomous vehicle emits laser pulses at speed $c = 3 \times 10^8 \;m/s$ and measures a round-trip time of $\Delta t = 666.7 \;ns$ to an obstacle ahead. Compute the distance to the obstacle. If the vehicle is moving at $v = 20 \;m/s$ and the perception loop runs at $f = 20 \;Hz$, comment on whether this perception rate is sufficient for safe reactive planning at this speed.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the time-of-flight range equation.**

$$
d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$
d = \dfrac{(3 \times 10^8 \;m/s) \cdot (666.7 \times 10^{-9} \;s)}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator.**

$$
3 \times 10^8 \cdot 666.7 \times 10^{-9} = 3 \cdot 666.7 \cdot 10^{-1} = 2000.1 \cdot 10^{-1} = 200.01 \;m
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by 2.**

$$
\boxed{\,d = 100.005 \;m \approx 100 \;m\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Analyse the perception rate.**

Time between consecutive perception updates:

$$
T = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{20} = 0.05 \;s
$$

Distance traveled per perception cycle:

$$
\Delta s = v \cdot T = 20 \;m/s \cdot 0.05 \;s = 1 \;m
$$

**Conclusion:** A perception rate of 20 Hz yields only **1 m of vehicle travel between updates**. For high-speed motion planning, this is *marginally acceptable* for highway driving but **insufficient for dense urban scenarios** or emergency braking, where 50–100 Hz is typically required. KTU-grade answer: comment on both the quantitative gap and the implication for planner reactivity. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Define the term "world model" in robot motion planning. Explain the occupancy grid representation in detail, including the log-odds update rule. State one advantage and one limitation of occupancy grids.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A *world model* is the internal, structured representation of the environment (and often the robot's own state) maintained by the robot to support planning. In KTU 2024 terminology, it is the symbolic/numeric bridge between perception and planning.

**Occupancy Grid Representation:**
The environment is discretized into a 2D (or 3D) grid of cells $\{m_i\}$. Each cell $m_i$ is a binary random variable:
- $m_i = \text{occupied}$ (obstacle)
- $m_i = \text{free}$ (traversable)

The robot maintains a probability $p(m_i \mid z_{1:t})$ for each cell, updated from sensor measurements.

**Log-odds update rule:**
Define the log-odds $l_t = \log \dfrac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t})}$. The recursive Bayesian update becomes:

$$
l_t = l_{t-1} + \log \dfrac{p(m_i \mid z_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t)} - l_0
$$

where $l_0$ is the prior log-odds. This form is **additive** (constant-time per cell) and **numerically stable** (no probability underflow).

**Advantage:** Simple, fast, well-suited to 2D LiDAR/sonar, and directly usable by grid-based planners (A*, D*).

**Limitation:** Memory scales as $O(n^2)$ in 2D and $O(n^3)$ in 3D; cannot represent *uncertainty in the robot's own pose* (a common KTU follow-up question). **(7 marks)**

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable block diagram, explain the closed-loop Sense–Plan–Act paradigm as applied to reactive motion planning. Differentiate between online and offline motion planning, and explain how the role of perception changes between the two.

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[World Environment] --> B[Sense Perception]
    B --> C[Plan Motion Planner]
    C --> D[Act Actuators]
    D --> A
    FB[Feedback from proprioception] -.-> B
```

**Description of the closed loop:**

1. **Sense** — The robot's sensors (LiDAR, camera, encoders) measure the current world and robot state, producing the perceptual estimate $\hat{x}_t, \hat{m}_t$.
2. **Plan** — The motion planner consumes this estimate and computes a trajectory or control command that drives the robot from its current state to the goal.
3. **Act** — The actuators execute the command, modifying the robot's pose and altering the world (e.g., the robot is now at a new location, dynamic obstacles have moved).
4. The loop repeats, with the *same perception module* re-observing the now-updated world.

**Offline vs Online Motion Planning:**

| Aspect | Offline Planning | Online (Reactive) Planning |
|---|---|---|
| Map availability | Complete a priori map given | Map built / updated online |
| Perception role | None (or pre-processing only) | Continuous, in the loop |
| Planner | Global, complete (A*, RRT) | Local, reactive (DWA, Bug, VFH) |
| Re-planning | Not required | Required at every cycle |
| Uncertainty handling | Deterministic | Probabilistic / robust |
| KTU example | Factory floor AGV with fixed layout | Search-and-rescue robot in unknown terrain |

**Conclusion:** The defining role of perception is to *close the loop* — without it, the robot acts on a stale or non-existent world model; with it, the robot can adapt to dynamic, partially known environments in real time. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in the "Definitions — Perception" topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing active/passive with exteroceptive/proprioceptive.** These are *orthogonal* classifications. A joint encoder is *passive + proprioceptive*; a sonar is *active + exteroceptive*. Many students lump them together and lose **2–3 marks** instantly.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in time-of-flight range.** The pulse travels *out and back*. Writing $d = c \cdot \Delta t$ instead of $d = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$ is a guaranteed **1–2 mark deduction** in any numerical Part-B sub-question.
>
> 3. **Stating that monocular cameras "measure depth."** They do not — depth must be inferred via stereo, motion, or learned priors. A loose answer here loses **1 mark** in definition-type questions.
>
> 4. **Treating perception as a one-shot preprocessing step.** KTU 2024 explicitly tests the **closed-loop Sense–Plan–Act** model. Always draw the feedback arrow.
>
> 5. **Ignoring units in numerical answers.** Even a correct formula gives a **0 mark** answer if units are absent or wrong. Always annotate $[d] = m$ and include a sanity check.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for "Definitions — Perception":**

- **Perception** = process of acquiring and interpreting sensor data into a world model for planning.
- **Two-axis taxonomy:** Exteroceptive vs Proprioceptive **AND** Active vs Passive (orthogonal).
- **Active sensors** emit energy (LiDAR, Radar, Sonar); **passive** receive ambient energy (Camera, IMU, GPS, Encoder).
- **Exteroceptive sensors** perceive the world (LiDAR, Camera); **proprioceptive** perceive the robot's own state (Encoder, IMU).
- **Observation model** $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ is the probabilistic bridge between world and sensor.
- **Time-of-flight range:** $d = c \Delta t / 2$ — *always include the factor 2.*
- **Pinhole projection:** $u = f X / Z$, $v = f Y / Z$ — monocular cameras cannot recover $Z$ from a single image.
- **Occupancy grid** is the most common KTU-tested world model; log-odds form is additive and numerically stable.
- **World model** = internal representation of environment + robot state; forms the interface between perception and planning.
- **Sense–Plan–Act** is a **closed loop** — perception provides feedback, not a one-shot input.
- **Online (reactive) planning** requires continuous perception; **offline** planning uses a prior map.
- **Bayes filter** is the foundational recursive estimator: posterior $\propto$ likelihood $\times$ prior.
- **Sensor noise** is conventionally modelled as additive Gaussian: $z = h(x) + \mathcal{N}(0, R)$.
- **Coordinate frames:** ego-centric (body) vs allocentric (world); transformation via $T_{robot}^{world} \in SE(2)$ or $SE(3)$.
- **Standard sensor speed constants to memorize:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \;m/s$ (light/EM), $v_s = 343 \;m/s$ (sound in air).
- **Frequent KTU framing:** "perception supports **reactive** motion planning in **dynamic, partially known** environments."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Localization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **Robot Motion Planning**, **Localization** is formally defined as the computational process of estimating the **pose** $x_t$ of a mobile robot at time $t$ within a known map $m$ of its environment, by fusing two probabilistic information streams:

1. The **control inputs** $u_{1:t} = \{u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_t\}$ (odometry, wheel encoder readings, commanded velocities).
2. The **sensor observations** $z_{1:t} = \{z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_t\}$ (LIDAR, cameras, sonar, IMU, GPS).

Mathematically, localization seeks the posterior probability density:
$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$$

The robot's pose in a 2D planar environment is conventionally represented as $x_t = (x, y, \theta)^T$, where $x$ and $y$ are the Cartesian coordinates and $\theta$ is the heading orientation in radians.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Localization is the second pillar of the canonical robotics pipeline **Sense $\rightarrow$ Localize $\rightarrow$ Plan $\rightarrow$ Act**. Without a reliable belief about "where the robot is," every downstream planning and control decision is mathematically invalid.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mall Finder" Intuition

Imagine you walk into a **giant shopping mall** (the environment/map) for the first time, and you must constantly answer the question: *"Where am I right now?"* You have two information sources:

- **Self-motion cues (Control $u_t$):** The pedometer on your phone tells you you walked **30 steps forward, then turned 90° right**. This is your *odometry* — it gives a rough estimate of how your pose has *changed*, but errors accumulate like a snowball (drift).
- **Landmark cues (Observations $z_t$):** You see the *food court sign*, the *Zara store*, and a *large glass dome*. You match these to features on the mall's directory board. This is your *sensor measurement* — it provides *global corrections* but is noisy.

**Localization is the brain's act of fusing these two noisy streams** into a single confident estimate of your position. The same exact fusion happens in software via the **Bayes Filter** recursion.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Mathematical Constants

- **Robot pose dimension:** $n = 3$ for planar ground robots $(x, y, \theta)^T$; $n = 6$ for aerial vehicles (add roll, pitch, yaw).
- **State covariance matrix dimension:** $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$.
- **Planar rotation matrix $SO(2)$ element:** Orthogonal $2 \times 2$ matrix with determinant $+1$.
- **Standard noise assumptions:** Zero-mean **Gaussian** noise with covariance matrices $R_t$ (motion), $Q_t$ (observation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Variants of Localization (Categorical Distinction — High-Yield for KTU):**
> 1. **Position Tracking (Local Localization):** Initial pose $x_0$ is known; the robot tracks small drifts. Solved efficiently by the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**.
> 2. **Global Localization:** The robot is placed arbitrarily anywhere in the map with no prior estimate. Requires a *multi-modal* belief — solved by **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** or **Grid-based Markov Localization**.
> 3. **Kidnapped Robot Problem:** The robot is teleported to an arbitrary pose mid-operation and must recover. The hardest variant; tests *global localization* and *recovery* capabilities.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Probabilistic Foundation — Why Bayes' Rule?

Sensor measurements and odometry are both **noisy and incomplete**. No single source is trustworthy. Bayes' rule provides the mathematically optimal way to combine them, assuming a *Markov* property (the future depends only on the present, not the full history).

The general **Bayes Filter** is split into two recursive phases executed at every time step $t$:

#### Phase 1 — Prediction (Motion Update, $u_t$)
Propagate the previous belief through the motion model $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$:
$$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

#### Phase 2 — Correction (Measurement Update, $z_t$)
Multiply by the sensor likelihood $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ and normalize:
$$bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)$$

where $\eta$ is the normalizer ensuring $\int bel(x_t) \, dx_t = 1$.

> [!NOTE]
> The hat notation $\overline{bel}$ denotes the *predicted* (pre-correction) belief; $bel$ is the *corrected* (post-measurement) belief. **KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.**

### 2.2 The Three Principal Localization Algorithms

#### (a) Markov Localization (Grid-Based)
- Discretizes the state space into a finite grid of cells.
- Stores a discrete probability in each cell.
- Computationally expensive but completely *general* — handles multi-modal beliefs.
- **No assumptions on noise distribution.**

#### (b) Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) Localization
- Represents belief as a *single* **unimodal Gaussian**: $bel(x_t) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$.
- Linearizes non-linear motion and observation models using the **Jacobian matrix**.
- **Best for position tracking**; fails under global localization (cannot represent multiple hypotheses).
- The mean $\mu_t$ is the best pose estimate; $\Sigma_t$ quantifies uncertainty.

#### (c) Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) / Particle Filter
- Represents belief as a discrete set of $M$ weighted samples (particles) $\{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^{M}$.
- Each particle is a *concrete pose hypothesis*; its weight $w$ encodes how well it explains the latest sensor reading.
- Handles multi-modal beliefs naturally; the workhorse of modern robotics (e.g., ROS `amcl` package).

### 2.3 The Robot Motion Model (Velocity / Odometry)

For a unicycle-type robot with translational velocity $v$ and rotational velocity $\omega$ over time $\Delta t$:

$$x_t = \begin{pmatrix} x_{t-1} \\ y_{t-1} \\ \theta_{t-1} \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} -\frac{v}{\omega}\sin\theta_{t-1} + \frac{v}{\omega}\sin(\theta_{t-1} + \omega \Delta t) \\ \frac{v}{\omega}\cos\theta_{t-1} - \frac{v}{\omega}\cos(\theta_{t-1} + \omega \Delta t) \\ \omega \Delta t \end{pmatrix} + \epsilon_t$$

with $\epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$. For straight-line motion ($\omega \to 0$), a L'Hôpital-derived limit is used.

### 2.4 The Sensor / Observation Model (Range-Finder Beam)

For a range sensor returning distance $z_t^k$ along beam $k$ from a robot at pose $(x, y, \theta)$:

$$z_t^k^{\ast} = \min\{z_{\max}, \sqrt{(x_k - x)^2 + (y_k - y)^2}\}$$

where $(x_k, y_k)$ is the intersection of the beam with the nearest obstacle in map $m$. The likelihood is typically a Gaussian:
$$p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} \left[ z_{\text{hit}} \exp\left(-\frac{(z_t^k - z_t^{k\ast})^2}{2\sigma_{\text{hit}}^2}\right) + z_{\max} \cdot z_{\text{rand}} \right]$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Identity | Key Variables & Units | Validity / Assumption |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes Filter — Prediction | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ | $bel$ = belief probability; $u_t$ = control | Markov property |
| 2 | Bayes Filter — Correction | $bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \vert x_t, m) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)$ | $\eta$ = normalizer; $z_t$ = measurement | $\eta = \int p(z_t \vert x', m)\overline{bel}(x') \, dx'$ |
| 3 | Univariate Gaussian PDF | $p(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | $\mu$ = mean; $\sigma^2$ = variance | Scalar random variable |
| 4 | Multivariate Gaussian PDF | $p(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2}\vert\Sigma\vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})\right)$ | $\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\mu} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$; $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ | $\Sigma$ must be positive-definite |
| 5 | EKF State Prediction | $\bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t = g(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{t-1}, u_t)$ | $g$ = non-linear motion model | First-order Taylor approx. |
| 6 | EKF Covariance Prediction | $\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t$ | $G_t$ = Jacobian $\partial g / \partial x$; $R_t$ = motion noise | Unimodal Gaussian approx. |
| 7 | EKF Kalman Gain | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | $H_t$ = Jacobian $\partial h / \partial x$; $Q_t$ = sensor noise | EKF innovation step |
| 8 | EKF State Update | $\boldsymbol{\mu}_t = \bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t))$ | Innovation $= z_t - h(\bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t)$ | Standard EKF |
| 9 | EKF Covariance Update | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$ | $I$ = identity matrix of dim. $n$ | Optimally weighted update |
| 10 | Particle Weight Update | $w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$ | $x_t^{[i]}$ = $i$-th particle pose | Importance sampling |
| 11 | Effective Sample Size | $N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{M} (w_t^{[i]})^2}$ | Threshold typically $0.5 M$ | Triggers resampling |
| 12 | Odometry Motion Update | $x' = x + \Delta_{trans}\cos(\theta + \Delta_{rot1})$ | $\Delta_{trans}, \Delta_{rot1}, \Delta_{rot2}$ from encoder ticks | Probabilistic Robotics (Thrun) |
| 13 | Beam Range Model | $p(z_t^k \vert x_t, m) = \eta \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z_t^k - z_t^{k\ast})^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | $z_t^{k\ast}$ = expected range from ray-cast | Gaussian noise on distance |
| 14 | Planar Pose Composition | $x_t = x_{t-1} \oplus \Delta x_t$ | $\oplus$ = pose composition operator | $SE(2)$ group structure |
| 15 | Rotation Matrix $SO(2)$ | $R(\theta) = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}$ | $\theta$ in radians | Determinant $= +1$ |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla):** MCL on 3D point-cloud maps for centimeter-level lane localization.
- **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva):** EKF on LIDAR for sub-decimeter accuracy among shelves.
- **Indoor Service Robots:** Particle filter with Wi-Fi + LIDAR fusion.
- **Drones (MAV):** Multi-hypothesis EKF for GPS-denied environments using visual-inertial odometry.
- **Planetary Rovers (NASA Perseverance):** Grid-based Markov localization on rocky Martian terrain.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bayes Filter Correction Step

**Goal:** Prove that $bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \vert x_t, m) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)$ is the correct recursive update for the posterior $p(x_t \vert z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$.

**Step 1 — Expand the posterior using the definition of conditional probability:**

$$p(x_t \vert z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m) = \frac{p(z_t \vert x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \cdot p(x_t \vert z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)}{p(z_t \vert z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Markov assumption** (the current measurement $z_t$ depends only on the current state $x_t$ and the map, not on past measurements or controls):

$$p(z_t \vert x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = p(z_t \vert x_t, m)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back:**

$$bel(x_t) = \frac{p(z_t \vert x_t, m) \cdot p(x_t \vert z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)}{p(z_t \vert z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the normalizer $\eta$** as the denominator, since it does not depend on $x_t$:

$$\eta = \left[ p(z_t \vert z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \right]^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Recognize that $p(x_t \vert z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)$ is exactly the predicted belief $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ from Phase 1:**

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \vert x_t, m) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t) \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The denominator $\eta$ acts as a *partition function* ensuring the corrected belief integrates to unity — a fundamental requirement of any valid probability density.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: EKF Localization Covariance Update

**Goal:** Starting from $bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \vert x_t, m) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)$ under Gaussian assumptions, derive the closed-form EKF covariance update $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$.

**Step 1 — Assume both the prior $\overline{bel}$ and the likelihood are Gaussian in $x_t$:**

$$\overline{bel}(x_t) \propto \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}(x_t - \bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t)^T \bar{\Sigma}_t^{-1} (x_t - \bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t)\right)$$

$$p(z_t \vert x_t, m) \propto \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}(z_t - h(x_t))^T Q_t^{-1} (z_t - h(x_t))\right)$$

**Step 2 — Linearize the observation model** $h(x_t)$ around $\bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t$ via first-order Taylor expansion:

$$h(x_t) \approx h(\bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t) + H_t (x_t - \bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t)$$

where $H_t = \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x} \Big\vert_{x = \bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t} \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times n}$ is the observation Jacobian.

**Step 3 — Substitute the linearized model into the likelihood exponent:**

$$(z_t - h(x_t))^T Q_t^{-1} (z_t - h(x_t)) \approx \left[(z_t - h(\bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t)) - H_t (x_t - \bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t)\right]^T Q_t^{-1} [\cdot]$$

**Step 4 — Multiply the two Gaussians.** The product of two Gaussians is again a Gaussian, with exponent being a sum of two quadratics. Collecting terms in $(x_t - \boldsymbol{\mu}_t)^T \Sigma_t^{-1} (x_t - \boldsymbol{\mu}_t)$ and equating coefficients:

$$\Sigma_t^{-1} = \bar{\Sigma}_t^{-1} + H_t^T Q_t^{-1} H_t$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Matrix Inversion Lemma** (Woodbury identity) to avoid explicit $\Sigma_t^{-1}$ computation:

$$(A^{-1} + B^T C^{-1} B)^{-1} = A - A B^T (B A B^T + C)^{-1} B A$$

with $A = \bar{\Sigma}_t$, $B = H_t$, $C = Q_t$:

$$\Sigma_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t - \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1} H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

**Step 6 — Define the Kalman Gain** $K_t$:

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$$

Substituting back yields the compact form:

$$\boxed{\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t} \quad \blacksquare$$

The corresponding mean update can be obtained by completing the square in the exponent:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_t = \bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t + K_t \left[ z_t - h(\bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t) \right]$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: 1-D EKF Position Tracking

A differential-drive robot moves along a 1D line. Its state is $x_t$ (position in meters).

**Given:**
- Initial belief: $\mu_0 = 0$ m, $\Sigma_0 = 1.0$ m²
- Control: $u_1 = +1$ m, with motion noise variance $R_1 = 0.5$ m²
- Motion model: $x_t = x_{t-1} + u_t + \epsilon_t$ (linear, $G_t = 1$)
- Observation: $z_1 = 1.4$ m, with sensor noise variance $Q_1 = 0.3$ m²
- Observation model: $h(x) = x$ (linear, $H_t = 1$)

**Step A — Prediction:**

$$\bar{\mu}_1 = \mu_0 + u_1 = 0 + 1 = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_1 = G_1^2 \Sigma_0 + R_1 = (1)^2 (1.0) + 0.5 = 1.5 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step B — Compute Kalman Gain:**

$$K_1 = \frac{\bar{\Sigma}_1 H_1}{H_1^2 \bar{\Sigma}_1 + Q_1} = \frac{1.5 \cdot 1}{(1)(1.5) + 0.3} = \frac{1.5}{1.8} = 0.8333$$

**Step C — Update mean (Innovation = $z_1 - h(\bar{\mu}_1) = 1.4 - 1.0 = 0.4$):**

$$\mu_1 = \bar{\mu}_1 + K_1 \cdot (z_1 - h(\bar{\mu}_1)) = 1.0 + 0.8333 \cdot 0.4 = 1.0 + 0.3333 = 1.3333 \text{ m}$$

**Step D — Update covariance:**

$$\Sigma_1 = (1 - K_1 H_1) \bar{\Sigma}_1 = (1 - 0.8333 \cdot 1) \cdot 1.5 = 0.1667 \cdot 1.5 = 0.25 \text{ m}^2$$

**Verification:** Notice the posterior variance $\Sigma_1 = 0.25$ m² is strictly less than both the prior $\bar{\Sigma}_1 = 1.5$ m² and the sensor noise $Q_1 = 0.3$ m². **This is the hallmark of Bayesian fusion: combining two noisy estimates always reduces uncertainty.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Localization Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the high-level functional architecture of a generic localization module, mapping how raw sensor and odometry streams are fused through the canonical Bayes filter pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Environment Map m] --> C[Sensor Model p of z given x, m]
    B[Robot Control Input u of t] --> D[Motion Model p of x of t given u, x of t-1]
    E[Sensor Measurement z of t] --> C
    F[Previous Belief bel of x of t-1] --> G[Prediction Step]
    D --> G
    G --> H[Predicted Belief bar bel of x of t]
    H --> I[Multiplication by Likelihood]
    C --> I
    I --> J[Normalization by eta]
    J --> K[Current Belief bel of x of t]
    K --> L[Pose Output x hat of t = argmax bel]
    K --> M[Covariance Output Sigma of t]
    K --> N[Feedback to Next Time Step as F]
    O[Algorithm Selector] --> P1[Grid Markov Localization]
    O --> P2[EKF Localization Gaussian]
    O --> P3[MCL Particle Filter]
    P1 --> G
    P2 --> G
    P3 --> G
```

### 4.2 Comparative Decision Flow: Choosing the Right Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Choose Localization Algorithm] --> Q1{Is initial pose known?}
    Q1 -- Yes, small uncertainty --> EKFPath[EKF Localization]
    Q1 -- No, arbitrary start --> Q2{Is environment small and grid-discretizable?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> GridPath[Grid-Based Markov Localization]
    Q2 -- No --> ParticlePath[Monte Carlo Localization]
    EKFPath --> EndNode[Algorithm Selected]
    GridPath --> EndNode
    ParticlePath --> EndNode
    EndNode --> Z[Output: Pose Estimate]
```

### 4.3 EKF Localization Internal Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[mu of t-1, Sigma of t-1] --> B1[Apply g, mu, ut]
    B1 --> C1[Compute Jacobian Gt]
    C1 --> D1[Predicted mu bar, Sigma bar]
    D1 --> E1[Apply h, mu bar]
    E1 --> F1[Compute Jacobian Ht]
    F1 --> G1[Compute Kalman Gain Kt]
    G1 --> H1[Update mu of t]
    G1 --> I1[Update Sigma of t]
    H1 --> J1[mu of t, Sigma of t]
    I1 --> J1
    J1 --> A1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip (KTU Examiner's Favorite):** When asked to "draw the EKF cycle," always show the *two parallel paths* — one for the mean $\mu$ (top) and one for the covariance $\Sigma$ (bottom). Both share the same Kalman gain $K_t$, which is the *information bridge* between the measurement and prediction.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define localization in mobile robotics and distinguish between local and global localization. Provide one example algorithm for each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Localization is the process of estimating a robot's pose $(x, y, \theta)$ within a known map by fusing sensor observations and control inputs through a probabilistic filter.
- **Local localization (Position Tracking):** The robot's initial pose is approximately known and uncertainty is small. The problem is to correct small drifts. **Algorithm: Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) Localization.**
- **Global localization:** The robot is placed at an unknown pose anywhere in the environment, with no prior estimate. The belief must be *multi-modal* until the robot gathers enough data. **Algorithm: Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) using a particle filter.**

*(Mark Split: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark distinction, 1 mark for algorithm example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Sessional-I]**
*What is the kidnapped robot problem, and why does it pose a fundamental challenge to localization algorithms?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **kidnapped robot problem** is a variant of global localization in which a fully operational robot is suddenly teleported to an arbitrary, unknown pose in its environment. It is the *gold-standard stress test* for a localization algorithm because:
1. The algorithm must **recover** from a catastrophically wrong belief without permanent failure.
2. It demands the belief representation be *multi-modal* — a single Gaussian (as in EKF) cannot recover because it will collapse around the previous wrong estimate and never re-explore.
3. It tests the algorithm's ability to **inject random hypotheses** (e.g., random particle injection in MCL) to recover from localization failure.

*(Mark Split: 1 mark definition, 2 marks for the algorithmic challenge.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Localization Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $\overline{bel}$ with $bel$.** The bar denotes *pre-correction*; missing the bar loses 1–2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the normalizer $\eta$.** The correction equation is *not* complete without the normalization constant — a common 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Mixing up $R_t$ and $Q_t$.** $R_t$ is *motion* (process) noise covariance; $Q_t$ is *sensor* (measurement) noise covariance. Reversing them is a fatal error.
> 4. **Writing Jacobian transpose incorrectly.** The Kalman gain requires $H_t^T$, not $H_t$. Easy 1-mark slip.
> 5. **Not mentioning Markov assumption** in Bayes filter derivations — KTU examiners reward this 1-mark keyword heavily.

---

### 5.3 Part B — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]`

**(a) Derive the Bayes filter recursion for robot localization. Clearly state the Markov assumption and explain the role of the normalizer. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 Marks):**

The Bayes filter estimates the posterior belief:
$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$$

**Step 1 — Apply Bayes' Rule:**
$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)}$$

**Step 2 — Invoke the Markov Assumption:** The current measurement $z_t$ depends only on the current state $x_t$ and the map, not on the history:
$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$

**Step 3 — Define the predicted belief:**
$$\overline{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the normalizer $\eta$:**
$$\eta = \frac{1}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)} = \left[\int p(z_t \mid x', m) \cdot \overline{bel}(x') \, dx'\right]^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Final Recursion:**
$$bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Role of $\eta$:** It is a scalar that ensures the corrected belief integrates to unity, making $bel$ a valid probability density. It is the reciprocal of the *measurement likelihood averaged over the predicted belief*.

*(Mark Split: 1 mark Markov assumption, 2 marks Bayes expansion, 2 marks prediction integral, 1 mark normalizer definition, 1 mark final equation.)*

---

**(b) Explain the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) localization algorithm with its prediction and update equations. Why does EKF fail under global localization? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 Marks):**

EKF localization maintains a *single unimodal Gaussian* belief:
$$bel(x_t) \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_t, \Sigma_t)$$

**Prediction Step (Motion Update):**

Given control $u_t$, the non-linear motion model $g(\cdot)$ is linearized via the Jacobian $G_t = \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial x}\Big\vert_{\boldsymbol{\mu}_{t-1}}$:

$$\bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t = g(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{t-1}, u_t)$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t$$

**Update Step (Measurement Update):**

Given observation $z_t$, the non-linear observation model $h(\cdot)$ is linearized via $H_t = \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}\Big\vert_{\bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t}$:

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$$

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_t = \bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t))$$

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

**Why EKF Fails Under Global Localization:**

1. **Single-mode assumption:** A single Gaussian can only represent *one* hypothesis. When the initial pose is unknown, the true belief is *multi-modal* (peaks at every plausible pose).
2. **Convergence to wrong mode:** The Gaussian will collapse to the *first* hypothesis consistent with sensor data, locking out alternatives — a phenomenon called **localization lock-out**.
3. **Linearization errors:** Jacobians are evaluated at the mean, which can be far from the true pose initially, making the Taylor approximation invalid.
4. **No recovery from failure:** Unlike MCL, EKF has no mechanism to re-inject diverse hypotheses once it has converged.

Hence EKF is restricted to **position tracking**, and global localization requires **grid-based** or **particle filter** methods.

*(Mark Split: 1 mark belief representation, 2 marks prediction, 2 marks update, 2 marks failure analysis.)*

---

### 5.4 Part B — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Expected Pattern]`

**(a) Describe the Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) algorithm in detail. Include the importance sampling step, weight update, and resampling procedure. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 Marks):**

MCL represents the belief $bel(x_t)$ as a set of $M$ weighted samples (particles):
$$\mathcal{X}_t = \{ x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]} \}_{i=1}^{M}$$

where each $x_t^{[i]}$ is a pose hypothesis and $w_t^{[i]}$ is its importance weight, with $\sum_i w_t^{[i]} = 1$.

**Step 1 — Initialization (Global localization):** Sample $M$ particles uniformly at random across the free space of map $m$. Each particle has weight $w_0^{[i]} = 1/M$.

**Step 2 — Motion Update (Sampling):** For each particle, sample a new pose from the motion model:
$$x_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})$$

This propagates the particle cloud according to odometry.

**Step 3 — Measurement Update (Importance Weighting):** Compute the importance factor for each particle by evaluating the sensor likelihood:
$$w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$$

Particles whose poses *predict* a sensor reading close to $z_t$ receive higher weights.

**Step 4 — Normalization:**
$$w_t^{[i]} = \frac{w_t^{[i]}}{\sum_{j=1}^{M} w_t^{[j]}}$$

**Step 5 — Resampling (Survival of the Fittest):** Draw $M$ new particles from the current set with replacement, with selection probability proportional to $w_t^{[i]}$. This eliminates low-weight particles and replicates high-weight ones. The new particles all receive weight $1/M$.

**Optional — Resampling Trigger:** Resampling is performed only when the effective sample size $N_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2}$ falls below a threshold (typically $0.5M$), preserving particle diversity.

**Output:** The pose estimate is the weighted mean:
$$\hat{x}_t = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} x_t^{[i]}$$

*(Mark Split: 1 mark belief representation, 2 marks motion update, 2 marks weight update, 1 mark resampling, 1 mark output.)*

---

**(b) A differential-drive robot operates in a known 1D corridor. At $t = 0$, its belief is $\mu_0 = 0$ m, $\Sigma_0 = 2.0$ m². The robot receives control $u_1 = 2$ m with motion noise variance $R_1 = 0.4$ m². The motion model is $x_t = x_{t-1} + u_t + \epsilon_t$ and observation model is $h(x) = x$ with $z_1 = 2.6$ m and sensor noise variance $Q_1 = 0.2$ m². Compute the EKF posterior $\mu_1, \Sigma_1$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 Marks):**

**Given data summary:**
- $\mu_0 = 0$ m, $\Sigma_0 = 2.0$ m²
- $u_1 = 2$ m, $R_1 = 0.4$ m²
- $G_1 = \partial g / \partial x = 1$, $H_1 = \partial h / \partial x = 1$
- $z_1 = 2.6$ m, $Q_1 = 0.2$ m²

**Step 1 — Prediction of mean:**
$$\bar{\mu}_1 = \mu_0 + u_1 = 0 + 2 = 2.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Prediction of covariance:**
$$\bar{\Sigma}_1 = G_1^2 \Sigma_0 + R_1 = (1)^2 (2.0) + 0.4 = 2.4 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Kalman Gain:**
$$K_1 = \frac{\bar{\Sigma}_1 H_1}{H_1^2 \bar{\Sigma}_1 + Q_1} = \frac{2.4 \cdot 1}{1 \cdot 2.4 + 0.2} = \frac{2.4}{2.6} = 0.9231$$

**Step 4 — Innovation:**
$$\nu_1 = z_1 - h(\bar{\mu}_1) = 2.6 - 2.0 = 0.6 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Posterior mean:**
$$\mu_1 = \bar{\mu}_1 + K_1 \cdot \nu_1 = 2.0 + 0.9231 \cdot 0.6 = 2.0 + 0.5538 = 2.5538 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Posterior covariance:**
$$\Sigma_1 = (1 - K_1 H_1) \bar{\Sigma}_1 = (1 - 0.9231)(2.4) = (0.0769)(2.4) = 0.1846 \text{ m}^2$$

**Verification of Bayesian optimality:**
- Prior variance: $\bar{\Sigma}_1 = 2.4$ m²
- Sensor noise: $Q_1 = 0.2$ m²
- Posterior variance: $\Sigma_1 = 0.1846$ m² $< \min(\bar{\Sigma}_1, Q_1)$ ✓

The uncertainty has been reduced by approximately **92.3%** through the fusion of odometry and sensor data.

*(Mark Split: 1 mark each for steps 1–5, 1 mark for verification, 1 mark for uncertainty reduction calculation.)*

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Localization**

- [ ] Localization estimates the **pose** $(x, y, \theta)^T$ using controls and observations.
- [ ] The general solution is the **Bayes filter** with two phases: **Prediction** ($\overline{bel}$) and **Correction** ($bel$).
- [ ] The **Markov assumption** states that the future is conditionally independent of the past given the present.
- [ ] The **normalizer** $\eta$ enforces $\int bel(x_t) \, dx_t = 1$ — never omit it.
- [ ] **EKF Localization:** unimodal Gaussian $(\boldsymbol{\mu}_t, \Sigma_t)$; only suitable for *position tracking*.
- [ ] **MCL:** particle set; suitable for *global localization* and the *kidnapped robot* problem.
- [ ] **Grid-based Markov Localization:** discrete grid; computationally heavy but most general.
- [ ] The **Kalman gain** $K_t$ is the optimal weight balancing prediction trust and sensor trust.
- [ ] **Covariance always decreases** after a successful EKF update: $\Sigma_t < \bar{\Sigma}_t$ and $\Sigma_t < Q_t$.
- [ ] **Motion noise** $R_t$ comes from odometry errors; **sensor noise** $Q_t$ comes from measurement errors. Never swap them.
- [ ] **Jacobians** $G_t, H_t$ are evaluated at $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{t-1}$ and $\bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t$ respectively.
- [ ] The **kidnapped robot problem** is the canonical stress test; only MCL with random particle injection handles it.
- [ ] **Particle depletion** in MCL is countered via the **effective sample size** criterion $N_{\text{eff}}$.
- [ ] The **multivariate Gaussian** is the backbone of EKF; its mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ is a vector and covariance $\Sigma$ is a positive-definite matrix.
- [ ] In KTU derivations, **always state the Markov assumption** before applying Bayes' rule — it is a frequently tested 1-mark keyword.
- [ ] **Pose dimension** $n = 3$ for planar ground robots; $n = 6$ for aerial vehicles.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cognition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cognition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cognition** in robotics is the *high-level information-processing capability* of an autonomous robot that enables it to **perceive**, **reason**, **represent**, **plan**, and **learn** about its environment, its own internal state, and the tasks it must execute, in order to produce **goal-directed motion and action**.

In the KTU 2024 OEBRT833 syllabus, cognition is positioned as the **deliberative layer** that sits *above* low-level motion control and *below* high-level task supervision. It is the "thinking" brain of the robot — converting raw sensor data and abstract goals into **symbolic representations** that drive motion planners.

Formally, a cognitive robot maintains a **World Model** $\mathcal{W}$ and a **Self Model** $\mathcal{S}$, and operates the closed loop:

$$
\text{Sense} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Perceive \& Update } \mathcal{W} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Reason \& Plan} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Act}
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Cognition as a Driver's Mind

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a robot as a **self-driving car**, and *cognition* as the **driver's conscious mind**:
>
> - **Eyes & ears** = Sensors (LIDAR, cameras, IMU)
> - **Memory of the city** = World Model $\mathcal{W}$ (a map of obstacles, lanes, traffic rules)
> - **Thinking about the route** = Reasoning & Planning layer
> - **Hands on the steering wheel** = Motion Controller (low-level actuator commands)
> - **"I will turn right at the next signal"** = An *intention* selected by the cognitive layer
>
> A robot without cognition is like a driver with reflexes only — it can brake if a wall is near, but it cannot decide *where* to drive. **Cognition is what converts a reactive body into a purposeful agent.**

## 1.3 Why Cognition Matters in Robot Motion Planning

A purely reactive robot (Braitenberg vehicles, simple bug algorithms) cannot:

- Plan a route across an unknown map.
- Reason about object permanence ("the cup I saw earlier is still on the table").
- Replan when the goal changes dynamically.
- Explain *why* it chose a particular trajectory.
- Learn from past failures.

Cognition provides all of these capabilities. In modern KTU-aligned curricula, the **cognitive layer** is what makes a robot truly *autonomous* rather than merely *automated*.

## 1.4 The Three Cognitive Levels (Hierarchical View)

| Level | Name | Time Horizon | Function | Example |
|:------|:-----|:-------------|:---------|:--------|
| **Low** | Reactive (Sensorimotor) | **< 100 ms** | Direct reflex-style mapping | Obstacle avoidance via potential fields |
| **Mid** | Executive / Deliberative | **0.1 – 10 s** | Path & motion planning | A\* path planner, RRT |
| **High** | Cognitive | **Minutes – Hours** | Task reasoning, world modelling, learning | "Fetch the red ball from room B" |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU module on *Cognition* focuses on the **High** level — the symbolic, deliberative, knowledge-based layer that *feeds goals and constraints* into the motion planner.

## 1.5 Distinction from Related Terms

- **Automation** → fixed, pre-programmed behaviour. **Cognition** → adaptive, goal-directed, knowledge-based.
- **Control** → continuous-time regulation. **Cognition** → discrete, symbolic, deliberative.
- **Reactive AI** → stimulus–response. **Cognitive AI** → belief–desire–intention (BDI) loops.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Robot Cognition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU-grade cognitive system for motion planning is built on **five functional pillars**:
>
> 1. **Perception** — extracting symbols from sensors.
> 2. **Representation** — building a World Model $\mathcal{W}$.
> 3. **Reasoning** — drawing inferences over $\mathcal{W}$.
> 4. **Planning** — generating a sequence of actions.
> 5. **Learning** — improving $\mathcal{W}$ and policies over time.

### Pillar 1 — Perception
Maps raw sensor streams $\mathbf{z}_t$ into symbolic observations $o_t$.
Example: a camera image $I_t \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$ is transformed into a list of detected objects with bounding boxes and class labels.

$$
o_t = f_{\text{percept}}(\mathbf{z}_t) = \{\,(\ell_i, \mathbf{p}_i, \mathbf{c}_i)\,\}_{i=1}^{N_t}
$$

where $\ell_i$ = label, $\mathbf{p}_i$ = 3D position, $\mathbf{c}_i$ = confidence score.

### Pillar 2 — Representation (World Model)
The world model is a structured, updatable knowledge base:

$$
\mathcal{W}_t = \big\langle \mathcal{O}, \mathcal{R}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{T}, \mathcal{G} \big\rangle
$$

where:
- $\mathcal{O}$ = set of objects (the **ontology**)
- $\mathcal{R}$ = spatial / semantic relations ("on", "inside", "near")
- $\mathcal{A}$ = agent's own state
- $\mathcal{T}$ = temporal / causal rules
- $\mathcal{G}$ = current goal stack

### Pillar 3 — Reasoning
Logical or probabilistic inference over $\mathcal{W}$. Two dominant paradigms:

| Paradigm | Engine | Strength | Weakness |
|:---------|:-------|:---------|:---------|
| **Symbolic** | First-order logic, PDDL, STRIPS | Explainable, verifiable | Brittle under uncertainty |
| **Probabilistic** | Bayesian Networks, POMDPs | Handles noise & partial observability | Computationally expensive |

### Pillar 4 — Planning
Given $\mathcal{W}_t$ and goal $g$, search for an action sequence $\pi = (a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_K)$ that achieves $g$. This is the *task planner*, which sits *above* the motion planner of later KTU modules.

### Pillar 5 — Learning
Update $\mathcal{W}$ or planner parameters from experience:
- **Supervised**: learn object classifiers.
- **Reinforcement**: learn navigation policies.
- **Imitation**: learn from human demonstrations.

---

## 2.2 Classical Cognitive Architectures (KTU High-Yield)

### 2.2.1 The Sense–Plan–Act (SPA) Paradigm — Nilsson, 1984
The foundational *deliberative* architecture used in Shakey the robot.

$$
\text{Sensors} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Perception}}\; \text{World Model} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Planner}}\; \text{Plan} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Actuators}}\; \text{Action}
$$

**Strengths**: globally optimal, explainable. **Weaknesses**: slow, brittle, cannot recover from surprises mid-plan.

### 2.2.2 Subsumption Architecture — Brooks, 1986
A *reactive* counter-revolution. Behaviors are layered; higher layers *subsume* (inhibit) lower layers.

| Layer | Behaviour | Example |
|:------|:----------|:--------|
| 0 | Avoid obstacles | Emergency stop |
| 1 | Wander | Random walk |
| 2 | Map building | Build occupancy grid |
| 3 | Plan / reason | Recharge when battery low |
| 4 | Mission execution | "Patrol corridor A" |

**Strengths**: fast, robust. **Weaknesses**: no global optimality, hard to debug, limited reasoning.

### 2.2.3 3T Architecture — Bonasso et al., 1997
A *hybrid* architecture that explicitly combines three layers:

- **Reactive Skills Layer** (control loops, < 100 ms)
- **Sequencing Layer** (state-machine task manager, ~1 s)
- **Deliberative Layer** (AI planner, minutes)

This is the architecture most KTU exam questions on "cognitive robots" map to.

### 2.2.4 BDI (Belief–Desire–Intention) Model — Rao & Georgeff, 1995
A philosophical cognitive model from agent theory:

$$
\pi_{\text{BDI}} = \text{SelectPlan}(\text{Options}(\text{Beliefs}, \text{Desires}), \text{Intentions})
$$

- **Beliefs** = current $\mathcal{W}$
- **Desires** = all possible goals
- **Intentions** = committed goals currently being pursued

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise the following table — it is the *high-yield* reference for KTU ESE questions on Cognition.

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Domain / Units |
|:-----------------|:--------|:---------------|
| $\mathcal{W}_t = \langle \mathcal{O}, \mathcal{R}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{T}, \mathcal{G} \rangle$ | World Model tuple | Symbolic, dimensionless |
| $o_t = f_{\text{percept}}(\mathbf{z}_t)$ | Perception function | Sensor $\to$ Symbol |
| $\pi^\* = \arg\min_{\pi} \; C(\pi)$ | Optimal plan minimising cost | Discrete actions |
| $C(\pi) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} c(a_k)$ | Plan cost as sum of step costs | Energy / time / risk |
| $\text{Belief}_{t+1} = \text{Update}(\text{Belief}_t, a_t, \mathbf{z}_{t+1})$ | Bayesian belief update | Probabilistic |
| $P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$ | Transition model (MDP) | Probability $\in [0,1]$ |
| $R(s_t, a_t) \in \mathbb{R}$ | Reward / utility of state-action | Scalar |
| $V^\pi(s) = \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t \, \mathbb{E}[R_t \mid \pi, s_0 = s]$ | Value function under discount $\gamma \in [0,1)$ | Scalar |
| $T_{\text{cog}} \propto \vert \mathcal{W} \vert \cdot \log \vert \mathcal{O} \vert$ | Cognitive processing time (heuristic) | Seconds |
| $\text{Reactivity} = 1 / T_{\text{cog}}$ | Inverse of cognitive latency | Hz |
| $\text{RSS} = \sum_{i} \Vert \mathbf{x}_{\text{robot},i} - \mathbf{x}_{\text{human},i} \Vert^2$ | Safety metric (RSS = Responsibility-Sensitive Safety) | $\text{m}^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical bar in $\vert \mathcal{W} \vert$ denotes **cardinality** (set size), not a markdown table separator. The notation is rendered using `\vert` to remain KTU-LaTeX compliant.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where Cognitive Robots Are Used

| Industry | Cognitive Function | Motion Planning Role |
|:--------|:-------------------|:---------------------|
| Autonomous vehicles | Scene understanding, intent prediction | Lane / overtaking planner |
| Warehouse AMR (e.g. Amazon Kiva) | Map building, task allocation | A\* on occupancy grid |
| Surgical robots | Anatomical landmark recognition | RRT\* in deformable tissue |
| Humanoid robots (ASIMO, Pepper) | Speech intent, gesture recognition | Whole-body ZMP planner |
| Space rovers (NASA Perseverance) | Long-horizon science planning | Strategic traverse planner |
| Domestic service robots | Object permanence, room semantics | Fetch-and-carry planner |

> [!NOTE]
> The unifying theme: **cognition converts *perception* into *purposeful motion goals*** — the bridge the rest of the KTU OEBRT833 modules will expand into algorithms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — The Sense–Plan–Act Closed-Loop Latency

A KTU 2024 exam favourite is to compute the *cognitive cycle time* $T_{\text{cog}}$ of a deliberative robot. We derive it here step by step.

### Step 1 — Identify the loop stages
The robot performs four operations each cycle:

- Sensing + filtering: $T_s$
- Perception (feature extraction, object detection): $T_p$
- Reasoning + planning: $T_r$
- Actuation (low-level command dispatch): $T_a$

### Step 2 — Sum the serial stages
Because each stage consumes the output of the previous, the *total* cognitive cycle time is the *sum*:

$$
T_{\text{cog}} \;=\; T_s + T_p + T_r + T_a
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute representative models

Assume a LIDAR sensor with sweep frequency $f_s = 10 \,\text{Hz}$, a CNN perception module, an A\* planner, and a controller running at $1 \,\text{kHz}$:

$$
T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.100 \;\text{s}
$$

$$
T_p = \frac{1}{f_p} = \frac{1}{30} = 0.033 \;\text{s} \quad (30 \,\text{Hz CNN})
$$

$$
T_r = \frac{\vert \mathcal{O} \vert \cdot \log \vert \mathcal{O} \vert}{f_{\text{CPU}}} \;\approx\; \frac{1000 \cdot 10}{10^9} = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{s}
$$

$$
T_a = \frac{1}{f_a} = \frac{1}{1000} = 0.001 \;\text{s}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the total

$$
T_{\text{cog}} \;=\; 0.100 + 0.033 + 1.0 \times 10^{-5} + 0.001
$$

$$
T_{\text{cog}} \;\approx\; 0.134 \;\text{s}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpret
The dominant term is **sensing** ($T_s = 0.100$ s). This is the *bottleneck* of the SPA loop. A KTU examiner would award full marks only if a student explicitly identifies the bottleneck and explains *why* a higher sensor rate (e.g. event camera, $f_s = 1000$ Hz) would shrink the loop.

> [!NOTE]
> A *reactive* (subsumption) robot skips the $T_p$ and $T_r$ stages, giving $T_{\text{cog}} \approx 1$ ms — three orders of magnitude faster. **This is the engineering trade-off that motivates hybrid 3T architectures.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Optimal Plan Cost in a Finite World Model

Given world model $\mathcal{W} = \langle S, A, T, c \rangle$ where $S$ is the discrete state space, $A$ the action set, $T$ the deterministic transition function, and $c(s, a) \ge 0$ the per-step cost, the **optimal plan** $\pi^\*$ from start $s_0$ to goal $g \subset S$ is:

$$
\pi^\* \;=\; \arg\min_{\pi \in \Pi} \; C(\pi) \;=\; \arg\min_{\pi \in \Pi} \; \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} c(s_k, a_k)
$$

subject to $s_{k+1} = T(s_k, a_k)$ and $s_K \in g$.

### Step 1 — Define the cost-to-go $V(s)$
The **Bellman optimality equation** for a deterministic shortest-path planner:

$$
V(s) \;=\; \min_{a \in A} \;\Big[\, c(s, a) + V(T(s, a)) \,\Big] \quad \text{with} \quad V(s) = 0 \;\forall s \in g
$$

### Step 2 — Expand recursively for a 3-state world
Let $S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2\}$ with $g = \{s_2\}$. The transitions are:

$$
T(s_0, a_1) = s_1, \quad T(s_0, a_2) = s_2, \quad T(s_1, a_3) = s_2
$$

Costs: $c(s_0, a_1) = 5$, $c(s_0, a_2) = 9$, $c(s_1, a_3) = 3$.

### Step 3 — Backward induction (the cognitive "reasoning" step)
Boundary: $V(s_2) = 0$.

Compute $V(s_1)$:

$$
V(s_1) = \min_{a \in A(s_1)} [c(s_1, a_3) + V(s_2)] = 3 + 0 = 3
$$

Compute $V(s_0)$:

$$
V(s_0) = \min\{\, c(s_0, a_1) + V(s_1),\; c(s_0, a_2) + V(s_2) \,\}
$$

$$
V(s_0) = \min\{\, 5 + 3,\; 9 + 0 \,\} = \min\{8, 9\} = 8
$$

### Step 4 — Extract the optimal plan

$$
\pi^\* = (a_1,\; a_3) \quad \text{with} \quad C(\pi^\*) = 8
$$

### Step 5 — KTU interpretation
The *cognitive layer* runs this backward induction on the *symbolic* world model $\mathcal{W}$ to produce an abstract plan $(a_1, a_3)$. The *motion planning layer* (later modules) then expands each abstract action into a geometric trajectory. **Cognition answers "what to do"; motion planning answers "how to move".**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cognitive vs Reactive Trade-off

| Property | Cognitive (SPA) | Reactive (Subsumption) | Hybrid (3T) |
|:---------|:----------------|:-----------------------|:------------|
| Loop time $T_{\text{cog}}$ | **> 100 ms** | **< 10 ms** | **10 ms – 1 s (adaptive)** |
| Optimality of plan | **Global** | **Local only** | **Global + local** |
| Robustness to surprise | Low | High | High |
| Reasoning depth | Deep (logic, search) | None | Moderate |
| Memory requirement | Large (world model) | Small | Large |
| Learning capability | Strong | Weak | Strong |
| Real-time guarantee | No | Yes | Bounded |
| KTU-typical exam marks | 7 | 7 | 14 (favourite) |

> [!NOTE]
> For a 14-mark KTU ESE question, the *3T hybrid architecture* is the most likely answer, because it forces the student to discuss **all three layers** and their interaction — exactly the depth a board examiner wants to test.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Cognitive Architecture (3T Hybrid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Goals and Tasks from Operator] --> B[Deliberative Layer Task Planner]
    B --> C[Sequencing Layer State Machine]
    C --> D[Reactive Skills Layer Control Loops]
    D --> E[Actuators and Motors]
    F[Sensor Suite LIDAR Camera IMU] --> G[Perception Module]
    G --> H[World Model Wt Symbolic KB]
    H --> B
    H --> C
    H --> D
    E --> I[Environment]
    I --> F
    style A fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#ff9966,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#ff6633,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style E fill:#cc3300,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style F fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#99ccff,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#6699ff,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style I fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#333
```

## 4.2 Subsumption Layered Architecture (Brooks)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Layer 0 Emergency Stop] --> L1
    L1[Layer 1 Wander] --> L2
    L2[Layer 2 Build Map] --> L3
    L3[Layer 3 Monitor Battery] --> L4
    L4[Layer 4 Mission Execution]
    SENS[Sensor Inputs] --> L0
    SENS --> L1
    SENS --> L2
    SENS --> L3
    SENS --> L4
    L4 --> ACT[Actuator Outputs]
    L3 --> ACT
    L2 --> ACT
    L1 --> ACT
    L0 --> ACT
    style L0 fill:#ff4d4d,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style L1 fill:#ff9933,stroke:#333
    style L2 fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#333
    style L3 fill:#99cc33,stroke:#333
    style L4 fill:#339966,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 BDI Agent Reasoning Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BEL[Beliefs Current World Model] --> REV[Belief Revision Function]
    OBS[New Observations] --> REV
    REV --> BEL
    BEL --> OPT[Option Generation Generate Desires]
    OPT --> DES[Desires Candidate Goals]
    DES --> FIL[Filter Select Intentions]
    FIL --> INT[Intentions Committed Goals]
    INT --> EXE[Action Execution]
    EXE --> OBS
    style BEL fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333
    style REV fill:#99ccff,stroke:#333
    style OPT fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#333
    style DES fill:#ff9966,stroke:#333
    style FIL fill:#ff6633,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style INT fill:#cc3300,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style EXE fill:#999999,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style OBS fill:#6699ff,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Sense-Plan-Act Closed Loop with Bottleneck Highlight

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sensors] -->|Ts 0.1 s BOTTLENECK| S2[Perception]
    S2 -->|Tp 0.033 s| S3[World Model Update]
    S3 -->|Tr ~10 us| S4[Reasoning and Task Planner]
    S4 -->|Ta 1 ms| S5[Actuators]
    S5 --> S6[Environment]
    S6 --> S1
    style S1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333
    style S2 fill:#99ccff,stroke:#333
    style S3 fill:#6699ff,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style S4 fill:#3366cc,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style S5 fill:#003399,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style S6 fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the above diagram, the *sensing* stage is intentionally the widest and labelled "BOTTLENECK" to mirror the derivation in Section 3.1. The cognitive layer is the **narrowest** (fastest) — it is the *perception* and *sensor* stages that dominate the loop.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define *cognition* in the context of robot motion planning. Mention the five functional pillars that constitute a cognitive robotic system.**

**Model Answer (10 lines, 3 marks):**

> *Cognition* in robot motion planning is the high-level information-processing capability that allows a robot to **perceive** its environment, **represent** it symbolically as a world model, **reason** over that model, **plan** goal-directed actions, and **learn** from experience. The five functional pillars are:
>
> 1. **Perception** — converting raw sensor data into symbols.
> 2. **Representation** — maintaining a World Model $\mathcal{W}_t$.
> 3. **Reasoning** — logical / probabilistic inference.
> 4. **Planning** — generating action sequences.
> 5. **Learning** — updating $\mathcal{W}$ and policies.
>
> Together, these pillars convert sensor streams into *purposeful motion goals* that the motion planner then executes geometrically.

*(Mark split: 1 for definition, 1 for listing, 1 for brief explanation of bridge to motion planner.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**Compare the Sense–Plan–Act (SPA) paradigm and Brooks' Subsumption Architecture along three dimensions.**

**Model Answer (8 lines, 3 marks):**

| Dimension | Sense–Plan–Act (SPA) | Subsumption |
|:----------|:---------------------|:------------|
| **Reasoning** | Global, symbolic | None, layer-by-layer reflexes |
| **Speed** | Slow ($> 100$ ms loop) | Very fast ($< 10$ ms) |
| **Robustness** | Brittle to surprise | Highly robust |

*(Mark split: 1 per dimension.)*

---

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Cognitive-Architecture Questions):**
> Students frequently lose marks by (i) drawing a flowchart but **not naming the layers** with their time-constants, (ii) confusing the *deliberative* layer with the *reactive* layer, and (iii) **failing to compute** an example latency or plan cost. Always *label* every block with a time-budget and *show one numerical example* in a 14-mark answer.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP — Module 1 Choice]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **3T (Three-Tier) hybrid cognitive architecture** for a mobile robot. For each tier, state its function, typical time-constant, and one example algorithm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3T robot operates in a grid world with the cost-to-go values shown in the table below. Starting state is $s_0$, goal is $g = \{s_4\}$. The deterministic transitions and per-step costs are:

$$
T(s_0, a) = s_1, \quad c(s_0, a) = 4
$$

$$
