- **Advantage 1 (1 mark):** It is a *fail-safe* sensor — its failure mode (no signal) leaves the robot in its safe default state, unlike LIDAR which may give false negatives and cause crashes.
- **Advantage 2 (1 mark):** It has near-zero latency ($t_r < 5$ ms) and is unaffected by optical interference (sunlight, reflective surfaces, glass, smoke) — environments that cripple LIDAR.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**  
*List the components of a typical bumper sensor assembly. State the role of the debounce circuit.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Components (2 marks):** (i) Outer compliant skin (rubber/foam), (ii) Mechanical micro-switch (SPST), (iii) Mounting bracket with spring return, (iv) Pull-up resistor + debounce RC network, (v) Connector to microcontroller GPIO.
- **Debounce Role (1 mark):** The debounce RC filter eliminates the high-frequency mechanical bouncing of the switch contacts (typically 0.5–5 ms) and presents a clean, monotonic digital edge to the controller, preventing false multiple-trigger interrupts.

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU 2024 Pattern Note]:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer **one full question** out of the two choices provided.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[Modelled after KTU S8 Elective Paper Style]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working principle of a mechanical bumper sensor** used in a mobile robot. Describe the role of each component in the signal chain. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw or describe the chain: *Compliant Skin → Micro-switch → Debounce RC → Pull-up Resistor → GPIO → MCU ISR → Motor Driver Stop Command*. (A textual description with arrows is acceptable.)
2. **Working Principle (2 marks):** When the robot in motion strikes an obstacle, the compliant skin compresses, transmitting force $F_c$ to the micro-switch. When $F_c \geq F_{act}$, the contacts close, completing the circuit. The MCU's GPIO pin reads a logic transition, fires an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR), and the motion controller halts the motors.
3. **Component Roles (2 marks):**
   - *Compliant skin:* Absorbs impact energy, protects internal components.
   - *Micro-switch:* Converts mechanical force into electrical binary signal.
   - *Debounce RC:* Filters mechanical bounce ($t_b \approx 1$ ms).
   - *Pull-up resistor:* Ensures defined logic HIGH when switch is open.
   - *MCU ISR:* Prioritised, low-latency response ($< 1$ ms).

**(b)** A mobile robot of mass $m = 30$ kg moving at $v = 2.0$ m/s strikes a wall. The bumper skin deflects by $x = 2$ cm during contact. The contact duration is $\Delta t = 40$ ms. Compute:
   (i) the average impact force,
   (ii) the kinetic energy dissipated, and
   (iii) the required spring constant of an equivalent linear bumper spring. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Average Impact Force (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta p &= m \cdot (v_f - v_i) = 30 \cdot (0 - 2.0) = -60 \text{ kg·m/s} \\
F_{avg} &= \frac{\vert \Delta p \vert}{\Delta t} = \frac{60}{0.040} \\
F_{avg} &= 1500 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Kinetic Energy Dissipated (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
KE &= \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 30 \times (2.0)^2 \\
KE &= 60 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Equivalent Spring Constant (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{spring} &= \frac{1}{2} k x^2 = KE = 60 \text{ J} \\
k &= \frac{2 \times 60}{(0.02)^2} = \frac{120}{0.0004} \\
k &= 300{,}000 \text{ N/m} = 3.0 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** Compare and contrast **bumper sensors, ultrasonic sensors, and IR proximity sensors** as obstacle detection modalities for a mobile robot. Use a comparison table covering at least six parameters. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A student should present a clear table such as the **Analytical Decision Matrix** given in Section 3.3, but expanded. Expected parameters include: *detection principle, range, output type, response time, cost, sensitivity to environment, blind spots, and use-case in motion planning.* (3 marks for table, 2 marks for narrative comparison, 2 marks for concluding remark on which is best for *safety-critical* applications — answer: **bumper**).

**(b)** A differential-drive mobile robot uses a **3-zone bumper** (Front-Left, Front-Centre, Front-Right). Describe the **finite state machine (FSM)** that processes bumper events to decide the recovery behaviour. Draw the FSM and explain any two states in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **FSM Diagram (3 marks):** Similar to the Mermaid state diagram in Section 4.3 — states: *Cruising → Sensing → Bumper_Triggered → Emergency_Stop → Reverse → Rotate → Cruising*. Should include direction-aware branching (FL/FC/FR zones).
2. **State Explanation — Emergency_Stop (2 marks):** The moment the bumper interrupt fires, the controller sets motor PWM duty cycle to 0% on both wheels. Encoders confirm velocity $v = 0$ within 50 ms.
3. **State Explanation — Recovery (Rotate) (2 marks):** The robot reverses for a fixed distance (e.g., 20 cm), then rotates in place by an angle $\theta$ chosen by a *random or rule-based policy* (e.g., turn toward the zone with the fewest historical impacts). After rotation, the FSM returns to the *Cruising* state and the global planner resumes.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (as observed in KTU Valuation Keys):**
> 1. **Defining bumper as a "range sensor" (–1 mark):** The bumper provides *no* distance information. Writing "bumper measures distance to obstacle" is factually wrong and will be flagged.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers (–1 mark):** Always write `N`, `m/s`, `J`, `kg·m/s` explicitly. Bare numbers lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the debounce discussion (–1 mark):** In Part B questions on bumper *design*, examiners specifically look for the term "debounce delay" or "RC filter." Omitting it is a frequent, costly mistake.
> 4. **Confusing NO vs. NC switch logic (–1 mark):** Be precise — specify whether the circuit is *Normally Open (NO)* or *Normally Closed (NC)* and how the pull-up/pull-down resistor interacts.
> 5. **Not relating back to motion planning (–2 marks):** Every bumper answer should end with a sentence on *how the sensor feeds into the motion planner* (e.g., triggering path replan, safety-rated stop). KTU 2024 OBE pattern rewards **Course Outcome (CO) mapping**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Bumper Sensor (Module 1)**

- ✅ A bumper is a **contact-triggered, binary-output exteroceptive sensor** — it is the *lowest tier* of the perception stack.
- ✅ Three functional layers: **Mechanical (skin/switch), Electrical (debounce + pull-up), Logical (ISR + control).**
- ✅ Key response time budget: $T_{response} = t_{sens} + t_{proc} + t_{act}$; must satisfy $T_{response} < \dfrac{d_{stop}}{v}$.
- ✅ **Kinematic equation** $d_{stop} = \dfrac{v^2}{2 a_{max}}$ is the most-tested formula in KTU numerical questions.
- ✅ **Impulse–momentum** $F_{avg} \cdot \Delta t = m \cdot \Delta v$ is the standard tool for impact-force calculations.
- ✅ **Equivalent spring energy** $\dfrac{1}{2} k x^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ is used to size bumper stiffness.
- ✅ Always mention **debounce delay** (10–50 ms) in design answers — it is the examiner's favourite buzzword.
- ✅ Recovery FSM = **Stop → Reverse → Rotate → Resume.** This is the canonical "bumper behaviour" expected in 14-mark questions.
- ✅ Bumper **fails safe** (no signal = no false stop), making it indispensable for *safety-rated* motion planning (ISO 13482).
- ✅ Bumper is **complementary, not competitive**, to LIDAR/sonar — it forms the *last line of defence*, not the primary planner.
- ✅ KTU CO Mapping: This topic primarily addresses **CO1** (*Understand sensor modalities for mobile robots*) and **CO2** (*Apply sensor data to reactive motion planning*).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Odometry Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Odometry Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Odometry** is a fundamental proprioceptive sensing technique in mobile robotics that estimates a robot's instantaneous position and orientation (pose) over time by integrating (summing) discrete wheel rotation measurements obtained from rotary encoders mounted on the robot's drive wheels. It is the most widely deployed dead-reckoning localization method in differential-drive, skid-steer, and Ackermann-steered platforms.

In formal KTU terminology, the **odometric pose** of a robot at time $t$ is described by the state vector:

$$
\xi(t) = \begin{bmatrix} x(t) \\ y(t) \\ \theta(t) \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $(x, y)$ denotes the robot's planar Cartesian position in the **global (world) reference frame**, and $\theta$ is its heading angle measured counter-clockwise from the global X-axis. Odometry incrementally updates this pose by measuring wheel displacements $\Delta s_L$ and $\Delta s_R$ from the left and right encoders between successive sampling instants.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Walking in the Fog:**
> Imagine you are blindfolded inside a large building with no GPS and no landmarks. The only thing you know is how many steps you have taken and how sharply you are turning at each moment. By tracking these internal cues (proprioception), you can roughly estimate where you are relative to your starting point.
>
> - **Your "step counter"** = the **wheel encoder** (counts ticks per wheel revolution).
> - **Your "turning neck"** = the **steering angle sensor** (or differential wheel speeds).
> - **The cumulative estimate of your location** = the **odometric pose $(x, y, \theta)$**.
>
> Just as your blind estimate drifts because you might slip, mis-step, or underestimate turns, **odometry drifts over time due to wheel slip, uneven floors, and encoder quantization**. It is reliable **short-term**, but degrades **long-term**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standardized parameters govern every odometric system:

- **Wheel radius $r$** — typically **between 0.03 m and 0.20 m** for indoor mobile robots.
- **Wheel base / track length $b$** (or $L$) — distance between the two drive wheel contact points; standard differential-drive units use **0.15 m – 0.50 m**.
- **Encoder resolution $N_{ticks}$** — usually **360, 500, 1000, or 2048 pulses per revolution (PPR)**.
- **Sampling period $\Delta t$** — common values: **10 ms, 20 ms, or 50 ms**.
- **Robot linear velocity $v$** — range: **0.05 m/s to 1.0 m/s** for indoor service robots.
- **Robot angular velocity $\omega$** — range: **0.1 rad/s to 2.0 rad/s**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU Module 1 of OEBRT833, students are expected to derive the **forward odometric update equations** for a differential-drive robot, explain **sources of systematic and non-systematic odometric error**, and compute the **pose update $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta \theta$** given encoder tick counts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — How Odometry Works

The odometric pipeline operates in **three structured logical stages**:

1. **Tick Acquisition (Sensing Stage)**
   - Rotary optical or magnetic encoders generate **$N_{ticks}$** discrete pulses per full wheel revolution.
   - The robot's microcontroller counts these ticks within each control period $\Delta t$.
   - Counts are differenced: $\Delta n_L$ = ticks on left wheel during $\Delta t$; $\Delta n_R$ = ticks on right wheel during $\Delta t$.

2. **Arc-Length Conversion (Kinematic Stage)**
   - Each wheel's linear displacement along the ground is computed by converting ticks into arc length:
     - $\Delta s_L = 2\pi r \cdot \dfrac{\Delta n_L}{N_{ticks}}$
     - $\Delta s_R = 2\pi r \cdot \dfrac{\Delta n_R}{N_{ticks}}$

3. **Pose Integration (Estimation Stage)**
   - The robot's instantaneous **linear** and **angular** velocities are:
     - $v = \dfrac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2 \, \Delta t}$
     - $\omega = \dfrac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b \, \Delta t}$
   - The pose is updated using the **exact (arc-based) odometry equations** (see Section 3 for derivation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works:** The encoder behaves as a *digital odometer* — it measures wheel rotation directly, which (assuming no slip) corresponds exactly to the distance the wheel center has moved along the ground. By combining two such measurements at the two wheels of a differential-drive base, the entire rigid-body planar motion of the robot is recovered.

## 2.2 Why Odometry Belongs to "Dead Reckoning"

Dead reckoning is a navigation paradigm in which the current state is computed purely from a **previously known state** plus **measured increments** — no external reference (no GPS, no beacon, no landmark) is consulted. Odometry is the canonical example because:

- It depends **only on internal sensors** (proprioception).
- Errors **accumulate without bound** over time and distance.
- It is **computationally trivial** (a few additions and multiplications per cycle).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-critical formula inventory** for the Odometry Sensor topic. Memorize the meaning, units, and boundary conditions of every entry.

| # | Formula Symbol | Mathematical Expression | Variable Meaning | Units | Validity / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wheel displacement (left) | $\Delta s_L = 2\pi r \cdot \Delta n_L / N_{ticks}$ | Linear distance rolled by left wheel | meters (m) | Valid for $0 \leq \Delta n_L \leq N_{ticks}$ per cycle |
| 2 | Wheel displacement (right) | $\Delta s_R = 2\pi r \cdot \Delta n_R / N_{ticks}$ | Linear distance rolled by right wheel | meters (m) | Valid for $0 \leq \Delta n_R \leq N_{ticks}$ per cycle |
| 3 | Linear velocity | $v = (\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L) / (2 \, \Delta t)$ | Forward speed of robot center | m/s | Positive = forward motion |
| 4 | Angular velocity | $\omega = (\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L) / (b \, \Delta t)$ | Rotational rate about ICC | rad/s | Sign convention: positive $\omega$ = counter-clockwise (CCW) |
| 5 | Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC) radius | $R = v / \omega = b(\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L) / (2(\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L))$ | Distance from robot center to ICC | meters (m) | UNDEFINED when $\omega = 0$ (straight line) |
| 6 | Heading change | $\Delta \theta = \omega \cdot \Delta t = (\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L) / b$ | Pose-orientation increment | radians (rad) | Range $[-\pi, \pi]$ |
| 7 | Position increment (approximate) | $\Delta x \approx v \, \Delta t \cos(\theta + \Delta\theta / 2)$ | X-displacement in world frame | meters (m) | Mid-point integration scheme |
| 8 | Position increment (approximate) | $\Delta y \approx v \, \Delta t \sin(\theta + \Delta\theta / 2)$ | Y-displacement in world frame | meters (m) | Mid-point integration scheme |
| 9 | Exact arc-length position increment | $\Delta x = R [\sin(\theta + \Delta\theta) - \sin\theta]$ | Exact X-displacement | meters (m) | Reduces to $\Delta s \cos\theta$ when $\Delta\theta \to 0$ |
| 10 | Exact arc-length position increment | $\Delta y = R [-\cos(\theta + \Delta\theta) + \cos\theta]$ | Exact Y-displacement | meters (m) | Reduces to $\Delta s \sin\theta$ when $\Delta\theta \to 0$ |
| 11 | Pose update (recursive) | $x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta x$, $y_{k+1} = y_k + \Delta y$, $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta\theta$ | Recursive state propagation | m, m, rad | Run at every encoder sample |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Odometry is the foundational localization signal in warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva robots), autonomous floor cleaners (iRobot Roomba), hospital delivery robots (TUG by Aethon), and research platforms (TurtleBot, Pioneer). In every commercial mobile robot you can buy today, the very first "where am I?" answer comes from odometry before any SLAM or GPS correction is applied.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Encoder Ticks to Wheel Arc Length

**Given:** An incremental encoder with $N_{ticks}$ pulses per revolution is mounted on a drive wheel of radius $r$. During one sampling interval, the encoder registers $\Delta n$ pulses.

**Step 1 — Circumference of the wheel.** One full revolution of the wheel advances the contact point by exactly the circumference $C = 2\pi r$.

**Step 2 — Linear distance per tick.** Each tick therefore represents:
$$
d_{tick} = \frac{2\pi r}{N_{ticks}}
$$

**Step 3 — Total arc length.** After $\Delta n$ ticks, the wheel has rolled:
$$
\Delta s = \Delta n \cdot d_{tick} = \frac{2\pi r \, \Delta n}{N_{ticks}}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply to both wheels.** Independent readings yield:
$$
\Delta s_L = \frac{2\pi r \, \Delta n_L}{N_{ticks}}, \qquad \Delta s_R = \frac{2\pi r \, \Delta n_R}{N_{ticks}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Robot Linear and Angular Velocity

**Given:** A differential-drive robot with wheel base $b$ (perpendicular distance between left and right wheel contact patches) has rolled $\Delta s_L$ and $\Delta s_R$ in time $\Delta t$.

**Step 1 — Average forward distance.** The robot's reference point (midpoint of the wheel base) advances by the average:
$$
\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_L + \Delta s_R}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Linear velocity.** Dividing by the time interval:
$$
v = \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta t} = \frac{\Delta s_L + \Delta s_R}{2 \, \Delta t}
$$

**Step 3 — Differential arc length.** The right wheel covers more arc than the left if the robot turns left; the difference is the relative slippage that generates rotation:
$$
\delta = \Delta s_R - \Delta s_L
$$

**Step 4 — Heading change.** The arc traced by the differential drive is a circular arc about the **Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)**. The geometry of the two-wheel system gives:
$$
\Delta \theta = \frac{\delta}{b} = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}
$$

**Step 5 — Angular velocity.**
$$
\omega = \frac{\Delta \theta}{\Delta t} = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b \, \Delta t}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Exact Odometric Pose Update (Arc Model)

**Given:** Initial pose $(x_k, y_k, \theta_k)$ and the wheel displacements $\Delta s_L, \Delta s_R$ during one cycle. We seek $(x_{k+1}, y_{k+1}, \theta_{k+1})$.

**Step 1 — Identify the rotation center.** The robot's instantaneous motion is a pure rotation about the ICC, located at perpendicular distance $R$ from the robot's reference point on the line through both wheels. By similar triangles:
$$
\frac{R + b/2}{\Delta s_R} = \frac{R - b/2}{\Delta s_L}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $R$.** Cross-multiplying:
$$
(R + b/2) \, \Delta s_L = (R - b/2) \, \Delta s_R
$$
$$
R (\Delta s_L - \Delta s_R) = - \frac{b}{2} (\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L)
$$
$$
R = \frac{b}{2} \cdot \frac{\Delta s_L + \Delta s_R}{\Delta s_L - \Delta s_R} = - \frac{b \, (\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L)}{2 \, (\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L)}
$$

**Step 3 — Express $R$ compactly.** Using $v$ and $\omega$:
$$
R = \frac{v}{\omega}
$$
(Equivalent expression: $R = b(\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L) / (2(\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L))$.)

**Step 4 — Heading update.** The orientation evolves by:
$$
\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta \theta, \qquad \Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}
$$

**Step 5 — Position update via arc geometry.** The robot's reference point travels along an arc of radius $R$ centered at the ICC, sweeping through $\Delta \theta$. Projecting onto global axes:
$$
x_{k+1} = x_k + R \, [\sin(\theta_k + \Delta \theta) - \sin \theta_k]
$$
$$
y_{k+1} = y_k - R \, [\cos(\theta_k + \Delta \theta) - \cos \theta_k]
$$

These two equations are the **exact** odometric update law.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Approximate Mid-Point Pose Update (for small $\Delta \theta$)

**Step 1 — Assume small heading change.** When $\Delta \theta \to 0$, $R \to \infty$ but $R \cdot \Delta \theta = \Delta s$ remains finite. Taking the limit:

$$
\lim_{\Delta \theta \to 0} \frac{\sin(\theta_k + \Delta \theta) - \sin \theta_k}{\Delta \theta} = \cos \theta_k
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the limit.**
$$
\Delta x = v \, \Delta t \, \cos \theta_k, \qquad \Delta y = v \, \Delta t \, \sin \theta_k
$$

**Step 3 — More accurate mid-point rule.** To reduce linearization error, evaluate the heading at the midpoint of the interval:
$$
\Delta x = v \, \Delta t \, \cos\!\left(\theta_k + \tfrac{1}{2}\Delta\theta\right), \qquad \Delta y = v \, \Delta t \, \sin\!\left(\theta_k + \tfrac{1}{2}\Delta\theta\right)
$$

This mid-point rule is **second-order accurate**, while the simple rule is only **first-order accurate**.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A differential-drive robot has $r = 0.08$ m, $b = 0.30$ m, and encoders with $N_{ticks} = 500$ PPR. In one cycle ($\Delta t = 0.1$ s), the left encoder reports $\Delta n_L = 60$ ticks and the right encoder reports $\Delta n_R = 80$ ticks. The current pose is $(x, y, \theta) = (1.0, 2.0, \pi/4)$. Compute the next pose.

**Step 1 — Wheel arc lengths.**
$$
\Delta s_L = \frac{2\pi (0.08)(60)}{500} = \frac{9.6\pi}{500} \approx 0.0603 \text{ m}
$$
$$
\Delta s_R = \frac{2\pi (0.08)(80)}{500} = \frac{12.8\pi}{500} \approx 0.0804 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Linear and angular displacement.**
$$
\Delta s = \frac{0.0603 + 0.0804}{2} = 0.0704 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta \theta = \frac{0.0804 - 0.0603}{0.30} = \frac{0.0201}{0.30} \approx 0.0670 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 3 — Heading update.**
$$
\theta_{k+1} = \frac{\pi}{4} + 0.0670 \approx 0.7854 + 0.0670 = 0.8524 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 4 — Exact position update.** Compute $R$:
$$
R = \frac{0.30 \cdot 0.1407}{2 \cdot 0.0201} = \frac{0.04221}{0.0402} \approx 1.050 \text{ m}
$$
$$
\Delta x = 1.050 \, [\sin(0.8524) - \sin(0.7854)] = 1.050 \, [0.7519 - 0.7071] \approx 0.0470 \text{ m}
$$
$$
\Delta y = -1.050 \, [\cos(0.8524) - \cos(0.7854)] = -1.050 \, [0.6593 - 0.7071] \approx 0.0502 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Final pose.**
$$
(x_{k+1}, y_{k+1}, \theta_{k+1}) = (1.0470, \, 2.0502, \, 0.8524)
$$

## 3.6 Sources of Odometric Error

| Error Type | Specific Cause | Systematic vs. Non-Systematic | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wheel diameter mismatch | $\Delta r$ between left and right wheels | Systematic | Calibrate using known-path test |
| Effective wheel-base error | Uncertainty in $b$ | Systematic | Calibration on circular path |
| Encoder quantization | Finite $N_{ticks}$ resolution | Systematic | Use higher-resolution encoder |
| Wheel slip on slick surface | Skidding during acceleration | Non-systematic | Reduce acceleration, add IMU fusion |
| Uneven floor | Bumps, cracks, carpet seams | Non-systematic | Suspension, terrain mapping |
| Initial pose uncertainty | Unknown starting $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0)$ | Systematic | External landmark or manual set |
| Sampling jitter | Variable $\Delta t$ | Non-systematic | Hardware real-time clock |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Systematic errors accumulate **linearly with distance** traveled, while non-systematic errors accumulate **with the square root of distance** (they behave like a random walk). This is why odometric error grows unboundedly without an external correction signal.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Odometric Pipeline — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wheel Rotation Physical Event] --> B[Optical or Magnetic Encoder]
    B --> C[Tick Counter on Microcontroller]
    C --> D[Compute Delta nL and Delta nR]
    D --> E[Convert Ticks to Arc Lengths Delta sL and Delta sR]
    E --> F[Compute Linear Velocity v and Angular Velocity omega]
    F --> G[Apply Pose Update Equations]
    G --> H[New Pose Estimate x y theta]
    H --> I{External Correction Available}
    I -- Yes --> J[Sensor Fusion with GPS or SLAM]
    I -- No --> K[Pure Dead Reckoning Output]
    J --> H
```

## 4.2 Differential-Drive Kinematic Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Top View of Differential Drive Robot
        nodeA[Robot Center Pose x y theta]
        nodeB[Left Wheel Contact Point]
        nodeC[Right Wheel Contact Point]
        nodeD[Instantaneous Center of Curvature ICC]
        nodeE[Radius R from ICC to Robot Center]
        nodeF[Wheel Base b between Left and Right]
    end
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeC
```

## 4.3 Odometric Error Growth Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[True Robot Path] --> B[Odometric Estimate Path]
    A --> C[True Pose at Time t]
    B --> D[Estimated Pose at Time t]
    C --> E[Position Error Vector]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Error Magnitude Grows with Distance]
```

## 4.4 Pose Update Recursion Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP[Read Encoder Counts nL and nR]
    nodeP --> nodeQ[Compute Delta nL and Delta nR]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[Compute Delta sL and Delta sR using r and N]
    nodeR --> nodeS[Compute Delta theta using b]
    nodeS --> nodeT[Compute Radius R if omega nonzero]
    nodeT --> nodeU[Update theta k plus 1]
    nodeU --> nodeV[Update x k plus 1 using sin and cos]
    nodeV --> nodeW[Update y k plus 1 using sin and cos]
    nodeW --> nodeX[Store New Pose as Current Pose]
    nodeX --> nodeP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** These schematics depict the **functional architecture** of odometric processing rather than physical photographs of sensors. They are the standard block diagrams expected in KTU 2024 scheme answer sheets when the question asks for a "block diagram" or "flow diagram of the odometric system."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*
**Define odometry. Mention any two sources of odometric error.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Odometry is the use of data from motion sensors (typically wheel encoders) to estimate change in position over time. In mobile robotics, it is the most common dead-reckoning technique, where the robot's pose $(x, y, \theta)$ is updated incrementally by integrating wheel rotations.
>
> **Two sources of error:**
> 1. **Systematic error** due to wheel diameter mismatch and uncertainty in effective wheel base.
> 2. **Non-systematic error** due to wheel slip on uneven or slick floors.

### Question A2 — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*
**Distinguish between systematic and non-systematic errors in odometry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Systematic errors** are deterministic and repeatable; they arise from imperfections in the mechanical and electronic hardware (e.g., unequal wheel diameters, encoder resolution limits, kinematic miscalibration). They accumulate proportionally with distance.
> - **Non-systematic errors** are stochastic and unpredictable; they arise from environmental interactions (e.g., wheel slip over unexpected bumps, floor irregularities, carpet deformation). They accumulate proportional to the square root of distance.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question B1A — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]*

**(a)** Derive the exact odometric pose-update equations for a differential-drive mobile robot. State clearly the assumptions and define every variable. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

A differential-drive robot with wheel base $b$ and wheel radius $r$ moves on a horizontal plane. Its pose is $\xi = [x, y, \theta]^T$. Two encoders of resolution $N$ produce tick counts $\Delta n_L, \Delta n_R$ during interval $\Delta t$.

**Assumption:** No wheel slip; both wheels maintain firm rolling contact with the floor.

**Step 1 — Wheel arc lengths.**
$$
\Delta s_L = \frac{2\pi r \, \Delta n_L}{N}, \qquad \Delta s_R = \frac{2\pi r \, \Delta n_R}{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Heading change.** The differential arc $\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L$ produces rotation about the ICC:
$$
\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}
$$

**Step 3 — Radius of curvature.** From similar-triangle geometry of the two-wheel system:
$$
R = \frac{b \, (\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L)}{2 \, (\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L)}
$$

**Step 4 — Exact pose update.**
$$
\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta \theta
$$
$$
x_{k+1} = x_k + R \left[ \sin(\theta_k + \Delta \theta) - \sin \theta_k \right]
$$
$$
y_{k+1} = y_k - R \left[ \cos(\theta_k + \Delta \theta) - \cos \theta_k \right]
$$

**(b)** A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and wheel base $b = 0.25$ m. Each wheel encoder has 1000 pulses per revolution. In a 0.1 s interval, left wheel produces 100 ticks and right wheel produces 130 ticks. Starting pose is $(0, 0, 0)$. Compute the next pose using both the exact and the approximate (straight-line) update methods. Comment on the difference. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Arc lengths.**
$$
\Delta s_L = \frac{2\pi (0.05)(100)}{1000} = 0.03142 \text{ m}
$$
$$
\Delta s_R = \frac{2\pi (0.05)(130)}{1000} = 0.04084 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Heading change.**
$$
\Delta \theta = \frac{0.04084 - 0.03142}{0.25} = \frac{0.00942}{0.25} = 0.03770 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 3 — Radius.**
$$
R = \frac{0.25 \times 0.07226}{2 \times 0.00942} = \frac{0.01807}{0.01884} \approx 0.959 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Exact pose update** (with $\theta_0 = 0$):
$$
x_1 = 0 + 0.959 [\sin(0.03770) - \sin(0)] = 0.959 \times 0.03768 \approx 0.03613 \text{ m}
$$
$$
y_1 = 0 - 0.959 [\cos(0.03770) - \cos(0)] = -0.959 \times (-0.99929) \approx 0.9583 \text{ m}
$$
$$
\theta_1 = 0 + 0.03770 = 0.03770 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 5 — Approximate straight-line update.** Average displacement $\Delta s = (0.03142 + 0.04084)/2 = 0.03613$ m; using midpoint heading:
$$
x_1^{approx} = 0.03613 \cos(0.01885) \approx 0.03612 \text{ m}
$$
$$
y_1^{approx} = 0.03613 \sin(0.01885) \approx 0.000681 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Comment.** The exact result yields $y_1 \approx 0.9583$ m (almost pure rotation about a near-center ICC), while the straight-line approximation collapses $y_1$ to nearly zero. This vividly demonstrates that **the approximation fails when $R$ is small (sharp turn)**, and the exact arc-based update must be used for high-curvature maneuvers.

---

### Question B1B — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]*

**(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the working of the odometry sensor in a differential-drive mobile robot. Discuss the role of the Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

A differential-drive robot has two independently driven wheels mounted on a common axle. Each wheel carries an incremental rotary encoder that emits a fixed number of pulses per revolution. As the robot moves, the encoders count these pulses, and the microcontroller converts the count differences into linear wheel displacements.

**Working Principle:**
1. The encoders sense wheel rotation continuously.
2. Each pulse represents a known linear increment of the wheel contact patch.
3. The differential between left and right wheel rotations reveals whether the robot is moving straight or turning.
4. The cumulative left and right displacements are combined using the kinematic equations of the differential-drive base to estimate the change in position $(x, y)$ and orientation $\theta$.

**Role of the ICC:**
The **Instantaneous Center of Curvature** is the fixed point in the plane about which the entire rigid robot instantaneously rotates. For a differential-drive robot, the ICC lies on the line extending through both wheel axles, on the side opposite the slower wheel. The distance from the robot's reference point to the ICC is $R = v/\omega$. The ICC concept unifies straight-line motion ($R \to \infty$ when $\omega = 0$) and pure rotation ($R = 0$ when $v = 0$) as limiting cases of the same circular-arc motion model.

**(b)** A differential-drive robot with wheel base $b = 0.4$ m rolls in such a way that its left wheel rotates by 5 revolutions and the right wheel rotates by 7 revolutions. Each wheel has radius $r = 0.1$ m. Determine (i) the linear displacement of the robot, (ii) the angular displacement, and (iii) the radius of curvature of the path. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute linear wheel displacements.**
$$
\Delta s_L = 5 \times 2\pi (0.1) = 5 \times 0.6283 = 3.1416 \text{ m}
$$
$$
\Delta s_R = 7 \times 2\pi (0.1) = 7 \times 0.6283 = 4.3982 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Linear displacement of the robot.**
$$
\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_L + \Delta s_R}{2} = \frac{3.1416 + 4.3982}{2} = 3.7699 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Angular displacement.**
$$
\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b} = \frac{4.3982 - 3.1416}{0.4} = \frac{1.2566}{0.4} = 3.1416 \text{ rad} \approx \pi \text{ rad} = 180^\circ
$$

**Step 4 — Radius of curvature.**
$$
R = \frac{b \, (\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L)}{2 \, (\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L)} = \frac{0.4 \times 7.5398}{2 \times 1.2566} = \frac{3.0159}{2.5133} \approx 1.200 \text{ m}
$$

**Interpretation:** The robot traversed an arc of length 3.77 m on a circle of radius 1.20 m, sweeping through 180°. It has effectively completed a half-turn about a pivot 1.20 m to the side of its starting point.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Odometry Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $2\pi$ factor** when converting encoder ticks to wheel displacement — partial credit will be deducted if the formula is written without the full $2\pi r / N$ derivation.
> 2. **Mixing sign conventions** for $\Delta\theta$. KTU expects **counter-clockwise positive** (right-hand rule). Students often use $\Delta\theta = (\Delta s_L - \Delta s_R)/b$, which is the wrong sign and yields negative heading.
> 3. **Confusing approximate vs. exact pose updates.** When the path curvature is high, the straight-line approximation is grossly wrong. The examiner expects you to recognize this and apply the exact arc-based equations.
> 4. **Ignoring units.** Always write the units of $v$ (m/s), $\omega$ (rad/s), $\theta$ (rad), and $R$ (m). Missing units → loss of 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Not stating the no-slip assumption** explicitly in derivations. The KTU evaluator marks the *quality of reasoning*, and a derivation without its foundational assumption is considered incomplete.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — The Odometry Sensor**

- [x] Odometry = proprioceptive dead reckoning using wheel encoders.
- [x] Output is the robot pose $\xi = [x, y, \theta]^T$ in the world frame.
- [x] Encoder converts wheel rotation into digital pulses; ticks → arc length via $\Delta s = 2\pi r \, \Delta n / N$.
- [x] Two key derived quantities: linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$.
- [x] The ICC is the geometric pivot of instantaneous motion; all differential-drive trajectories are circular arcs about ICC.
- [x] Exact pose update: $\Delta x = R[\sin(\theta + \Delta\theta) - \sin\theta]$, $\Delta y = -R[\cos(\theta + \Delta\theta) - \cos\theta]$.
- [x] Approximate (straight-line) update: $\Delta x \approx \Delta s \cos\theta$, $\Delta y \approx \Delta s \sin\theta$ — only valid for small $\Delta\theta$.
- [x] Pose updates are **recursive**: each cycle's output becomes the next cycle's input.
- [x] Errors are of **two types**: systematic (deterministic, calibration-fixable) and non-systematic (stochastic, environment-driven).
- [x] Systematic error grows **linearly** with distance; non-systematic error grows as **$\sqrt{\text{distance}}$**.
- [x] Odometry alone is insufficient for long-term localization — it must be fused with absolute sensors (GPS, lidar SLAM, vision).
- [x] Common engineering parameters to memorize: $r \in [0.03, 0.20]$ m, $b \in [0.15, 0.50]$ m, $N \in \{360, 500, 1000, 2048\}$ PPR, $\Delta t \in [10, 50]$ ms.
- [x] **KTU trick question:** "Why is odometry called *dead* reckoning?" — because it relies on *previously* known states with no external reference, allowing errors to *die out* slowly and propagate forever.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heading Sensors - Gyroscopes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Heading Sensors - Gyroscopes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Heading Sensors - Gyroscopes

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **gyroscope** is a heading sensor based on the principle of conservation of angular momentum, used to measure or maintain the orientation of a body relative to an inertial reference frame. In the context of **Robot Motion Planning**, gyroscopes are classified as *proprioceptive heading sensors* that provide angular velocity and orientation data essential for dead-reckoning, attitude determination, and feedback control in autonomous mobile robots, UAVs, and manipulator arms.

A gyroscope measures **angular rate** (deg/s or rad/s) about one or more axes and, when integrated over time, yields angular **displacement** (heading/orientation). The modern taxonomy of gyroscopes in KTU 2024 robotics curriculum includes mechanical, optical, and MEMS-class devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note (Module 1 – Heading Sensors):** Gyroscopes are listed as a primary heading sensor family alongside compasses, inclinometers, and IMU units. Students must distinguish between **rate gyros** (output = angular velocity) and **rate-integrating gyros** (output = angle).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine spinning a bicycle wheel on a string and then trying to tilt the axle. The wheel resists tilting and instead its axis sweeps around in a circle. This is **precession** — the basis of every mechanical gyroscope.

> [!NOTE]
> **Everyday analogy:** A spinning top does not fall over immediately because its spinning mass carries **angular momentum**. Hold that momentum in three orthogonal rings, and you have a gyroscope that "remembers" which way is up. In a smartphone, a tiny vibrating silicon proof-mass behaves the same way using the **Coriolis effect**, even though nothing visibly spins.

| Sensor Family | Real-World Robotic Use |
|---|---|
| MEMS Gyro (MPU6050) | Drone flight controllers, balance robots |
| Fiber-Optic Gyro (FOG) | Autonomous cars, marine robotics |
| Ring Laser Gyro (RLG) | Aerospace navigation, high-grade AHRS |
| Hemispherical Resonator Gyro (HRG) | Spacecraft attitude control |

The Earth's rotation rate of **15.041°/hour** is the natural **noise floor** any gyroscope must beat for true inertial navigation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles Across Gyroscope Classes

Gyroscopes exploit one of three physical phenomena. The KTU 2024 syllabus groups them by the underlying principle:

### A. Mechanical (Spinning-Rotor) Gyroscopes
- Based on **conservation of angular momentum** $\vec{L} = I \vec{\omega}$.
- A high-speed rotor is suspended in **gimbal rings** to give 2 or 3 degrees of freedom.
- External torques cause **precession** rather than direct tilt.

### B. Optical Gyroscopes (RLG and FOG)
- Based on the **Sagnac effect** — two counter-propagating light beams accumulate a path difference when the loop rotates.
- No moving parts → extremely high reliability, no wear.

### C. MEMS / Vibrating Structure Gyroscopes
- A silicon proof-mass is driven to oscillate linearly.
- Rotation induces a **Coriolis force** perpendicular to the vibration and rotation axes.
- This secondary motion is capacitively sensed.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Working Logic

1. **Rotor/Proof-mass is energized** — A spinning rotor is brought to high RPM, or a MEMS comb-drive sets the proof-mass oscillating.
2. **Angular momentum is established** — The mass possesses $\vec{L} = I\vec{\omega}$ along the spin axis.
3. **Rotation of the base frame** — As the robot turns, the support tries to rotate the spin axis.
4. **Reaction torque / Coriolis force** — Either a perpendicular precession torque (mechanical) or a Coriolis force (MEMS) is generated.
5. **Sensor transduction** — Torque motor, piezoelectric, or capacitive pickoff converts the response into a voltage.
6. **Signal conditioning** — Output voltage is scaled to a calibrated angular rate in deg/s or rad/s.

## 2.3 Key Performance Parameters (KTU Examinable)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Angular rate range | $\omega_{max}$ | deg/s or rad/s | Maximum measurable rotation rate |
| Bias stability | $b$ | deg/hr | Drift of the zero-rate output |
| Scale-factor error | $k_{sf}$ | ppm | Non-linearity of the voltage-to-rate mapping |
| Bandwidth | $f_{bw}$ | Hz | Frequency range of usable response |
| Random walk | $ARW$ | deg/$\sqrt{hr}$ | Inherent angle noise |
| Drift rate | $\dot{\theta}_{drift}$ | deg/hr | Long-term orientation error accumulation |

## 2.4 High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vec{L} = I\vec{\omega}$ | Angular momentum of a rigid rotor |
| 2 | $\vec{\tau} = \dfrac{d\vec{L}}{dt} = \vec{\omega}_p \times \vec{L}$ | Gyroscopic precession torque |
| 3 | $\omega_p = \dfrac{m g l}{I \omega_s}$ | Precession rate of a classical gyro (gravity-induced) |
| 4 | $\vec{F}_c = 2m (\vec{v} \times \vec{\Omega})$ | Coriolis force on vibrating proof-mass |
| 5 | $\Delta \phi = \dfrac{4 \vec{\Omega} \cdot \vec{A}}{c \lambda}$ | Sagnac phase shift (ring area $A$, wavelength $\lambda$, speed of light $c$) |
| 6 | $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \int_0^t \omega(t)\, dt$ | Heading from integration of rate |
| 7 | $\sigma_\theta(t) = \sigma_\omega \sqrt{t}$ | Random-walk variance of integrated angle |
| 8 | $\dot{\theta}_{drift} = b + \dfrac{ARW}{\sqrt{t}}$ | Total drift as function of time |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Examination Tip:** Equation (6) above is a *dead-reckoning* equation. Because integration is **unbounded**, any bias $b$ in the rate signal causes an angle error that grows **linearly with time** $t$. This is why gyros are always fused with an **accelerometer or magnetometer** in an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drone flight control** — 3-axis MEMS gyro runs at 1 kHz in the inner-loop PID.
- **Autonomous mobile robots (AMR)** — FOG gives heading stability over long corridors where wheel odometry slips.
- **Robotic surgery (Da Vinci)** — MEMS gyros cancel hand tremor.
- **Spacecraft** — HRG provides attitude control with drift under 0.0001°/hr.
- **Industrial robot arms** — Gyros mounted on the end-effector detect high-frequency vibration for chatter suppression.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU 14-mark problems, always write the **precession direction** using the right-hand rule, not just the magnitude. Examiners award partial credit for correct vector direction even if the numerical value is wrong.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Gyroscopic Precession Rate from Newton's Laws

Consider a classical spinning rotor of mass $m_r$ at the end of a light shaft of length $l$ pivoted at the origin. The rotor spins with angular speed $\omega_s$ about its own axis (z-axis), and the shaft is free to rotate about the y-axis (vertical). Gravity $g$ acts downward.

**Step 1 — Angular momentum of the spinning rotor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{L} &= I_s \,\omega_s \,\hat{k} \\
I_s &= m_r r^2 \quad \text{(moment of inertia of a point mass at radius } r\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Gravitational torque about the pivot:**

The weight $m_r g$ acts at distance $l$ horizontally from the pivot.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{\tau} &= \vec{r} \times \vec{F} \\
&= l\,\hat{i} \times (-m_r g\,\hat{k}) \\
&= m_r g l \,\hat{j}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Euler's equation for the rate of change of angular momentum:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{\tau} &= \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt} \\
&= \frac{d}{dt}\bigl(I_s\,\omega_s\,\hat{k}\bigr) \\
&= I_s\,\omega_s\,\frac{d\hat{k}}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — The unit vector $\hat{k}$ rotates about the y-axis. The precession angular velocity is $\omega_p \hat{j}$, so:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\hat{k}}{dt} &= \vec{\omega}_p \times \hat{k} = \omega_p \hat{j} \times \hat{k} = \omega_p \hat{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Equate torque and rate of change of L:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_r g l\,\hat{j} &= I_s\,\omega_s\,\omega_p\,\hat{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — the directions are not consistent. This is because the gravitational torque rotates the *shaft*, not directly the spin axis. The correct resolved form along the y-axis gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_r g l &= I_s\,\omega_s\,\omega_p \\
\therefore \quad \omega_p &= \frac{m_r g l}{I_s \omega_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The vector logic forces the precession axis to be **perpendicular to both the spin axis and the applied torque**, which is the famous statement: *"A gyroscope precesses about the axis of the applied torque, not about the torque vector itself."*

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Coriolis-Induced Displacement in a MEMS Gyroscope

A MEMS proof-mass of mass $m$ vibrates with velocity $\vec{v}(t) = v_0 \cos(\omega_d t)\,\hat{x}$ along the drive axis. The sensor body rotates with angular velocity $\vec{\Omega} = \Omega\,\hat{z}$.

**Step 1 — Coriolis force expression:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{F}_c &= 2m\,(\vec{v} \times \vec{\Omega}) \\
&= 2m \bigl(v_0 \cos(\omega_d t)\,\hat{x}\bigr) \times \bigl(\Omega\,\hat{z}\bigr) \\
&= 2m v_0 \Omega \cos(\omega_d t)\,(\hat{x} \times \hat{z}) \\
&= 2m v_0 \Omega \cos(\omega_d t)\,(-\hat{y}) \\
&= -2m v_0 \Omega \cos(\omega_d t)\,\hat{y}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Equation of motion along the sense axis (y):**

The Coriolis force drives a second harmonic oscillator of stiffness $k_y$ and damping $c$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m\ddot{y} + c\dot{y} + k_y y &= F_c(t) \\
m\ddot{y} + c\dot{y} + k_y y &= 2m v_0 \Omega \cos(\omega_d t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — At resonance, $k_y = m \omega_d^2$, the steady-state amplitude is:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{ss} &= \frac{2m v_0 \Omega}{c\,\omega_d} \\
&= \frac{2 v_0 \Omega}{Q\,\omega_d}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $Q = \dfrac{m\omega_d}{c}$ is the quality factor of the sense resonator.

**Step 4 — Capacitive pickoff converts displacement $y_{ss}$ into voltage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= K_{sf}\, \Omega \\
K_{sf} &= \frac{2 v_0\, C_{sense}}{Q\,\omega_d\, C_{ref}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The output is **directly proportional to the input rotation rate** $\Omega$. This is the design equation of every MEMS gyro ASIC. Doubling the drive velocity $v_0$ doubles the scale factor $K_{sf}$, which improves resolution but increases power consumption.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sagnac Phase Shift (Optical Gyroscope)

A light beam splits into two counter-rotating beams that travel around a fiber coil of radius $R$ and $N$ turns. The loop rotates with angular velocity $\Omega$ about its symmetry axis.

**Step 1 — Time delay for co-rotating beam:**

The beam must "catch up" by one circumference per turn, so its path length increases.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{cw} &= \frac{2\pi R N}{c - R\Omega} \\
t_{ccw} &= \frac{2\pi R N}{c + R\Omega}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Differential time delay:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t &= t_{cw} - t_{ccw} \\
&= \frac{2\pi R N}{c - R\Omega} - \frac{2\pi R N}{c + R\Omega} \\
&= \frac{2\pi R N \cdot 2R\Omega}{c^2 - R^2\Omega^2} \\
&\approx \frac{4\pi R^2 N \Omega}{c^2} \quad (R\Omega \ll c)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to optical phase shift using wavelength $\lambda$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \phi &= \frac{2\pi c \Delta t}{\lambda} \\
&= \frac{2\pi c}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{4\pi R^2 N \Omega}{c^2} \\
&= \frac{8\pi^2 R^2 N \Omega}{c \lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Define effective area $A = \pi R^2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \phi &= \frac{4 \pi A N \,\Omega}{c\,\lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Notice that the Sagnac shift depends on the **area-wavelength product**, not on the speed of light squared inside the fiber. This is why **fiber gyros** wrap thousands of turns to amplify the area-times-N product into a measurable phase.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Gyroscope Class Comparison

| Class | Principle | Bias Stability | Cost | KTU-Recommended Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Spinning-mass | Conservation of $\vec{L}$ | 1 – 10 deg/hr | High | Aircraft heading indicator |
| MEMS | Coriolis on vibrating mass | 1 – 100 deg/hr | Very low | MPU6050, ADXRS453 |
| Ring Laser (RLG) | Sagnac (optical) | 0.001 – 0.01 deg/hr | Very high | Honeywell GG1320 |
| Fiber-Optic (FOG) | Sagnac (fiber) | 0.01 – 0.1 deg/hr | High | KVH DSP-1760 |
| Hemispherical Resonator (HRG) | Standing-wave precession | 0.0001 – 0.001 deg/hr | Very high | Northrop Grumman Scalable SIRU |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a MEMS Gyroscope

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[DCMotor or Comb Drive] --> nodeB[Proof Mass Oscillation Along X]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Coriolis Force Induced by Rotation]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Secondary Motion Along Y]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Capacitive Sense Electrodes]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Charge Amplifier]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Synchronous Demodulator at Drive Frequency]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Low Pass Filter]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Scaled Analog or Digital Output in deg per s]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Microcontroller or EKF Module]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagram above shows the **functional flow** rather than a physical layout, in keeping with the Mermaid limitation for non-physical drawings. Every block has a one-to-one correspondence with the mathematical model derived in Section 3.2.

## 4.2 Gimbal Architecture of a Mechanical Gyroscope

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeOuterFrame[Outer Frame Base Mounted to Robot]
    nodeOuterFrame --> nodeOuterGimbal[Outer Gimbal Ring]
    nodeOuterGimbal --> nodeInnerGimbal[Inner Gimbal Ring]
    nodeInnerGimbal --> nodeRotor[Spinning Rotor on Spin Axis]
    nodeTorqueX[Torque About X Axis]
    nodeTorqueY[Torque About Y Axis]
    nodeTorqueZ[Torque About Z Axis]
    nodeOuterGimbal --- nodeTorqueX
    nodeInnerGimbal --- nodeTorqueY
    nodeRotor --- nodeTorqueZ
```

## 4.3 Sagnac Loop for Optical Gyroscope

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSource[Laser Source] --> nodeSplitter[Beam Splitter 50 50]
    nodeSplitter --> nodeCW[Clockwise Beam]
    nodeSplitter --> nodeCCW[Counterclockwise Beam]
    nodeCW --> nodeCoil[Fiber Coil N Turns Radius R]
    nodeCCW --> nodeCoil
    nodeCoil --> nodeDetector[Photodetector]
    nodeDetector --> nodePhase[Phase Difference to Rate Converter]
    nodePhase --> nodeOutput[Angular Rate Output in deg per s]
```

## 4.4 Gyroscope Integration in a Robot Motion-Planning Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeIMU[3 Axis MEMS Gyroscope and Accelerometer]
    nodeEnc[Wheel Encoders]
    nodeMag[3 Axis Magnetometer]
    nodeIMU --> nodeEKF[Extended Kalman Filter]
    nodeEnc --> nodeEKF
    nodeMag --> nodeEKF
    nodeEKF --> nodePose[Robot Pose Estimate x y theta]
    nodePose --> nodePlanner[Global Path Planner A Star or RRT]
    nodePlanner --> nodeController[Trajectory Controller MPC or PID]
    nodeController --> nodeMotors[Motor Drivers]
    nodeMotors --> nodeRobot[Mobile Robot]
    nodeRobot --> nodeIMU
    nodeRobot --> nodeEnc
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagram above makes clear that the gyroscope is **not a stand-alone truth source**. It feeds the EKF, which fuses it with magnetometer and encoder data to bound the *unbounded* integration drift described by Equation (6) of Section 2.4.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define a gyroscope. Differentiate between a rate gyro and a rate-integrating gyro.

**Model Answer:**
A **gyroscope** is a heading sensor that uses the principle of conservation of angular momentum to measure angular velocity or angular displacement. A **rate gyro** outputs a voltage proportional to the *angular velocity* (deg/s) about its sensitive axis, while a **rate-integrating gyro** outputs an angle proportional to the *integrated angular displacement* (deg). The former is used for stabilization loops; the latter is used for heading reference in inertial navigation.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is meant by **gyroscopic drift**? List two methods used to compensate it.

**Model Answer:**
Gyroscopic drift is the unwanted accumulation of heading error over time due to bias, scale-factor error, and random noise in the gyro output. Two compensation methods are:
1. **Sensor fusion via EKF** with a magnetometer or GPS heading.
2. **Temperature compensation and bias auto-calibration** during stationary phases (zero-rate update / ZUPT).

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the **precession rate** of a classical mechanical gyroscope. Show all vector steps clearly.

**Model Solution:**

1. Consider a spinning rotor of moment of inertia $I_s$ spinning at $\omega_s$ about the spin axis $\hat{k}$.
2. The rotor is mounted on a shaft of length $l$ whose other end is at the pivot.
3. Weight of the rotor $m g$ acts at the center of mass a horizontal distance $l$ from the pivot.
4. Gravitational torque about the pivot:
   $$\vec{\tau} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F} = l\hat{i} \times (-m g \hat{k}) = m g l\,\hat{j}$$
5. The rate of change of angular momentum of the spinning rotor, when the spin axis precesses at $\omega_p\,\hat{j}$, is:
   $$\frac{d\vec{L}}{dt} = I_s \omega_s (\vec{\omega}_p \times \hat{k}) = I_s \omega_s \omega_p \hat{i}$$
6. **Important correction:** The precession actually occurs about the y-axis, and the resulting rate of change of $\vec{L}$ has its component along y depending on the geometry. Resolving the components correctly:
   $$I_s \omega_s \omega_p = m g l$$
7. Therefore, the precession rate is:
   $$\omega_p = \frac{m g l}{I_s \omega_s}$$
8. Direction of precession follows the **right-hand rule** along the applied torque axis.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A MEMS gyroscope has a proof-mass of $m = 5\times10^{-9}\,\text{kg}$, drive velocity amplitude $v_0 = 5\times10^{-3}\,\text{m/s}$, drive frequency $f_d = 25\,\text{kHz}$, and quality factor $Q = 200$. If the applied rotation rate is $\Omega = 100°/\text{s}$, calculate the steady-state sense-axis displacement $y_{ss}$.

**Model Solution:**

1. Convert $\Omega$ to rad/s:
   $$\Omega = 100 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.7453\,\text{rad/s}$$
2. Compute $\omega_d$:
   $$\omega_d = 2\pi f_d = 2\pi \times 25{,}000 = 1.5708\times10^5\,\text{rad/s}$$
3. Use the steady-state amplitude formula from Section 3.2:
   $$y_{ss} = \frac{2 m v_0 \Omega}{Q \omega_d}$$
4. Substitute the values:
   $$y_{ss} = \frac{2 \times 5\times10^{-9} \times 5\times10^{-3} \times 1.7453}{200 \times 1.5708\times10^5}$$
5. Numerator: $2 \times 5\times10^{-9} \times 5\times10^{-3} \times 1.7453 = 8.7265\times10^{-11}$
6. Denominator: $200 \times 1.5708\times10^5 = 3.1416\times10^7$
7. Final answer:
   $$y_{ss} = \frac{8.7265\times10^{-11}}{3.1416\times10^7} \approx 2.78\times10^{-18}\,\text{m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to convert degrees-per-second into **radians-per-second** before plugging into the Coriolis formula. This unit error alone costs 1 to 2 marks out of 7. Always state the unit conversion explicitly in your answer script.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Sagnac effect** and derive the phase shift equation for a ring-laser gyroscope.

**Model Solution:**

1. The **Sagnac effect** states that two counter-propagating light beams in a rotating closed loop arrive at the detector with a time difference proportional to the loop's rotation rate.
2. Block diagram (refer Section 4.3 of these notes for full Mermaid).
3. Time taken by the clockwise beam:
   $$t_{cw} = \frac{2\pi R N}{c - R\Omega}$$
4. Time taken by the counterclockwise beam:
   $$t_{ccw} = \frac{2\pi R N}{c + R\Omega}$$
5. Differential time:
   $$\Delta t = t_{cw} - t_{ccw} \approx \frac{4\pi R^2 N \Omega}{c^2}$$
6. Optical phase difference:
   $$\Delta \phi = \frac{2\pi c \Delta t}{\lambda} = \frac{8\pi^2 R^2 N \Omega}{c \lambda} = \frac{4\pi A N \Omega}{c \lambda}$$
7. The angular rate is then recovered by:
   $$\Omega = \frac{c \lambda \Delta \phi}{4\pi A N}$$

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Compare **MEMS gyroscopes** and **fiber-optic gyroscopes (FOG)** with respect to: (i) operating principle, (ii) bias stability, (iii) cost, and (iv) typical robotics application.

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | MEMS Gyroscope | Fiber-Optic Gyro |
|---|---|---|
| Operating principle | Coriolis force on vibrating proof-mass | Sagnac effect in fiber coil |
| Bias stability | 1 – 100 deg/hr | 0.01 – 0.1 deg/hr |
| Cost | < 5 USD (consumer grade) | 5,000 – 50,000 USD |
| Typical application | Consumer drones, smartphones, balance robots | Autonomous vehicles, marine robotics, ship INS |
| Size | Few mm² wafer | 10 – 20 cm coil diameter |
| Shock tolerance | High | Moderate |
| Warm-up time | < 1 s | 30 – 60 s |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Comparison Questions:** Do not write only advantages of one type. KTU evaluators explicitly check for **balanced comparison**. If the question says "compare", you must show both similarities and differences, ideally in a **two-column table**, and *conclude* with a one-line justification of which is preferred for a given application.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **gyroscope** is a heading sensor that measures angular velocity, not linear motion.
- **Mechanical gyros** use $\vec{L} = I\vec{\omega}$ and exhibit **precession** at $\omega_p = \dfrac{m g l}{I \omega_s}$.
- **MEMS gyros** use the **Coriolis force** $\vec{F}_c = 2m(\vec{v}\times\vec{\Omega})$; displacement is $\dfrac{2m v_0 \Omega}{Q \omega_d}$.
- **Optical gyros (RLG/FOG)** use the **Sagnac phase shift** $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{4\pi A N \Omega}{c \lambda}$.
- Heading is obtained by **integration** $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \int_0^t \omega(\tau) d\tau$ — this integration **amplifies bias linearly with time**.
- **Drift compensation** is mandatory and is done via **EKF fusion** with magnetometers / GPS.
- Key metrics to memorize: bias stability, ARW, scale-factor error, bandwidth.
- Always convert **deg/s to rad/s** before substituting into Coriolis or Sagnac equations.
- Always state the **right-hand-rule direction** of precession in addition to the magnitude.
- Gyroscopes form one-third of the **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** alongside accelerometers and magnetometers.
- The Earth's rotation rate $\approx 15.04°/\text{hr}$ is the **theoretical lower noise floor** of any inertial-grade gyro.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Hack:** For the KTU 2024 ESE, remember the **"P-C-S" mnemonic** — **P**recession for mechanical, **C**oriolis for MEMS, **S**agnac for optical. Almost every Module 1 question on gyroscopes maps to one of these three principles.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IMUs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IMUs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit):** A self-contained, electronic sensor module that measures and reports a body's specific force (linear acceleration), angular rate (rotational velocity), and — in advanced configurations — the orientation of the Earth's magnetic field, by combining data from three orthogonal primary sensing axes.

In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme and modern **Robot Motion Planning**, an Inertial Measurement Unit is the foundational proprioceptive sensor that enables a robot to estimate its own pose (position, velocity, and orientation) in real time without relying on external references. A 6-DOF (Degrees of Freedom) IMU typically integrates:

| # | Sub-Sensor | Measurand (Quantity Measured) | DOF |
|---|------------|-------------------------------|-----|
| 1 | **3-Axis Accelerometer** | Linear Acceleration (m/s²) | 3 |
| 2 | **3-Axis Gyroscope** | Angular Velocity (rad/s) | 3 |
| 3 | (Optional) **3-Axis Magnetometer** | Magnetic Field Vector (μT) | 3 |

A 9-DOF IMU combines all three to deliver a complete attitude and heading reference system (AHRS). The standard reference coordinate frames are the **Body Frame** (sensor-fixed) and the **Inertial/Navigation Frame** (world-fixed).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Inner Ear + Inner Compass" Analogy**
> Imagine you are blindfolded and placed in an unknown moving vehicle. You would still feel yourself being pushed back when it accelerates, feel your body tilt when it turns, and — using a compass — know which way is north. An IMU does exactly this for a robot.

- **Accelerometer** = the robot's "inner ear" — it feels pushes and pulls (forces), much like the vestibular system in your inner ear detects linear motion.
- **Gyroscope** = the robot's "sense of spin" — it knows how fast it is rotating, similar to how you feel yourself turning in a chair even with your eyes closed.
- **Magnetometer** = the robot's "digital compass" — it points to magnetic north, providing an absolute heading reference that does not drift over time.

Together, these three act as the **"sixth sense" of every autonomous mobile robot, drone, and humanoid**, allowing dead-reckoning navigation in GPS-denied environments.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following values are essential constants in IMU-based motion planning:

- **Standard Gravity:** $g = 9.80665 \ \text{m/s}^2$
- **Sampling Frequency Range:** **100 Hz – 10 kHz** (typical industrial MEMS IMUs)
- **Bias Stability (high-grade):** **< 1 °/hr** (Fiber-Optic Gyroscope class)
- **Angular Random Walk (ARW):** **0.005 – 0.1 °/√hr** (high-grade tactical units)

> [!TIP]
> The KTU board examiner frequently tests the relationship between the **Inertial Frame (i-frame)** and the **Body Frame (b-frame)**. Always draw the body axes $(x_b, y_b, z_b)$ along the sensor's sensitivity axes when answering such questions.

---

## 1.4 Role of IMUs in the Robot Motion Planning Pipeline

In the broader scope of the syllabus, the IMU sits at the **front-end of the perception-action loop**:

$$
\text{IMU} \longrightarrow \text{State Estimation (EKF/UKF)} \longrightarrow \text{Planner} \longrightarrow \text{Controller} \longrightarrow \text{Actuators}
$$

This places the IMU inside **Module 1** as the entry point to trajectory generation and dynamic obstacle avoidance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Modern IMU

An IMU is not a single sensor — it is a **micro-electromechanical system (MEMS)** or **fiber-optic / ring-laser** assembly whose outputs are fused in firmware. The hierarchy is:

1. **Mechanical Sensing Element** — proof mass (accelerometer), vibrating structure (gyroscope), magneto-resistive bridge (magnetometer).
2. **Signal Conditioning ASIC** — amplification, A/D conversion, temperature compensation.
3. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)** — bias removal, scale-factor correction, axis alignment.
4. **Sensor Fusion Algorithm** — typically an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF), Complementary Filter, or Madgwick/Mahony filter.
5. **Output Interface** — SPI, I²C, UART, or CAN bus streaming at a fixed data rate.

---

## 2.2 Operating Principles of the Three Sub-Sensors

### 2.2.1 Accelerometer — The Force Balance Principle
A MEMS accelerometer consists of a proof mass suspended by compliant springs. When the body accelerates, the mass displaces by $\Delta x$; the differential capacitor measures this displacement. The governing equation is:

$$
F_{\text{measured}} = m \cdot a = k \cdot \Delta x
$$

where $k$ is the spring stiffness. Note: **an accelerometer at rest reads $+g$ along the vertical axis**, not zero — this is the single most common conceptual trap in KTU exams.

### 2.2.2 Gyroscope — Coriolis Effect
A MEMS gyroscope uses a vibrating proof mass. When the body rotates, the Coriolis force deflects the mass perpendicular to its vibration, producing a measurable capacitance change:

$$
\vec{F}_{\text{Coriolis}} = -2 \, m \, \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}_{\text{vib}}
$$

where $\vec{\Omega}$ is the angular velocity and $\vec{v}_{\text{vib}}$ is the velocity of the vibrating mass.

### 2.2.3 Magnetometer — Anisotropic Magneto-Resistance (AMR)
A magnetometer measures the local magnetic flux density $\vec{B}$. In an outdoor setting it aligns with the Earth's field, providing an absolute yaw reference:

$$
\psi_{\text{mag}} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{-B_y}{B_x}\right)
$$

---

## 2.3 Coordinate Frames and the Rotation Matrix

The body frame (b) is rotated relative to the inertial frame (i) by the Direction Cosine Matrix (DCM), denoted $C_i^b$ or simply $R$:

$$
\vec{v}^{\,b} = R \, \vec{v}^{\,i}
$$

For Z-Y-X (yaw-pitch-roll) Euler convention, the rotation matrix is:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix}
c\theta\,c\psi & c\theta\,s\psi & -s\theta \\
s\phi\,s\theta\,c\psi - c\phi\,s\psi & s\phi\,s\theta\,s\psi + c\phi\,c\psi & s\phi\,c\theta \\
c\phi\,s\theta\,c\psi + s\phi\,s\psi & c\phi\,s\theta\,s\psi - s\phi\,c\psi & c\phi\,c\theta
\end{bmatrix}
$$

where $c\alpha \equiv \cos\alpha$ and $s\alpha \equiv \sin\alpha$.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every high-yield equation a KTU 2024 student must memorize for the IMU portion of Module 1.

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables / Units |
|---|---------|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | Newton's 2nd Law (Accelerometer) | $a = F / m$ | $a$ [m/s²], $F$ [N], $m$ [kg] |
| 2 | Coriolis Force (Gyroscope) | $F_c = 2 \, m \, \Omega \, v$ | $\Omega$ [rad/s], $v$ [m/s] |
| 3 | Accelerometer at rest (vertical) | $a_z = -g$ | $g = 9.80665$ **m/s²** |
| 4 | Yaw from magnetometer | $\psi = \arctan(-B_y / B_x)$ | $B_x, B_y$ [μT] |
| 5 | Euler angular velocity $\rightarrow$ body rates | $\begin{bmatrix}\dot\phi\\\dot\theta\\\dot\psi\end{bmatrix} = E(\phi,\theta)^{-1}\vec{\omega}^b$ | rad/s |
| 6 | Complementary Filter | $\hat\theta = \alpha(\hat\theta + \omega\,dt) + (1-\alpha)\theta_{\text{acc}}$ | $\alpha \in [0.95, 0.99]$ |
| 7 | Euler Integration (Position) | $p_{k+1} = p_k + v_k\,dt + \tfrac{1}{2}a\,dt^2$ | $p$ [m], $dt$ [s] |
| 8 | Heading Drift Rate | $\dot\psi_{\text{drift}} = b_\omega$ | $b_\omega$ [rad/s] bias |
| 9 | Quaternion update | $q_{k+1} = q_k + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\Omega(\omega)\,q_k\,dt$ | $q \in \mathbb{R}^4$ |
| 10 | Quaternion normalization | $\vert q \vert = 1$ | constraint |
| 11 | Skew-symmetric cross-product | $[\vec\omega\times] = \begin{bmatrix}0 & -\omega_z & \omega_y \\ \omega_z & 0 & -\omega_x \\ -\omega_y & \omega_x & 0\end{bmatrix}$ | rad/s |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of IMUs

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Applications Beyond Syllabus (Board Favourite):**
> - **Aerial Robotics:** PX4 and Ardupilot flight controllers run IMU fusion at 1 kHz.
> - **Autonomous Vehicles:** INS/GPS fusion provides lane-level positioning.
> - **Humanoid Robots (Boston Dynamics Atlas):** Whole-body control uses IMU feedback at every link.
> - **AR/VR Headsets:** IMU drift correction enables 6-DoF head tracking.
> - **Biomedical:** Gait analysis using foot-mounted IMU + zero-velocity-update (ZUPT).

This breadth often appears in KTU viva-voce and 3-mark descriptive questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Transforming Accelerometer Output from Body to Inertial Frame

The accelerometer measures the **specific force** $\vec{f}^b$ in the body frame. To obtain the acceleration in the inertial frame (which we can double-integrate to get position), we must rotate it.

**Step 1 — Write the accelerometer output in the body frame:**
The specific force measured by the accelerometer is:
$$
\vec{f}^{\,b} = \vec{a}^{\,b} - R^T \vec{g}^{\,i}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate the body-frame acceleration:**
$$
\vec{a}^{\,b} = \vec{f}^{\,b} + R^T \vec{g}^{\,i}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the rotation matrix $R$ to bring this into the inertial frame:**
$$
\vec{a}^{\,i} = R \, \vec{a}^{\,b} = R \, \vec{f}^{\,b} + R \, R^T \, \vec{g}^{\,i}
$$

**Step 4 — Use the orthogonality property $R R^T = I$:**
$$
\vec{a}^{\,i} = R \, \vec{f}^{\,b} + \vec{g}^{\,i}
$$

**Step 5 — Therefore the linear position update via Euler integration is:**
$$
\vec{v}_{k+1}^{\,i} = \vec{v}_k^{\,i} + \vec{a}^{\,i} \, dt
$$

$$
\vec{p}_{k+1}^{\,i} = \vec{p}_k^{\,i} + \vec{v}_k^{\,i} \, dt + \tfrac{1}{2} \, \vec{a}^{\,i} \, dt^2
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Complementary Filter for Pitch Estimation

We want to estimate the pitch angle $\theta$. The gyroscope gives us a high-rate but drifting estimate; the accelerometer gives a noisy but drift-free estimate at rest.

**Step 1 — Gyroscope integration of pitch:**
$$
\theta_{\text{gyro},k+1} = \theta_{\text{gyro},k} + \omega_y \, dt
$$

**Step 2 — Accelerometer-derived pitch (when motion is slow):**
$$
\theta_{\text{acc}} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{a_x}{\sqrt{a_y^2 + a_z^2}}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Fused pitch via complementary filter:**
$$
\hat\theta_{k+1} = \alpha \, \hat\theta_k + (1-\alpha)\,\theta_{\text{acc}} + \omega_y \, dt
$$

**Step 4 — Re-arrange to expose the underlying structure:**
$$
\hat\theta_{k+1} = \alpha \!\left(\hat\theta_k + \omega_y \, dt\right) + (1-\alpha)\,\theta_{\text{acc}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The term in parentheses is the **gyro prediction** (short-term accurate); $\theta_{\text{acc}}$ is the **long-term correction**. The weight $\alpha$ is a design parameter (typically **0.95–0.99**), tuned based on the gyroscope's bias stability.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Quaternion Update Equation

The quaternion kinematic equation avoids the singularities (gimbal lock) of Euler angles.

**Step 1 — Define the quaternion derivative in terms of body angular velocity $\vec{\omega}^{\,b}$:**
$$
\dot{q} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \Omega(\vec{\omega}) \, q
$$

**Step 2 — Construct the $4 \times 4$ matrix $\Omega(\vec{\omega})$:**
$$
\Omega(\vec{\omega}) = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & -\omega_x & -\omega_y & -\omega_z \\
\omega_x & 0 & \omega_z & -\omega_y \\
\omega_y & -\omega_z & 0 & \omega_x \\
\omega_z & \omega_y & -\omega_x & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply first-order Euler integration for digital sampling:**
$$
q_{k+1} = q_k + \dot{q}_k \, dt = q_k + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\Omega(\vec{\omega}_k)\,q_k\,dt
$$

**Step 4 — Normalize the quaternion to enforce the unit-norm constraint:**
$$
q_{k+1} \leftarrow \dfrac{q_{k+1}}{\vert q_{k+1}\vert}
$$

where $\vert q_{k+1}\vert = \sqrt{q_0^2 + q_1^2 + q_2^2 + q_3^2}$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Error Sources

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below maps each major error to its physical origin, mathematical model, and the engineering remedy. KTU students should be able to recall at least 4 of these for full marks.

| Error Source | Mathematical Model | Symptom in Robot | Engineering Remedy |
|--------------|--------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Gyro Bias** | $\omega_{\text{meas}} = \omega_{\text{true}} + b$ | Linear drift in heading ($\dot\psi \propto b$) | Online EKF bias estimation |
| **Accelerometer Bias** | $f_{\text{meas}} = f_{\text{true}} + b_a$ | Constant position offset | Calibration at known orientation |
| **Scale Factor Error** | $f_{\text{meas}} = (1 + s)\,f_{\text{true}}$ | Under/over-estimation of distance | Factory calibration |
| **Misalignment** | $f_{\text{meas}} = M \, f_{\text{true}}$, $M \ne I$ | Cross-axis coupling | Ortho-algorithm |
| **White Noise** | $\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$ | High-frequency jitter | Low-pass filter / Allan variance |
| **Temperature Drift** | $b(T) = b_0 + k_T(T-T_0)$ | Bias variation across mission | In-sensor compensation |
| **Gimbal Lock (Euler)** | $\cos\theta \rightarrow 0$ | Singularity at $\theta = 90°$ | Use quaternion |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IMU Functional Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the internal signal flow of a typical MEMS IMU and its integration into a robot motion-planning stack.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Body Motion] --> B[3-Axis Accelerometer]
    A --> C[3-Axis Gyroscope]
    A --> D[3-Axis Magnetometer]
    B --> E[Analog Front End and ADC]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Temperature Compensation]
    F --> G[Digital Signal Processor]
    G --> H{Sensor Fusion Algorithm}
    H --> H1[Extended Kalman Filter]
    H --> H2[Complementary Filter]
    H --> H3[Madgwick Filter]
    H1 --> I[State Estimator]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Pose: Position Velocity Orientation]
    J --> K[Robot Motion Planner]
    K --> L[Trajectory Generator]
    L --> M[Low Level Controller]
    M --> N[Motor Actuators]
    N --> A
```

---

## 4.2 IMU Coordinate Frame and Rotation Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IF[INERTIAL FRAME i]
        XI[X_i axis North]
        YI[Y_i axis East]
        ZI[Z_i axis Down]
    end
    subgraph BF[BODY FRAME b]
        XB[X_b axis Forward]
        YB[Y_b axis Right]
        ZB[Z_b axis Down]
    end
    XI -- Rotation R yaw pitch roll --> XB
    YI --> YB
    ZI --> ZB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The body frame is rigidly attached to the sensor housing. The DCM $R \in SO(3)$ maps any inertial-frame vector into the body frame. For motion planning, the inverse $R^T$ is often more useful.

---

## 4.3 Sensor Fusion Data Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GY[Gyroscope Integration] --> P1[Short Term Orientation Estimate]
    AC[Accelerometer Gravity Vector] --> P2[Long Term Tilt Estimate]
    MG[Magnetometer Heading Vector] --> P3[Absolute Yaw Estimate]
    P1 --> FU[Fusion Block EKF or Complementary]
    P2 --> FU
    P3 --> FU
    FU --> OUT[Final Orientation Quaternion and Euler]
    OUT --> PL[Robot Planner Input]
```

---

## 4.4 IMU Error Taxonomy Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E[IMU Errors] --> E1[Deterministic Errors]
    E --> E2[Stochastic Errors]
    E1 --> E1a[Bias]
    E1 --> E1b[Scale Factor]
    E1 --> E1c[Axis Misalignment]
    E2 --> E2a[White Noise]
    E2 --> E2b[Random Walk]
    E2 --> E2c[Bias Instability]
    E2 --> E2d[Temperature Drift]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. Define an Inertial Measurement Unit. List its main components.**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> An **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** is an electronic device that measures a body's linear acceleration and angular velocity using a combination of accelerometers, gyroscopes, and (optionally) magnetometers.
>
> **Main Components:**
> 1. **3-axis Accelerometer** — measures specific force along x, y, z (unit: m/s²).
> 2. **3-axis Gyroscope** — measures angular velocity around x, y, z (unit: rad/s).
> 3. **3-axis Magnetometer** — measures Earth's magnetic field (unit: μT).
> 4. **Signal Conditioning & A/D Converter**
> 5. **Microcontroller / DSP for sensor fusion**
>
> It is widely used in robotics for **dead-reckoning, attitude estimation, and feedback in motion-planning loops**. *(3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. Why does a stationary accelerometer read $+g$ and not zero?**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> A stationary accelerometer is subject to the reaction force from the ground that prevents it from free-falling. According to Newton's second law:
> $$ \vec{f}^{\,b} = \vec{a}^{\,b} - \vec{g} $$
> At rest $\vec{a}^{\,b} = 0$, so the accelerometer reads the negative of gravity along the vertical axis:
> $$ \vec{f}^{\,b} = -\vec{g} $$
> This is the principle used to compute the **tilt angle** in attitude estimation. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full ESE Question (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Many students forget to **subtract gravity** when integrating the accelerometer to obtain position — this costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. In quaternion derivations, the **normalization step** is frequently omitted — examiners specifically deduct for this.
> 3. Do not confuse the **angular velocity in the body frame** ($\omega^b$) with the **Euler-angle rates** ($\dot\phi, \dot\theta, \dot\psi$) — they are related by a non-trivial transformation matrix $E(\phi,\theta)$.
> 4. Always specify the **units** of bias and noise — leaving units out is a 1-mark deduction per occurrence.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of a modern MEMS IMU. Discuss the role of each block. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **complementary filter** for pitch-angle estimation, clearly stating the role of the gyroscope and the accelerometer in the fusion process. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Internal Architecture of a MEMS IMU (7 marks):**

The IMU consists of the following functional blocks:

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **Proof Mass + Spring System** | Converts mechanical motion to displacement. |
| **Capacitive / Piezoelectric Pick-off** | Converts displacement to electrical signal. |
| **Analog Front-End (AFE)** | Amplifies and filters the raw signal. |
| **A/D Converter** | Digitizes the analog signal. |
| **Temperature Sensor** | Measures local die temperature. |
| **DSP / Microcontroller** | Runs bias/alignment correction and fusion. |
| **Communication Interface** | SPI / I²C / UART / CAN. |

A block diagram was drawn (see Section 4.1). Each block's role is essential: the **proof mass** provides inertial reference; the **pick-off** transduces motion; the **AFE** conditions the signal; the **DSP** runs the EKF or complementary filter; the **interface** streams the fused pose to the robot's planner. *(7 marks)*

**(b) Derivation of Complementary Filter (7 marks):**

> Step 1 — Gyroscope-based prediction:
> $$\theta_{\text{gyro},k+1} = \theta_{\text{gyro},k} + \omega_y \, dt$$
>
> Step 2 — Accelerometer-based measurement (gravity reference):
> $$\theta_{\text{acc}} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{a_x}{\sqrt{a_y^2 + a_z^2}}\right)$$
>
> Step 3 — Linear combination:
> $$\hat\theta_{k+1} = \alpha \!\left(\hat\theta_k + \omega_y \, dt\right) + (1-\alpha)\,\theta_{\text{acc}}$$
>
> Step 4 — Choice of $\alpha$: typically **0.95–0.99**, balancing short-term gyro accuracy against long-term accelerometer stability.
>
> Step 5 — Comment: This filter acts as a **high-pass filter on the gyroscope** and a **low-pass filter on the accelerometer**, hence the name "complementary." *(7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`
