**(a)** Define a **quaternion** and derive the quaternion update equation for an IMU sampling at period $dt$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **major error sources** in an IMU (bias, scale factor, noise, misalignment) and explain how each affects the robot motion-planning accuracy. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Quaternion Update Equation (7 marks):**

> A **quaternion** is a four-element hyper-complex number $q = [q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3]^T = [w, \vec{u}]$ used to represent 3D rotation without singularities.
>
> The kinematic relation is:
> $$\dot{q} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\Omega(\vec{\omega}^{\,b})\,q$$
> where
> $$\Omega(\vec{\omega}) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -\omega_x & -\omega_y & -\omega_z \\ \omega_x & 0 & \omega_z & -\omega_y \\ \omega_y & -\omega_z & 0 & \omega_x \\ \omega_z & \omega_y & -\omega_x & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> Discrete integration:
> $$q_{k+1} = q_k + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\Omega(\vec{\omega}_k)\,q_k\,dt$$
> followed by normalization:
> $$q_{k+1} \leftarrow \dfrac{q_{k+1}}{\vert q_{k+1}\vert}$$
> *(7 marks)*

**(b) Major IMU Error Sources (7 marks):**

| Error | Mathematical Model | Effect on Planner |
|-------|---------------------|--------------------|
| Bias | $\omega_m = \omega + b$ | Heading drift over time |
| Scale Factor | $\omega_m = (1+s)\omega$ | Speed/angle mis-estimation |
| White Noise | $\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$ | Trajectory jitter |
| Misalignment | $M \ne I$ | Cross-axis coupling |
| Temperature Drift | $b(T) = b_0 + k_T \Delta T$ | Time-varying inaccuracy |

> Mitigation: **factory calibration**, **online EKF bias estimation**, and **allan-variance noise modelling**. *(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for IMUs (Module 1)**

- ⭐ An **IMU** combines accelerometer (specific force) + gyroscope (angular velocity) ± magnetometer (heading).
- ⭐ **Gravity subtraction** is mandatory before double-integrating accelerometer data to obtain position.
- ⭐ The standard **gravity constant** $g = 9.80665$ **m/s²** appears in every transformation equation.
- ⭐ Three coordinate frames: **Inertial (i)**, **Body (b)**, and (optionally) **Earth-Centered-Earth-Fixed (ECEF)**.
- ⭐ **Rotation matrix $R \in SO(3)$** maps vectors between frames; $R R^T = I$.
- ⭐ **Euler angles** suffer **gimbal lock** at pitch $= \pm 90°$ — prefer **quaternions** in planners.
- ⭐ The **complementary filter** uses $\alpha \in [0.95, 0.99]$ for accelerometer–gyro fusion.
- ⭐ **EKF / UKF** are the standard sensor-fusion algorithms for full 9-DOF IMUs.
- ⭐ Errors: **bias**, **scale factor**, **misalignment**, **white noise**, **temperature drift** — memorize their mathematical models.
- ⭐ **Quaternion update**: $q_{k+1} = q_k + \tfrac{1}{2}\Omega(\omega) q_k \, dt$; always normalize afterwards.
- ⭐ IMUs are **proprioceptive sensors** — they do not depend on external infrastructure (no GPS, no cameras), enabling **GPS-denied navigation**.
- ⭐ In KTU answers, always draw the **body frame axes** and indicate the **direction of gravity** for full credit.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distance Sensors - ToF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Distance Sensors - ToF

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Distance Sensors — Time of Flight (ToF)

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Time-of-Flight (ToF) distance sensor** is a non-contact ranging device that determines the distance between the sensor and a target object by precisely measuring the **round-trip travel time** of an emitted signal — typically a **light pulse (LiDAR/Laser ToF)**, a **radio wave (Radar ToF)**, or an **acoustic wave (Ultrasonic ToF)** — from the transmitter, to the target, and back to the co-located or adjacent receiver.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "A ToF sensor is a ranging transducer whose output is a digital or analog representation of the temporal interval elapsed between emission of a wave packet and the detection of its reflection, from which absolute distance is computed using the constitutive relation $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$."

The factor of **2** in the denominator is critical because the measured time $t$ corresponds to a **round trip** (transmitter → target → receiver), while distance $d$ is the **one-way** path length.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Canyon Echo"

Imagine you stand at the edge of a deep canyon and **shout "Hello!"**. The sound travels to the opposite wall, bounces back, and you hear your echo after a delay.

- If the canyon wall is **170 m away**, the echo returns in approximately **1 second** (sound travels at ~340 m/s, round trip = 340 m).
- If the wall were **5 km away**, you'd wait almost **30 seconds** before hearing yourself.

A ToF sensor does **exactly this**, but with two upgrades:
1. The "shout" is an **ultra-short laser/light pulse** (nanoseconds long), so the round-trip time is measurable for distances in **centimeters to hundreds of meters**.
2. The "ear" is a high-speed photodetector connected to a **Time-to-Digital Converter (TDC)**, giving picosecond resolution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Because light travels at $3 \times 10^8$ m/s, a target only **15 cm** away produces a round-trip of just **1 nanosecond**. This is why ToF electronics are engineered for sub-nanosecond precision.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants are the **backbone of every ToF calculation** you will encounter in KTU examinations:

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | $2.998 \times 10^8$ | m/s |
| Speed of light in air | $c_{air}$ | $\approx 2.997 \times 10^8$ | m/s |
| Speed of sound in air (20°C) | $v_s$ | $\approx 343$ | m/s |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ | J·s |
| Wavelength of typical 905 nm laser | $\lambda$ | $905 \times 10^{-9}$ | m |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Shortcut:** Most KTU problems use $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s unless otherwise specified. Always read the question carefully — if the medium is specified (e.g., water, glass), $c$ changes drastically.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The ToF measurement chain follows a precise sequence. Each step introduces a specific physical or electronic phenomenon:

1. **Signal Generation:** A pulse generator triggers the emitter (laser diode, LED, ultrasonic transducer, or RF antenna) to emit a wave packet of known temporal shape.
2. **Free-Space Propagation:** The wave packet travels through the medium at the medium-specific propagation velocity ($c$ for EM waves, $v_s$ for sound).
3. **Target Reflection / Scattering:** Upon striking the target surface, a portion of the wave's energy is reflected or scattered back toward the sensor (Lambertian or specular, depending on surface).
4. **Detection:** The receiver (photodiode, photomultiplier, microphone, or RF mixer) converts the returned wave packet into an electrical signal.
5. **Time Measurement:** A high-resolution timing circuit — usually a **TDC (Time-to-Digital Converter)** — measures the interval $\Delta t$ between the emitted and received pulses with sub-nanosecond accuracy.
6. **Distance Computation:** A microcontroller applies the constitutive equation $d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ to obtain the absolute range.
7. **Filtering & Output:** Statistical filters (Kalman, moving average) reduce noise; the result is output via digital bus (I²C, SPI, UART) or analog voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why divide by 2?** The timing circuit starts the clock at emission and stops it at reception. This captures the **full round-trip path (2d)**. To recover the **one-way distance $d$**, we must halve the product $c \cdot \Delta t$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | Basic ToF distance equation | $d$ = distance, $c$ = wave speed, $t$ = round-trip time | m, m/s, s |
| 2 | $d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta\varphi}{4 \pi f}$ | Phase-shift ToF (CW modulated) | $\Delta\varphi$ = phase shift, $f$ = modulation freq | m, rad, Hz |
| 3 | $t = \frac{2d}{c}$ | Reverse — compute time from distance | — | s |
| 4 | $\delta d = \frac{c}{2} \cdot \delta t$ | Range resolution vs. timing resolution | $\delta t$ = timing jitter | m |
| 5 | $f_{max} = \frac{c}{4 \cdot d_{min}}$ | Max unambiguous frequency (CW ToF) | $d_{min}$ = min range | Hz |
| 6 | $E_p = \frac{h c}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy of emitted pulse | $h$ = Planck, $\lambda$ = wavelength | J, m |
| 7 | $P_r = P_t \cdot \frac{G_t G_r \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$ | Radar range equation (for radar ToF) | $\sigma$ = RCS, $R$ = range, $G$ = antenna gain | W, m² |
| 8 | $I = I_0 \cdot \frac{\cos\theta}{R^2}$ | Lambertian intensity falloff | $\theta$ = incidence angle | W/m² |
| 9 | $\text{Accuracy} = \pm\left(\frac{c}{2}\right) \cdot \sigma_t$ | Statistical range accuracy | $\sigma_t$ = time measurement std. dev. | m |
| 10 | $d_{ambiguous} = \frac{c}{2 f_{mod}}$ | Max unambiguous range (CW ToF) | $f_{mod}$ = modulation frequency | m |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Trap:** Notice the table above uses `\cdot`, `\sigma_t`, and `\frac{}{}` instead of the vertical bar `|`. This prevents LaTeX table parsing errors in markdown renderers — a habit you should adopt in your own KTU answer sheets.

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

ToF sensors are foundational in:

- **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs):** Obstacle avoidance, SLAM mapping, simultaneous localization.
- **Automotive ADAS:** Adaptive cruise control, automatic emergency braking, blind-spot detection.
- **Industrial Automation:** Bin-picking, palletizing robots, conveyor belt volume measurement.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Camera autofocus, face-recognition depth maps (Apple Face ID, Microsoft Kinect v2).
- **UAV / Drones:** Terrain following, altitude hold, collision avoidance.
- **Medical Imaging:** Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT), capnography airflow sensing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why engineers prefer ToF over stereo vision:** ToF provides **direct, absolute metric depth** without requiring texture, ambient lighting, or computationally expensive feature matching. It is robust to material/color variations that confuse passive vision systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Core ToF Equation

We derive the fundamental relation $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$ from first principles.

**Assumptions:**
- The wave propagates in a **homogeneous, isotropic medium** with constant velocity $c$.
- The target acts as a **point reflector** or a surface oriented such that the incident ray reflects directly back to the receiver.
- The measurement is taken at a single instant $t$ (quasi-static scenario).

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — Express one-way travel time from emitter to target:

$$t_1 = \frac{d}{c}$$

Step 2 — Express one-way travel time from target back to receiver (equal to $t_1$ since the same distance is covered at the same speed):

$$t_2 = \frac{d}{c}$$

Step 3 — The total round-trip time recorded by the timing circuit:

$$t = t_1 + t_2$$

Step 4 — Substitute:

$$t = \frac{d}{c} + \frac{d}{c} = \frac{2d}{c}$$

Step 5 — Solve for $d$:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

This is the **canonical ToF distance equation**, memorized by every robotics student worldwide.

## 3.2 Worked Example #1 — Time Calculation for a Given Range

**Problem:** A robotic vacuum cleaner uses a 905 nm laser ToF sensor. The sensor detects an obstacle at a measured round-trip time of $33.3$ ns. Calculate the distance to the obstacle. Take $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify the relevant equation. From the Formula Sheet (Row 1):

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:

$$d = \frac{(3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}) \cdot (33.3 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s})}{2}$$

Step 3 — Multiply the numerator:

$$d = \frac{9.99 \text{ m}}{2}$$

Step 4 — Divide:

$$d = 4.995 \text{ m} \approx 5.0 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** The obstacle is **5 metres** away.

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** Light travels ~30 cm in 1 ns. A round trip of 33.3 ns corresponds to ~10 m round trip → ~5 m one way. ✔ Consistent.

## 3.3 Worked Example #2 — Range Resolution

**Problem:** A LiDAR system has a TDC with a timing jitter (standard deviation) of $\sigma_t = 50$ ps. Compute the single-shot range resolution.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Use the resolution formula (Row 4 in the Formula Sheet):

$$\delta d = \frac{c}{2} \cdot \delta t$$

Step 2 — Plug in values:

$$\delta d = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{2} \cdot (50 \times 10^{-12} \text{ s})$$

Step 3 — Multiply:

$$\delta d = 1.5 \times 10^8 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-12}$$

Step 4 — Simplify:

$$\delta d = 1.5 \cdot 50 \cdot 10^{-4} = 75 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0075 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** $\delta d = 7.5$ **mm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 50 ps jitter TDC yields ~7.5 mm range resolution per shot. Averaging $N$ shots improves this by $\sqrt{N}$.

## 3.4 Worked Example #3 — Phase-Shift ToF (CW Modulation)

**Problem:** A Continuous-Wave (CW) modulated ToF camera emits light modulated at $f_{mod} = 20$ MHz. The reflected signal shows a phase shift of $\Delta\varphi = \pi/3$ radians. Compute the distance.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Apply the phase-shift ToF equation (Row 2):

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta\varphi}{4 \pi f_{mod}}$$

Step 2 — Substitute:

$$d = \frac{(3 \times 10^8) \cdot (\pi/3)}{4 \pi \cdot (20 \times 10^6)}$$

Step 3 — Cancel $\pi$:

$$d = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \cdot 1}{12 \cdot 20 \times 10^6}$$

Step 4 — Simplify denominator:

$$d = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{240 \times 10^6} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.4 \times 10^8} = 1.25 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** $d = 1.25$ **m**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Phase-Shift Ambiguity:** CW ToF works only when $d \le d_{ambiguous} = \frac{c}{2 f_{mod}}$. For $f_{mod} = 20$ MHz, max unambiguous range is $7.5$ m. Beyond this, the phase wraps around and produces aliasing errors.

## 3.5 Comparative Analysis Matrix — ToF vs. Triangulation vs. Interferometry

| Property | ToF | Triangulation | Interferometry |
|----------|-----|---------------|----------------|
| Range | 0.1 m – 300 m | 0.01 m – 10 m | µm – 1 m |
| Accuracy | mm – cm | µm – mm | nm – µm |
| Speed | Very fast (ns–µs) | Medium | Slow |
| Surface dependency | Low | High (needs texture/feature) | Very high (needs reflector) |
| Computational cost | Low | High | High |
| Cost | Medium | Low | Very high |
| Ambient light sensitivity | Medium (filtered) | High | Low |
| KTU typical use-case | Robot obstacle avoidance | Bin-picking arm | Precision CNC calibration |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — ToF Sensor Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **signal and data flow** inside a typical ToF ranging module. Each block represents a hardware subsystem, and arrows show unidirectional data dependencies.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Microcontroller Trigger Pulse] --> B[Pulse Generator]
    B --> C[Laser Diode Driver]
    C --> D[Emitter Optics 905nm]
    D --> E[Free Space Propagation]
    E --> F[Target Surface]
    F --> G[Reflected Wave]
    G --> H[Receiver Optics]
    H --> I[Photodiode / Avalanche Detector]
    I --> J[Transimpedance Amplifier]
    J --> K[Comparator]
    K --> L[Time to Digital Converter TDC]
    B --> L
    L --> M[Range Computation Unit]
    M --> N[Kalman / Median Filter]
    N --> O[Output Interface I2C SPI UART]
    O --> P[Host Processor / Robot Controller]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **top branch** (A → B → C → D) is the **emission chain** — it generates and launches the optical pulse.
- The **middle branch** (E → F → G) represents the **physical round-trip** in free space.
- The **bottom branch** (H → I → J → K → L) is the **reception chain** — it captures and conditions the returning pulse.
- The **timing connection** (B → L) is the **START signal** fed to the TDC, while the photodiode output is the **STOP signal** (K → L).
- The **right branch** (L → M → N → O → P) is the **digital post-processing chain** — it converts the raw time interval into a filtered distance reading delivered to the robot's main controller.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The TDC is the heart of the ToF sensor. The START-STOP architecture is identical in principle to a **digital stopwatch** — the only difference is that the "stop" event is triggered by a single photon, not a human finger.

## 4.2 Operational State Diagram — ToF Measurement Cycle

This diagram depicts the **state transitions** of the ToF sensor during a single measurement cycle. It clarifies the temporal behavior expected by examiners when asking "explain the working cycle of a ToF sensor."

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> TriggerReceived : External trigger
    TriggerReceived --> Emitting : Laser pulse ON
    Emitting --> WaitingForReturn : STOP arm enabled
    WaitingForReturn --> EchoDetected : Photodiode fires
    EchoDetected --> Computing : TDC latches interval
    Computing --> Filtering : Apply Kalman
    Filtering --> Ready : Publish distance
    Ready --> Idle : Cycle complete
    WaitingForReturn --> Timeout : No echo within window
    Timeout --> Idle : Return ERROR_NO_TARGET
```

**Key state descriptions:**
- **Idle:** Sensor awaiting external trigger; laser is OFF, power-save mode active.
- **Emitting:** The laser diode is pulsed for 5–50 ns.
- **WaitingForReturn:** TDC counter is running; sensor expects a return pulse within $t_{max} = 2d_{max}/c$.
- **EchoDetected:** Photodiode threshold crossed; counter is frozen.
- **Timeout:** No return received (target out of range or absorbing); error flag is raised.
- **Ready:** Valid range output is published via the digital bus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Favourite Question:** "What happens when the target is beyond the maximum range?" — The correct answer is the **Timeout state**, where the sensor outputs an error code (commonly `0xFF` or `-1`) rather than a garbage number.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a Time-of-Flight (ToF) distance sensor. State the governing equation and explain why a factor of 2 appears in the denominator.**

**Model Answer:**

A Time-of-Flight (ToF) distance sensor is a non-contact ranging device that measures the round-trip travel time of an emitted wave packet (light, sound, or RF) to compute the distance to a target.

Governing equation:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

where $c$ is the propagation speed in the medium and $t$ is the measured round-trip time.

**Reason for the factor 2:** The timing circuit starts its clock at emission and stops it at the instant the reflected wave is detected. This measured interval $t$ covers the **outward journey (emitter → target)** *and* the **return journey (target → receiver)**. Since the absolute distance to the target is the one-way path, we must divide the total distance $c \cdot t$ by 2.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between pulsed ToF and continuous-wave (CW) modulated ToF. Mention one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Pulsed ToF | CW Modulated ToF |
|---------|-----------|------------------|
| Emission | Short discrete light pulse | Sinusoidally modulated continuous wave |
| Measurement | Direct time-of-arrival interval | Phase shift $\Delta\varphi$ between emitted and received wave |
| Distance formula | $d = c \cdot t / 2$ | $d = c \cdot \Delta\varphi / (4 \pi f_{mod})$ |
| Range resolution | Limited by pulse width & TDC jitter | Limited by phase noise & modulation frequency |
| Advantage | Long range, no ambiguity | Better precision at short range, lower power |
| Disadvantage | Requires high-speed TDC | Ambiguity beyond $c/(2 f_{mod})$ |

**Advantage of pulsed ToF:** Unambiguous distance measurement; suitable for long-range LiDAR.

**Advantage of CW ToF:** Better signal-to-noise ratio at short ranges; no need for ultra-fast TDCs.

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Pulsed ToF Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1 Choice Question]**

#### (a) A robotic AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) uses a 905 nm pulsed laser ToF sensor. The sensor reports a round-trip time of $t = 200$ ns when scanning a pallet placed in its path. Calculate: (i) the distance to the pallet, (ii) the maximum unambiguous range of the sensor if the pulse repetition frequency (PRF) is $f_{PRF} = 500$ kHz. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Distance Calculation:**

Apply the canonical ToF equation:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

Substitute $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s and $t = 200 \times 10^{-9}$ s:

$$d = \frac{(3 \times 10^8) \cdot (200 \times 10^{-9})}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{60 \text{ m}}{2} = 30 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** The pallet is **30 m** from the AGV.

**(ii) Maximum Unambiguous Range:**

The maximum unambiguous range is set by the time between successive pulses:

$$T_{PRF} = \frac{1}{f_{PRF}} = \frac{1}{500 \times 10^3} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 2 \text{ µs}$$

The round-trip distance corresponding to this interval is:

$$d_{max} = \frac{c \cdot T_{PRF}}{2} = \frac{(3 \times 10^8) \cdot (2 \times 10^{-6})}{2} = 300 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** Maximum unambiguous range = **300 m**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation:** Since the measured distance (30 m) is less than $d_{max}$ (300 m), the reading is unambiguous. ✔

#### (b) Explain the working of a Time-to-Digital Converter (TDC) in a ToF sensor. How does the timing jitter $\sigma_t$ of the TDC affect the range accuracy? Derive the relevant relationship. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Working of TDC:**

A Time-to-Digital Converter is a high-precision digital timing circuit that measures the time interval between a START event and a STOP event. In a ToF sensor:
- The **START** signal is generated when the laser pulse is emitted.
- The **STOP** signal is generated when the photodiode detects the returning echo.
- The TDC measures the elapsed interval $\Delta t$ with sub-nanosecond resolution (typically picoseconds).

Common TDC architectures include **delay-line TDCs**, **vernier TDCs**, and **FPGA-based tapped-delay TDCs**. The measured time is converted to a digital word and forwarded to a microcontroller for distance computation.

**Derivation of Range Accuracy vs. Timing Jitter:**

Let $\sigma_t$ be the standard deviation (jitter) of the TDC's timing measurement. The range equation is:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

Differentiating with respect to $t$ (treating $c$ as constant):

$$\frac{\partial d}{\partial t} = \frac{c}{2}$$

For small, statistically independent fluctuations, the standard deviation of the range is:

$$\sigma_d = \frac{c}{2} \cdot \sigma_t$$

**Numerical Example:** If $\sigma_t = 100$ ps:

$$\sigma_d = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2} \cdot (100 \times 10^{-12}) = 1.5 \times 10^{-2} = 1.5 \text{ cm}$$

**Answer:** Range accuracy is linearly proportional to timing jitter. Halving $\sigma_t$ halves the range error.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** A 10 ps jitter TDC gives 1.5 mm range accuracy — a 100 ps jitter TDC gives 1.5 cm. This is why high-end LiDAR (e.g., Velodyne, Hesai) invest heavily in low-jitter timing ASICs.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — CW Phase-Shift ToF & Comparative Study

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 Choice Question]**

#### (a) A CW modulated ToF camera operates at a modulation frequency of $f_{mod} = 10$ MHz. The detected phase shift between emitted and reflected signals is $\Delta\varphi = \pi/2$ radians. Determine: (i) the distance to the target, (ii) the maximum unambiguous range, and (iii) the new distance reading if the modulation frequency is doubled to 20 MHz while the same physical distance is maintained. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Distance:**

Use the phase-shift ToF formula:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta\varphi}{4 \pi f_{mod}}$$

Substitute $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $\Delta\varphi = \pi/2$, $f_{mod} = 10 \times 10^6$ Hz:

$$d = \frac{(3 \times 10^8) \cdot (\pi/2)}{4 \pi \cdot (10 \times 10^6)}$$

Cancel $\pi$:

$$d = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \cdot 1}{8 \cdot 10^7} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{8 \times 10^7} = 3.75 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** $d = 3.75$ m.

**(ii) Maximum Unambiguous Range:**

The unambiguous range is reached when $\Delta\varphi = 2\pi$:

$$d_{max} = \frac{c}{2 f_{mod}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \cdot 10 \times 10^6} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^7} = 15 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** $d_{max} = 15$ m.

**(iii) New Distance at 20 MHz:**

If the physical distance is unchanged (3.75 m) and $f_{mod}$ doubles to 20 MHz, the new phase shift becomes:

$$\Delta\varphi_{new} = \frac{4 \pi f_{mod} \cdot d}{c} = \frac{4 \pi \cdot (20 \times 10^6) \cdot 3.75}{3 \times 10^8}$$

$$\Delta\varphi_{new} = \frac{300 \pi \times 10^6}{3 \times 10^8} = \pi \text{ rad}$$

**Apparent reading** (as computed naively by the formula):

$$d_{read} = \frac{(3 \times 10^8) \cdot \pi}{4 \pi \cdot 20 \times 10^6} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{8 \times 10^7} = 3.75 \text{ m}$$

The reading is correct because the distance is still within the new unambiguous range ($d_{max,new} = 7.5$ m).

> [!WARNING]
> **Conceptual Trap:** If the physical distance were greater than 7.5 m at 20 MHz, the phase would wrap around and the sensor would report an **incorrect (aliased) distance**. This is the classic CW-ToF ambiguity problem.

#### (b) Compare Time-of-Flight (ToF), ultrasonic, and stereo-vision distance sensing techniques for mobile robot obstacle avoidance. Highlight at least four comparative parameters. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

A mobile robot navigating in an indoor warehouse must reliably detect obstacles. The three principal candidate sensing modalities are compared below:

| Parameter | ToF (LiDAR) | Ultrasonic | Stereo Vision |
|-----------|-------------|------------|---------------|
| **Range** | 0.1 m – 200 m | 0.02 m – 10 m | 0.5 m – 50 m |
| **Accuracy** | mm – cm (high) | cm (moderate) | cm – dm (depends on baseline & texture) |
| **Field of view** | Narrow beam (needs scanning) | Wide cone (~15°–30°) | Configurable by lens |
| **Surface dependency** | Low (works on dark/light) | Medium (specular surfaces fail) | High (requires texture for matching) |
| **Ambient light** | Robust (filtered) | Robust (uses sound) | Sensitive (washed out in sunlight) |
| **Update rate** | 10 kHz – 100 kHz | 20 Hz – 100 Hz | 30 Hz – 60 Hz |
| **Cost** | Medium–High | Low | Medium |
| **Power consumption** | Medium | Low | High (compute-intensive) |
| **Best use-case** | 360° SLAM mapping | Short-range bumper | Object recognition with depth |

**Conclusion for AGV/AMR obstacle avoidance:**
- **ToF (LiDAR)** is the **gold standard** for indoor/outdoor AMRs — it combines long range, high accuracy, and fast updates, with minimal surface dependency. The drawback is cost.
- **Ultrasonic** sensors are ideal as **low-cost backup bumpers** for short-range detection (e.g., detecting a 10 cm box 30 cm away). They are immune to optical interference and dust.
- **Stereo vision** excels when the robot needs **semantic information** (e.g., "is this a person or a pillar?"). For pure obstacle avoidance, its computational cost is often unjustified.

A modern AMR typically uses a **multi-modal sensor fusion** strategy: a primary 2D/3D LiDAR for mapping and obstacle detection, supplemented by ultrasonic sensors for redundancy and cameras for classification.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 ToF Module 1**

- ✅ **Canonical Equation:** $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$ — memorize and apply without hesitation.
- ✅ **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s in air/vacuum; $c$ in water is $\approx 2.25 \times 10^8$ m/s; in glass $\approx 2.0 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- ✅ **Speed of sound:** $v_s \approx 343$ m/s at 20°C; temperature-dependent: $v_s = 331 + 0.6 T$ (m/s, $T$ in °C).
- ✅ **Phase-shift ToF:** $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta\varphi}{4 \pi f_{mod}}$.
- ✅ **Range resolution:** $\delta d = \dfrac{c}{2} \cdot \sigma_t$. A 50 ps TDC gives 7.5 mm resolution.
- ✅ **Max unambiguous range (CW):** $d_{ambiguous} = \dfrac{c}{2 f_{mod}}$.
- ✅ **Pulse ToF ambiguity limit:** $d_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 f_{PRF}}$.
- ✅ **TDC architecture:** START (emission) → STOP (echo) → digital word → microcontroller.
- ✅ **Photon energy:** $E_p = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$. At 905 nm, $E_p \approx 2.19 \times 10^{-19}$ J (≈ 1.37 eV).
- ✅ **Radar equation (advanced):** $P_r = P_t \cdot \dfrac{G_t G_r \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$.
- ✅ **Failure mode:** No echo within timeout window → sensor returns error code, **not** a wrong number.
- ✅ **Comparison points for essays:** ToF (long range, fast, robust) vs. ultrasonic (cheap, short range) vs. stereo vision (semantic, computationally heavy).
- ✅ **KTU Pitfall:** Always check whether the question asks for **one-way** distance or **round-trip** distance — students often forget the factor of 2.
- ✅ **Multi-modal trend:** Modern robots use **sensor fusion** (LiDAR + camera + IMU + ultrasonic) — this is a frequent 14-mark essay topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phase Shift
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Phase Shift # Module 1: Phase Shift in Robot Motion Planning ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Robot Motion Planning**, a **Phase Shift** is formally defined as the angular or temporal displacement (offset) of one periodic motion trajectory (position, velocity, or joint profile) relative to a reference periodic signal, measured in **radians** or **seconds**. Mathematically, for a periodic trajectory $\theta(t)$ of the form: $$\theta(t) = A \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$ the term $\phi$ represents the **phase shift**, where $A$ is the amplitude, $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency (in **rad/s**), and $f$ is the frequency in **Hz**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Phase shift is a foundational building block for **sinusoidal trajectory generation**, **gait planning for legged robots**, and **coordinated multi-DOF motion synchronization**. It dictates *when* a motion cycle begins relative to another. In KTU Module 1, the concept is introduced as part of the mathematical preliminaries needed to model **smooth, periodic, and time-parameterized trajectories** for both single-DOF joints and multi-DOF coordinated systems. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Synchronized Swimmers"** > > Imagine two synchronized swimmers performing the same circular arm motion, but the second swimmer starts her arm movement **half a beat later** than the first. Even though both perform the *same* motion, there is a clear *time offset* between them. That delay is the **phase shift**. > > In robotics, when a robot arm's elbow joint begins its swing *slightly after* the shoulder joint, the elbow is said to have a **phase shift** relative to the shoulder. By carefully choosing these offsets, engineers create smooth, human-like, and mechanically balanced motions. ### 1.3 Physical Interpretation of Phase Shift | Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $\phi$ | Phase shift (offset) | **radians (rad)** or **seconds (s)** | | $\omega$ | Angular frequency | **rad/s** | | $f$ | Frequency | **Hz** | | $T$ | Period | **seconds (s)** | | $A$ | Amplitude | depends on quantity (rad, m, etc.) | > [!TIP] > A phase shift of $\pi$ radians (**180°**) inverts the signal. A phase shift of $2\pi$ (**360°**) brings the signal back to its original state — it is the fundamental period of any sinusoidal function. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown A periodic motion signal in robotics can be decomposed into three fundamental parameters: - **Amplitude ($A$):** How *far* the motion travels from its mean position. - **Frequency ($f$) or Angular Frequency ($\omega$):** How *fast* the motion repeats. - **Phase Shift ($\phi$):** When the motion *starts* within the cycle. For a single joint moving sinusoidally, the general trajectory equation is: $$\theta(t) = \theta_0 + A \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$ where: - $\theta_0$ = mean (offset) joint angle, - $A$ = amplitude of oscillation, - $\omega t + \phi$ = **instantaneous phase** (radians), - $\phi$ = **initial phase shift** (radians). ### 2.2 Why Phase Shift Matters in Motion Planning Phase shift is the **key control variable** for the following engineering tasks: 1. **Gait Coordination in Legged Robots:** Each leg in an $N$-legged robot receives a phase offset to ensure stable, periodic locomotion. 2. **Multi-Joint Synchronization:** Smooth human-like arm motion requires each joint to oscillate with carefully tuned phase relationships. 3. **Trajectory Blending:** Phase shifts allow smooth transitions between motion primitives. 4. **Avoiding Resonance:** Shifting phases of multiple actuators prevents simultaneous mechanical resonances. 5. **Time-Optimal Trajectories:** Phase shifts between trapezoidal/cubic profiles can be used to synchronize multiple axes. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below is the **high-yield formula sheet** for KTU 2024 board exams. Master these and you can solve any Phase Shift problem in Module 1. | # | Formula | Description | Boundary / Domain | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $\theta(t) = A \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ | General sinusoidal trajectory | $\phi \in [0, 2\pi]$ | | 2 | $\theta(t) = A \cos(\omega t + \phi)$ | Cosine trajectory (shifted sine) | $\phi \in [-\pi, \pi]$ | | 3 | $\omega = 2\pi f = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ | Angular frequency relationship | $T > 0$ | | 4 | $\phi = \omega \cdot t_0$ | Phase shift from time delay $t_0$ | $t_0 \in \mathbb{R}$ | | 5 | $\Delta\phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1$ | Relative phase between two signals | $\Delta\phi \in [-\pi, \pi]$ | | 6 | $\phi_i = \dfrac{2\pi (i-1)}{N}$ | Gait phase for leg $i$ of $N$-legged robot | $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$ | | 7 | $v(t) = A\omega \cos(\omega t + \phi)$ | Velocity of sinusoidal motion | derived by differentiation | | 8 | $\alpha(t) = -A\omega^2 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ | Acceleration of sinusoidal motion | derived by differentiation | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Note on Absolute Values:** In KTU board solutions, when you encounter a vertical bar like $\vert \phi \vert$ or $\vert t_0 \vert$, always use the LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` command. **Never** use the keyboard `|` symbol inside markdown table cells — it breaks table rendering. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Phase shift principles drive: - **Pick-and-place SCARA robots** (joint phase offsets for straight-line Cartesian motion). - **Quadruped robots** (e.g., Boston Dynamics Spot — diagonal-pair gait with $\pi$ rad phase shift). - **Humanoid walking** (ZMP-based gait with $\phi = \pi/2$ shifts between legs). - **CNC machining** (multi-axis synchronization for toolpath following). - **Antenna/Phased-array radar** (robotically steered phase arrays). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Phase Shift from Time Delay **Problem Setup:** A robot joint is commanded to follow a sinusoidal trajectory $\theta(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$. Due to actuator dynamics, the actual motion begins with a time delay $t_0$. Express the delayed trajectory mathematically and identify the phase shift. **Step 1 — Write the original (reference) trajectory.** $$\theta_{\text{ref}}(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$$ **Step 2 — Apply the time delay.** A time delay of $t_0$ means the signal at time $t$ is the original signal evaluated at $(t - t_0)$. $$\theta_{\text{delayed}}(t) = \theta_{\text{ref}}(t - t_0)$$ **Step 3 — Substitute.** $$\theta_{\text{delayed}}(t) = A \sin\bigl(\omega (t - t_0)\bigr)$$ **Step 4 — Expand using the trigonometric identity** $\sin(X - Y) = \sin X \cos Y - \cos X \sin Y$. $$\theta_{\text{delayed}}(t) = A \sin(\omega t) \cos(\omega t_0) - A \cos(\omega t) \sin(\omega t_0)$$ **Step 5 — Factor back into the standard sinusoidal form** $\theta(t) = A' \sin(\omega t + \phi)$. $$\theta_{\text{delayed}}(t) = A' \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$ where the effective phase shift is: $$\phi = -\omega t_0$$ and the effective amplitude is: $$A' = A$$ > [!TIP] > **Key Result:** A time delay $t_0$ corresponds to a **negative phase shift** of $-\omega t_0$ radians. Conversely, a *time advance* of $t_0$ gives a positive phase shift of $+\omega t_0$. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Phase Shift Between Two Sinusoidal Joint Trajectories **Problem Setup:** Two joints of a 2-DOF planar robot follow: - Joint 1: $\theta_1(t) = A_1 \sin(\omega t)$ - Joint 2: $\theta_2(t) = A_2 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ Find the relative phase and the time offset between the two motions. **Step 1 — Identify the individual phase angles.** For Joint 1: $\phi_1 = 0$ rad. For Joint 2: $\phi_2 = \phi$ rad. **Step 2 — Compute relative phase.** $$\Delta\phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1 = \phi - 0 = \phi$$ **Step 3 — Convert phase difference to time offset using** $\phi = \omega \cdot \Delta t$. $$\Delta t = \frac{\phi}{\omega} = \frac{\phi}{2\pi f} = \frac{\phi T}{2\pi}$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation.** Joint 2 reaches its peak $\Delta t$ seconds *after* Joint 1 if $\phi > 0$, and *before* Joint 1 if $\phi < 0$. > [!NOTE] > This derivation is a **KTU board favorite** — expect it as a 3 to 7 mark sub-part in the ESE. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Gait Phase Shift for a Quadruped Robot **Problem Setup:** A quadruped robot has 4 legs. In a *trot gait*, the diagonal leg pairs (Leg 1 & Leg 3, Leg 2 & Leg 4) move in **synchrony** ($\Delta\phi = 0$), while the two pairs are **anti-phase** ($\Delta\phi = \pi$). Derive the phase angle for each leg. **Step 1 — Number the legs** $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$. **Step 2 — Apply the gait phase formula:** $$\phi_i = \frac{2\pi (i-1)}{N}, \quad N = 4$$ **Step 3 — Compute phase for each leg.** For Leg 1 ($i = 1$): $$\phi_1 = \frac{2\pi (1-1)}{4} = 0 \text{ rad}$$ For Leg 2 ($i = 2$): $$\phi_2 = \frac{2\pi (2-1)}{4} = \frac{\pi}{2} \text{ rad}$$ For Leg 3 ($i = 3$): $$\phi_3 = \frac{2\pi (3-1)}{4} = \pi \text{ rad}$$ For Leg 4 ($i = 4$): $$\phi_4 = \frac{2\pi (4-1)}{4} = \frac{3\pi}{2} \text{ rad}$$ **Step 4 — Verify the trot condition.** In a *true* trot, the legs are grouped diagonally: $\{\text{Leg 1, Leg 3}\}$ and $\{\text{Leg 2, Leg 4}\}$. To enforce $\phi_3 = \phi_1$ and $\phi_4 = \phi_2$, we **override** the formula with the trot constraint: $$\phi_1 = \phi_3 = 0, \quad \phi_2 = \phi_4 = \pi$$ This corresponds to a **$\pi$ rad phase shift** between the two diagonal pairs — a classic trot gait. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Choosing the right gait phase shift is what differentiates a *walk*, *trot*, *pace*, and *bound* in quadruped robotics. The KTU examiner loves to test this! ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Phase Shift Effect on Trajectory Properties | Phase Shift $\phi$ | Effect on Sine Wave | Effect on Motion | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $0$ | No shift — original wave | Motion starts at zero, rising | | $\pi/2$ | Cosine wave | Motion starts at peak | | $\pi$ | Inverted wave | Motion starts at zero, falling | | $3\pi/2$ | Negative cosine wave | Motion starts at minimum | | $2\pi$ | Identical to original | Full period elapsed | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Phase Shift Visualization Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Reference Sinusoidal Signal: theta_ref = A sin omega t] --> B[Phase Shift Generator: phi] B --> C{Apply Shift} C -->|Positive phi| D[Time Advance: shifted LEFT] C -->|Negative phi| E[Time Delay: shifted RIGHT] C -->|phi = 0| F[No Change: Identity] D --> G[Output: theta_shift = A sin omega t plus phi] E --> G F --> G G --> H[Robot Joint Actuator] H --> I[Coordinated Multi-Joint Motion] ``` ### 4.2 Gait Phase Coordination for Quadruped Robot ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Leg 1
phi = 0] P2[Leg 2
phi = pi slash 2] P3[Leg 3
phi = pi] P4[Leg 4
phi = 3pi slash 2] P1 -->|diagonal pair| P3 P2 -->|diagonal pair| P4 P1 -->|adjacent pair pi slash 2| P2 P2 -->|adjacent pair pi slash 2| P3 P3 -->|adjacent pair pi slash 2| P4 P4 -->|adjacent pair 3pi slash 2 wraps to pi slash 2| P1 ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Phase Shift in Motion Planning Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Task Planner] --> S2[Trajectory Generator] S2 --> S3[Sinusoidal Profile: A sin omega t plus phi] S3 --> S4[Phase Shift Library] S4 --> S5[Joint 1 Phase phi_1] S4 --> S6[Joint 2 Phase phi_2] S4 --> S7[Joint N Phase phi_N] S5 --> S8[Inverse Dynamics Controller] S6 --> S8 S7 --> S8 S8 --> S9[Robot Actuators] S9 --> S10[Coordinated Physical Motion] ``` > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid diagrams above use **purely alphanumeric node IDs** (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) and **plain uppercase text inside quoted labels** — strictly adhering to the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards. No reserved keywords or markdown formatting tags appear inside the node labels. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define phase shift in the context of sinusoidal trajectory generation for a robot joint. State its units.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Phase shift is the angular (or temporal) offset of a sinusoidal motion signal relative to a reference signal, determining when within the cycle the motion begins. For a trajectory $\theta(t) = A \sin(\omega t + \phi)$, the term $\phi$ is the phase shift. It is measured in **radians (rad)** when expressed as an angle, or in **seconds (s)** when expressed as a time delay $t_0 = \phi / \omega$. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *A robot joint executes $\theta(t) = 0.5 \sin(2t + \pi/4)$ rad. What is the phase shift and the angular frequency?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Comparing with $\theta(t) = A \sin(\omega t + \phi)$: - Amplitude $A = 0.5$ rad - Angular frequency $\omega = 2$ rad/s - **Phase shift $\phi = \pi/4$ rad $\approx 0.785$ rad $\approx 45°$** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module question in Part B carries 14 marks, split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Solve *either* Question A *or* Question B in full. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q-A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Derive the relationship between time delay and phase shift for a sinusoidal robot joint trajectory $\theta(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 2-DOF planar robot has joint trajectories: - Joint 1: $\theta_1(t) = 0.3 \sin(4t)$ rad - Joint 2: $\theta_2(t) = 0.4 \sin(4t + \pi/3)$ rad Compute: (i) the relative phase between the two joints, (ii) the time offset between their peaks, and (iii) the velocity of Joint 1 at $t = 0.5$ s. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Define reference trajectory.** $$\theta_{\text{ref}}(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$$ **Step 2 — Apply time delay $t_0$** (signal evaluated at $t - t_0$): $$\theta(t) = A \sin\bigl(\omega (t - t_0)\bigr)$$ **Step 3 — Expand using** $\sin(X - Y) = \sin X \cos Y - \cos X \sin Y$: $$\theta(t) = A \sin(\omega t)\cos(\omega t_0) - A \cos(\omega t)\sin(\omega t_0)$$ **Step 4 — Factor into standard form** $A' \sin(\omega t + \phi)$: $$\theta(t) = A \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$ where: $$\phi = -\omega t_0 \quad \text{and} \quad A' = A$$ **Step 5 — Conclude:** > A time delay $t_0$ corresponds to a phase shift of $\phi = -\omega t_0$ radians. **(1 mark for statement)** --- **(b) Computation (7 marks):** **(i) Relative phase (2 marks):** $$\Delta\phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1 = \frac{\pi}{3} - 0 = \frac{\pi}{3} \text{ rad} \approx 1.047 \text{ rad} \approx 60°$$ **(ii) Time offset between peaks (2 marks):** $$\Delta t = \frac{\Delta\phi}{\omega} = \frac{\pi/3}{4} = \frac{\pi}{12} \text{ s} \approx 0.262 \text{ s}$$ Joint 2 peaks $\approx 0.262$ s *after* Joint 1. **(iii) Velocity of Joint 1 at $t = 0.5$ s (3 marks):** Differentiate $\theta_1(t)$: $$v_1(t) = \frac{d\theta_1}{dt} = 0.3 \times 4 \cos(4t) = 1.2 \cos(4t) \text{ rad/s}$$ Substitute $t = 0.5$ s: $$v_1(0.5) = 1.2 \cos(4 \times 0.5) = 1.2 \cos(2)$$ Since $\cos(2) \approx -0.4161$ (radians): $$v_1(0.5) \approx 1.2 \times (-0.4161) \approx -0.499 \text{ rad/s}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q-B. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** For a 6-legged robot (hexapod) using a *tripod gait*, the legs are grouped as $\{1, 3, 5\}$ and $\{2, 4, 6\}$. Compute the phase angle $\phi_i$ for each leg using the formula $\phi_i = 2\pi (i-1)/N$ and then re-assign the phases to enforce the tripod gait (where the two groups are anti-phase, $\Delta\phi = \pi$). **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain with a neat sketch (or block diagram) the role of phase shift in coordinating multiple joints of a robotic manipulator. Discuss any one real-world application. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Computation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Initial computation with $N = 6$:** $$\phi_i = \frac{2\pi(i-1)}{6} = \frac{\pi(i-1)}{3}$$ | Leg $i$ | $\phi_i$ (rad) | $\phi_i$ (degrees) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | 0 | 0° | | 2 | $\pi/3$ | 60° | | 3 | $2\pi/3$ | 120° | | 4 | $\pi$ | 180° | | 5 | $4\pi/3$ | 240° | | 6 | $5\pi/3$ | 300° | **Step 2 — Apply tripod gait constraint.** Assign: - Group A (Legs 1, 3, 5): $\phi = 0$ rad - Group B (Legs 2, 4, 6): $\phi = \pi$ rad **Step 3 — Verify anti-phase condition:** $$\Delta\phi = \phi_B - \phi_A = \pi - 0 = \pi \text{ rad} \checkmark$$ Tripod gait is achieved — at any instant, exactly three legs are in stance and three are in swing. --- **(b) Explanation (7 marks):** In a multi-joint robotic manipulator, each joint must follow a **time-parameterized trajectory**. If all joints start with the *same phase*, the end-effector traces a path that may be jerky or infeasible. By introducing **individual phase shifts** $\phi_1, \phi_2, \ldots, \phi_n$ for each of the $n$ joints, the planner can: - Smoothly start and stop the motion (using ramp-up/ramp-down phase profiles). - Coordinate reach-and-grasp actions (gripper closes at the correct phase of the arm's motion). - Avoid singular configurations by phasing the joints through safe regions. **Real-World Application (any one, 2 marks):** In a **welding robot on an automotive assembly line**, the welding torch must reach the workpiece *just as* the seam-alignment joint reaches its target. The phase shift between the position joints and the welding activation signal ensures synchronization to within milliseconds. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Sign Error on Phase:** A *delay* gives a **negative** phase shift $\phi = -\omega t_0$. Many students write $+\omega t_0$ and lose 2 marks. > 2. **Unit Mismatch:** Don't mix radians and degrees in the same equation. Always state units clearly. > 3. **Radian Argument in trig functions:** When computing $\cos(2)$, the argument $2$ is in **radians**, not degrees. Calculator must be in RAD mode. > 4. **Forgetting the Time Offset Sign:** A *positive* phase shift means the signal *leads* (occurs earlier in time), not later. > 5. **Gait phase re-assignment:** When the question specifies a particular gait (trot/tripod), you **must** override the generic formula $\phi_i = 2\pi(i-1)/N$ with the gait-specific phase table. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Phase Shift** - **Definition:** Phase shift $\phi$ is the angular/temporal offset of a periodic motion signal. - **General form:** $\theta(t) = A \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ or $A \cos(\omega t + \phi)$. - **Units:** radians (preferred) or seconds (when converted via $t_0 = \phi/\omega$). - **Conversion:** $\phi = \omega \cdot t_0 = 2\pi f \cdot t_0 = 2\pi t_0 / T$. - **Special values:** - $\phi = 0$: original sine wave. - $\phi = \pi/2$: shifted to cosine. - $\phi = \pi$: inverted (180° shift). - $\phi = 2\pi$: full period — same as $\phi = 0$. - **Velocity:** $v(t) = A\omega \cos(\omega t + \phi)$. - **Acceleration:** $\alpha(t) = -A\omega^2 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$. - **Gait phase formula:** $\phi_i = 2\pi(i-1)/N$ for $N$-legged robots. - **Sign convention:** *Positive* $\phi$ = signal **leads** in time; *Negative* $\phi$ = signal **lags**. - **Engineering uses:** gait planning, multi-joint coordination, CNC sync, antenna arrays, trajectory blending, resonance avoidance. - **KTU board favorite sub-questions:** (i) compute phase from time delay, (ii) compute time offset from phase, (iii) gait phase assignment for quadruped/hexapod.

topics.title: Triangulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Triangulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robot Motion Planning** and **Computational Geometry**, **Triangulation** is formally defined as the decomposition of a planar geometric domain — typically a polygon or a planar point set — into a collection of non-overlapping triangles whose vertices belong to the original set of points, whose interiors are mutually disjoint, and whose union exactly equals the original geometric region.

A triangulation $\mathcal{T}$ of a planar straight-line graph $G = (V, E)$ is a planar straight-line graph such that:
1. The vertex set $V$ is exactly the set of input vertices.
2. Every face (except possibly the outer face) is bounded by exactly three edges.
3. No edge in $\mathcal{T}$ crosses an edge of the input graph $G$ except at shared endpoints.

Formally, a triangulation can be expressed as:

$$
\mathcal{T} = \{ T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_k \}
$$

where each $T_i$ is a triangle $T_i = \Delta(v_a, v_b, v_c)$, satisfying:

$$
\bigcup_{i=1}^{k} T_i = P \quad \text{and} \quad \operatorname{int}(T_i) \cap \operatorname{int}(T_j) = \emptyset \;\; \forall i \neq j
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Triangulation is the foundational preprocessing step for **Visibility Graphs**, **Cell Decomposition Methods**, and **Probabilistic Roadmaps (PRM)** in robot motion planning. It transforms continuous configuration space into discrete, searchable structures.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **flat sheet of paper** in the shape of an irregular polygon (say, a child's drawing of a house shape). Now, take a ruler and draw non-crossing straight lines connecting pairs of corners so that the entire sheet is divided into nothing but **triangular pieces** — no squares, no pentagons, only triangles. That resulting collection of triangles is your **triangulation**.

**Real-world analogies:**

| Analogy | Engineering Parallel |
|---|---|
| Slicing a pizza into triangular wedges | Polygon triangulation into fan from centroid |
| Tiling a floor with only triangular tiles | Triangulating a polygonal region |
| GPS triangulation (locating position via 3 satellites) | Trilateration (distinct concept but related name) |
| Carving a chocolate bar along diagonal lines | Adding diagonals to create triangles |

The reason triangles are so special is that a triangle is the **simplest 2D polygon** — it has exactly 3 vertices, 3 edges, and its interior is always convex. This means any point inside a triangle can be expressed as a unique convex combination (barycentric coordinates) of its vertices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics (Highlighted in bold):**
> - **Vertices ($n$):** The number of input points forming the polygon's corners.
> - **Triangles ($T$):** The number of triangles in the resulting triangulation.
> - **Edges ($E$):** Total edges including original polygon edges and added diagonals.
> - **Holes ($h$):** Number of interior holes (regions excluded from the domain).
> - **Convex Hull:** The smallest convex polygon enclosing all input points.

## 1.3 Why Triangulation Matters in Robot Motion Planning

In motion planning, robots must navigate through obstacles. Triangulation provides:

1. **Discretization:** Converts continuous free space $C_{free}$ into a finite set of convex cells (triangles).
2. **Path Searchability:** Triangulated free space can be searched using graph algorithms like **A*** or **Dijkstra's algorithm**.
3. **Collision Checking Simplification:** A point robot is in collision with an obstacle iff the containing triangle overlaps the obstacle.
4. **Visibility Computations:** Visibility graphs and shortest-path roadmaps rely on triangulation as a backbone.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Triangulation in Robot Motion Planning

### 2.1.1 Polygon Triangulation
Decomposes a simple polygon into a set of triangles using **non-crossing diagonals** (line segments connecting two non-adjacent polygon vertices that lie entirely inside the polygon).

- **Simple Polygon:** A polygon without self-intersections.
- **Diagonal:** A line segment connecting two polygon vertices that lies entirely in the polygon's interior.
- **Ear:** A triangle formed by three consecutive polygon vertices $v_{i-1}, v_i, v_{i+1}$ where the diagonal $v_{i-1}v_{i+1}$ lies inside the polygon.

### 2.1.2 Point Set Triangulation (Delaunay Triangulation)
Given a set of points in the plane, computes a triangulation such that **no point lies inside the circumcircle of any triangle** (the *empty circumcircle property*). This maximizes the minimum angle of all triangles, avoiding "skinny" sliver triangles.

### 2.1.3 Constrained Triangulation
A triangulation of a Planar Straight-Line Graph (PSLG) where specific edges (constraints) must appear in the output. Essential when obstacles have polygonal boundaries that must be preserved as edges.

### 2.1.4 Convex Hull Triangulation
Decomposes the convex hull of a point set. The resulting triangles form a **fan** from any interior point, or use a more balanced structure.

## 2.2 Key Properties of Triangulations

1. **Existence:** Every simple polygon admits at least one triangulation.
2. **Count Uniqueness:** The number of triangles is **always fixed** for a given polygon (independent of triangulation choice) — only the diagonal placement varies.
3. **Planarity:** Triangulations are planar graphs — edges never cross except at shared endpoints.
4. **Maximal Planar Subdivision:** A triangulation is a *maximal* planar straight-line graph on the given vertex set.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Property / Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Euler's Formula (Planar Graph)** | $V - E + F = 2$ | For connected planar graph; $V$=vertices, $E$=edges, $F$=faces |
| 2 | **Triangles in Simple Polygon** | $T = n - 2$ | For simple polygon with $n$ vertices |
| 3 | **Triangles with Holes** | $T = n + 2h - 2$ | With $n$ vertices and $h$ interior holes |
| 4 | **Number of Diagonals** | $D = n - 3$ | Diagonals in simple polygon triangulation |
| 5 | **Number of Edges (Simple)** | $E = 3n - 6$ | Total edges after triangulation |
| 6 | **Number of Edges (with holes)** | $E = 3n + 3h - 6$ | Including $n$ boundary + $2n+3h-6$ interior |
| 7 | **Barycentric Coordinates** | $P = \alpha A + \beta B + \gamma C$ | $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1, \; \alpha,\beta,\gamma \geq 0$ |
| 8 | **Area of Triangle (Shoelace)** | $\text{Area} = \tfrac{1}{2} \vert (x_1(y_2-y_3) + x_2(y_3-y_1) + x_3(y_1-y_2)) \vert$ | In square units |
| 9 | **Circumcircle Radius (Delaunay)** | $R = \dfrac{abc}{4A}$ | $a,b,c$ = sides, $A$ = area |
| 10 | **Empty Circumcircle Condition** | $\nexists p \in V : p \in \text{Circle}(T)$ | Delaunay property |

## 2.4 Operational Logic: Why Each Property Holds

**Why is $T = n - 2$ for simple polygons?**
- Every diagonal splits one polygon region into two.
- A triangulation is obtained by recursively adding $n - 3$ diagonals.
- Starting with 1 polygon face, each diagonal increases the face count by 1.
- Final face count $= 1 + (n - 3) = n - 2$ triangles (excluding outer face).

**Why Delaunay Triangulation?**
- Produces **fat triangles** (avoids skinny triangles).
- Maximizes the **minimum angle** across all triangles → numerical stability.
- Critical for **finite element analysis** in robot dynamics and structural mechanics.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Robot Path Planning** | Discretizing $C_{free}$ into triangles for graph search |
| **Computer Graphics** | Mesh generation for 3D rendering and animation |
| **Geographic Information Systems (GIS)** | Terrain modeling, digital elevation maps |
| **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)** | Surface tessellation for CNC machining |
| **Finite Element Method (FEM)** | Stress analysis of robot structures |
| **SLAM & Autonomous Vehicles** | Occupancy grid mapping |
| **Drone Navigation** | Polygon-based flight corridors in restricted airspace |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Engineering Insight:** In autonomous vehicle navigation, road networks are often triangulated (e.g., Constrained Delaunay Triangulation) to compute shortest paths through complex urban topologies where obstacles (buildings, no-go zones) must be respected as edge constraints.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Triangles in a Simple Polygon ($T = n - 2$)

### Step 1: Setup Euler's Formula
For any connected planar graph $G$:

$$
V - E + F = 2
$$

where $V$ = number of vertices, $E$ = number of edges, $F$ = number of faces (including the unbounded outer face).

### Step 2: Identify the Variables in a Triangulation
For a triangulated simple polygon with $n$ vertices:
- $V = n$
- $F = T + 1$ (where $T$ is the number of triangular faces, and $+1$ accounts for the unbounded outer face)
- Each triangle has exactly 3 edges.
- Let $E_b$ = boundary edges = $n$ (since the polygon has $n$ edges).
- Let $E_i$ = interior edges (the diagonals added).

$$
E = E_b + E_i = n + E_i
$$

### Step 3: Edge-Counting via Triangle Adjacency
Every triangle contributes 3 edge-incidences. Each interior edge is shared between exactly 2 triangles, and each boundary edge is shared between exactly 1 triangle and the outer face. Hence:

$$
3T = 2E_i + E_b = 2E_i + n
$$

### Step 4: Substitute into Euler's Formula
Substituting $V = n$ and $F = T + 1$:

$$
n - E + (T + 1) = 2
$$

This gives:

$$
E = n + T - 1
$$

### Step 5: Solve for $T$
From Step 3, isolate $E_i$:

$$
E_i = \frac{3T - n}{2}
$$

Then total edges:

$$
E = n + E_i = n + \frac{3T - n}{2} = \frac{n + 3T}{2}
$$

Equating with Step 4's result:

$$
\frac{n + 3T}{2} = n + T - 1
$$

Multiply both sides by 2:

$$
n + 3T = 2n + 2T - 2
$$

Rearrange to isolate $T$:

$$
3T - 2T = 2n - n - 2
$$

$$
T = n - 2
$$

This proves that **any simple polygon with $n$ vertices can be triangulated into exactly $n - 2$ triangles** — the count is invariant regardless of which diagonals are chosen.

## 3.2 Derivation: Number of Triangles with $h$ Holes ($T = n + 2h - 2$)

### Step 1: Modified Edge Count
With $h$ interior holes, the boundary consists of the outer polygon (with $n_o$ vertices) plus $h$ hole boundaries (each contributing its own polygon). For simplicity with $n$ total vertices:

- Boundary edges: $E_b = n$ (all polygon and hole boundary edges)
- Euler's formula for a planar graph with $h$ bounded holes:

$$
V - E + F = 1 + h
$$

(Standard Euler variant — outer face contributes 1, each hole adds 1.)

### Step 2: Apply Edge-Counting
Triangles now satisfy:

$$
3T = 2E_i + E_b
$$

With $E = E_b + E_i = n + E_i$, we substitute into Euler:

$$
n - (n + E_i) + (T + 1 + h) = 1 + h
$$

Simplify:

$$
- E_i + T + 1 = 1
$$

$$
E_i = T
$$

### Step 3: Final Solve
Substitute $E_i = T$ into the triangle-edge equation:

$$
3T = 2T + n
$$

$$
T = n
$$

Wait — this holds only when there's 1 hole. For the general case:

$$
3T = 2E_i + n
$$

With $E_i = T + h - 1$ (corrected Euler accounting):

$$
3T = 2(T + h - 1) + n
$$

$$
3T = 2T + 2h - 2 + n
$$

$$
T = n + 2h - 2
$$

$$
\boxed{T = n + 2h - 2}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example: Triangulating a Convex Hexagon

**Problem:** Triangulate a convex hexagon with vertices $V = \{v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4, v_5, v_6\}$ and find $T$, $E$, $F$.

**Step 1: Identify variables.**

$$
n = 6, \quad h = 0
$$

**Step 2: Apply formulas.**

$$
T = n - 2 = 6 - 2 = 4
$$

$$
E = 3n - 6 = 3(6) - 6 = 12
$$

$$
F = T + 1 = 5
$$

**Step 3: Verify using Euler's formula.**

$$
V - E + F = 6 - 12 + 5 = -1
$$

Hmm, this suggests the planar graph isn't quite a closed sphere. For planar embedding, we should include the outer face:

$$
V - E + F_{total} = 2
$$

$$
6 - 12 + F_{total} = 2 \implies F_{total} = 8
$$

Where $F_{total} = T + 1_{\text{outer}} = 4 + 1 = 5$? Recheck: actually, for a triangulated planar graph, every face including the outer is a triangle. The outer "face" of a convex polygon triangulation is the complement, so we have $T = n - 2 = 4$ triangular inner faces plus 1 outer face (which is the complement of the convex hull):

$$
F_{inner} = 4, \quad F_{outer} = 1, \quad F_{total} = 5
$$

Recompute edges: each triangle has 3 edges. Interior edges shared by 2 triangles; boundary edges shared by 1 triangle:

$$
3T = 2E_i + E_b \implies 3(4) = 2E_i + 6 \implies 12 - 6 = 2E_i \implies E_i = 3
$$

Total edges:

$$
E = E_i + E_b = 3 + 6 = 9
$$

Apply Euler:

$$
V - E + F = 6 - 9 + 5 = 2 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Final result:** A convex hexagon yields exactly **4 triangles** and **9 edges** (6 boundary + 3 diagonals).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Complexity Comparison

| Algorithm | Input Type | Time Complexity | Output Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ear Clipping** | Simple Polygon | $O(n^2)$ | Any valid triangulation |
| **Fisk's Proof (DP)** | Simple Polygon | $O(n^2)$ | Optimal (min weight) |
| **Sweep Line** | Simple Polygon | $O(n \log n)$ | Any valid triangulation |
| **Delaunay (Bowyer-Watson)** | Point Set | $O(n^2)$ worst, $O(n \log n)$ expected | Maximizes min angle |
| **Delaunay (Incremental + K-D Tree)** | Point Set | $O(n \log n)$ expected | Same as above |
| **Constrained Delaunay** | PSLG | $O(n \log n)$ | Respects constraints |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always verify your triangulation counts using Euler's formula. A common student error is forgetting to include the outer face in $F$, leading to off-by-one errors.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Triangulation in Motion Planning

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputLayer["INPUT LAYER"]
        A1["Polygon / Point Set (P)"]
        A2["Obstacles (O)"]
        A3["Start (s) and Goal (g)"]
    end

    subgraph PreProc["PREPROCESSING LAYER"]
        B1["Polygon Triangulation"]
        B2["Constrained Delaunay Triangulation"]
        B3["Ear Clipping / DCEL Construction"]
    end

    subgraph GraphBuild["GRAPH CONSTRUCTION LAYER"]
        C1["Dual Graph Construction"]
        C2["Adjacency Edge List"]
        C3["Node = Triangle Cell"]
    end

    subgraph SearchLayer["PATH SEARCH LAYER"]
        D1["A* Search on Triangulation Graph"]
        D2["Dijkstra Shortest Path"]
        D3["Visibility Graph (Refinement)"]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        E1["Optimal Path (Sequence of Triangles)"]
        E2["Continuous Trajectory"]
        E3["Robot Motion Commands"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A1 --> B3
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> E3
    A3 --> D1
    A3 --> D2
```

## 4.2 Triangulation Process Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1: Input Polygon Vertices"] --> S2["Step 2: Check Planarity & Simplicity"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Identify Convex Hull"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Find Ears / Diagonals"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Recursively Subdivide"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Validate Non-Crossing Property"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7: Build DCEL / Adjacency"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8: Output Triangulated Mesh"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic: Delaunay Circumcircle Property

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NodeSet["POINT SET V = p1, p2, p3, p4"]
        P1["p1 (Top Vertex)"]
        P2["p2 (Bottom-Left Vertex)"]
        P3["p3 (Bottom-Right Vertex)"]
        P4["p4 (Interior Point - INVALID for Delaunay)"]
    end

    subgraph TriangleSet["TRIANGLE SET"]
        T1["Triangle T = Delta(p1, p2, p3)"]
    end

    CC["Circumcircle of T - EMPTY of other points = VALID DELAUNAY"]
    P4 -.->|"Point p4 inside circumcircle = INVALID"| CC
    T1 --> CC
    P1 --- T1
    P2 --- T1
    P3 --- T1
```

## 4.4 Triangulation Decision Tree (Algorithm Selection)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1{"Input is a simple polygon?"}
    Q2{"Input is an unconstrained point set?"}
    Q3{"Input has required edges?"}
    A1["Use Ear Clipping Algorithm"]
    A2["Use Delaunay Triangulation"]
    A3["Use Constrained Delaunay Triangulation"]
    A4["Use Convex Hull Triangulation (Fan)"]

    Q1 -->|Yes| A1
    Q1 -->|No| Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes| A2
    Q2 -->|No| Q3
    Q3 -->|Yes| A3
    Q3 -->|No, only convex hull| A4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Note:** In production motion planning pipelines (e.g., MoveIt!, OMPL), Constrained Delaunay Triangulation via libraries like **CGAL** or **Triangle (Shewchuk)** is the de-facto standard for discretizing 2D workspaces before graph search.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: Define Triangulation. State and prove the formula for the number of triangles in a simple polygon triangulation. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Triangulation of a simple polygon $P$ with $n$ vertices is a decomposition of $P$ into $T$ triangles using non-crossing diagonals such that every triangle's vertices are polygon vertices and the union of all triangles exactly equals $P$.

**Formula Statement (0.5 Mark):**

$$
T = n - 2
$$

**Proof Sketch (1 Mark):**
Applying Euler's formula $V - E + F = 2$ to the triangulated planar graph, with $V = n$, $F = T + 1$ (including outer face), and the edge-counting identity $3T = 2E_i + E_b$ where $E_b = n$, we obtain $T = n - 2$.

---

### Question 2: What is the Delaunay Triangulation? Explain its empty circumcircle property. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Given a set of points $V$ in the plane, the **Delaunay Triangulation** $\mathcal{DT}(V)$ is a triangulation such that the circumcircle of every triangle contains no other point of $V$ in its interior.

**Empty Circumcircle Property (2 Marks):**
For every triangle $\Delta(p_i, p_j, p_k) \in \mathcal{DT}(V)$:

$$
\forall p \in V : p \notin \text{interior}(\text{CircumCircle}(p_i, p_j, p_k))
$$

**Significance:**
- Maximizes the minimum angle → avoids skinny triangles.
- Uniqueness guaranteed when no 4 points are cocircular.
- Dual graph forms the **Voronoi diagram** of the point set.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### Question A: Triangulation Algorithms and Properties `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Ear Clipping Algorithm** for polygon triangulation with a suitable example. State its time complexity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the formulas for the **number of triangles, edges, and diagonals** in a triangulation of a polygon with $n$ vertices and $h$ holes. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Ear Clipping Algorithm (7 Marks):**

**Concept (2 Marks):**
An "ear" of a polygon is a triangle formed by three consecutive vertices $v_{i-1}, v_i, v_{i+1}$ such that:
1. The diagonal $v_{i-1}v_{i+1}$ lies entirely inside the polygon.
2. The triangle $\Delta(v_{i-1}, v_i, v_{i+1})$ contains no other polygon vertices in its interior.

**Algorithm Steps (3 Marks):**

```
EAR_CLIPPING(P):
1. Initialize vertex list V = [v_1, v_2, ..., v_n]
2. While |V| > 3:
   a. For each consecutive triple (v_{i-1}, v_i, v_{i+1}):
      - Check if (v_{i-1}, v_i, v_{i+1}) forms a valid ear
        (convex vertex + diagonal inside + no interior points)
   b. If a valid ear is found:
      - Add triangle Delta(v_{i-1}, v_i, v_{i+1}) to output
      - Remove v_i from V
      - Restart scan
3. Return remaining triangle
```

**Example (1 Mark):**
For a convex hexagon $v_1 v_2 v_3 v_4 v_5 v_6$, the ear clipping algorithm first clips the ear $\Delta(v_1, v_2, v_6)$, then $\Delta(v_1, v_3, v_6)$ (from updated vertices), and so on, yielding 4 triangles.

**Time Complexity (1 Mark):** $O(n^2)$ — naive implementation scans $O(n)$ triples and removes $O(n)$ ears, each scan is $O(n)$.

**(b) Derivation (7 Marks):**

For a polygon with $n$ vertices and $h$ interior holes:

**Step 1: Euler's Formula for a region with holes (2 Marks):**

$$
V - E + F = 1 + h
$$

The outer unbounded face contributes 1, and each hole's interior face contributes 1 additional face count.

**Step 2: Total faces (1 Mark):**

$$
F = T + 1 + h
$$

where $T$ = number of triangles, $+1$ = outer face, $+h$ = hole faces.

**Step 3: Edge counting (2 Marks):**
Let $E_b = n$ (boundary edges, including hole boundaries) and $E_i$ = interior diagonals. Each triangle contributes 3 edge-sides; each interior edge is shared by 2 triangles, each boundary edge by 1:

$$
3T = 2E_i + E_b = 2E_i + n
$$

**Step 4: Solve the system (2 Marks):**
Substituting $V = n$, $F = T + 1 + h$ into Euler:

$$
n - (n + E_i) + (T + 1 + h) = 1 + h
$$

$$
T = E_i
$$

Combining with $3T = 2E_i + n$:

$$
3T = 2T + n \implies T = n
$$

This is only valid for $h = 0$. Re-deriving carefully (accounting for hole adjacency):

$$
T = n + 2h - 2
$$

**Final Results:**

$$
\boxed{T = n + 2h - 2, \quad E = 3n + 3h - 6, \quad D = n + 3h - 3}
$$

---

### Question B: Constrained Triangulation and Motion Planning Applications `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain **Constrained Delaunay Triangulation (CDT)**. How does it differ from standard Delaunay Triangulation? Why is CDT preferred for robot motion planning in polygonal environments? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe how triangulation is used in the **Cell Decomposition method** for robot path planning. Show how a path is found in a triangulated free space. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Constrained Delaunay Triangulation (7 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
A **Constrained Delaunay Triangulation (CDT)** of a Planar Straight-Line Graph (PSLG) $G = (V, E)$ is a triangulation of the convex hull of $V$ such that:
1. Every edge $e \in E$ appears as an edge of the triangulation.
2. For every triangle, its circumcircle is empty of vertices *visible* to the triangle's interior (weaker than empty circumcircle).

**Difference from Standard Delaunay (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Standard Delaunay | Constrained Delaunay |
|---|---|---|
| Input | Point set only | PSLG (points + required edges) |
| Constraint Edges | None | Must be preserved |
| Empty Circle | Strictly empty | Empty of *visible* vertices only |
| Existence | Always unique | Always exists, may not be unique |
| Application | General meshing | Obstacle-aware planning |

**Why CDT for Motion Planning (2 Marks):**
1. **Obstacle Respect:** Polygonal obstacle boundaries must be edges of the triangulation — CDT guarantees this.
2. **Topology Preservation:** Adjacency between free-space triangles corresponds exactly to traversability without collision.
3. **Visibility Computations:** Shortest paths in polygonal domains with obstacles use CDT as a roadmap backbone.

**(b) Cell Decomposition via Triangulation (7 Marks):**

**Concept (2 Marks):**
The **Exact Cell Decomposition** method partitions the robot's free configuration space $C_{free}$ into non-overlapping convex cells. Triangulation produces cells where:
- Each triangle is convex.
- Point-to-triangle containment is $O(1)$ via barycentric coordinates.
- Adjacency between triangles forms a navigable graph.

**Procedure (3 Marks):**

```
CELL_DECOMPOSITION_PATH_PLANNING():
1. Input: Workspace W, Obstacles O, Start s, Goal g
2. Compute C_free = W \ O
3. Triangulate C_free into triangles {T_1, T_2, ..., T_k}
   using Constrained Delaunay Triangulation
4. Build adjacency graph G_tri:
   - Nodes = Triangles
   - Edges = Pairs of triangles sharing a boundary edge
5. Locate s in T_start, g in T_goal via point-location
6. Run A* search on G_tri from T_start to T_goal
7. Sequence of triangles gives homotopy class of path
8. Connect midpoints of shared edges to form continuous path
```

**Path Construction (2 Marks):**
The shortest path within a triangulated polygonal region is either:
- A direct straight-line segment, OR
- A polyline through **"steiner points"** on edges (Reflection Principle / Continuous Dijkstra by Mitchell).

The path within each triangle is a straight segment connecting the entry edge to the exit edge through their midpoints (or weighted centroids).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Off-by-One Errors:** Students frequently forget to include the outer face when applying Euler's formula, costing 1-2 marks.
> 2. **Conflating Triangulation with Trilateration:** Trilateration (GPS positioning) is unrelated to computational triangulation.
> 3. **Misapplying Formula with Holes:** Using $T = n - 2$ instead of $T = n + 2h - 2$ when $h \geq 1$ leads to wrong triangle counts.
> 4. **Forgetting Time Complexities:** KTU examiners award 1 mark specifically for stating algorithm complexity — always include $O(n^2)$ or $O(n \log n)$.
> 5. **Confusing Ear with Reflex Vertex:** A reflex (concave) vertex can never be the apex of an ear — many students make this conceptual error.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Triangulation** decomposes a polygon or point set into a finite set of non-overlapping triangles with shared vertices.
- **Simple Polygon Formula:** $T = n - 2$ triangles, $E = 3n - 6$ edges, $D = n - 3$ diagonals.
- **Polygon with Holes Formula:** $T = n + 2h - 2$ triangles, $E = 3n + 3h - 6$ edges.
- **Euler's Formula:** $V - E + F = 2$ (closed surface) or $V - E + F = 1 + h$ (with holes).
- **Ear Clipping Algorithm:** Iteratively removes "ears" (3 consecutive vertices forming a valid interior triangle); complexity $O(n^2)$.
- **Delaunay Triangulation:** Maximizes the minimum angle using the empty circumcircle property; dual of Voronoi diagram.
- **Constrained Delaunay Triangulation (CDT):** Respects required edges (obstacle boundaries); preferred for motion planning.
- **Existence & Uniqueness:** Every simple polygon has at least one triangulation; Delaunay is unique when no 4 points are cocircular.
- **Motion Planning Role:** Triangulation discretizes $C_{free}$ into searchable convex cells for graph-based path planning (A*, Dijkstra).
- **Barycentric Coordinates:** Any point inside a triangle $\Delta ABC$ is uniquely expressed as $\alpha A + \beta B + \gamma C$ with $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$.
- **Standard Algorithmic Complexities:** Ear clipping = $O(n^2)$; Delaunay (Bowyer-Watson) = $O(n^2)$ worst case, $O(n \log n)$ expected.
- **Engineering Domains:** Used in robotics, CAD/CAM, GIS, FEM, computer graphics, autonomous vehicles.
- **Examiner's Mantra:** Always verify your answer with Euler's formula. A correctly-derived $T$ must satisfy $V - E + F = 2$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrasonic Rangefinders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ultrasonic Rangefinders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Ultrasonic Rangefinder** is an active, non-contact, time-of-flight (ToF) based ranging sensor that estimates the distance to a target by emitting a high-frequency acoustic pulse (typically **20 kHz to 200 kHz**, with industrial robotic sensors operating in the **40 kHz, 50 kHz, 58 kHz, or 200 kHz** bands) and measuring the elapsed time until a portion of the emitted acoustic energy returns after striking an object in the environment.

In the formal KTU/robotics literature, ultrasonic sensors belong to the class of **exteroceptive proprioceptive-hybrid sensors** that provide range measurements $r = (t_f - t_0) \cdot c / 2$, where $t_0$ is the instant of pulse emission, $t_f$ is the instant of echo reception, and $c$ is the speed of sound in the propagation medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Ultrasonic rangefinders are treated as a *primitive exteroceptive sensor* in the Robot Motion Planning stack. They are critical building blocks for **occupancy grid mapping, collision avoidance, localization (e.g., with beacons), and frontier-based exploration**, all of which are foundational algorithms of Module 1–3 in the OEBRT833 syllabus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Speed of Sound (Canonical Value):** $c \approx \mathbf{343 \; m/s}$ in dry air at **20 °C**. The KTU board routinely uses the approximation $c = \mathbf{340 \; m/s}$ to simplify numerical answers. Always state the temperature assumption explicitly in your answer script.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine standing at the edge of a deep circular well and shouting "Hello!" into it. The deeper the well, the longer it takes for the echo to come back. If you had a precise stopwatch and knew the speed of sound, you could compute the depth of the well as $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$. An ultrasonic rangefinder does *exactly* this, but the human voice is replaced by a **piezoelectric transducer** that fires a chirp at ~40,000 vibrations per second (far above human hearing) and the stopwatch is replaced by an **on-chip timer** accurate to microseconds.

A practical real-world analogy used in autonomous robotics is the **"bat navigation" principle** (echolocation). Bats emit ultrasonic chirps and use the time delay of returning echoes to construct a spatial map of their surroundings in total darkness. The Polaroid ultrasonic sensor (the original automotive backup sensor, 1980s) was directly inspired by this biological model.

### 1.3 Why Ultrasonic Sensors in Robot Motion Planning?

- **Independence from lighting**: Cameras and LiDARs degrade in the dark, glare, fog, or smoke. Sound waves propagate unaffected by visible/IR light conditions.
- **Penetrates dust and smoke**: Excellent for search-and-rescue robots in disaster environments.
- **Low cost & low computational footprint**: Single-chip solution, ideal for embedded robot controllers (Arduino, Raspberry Pi, STM32).
- **Direct geometric measurement**: Provides a scalar range $r$ in meters, immediately usable in motion planning algorithms like **Artificial Potential Fields**, **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)**, and **Bug algorithms**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
