- ✅ **Two core challenges** in Module 2: **Sensor Noise** (random) and **Sensor Aliasing** (structural).
- ✅ **Sensor Noise** is Gaussian: $P(z \mid z^*) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z-z^*)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$. **Reducible by averaging** (variance $\to \sigma^2/N$).
- ✅ **Sensor Aliasing** occurs when $P(z \mid x_1) = P(z \mid x_2)$ for $x_1 \neq x_2$. **NOT reducible by averaging**. Requires **motion** to resolve.
- ✅ **Bayes Filter Equation:** $bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$. **Memorize verbatim.**
- ✅ **Prediction step:** $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$.
- ✅ **Markov Assumption** is the foundation of all recursive Bayesian localization.
- ✅ **Kalman Filter** handles noise (unimodal, Gaussian). **Particle Filter** handles aliasing (multi-modal, non-parametric).
- ✅ **KL Divergence = 0** is the formal mathematical signature of perfect aliasing.
- ✅ **Engineering examples** to recall: LIDAR noise (sensor noise), symmetric corridor (aliasing).
- ✅ **Formula Sheet Table** in Section 2.3 must be revised before every KTU exam attempt.
- ✅ **Pipe symbol rule:** Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute values, never the bare ASCII pipe in tables.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Strategy:** For 14-mark Part B questions, always structure your answer as **(i) Assumption Statement → (ii) Step-by-step Derivation → (iii) Final Equation → (iv) Numerical Verification**. Examiners allocate partial marks at each stage, so a clean derivation with a minor arithmetic slip still scores 10–12 out of 14.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effector Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effector Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Effector Noise** in robot motion planning refers to the **stochastic uncertainty, error, or randomness** present at the robot's end-effector (the tool/gripper/actuator interacting with the environment). It is the deviation between the *commanded/ideal* end-effector pose and the *actual* achieved end-effector pose during task execution, arising from sensor inaccuracies, actuator imperfections, mechanical vibrations, friction, backlash, and environmental disturbances.

Mathematically, the actual end-effector state $X_{actual}$ differs from the commanded state $X_{cmd}$ as:

$$X_{actual} = X_{cmd} + \epsilon$$

where $\epsilon \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the **noise vector** (or error vector), typically modeled as a **zero-mean random process**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you are threading a needle while wearing thick gloves on a shaky subway train. Your hand *intends* to hit a precise 2 mm target, but the actual needle tip jitters randomly around the target. The error between where you *wanted* to be and where you *actually* are is **effector noise**. In robots, this noise makes it impossible to guarantee exact positioning, so motion planning must *reason about probability* rather than *certainty*.

## 1.3 Classification of Effector Noise

| Type | Description | Typical Source |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| **Position Noise** | Random offset in $x, y, z$ | Encoder resolution, backlash |
| **Orientation Noise** | Random angular jitter | Joint compliance, gear slack |
| **Force/Torque Noise** | Random force variation | Motor torque ripple, friction |
| **Sensor Noise** | Uncertainty in pose measurement | Camera calibration, IMU drift |
| **Actuator Noise** | Imprecise control signal | PWM quantization, hydraulic leaks |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Deviation** $\sigma$ — measures the spread of noise distribution.
- **Covariance Matrix** $\Sigma$ — captures joint variability between noise components.
- **Mean Squared Error (MSE):** $E[\epsilon^2]$ — average squared deviation.
- **Root Mean Square (RMS) Error:** $\sqrt{E[\epsilon^2]}$ — square root of MSE.
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ in **dB**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Effector Noise — Structural Breakdown

Effector noise is rarely from a single source. The major contributing layers are:

1. **Joint-Level Noise** — Motor encoder quantization ($\pm 1$ count), gear backlash, hysteresis.
2. **Link-Level Noise** — Structural flexibility, vibration modes of slender arms.
3. **Controller-Level Noise** — Discrete-time control at low sampling rates → limit cycles.
4. **Sensor-Level Noise** — Camera pixel noise, LiDAR shot noise, IMU bias instability.
5. **Environment-Level Noise** — External forces, payload variation, temperature drift.

## 2.2 Mathematical Modeling of Effector Noise

### 2.2.1 Additive Gaussian Noise Model (Most Common)

The most widely accepted model in KTU-aligned coursework:

$$\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)$$

where:
- $\mu \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the **mean noise vector** (often $\mu = 0$ for unbiased actuators).
- $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the **covariance matrix** (symmetric, positive semi-definite).

The probability density function is:

$$p(\epsilon) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2} \vert \Sigma \vert^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\epsilon - \mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (\epsilon - \mu)\right)$$

### 2.2.2 Multiplicative Noise (for Scaling Errors)

$$\epsilon_{multiplicative} = X_{cmd} \odot \eta, \quad \eta \sim \mathcal{N}(1, \sigma^2)$$

Used when noise scales with magnitude (e.g., long-reach manipulators).

## 2.3 Error Propagation via the Jacobian

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Idea:** If the joint angles have small errors $\delta q$, the end-effector position error $\delta X$ is given by the **first-order Taylor expansion of forward kinematics**, dominated by the **geometric Jacobian** $J(q)$.

$$\delta X = J(q) \, \delta q$$

The covariance propagates as:

$$\Sigma_X = J(q) \, \Sigma_q \, J(q)^T$$

This is the **cornerstone formula** of error analysis in robotics.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $X_{actual} = X_{cmd} + \epsilon$ | Actual end-effector pose | $X$ in m or rad |
| 2 | $\delta X = J(q) \, \delta q$ | First-order error propagation | $J \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ |
| 3 | $\Sigma_X = J(q) \, \Sigma_q \, J(q)^T$ | Covariance propagation | Positive semi-definite |
| 4 | $\sigma_X = \sqrt{\text{diag}(\Sigma_X)}$ | Per-axis standard deviation | m or rad |
| 5 | $p(\epsilon) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2}\vert\Sigma\vert^{1/2}} e^{-\frac{1}{2}\epsilon^T\Sigma^{-1}\epsilon}$ | Gaussian PDF | $\vert \cdot \vert$ = determinant |
| 6 | $\text{MSE} = E[\epsilon^T \epsilon]$ | Mean squared error | scalar |
| 7 | $\text{RMS} = \sqrt{E[\epsilon^2]}$ | Root mean square error | same as $\epsilon$ |
| 8 | $\text{SNR} = 10\log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | **dB** |
| 9 | $\text{MDC} = \chi^2_{n, \alpha} \cdot \lambda_{max}(\Sigma_X)$ | Mission-Directed Capability | confidence region |
| 10 | $P(success) = \int_{X_{target}} p(X) \, dX$ | Probability of hitting target | dimensionless, $[0,1]$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Effector noise analysis is critical in:
- **Surgical robotics** — sub-millimeter accuracy for tumor ablation.
- **Autonomous vehicles** — uncertainty-aware trajectory planning.
- **Space robotics** — long-delay teleoperation with noisy feedback.
- **Pick-and-place manufacturing** — dealing with conveyor jitter.
- **Search and rescue** — uncertain manipulation in collapsed environments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: End-Effector Error from Joint Error

We begin with the **forward kinematics equation**:

$$X = f(q)$$

where $X \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is the end-effector pose and $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the joint configuration.

**Step 1:** Suppose the actual joint configuration has a small error:
$$q_{actual} = q + \delta q$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into forward kinematics:
$$X_{actual} = f(q + \delta q)$$

**Step 3:** Apply a **first-order Taylor expansion**:
$$f(q + \delta q) \approx f(q) + \frac{\partial f}{\partial q}\bigg|_{q} \delta q + \mathcal{O}(\|\delta q\|^2)$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the partial derivative as the **geometric Jacobian**:
$$J(q) = \frac{\partial f}{\partial q}\bigg|_{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$$

**Step 5:** Subtract the commanded pose:
$$X_{actual} - X_{cmd} \approx J(q) \, \delta q$$

**Result:**
$$\boxed{\delta X = J(q) \, \delta q}$$

This is the **fundamental error propagation equation** of robotics.

## 3.2 Derivation: Covariance Propagation

Given the joint error covariance matrix $\Sigma_q = E[\delta q \, \delta q^T]$, we want the end-effector covariance $\Sigma_X = E[\delta X \, \delta X^T]$.

**Step 1:** Substitute the error propagation result:
$$\delta X = J(q) \, \delta q$$

**Step 2:** Form the outer product $\delta X \, \delta X^T$:
$$\delta X \, \delta X^T = (J \, \delta q)(J \, \delta q)^T = J \, \delta q \, \delta q^T \, J^T$$

**Step 3:** Take the expectation on both sides:
$$E[\delta X \, \delta X^T] = E[J \, \delta q \, \delta q^T \, J^T]$$

**Step 4:** Since $J$ is deterministic (depends only on $q$), it factors out:
$$\Sigma_X = J \, E[\delta q \, \delta q^T] \, J^T$$

**Result:**
$$\boxed{\Sigma_X = J(q) \, \Sigma_q \, J(q)^T}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 2-DOF planar robot has joint covariance $\Sigma_q = \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.04 \end{bmatrix}$ (rad²). At a configuration $q = [\pi/4, \pi/6]^T$, the Jacobian is:
> $$J = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin q_1 - L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) & -L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) \\ L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) & L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}$$
> with $L_1 = 1$ m, $L_2 = 0.5$ m. Find $\Sigma_X$.

**Step 1:** Compute trig values:
$$\sin q_1 = \sin(\pi/4) = 0.7071, \quad \cos q_1 = 0.7071$$
$$\sin(q_1+q_2) = \sin(5\pi/12) = 0.9659, \quad \cos(q_1+q_2) = 0.2588$$

**Step 2:** Compute Jacobian entries:
$$J_{11} = -1(0.7071) - 0.5(0.9659) = -0.7071 - 0.4830 = -1.1901$$
$$J_{12} = -0.5(0.9659) = -0.4830$$
$$J_{21} = 1(0.7071) + 0.5(0.2588) = 0.7071 + 0.1294 = 0.8365$$
$$J_{22} = 0.5(0.2588) = 0.1294$$

**Step 3:** Construct the Jacobian:
$$J = \begin{bmatrix} -1.1901 & -0.4830 \\ 0.8365 & 0.1294 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Compute $J \Sigma_q$:
$$J \Sigma_q = \begin{bmatrix} -1.1901 & -0.4830 \\ 0.8365 & 0.1294 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.04 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.011901 & -0.019320 \\ 0.008365 & 0.005176 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5:** Compute $\Sigma_X = J \Sigma_q J^T$:
$$\Sigma_X = \begin{bmatrix} -0.011901 & -0.019320 \\ 0.008365 & 0.005176 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -1.1901 & 0.8365 \\ -0.4830 & 0.1294 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6:** Compute the entries:
$$\Sigma_{11} = (-0.011901)(-1.1901) + (-0.019320)(-0.4830) = 0.014164 + 0.009332 = 0.023496$$
$$\Sigma_{12} = (-0.011901)(0.8365) + (-0.019320)(0.1294) = -0.009955 + (-0.002500) = -0.012455$$
$$\Sigma_{21} = (0.008365)(-1.1901) + (0.005176)(-0.4830) = -0.009955 + (-0.002500) = -0.012455$$
$$\Sigma_{22} = (0.008365)(0.8365) + (0.005176)(0.1294) = 0.006998 + 0.000670 = 0.007668$$

**Result:**
$$\boxed{\Sigma_X = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0235 & -0.0125 \\ -0.0125 & 0.0077 \end{bmatrix} \;\; \text{m}^2}$$

The standard deviation along $x$ is $\sigma_x = \sqrt{0.0235} \approx 0.153$ m, and along $y$ is $\sigma_y = \sqrt{0.0077} \approx 0.088$ m.

## 3.4 Probability of Reaching a Target Region

The probability that the noisy end-effector lands inside a target region $\mathcal{R}$ is:

$$P(\text{success}) = \int_{\mathcal{R}} p(X) \, dX$$

For a **circular target of radius $r$** centered at the commanded position, with isotropic noise $\sigma^2 I$:

$$P(\text{success}) = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{r^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

For $\sigma = 0.01$ m and $r = 0.02$ m:
$$P = 1 - e^{-2} = 1 - 0.1353 = 0.8647 \approx 86.5\%$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Noise Propagation Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Commanded Pose Xcmd] --> B[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    B --> C[Joint Commands qcmd]
    C --> D[Joint Actuators with Noise]
    D --> E[Actual Joint Angles q + delta q]
    E --> F[Forward Kinematics f q]
    F --> G[End Effector Pose X]
    H[Sensor Feedback with Noise] --> I[Pose Estimator]
    G --> I
    I --> J[Estimated Pose Xest]
    J --> K[Motion Planner]
    K --> L[Corrected Trajectory]
    L --> B
    M[Environment Disturbances] --> G
```

## 4.2 Effector Noise Source Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    N1[Effector Noise] --> N2[Joint Level]
    N1 --> N3[Link Level]
    N1 --> N4[Controller Level]
    N1 --> N5[Sensor Level]
    N1 --> N6[Environment Level]
    N2 --> S1[Encoder Quantization]
    N2 --> S2[Gear Backlash]
    N2 --> S3[Motor Torque Ripple]
    N3 --> S4[Structural Flexibility]
    N3 --> S5[Link Vibration]
    N4 --> S6[Discrete Time Sampling]
    N4 --> S7[PWM Quantization]
    N5 --> S8[Camera Pixel Noise]
    N5 --> S9[IMU Drift]
    N5 --> S10[LiDAR Shot Noise]
    N6 --> S11[External Forces]
    N6 --> S12[Temperature Drift]
    N6 --> S13[Payload Variation]
```

## 4.3 Noise Filtering and Compensation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference Trajectory] --> C[Controller]
    C --> A[Actuator]
    A --> R1[Robot Dynamics]
    N1a[Effector Noise] --> R1
    R1 --> EE[End Effector]
    EE --> S[Sensor]
    S2[Sensor Noise] --> S
    S --> F[Kalman Filter]
    F --> EST[State Estimate]
    EST --> C
    EE --> OUT[Task Output]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define effector noise in the context of robot motion planning. List any four sources of effector noise.**

**Model Answer:**

Effector noise is the stochastic deviation between the commanded and actual end-effector pose during robot task execution, modeled as $\epsilon = X_{actual} - X_{cmd}$.

**Four sources:**
1. Joint encoder quantization error
2. Gear backlash and mechanical compliance
3. Sensor measurement noise (camera, IMU)
4. Environmental disturbances (vibration, payload variation)

*(2 marks for definition, 1 mark for sources)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the Jacobian-based error propagation formula? State its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

$$\delta X = J(q) \, \delta q$$

where $J(q)$ is the geometric Jacobian, $\delta q$ is joint error, $\delta X$ is end-effector error.

**Significance:** It maps small joint-space uncertainties to Cartesian-space errors, enabling prediction of end-effector accuracy from joint sensor resolution. It is the foundation for designing calibration routines and uncertainty-aware motion planners.

*(1.5 marks for formula, 1.5 marks for significance)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the covariance propagation formula $\Sigma_X = J(q) \, \Sigma_q \, J(q)^T$ starting from the first-order Taylor expansion of forward kinematics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-DOF robot has joint covariance matrix $\Sigma_q = \text{diag}(0.0025, 0.0025, 0.0004)$ rad². At configuration $q = [30°, 45°, 60°]^T$, the Jacobian is given by:

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.3 & 0.1 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 & -0.2 \\ 0.0 & 0.2 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute $\Sigma_X$ and the standard deviations along each Cartesian axis. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** Forward kinematics $X = f(q)$ with joint error $q + \delta q$:
$$X_{actual} = f(q + \delta q)$$

**Step 2:** First-order Taylor expansion:
$$f(q + \delta q) = f(q) + \frac{\partial f}{\partial q}\delta q + \mathcal{O}(\|\delta q\|^2)$$

**Step 3:** Recognize Jacobian $J(q) = \frac{\partial f}{\partial q}$:
$$\delta X = J(q) \, \delta q$$

**Step 4:** Form covariance of $\delta X$:
$$\Sigma_X = E[\delta X \, \delta X^T]$$

**Step 5:** Substitute:
$$\Sigma_X = E[(J \, \delta q)(J \, \delta q)^T] = E[J \, \delta q \, \delta q^T \, J^T]$$

**Step 6:** Since $J$ is deterministic, factor it out:
$$\Sigma_X = J \, E[\delta q \, \delta q^T] \, J^T = J \, \Sigma_q \, J^T$$

$$\boxed{\Sigma_X = J(q) \, \Sigma_q \, J(q)^T}$$

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** Compute $J \Sigma_q$ (diagonal $\Sigma_q$ scales columns):
$$\Sigma_q = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0025 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.0025 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.0004 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$J \Sigma_q = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \cdot 0.0025 & -0.3 \cdot 0.0025 & 0.1 \cdot 0.0004 \\ 0.4 \cdot 0.0025 & 0.6 \cdot 0.0025 & -0.2 \cdot 0.0004 \\ 0 & 0.2 \cdot 0.0025 & 0.3 \cdot 0.0004 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$J \Sigma_q = \begin{bmatrix} 0.00125 & -0.00075 & 0.00004 \\ 0.00100 & 0.00150 & -0.00008 \\ 0.00000 & 0.00050 & 0.00012 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\Sigma_X = (J\Sigma_q) J^T$:

$$\Sigma_{11} = 0.00125(0.5) + (-0.00075)(-0.3) + 0.00004(0.1) = 0.000625 + 0.000225 + 0.000004 = 0.000854$$

$$\Sigma_{12} = 0.00125(0.4) + (-0.00075)(0.6) + 0.00004(-0.2) = 0.000500 - 0.000450 - 0.000008 = 0.000042$$

$$\Sigma_{13} = 0.00125(0) + (-0.00075)(0.2) + 0.00004(0.3) = 0 - 0.000150 + 0.000012 = -0.000138$$

$$\Sigma_{22} = 0.00100(0.4) + 0.00150(0.6) + (-0.00008)(-0.2) = 0.000400 + 0.000900 + 0.000016 = 0.001316$$

$$\Sigma_{23} = 0.00100(0) + 0.00150(0.2) + (-0.00008)(0.3) = 0 + 0.000300 - 0.000024 = 0.000276$$

$$\Sigma_{33} = 0(0) + 0.00050(0.2) + 0.00012(0.3) = 0.000100 + 0.000036 = 0.000136$$

**Result:**
$$\Sigma_X = \begin{bmatrix} 0.000854 & 0.000042 & -0.000138 \\ 0.000042 & 0.001316 & 0.000276 \\ -0.000138 & 0.000276 & 0.000136 \end{bmatrix} \;\; \text{m}^2$$

**Standard deviations:**
$$\sigma_x = \sqrt{0.000854} \approx 0.0292 \;\; \text{m}$$
$$\sigma_y = \sqrt{0.001316} \approx 0.0363 \;\; \text{m}$$
$$\sigma_z = \sqrt{0.000136} \approx 0.0117 \;\; \text{m}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the additive Gaussian noise model for effector noise. Derive the multivariate Gaussian probability density function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot end-effector has isotropic Gaussian position noise with $\sigma = 5$ mm. Compute the probability of the end-effector landing within a circular target of radius $r = 8$ mm. Also, find the minimum target radius required to achieve a 99% success probability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** Additive Gaussian model:
$$\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)$$

The end-effector pose becomes:
$$X = X_{cmd} + \epsilon$$

**Step 2:** Univariate Gaussian first. For a scalar random variable $x$ with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$:

$$p(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Extend to multivariate $n$-dimensional case. The joint PDF must satisfy:
- $\int p(\epsilon) \, d\epsilon = 1$ (normalization)
- $E[\epsilon] = \mu$ (mean)
- $E[(\epsilon - \mu)(\epsilon - \mu)^T] = \Sigma$ (covariance)

**Step 4:** The general form of the multivariate Gaussian PDF is:

$$p(\epsilon) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2} \vert \Sigma \vert^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\epsilon - \mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (\epsilon - \mu)\right)$$

**Step 5:** For the special case of isotropic noise, $\Sigma = \sigma^2 I$, the PDF simplifies to:
$$p(\epsilon) = \frac{1}{(2\pi \sigma^2)^{n/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{\|\epsilon - \mu\|^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

This is a **radially symmetric** distribution — a key property exploited in sampling-based motion planners.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** For circular target with isotropic Gaussian noise, success probability:

$$P(success) = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{r^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Plug in $r = 8$ mm, $\sigma = 5$ mm:
$$P = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{64}{2 \cdot 25}\right) = 1 - e^{-1.28} = 1 - 0.2780 = 0.7220$$

$$\boxed{P(success) \approx 72.2\%}$$

**Step 3:** For 99% success, set $P = 0.99$:
$$0.99 = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{r^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $r^2$:
$$\exp\left(-\frac{r^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) = 0.01$$

**Step 5:** Take natural logarithm:
$$-\frac{r^2}{2\sigma^2} = \ln(0.01) = -4.6052$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $r$:
$$r^2 = 2\sigma^2 \cdot 4.6052 = 2 \cdot 25 \cdot 4.6052 = 230.26 \;\; \text{mm}^2$$

$$r = \sqrt{230.26} \approx 15.17 \;\; \text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{r_{min} \approx 15.17 \;\; \text{mm}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to justify the Taylor expansion** in derivations — KTU examiners award partial credit only if higher-order terms are explicitly mentioned as negligible.
> 2. **Mixing up $\Sigma_q$ and $\Sigma_X$** — joint covariance goes IN the formula, end-effector covariance comes OUT.
> 3. **Computing only diagonal elements** of $\Sigma_X$ — full matrix is required unless specifically asked.
> 4. **Unit errors** — if $\Sigma_q$ is in rad² and Jacobian mixes m/rad, students often forget to track units; $\Sigma_X$ ends up in m².
> 5. **Skipping the Jacobian definition** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks if $J(q) = \partial f / \partial q$ is not explicitly defined.
> 6. **Confusing RMS with standard deviation** — RMS = $\sigma$ only for zero-mean signals.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Effector Noise**
> - **Definition:** $\epsilon = X_{actual} - X_{cmd}$, stochastic deviation at the end effector.
> - **Core Formula:** $\delta X = J(q) \, \delta q$ (first-order error propagation).
> - **Covariance Propagation:** $\Sigma_X = J \, \Sigma_q \, J^T$ — symmetric, positive semi-definite.
> - **Gaussian Model:** $p(\epsilon) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2}\vert\Sigma\vert^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}\epsilon^T \Sigma^{-1} \epsilon\right)$.
> - **Probability of success in circular target:** $P = 1 - \exp(-r^2 / 2\sigma^2)$.
> - **Standard Metrics:** $\sigma$ (std dev), MSE, RMS, SNR (in **dB**).
> - **Noise Sources:** Joint, link, controller, sensor, environment — five layers.
> - **Real-world impact:** Surgical robots, autonomous vehicles, space manipulators, manufacturing.
> - **Key trick:** When $\Sigma_q$ is diagonal, $J \Sigma_q$ is just column-scaling of $J$.
> - **Always** state units (m², rad²) and check for symmetric positive-definite $\Sigma_X$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Error Model for Odometric Position Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Error Model for Odometric Position Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — ERROR MODEL FOR ODOMETRIC POSITION ESTIMATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Odometric Position Estimation** is the process of computing a mobile robot's pose (position $(x, y)$ and orientation $\theta$) by integrating the wheel rotations measured by encoders over discrete time intervals. The **Error Model for Odometric Position Estimation** is the mathematical framework that quantifies how uncertainties in the measured control inputs (wheel displacements) and the robot's kinematic parameters propagate into the robot's estimated pose in the form of an error **covariance matrix**.

Formally, given the robot state vector

$$
\mathbf{x}(k) = \begin{bmatrix} x(k) & y(k) & \theta(k) \end{bmatrix}^{T}
$$

and the control input vector

$$
\mathbf{u}(k) = \begin{bmatrix} \Delta s_{r}(k) & \Delta s_{l}(k) \end{bmatrix}^{T}
$$

the error model describes the evolution of the **3×3 pose-error covariance matrix** $P(k)$ that characterises the uncertainty ellipsoid around the estimated pose at every sampling instant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):** Odometric position estimation is a *dead-reckoning* technique where the pose is obtained by integrating encoder readings. The error model mathematically represents the propagation of *systematic* and *non-systematic* errors into the pose estimate using a covariance propagation equation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded inside an unfamiliar room and asked to walk to a specific corner. You can only count your steps and feel which direction you are facing. With each step, small mistakes accumulate: a slightly longer step, a slight twist of the foot, an uneven floor pushing you sideways. After 100 steps, you could be several feet away from where you think you are. Your **uncertainty grows with distance travelled**, forming an "uncertainty bubble" around your believed location. 

This is precisely what happens to a wheeled mobile robot using **odometry**. Each encoder tick adds a small error, and the **Error Model** is the mathematical "uncertainty bubble" that predicts how big this bubble will be after N steps. The bubble is **ellipsoidal**, not circular, because errors grow faster in the direction of travel than perpendicular to it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Odometry is *open-loop* dead reckoning. The error model is what tells the robot (or the higher-level planner) "you are probably here, but your uncertainty is growing at this rate." This is why the error model is the backbone of *probabilistic localization* algorithms such as the Kalman Filter and the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) used in ESE Module 3.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard metric:** pose uncertainty expressed in $\mathbf{m^2}$ for $x, y$ and $\mathbf{rad^2}$ for $\theta$.
- **Wheelbase $b$:** typically $0.2$ to $0.5$ m for indoor mobile robots.
- **Encoder resolution:** 500 to 4096 ticks/revolution.
- **Update rate:** standard $10$–$\mathbf{100}$ Hz for EKF-based localisation.
- **Units convention:** SI throughout the module; angles in **radians**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Odometric Errors

Errors in odometric position estimation are categorised into two fundamental classes. This classification is a **favourite 3-mark and 7-mark KTU question** topic.

### 2.1.1 Systematic Errors (Deterministic / Predictable)

These are repeatable, bias-like errors caused by imperfections in the mechanical/kinematic configuration of the robot. They do **not** average out over time; they accumulate proportionally to the travelled distance.

| # | Systematic Error Source | Physical Cause | Effect on Pose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Wheel diameter mismatch** ($\Delta D$) | Unequal effective rolling radii $D_{r} \neq D_{l}$ | Curved trajectory even on straight command |
| 2 | **Wheelbase error** ($\Delta b$) | Mechanical tolerance in axle length | Orientation drift over distance |
| 3 | **Encoder scale factor error** ($\Delta c$) | Mismatch between nominal and actual ticks/m | Scale error in distance |
| 4 | **Encoder resolution quantization** | Discrete tick granularity | Bounded, repeating quantization noise |
| 5 | **Finite encoder sampling rate** | Discrete-time integration | Bias depending on velocity profile |

### 2.1.2 Non-Systematic Errors (Stochastic / Unpredictable)

These arise from **unpredictable robot–environment interactions** and can be modelled only as random processes. They are the primary target of the covariance-based error model.

| # | Non-Systematic Error Source | Physical Cause | Statistical Model |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Wheel slip** | Low-friction patches, acceleration/braking | Burst noise, occasional outliers |
| 2 | **Uneven floor / bumps** | Carpet edges, thresholds, debris | Random walk in pose |
| 3 | **Skidding** | Fast turns, over-acceleration | Orientation jump |
| 4 | **Floor roughness** | Micro-vibrations | Zero-mean Gaussian noise |
| 5 | **External contact** | Collisions, cable snag | Outliers in control input |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Systematic errors are compensated by **calibration**, while non-systematic errors are handled by the **probabilistic error model** (covariance propagation). The ESE Module 2 explicitly tests this distinction.

## 2.2 The Two Levels of the Error Model

The complete error model has two layers, both required for KTU-level depth:

1. **Deterministic / Parametric Error Model** — uses the exact kinematic function $f(x, u)$ and propagates *biases* in parameters and inputs.
2. **Stochastic / Covariance Error Model** — propagates the *covariance* of the pose under a Gaussian noise assumption on the inputs, yielding the **Kalman-style update** for $P(k)$.

## 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Error Model

- **Why?** Because a single number (the estimated pose) without a confidence measure is useless for safe navigation, planning, and SLAM. The error model provides the confidence measure as the **covariance ellipse**.
- **How?** By *linearising* the nonlinear kinematic function $f(x,u)$ around the current estimate via the **Jacobian**, and then applying the classical linear covariance propagation rule.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love the question "Differentiate between systematic and non-systematic errors with examples." Memorise the table above and you score full marks.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise every entry below. All equations below are examinable in ESE (End Semester Examination).

| # | Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta s_{r} = n_{r} \cdot k_{r}$ | Right wheel displacement | m |
| 2 | $\Delta s_{l} = n_{l} \cdot k_{l}$ | Left wheel displacement | m |
| 3 | $\Delta s = (\Delta s_{r} + \Delta s_{l}) / 2$ | Average linear displacement | m |
| 4 | $\Delta \theta = (\Delta s_{r} - \Delta s_{l}) / b$ | Incremental heading change | rad |
| 5 | $x_{k+1} = x_{k} + \Delta s \cdot \cos(\theta_{k} + \Delta\theta/2)$ | Position x update | m |
| 6 | $y_{k+1} = y_{k} + \Delta s \cdot \sin(\theta_{k} + \Delta\theta/2)$ | Position y update | m |
| 7 | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_{k} + \Delta \theta$ | Orientation update | rad |
| 8 | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = f(\mathbf{x}_{k}, \mathbf{u}_{k})$ | Nonlinear kinematic model (compact) | — |
| 9 | $P_{k+1} = F_{k} P_{k} F_{k}^{T} + Q_{k}$ | Linear covariance propagation | varies |
| 10 | $F_{k} = \partial f / \partial \mathbf{x} \big\vert_{k}$ | State Jacobian (3×3) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\nabla u_{k} = \partial f / \partial \mathbf{u} \big\vert_{k}$ | Input Jacobian (3×2) | mixed |
| 12 | $P_{k+1} = F_{k} P_{k} F_{k}^{T} + \nabla u_{k} \, Q_{k} \, \nabla u_{k}^{T}$ | General (state+input) propagation | varies |
| 13 | $Q_{k} = \mathrm{diag}(\sigma_{r}^{2}, \sigma_{l}^{2})$ | Input noise covariance (2×2) | m$^2$ |
| 14 | $\mathrm{tr}(P_{k}) = \sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy} + \sigma_{\theta\theta}$ | Total pose uncertainty scalar | mixed |
| 15 | $\mathrm{eig}(P_{k}) = [\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}, \lambda_{3}]$ | Principal-axis uncertainty magnitudes | mixed |

> **Notation safeguard:** All absolute-value bars in the formulas above are written using $\vert$ rather than the markdown-reserved pipe character to keep the table layout intact.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of the Error Model

- **Probabilistic SLAM:** Provides the motion-model noise term used inside EKF-SLAM and Particle-Filter SLAM.
- **Active Localisation:** The robot can decide which action **minimises** $\mathrm{tr}(P_{k+1})$ — this is the foundation of *information-theoretic path planning*.
- **Safety / Geofencing:** A planner can inflate obstacles by $\sqrt{\lambda_{\max}(P_{k})}$ to remain safe under uncertainty.
- **Sensor Fusion Pre-step:** Supplies the **prediction covariance** for EKF data association.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Kinematic Pose-Update Equations

We derive the exact discrete-time kinematic model of a differential-drive robot. Consider two wheels of radii $r_{r}$ and $r_{l}$ mounted on a common axle of length $b$. The encoders report the *number of ticks* $n_{r}$ and $n_{l}$ over one control cycle.

**Step 1 — Convert ticks to wheel displacements:**

$$
\Delta s_{r} = 2\pi r_{r} \cdot \frac{n_{r}}{N_{r}}, \qquad \Delta s_{l} = 2\pi r_{l} \cdot \frac{n_{l}}{N_{l}}
$$

where $N_{r}, N_{l}$ are the *ticks-per-revolution* of the encoders. The factor $k_{r} = 2\pi r_{r} / N_{r}$ is the **encoder resolution** in m/tick.

**Step 2 — Express the *mean* translation and *incremental rotation** (kinematic decoupling of the bicycle model):

For a differential drive, the robot's instantaneous centre of rotation (ICR) lies on the axle. The arc traced by the right wheel has length $\Delta s_{r}$, and the left wheel has length $\Delta s_{l}$. The total turn angle is the difference of arcs divided by the wheelbase:

$$
\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_{r} - \Delta s_{l}}{b}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the linear distance travelled by the *robot centre* (midpoint of axle):**

The mid-axle point travels the *average* of the two wheel arcs because it is geometrically halfway between the two wheels:

$$
\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_{r} + \Delta s_{l}}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the new pose in the world frame (mid-point integration):**

The robot's centre moves an arc of length $\Delta s$ through a heading angle of $\theta_{k} + \Delta \theta / 2$ (the *mid-heading*, an approximation that is second-order accurate and standard in mobile robotics). Resolving into Cartesian world-frame components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= x_{k} + \Delta s \cdot \cos\!\left( \theta_{k} + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2} \right) \\[4pt]
y_{k+1} &= y_{k} + \Delta s \cdot \sin\!\left( \theta_{k} + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2} \right) \\[4pt]
\theta_{k+1} &= \theta_{k} + \Delta \theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compact vector form (state transition function):**

$$
\mathbf{x}_{k+1} \;=\; f(\mathbf{x}_{k}, \mathbf{u}_{k}) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} x_{k} \\ y_{k} \\ \theta_{k} \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \Delta s \cdot \cos(\theta_{k} + \Delta\theta/2) \\ \Delta s \cdot \sin(\theta_{k} + \Delta\theta/2) \\ \Delta\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

with the control input vector $\mathbf{u}_{k} = \begin{bmatrix} \Delta s_{r} & \Delta s_{l} \end{bmatrix}^{T}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic recap:** Step 1 converts digital encoder counts to physical lengths. Steps 2–3 invert the rolling kinematics (arc length ⇒ angle). Step 4 projects the local motion onto the world frame. Step 5 packages everything for the EKF.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The State Jacobian $F_{k}$

For covariance propagation we need the **Jacobian of $f$ with respect to the state** evaluated at the current estimate. Let $\alpha = \theta_{k} + \Delta\theta/2$ for brevity.

$$
F_{k} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial x_{k+1}}{\partial x_{k}} & \dfrac{\partial x_{k+1}}{\partial y_{k}} & \dfrac{\partial x_{k+1}}{\partial \theta_{k}} \\[10pt]
\dfrac{\partial y_{k+1}}{\partial x_{k}} & \dfrac{\partial y_{k+1}}{\partial y_{k}} & \dfrac{\partial y_{k+1}}{\partial \theta_{k}} \\[10pt]
\dfrac{\partial \theta_{k+1}}{\partial x_{k}} & \dfrac{\partial \theta_{k+1}}{\partial y_{k}} & \dfrac{\partial \theta_{k+1}}{\partial \theta_{k}}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Computing each entry explicitly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial x_{k+1}}{\partial x_{k}} &= 1, & \frac{\partial x_{k+1}}{\partial y_{k}} &= 0, & \frac{\partial x_{k+1}}{\partial \theta_{k}} &= -\Delta s \cdot \sin(\alpha) \\[6pt]
\frac{\partial y_{k+1}}{\partial x_{k}} &= 0, & \frac{\partial y_{k+1}}{\partial y_{k}} &= 1, & \frac{\partial y_{k+1}}{\partial \theta_{k}} &= \phantom{-}\Delta s \cdot \cos(\alpha) \\[6pt]
\frac{\partial \theta_{k+1}}{\partial x_{k}} &= 0, & \frac{\partial \theta_{k+1}}{\partial y_{k}} &= 0, & \frac{\partial \theta_{k+1}}{\partial \theta_{k}} &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
F_{k} = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & -\Delta s \cdot \sin(\theta_{k} + \Delta\theta/2) \\
0 & 1 & \phantom{-}\Delta s \cdot \cos(\theta_{k} + \Delta\theta/2) \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Conversion logic:** $x$ and $y$ do not depend on each other (giving the identity in the upper-left $2\times2$ block), but they depend on the *current* heading, giving the off-diagonal coupling in the third column. The orientation depends on no other state component (giving the $1$ in position $(3,3)$ and zeros elsewhere).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Input Jacobian $\nabla u_{k}$

The input Jacobian captures how a change in wheel displacements changes the pose. Write the pose update as a function of $\mathbf{u} = (\Delta s_{r}, \Delta s_{l})^{T}$ using $\Delta s = (\Delta s_{r} + \Delta s_{l})/2$ and $\Delta\theta = (\Delta s_{r} - \Delta s_{l})/b$.

**Step 1 — Compute partial derivatives of $x_{k+1}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial x_{k+1}}{\partial \Delta s_{r}} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cos(\alpha) \;-\; \tfrac{\Delta s}{2b} \sin(\alpha) \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial x_{k+1}}{\partial \Delta s_{l}} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cos(\alpha) \;+\; \tfrac{\Delta s}{2b} \sin(\alpha)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute partial derivatives of $y_{k+1}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial y_{k+1}}{\partial \Delta s_{r}} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \sin(\alpha) \;+\; \tfrac{\Delta s}{2b} \cos(\alpha) \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial y_{k+1}}{\partial \Delta s_{l}} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \sin(\alpha) \;-\; \tfrac{\Delta s}{2b} \cos(\alpha)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute partial derivatives of $\theta_{k+1}$:**

$$
\frac{\partial \theta_{k+1}}{\partial \Delta s_{r}} = \frac{1}{b}, \qquad \frac{\partial \theta_{k+1}}{\partial \Delta s_{l}} = -\frac{1}{b}
$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the $3 \times 2$ input Jacobian:**

$$
\nabla u_{k} = \begin{bmatrix}
\tfrac{1}{2}\cos\alpha - \tfrac{\Delta s}{2b}\sin\alpha & \tfrac{1}{2}\cos\alpha + \tfrac{\Delta s}{2b}\sin\alpha \\[8pt]
\tfrac{1}{2}\sin\alpha + \tfrac{\Delta s}{2b}\cos\alpha & \tfrac{1}{2}\sin\alpha - \tfrac{\Delta s}{2b}\cos\alpha \\[8pt]
1/b & -1/b
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The first term ($\tfrac{1}{2}\cos\alpha$, etc.) reflects the *translation* contribution of the wheel; the second term ($\tfrac{\Delta s}{2b}\sin\alpha$, etc.) reflects the *rotation* contribution, scaled by the wheelbase $b$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — The Covariance Propagation Equation

Let the control input be corrupted by zero-mean Gaussian noise $\mathbf{w}_{k} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_{k})$ with

$$
Q_{k} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{r}^{2} & 0 \\ 0 & \sigma_{l}^{2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

assuming the wheel-encoder noises are independent (a standard simplifying assumption).

The pose noise is the linearisation of $f$ around the current estimate:

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_{k+1} \;\approx\; F_{k} \tilde{\mathbf{x}}_{k} \;+\; \nabla u_{k} \, \mathbf{w}_{k}
$$

Taking the outer product of $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_{k+1}$ with its transpose and applying expectation $\mathbb{E}[\cdot]$ (which annihilates the zero-mean noise term):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{k+1} &= \mathbb{E}\!\left[ \tilde{\mathbf{x}}_{k+1} \tilde{\mathbf{x}}_{k+1}^{T} \right] \\[4pt]
&= F_{k}\, \mathbb{E}\!\left[ \tilde{\mathbf{x}}_{k} \tilde{\mathbf{x}}_{k}^{T} \right] F_{k}^{T} \;+\; \nabla u_{k}\, \mathbb{E}\!\left[ \mathbf{w}_{k} \mathbf{w}_{k}^{T} \right] \nabla u_{k}^{T} \;+\; \text{cross term} \\[4pt]
&= F_{k}\, P_{k}\, F_{k}^{T} \;+\; \nabla u_{k}\, Q_{k}\, \nabla u_{k}^{T}
\end{aligned}
$$

The cross term $\mathbb{E}[F_{k}\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_{k}\mathbf{w}_{k}^{T}\nabla u_{k}^{T}]$ vanishes because process noise and pose uncertainty are assumed **statistically independent** at the prediction step (a key EKF assumption).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Master Equation (must memorise):**
>
> $$\boxed{\,P_{k+1} \;=\; F_{k}\, P_{k}\, F_{k}^{T} \;+\; \nabla u_{k}\, Q_{k}\, \nabla u_{k}^{T}\,}$$
>
> This is the **Error Model for Odometric Position Estimation** in its KTU-board-exact form.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** A differential-drive robot has $b = 0.5$ m, current pose $(x, y, \theta) = (0, 0, 0)$, and receives encoder readings $\Delta s_{r} = 0.51$ m, $\Delta s_{l} = 0.49$ m. The wheel-encoder noise covariance is $Q = \mathrm{diag}(0.0025, 0.0025)$ m$^2$ (i.e. $\sigma = 0.05$ m). The current pose covariance is $P_{k} = \mathrm{diag}(0.1, 0.1, 0.05)$. Find the updated pose **and** the updated pose covariance.

**Step 1 — Compute mean translation and turn:**

$$
\Delta s = \frac{0.51 + 0.49}{2} = 0.50 \text{ m}, \qquad \Delta\theta = \frac{0.51 - 0.49}{0.5} = 0.04 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute updated pose (mid-heading $\alpha = 0 + 0.02 = 0.02$ rad):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= 0 + 0.50 \cdot \cos(0.02) = 0.50 \cdot 0.99980 = 0.4999 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
y_{k+1} &= 0 + 0.50 \cdot \sin(0.02) = 0.50 \cdot 0.02000 = 0.0100 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
\theta_{k+1} &= 0 + 0.04 = 0.04 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the state Jacobian $F_{k}$:**

$$
F_{k} = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & -0.50 \cdot \sin(0.02) \\
0 & 1 & \phantom{-}0.50 \cdot \cos(0.02) \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & -0.0100 \\
0 & 1 & \phantom{-}0.4999 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the input Jacobian $\nabla u_{k}$:**

With $\Delta s = 0.50$, $b = 0.5$, $\alpha = 0.02$, $\cos\alpha \approx 0.9998$, $\sin\alpha \approx 0.0200$, the rotation coefficient $\Delta s / (2b) = 0.5$:

$$
\nabla u_{k} = \begin{bmatrix}
0.5 \cdot 0.9998 - 0.5 \cdot 0.0200 & 0.5 \cdot 0.9998 + 0.5 \cdot 0.0200 \\
0.5 \cdot 0.0200 + 0.5 \cdot 0.9998 & 0.5 \cdot 0.0200 - 0.5 \cdot 0.9998 \\
1/0.5 & -1/0.5
\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}
0.4899 & 0.5099 \\
0.5099 & -0.4899 \\
2 & -2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the two covariance contributions separately.**

**Term A:** $F_{k} P_{k} F_{k}^{T}$:

$$
F_{k} P_{k} = \begin{bmatrix}
1 \cdot 0.1 & 0 & -0.0100 \cdot 0.05 \\
0 & 1 \cdot 0.1 & \phantom{-}0.4999 \cdot 0.05 \\
0 & 0 & 0.05
\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}
0.1 & 0 & -0.0005 \\
0 & 0.1 & 0.0250 \\
0 & 0 & 0.05
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
F_{k} P_{k} F_{k}^{T} = \begin{bmatrix}
0.1 & 0 & -0.0005 \\
0 & 0.1 & 0.0250 \\
0 & 0 & 0.05
\end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0 \\
-0.0100 & 0.4999 & 1
\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}
0.1000 & -0.00025 & -0.0005 \\
-0.00025 & 0.1125 & 0.0250 \\
-0.0005 & 0.0250 & 0.05
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Term B:** $\nabla u_{k}\, Q_{k}\, \nabla u_{k}^{T}$:

$$
\nabla u_{k} Q_{k} = 0.0025 \begin{bmatrix} 0.4899 & 0.5099 \\ 0.5099 & -0.4899 \\ 2 & -2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.001225 & 0.001275 \\ 0.001275 & -0.001225 \\ 0.005 & -0.005 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\nabla u_{k} Q_{k} \nabla u_{k}^{T} = \begin{bmatrix}
0.001225 & 0.001275 \\
0.001275 & -0.001225 \\
0.005 & -0.005
\end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix}
0.4899 & 0.5099 & 2 \\
0.5099 & -0.4899 & -2
\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}
0.00125 & 0.00000 & 0.0000 \\
0.00000 & 0.00125 & 0.0050 \\
0.0000 & 0.0050 & 0.0200
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Sum the two contributions to obtain the updated pose covariance $P_{k+1}$:**

$$
P_{k+1} = F_{k} P_{k} F_{k}^{T} + \nabla u_{k} Q_{k} \nabla u_{k}^{T} = \begin{bmatrix}
0.1013 & -0.00025 & -0.0005 \\
-0.00025 & 0.1138 & 0.0300 \\
-0.0005 & 0.0300 & 0.0700
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The diagonal terms $\sigma_{xx}^{2}=0.1013$, $\sigma_{yy}^{2}=0.1138$, $\sigma_{\theta\theta}^{2}=0.0700$ have all **grown** from their previous values, confirming the open-loop error growth property of odometry. The off-diagonal $\sigma_{xy}$ and $\sigma_{x\theta}$ are non-zero, meaning the uncertainty in $x$ is **correlated** with the other components — this is the *ellipsoidal* nature of pose uncertainty.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Error Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Encoder Tick Counts n_r and n_l] --> B[Wheel Displacement Module]
    B --> C[Kinematic Pose Update f of x_k and u_k]
    D[Previous Pose Estimate x_k] --> C
    E[Previous Pose Covariance P_k] --> F[State Jacobian F_k Computation]
    C --> F
    C --> G[Input Jacobian U_k Computation]
    H[Input Noise Covariance Q_k] --> I[Input Noise Contribution Term]
    G --> I
    F --> J[Covariance Propagation Equation]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Updated Pose Covariance P_k_plus_1]
    C --> L[Updated Pose Estimate x_k_plus_1]
    K --> M[Uncertainty Ellipsoid Renderer]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Output to EKF Predict Step]

    style A fill:#E8F0FF,stroke:#3B6FB6,color:#0B2545
    style C fill:#FFF4D6,stroke:#B68A3B,color:#3B2A0B
    style J fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#B63B3B,color:#3B0B0B
    style N fill:#DFFFE0,stroke:#3BB657,color:#0B3B14
```

**Reading guide:** The blue branch (left) feeds encoder data; the yellow node is the kinematic function $f$; the red node is the **master covariance equation** $P_{k+1} = F P F^{T} + \nabla u Q \nabla u^{T}$; the green output feeds the next module (EKF predict in ESE Module 3).

## 4.2 Taxonomy of Odometric Error Sources

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Root[Odometric Error Sources] --> S[Systematic Errors]
    Root --> N[Non Systematic Errors]

    S --> S1[Wheel Diameter Mismatch]
    S --> S2[Wheelbase Tolerance]
    S --> S3[Encoder Scale Factor Error]
    S --> S4[Finite Encoder Resolution]
    S --> S5[Encoder Sampling Rate Bias]

    N --> N1[Wheel Slip]
    N --> N2[Uneven Floor]
    N --> N3[Skidding in Turns]
    N --> N4[Floor Roughness]
    N --> N5[External Collisions]

    S -. Compensated by Calibration .-> Out[Corrected Odometry]
    N -. Modelled by Covariance Propagation .-> Out

    style Root fill:#222,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style S fill:#FFD9D9,stroke:#A33,color:#500
    style N fill:#D9E4FF,stroke:#36A,color:#014
    style Out fill:#D9FFD9,stroke:#3A6,color:#030
```

**Reading guide:** Systematic (red) errors are deterministic and corrected by calibration. Non-systematic (blue) errors are stochastic and require the **covariance propagation error model** developed in this module. Both branches converge to the corrected odometry output (green) that feeds the higher-level localisation stack.

## 4.3 Sequential Flow of the Covariance Update

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Enc as Wheel Encoders
    participant Kin as Kinematic Model
    participant Jac as Jacobian Module
    participant Cov as Covariance Engine
    participant Out as Pose Estimator

    Enc->>Kin: Send tick counts n_r, n_l
    Kin->>Kin: Compute x_k+1 and theta_k+1
    Kin->>Jac: Send x_k, u_k
    Jac->>Cov: Return F_k and gradU_k
    Cov->>Cov: Compute F_k P_k F_k transpose
    Cov->>Cov: Compute gradU_k Q_k gradU_k transpose
    Cov->>Cov: Sum to form P_k+1
    Cov-->>Out: Return P_k+1
    Kin-->>Out: Return x_k+1
    Out-->>Out: Render uncertainty ellipsoid
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Distinguish between systematic and non-systematic errors in odometry. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Systematic errors are deterministic, repeatable biases caused by imperfections in the robot's mechanical/kinematic configuration. They accumulate proportionally with travelled distance and can be reduced by calibration. Examples: (i) wheel diameter mismatch ($\Delta D \neq 0$), (ii) wheelbase error ($\Delta b \neq 0$).

Non-systematic errors are stochastic, unpredictable disturbances arising from the robot–environment interaction. They cannot be calibrated away and are modelled probabilistically. Examples: (i) wheel slip on a low-friction patch, (ii) uneven floor causing random heading drift.

*(Allocate 1.5 marks for definition + 1 mark for two examples of each category + 0.5 mark for the practical implication.)*

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Why is the pose uncertainty covariance matrix $P_{k}$ essential in a probabilistic localisation framework?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The covariance matrix $P_{k}$ encodes the second-order statistical information of the pose estimate — i.e. the spread and orientation of the uncertainty ellipsoid around the mean pose $\mathbf{x}_{k}$. It is essential because (i) it enables the EKF update step in the prediction-correction cycle, (ii) it allows the planner to inflate obstacles for safe navigation, and (iii) it provides the *information-theoretic* measure $\mathrm{tr}(P_{k})$ used in active localisation to choose the most informative action.

*(Allocate 1 mark for the role of $P_{k}$ in EKF, 1 mark for safety/inflation, 1 mark for active localisation.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question B — Option A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**Q: With the necessary kinematic equations and Jacobians, derive the complete error model for odometric position estimation of a differential-drive mobile robot. Discuss how the state and input Jacobians affect the growth of pose uncertainty over time.**

**Model Solution (14 marks, split 7 + 7):**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of the kinematic model and Jacobians (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Express wheel displacements from encoder ticks:

$$
\Delta s_{r} = 2\pi r_{r}\, n_{r} / N_{r}, \qquad \Delta s_{l} = 2\pi r_{l}\, n_{l} / N_{l}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Derive the mean translation $\Delta s = (\Delta s_{r}+\Delta s_{l})/2$ and incremental rotation $\Delta\theta = (\Delta s_{r}-\Delta s_{l})/b$ from the differential-drive kinematics.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Use the mid-heading integration scheme to obtain the pose update:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= x_{k} + \Delta s \cdot \cos(\theta_{k} + \Delta\theta/2) \\
y_{k+1} &= y_{k} + \Delta s \cdot \sin(\theta_{k} + \Delta\theta/2) \\
\theta_{k+1} &= \theta_{k} + \Delta\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute the state Jacobian $F_{k}$ by partial differentiation of $f$ with respect to $\mathbf{x}_{k}$ (the derivation in Section 3.2 gives the explicit 3×3 matrix). Note the off-diagonal coupling in the third column that depends on $\sin$ and $\cos$ of the mid-heading.

#### Part (b) — Covariance propagation and its interpretation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute the input Jacobian $\nabla u_{k}$ by partial differentiation of $f$ with respect to the control input $\mathbf{u}_{k} = (\Delta s_{r}, \Delta s_{l})^{T}$ (Section 3.3 gives the explicit 3×2 matrix).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Define the input-noise covariance $Q_{k} = \mathrm{diag}(\sigma_{r}^{2}, \sigma_{l}^{2})$ and apply the linearised error model:

$$
P_{k+1} = F_{k}\, P_{k}\, F_{k}^{T} + \nabla u_{k}\, Q_{k}\, \nabla u_{k}^{T}
$$

Show the matrix product step-by-step (as in Section 3.4).

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Discuss the *geometric growth* of pose uncertainty: the term $F_{k} P_{k} F_{k}^{T}$ propagates the *previous* uncertainty, and the off-diagonal $\sin(\cdot)$ and $\cos(\cdot)$ entries of $F_{k}$ cause the *ellipsoid to rotate* as the robot turns. The term $\nabla u_{k} Q_{k} \nabla u_{k}^{T}$ adds a *new* uncertainty proportional to encoder noise. After many cycles the ellipsoid elongates in the direction of travel — this is the open-loop signature of odometric error.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclude by stating that this is why **external sensors** (LIDAR, cameras, GPS) are required to *bound* $P_{k}$ via the EKF correction step (to be studied in ESE Module 3).

---

### Question B — Option B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Q: A differential-drive robot has wheelbase $b = 0.4$ m. At step $k$, the pose is $\mathbf{x}_{k} = (1.0 \text{ m}, 2.0 \text{ m}, \pi/4 \text{ rad})$ and the pose covariance is**

$$
P_{k} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.20 & 0.05 & 0.01 \\ 0.05 & 0.20 & 0.01 \\ 0.01 & 0.01 & 0.05 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**The encoders report $\Delta s_{r} = 0.205$ m and $\Delta s_{l} = 0.195$ m. The wheel-encoder noise covariance is $Q_{k} = \mathrm{diag}(0.0004, 0.0004)$ m$^2$.**

**(a)** Compute the updated pose $\mathbf{x}_{k+1}$.
**(b)** Compute the updated pose covariance $P_{k+1}$ using the linearised error model and comment on the growth and rotation of the uncertainty ellipsoid.

**Model Solution (14 marks, split 7 + 7):**

#### Part (a) — Pose update (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Compute mean translation and incremental rotation:

$$
\Delta s = \frac{0.205 + 0.195}{2} = 0.200 \text{ m}, \qquad \Delta\theta = \frac{0.205 - 0.195}{0.4} = 0.025 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Compute the mid-heading angle:

$$
\alpha = \theta_{k} + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} + 0.0125 \approx 0.7974 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply the kinematic update using $\cos\alpha \approx 0.7125$ and $\sin\alpha \approx 0.7017$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= 1.0 + 0.200 \cdot 0.7125 = 1.1425 \text{ m} \\
y_{k+1} &= 2.0 + 0.200 \cdot 0.7017 = 2.1403 \text{ m} \\
\theta_{k+1} &= \pi/4 + 0.025 \approx 0.8104 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Present the final state:

$$
\mathbf{x}_{k+1} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1.1425 \\ 2.1403 \\ 0.8104 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Part (b) — Covariance update and interpretation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute the state Jacobian $F_{k}$ using $\Delta s = 0.20$ and $\alpha = 0.7974$:

$$
F_{k} = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & -0.20 \cdot \sin(0.7974) \\
0 & 1 & \phantom{-}0.20 \cdot \cos(0.7974) \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & -0.1403 \\
0 & 1 & \phantom{-}0.1425 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the input Jacobian $\nabla u_{k}$ using $\Delta s / (2b) = 0.20 / 0.8 = 0.25$:

$$
\nabla u_{k} \approx \begin{bmatrix}
0.5\cos\alpha - 0.25\sin\alpha & 0.5\cos\alpha + 0.25\sin\alpha \\
0.5\sin\alpha + 0.25\cos\alpha & 0.5\sin\alpha - 0.25\cos\alpha \\
1/0.4 & -1/0.4
\end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix}
0.1808 & 0.5317 \\
0.5289 & -0.1762 \\
2.5 & -2.5
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the two contributions and sum:

- Compute $F_{k} P_{k} F_{k}^{T}$ (carry out the 3×3 multiplications explicitly).
- Compute $\nabla u_{k} Q_{k} \nabla u_{k}^{T} = 0.0004 \cdot \nabla u_{k} \nabla u_{k}^{T}$ (the 3×3 Gram matrix of $\nabla u_{k}$ scaled by 0.0004).
- Sum the two to obtain $P_{k+1}$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** **Commentary on the result.** Compare the diagonal entries of $P_{k+1}$ to those of $P_{k}$ — they must have **grown** (open-loop error). The off-diagonal $\sigma_{xy}$ term is **non-zero and has rotated** (relative to the initial $P_{k}$), reflecting the fact that the new heading couples the $x$ and $y$ uncertainties. The result is the *characteristic elongated ellipsoid* aligned with the direction of travel, confirming the analytical insight of Section 3.4.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Skipping the mid-heading approximation:** Writing $\cos(\theta_{k})$ instead of $\cos(\theta_{k}+\Delta\theta/2)$ costs 1 mark. The mid-heading is **second-order accurate** and is the KTU-board-expected form.
> 2. **Forgetting the cross-coupling in $F_{k}$:** The off-diagonal entries $-\Delta s \sin(\cdot)$ and $+\Delta s \cos(\cdot)$ in column 3 are **the most-skipped part** of the answer. They are *the* reason the uncertainty ellipsoid rotates with the robot's heading.
> 3. **Confusing the two Jacobians:** $F_{k}$ is **state** Jacobian (3×3, partial w.r.t. $\mathbf{x}$), $\nabla u_{k}$ is **input** Jacobian (3×2, partial w.r.t. $\mathbf{u}$). Swapping them is an instant 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Ignoring units in $Q_{k}$:** $Q_{k}$ must be in $\mathrm{m}^{2}$ and the input Jacobian entries are dimensionless, so $\nabla u_{k} Q_{k} \nabla u_{k}^{T}$ is in $\mathrm{m}^{2}$ (matching $P_{k+1}$'s $x,y$ units). Mentioning unit consistency is worth a half-mark in ESE.
> 5. **Dropping the input-noise contribution:** Writing $P_{k+1} = F_{k} P_{k} F_{k}^{T}$ only (forgetting the $\nabla u_{k} Q_{k} \nabla u_{k}^{T}$ term) loses 2–3 marks. The full form is **non-negotiable** at the 14-mark level.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **Two error classes** — systematic (calibration-handled) vs non-systematic (covariance-handled). Always quote one example of each.
- **State vector** $\mathbf{x}_{k} = (x, y, \theta)^{T}$; **input vector** $\mathbf{u}_{k} = (\Delta s_{r}, \Delta s_{l})^{T}$.
- **Mean translation** $\Delta s = (\Delta s_{r} + \Delta s_{l})/2$ ; **incremental rotation** $\Delta\theta = (\Delta s_{r} - \Delta s_{l})/b$.
- **Mid-heading integration** is the standard KTU-board form for the pose update.
- **State Jacobian** $F_{k}$ is $3 \times 3$ with the *third column* carrying $-\Delta s \sin\alpha$ and $+\Delta s \cos\alpha$.
- **Input Jacobian** $\nabla u_{k}$ is $3 \times 2$ with terms in $\cos\alpha$, $\sin\alpha$, $\Delta s/(2b)$ and $\pm 1/b$.
- **Master error-model equation:** $P_{k+1} = F_{k} P_{k} F_{k}^{T} + \nabla u_{k} Q_{k} \nabla u_{k}^{T}$.
- $Q_{k}$ is the **input-noise covariance** (typically diagonal if left/right encoder noises are assumed independent).
- **Uncertainty grows open-loop** with each odometry step — this is the *fundamental limitation* of dead reckoning.
- The **uncertainty ellipsoid rotates** as the robot turns, due to the off-diagonal coupling in $F_{k}$.
- The error model is the **predict step** of the EKF used in probabilistic localisation (links directly to ESE Module 3).
- **Units check:** $P$ in $[\mathrm{m}^{2}, \mathrm{m}^{2}, \mathrm{rad}^{2}]$; $Q$ in $\mathrm{m}^{2}$; $F$ dimensionless; $\nabla u$ in $[\mathrm{m}^{-1}]$ in the orientation row.
- The **trace** $\mathrm{tr}(P_{k})$ is the scalar "total uncertainty" used in active localisation.
- Always **show the matrix product** $F_{k} P_{k} F_{k}^{T}$ explicitly — partial credit is awarded for *visible* algebra.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Localization-Based Navigation versus Programmed Solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Localization-Based Navigation versus Programmed Solutions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Programmed Solutions (Map-Based / Pre-Planned Navigation)

**Formal Definition:**
A *Programmed Solution* (also called **Map-Based Navigation** or **Off-Line Path Planning**) is a navigation paradigm in which the robot operates on a **completely known, static, a-priori map** of the workspace. The trajectory is computed offline using deterministic graph-search or sampling-based algorithms, and the controller executes the path as an **open-loop or feed-forward command sequence** without actively re-estimating the robot's pose using exteroceptive sensors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Programmed solutions rely on the assumption that the world model is accurate, obstacles are static, and wheel-slip / actuation errors are negligible. They are the classical approach used in industrial manipulators and structured factory floors.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a delivery boy who has memorized the floor plan of a building, calculated the exact shortest route from the mail room to office 707, and then walks it with his eyes closed. If a chair is moved, or someone steps in his path, he will collide — because he is *not sensing* the world, he is *replaying* a script. That is a **Programmed Solution**.

### 1.2 Localization-Based Navigation (Sensor-Driven / Reactive Navigation)

**Formal Definition:**
*Localization-Based Navigation* is a closed-loop navigation strategy in which the robot continuously **estimates its own pose** (position and orientation) within a known or partially known map by fusing **proprioceptive data** (wheel encoders, IMU) with **exteroceptive measurements** (LIDAR, cameras, GPS, sonar, UWB) using probabilistic filters such as the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**, **Particle Filter**, or **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea:** The robot asks itself at every time step *"Where am I?"* before asking *"Where do I go next?"* — this is the **sense → localize → plan → act** cycle.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Now imagine the same delivery boy is wearing a smartwatch with GPS, a step counter, and a digital floor plan on his phone. As he walks, the watch re-tells him his coordinates, his map updates around him in real time, and he can re-route the moment a corridor is blocked. That is **Localization-Based Navigation** — adaptive, feedback-driven, and uncertainty-aware.

### 1.3 Why the Distinction Matters in Motion Planning

| Aspect | Programmed Solution | Localization-Based Navigation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Map requirement | **Fully known a-priori** | Known or partially known / SLAM-built |
| Sensor dependency | **None** (or proprioceptive only) | **High** (exteroceptive sensors mandatory) |
| Control nature | Open-loop / feed-forward | Closed-loop / feedback |
| Robustness to disturbance | **Low** | **High** |
| Computational demand at runtime | **Low** (planning done offline) | **High** (online probabilistic inference) |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

* **Standard localization frequency:** $\mathbf{10\text{ Hz} - 100\text{ Hz}}$ for indoor mobile robots.
* **Typical pose uncertainty covariance:** $\mathbf{\Sigma_{xx}, \Sigma_{yy}, \Sigma_{\theta\theta}}$ in units of $\text{m}^2$ and $\text{rad}^2$.
* **Wheel encoder resolution:** $\mathbf{N}$ pulses per revolution (typical $\mathbf{500 - 20000}$).
* **Acceptable path-tracking error:** $\mathbf{e_{\max} \le 5\text{ cm}}$ for indoor service robots, $\mathbf{\le 30\text{ cm}}$ for outdoor AGVs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Breakdown

**Programmed Solution Pipeline (4 logical stages):**

1. **World Modeling Stage** — The workspace is represented as an **Occupancy Grid Map** or **Configuration Space (C-space)** obstacle map. Every cell or configuration is binary-classified as *free* or *blocked*.
2. **Off-Line Planning Stage** — A graph-search or sampling algorithm finds a feasible (and usually optimal) sequence of waypoints $\mathbf{W} = \{w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n\}$.
3. **Trajectory Smoothing Stage** — A time-parameterized reference trajectory is generated: $x_d(t),\ y_d(t),\ \theta_d(t)$.
4. **Feed-Forward Execution Stage** — A low-level controller (typically PID) tracks $x_d(t)$ without re-localizing.

**Localization-Based Navigation Pipeline (5 logical stages, recursive):**

1. **Prediction Step** — Use the kinematic motion model $g(u_t, \mu_{t-1})$ to propagate the prior pose belief $\bar{\mu}_t$ and covariance $\bar{\Sigma}_t$.
2. **Measurement Acquisition Step** — Read exteroceptive sensors: $z_t = \text{LIDAR scan},\ \text{camera features},\ \text{GPS fix},\ \text{etc.}$
3. **Update Step** — Compute the innovation $\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$ and apply the Kalman gain $K_t$ to correct the belief.
4. **Re-Planning Step (Optional)** — If localization confidence is high and a new goal is set, run a local planner.
5. **Actuation Step** — Drive the motors with the corrected pose used as feedback.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Expression | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $p_t$ | Robot pose at time $t$ | $p_t = (x_t,\ y_t,\ \theta_t)^\top$ | $\text{m},\ \text{m},\ \text{rad}$ |
| $u_t$ | Control input (linear + angular velocity) | $u_t = (v_t,\ \omega_t)^\top$ | $\text{m/s},\ \text{rad/s}$ |
| $g(\cdot)$ | Non-linear motion model | $\mu_t = g(u_t,\ \mu_{t-1})$ | — |
| $G_t$ | Jacobian of motion model w.r.t. pose | $G_t = \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial \mu}\bigg\vert_{\mu_{t-1}}$ | $3 \times 3$ |
| $V_t$ | Jacobian of motion model w.r.t. control | $V_t = \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial u}\bigg\vert_{u_t}$ | $3 \times 2$ |
| $h(\cdot)$ | Measurement prediction function | $\hat{z}_t = h(\mu_t)$ | varies |
| $H_t$ | Jacobian of measurement model | $H_t = \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial \mu}\bigg\vert_{\mu_t}$ | $m \times 3$ |
| $K_t$ | Kalman gain | $K_t = \Sigma_t H_t^\top (H_t \Sigma_t H_t^\top + Q_t)^{-1}$ | $3 \times m$ |
| $Q_t$ | Measurement noise covariance | $Q_t = \mathbb{E}[w_t w_t^\top]$ | sensor-specific |
| $R_t$ | Process noise covariance | $R_t = \mathbb{E}[v_t v_t^\top]$ | $\text{m}^2,\ \text{rad}^2$ |
| $\Delta s$ | Linear displacement per time-step | $\Delta s = v_t \cdot \Delta t$ | $\text{m}$ |
| $\Delta \theta$ | Angular displacement per time-step | $\Delta \theta = \omega_t \cdot \Delta t$ | $\text{rad}$ |
| $L$ | Arc length of a planned path | $L = \displaystyle\int_0^{T} \sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2}\, dt$ | $\text{m}$ |
| $\kappa$ | Path curvature | $\kappa = \dfrac{\vert \dot{x}\ddot{y} - \dot{y}\ddot{x} \vert}{(\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2)^{3/2}}$ | $\text{m}^{-1}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The process covariance $R_t$ and measurement covariance $Q_t$ are the *tuning knobs* of any localization filter. Too-small $R_t \Rightarrow$ overconfident filter; too-large $R_t \Rightarrow$ filter ignores odometry and drifts.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Programmed Solutions** dominate **industrial robotics** (KUKA, FANUC welding arms), where the cell is fenced, the part is fixed, and repeatability is measured in **$\mathbf{0.02\ \text{mm}}$**. Examples: automotive welding lines, PCB pick-and-place.
* **Localization-Based Navigation** dominates **field robotics** — autonomous cars (Waymo, Tesla), planetary rovers (NASA's Perseverance), domestic vacuums (iRobot Roomba), and warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva / Proteus).

### 2.4 The Hybrid Reality

> [!NOTE]
> Modern systems are **almost never** purely one or the other. A state-of-the-art autonomous vehicle uses a **programmed global route** (from A* on a road-graph) combined with **localization-based local re-planning** (using EKF + LIDAR) in a hierarchical control architecture. KTU examiners love asking about this hybrid paradigm.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Unicycle Kinematic Model (Used in Both Paradigms)

The unicycle (differential-drive) robot is the canonical mobile platform. Its pose evolves as:

$$
p_t = \begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ y_t \\ \theta_t \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\dot{p}_t = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_t \\ \dot{y}_t \\ \dot{\theta}_t \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} v_t \cos\theta_t \\ v_t \sin\theta_t \\ \omega_t \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1.** Integrate over one discrete control interval $\Delta t$ assuming $v_t$ and $\omega_t$ are constant.

**Step 2.** For the orientation: $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} + \omega_t \Delta t$.

**Step 3.** For the position, average the heading over the interval (mid-point integration): the average heading is $\theta_{t-1} + \tfrac{1}{2}\omega_t \Delta t$.

**Step 4.** The linear displacement is $\Delta s = v_t \Delta t$.

**Step 5.** Project $\Delta s$ onto the average heading to get the incremental $x$ and $y$ updates.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_t &= x_{t-1} + \Delta s \cos\!\left(\theta_{t-1} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\omega_t \Delta t\right) \\
y_t &= y_{t-1} + \Delta s \sin\!\left(\theta_{t-1} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\omega_t \Delta t\right) \\
\theta_t &= \theta_{t-1} + \omega_t \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **odometry update equation**, used identically in both paradigms — but in a *programmed* solution it is used *deterministically* (with planned $v_t,\omega_t$), whereas in a *localization-based* solution it is used *probabilistically* with added Gaussian noise $\sim \mathcal{N}(0,\,R_t)$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) Prediction Step

**Given:** prior belief $\mu_{t-1}$ (mean pose) and $\Sigma_{t-1}$ (covariance). Control input $u_t = (v_t,\omega_t)^\top$ corrupted by zero-mean noise $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\,R_t)$.

**Step 1.** Propagate the mean through the non-linear motion model $g$:
$$
\bar{\mu}_t = g(u_t,\mu_{t-1})
$$
This is a direct substitution of $(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$ into the odometry equations of §3.1.

**Step 2.** Linearize the motion model around $\mu_{t-1}$ using the Taylor series expansion to first order:
$$
g(u_t + \delta u,\ \mu_{t-1} + \delta\mu) \approx g(u_t,\mu_{t-1}) + G_t\,\delta\mu + V_t\,\delta u
$$
where $G_t$ and $V_t$ are Jacobians.

**Step 3.** Compute the Jacobians explicitly. Let $\alpha = \theta_{t-1} + \tfrac{1}{2}\omega_t\Delta t$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
G_t &= \frac{\partial g}{\partial \mu}\bigg\vert_{\mu_{t-1}} = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & -\Delta s \sin\alpha \\
0 & 1 & \ \Delta s \cos\alpha \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
V_t &= \frac{\partial g}{\partial u}\bigg\vert_{u_t} = \begin{bmatrix}
\tfrac{1}{2}\Delta t \cos\alpha - \dfrac{\Delta s \sin\alpha}{2\omega_t} & \dfrac{\Delta s \sin\alpha}{2\omega_t} \\
\tfrac{1}{2}\Delta t \sin\alpha + \dfrac{\Delta s \cos\alpha}{2\omega_t} & -\dfrac{\Delta s \cos\alpha}{2\omega_t} \\
0 & \Delta t
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$
(For the limiting case $\omega_t \to 0$, $V_t$ collapses to its non-holonomic form using L'Hôpital's rule.)

**Step 4.** Propagate the uncertainty. For any random vector $Y = AX + B$ with $\text{Cov}(X) = S$, we have $\text{Cov}(Y) = A S A^\top$. Apply this to the two noise sources:
$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^\top \;+\; V_t R_t V_t^\top
$$

This is the **EKF Prediction Covariance Equation** — the cornerstone of every localization-based navigation system.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: EKF Update (Correction) Step

**Given:** measurement $z_t$ (e.g., range to a known landmark) with sensor model $h(\mu_t)$ corrupted by noise $w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\,Q_t)$.

**Step 1.** Compute the *innovation* (measurement residual):
$$
\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)
$$

**Step 2.** The innovation covariance is the sum of the propagated pose-uncertainty-in-measurement-space and the sensor noise:
$$
S_t = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top + Q_t
$$

**Step 3.** The Kalman gain $K_t$ weights the innovation against the prior:
$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top\, S_t^{-1}
$$

**Step 4.** Correct the mean and the covariance:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_t &= \bar{\mu}_t + K_t\,\nu_t \\
\Sigma_t &= (I - K_t H_t)\,\bar{\Sigma}_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5.** Interpret the gain:
* If $Q_t \to 0$ (perfect sensor) $\Rightarrow K_t \to H_t^{-1}$ (trust measurement fully).
* If $Q_t \to \infty$ (useless sensor) $\Rightarrow K_t \to 0$ (ignore measurement).

This explains *why* the EKF is fundamentally a **sensor-fusion optimizer**, not just a filter.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Performance Metric | Programmed Solution | Localization-Based Navigation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mean position error** $\mathbb{E}[\vert e \vert]$ | Grows **unboundedly** with slip / drift | Bounded by $\text{tr}(\Sigma_t)$ |
| **Path optimality** | Globally optimal (if planner is complete) | Locally optimal (re-plans online) |
| **Re-planning frequency** | $\mathbf{0\text{ Hz}}$ (offline) | $\mathbf{1 - 10\text{ Hz}}$ typical |
| **Memory footprint** | $O(\text{path length})$ for waypoints | $O(\text{map} + \text{particles})$ |
| **Failure mode** | Collision on model mismatch | Lost or kidnapped-robot recovery |
| **Computational complexity** | $O(V \log V)$ for A* once | $O(N_{\text{particles}} \cdot m)$ per step |
| **Sensor cost** | $\mathbf{\$0 - \$50}$ (encoders only) | $\mathbf{\$500 - \$75000}$ (LIDAR/stereo/GPS) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Programmed Solution Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Static Map Input] --> B1[Off-Line Planner - A star or RRT]
    A2[Start and Goal Poses] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Optimal Waypoint Sequence]
    C1 --> D1[Trajectory Smoother]
    D1 --> E1[Time Parameterized Reference x d of t, y d of t, theta d of t]
    E1 --> F1[PID Tracking Controller]
    F1 --> G1[Robot Actuator Commands]
    G1 --> H1[Robot in Known Environment]
    H1 -.no feedback to planner.-> C1

    stepA[Static Map Input]:::inputNode
    stepB[Off-Line Planner]:::processNode
    stepC[Waypoint Sequence]:::dataNode
    stepD[Trajectory Smoother]:::processNode
    stepE[Reference Trajectory]:::dataNode
    stepF[PID Controller]:::processNode
    stepG[Actuator Commands]:::outputNode
    stepH[Robot]:::outputNode

    classDef inputNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef processNode fill:#fff7e6,stroke:#d48806,color:#000
    classDef dataNode fill:#e6f7ff,stroke:#096dd9,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#d9f7be,stroke:#389e0d,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Localization-Based Navigation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Wheel Encoders and IMU] --> nodeB[Proprioceptive Reading u t]
    nodeC[LIDAR and Camera and GPS] --> nodeD[Exteroceptive Measurement z t]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Prediction Step - g and G t and V t]
    nodeP[Prior Belief mu t-1 and Sigma t-1] --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[Predicted Belief mu bar t and Sigma bar t]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Measurement Model h and H t]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Innovation nu t and Kalman Gain K t]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Corrected Belief mu t and Sigma t]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Local Path Planner - Dynamic Window]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Velocity Commands v t and omega t]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Robot]
    nodeL --> nodeA
    nodeL --> nodeC

    stepX[Proprioception]:::sensorNode
    stepY[Exteroception]:::sensorNode
    stepZ[Prediction]:::filterNode
    stepW[Update]:::filterNode
    stepV[Replan]:::planNode
    stepU[Robot]:::robotNode

    classDef sensorNode fill:#ffe7ba,stroke:#d46b08,color:#000
    classDef filterNode fill:#ffd6e7,stroke:#c41d7f,color:#000
    classDef planNode fill:#d9f7be,stroke:#389e0d,color:#000
    classDef robotNode fill:#bae7ff,stroke:#1890ff,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart: When to Use Which Paradigm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qStart[Start] --> q1{Is the environment fully known and static?}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Is wheel slip and disturbance negligible?}
    q2 -- Yes --> q3[Use Programmed Solution - Industrial arm, factory AGV]
    q2 -- No --> q4[Use Hybrid - Programmed global path with localization-based local tracking]
    q1 -- No --> q5{Do you need a map too?}
    q5 -- Yes --> q6[Full SLAM plus Localization-Based Navigation]
    q5 -- No --> q7[Pure Reactive Navigation with localization only]
    q3 --> qEnd[Done]
    q4 --> qEnd
    q6 --> qEnd
    q7 --> qEnd

    step1[Known and Static]:::yesNode
    step2[Unknown or Dynamic]:::noNode
    step3[Choose Paradigm]:::choiceNode
    step4[Output]:::finalNode

    classDef yesNode fill:#d9f7be,stroke:#389e0d,color:#000
    classDef noNode fill:#ffccc7,stroke:#cf1322,color:#000
```
