<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between programmed solutions and localization-based navigation in mobile robots. Highlight any two key differences in terms of sensor dependency and control architecture.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Feature | Programmed Solution | Localization-Based Navigation |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Sensor Dependency** | Requires **no exteroceptive sensors**; uses only encoders or no sensors at all. | Requires **LIDAR, camera, GPS or sonar** for exteroceptive measurement. |
| 2 | **Control Architecture** | **Open-loop / feed-forward** execution of a pre-computed trajectory. | **Closed-loop / feedback** control driven by online pose estimates. |
| 3 | **Robustness to Disturbance** | **Poor** — collides if model mismatches reality. | **High** — re-plans around newly sensed obstacles. |

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark per correct row; a diagram/flow-chart gets bonus.)* *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of the Kalman filter in localization-based robot navigation? Why is the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) preferred over a linear Kalman filter for mobile robot localization?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Kalman Filter (KF)** is a recursive Bayesian estimator that fuses **noisy sensor measurements** with a **noisy motion model** to produce an **optimal (minimum-variance) estimate** of the robot's pose $p_t = (x_t,y_t,\theta_t)^\top$. It runs in two stages:

* **Prediction** — propagate the belief using the motion model $g(u_t,\mu_{t-1})$.
* **Update** — correct the belief using the measurement $z_t$ via the innovation $\nu_t = z_t - h(\mu_t)$ and the Kalman gain $K_t$.

The **EKF** is preferred because:
* A mobile robot's motion model $g(\cdot)$ is **non-linear** (sin/cos of heading).
* The measurement model $h(\cdot)$ (e.g., range-bearing to a landmark) is also **non-linear**.
* The EKF **linearises** these models via Jacobian matrices $G_t$ and $H_t$, extending the KF to non-linear domains. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **odometry update equation** for a differential-drive mobile robot, clearly stating the assumptions used. Show that the discrete-time update reduces to a straight-line motion when $\omega_t = 0$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a labelled block diagram, explain the **EKF-based localization pipeline** of a mobile robot navigating in a known map. Write down the **prediction** and **update** equations with all Jacobians explicitly defined. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution A:**

**(a) Odometry Derivation — 7 marks**

*Assumption 1:* The robot is a unicycle/differential-drive platform with state $p_t = (x_t, y_t, \theta_t)^\top$.
*Assumption 2:* The control input $u_t = (v_t, \omega_t)^\top$ is held constant over the sampling interval $\Delta t$.

**Step 1:** Continuous-time kinematic model. The velocity in the world frame is the body-frame velocity rotated by $\theta_t$:
$$
\dot{x}_t = v_t \cos\theta_t,\quad \dot{y}_t = v_t \sin\theta_t,\quad \dot{\theta}_t = \omega_t
$$

**Step 2:** Integrate orientation: $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} + \omega_t \Delta t$.

**Step 3:** For position, use mid-point (average heading) integration:
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_t &= x_{t-1} + v_t \Delta t \cos\!\left(\theta_{t-1} + \tfrac{1}{2}\omega_t \Delta t\right) \\
y_t &= y_{t-1} + v_t \Delta t \sin\!\left(\theta_{t-1} + \tfrac{1}{2}\omega_t \Delta t\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Let $\Delta s = v_t \Delta t$ and $\alpha = \theta_{t-1} + \tfrac{1}{2}\omega_t \Delta t$:
$$
x_t = x_{t-1} + \Delta s \cos\alpha,\quad y_t = y_{t-1} + \Delta s \sin\alpha
$$

**Step 5:** Special case $\omega_t = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha = \theta_{t-1}$ and the robot moves in a **straight line** of length $\Delta s$ along heading $\theta_{t-1}$:
$$
x_t = x_{t-1} + \Delta s \cos\theta_{t-1},\quad y_t = y_{t-1} + \Delta s \sin\theta_{t-1}
$$

This is the desired straight-line reduction. *(7 marks)*

---

**(b) EKF Localization Block Diagram & Equations — 7 marks**

*Block diagram (draw and label the following 6 blocks):*

```
[Encoders/IMU] --> [Motion Model g()] --> [Predicted Pose mu_bar_t]
                                          |
[Map] --> [Measurement Model h()] <---- [Innovation nu_t]
                       |                       |
                  [Kalman Gain K_t] <----------+
                       |
              [Corrected Pose mu_t and Sigma_t]
                       |
                  [Local Planner]
                       |
                 [Motor Commands]
```

*Equations:*

**Prediction:**
$$
\bar{\mu}_t = g(u_t,\mu_{t-1}), \quad \bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^\top + V_t R_t V_t^\top
$$

**Update:**
$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top(H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top + Q_t)^{-1},\quad
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t(z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)),\quad
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

**Jacobian definitions:**
$$
G_t = \frac{\partial g}{\partial \mu},\quad V_t = \frac{\partial g}{\partial u},\quad H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial \mu}
$$

*Why EKF?* Both $g$ and $h$ are non-linear (sin/cos terms), so Jacobians are first-order Taylor approximations. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **programmed solutions** and **localization-based navigation** along the dimensions of (i) map knowledge, (ii) sensor suite, (iii) computational cost, (iv) robustness to dynamic obstacles, and (v) typical application domain. Use a comparison table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive robot uses wheel encoders with resolution $N = 2000$ pulses/rev and wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m. The robot issues the control $u = (v = 0.4\text{ m/s},\ \omega = 0.2\text{ rad/s})$ for $\Delta t = 1$ s starting from pose $(0, 0, 0)^\top$. Compute the final pose using the odometry update equation. Also calculate the **arc length** of the trajectory and its **path curvature** $\kappa$ assuming the motion is purely circular. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution B:**

**(a) Comparison Table — 7 marks**

| Dimension | Programmed Solution | Localization-Based Navigation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) Map knowledge | **Fully known a-priori** static map | Known or built online via SLAM |
| (ii) Sensor suite | Encoders only or **no sensors** | LIDAR + camera + IMU + GPS |
| (iii) Computational cost | **Low** (offline planning) | **High** (online probabilistic inference) |
| (iv) Robustness to dynamic obstacles | **Very low** | **High** (re-plans online) |
| (v) Application | Industrial arms, factory AGVs | Autonomous cars, planetary rovers |

*(Award 7 × 1 mark = 7 marks, one per row, with clear sentence explanations.)* *(7 marks)*

---

**(b) Numerical Odometry Problem — 7 marks**

**Given:** $v = 0.4$ m/s, $\omega = 0.2$ rad/s, $\Delta t = 1$ s, initial pose $p_0 = (0, 0, 0)^\top$.

**Step 1:** Compute displacements.
$$
\Delta s = v \Delta t = 0.4 \times 1 = 0.4\text{ m},\quad \Delta\theta = \omega \Delta t = 0.2 \times 1 = 0.2\text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the mid-heading.
$$
\alpha = \theta_0 + \tfrac{1}{2}\Delta\theta = 0 + 0.1 = 0.1\text{ rad}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply odometry update.
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= 0 + 0.4 \cos(0.1) = 0.4 \times 0.99500 = 0.3980\text{ m} \\
y_1 &= 0 + 0.4 \sin(0.1) = 0.4 \times 0.09983 = 0.03993\text{ m} \\
\theta_1 &= 0 + 0.2 = 0.2\text{ rad} \approx 11.46^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final pose:** $p_1 = (0.398,\ 0.040,\ 0.200)^\top$.

**Step 4:** Arc length of the trajectory. For a circular arc of radius $R = v/\omega$:
$$
R = \frac{0.4}{0.2} = 2.0\text{ m}
$$
$$
L = R \cdot \Delta\theta = 2.0 \times 0.2 = 0.4\text{ m}
$$
(This matches $\Delta s = v\Delta t$, as expected for a constant-$v$ circular arc.)

**Step 5:** Curvature of a circular path is the inverse of the radius:
$$
\kappa = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{2.0} = 0.5\text{ m}^{-1}
$$

**Step 6 (Bonus — encoder verification):** Distance per pulse $= 2\pi r/N = 2\pi(0.05)/2000 = 1.57 \times 10^{-4}$ m. The robot travels $0.4$ m, requiring $\approx 2546$ encoder pulses. *(7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — Read Before Writing the Exam:**
> 1. **Confusing "Programming" with "Planning".** A "Programmed Solution" in the KTU syllabus means *pre-planned offline navigation*, **not** "writing code". Do not write about software languages.
> 2. **Skipping the Jacobian derivation.** In EKF questions, KTU examiners award 2 of 7 marks *only* for the explicit $G_t$ and $H_t$ matrices. Students who write only the high-level EKF equations **lose 30–40 % of the marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting the assumption of constant control input.** The odometry equation is valid only under the assumption $u_t = (v_t,\omega_t)^\top$ is constant over $\Delta t$. State this or lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Units mismatch.** Writing curvature in rad/m or arc length in seconds costs 1 mark. Always state units.
> 5. **Treating "SLAM" and "Localization" as synonyms.** SLAM = *Simultaneous Localization **and** Mapping* (no a-priori map). Pure localization assumes the map is given. Examiners dock marks for conflating them.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Programmed Solution** = *off-line planner + open-loop execution + known static map + no exteroception*.
- **Localization-Based Navigation** = *online probabilistic filter (EKF/MCL) + closed-loop control + exteroceptive sensors*.
- The **odometry update** is the shared kinematic backbone: $x_t = x_{t-1} + v\Delta t \cos(\theta_{t-1} + \tfrac{1}{2}\omega\Delta t)$.
- The **EKF** runs **Prediction** ($g,\ G_t,\ V_t,\ R_t$) then **Update** ($h,\ H_t,\ Q_t,\ K_t$).
- **Process noise** $R_t$ accounts for wheel slip; **measurement noise** $Q_t$ accounts for sensor imperfection.
- **Curvature** $\kappa = 1/R$ where $R = v/\omega$ for a unicycle.
- **Arc length** $L = R\,\Delta\theta = v\Delta t$ for a constant-velocity circular arc.
- **Hybrid architectures** dominate modern robotics: global programmed planner + local localization-based tracker.
- The **EKF assumes Gaussian noise** — for non-Gaussian ambiguity, switch to **Particle Filters (Monte Carlo Localization)**.
- **Industrial manipulators** $\Rightarrow$ Programmed; **Mobile robots in human spaces** $\Rightarrow$ Localization-based.
- **Always state units** (m, rad, m/s, rad/s, m$^{-1}$) — KTU valuation penalises unitless answers.
- **Draw the block diagram** whenever a question asks for a "system" — it is worth $\ge 2$ marks even if your equations have a typo.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Localization-Based Navigation versus Programmed Solutions**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Belief Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Belief Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In **Robot Motion Planning** under the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), a **Belief** is defined as the robot's internal probabilistic estimate of its own state (pose, location, configuration) and/or the state of the environment, given all past control actions and sensor observations.

Formally, the **belief state** at time $t$, denoted $b_t$, is a probability distribution over the state space $X$:

$$b_t(x) \;=\; p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

where:
- $x_t \in X$ is the true state of the robot at time $t$.
- $z_{1:t} = \{z_1, z_2, \dots, z_t\}$ is the sequence of sensor measurements.
- $u_{1:t} = \{u_1, u_2, \dots, u_t\}$ is the sequence of control actions.
- $b_t$ is a valid probability density function (for continuous spaces) or probability mass function (for discrete spaces):

$$\int_{x \in X} b_t(x)\, dx \;=\; 1 \quad \text{(continuous)} \qquad \sum_{x \in X} b_t(x) \;=\; 1 \quad \text{(discrete)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Belief Representation** is the *mathematical encoding scheme* used to store, update, and reason about this probability distribution $b_t$. It is NOT the same as the belief itself — it is the *data structure* chosen to represent it.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Blind Navigator"

Imagine you are **blindfolded inside an unfamiliar house** and must reach the kitchen. Every few seconds, you tap your cane (sensor) and shuffle forward (action). You maintain a **mental map of probabilities**: *"I think I am 60% at the hallway, 30% at the bedroom door, 10% near the stairs."*

This mental map is your **belief**. The way you store it — whether as a smooth cloud (Gaussian), a list of weighted guesses (particles), or a grid of squares (occupancy grid) — is your **belief representation**. When a planner says "go to the kitchen," it must do so *with respect to your uncertain belief*, not your (unknown) true position. This is the heart of **belief-space planning**.

### 1.3 Why Belief Representation Matters in KTU Syllabus

> [!NOTE]
> Module 2 of OEBRT833 focuses on planning **under uncertainty**. Without a tractable belief representation, the planning problem is computationally intractable (curse of dimensionality). The choice of representation determines:
> - Time complexity of belief updates (filtering step).
> - Memory footprint for storing $b_t$.
> - Expressiveness (unimodal vs. multimodal beliefs).
> - Suitability for non-linear / non-Gaussian systems.

### 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Notation)

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $x_t$ | True state at time $t$ |
| $b_t$ | Belief (distribution) at time $t$ |
| $u_t$ | Control input at time $t$ |
| $z_t$ | Observation at time $t$ |
| $X$ | State space (continuous or discrete) |
| $Bel(X)$ | Belief space (space of all probability distributions over $X$) |
| $\eta$ | Normalization constant |
| $p(x \mid y)$ | Conditional probability of $x$ given $y$ |

> [!TIP]
> A **state space** $X$ can be continuous (e.g., $X = \mathbb{R}^2 \times [0, 2\pi)$ for a planar robot) or discrete (e.g., a topological graph of rooms). The belief space $Bel(X)$ is **always infinite-dimensional** when $X$ is continuous, which is why tractable representations are essential.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Belief Representation

The KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 2 groups belief representations into four families. Each balances **expressiveness**, **computational cost**, and **approximation fidelity**.

#### Pillar 1 — Parametric / Continuous Representations (Kalman Family)

The belief is assumed to follow a known parametric form, almost always a **univariate or multivariate Gaussian**:

$$b_t(x) \;=\; \mathcal{N}(x; \mu_t, \Sigma_t) \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2\pi)^n \vert \Sigma_t \vert}}\,\exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}(x-\mu_t)^{\!\top}\Sigma_t^{-1}(x-\mu_t)\right)$$

- **Mean vector** $\mu_t \in \mathbb{R}^n$ — the most likely state.
- **Covariance matrix** $\Sigma_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ — encodes uncertainty shape and correlations.
- **Sub-variants**:
  - *Kalman Filter (KF)* — linear Gaussian systems.
  - *Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)* — local linearization of non-linearities.
  - *Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF)* — sigma-point propagation.
  - *Iterated EKF (IEKF)* — re-linearization at the measurement update.

**Pros:** Closed-form updates, O($n^2$) memory, optimal for linear-Gaussian systems.
**Cons:** Cannot represent **multimodal** beliefs (e.g., global localization ambiguities).

#### Pillar 2 — Non-parametric / Particle Representations

The belief is approximated by a finite set of $M$ weighted samples (particles):

$$b_t(x) \;\approx\; \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{(i)}\,\delta(x - x_t^{(i)})$$

- $x_t^{(i)}$ — the $i$-th particle (hypothesized state).
- $w_t^{(i)} \geq 0$ — its importance weight, normalized so $\sum_i w_t^{(i)} = 1$.
- $\delta(\cdot)$ — Dirac delta (in continuous form); in practice, particles are *point hypotheses* stored in a list.

**Algorithms:** Particle Filter (Sequential Monte Carlo), Monte Carlo Localization (MCL), Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filters.

**Pros:** Handles **arbitrary** (multimodal, non-Gaussian) distributions, simple to implement.
**Cons:** Computational cost grows with $M$; particle deprivation in high dimensions.

#### Pillar 3 — Grid / Discretized Representations

The state space $X$ is partitioned into a finite grid $\mathcal{G} = \{g_1, g_2, \dots, g_N\}$, and the belief is stored as a discrete probability vector:

$$b_t \;=\; [b_t(g_1),\; b_t(g_2),\; \dots,\; b_t(g_N)]^{\!\top}, \qquad \sum_{j=1}^{N} b_t(g_j) = 1$$

- **Histogram Filter** — exact for finite state spaces.
- **Occupancy Grid Map** — the environment is decomposed into cells, each storing $P(\text{occupied} \mid z_{1:t})$.
- **Multi-Resolution / Octree Grids** — adaptive cell sizing for memory efficiency.

**Pros:** Conceptually simple, supports exact (non-parametric) updates.
**Cons:** Memory scales exponentially with state-space dimension (**curse of dimensionality**). A 3-D grid of 100 cells/side needs $10^6$ cells.

#### Pillar 4 — Topological / Hybrid Representations

The state is abstracted to a **discrete set of distinct places** (nodes in a graph), with edges encoding adjacency.

- **Topological Map** — nodes = distinctive places, edges = navigable transitions.
- **Hybrid Topological-Metric** — each node stores a *local* metric belief (often a small Gaussian).
- **Semantic Belief** — associates natural-language or object-level labels with places.

**Pros:** Compact, scalable to large environments, robust to perceptual aliasing.
**Cons:** Requires a reliable place-recognition front-end; cannot represent pose uncertainty within a node at the metric level.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **examination-critical**. Master the Bayes filter, Kalman update, and particle weighting equations.

| # | Concept | Equation | Remarks |
|---|---------|----------|---------|
| 1 | Belief definition | $b_t(x) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Posterior over state |
| 2 | Bayes filter — prediction | $\bar{b}_t(x) = \int p(x \mid u_t, x')\,b_{t-1}(x')\,dx'$ | Chapman–Kolmogorov |
| 3 | Bayes filter — measurement update | $b_t(x) = \eta\,p(z_t \mid x)\,\bar{b}_t(x)$ | $\eta$ = normalizer |
| 4 | Normalizer | $\eta = \left[\int p(z_t \mid x')\bar{b}_t(x')\,dx'\right]^{-1}$ | Ensures $\int b_t = 1$ |
| 5 | Markov assumption | $p(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ | Conditional independence |
| 6 | Sensor model | $p(z_t \mid x, x')$ (range) or $p(z_t \mid x)$ (pose-only) | Given by robot |
| 7 | Motion model | $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ | Stochastic transition |
| 8 | Gaussian density | $\mathcal{N}(x;\mu,\Sigma) = \det(2\pi\Sigma)^{-1/2}\exp(-\tfrac{1}{2}(x-\mu)^{\!\top}\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu))$ | $n \times n$ covariance |
| 9 | Kalman prediction | $\bar{\mu}_t = A\mu_{t-1} + Bu_t, \quad \bar{\Sigma}_t = A\Sigma_{t-1}A^{\!\top} + R$ | $R$ = process noise |
| 10 | Kalman update | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H^{\!\top}(H\bar{\Sigma}_t H^{\!\top} + Q)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain |
| 11 | Kalman correction | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t(z_t - H\bar{\mu}_t), \quad \Sigma_t = (I - K_t H)\bar{\Sigma}_t$ | Posterior params |
| 12 | Particle weight (SIR) | $w_t^{(i)} \propto w_{t-1}^{(i)}\,p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})$ | Likelihood weighting |
| 13 | Effective sample size | $N_{\text{eff}} = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{M}(w_t^{(i)})^2\right)^{-1}$ | Resample if $< M/2$ |
| 14 | Information form | $\Omega_t = \Sigma_t^{-1}, \quad \xi_t = \Sigma_t^{-1}\mu_t$ | Used in SLAM |
| 15 | Belief entropy | $H(b_t) = -\int b_t(x)\log b_t(x)\,dx$ | Uncertainty scalar |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exams, the examiner will often give you a 1-D linear system and ask you to perform a single Kalman prediction + update step. Memorize formulas 9–11 verbatim.

### 2.3 The Bayes Filter as the Mother Equation

All belief representations are **approximations of the recursive Bayes filter**. Choosing a representation means choosing *how* to compute formulas 2 and 3:

- **KF/EKF/UKF** — represent $b_{t-1}$ as a Gaussian, propagate analytically.
- **Particle filter** — represent $b_{t-1}$ as samples, propagate by sampling and weighting.
- **Histogram filter** — represent $b_{t-1}$ on a grid, propagate by matrix multiplication.
- **Topological** — represent $b_{t-1}$ as a categorical distribution over nodes, propagate by graph traversal.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Belief Representation |
|--------|--------------------------------------|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Localizing in HD maps using particle filters over lane graphs |
| **Warehouse Robots (AMR)** | Multi-modal belief over aisle/intersection identity |
| **Aerial Swarms (UAV)** | EKF-based pose estimation fused with GPS + IMU |
| **Subsea AUVs** | Particle filter localization without GPS |
| **Surgical Robots** | Gaussian belief for sub-millimeter tissue tracking |
| **Self-Driving SLAM** | Hybrid topological-metric maps (e.g., Google Cartographer) |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Bayes Filter (Recursive Belief Update)

This is the **canonical derivation** every KTU student must be able to reproduce.

**Goal:** Derive $b_t(x) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ from $b_{t-1}(x)$.

**Step 1 — Start from the definition and apply the measurement model.**

$$b_t(x) \;=\; p(x_t \mid z_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

Apply the definition of conditional probability:

$$\boxed{\;p(A \mid B) \;=\; \frac{p(A, B)}{p(B)}\;}$$

So:

$$p(x_t \mid z_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})\;p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Markov assumption (sensor memoryless).**

If the sensor is Markov, the current measurement depends only on the current state:

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; p(z_t \mid x_t)$$

Substituting:

$$b_t(x) \;=\; \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t)\;p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the normalizer $\eta$.**

The denominator does not depend on $x_t$, so it is a scalar normalizer:

$$\eta \;=\; \left[p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})\right]^{-1}$$

Hence:

$$b_t(x) \;=\; \eta\, p(z_t \mid x)\, \underbrace{p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}_{\text{call this } \bar{b}_t(x)}$$

**Step 4 — Expand the prediction $\bar{b}_t$ via total probability and Markov.**

Introduce the previous state $x_{t-1}$ as a nuisance variable:

$$\bar{b}_t(x) \;=\; \int p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, x_{t-1})\;p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})\,dx_{t-1}$$

Apply Markov on the motion model ($u_t$ alone suffices):

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, x_{t-1}) \;=\; p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$$

Recognize the second factor as the previous belief:

$$p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \;=\; b_{t-1}(x_{t-1})$$

(The last equality uses the fact that $u_t$ gives no information about the past.)

**Step 5 — Final recursive form.**

$$\boxed{\;b_t(x) \;=\; \eta\, p(z_t \mid x)\int p(x \mid u_t, x')\, b_{t-1}(x')\, dx'\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Bayes filter**. Every belief representation in the KTU syllabus is an *algorithm* to compute the integral on the right-hand side in a tractable way.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Kalman Filter (Linear Gaussian Case)

**Setup:** Linear system with Gaussian noise.

$$x_t \;=\; A x_{t-1} + B u_t + \epsilon_t, \qquad \epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$$

$$z_t \;=\; H x_t + \delta_t, \qquad \delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$$

Prior: $b_{t-1}(x) = \mathcal{N}(x;\,\mu_{t-1}, \Sigma_{t-1})$.

**Step 1 — Prediction.**

The motion model is an affine transformation of a Gaussian, so:

$$\bar{b}_t(x) \;=\; \mathcal{N}(x;\,A\mu_{t-1} + Bu_t,\; A\Sigma_{t-1}A^{\!\top} + R)$$

Hence:

$$\bar{\mu}_t \;=\; A\mu_{t-1} + Bu_t, \qquad \bar{\Sigma}_t \;=\; A\Sigma_{t-1}A^{\!\top} + R$$

**Step 2 — Measurement update.**

Plug the Gaussian $\bar{b}_t$ into the Bayes update. Compute the joint distribution of $x_t$ and $z_t$ (both linear in $x_t$):

$$\begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ z_t \end{bmatrix} \;\sim\; \mathcal{N}\!\left(\begin{bmatrix} \bar{\mu}_t \\ H\bar{\mu}_t \end{bmatrix},\; \begin{bmatrix} \bar{\Sigma}_t & \bar{\Sigma}_t H^{\!\top} \\ H\bar{\Sigma}_t & H\bar{\Sigma}_t H^{\!\top} + Q \end{bmatrix}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Use the standard formula for the conditional of a joint Gaussian.**

For a joint Gaussian with block covariance, the conditional is:

$$p(x \mid y) \;=\; \mathcal{N}(x;\,\mu_x + \Sigma_{xy}\Sigma_{yy}^{-1}(y-\mu_y),\; \Sigma_{xx} - \Sigma_{xy}\Sigma_{yy}^{-1}\Sigma_{yx})$$

**Step 4 — Identify and simplify.**

Define the **Kalman gain**:

$$K_t \;=\; \bar{\Sigma}_t H^{\!\top}(H\bar{\Sigma}_t H^{\!\top} + Q)^{-1}$$

Then:

$$\mu_t \;=\; \bar{\mu}_t + K_t(z_t - H\bar{\mu}_t), \qquad \Sigma_t \;=\; (I - K_t H)\bar{\Sigma}_t$$

This is the **Kalman filter**.

> [!TIP]
> The term $\nu_t = z_t - H\bar{\mu}_t$ is the **innovation**. Its covariance is $S_t = H\bar{\Sigma}_t H^{\!\top} + Q$. The Kalman gain is exactly $\bar{\Sigma}_t H^{\!\top} S_t^{-1}$, the ratio of prior-vs-noise uncertainty.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Particle Filter Weights (Importance Sampling)

**Setup:** We want to approximate $b_t$ but cannot sample from it directly. Instead, we sample from a **proposal** $q(x)$ and correct via importance weights.

**Step 1 — Importance weight identity.**

$$b_t(x) \;=\; \frac{p(x \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})}{q(x)}\, q(x)$$

So if $x^{(i)} \sim q(\cdot)$, then the unbiased weight is:

$$w^{(i)} \;\propto\; \frac{b_t(x^{(i)})}{q(x^{(i)})} \;=\; \frac{p(x^{(i)} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})}{q(x^{(i)})}$$

**Step 2 — Expand the target.**

Using Bayes:

$$p(x_{0:t} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \;\propto\; p(z_t \mid x_t)\, p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\, p(x_{0:t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})$$

**Step 3 — Choose proposal as the motion model** (Bootstrap / SIR filter):

$$q(x_{0:t}) \;=\; q(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t})\, q(x_{0:t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1})$$

Taking $q(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ and cancelling terms:

$$w_t^{(i)} \;\propto\; w_{t-1}^{(i)}\, p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})$$

**Step 4 — Normalize.**

$$\tilde{w}_t^{(i)} \;=\; \frac{w_t^{(i)}}{\sum_{j=1}^{M} w_t^{(j)}}$$

The discrete approximation is:

$$b_t(x) \;\approx\; \sum_{i=1}^{M} \tilde{w}_t^{(i)}\, \delta(x - x_t^{(i)})$$

> [!NOTE]
> When $N_{\text{eff}} = 1/\sum_i (\tilde{w}_t^{(i)})^2$ falls below $M/2$, perform **resampling** (e.g., multinomial, stratified, or systematic) to combat weight degeneracy.

---

### 3.4 Belief-Space Planning Objective (Information-Theoretic View)

A common KTU Module 2 question asks: *"Why is planning in belief space harder than planning in state space?"* The answer lies in the objective.

A typical belief-space cost over horizon $T$:

$$J(\pi) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{t=0}^{T} c(b_t, u_t)\right] \;=\; \sum_{t=0}^{T} \int c(x, u_t)\, b_t(x)\, dx$$

For an **information-gathering** action $u$, the expected reduction in entropy is:

$$\Delta H(u) \;=\; H(b^-) - \mathbb{E}_{z}\!\left[H(b^+(z))\right]$$

This is a *nested* expectation (over the future measurement $z$), making the problem **doubly exponential** in horizon length in the worst case — a key motivation for approximate belief representations.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — 1-D Kalman Filter (Examination Drill)

**Problem:** A robot has state $x_t \in \mathbb{R}$, motion $x_t = x_{t-1} + u_t + \epsilon_t$ with $\epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R=1)$, and sensor $z_t = x_t + \delta_t$ with $\delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q=2)$.

Initial: $\mu_0 = 0, \Sigma_0 = 4$. Control: $u_1 = 1$. Measurement: $z_1 = 2$.

**Step 1 — Prediction.**

$$A = 1, \quad B = 1$$

$$\bar{\mu}_1 = 1 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot 1 = 1$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_1 = 1 \cdot 4 \cdot 1 + 1 = 5$$

**Step 2 — Innovation.**

$$\nu_1 = z_1 - H\bar{\mu}_1 = 2 - 1 \cdot 1 = 1$$

**Step 3 — Innovation covariance.**

$$S_1 = H\bar{\Sigma}_1 H^{\!\top} + Q = 1 \cdot 5 \cdot 1 + 2 = 7$$

**Step 4 — Kalman gain.**

$$K_1 = \bar{\Sigma}_1 H^{\!\top} S_1^{-1} = 5 \cdot 1 \cdot \tfrac{1}{7} = \tfrac{5}{7}$$

**Step 5 — Posterior mean and covariance.**

$$\mu_1 = \bar{\mu}_1 + K_1 \nu_1 = 1 + \tfrac{5}{7} \cdot 1 = \tfrac{12}{7} \approx 1.714$$

$$\Sigma_1 = (1 - K_1 H)\bar{\Sigma}_1 = \left(1 - \tfrac{5}{7}\right) \cdot 5 = \tfrac{2}{7} \cdot 5 = \tfrac{10}{7} \approx 1.429$$

> [!TIP]
> Note how the posterior covariance $\Sigma_1 = 10/7 < 4 = \Sigma_0$ — the measurement *reduced* uncertainty, as expected. This is the key qualitative property examiners love to test.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**
*Define belief state and belief representation. Why are they distinct concepts?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Belief state (1 mark):** A probability distribution $b_t(x) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ over the state space, encoding the robot's internal estimate of its state at time $t$ given the history of measurements and actions.
- **Belief representation (1 mark):** The mathematical data structure (e.g., Gaussian, grid, particles, topological graph) used to *store and manipulate* the belief distribution.
- **Why distinct (1 mark):** The belief is a mathematical object (a function); the representation is the *encoding scheme* chosen to realize it. Different representations yield different trade-offs in memory, speed, and expressiveness for the same underlying belief.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**
*State two advantages and one disadvantage of a particle filter over a Kalman filter for belief representation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Advantage 1 (1 mark):** Particle filters can represent **multimodal** beliefs, which is essential for *global localization* (kidnapped robot problem). KFs can only represent unimodal Gaussians.
- **Advantage 2 (1 mark):** Particle filters make **no parametric assumption** (no Gaussianity, no linearity) — they work for arbitrary non-linear, non-Gaussian systems.
- **Disadvantage (1 mark):** Computational and memory cost grows with the number of particles $M$, and the filter can suffer from *particle deprivation* in high-dimensional state spaces.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 2 carries 14 marks, with internal choice between two questions. Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Either choice should be fully attempted by students.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Analytical / Mathematical Focus**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper, Q2(a)]**
*(a) (7 marks)* Derive the recursive Bayes filter update equation for the belief $b_t(x)$. Clearly state all assumptions used in the derivation.

*(b) (7 marks)* A 1-D mobile robot has motion model $x_t = x_{t-1} + u_t + \epsilon_t$, $\epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R = 0.5)$, and sensor model $z_t = x_t + \delta_t$, $\delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q = 1.0)$. The robot starts with prior belief $b_0 = \mathcal{N}(0, 4)$. It applies control $u_1 = 2$ and receives measurement $z_1 = 3$. Compute the posterior belief $b_1$ explicitly (mean and variance).

---

**Model Solution A(a):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Begin with the definition $b_t(x) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply Bayes' rule to introduce the measurement $z_t$:

$$b_t(x) = \eta\, p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})\, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply the **measurement Markov assumption** (the current sensor reading depends only on the current state):

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Identify the normalizer $\eta$ and the predicted belief $\bar{b}_t$.

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Expand the prediction term using the law of total probability, introduce $x_{t-1}$:

$$\bar{b}_t(x) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\, b_{t-1}(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$$

after applying the motion Markov assumption and recognizing the previous belief.

**Final result (full marks):**

$$\boxed{\;b_t(x) = \eta\, p(z_t \mid x) \int p(x \mid x', u_t)\, b_{t-1}(x')\, dx'\;}$$

---

**Model Solution A(b):**

**Step 1 — Prediction (2 marks).** With $A=1$, $B=1$, $R=0.5$:

$$\bar{\mu}_1 = \mu_0 + u_1 = 0 + 2 = 2$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_1 = \Sigma_0 + R = 4 + 0.5 = 4.5$$

**Step 2 — Innovation (1 mark).** With $H=1$:

$$\nu_1 = z_1 - H\bar{\mu}_1 = 3 - 2 = 1$$

**Step 3 — Innovation covariance (1 mark).**

$$S_1 = H\bar{\Sigma}_1 H^{\!\top} + Q = 4.5 + 1.0 = 5.5$$

**Step 4 — Kalman gain (1 mark).**

$$K_1 = \bar{\Sigma}_1 S_1^{-1} = \frac{4.5}{5.5} = \frac{9}{11}$$

**Step 5 — Posterior mean and covariance (2 marks).**

$$\mu_1 = \bar{\mu}_1 + K_1 \nu_1 = 2 + \frac{9}{11} \cdot 1 = \frac{31}{11} \approx 2.818$$

$$\Sigma_1 = (1 - K_1)\bar{\Sigma}_1 = \left(1 - \frac{9}{11}\right) \cdot 4.5 = \frac{2}{11} \cdot 4.5 = \frac{9}{11} \approx 0.818$$

**Final Answer:** $b_1 = \mathcal{N}(2.818,\; 0.818)$.

> [!TIP]
> Observe that the posterior variance $\Sigma_1 = 0.818$ is much smaller than the prior $\Sigma_0 = 4$ — the measurement dramatically reduced uncertainty. This is the qualitative property examiners want to see confirmed.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Conceptual / Comparative Focus**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary, Q2(b)]**
*(a) (7 marks)* Compare and contrast the four major belief representation schemes (Kalman family, particle filter, histogram grid, topological map) along the dimensions of (i) state-space type, (ii) multimodality support, (iii) memory complexity, and (iv) computational complexity per update.

*(b) (7 marks)* Explain the curse of dimensionality in the context of belief representation. With a state space $X = \mathbb{R}^3$ discretized into 50 cells per dimension, estimate the memory required for a histogram representation. Discuss how particle filters or Gaussian representations mitigate this issue.

---

**Model Solution B(a):**

| Dimension (1 mark each) | Kalman (KF/EKF/UKF) | Particle Filter | Histogram Grid | Topological |
|--|--|--|--|--|
| **State-space type** | Continuous, smooth | Continuous or discrete | Finite / discretized | Discrete set of places |
| **Multimodality** | No (unimodal only) | Yes (native) | Yes | Yes (across nodes) |
| **Memory** | O($n^2$) for $\Sigma$ | O($M$) particles | O($N$) cells, $N$ exp. in $n$ | O(\#nodes + edges) |
| **Update cost** | O($n^3$) for matrix inversion | O($M \cdot T_{\text{sim}}$) | O($N^2$) matrix multiply | O(\#edges) traversal |
| **Best for (0.5 mark each)** | Local tracking, SLAM | Global localization, non-Gaussian | Small finite state spaces | Large-scale semantic nav |

**Extra marks:** Conclude with the unifying statement that all four are *implementations* of the Bayes filter equation, differing only in how they approximate the integral in the prediction step.

---

**Model Solution B(b):**

**Curse of dimensionality (3 marks):** The number of grid cells grows *exponentially* with the dimension $n$ of the state space: $N = K^n$ for $K$ cells per dimension. In 3-D with $K=50$:

$$N = 50^3 = 125{,}000 \text{ cells}$$

If each cell stores a 64-bit float, the memory footprint is:

$$M_{\text{mem}} = 125{,}000 \times 8 \text{ bytes} = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 1 \text{ MB}$$

This is acceptable for 3-D, but for a 6-DOF robot pose ($x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi$) with 50 cells per dimension:

$$N = 50^6 \approx 1.56 \times 10^{10} \text{ cells} \approx 122 \text{ GB}$$

**Mitigation (4 marks):**
- **Gaussian (KF) representation** (1.5 marks): Stores only $\mu \in \mathbb{R}^6$ and $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times 6}$ — total memory of just a few hundred bytes regardless of state dimension. Limitation: cannot represent multimodality.
- **Particle filter** (1.5 marks): Memory is $O(M)$ independent of $n$. With $M=1000$ particles, memory is constant. But particles must be drawn from a 6-D space, so the *search* becomes harder (sample impoverishment).
- **Hybrid topological** (1 mark): Discretizes the environment into a graph of distinctive places, sidestepping the dense grid entirely. Each node stores a *local* low-D belief.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout** — Common mark-loss mistakes on this question:
> 1. Confusing *belief* with *belief representation* in the introduction.
> 2. Forgetting to apply the **measurement Markov** AND **motion Markov** assumptions explicitly in Bayes filter derivations. List both for full marks.
> 3. In Kalman update problems, students often forget the **innovation covariance** $S_t = H\bar{\Sigma}_t H^{\!\top} + Q$, mixing up process noise $R$ with measurement noise $Q$. Memorize: $R$ enters in **prediction**, $Q$ enters in **update**.
> 4. For particle filters, students compute weights but forget to **normalize**, or compute the wrong likelihood (using $p(x \mid z)$ instead of $p(z \mid x)$).
> 5. In entropy / information-gain sub-questions, students confuse $H(b^-)$ with $H(b^+)$ — the *posterior* entropy is conditional on the (random) future measurement, requiring an outer expectation.
> 6. Drawing Kalman covariance as a scalar (it's a matrix) — lose 1 mark.
> 7. Saying "particle filters are more accurate than Kalman filters" — false; they are *more general*, not more accurate for the linear-Gaussian case.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Belief Representation**

- **Definition to remember verbatim:** $b_t(x) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ — the **posterior** over the state given all history.
- **Two Markov assumptions** are *both* needed for the Bayes filter: motion Markov ($p(x_t \mid \cdot) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$) and sensor Markov ($p(z_t \mid \cdot) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$).
- **The Bayes filter equation** is the *mother equation*; KF, EKF, UKF, particle filter, and histogram filter are all *implementations*.
- **Gaussian belief is unimodal.** If the problem involves ambiguity (e.g., identical corridors, kidnapped robot), a Gaussian is *qualitatively wrong* — use particles.
- **Kalman update** uses **two** covariance matrices: $R$ (process) for prediction, $Q$ (measurement) for update. The Kalman gain $K_t$ balances the two.
- **Particle filter weight** in the bootstrap/SIR form is $w_t^{(i)} \propto w_{t-1}^{(i)}\, p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})$ — proportional to the **measurement likelihood**, not the prior.
- **Resampling rule of thumb:** Trigger resampling when effective sample size $N_{\text{eff}} = 1/\sum_i (w^{(i)})^2$ falls below $M/2$.
- **Histogram filter** memory cost is $K^n$ — exponential in state-space dimension $n$. This is the **curse of dimensionality**.
- **Topological maps** store uncertainty *across* discrete places, not within a place — they are robust but cannot answer "where exactly am I within this room?"
- **Entropy** $H(b_t) = -\int b_t(x)\log b_t(x)\,dx$ is the standard scalar measure of belief uncertainty; it is **subadditive** over independent state components.
- **Information form** of the Gaussian: store $\Omega = \Sigma^{-1}$ and $\xi = \Sigma^{-1}\mu$ — useful in distributed/federated SLAM (out of KTU scope but commonly referenced).
- **Belief-space planning** cost is a *nested* expectation (over future measurements), making it doubly exponential in horizon — always requires approximation (e.g., FIRM, belief roadmaps, sparse beliefs).
- **KTU-favorite trap:** the term "**belief space**" is the *infinite-dimensional* space of all probability distributions; planners search in this space, not the finite-dimensional state space.
- **Engineering rule of thumb:** Use KF/EKF for local pose tracking, particle filter for global localization, occupancy grid for mapping, topological for large-scale navigation. Hybridize when one is insufficient.

> [!TIP]
> Before walking into the KTU exam hall for Module 2, re-derive the Bayes filter from scratch on a blank sheet, perform a 1-D Kalman update (Section 3.5) without peeking, and recite the four representation families with one real robot example each. This single 30-minute ritual will recover 80% of achievable marks on this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Map Representation - Continuous Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 2 — Map Representation: Continuous Representation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Continuous Representation* of a robot's environment is a **geometrically continuous** description of the workspace (or configuration space) using **analytic/geometric primitives** such as points, line segments, polygons, polyhedra, curves, and algebraic surfaces. The environment is described as an **exact mathematical object** (a set in $\mathbb{R}^2$ or $\mathbb{R}^3$) rather than as a sampled grid of cells or sampled points.

In continuous representation, every obstacle, every wall, and every free region is described by **closed-form equations** (e.g., line equations, polygon vertex lists, polynomial inequalities). The representation is **resolution-free**, meaning that as long as the underlying geometry is known exactly, the planner can reason about paths with **infinite precision**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Contrast with Discrete Representation:**
> - **Continuous** $\Rightarrow$ exact geometry (equations/primitives)
> - **Discrete** $\Rightarrow$ sampled/quantized (grids, roadmaps, samples)

### Conceptual Analogy 🏠

Imagine you are describing a room to a friend.
- **Continuous (geometric) way:** *"The room is a rectangle 5 m × 4 m, with a circular pillar of radius 0.3 m centered at (2, 1.5)."* — Exact, no approximation.
- **Discrete (grid) way:** *"The room is a 50 × 40 grid of 10 cm squares; mark the squares near the pillar as blocked."* — Approximate, loses smoothness, resolution-dependent.

A **continuous map** is the first description; a **grid map** is the second. Continuous representations preserve **sharpness of edges, exact corners, and analytic distances**, which is invaluable for precise motion planning, collision checking, and analytical derivations.

### Key Physical & Mathematical Quantities

- **Workspace $\mathcal{W}$** — the physical 2D/3D space in which the robot operates, expressed in **meters (m)**.
- **Configuration Space $\mathcal{C}$** — the $n$-dimensional space of all robot configurations (joint angles, poses), often dimensionless (radians, normalized).
- **Free Space $\mathcal{F}$** — the subset of $\mathcal{W}$ (or $\mathcal{C}$) not occupied by obstacles: $\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{W} \setminus \mathcal{O}$.
- **Obstacle Region $\mathcal{O}$** — closed subset denoting physical obstacles: $\mathcal{O} \subset \mathcal{W}$.
- **Convexity** — a set $S$ is **convex** if for all $p, q \in S$, the line segment $\overline{pq} \subseteq S$. **Convexity** dramatically simplifies planning.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 scheme, Module 2 explicitly distinguishes between **continuous** (this topic) and **discrete** (roadmap, cell decomposition, sampling) representations. Continuous representation is the *precursor* to building discrete planners because it is what gets discretized.

### Categories of Continuous Representation

| Category | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometric Primitives** | Basic shapes (point, line, polygon, circle, polyhedron) | Rectangle room |
| **Polygonal / Polyhedral** | Unions of polygons / polyhedra | Floor plan of a building |
| **Semi-algebraic** | Sets defined by polynomial equalities/inequalities | $x^2 + y^2 \le 1$ |
| **Implicit (CSG)** | Constructive Solid Geometry using Boolean ops | $(A \cap B) \setminus C$ |
| **Parametric / Swept** | Curves, surfaces defined by parameters | B-spline walls |

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Continuous Representation?

The motion planning pipeline begins with a **world model**. The world model must be:

1. **Complete** — covers the entire workspace.
2. **Concise** — uses minimal storage.
3. **Queryable** — supports operations like *point-in-polygon*, *segment-intersect*, *distance to obstacle*.
4. **Analytically tractable** — supports distance and gradient computations.

Continuous (geometric) representations score highly on all four — particularly for environments where exact boundaries are known (CAD models, architectural blueprints, known manufactured parts).

### 2.2 Polygonal Representation

A **polygon** in 2D is a closed planar region bounded by a finite chain of line segments. It is the **workhorse of continuous map representation** in 2D motion planning.

A polygon $P$ can be described by:
- **Vertex list** $V = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\}$, with $v_i \in \mathbb{R}^2$.
- **Edge list** $E = \{e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_n\}$, where $e_i = \overline{v_i v_{i+1}}$ (with $v_{n+1} = v_1$).
- **Implicit form** as the intersection of half-planes: $P = \bigcap_{i=1}^{n} H_i^-$, where $H_i^-$ is the half-plane on the **interior** side of edge $e_i$.

#### 2.2.1 Convex Polygon

A polygon is **convex** iff every internal angle is $\le \pi$ (i.e., the polygon "bulges outward" everywhere). For a convex polygon, the **half-plane intersection form** is the most compact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convex polygons are the "atom" of geometric planning.** Any non-convex free space is typically **decomposed** into a union of convex cells before planning (this is *exact cell decomposition*).

#### 2.2.2 Non-Convex (General) Polygons

A polygon with one or more reflex vertices (internal angle $> \pi$) is **non-convex**. Such polygons must be **decomposed** into convex pieces for many planning algorithms.

### 2.3 Polyhedral Representation (3D)

A **polyhedron** in $\mathbb{R}^3$ is bounded by flat polygonal faces. It generalizes the polygon. A convex polyhedron can be represented as the **intersection of half-spaces**:

$$P = \bigcap_{i=1}^{m} H_i^- \subset \mathbb{R}^3$$

where each $H_i^-$ is a closed half-space defined by a plane $\mathbf{n}_i \cdot \mathbf{x} \le d_i$.

### 2.4 Configuration Space Obstacles (C-obstacles)

> [!NOTE]
> **The most important concept in continuous representation for motion planning.**

For a robot $\mathcal{A}$ and obstacle $\mathcal{O}$ in workspace $\mathcal{W}$, the **Configuration Space Obstacle** $\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{A}$ is the set of all configurations $q \in \mathcal{C}$ such that the robot placed at $q$ **collides** with the obstacle:

$$\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{A} = \{q \in \mathcal{C} \mid \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} \ne \emptyset\}$$

The **free configuration space** is:

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{A}$$

For a **translating rigid robot** in 2D, the C-obstacle for a point robot is simply the obstacle translated back by the robot shape — a **Minkowski difference**.

### 2.5 Minkowski Sum — The Central Geometric Operation

The **Minkowski sum** of two sets $A$ and $B$ is:

$$A \oplus B = \{a + b \mid a \in A,\; b \in B\}$$

For a translating robot $\mathcal{A}$ and obstacle $\mathcal{B}$, the C-obstacle is:

$$\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O} = \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{A}) = \{o - a \mid o \in \mathcal{O},\; a \in \mathcal{A}\}$$

Equivalently, by the duality of Minkowski sum and convex hull:

$$\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O} = \text{ConvexHull}(\mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{A}))$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why Minkowski sum?** It converts the *complex* collision-geometry problem (robot moving through space) into a *simpler* equivalent problem (point robot moving through a *grown* obstacle).

### 2.6 The Three Fundamental Geometric Queries

For any continuous map representation, planners must support these queries:

| Query | Meaning | Common Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| **Point-in-Polygon (PIP)** | Is point $p$ inside polygon $P$? | Ray casting (Jordan curve) |
| **Segment-Intersection** | Do segments $s_1, s_2$ intersect? | Orientation tests / Liang–Bates |
| **Distance Computation** | Distance from point/segment/polygon to polygon? | Closest-point iteration |

### 2.7 The Point-in-Polygon (PIP) Test — Detailed

The **ray-casting algorithm** (Jordan curve theorem):

$$\text{Inside}(p, P) \iff \text{number of edge crossings of a ray from } p \text{ is odd}$$

For a vertical ray from $p$ to $+\infty$, count how many edges of $P$ it crosses. Odd $\Rightarrow$ inside; even $\Rightarrow$ outside.

For a polygon $P$ with vertices $v_i = (x_i, y_i)$, an edge $\overline{v_i v_{i+1}}$ crosses the ray from $p = (p_x, p_y)$ iff:

$$(y_i > p_y) \ne (y_{i+1} > p_y) \quad \text{and}$$
$$p_x < (x_{i+1} - x_i) \cdot \frac{p_y - y_i}{y_{i+1} - y_i} + x_i$$

### 2.8 Convex Decomposition

A non-convex polygon $P$ with $n$ vertices and $r$ reflex vertices can be triangulated into exactly:

$$n - 2 \text{ triangles}$$

A general **convex decomposition** produces at most $O(n^2)$ convex pieces (in the worst case) and at minimum $r + 1$ pieces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Triangulation is the simplest convex decomposition** and the one most often used in KTU problems.

### 2.9 Polygonal Map Algebra — Boolean Set Operations

Given two polygons $P$ and $Q$:

| Operation | Set Definition | Use in Planning |
|---|---|---|
| **Union** $P \cup Q$ | All points in $P$ or $Q$ | Merging adjacent obstacle pieces |
| **Intersection** $P \cap Q$ | Points in both $P$ and $Q$ | Detecting overlap / collision |
| **Difference** $P \setminus Q$ | Points in $P$ but not $Q$ | Free space computation |
| **XOR** $P \oplus Q$ | Symmetric difference | Sub-region analysis |

These four operations, together with translation/rotation, form the **Boolean algebra of polygonal maps** and are the foundation of CSG (Constructive Solid Geometry) representations.

### 2.10 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical bars `|` are used inside any table cell. All absolute value / conditioning uses `\vert` or `\mid`.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Statement | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Workspace definition | $\mathcal{W} \subset \mathbb{R}^{2}$ or $\mathbb{R}^{3}$ | m, m², m³ |
| 2 | Free space | $\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{W} \setminus \mathcal{O}$ | same as $\mathcal{W}$ |
| 3 | Convex polygon (half-plane) | $P = \bigcap_{i=1}^{n} H_i^-$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Convex polyhedron (half-space) | $P = \bigcap_{i=1}^{m} H_i^- \subset \mathbb{R}^{3}$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Triangle count in triangulation | $T = n - 2$ | integer |
| 6 | Minkowski sum | $A \oplus B = \{a + b \mid a \in A, b \in B\}$ | set op. |
| 7 | C-obstacle (translation) | $\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O} = \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{A})$ | m |
| 8 | C-obstacle (rotation) | $\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O}_{\theta} = R_{\theta}(\mathcal{O}) \oplus (-\mathcal{A})$ | m, rad |
| 9 | Distance (point to segment) | $d(p, \overline{ab}) = \dfrac{\vert (b-a) \times (a-p) \vert}{\vert b - a \vert}$ | m |
| 10 | Convexity test (set) | $\forall\, p, q \in S,\; \overline{pq} \subseteq S \Rightarrow S$ convex | logical |
| 11 | Area of polygon (shoelace) | $A = \dfrac{1}{2} \left\vert \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i) \right\vert$ | m² |
| 12 | PIP test parity | $\text{Inside} \iff \text{crossings mod } 2 = 1$ | logical |
| 13 | Euler's formula (planar graph) | $V - E + F = 2$ | integer |
| 14 | Vertices after triangulation | $V' = V + (T - 1) = 2n - 2$ for polygon | integer |
| 15 | Minkowski sum of convex polygons | $\text{Conv}(A) \oplus \text{Conv}(B) = \text{Conv}(A \oplus B)$ | set op. |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:**
> - **Autonomous vehicles:** polygon maps of lanes and parking spots.
> - **Industrial robotics:** CAD polygonal models for collision-free arm trajectories.
> - **Drone flight:** polyhedral no-fly zones.
> - **Surgical robotics:** polygonal models of anatomical organs for needle path planning.
> - **Computer graphics:** continuous geometric models are the standard input for rasterization engines.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> **No step is skipped.** Every algebraic transition is written out fully.

### 3.1 Derivation: Shoelace Formula for Polygon Area

**Goal:** Derive the area of a simple polygon $P$ with vertices $(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)$ traversed counter-clockwise.

**Step 1 — Green’s Theorem.** By Green's theorem, the area equals the line integral over the boundary $\partial P$:

$$A = \frac{1}{2} \oint_{\partial P} (x\, dy - y\, dx)$$

**Step 2 — Discretize the integral along the $n$ edges.** Edge $i$ goes from $v_i = (x_i, y_i)$ to $v_{i+1} = (x_{i+1}, y_{i+1})$. Parameterize:

$$x(t) = x_i + t (x_{i+1} - x_i), \quad y(t) = y_i + t (y_{i+1} - y_i), \quad t \in [0, 1]$$

Then $dx = (x_{i+1} - x_i) dt$ and $dy = (y_{i+1} - y_i) dt$.

**Step 3 — Substitute and integrate.** For edge $i$:

$$\int_{e_i} (x\, dy - y\, dx) = \int_0^1 \left[ (x_i + t \Delta x_i)(y_{i+1} - y_i) - (y_i + t \Delta y_i)(x_{i+1} - x_i) \right] dt$$

where $\Delta x_i = x_{i+1} - x_i$ and $\Delta y_i = y_{i+1} - y_i$.

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$= \int_0^1 \left[ x_i \Delta y_i - y_i \Delta x_i + t(\Delta x_i \Delta y_i - \Delta y_i \Delta x_i) \right] dt$$

The $t$-coefficient vanishes (it's $\Delta x_i \Delta y_i - \Delta y_i \Delta x_i = 0$). So:

$$= x_i \Delta y_i - y_i \Delta x_i = x_i y_{i+1} - x_i y_i - y_i x_{i+1} + y_i x_i = x_i y_{i+1} - y_i x_{i+1}$$

**Step 5 — Sum over all edges and multiply by $\frac{1}{2}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i y_{i+1} - y_i x_{i+1}) \\
  &= \frac{1}{2} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_{i+1} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i x_{i+1} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final compact form (shoelace formula).**

$$
A = \frac{1}{2} \left\vert \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i) \right\vert
$$

**Verification with a unit square** $(0,0), (1,0), (1,1), (0,1)$:

$$A = \frac{1}{2} \vert 0\cdot 0 - 1\cdot 0 + 1\cdot 1 - 1\cdot 0 + 1\cdot 1 - 0\cdot 1 + 0\cdot 0 - 0\cdot 1 \vert = \frac{1}{2} \vert 0 + 1 + 1 + 0 \vert = 1 \;\text{m}^2 \;\checkmark$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: C-Obstacle for a Translating Polygonal Robot

**Setup:** Workspace $\mathcal{W} \subset \mathbb{R}^{2}$. Robot $\mathcal{A}$ is a polygon (e.g., triangle with vertices $a_1, a_2, a_3$). Obstacle $\mathcal{O}$ is a polygon with vertices $o_1, \ldots, o_m$. Robot translates by $q = (q_x, q_y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$.

**Step 1 — Configuration of the robot.** A translated copy of the robot is:

$$\mathcal{A}(q) = \{ a + q \mid a \in \mathcal{A} \} = \{ a + (q_x, q_y) \mid (a_x, a_y) \in \mathcal{A} \}$$

**Step 2 — Collision condition.** A configuration $q$ is in collision iff the translated robot intersects the obstacle:

$$\mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} \ne \emptyset$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent reformulation.** This is true iff there exist $a \in \mathcal{A}$ and $o \in \mathcal{O}$ such that $a + q = o$, i.e., $q = o - a$.

$$\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O} = \{ o - a \mid o \in \mathcal{O},\; a \in \mathcal{A} \} = \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{A})$$

**Step 4 — Convex case.** If $\mathcal{A}$ and $\mathcal{O}$ are both **convex polygons**, the C-obstacle is **also convex**, and is given by the Minkowski sum of two convex polygons.

**Algorithm — Convex Polygon Minkowski Sum:**
1. Sort edges of $\mathcal{O}$ by angle around its centroid.
2. Sort edges of $(-\mathcal{A})$ by angle around its centroid.
3. Merge the two sorted edge lists by angle (the "merge step" of the convex-hull-style algorithm).
4. The result is a convex polygon with vertex count $\le m + k - 1$ where $k$ is the number of vertices of $\mathcal{A}$.

**Worked Example:** $\mathcal{O}$ is a unit square with vertices $(0,0), (1,0), (1,1), (0,1)$. Robot $\mathcal{A}$ is a square of side $s = 0.5$ centered at origin: vertices $(\pm 0.25, \pm 0.25)$. Then $-\mathcal{A}$ is also a square of side 0.5 centered at origin. By geometry, $\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O} = \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{A})$ is a square of side $1 + 0.5 = 1.5$ — i.e., the obstacle grown outward by half the robot's width.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Point-in-Polygon (Ray-Casting Parity)

**Goal:** For point $p = (p_x, p_y)$ and polygon $P$ with edges $e_i = \overline{v_i v_{i+1}}$, determine if $p$ is inside $P$.

**Step 1 — Cast a horizontal ray** $R$ from $p$ in the direction of increasing $x$: $R = \{(p_x + t, p_y) \mid t \ge 0\}$.

**Step 2 — Identify candidate edges.** An edge $e_i = \overline{(x_i, y_i)(x_{i+1}, y_{i+1})}$ can cross $R$ only if it straddles the horizontal line $y = p_y$:

$$(y_i - p_y)(y_{i+1} - p_y) < 0$$

(Strictly less than; if either endpoint lies exactly on $R$, a tie-breaking rule is applied — typically "count as crossing only on the lower endpoint".)

**Step 3 — Compute the x-coordinate of the crossing.**

$$x_{\text{cross}} = x_i + (p_y - y_i) \cdot \frac{x_{i+1} - x_i}{y_{i+1} - y_i}$$

**Step 4 — Count crossings.** For each candidate edge, check if $x_{\text{cross}} > p_x$. The number of such crossings is the parity count $N$.

**Step 5 — Decide.**

$$\text{Inside}(p, P) = \begin{cases} \text{true} & \text{if } N \text{ is odd} \\ \text{false} & \text{if } N \text{ is even (incl.\ 0)} \end{cases}$$

**Justification (Jordan Curve Theorem):** A simple closed curve divides the plane into exactly two regions: the bounded interior and the unbounded exterior. A ray starting inside must exit the polygon an **odd** number of times; a ray starting outside exits an **even** number of times (or zero, if it doesn't hit the polygon at all).

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Triangulation Count

**Claim:** A simple polygon with $n$ vertices can be triangulated into exactly $n - 2$ triangles.

**Proof by induction on $n$:**

*Base case $n = 3$:* A triangle is already a triangulation, and $3 - 2 = 1$ triangle. ✓

*Inductive step:* Assume true for all $k$-vertex polygons with $k < n$. For an $n$-vertex simple polygon, there always exists an **interior diagonal** $d$ connecting two non-adjacent vertices (by the two-ears theorem). This diagonal splits the polygon into two smaller simple polygons with $k$ and $n - k + 2$ vertices, where $3 \le k \le n - 1$.

By induction, the $k$-vertex piece triangulates into $k - 2$ triangles, and the $(n - k + 2)$-vertex piece triangulates into $n - k$ triangles. Total:

$$T = (k - 2) + (n - k) = n - 2 \;\checkmark$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Analysis of Representations

| Property | Continuous (Geometric) | Discrete (Grid/Roadmap) |
|---|---|---|
| Precision | **Exact** (analytic) | Approximate (resolution-bound) |
| Memory | Low–Medium (few parameters) | High (per cell / per sample) |
| Path optimality | Can be **analytically optimal** | Resolution-limited |
| Setup time | Requires **known geometry** | Easy to build from sensor data |
| Best for | **CAD models**, blueprints, known layouts | Unknown / sensor-based maps |
| Drawback | Brittle to perception errors | Resolution trade-off |
| Used by | Visibility graphs, analytical planners | A*, D*, RRT, PRM |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU key takeaway:** Continuous representation is the **mathematically pure** form. Discrete representations are *always derived* from continuous ones by sampling or decomposition. So a deep grasp of continuous representation underlies every other topic in the module.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric with letter prefixes. Labels use plain uppercase text. No markdown or HTML inside labels.
