---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

> Define **continuous representation** of a robot's environment. How does it differ from a **discrete grid-based representation**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Continuous representation* describes the environment as a set of **analytic/geometric primitives** (points, line segments, polygons, polyhedra, algebraic surfaces) with **closed-form equations** or vertex/edge lists. The geometry is **exact** and **resolution-independent** — i.e., the planner can reason about paths with infinite precision as long as the geometric description is exact.

In contrast, a **discrete grid-based representation** quantizes the workspace into uniform cells (pixels/voxels) and stores an occupancy value (occupied / free / unknown) per cell. It is **resolution-dependent**: finer grids give more accuracy but at higher memory cost, and sharp geometric features get *staircased*.

**Key difference:** Continuous = analytic / exact; Discrete = sampled / approximate. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for example.)

---

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]

> What is a **Configuration Space Obstacle (C-obstacle)**? Write the mathematical expression for the C-obstacle of a translating polygonal robot in 2D.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **C-obstacle** $\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{A}$ is the set of all robot configurations $q$ in the configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ for which the robot, when placed at $q$, **physically collides** with at least one workspace obstacle. (1 mark)

Mathematically:

$$\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{A} = \{q \in \mathcal{C} \mid \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} \ne \emptyset\}$$

For a **translating** polygonal robot $\mathcal{A}$ in a 2D workspace, this reduces to a **Minkowski sum** of the obstacle $\mathcal{O}$ with the negated robot shape:

$$\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O} = \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{A}) = \{o - a \mid o \in \mathcal{O},\; a \in \mathcal{A}\}$$

(2 marks for expression with explanation)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Following the **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice** pattern, TWO completely independent 14-mark question choices are provided. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **polygonal representation** of a 2D environment. With neat diagrams, distinguish between **convex and non-convex polygons**. Show how a non-convex polygon can be decomposed into convex pieces using **triangulation**, and state the formula for the number of triangles produced.

**(b) [7 marks]** Consider a square workspace of side $L = 10$ m, with a square obstacle of side $a = 2$ m centred at $(5, 5)$. A **circular** robot of radius $r = 1$ m must navigate from start $q_s = (0, 0)$ to goal $q_g = (10, 0)$. Compute the **C-obstacle** (grown obstacle) for the point-robot equivalent, and identify whether the start and goal are in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.

---

**Model Solution — Question A (a) [7 marks]:**

*Polygonal representation.* A 2D polygonal environment is described by a list of **polygons** — one for each obstacle and (optionally) one for the workspace boundary. Each polygon is specified by:
- A **vertex list** $V = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\}$, with $v_i \in \mathbb{R}^2$.
- An **edge list** $E = \{\overline{v_i v_{i+1}}\}$.
- Optionally, an **implicit half-plane** form $P = \bigcap_{i=1}^n H_i^-$. (1 mark)

*Convex vs non-convex.* A polygon is **convex** if and only if the line segment between any two interior points lies entirely inside the polygon (equivalently, all interior angles $\le \pi$). A **non-convex** polygon has at least one *reflex vertex* (interior angle $> \pi$). (1 mark)

*Triangulation.* A non-convex polygon with $n$ vertices can always be **triangulated** by drawing non-crossing diagonals between non-adjacent vertices. Each triangle is convex. The number of triangles is exactly $n - 2$. (2 marks)

*Diagram.* (Draw a non-convex L-shaped polygon, e.g., vertices $(0,0), (4,0), (4,2), (2,2), (2,4), (0,4)$, and show the two diagonals splitting it into 3 triangles.) (1 mark)

*Decomposition value.* If $r$ is the number of reflex vertices, a **minimum convex decomposition** has between $r + 1$ and $O(r^2)$ pieces; for simple triangulation: $n - 2$ pieces, with $2n - 2$ vertices added (Steiner points possibly). (2 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Question A (b) [7 marks]:**

*Step 1 — Identify parameters.* Workspace side $L = 10$ m, obstacle centred at $(5, 5)$ with side $a = 2$ m, robot radius $r = 1$ m. (0.5 marks)

*Step 2 — C-obstacle computation.* For a circular robot translating in 2D, the C-obstacle is the **obstacle grown outward** by the robot radius $r$ (Minkowski sum of a polygon with a disc). For a square of side $a$ grown by radius $r$, the C-obstacle is a square of side $a + 2r$ with rounded corners of radius $r$. (2 marks)

Numerically:
- Side of grown square: $a + 2r = 2 + 2(1) = 4$ m.
- C-obstacle corners: $(5 - 2, 5 - 2) = (3, 3)$ to $(5 + 2, 5 + 2) = (7, 7)$ as the bounding box, with **quarter-circle arcs of radius 1 m** at the four corners. (1 mark)

*Step 3 — Check start $q_s = (0, 0)$.* Is $q_s$ inside the C-obstacle? The C-obstacle lies in $[3, 7] \times [3, 7]$ (plus rounded corners). $q_s = (0, 0)$ is **outside**. So $q_s \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. (1 mark)

*Step 4 — Check goal $q_g = (10, 0)$.* Is $q_g$ inside the C-obstacle? $q_g = (10, 0)$ is at $x = 10$, far from $[3, 7]$. **Outside**, so $q_g \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. (1 mark)

*Step 5 — Conclusion.* Both configurations are in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. A planner can attempt to find a path along the bottom edge $y = 0$ (e.g., straight line from $(0,0)$ to $(10,0)$) which is collision-free since the C-obstacle lies entirely above $y = 3 - 1 = 2$. (1.5 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model — Alternative]

**(a) [7 marks]** Define the **Minkowski sum** of two sets. With the help of a diagram, explain how the **C-obstacle** for a translating polygonal robot is computed using the Minkowski sum. State the property that makes this computation **efficient for convex polygons**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Given a triangular robot $\mathcal{A}$ with vertices $\{(0,0), (1,0), (0.5, 0.5)\}$ and a square obstacle $\mathcal{O}$ with vertices $\{(3,3), (5,3), (5,5), (3,5)\}$, both in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$, compute the **Minkowski sum** $\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O} = \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{A})$ and list its vertices.

---

**Model Solution — Question B (a) [7 marks]:**

*Minkowski sum definition.* The Minkowski sum of two sets $A, B \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ is:

$$A \oplus B = \{a + b \mid a \in A,\; b \in B\}$$

Geometrically, it is the set obtained by **sweeping** $B$ along every point of $A$ (or equivalently, sweeping $A$ along every point of $B$). (1.5 marks)

*C-obstacle for a translating polygonal robot.* For a rigid robot $\mathcal{A}$ translating in 2D, a configuration $q = (q_x, q_y)$ is in collision iff there exist $a \in \mathcal{A}$ and $o \in \mathcal{O}$ with $a + q = o$, i.e., $q = o - a$. Hence:

$$\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O} = \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{A})$$

This is the **grown obstacle** — equivalent to enlarging $\mathcal{O}$ outward by the shape of the robot. (2 marks)

*Diagram.* (Show the obstacle polygon, the robot polygon placed at the origin, and a few translated copies around the obstacle boundary; their union envelope is the C-obstacle.) (1.5 marks)

*Efficiency for convex polygons.* If both $\mathcal{O}$ and $\mathcal{A}$ are **convex polygons**, then $\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O}$ is also convex, and it can be computed in $O(m + n)$ time by **merging the edge lists sorted by angle** (the convex-hull-style algorithm). This is dramatically more efficient than the $O(mn)$ cost of computing the sum in general. (2 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Question B (b) [7 marks]:**

*Step 1 — Compute $-\mathcal{A}$.* Negate each vertex of $\mathcal{A}$:

$$-\mathcal{A} = \{(0,0), (-1, 0), (-0.5, -0.5)\}$$

This is a triangle reflected through the origin. (1 mark)

*Step 2 — Edge-angle sort for $\mathcal{O}$.* Edges of $\mathcal{O}$ in counter-clockwise order around its centroid $(4,4)$:
- $E_1: (3,3) \to (5,3)$ — direction $(2, 0)$, angle $0°$
- $E_2: (5,3) \to (5,5)$ — direction $(0, 2)$, angle $90°$
- $E_3: (5,5) \to (3,5)$ — direction $(-2, 0)$, angle $180°$
- $E_4: (3,5) \to (3,3)$ — direction $(0, -2)$, angle $270°$ (1 mark)

*Step 3 — Edge-angle sort for $-\mathcal{A}$.* Edges of $-\mathcal{A}$ CCW:
- $F_1: (0,0) \to (-1, 0)$ — direction $(-1, 0)$, angle $180°$
- $F_2: (-1, 0) \to (-0.5, -0.5)$ — direction $(0.5, -0.5)$, angle $-45°$ (or $315°$)
- $F_3: (-0.5, -0.5) \to (0, 0)$ — direction $(0.5, 0.5)$, angle $45°$ (1 mark)

*Step 4 — Merge and compute vertices of Minkowski sum.* Walking around the merged edge list, the vertices of $\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O} = \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{A})$ are obtained by translating vertices of $\mathcal{O}$ and $-\mathcal{A}$ in lockstep with the angle merge.

A simpler way: pick one vertex of $-\mathcal{A}$, e.g., $(0,0)$, and **add it to every vertex of $\mathcal{O}$** to get an "origin copy" of $\mathcal{O}$:
$$\{(3,3), (5,3), (5,5), (3,5)\}$$

Then **add the other vertices of $-\mathcal{A}$ to vertices of $\mathcal{O}$** to get the other polygon copies:
- $(3,3) + (-1, 0) = (2, 3)$
- $(5,3) + (-1, 0) = (4, 3)$
- $(5,5) + (-1, 0) = (4, 5)$
- $(3,5) + (-1, 0) = (2, 5)$
- $(3,3) + (-0.5, -0.5) = (2.5, 2.5)$
- $(5,3) + (-0.5, -0.5) = (4.5, 2.5)$
- $(5,5) + (-0.5, -0.5) = (4.5, 4.5)$
- $(3,5) + (-0.5, -0.5) = (2.5, 4.5)$

The Minkowski sum is the **convex hull** of all these points (since both inputs are convex). (2 marks)

*Step 5 — Convex hull of the resulting point set.* The extreme points are:

$$\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O} \text{ vertices} = \{(2, 3), (4.5, 2.5), (4.5, 4.5), (2.5, 4.5), (2, 5)\}$$

Wait — let us recompute the convex hull carefully. The points to consider are all four squares shifted by each of the three vertices of $-\mathcal{A}$. The merged convex hull is a **hexagon** with vertices (going CCW):

$$\boxed{\{(2, 3), (4.5, 2.5), (4.5, 4.5), (2.5, 4.5), (2, 5), (2, 3)\} \text{ (re-examined)}}$$

Let me re-do the convex hull of the point set $\{p_i + v_j \mid p_i \in \mathcal{O}, v_j \in -\mathcal{A}\}$:

All 12 points:
- $(3,3), (5,3), (5,5), (3,5)$  (from $(0,0)$)
- $(2,3), (4,3), (4,5), (2,5)$  (from $(-1,0)$)
- $(2.5, 2.5), (4.5, 2.5), (4.5, 4.5), (2.5, 4.5)$ (from $(-0.5, -0.5)$)

Computing the convex hull (lowest $y$, then sweep CCW):
- Lowest point: $(2.5, 2.5)$ → (next CCW) $(2, 3)$ → $(2, 5)$ → $(2.5, 5)$ is **not** present. Check: $(2.5, 4.5)$ and $(3,5)$ and $(4,5)$ and $(4.5, 4.5)$ — the upper-left extreme is $(2, 5)$.
- Top: $(3,5), (4,5), (4.5, 4.5), (2.5, 4.5)$ — the highest is $y = 5$: points $(2,5), (3,5), (4,5)$.
- Rightmost: $(5, 3), (5, 5), (4.5, 2.5), (4.5, 4.5), (4, 3), (4, 5)$ — rightmost is $x = 5$.

Convex hull (CCW) is:
$$\{(2.5, 2.5), (4.5, 2.5), (5, 3), (5, 5), (4.5, 4.5), (4, 5), (2, 5), (2, 3)\}$$

So $\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O}$ is an **octagon** with these 8 vertices. (1 mark for the listed vertex set, 1 mark for the proper CCW order).

(2 marks for showing the computation, 1 mark for the final vertex list)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks on "Continuous Representation" questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing C-obstacle with workspace obstacle.** The C-obstacle is in **configuration space**, not workspace. For a translating robot, the C-obstacle is the obstacle **grown** by the robot shape. Many students write $\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O} = \mathcal{O}$ — **wrong, loses 2–3 marks**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the negation in Minkowski sum.** It is $\mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{A})$, not $\mathcal{O} \oplus \mathcal{A}$. This is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.
>
> 3. **Not handling rotation.** For a *rotating* robot, the C-obstacle depends on orientation $\theta$, and $\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O}$ becomes a 3D set $(x, y, \theta)$. Students often forget to mention this, losing 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Triangulation count.** Writing "$n$ triangles" for an $n$-gon instead of $n - 2$. **1 mark off**.
>
> 5. **PIP test edge cases.** Students often forget the strict inequality $(y_i - p_y)(y_{i+1} - p_y) < 0$ and use $\le$, which double-counts vertices lying exactly on the ray. **1–2 marks off**.
>
> 6. **Mermaid / figure labels** in the answer sheet: students often label vertices in clockwise instead of counter-clockwise order in shoelace formula, getting negative area. **Sign convention matters** — use absolute value at the end.
>
> 7. **Configuration space dimensions:** for a 2D translating robot, $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$; for 2D translating **and** rotating, $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ (3-dimensional). Many students mistakenly say $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^3$ for the latter.
>
> 8. **Convexity assumption:** Algorithms like the angle-merge Minkowski sum **only** work for convex polygons. State the assumption explicitly to gain full credit.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Continuous Representation**

- ✅ **Continuous representation** = exact geometric primitives (polygons, polyhedra, algebraic sets) — no resolution.
- ✅ **Workspace $\mathcal{W}$** contains obstacles $\mathcal{O}$ and free space $\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{W} \setminus \mathcal{O}$.
- ✅ **Configuration space $\mathcal{C}$** is the space of all robot configurations; $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{A}$.
- ✅ **C-obstacle** = set of colliding configurations: $\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{A} = \{q \mid \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} \ne \emptyset\}$.
- ✅ **Minkowski sum**: $A \oplus B = \{a + b\}$; for a translating robot, $\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O} = \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{A})$.
- ✅ **Polygon forms**: vertex list, edge list, half-plane intersection $\bigcap H_i^-$.
- ✅ **Convex polygon**: all interior angles $\le \pi$ — compact to describe, easy to plan through.
- ✅ **Triangulation** of an $n$-gon → exactly $n - 2$ triangles (always possible for simple polygons).
- ✅ **Minkowski sum of convex polygons** can be computed in $O(m + n)$ using angle-merge.
- ✅ **Shoelace formula**: $A = \frac{1}{2} \left\vert \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i) \right\vert$.
- ✅ **PIP test**: ray-casting — odd crossings ⇒ inside, even ⇒ outside (Jordan curve theorem).
- ✅ **Planar graph Euler formula**: $V - E + F = 2$ — useful for decomposition proofs.
- ✅ **For rotating robot**: $\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O}$ becomes orientation-dependent, $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ in 2D.
- ✅ **Boolean map algebra**: union, intersection, difference, XOR for combining polygonal regions.
- ✅ **CSG (Constructive Solid Geometry)**: build complex regions from primitives using Boolean ops.
- ✅ **Advantages** of continuous: exact, memory-efficient, supports analytic optimality.
- ✅ **Disadvantage** of continuous: brittle to unknown / uncertain geometry — discrete/sampling is used there.
- ✅ **Real-world uses**: CAD-driven robot work cells, autonomous driving HD maps, surgical path planning, drone no-fly zones, AGV warehouse layouts.
- ✅ **Examiners love** testing: (i) C-obstacle computation by Minkowski sum, (ii) triangulation count, (iii) shoelace area, (iv) PIP parity argument.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-line mantra for the exam:**
> *"Continuous representation is the **mathematically exact** map of $\mathcal{W}$ and $\mathcal{C}$, built from geometric primitives, queried by point-in-polygon / distance / intersection tests, and transformed into C-space via **Minkowski sum**."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decomposition Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTOR PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Decomposition Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: DECOMPOSITION STRATEGIES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decomposition Strategies** are a class of **geometric motion planning algorithms** that partition the robot's **Configuration Space ($C$-space)** into a finite collection of simpler, non-overlapping regions called **cells**, so that path planning can be reduced to a discrete graph-search problem over the resulting **cell adjacency graph**.

Mathematically, the decomposition is defined as:

$$C = C_{free} \cup C_{obs}, \quad C_{free} \cap C_{obs} = \emptyset$$

A decomposition of the free space $C_{free}$ is a finite collection of cells $F_1, F_2, \dots, F_n$ such that:

$$C_{free} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} F_i, \quad F_i \cap F_j = \emptyset \;\; \forall i \neq j$$

Each cell $F_i$ is a convex (or topologically simple) region inside $C_{free}$. Two cells $F_i$ and $F_j$ are declared **adjacent** if they share a common boundary segment of non-zero measure, and a discrete graph $G(V, E)$ is built where $V = \{F_1, \dots, F_n\}$ and $E$ connects adjacent cells.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Decomposition strategies fall under **Module 2 – Geometric Representations and Planning**. Mastery of *Exact Cell Decomposition* and *Approximate Cell Decomposition* is essential for Part B (14-mark) questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are inside a large museum with many irregularly shaped halls and want to go from the **entrance** to the **cafeteria**. Instead of trying to compute one giant continuous path, the museum staff:

1. **Splits the floor plan into rectangular rooms** (cells).
2. Marks each room as **WALKABLE** (free) or **BLOCKED** (obstacle).
3. Draws a **graph** where each room is a node and each shared doorway is an edge.
4. Runs a simple graph search (Dijkstra / A*) on this map.

Decomposition strategies do exactly this for a robot. The "museum" is the **Configuration Space**, the "rooms" are **cells**, and the "doorways" are **adjacency links**. Path planning is converted from a hard continuous geometry problem into an easy discrete graph problem.

### 1.3 Why Decomposition is Needed in Motion Planning

- **$C$-space is continuous**: An infinite number of configurations exist.
- **Obstacles make the geometry non-convex**: A direct straight-line motion is rarely valid.
- **Decomposition makes the problem finite and searchable**: Graph search is polynomial; direct continuous planning is PSPACE-hard.
- **Decomposition supports multi-resolution planning**: Coarse cells for global navigation, fine cells for local manoeuvres.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Term to Memorize:** The decomposition graph $G(V, E)$ is also called a **connectivity graph** or **cell adjacency graph**, and finding a path in $G$ from start cell to goal cell is equivalent to finding a collision-free path in $C_{free}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Decomposition Strategies

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups decomposition strategies into two principal families:

### 2.1.1 Exact Cell Decomposition

In exact decomposition, the union of cells equals $C_{free}$ **exactly** (no approximation). Every cell boundary coincides with a $C$-space obstacle boundary.

| Sub-Type | Cell Shape | Sweep Method | Adjacency Rule |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Trapezoidal** | Vertical slabs / trapezoids | Vertical line sweeping $x = x_s \to x_e$ | Cells share a vertical edge of positive length |
| **Boustrophedon** | Larger merged cells | Vertical line sweeping with **critical-point detection** | Cells sharing a non-zero-length edge above the sweep line are merged |
| **Triangulation** | Triangles | Delaunay / Constrained triangulation | Triangles sharing a side are adjacent |

### 2.1.2 Approximate Cell Decomposition

In approximate decomposition, the obstacle boundaries are approximated onto a fixed cell grid. Some free space is wrongly classified as obstacle, and some obstacle as free, depending on cell resolution.

| Sub-Type | Subdivision Rule | Refinement |
|---|---|---|
| **Uniform Grid** | Fixed $h \times v$ cells of size $\delta$ | None (fixed resolution) |
| **Quadtree** | 4-way recursive subdivision of squares containing mixed (free/obstacle) content | Adaptive |
| **Octree (3D)** | 8-way recursive subdivision of cubes | Adaptive |

> [!NOTE]
> **As the cell size $\delta \to 0$**, an approximate decomposition converges to an exact decomposition. This is the standard *completeness-resolution trade-off* in motion planning.

## 2.2 Why Decomposition Works — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Step 1 — Discretize $C$-space:** Slice the continuous space $C$ into cells.
2. **Step 2 — Classify cells:** For each cell, determine its status: $FREE$, $OBS$ (obstacle), or $MIXED$ (partially occupied).
3. **Step 3 — Build adjacency graph $G$:** Add an edge between any two $FREE$ cells sharing a non-zero-length common boundary.
4. **Step 4 — Locate start and goal cells:** Find $F_s \ni q_{start}$ and $F_g \ni q_{goal}$.
5. **Step 5 — Graph search:** Run BFS / Dijkstra / A* on $G$ to find a sequence of cells $F_{s}, F_{1}, F_{2}, \dots, F_{g}$.
6. **Step 6 — Continuous refinement:** Inside each cell on the path, compute a local collision-free path (straight line usually suffices because cells are convex).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every equation, bound, and complexity result you are expected to memorize for KTU ESE questions on decomposition strategies.

| # | Concept | Formula / Bound | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C$-space partition | $C = C_{free} \cup C_{obs}$ | Dimensionless subsets of $\mathbb{R}^n$ | Disjoint union |
| 2 | Cell union (decomposition) | $C_{free} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} F_i$ | $n$ = number of cells | Pairwise disjoint |
| 3 | Trapezoidal cell count | $n_T = O(n + m)$ | $n$ = total vertices, $m$ = reflex vertices | Upper bound on cells in a polygon |
| 4 | Boustrophedon cell count | $n_B \leq n_T$ | Always fewer than trapezoidal | Cells are larger (merged slabs) |
| 5 | Quadtree cell count | $n_Q = O\!\left(\frac{A \cdot d}{\delta^2}\right)$ | $A$ = area, $d$ = decomposition depth, $\delta$ = leaf cell size | Worst case for mixed regions |
| 6 | Uniform grid cell count | $N = \left\lceil \frac{X}{\delta} \right\rceil \times \left\lceil \frac{Y}{\delta} \right\rceil$ | $X, Y$ = workspace extents (m), $\delta$ = cell size (m) | For 2D workspace |
| 7 | Path-length upper bound (grid) | $L \leq \sqrt{2} \cdot d_{Manhattan}(s,g)$ | $s, g$ = start, goal | Worst-case detour through grid |
| 8 | Trapezoidal decomposition time | $O(n \log n)$ | $n$ = number of obstacle vertices | After sorting vertices by $x$ |
| 9 | Boustrophedon decomposition time | $O(n \log n)$ | Same as trapezoidal | Slightly more bookkeeping |
| 10 | Quadtree build time | $O(N \log N)$ | $N$ = total cells in tree | Recursive splitting |
| 11 | Graph search time (BFS/A*) | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $\vert V \vert = n$ cells, $\vert E \vert \leq 4n$ grid edges | Standard graph complexity |
| 12 | Completeness (exact) | Resolution-complete | Returns a path iff one exists | Exact dec. is exact |
| 13 | Completeness (approximate) | Resolution-complete at scale $\delta$ | Smaller $\delta \Rightarrow$ higher completeness | Approximate dec. converges |
| 14 | Approximation error bound | $\epsilon \leq \delta \cdot \sqrt{2}$ | $\delta$ = cell diagonal | Max deviation of a grid cell from a true obstacle |

> **CRITICAL KTU Tip:** Examiners *love* the contrast between **exact** and **approximate** decomposition. Always be ready to state the trade-off: *exact = slower, complete, exact boundaries* vs *approximate = faster, resolution-complete, possibly unsafe at coarse resolution.*

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (AV):** Lane-level grid decomposition of road networks for behavioural planning.
- **Warehouse Robotics (KIVA / Amazon):** Trapezoidal decomposition of storage floor plans for AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) routing.
- **Drone Swarms:** Octree decomposition of 3D airspace for collision-free trajectory planning.
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci):** Quadtree decomposition of patient CT scan slices for instrument path planning.
- **CAD/CAM Tool Path Generation:** Triangulation of free-form surfaces for 5-axis CNC machining.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of Trapezoidal Cells in a Polygonal Workspace

**Statement:** For a polygonal free space with $n$ vertices and $m$ reflex vertices, the trapezoidal decomposition produces at most $n + m - 1$ cells (a standard textbook result; an equivalent bound is $O(n + m)$).

**Derivation:**

Consider a simple polygon with $n$ vertices. Sort all vertices by their $x$-coordinate; this requires $O(n \log n)$ time. Sweep a vertical line $\ell(x)$ from $x = -\infty$ to $x = +\infty$.

> A "cell event" happens whenever $\ell(x)$ passes through a vertex of the polygon. Between two consecutive cell events, the intersection of $\ell(x)$ with the polygon is a set of disjoint vertical line segments (intervals), each of which becomes the interior of a trapezoid.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of intervals in } \ell(x) \cap C_{free} &= k(x) \\
\text{A reflex vertex can either (a) split an existing interval} & \text{, or (b) merge two intervals} \\
\Rightarrow \text{Number of trapezoidal cells} &\leq 2m + 1
\end{aligned}$$

For a polygon with $n$ vertices and $m$ reflex vertices, the number of cells is bounded by:

$$
n_T \leq n + m - 1
$$

**Why this holds:** Each vertex contributes at most one "new event" to the sweep. Each reflex vertex contributes one extra cell (because of the split or merge operation). The bound $n + m - 1$ accounts for the $-1$ to avoid double-counting the starting interval.

**Step-by-step logic table:**

| Step | Geometric Action | Algebraic Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sort all polygon vertices by $x$ | $O(n \log n)$ preprocessing |
| 2 | Initialise sweep with $k(-\infty) = 0$ | One initial cell on the far left |
| 3 | Encounter a *convex* vertex | $k(x)$ changes by $\pm 1$ |
| 4 | Encounter a *reflex* vertex | $k(x)$ changes by $\pm 1$ AND a split/merge of cells occurs |
| 5 | Finalise at $x = +\infty$ | Total trapezoid count = $2m + (\text{convex contributions})$ |
| 6 | Bound simplifies to | $n_T \leq n + m - 1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The 14-mark Part B question frequently asks *"Compare trapezoidal and Boustrophedon decomposition and derive the cell count bound."* This derivation is the model answer skeleton.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quadtree Cell Count and Refinement Depth

**Statement:** For a 2D workspace of area $A$ decomposed using a quadtree with a minimum cell size $\delta$, the maximum number of cells in the tree is bounded by:

$$
n_Q \leq \sum_{k=0}^{d} 4^{k} = \frac{4^{d+1} - 1}{3}
$$

where $d$ is the maximum decomposition depth, related to $\delta$ by:

$$
d = \left\lceil \log_2 \left( \frac{L}{\delta} \right) \right\rceil
$$

and $L = \sqrt{A}$ is the side length of the bounding square.

**Derivation (step by step):**

At depth $k$, the quadtree contains $4^{k}$ cells (each cell is subdivided into 4 quadrants).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total cells } n_Q &= \sum_{k=0}^{d} 4^{k} \\
&= 4^{0} + 4^{1} + 4^{2} + \dots + 4^{d} \\
&= \frac{4^{d+1} - 1}{4 - 1} \quad \text{(geometric series)} \\
&= \frac{4^{d+1} - 1}{3}
\end{aligned}$$

Now, relate $d$ to the minimum cell size $\delta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Side of a cell at depth } k &= \frac{L}{2^{k}} \\
\text{At max depth } d: \quad \frac{L}{2^{d}} &\geq \delta \\
2^{d} &\leq \frac{L}{\delta} \\
d &= \left\lceil \log_2 \left( \frac{L}{\delta} \right) \right\rceil
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting back:

$$
n_Q \leq \frac{4^{\lceil \log_2(L/\delta) \rceil + 1} - 1}{3} = O\!\left( \frac{L^2}{\delta^2} \right) = O\!\left( \frac{A}{\delta^2} \right)
$$

**Interpretation:** The number of quadtree cells scales as $A / \delta^{2}$. Halving $\delta$ **quadruples** the cell count — the fundamental resolution-vs-memory trade-off.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Footprint Rule:** For a 1 m × 1 m workspace with $\delta = 1$ cm, $n_Q \leq O(10^4)$ cells. For $\delta = 1$ mm, $n_Q \leq O(10^6)$ cells. This is why real systems use *adaptive* (quadtree) rather than *uniform* grids.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Graph-Search Complexity on the Cell Adjacency Graph

**Statement:** A* on the cell adjacency graph of a 2D uniform grid of size $N = X/\delta \times Y/\delta$ has worst-case time and space complexity $O(N \log N)$.

**Derivation:**

For a uniform grid:
- Number of cells $\vert V \vert = N$.
- Each interior cell has 4 neighbours (up/down/left/right); boundary cells have 3 or 2.
- Upper bound on edges: $\vert E \vert \leq 2N$ (each edge counted twice in vertex-neighbour sum).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{A* time complexity} &= O\!\left( \vert E \vert \cdot \log \vert V \vert \right) \\
&= O\!\left( 2N \cdot \log N \right) \\
&= O(N \log N)
\end{aligned}$$

The admissible heuristic used is typically the **Euclidean distance** or **octile distance**:

$$
h(q_s, q_g) = \sqrt{(x_g - x_s)^2 + (y_g - y_s)^2}
$$

For a 2D grid, the 8-connected **octile distance** heuristic (more accurate for 8-neighbour grids) is:

$$
h_{octile}(q_s, q_g) = (\Delta x + \Delta y) + (\sqrt{2} - 2) \cdot \min(\Delta x, \Delta y)
$$

where $\Delta x = \vert x_g - x_s \vert$ and $\Delta y = \vert y_g - y_s \vert$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why octile heuristic?** With $\sqrt{2} - 2 \approx -0.586$, the heuristic is **admissible** (never overestimates) and **consistent**, guaranteeing A* optimality.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Trapezoidal (Exact) | Boustrophedon (Exact) | Uniform Grid (Approx.) | Quadtree (Approx.) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cell shape | Trapezoid / triangle | Trapezoid (merged) | Square | Variable-size square |
| Number of cells | $O(n+m)$ | $\leq n_T$ | $O(A/\delta^{2})$ | $O(A/\delta^{2})$ worst case |
| Build time | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(N)$ | $O(N \log N)$ |
| Completeness | Exact | Exact | Resolution-$\delta$ | Resolution-$\delta$ |
| Path quality | Optimal (in cell sequence) | Optimal | Sub-optimal (detours) | Sub-optimal |
| Memory | Low | Lower | Predictable | Variable |
| Best for | Sparse obstacles | Sparse obstacles | Dense known maps | Mixed-detail maps |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Cell Decomposition Planning Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the functional architecture of a complete cell-decomposition-based motion planner, from raw map input to final executed trajectory.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workspace Map W] --> B[Build Configuration Space C]
    B --> C[Decomposition Strategy Selector]
    C --> D1[Trapezoidal Sweep]
    C --> D2[Boustrophedon Sweep]
    C --> D3[Quadtree Subdivision]
    C --> D4[Uniform Grid Tiling]
    D1 --> E[Cell Classifier FREE OBS MIXED]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Cell Adjacency Graph G V E]
    F --> G[A Star Search on G]
    G --> H[Cell Sequence Path]
    H --> I[Local Path Smoother inside each cell]
    I --> J[Final Collision Free Trajectory]
    J --> K[Robot Controller Execution]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The top of the pipeline is the **continuous** world; the bottom is the **discrete** output.
- The central block is the **decomposition strategy** — the choice between D1/D2/D3/D4 is a designer trade-off.
- The transition from cell sequence (H) to continuous trajectory (J) is called **"graph-to-continuous refinement"** and is essential for actuation.

## 4.2 Quadtree Decomposition — Recursive Subdivision Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Root Square] --> R1[Quadrant NW]
    R0 --> R2[Quadrant NE]
    R0 --> R3[Quadrant SW]
    R0 --> R4[Quadrant SE]
    R1 --> R1A[Sub Q NW]
    R1 --> R1B[Sub Q NE]
    R1 --> R1C[Sub Q SW]
    R1 --> R1D[Sub Q SE]
    R3 --> R3A[Sub Q NW]
    R3 --> R3B[Sub Q NE]
    R3 --> R3C[Sub Q SW]
    R3 --> R3D[Sub Q SE]
    R1B --> R1B1[FREE Leaf]
    R1B --> R1B2[FREE Leaf]
    R1B --> R1B3[MIXED Subdivide]
    R1B --> R1B4[FREE Leaf]
    R3A --> R3A1[FREE Leaf]
    R3A --> R3A2[FREE Leaf]
    R3A --> R3A3[FREE Leaf]
    R3A --> R3A4[FREE Leaf]
```

> **Reading guide:** Each `Sub Q` node represents a recursive quadrant split. Termination occurs at `FREE` or `OBS` leaves. Notice that quadrants `R1B3` and the entirety of `R4` are pruned (their leaves are not drawn) because they are fully classified.

## 4.3 Boustrophedon Sweep — Cell Merging Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L0[Start x = minus infinity] --> S1[Initialize vertical sweep line]
    S1 --> Q1{Vertex encountered}
    Q1 -- Convex vertex --> A1[Increment or decrement interval count k]
    Q1 -- Reflex vertex --> A2[Split or Merge two adjacent slabs]
    A1 --> C1[Create new trapezoidal cell]
    A2 --> M1[Merge two trapezoids into one cell]
    C1 --> N1[Add cell node to graph G]
    M1 --> N1
    N1 --> Q2{End of sweep x = plus infinity}
    Q2 -- No --> S1
    Q2 -- Yes --> FIN[Output G with Boustrophedon cells]
```

> **Key insight:** The Boustrophedon decomposition saves cells at every *reflex vertex* where two adjacent slabs are connected above the sweep line. This is exactly why $n_B \leq n_T$.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q:** Differentiate between **exact** and **approximate** cell decomposition with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Exact Cell Decomposition | Approximate Cell Decomposition |
|---|---|---|
| Cell boundaries | Coincide with obstacle boundaries | Fixed grid; obstacle boundaries approximated |
| Completeness | Exact (returns path iff one exists) | Resolution-complete (depends on cell size $\delta$) |
| Cell shape | Variable (trapezoid, triangle) | Uniform (square) or hierarchical (quadtree) |
| Example | Trapezoidal, Boustrophedon | Uniform grid, Quadtree |
| Computational cost | Higher (must trace obstacle contours) | Lower (regular sampling) |
| Path optimality | Optimal cell sequence | Sub-optimal (detours) |

> *Examiner's note: a 3-mark answer should mention at least **three** of the above contrasts.*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q:** Define **configuration space** and explain why decomposition is necessary for motion planning.

**Model Answer:**
The **configuration Space ($C$-space)** is the set of all possible configurations $q$ a robot can attain. A configuration $q$ is a vector of all joint parameters (e.g., $q = (\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_n)$ for an $n$-DOF arm). $C$-space is partitioned into $C_{free}$ (collision-free) and $C_{obs}$ (in collision).

**Why decomposition is necessary:**
1. $C$-space is **continuous and infinite**; direct search is infeasible.
2. Obstacles make $C_{free}$ **non-convex**, so straight-line paths are blocked.
3. Decomposition **discretizes** $C_{free}$ into finitely many cells.
4. Planning reduces to a **discrete graph search**, solvable in polynomial time.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Trapezoidal & Boustrophedon Decomposition

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q (a)** [7 Marks] Describe the **trapezoidal decomposition** algorithm for a 2D polygonal workspace. Explain its steps with a labelled diagram and state the cell-count bound.

**Q (b)** [7 Marks] Describe the **Boustrophedon decomposition** algorithm. How does it differ from trapezoidal decomposition, and what is its advantage? State the cell count bound for Boustrophedon.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Definition:**
Trapezoidal decomposition partitions the free space $C_{free}$ into a set of **trapezoidal cells** (or triangular cells at corners) whose vertical edges coincide with the $x$-coordinates of polygon vertices.

**Step 2 — Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Sort** all polygon vertices by $x$-coordinate. Complexity: $O(n \log n)$.
2. **Sweep** a vertical line $\ell(x)$ from $x_{min}$ to $x_{max}$.
3. At each $x$-coordinate of a vertex, the intersection $\ell(x) \cap C_{free}$ changes.
4. For each **continuous slab** between two consecutive $x$-events, the cells are the maximal trapezoids whose left and right vertical edges are at consecutive event $x$-coordinates.
5. The result is a set of trapezoidal cells $\{F_1, F_2, \dots, F_{n_T}\}$.

**Step 3 — Cell Count Bound:**

$$
n_T \leq n + m - 1
$$

where $n$ = total polygon vertices, $m$ = number of reflex vertices.

**Step 4 — Adjacency Graph:**
Build a graph $G(V, E)$ where each cell $F_i$ is a vertex. Two cells are connected by an edge if they share a non-zero-length vertical edge.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Boustrophedon Idea:**
The Boustrophedon decomposition ("ox-plowing" pattern) generalizes trapezoidal decomposition by **merging adjacent slabs** that are connected through a reflex vertex above the sweep line.

**Step 2 — Critical Point Detection:**
A **critical point** is a reflex vertex whose removal disconnects the free space above the sweep line. At such a point, the algorithm *merges* the two adjacent slabs into one cell.

**Step 3 — Algorithm Steps:**

1. Sort vertices by $x$.
2. Sweep from left to right.
3. At convex vertices: split/merge normally (like trapezoidal).
4. At reflex vertices: check if the change in connectivity is **0** (topology preserved) or **non-zero** (merge cells).
5. Continue until sweep complete.

**Step 4 — Cell Count Bound:**

$$
n_B \leq n + m' - 1 \leq n_T
$$

where $m' \leq m$ is the number of **"merging" reflex vertices**.

**Step 5 — Advantage:**

- **Fewer cells** than trapezoidal decomposition: $n_B \leq n_T$.
- **Smaller adjacency graph** $\Rightarrow$ faster graph search.
- **Same exactness guarantee** as trapezoidal.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse **Boustrophedon** with **trapezoidal** and lose 2–3 marks. The key distinction is **cell merging at reflex vertices**. Always state the cell count bound explicitly: $n_B \leq n_T$. Failure to mention *why* the bound holds (merging, not splitting) is the most common deduction.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Approximate Decomposition & Quadtree

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q (a)** [7 Marks] Explain **approximate cell decomposition** using a uniform grid. State its complexity, advantages, and limitations.

**Q (b)** [7 Marks] Describe **quadtree decomposition** as an adaptive approximate method. Derive the cell count bound and explain its benefit over a uniform grid.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Algorithm:**
Divide the 2D workspace $W$ of size $X \times Y$ into a uniform grid of square cells of side $\delta$.

**Step 2 — Cell Count:**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{X}{\delta} \right\rceil \times \left\lceil \frac{Y}{\delta} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 3 — Classification Rule:**

- If a cell lies entirely in $C_{free}$ $\Rightarrow$ mark $FREE$.
- If a cell lies entirely in $C_{obs}$ $\Rightarrow$ mark $OBS$.
- If a cell overlaps both $\Rightarrow$ mark $MIXED$ (treated as obstacle for safety).

> *Alternative safer rule:* treat $MIXED$ as $OBS$ to guarantee collision-free paths.

**Step 4 — Adjacency Graph:**
8-neighbour (or 4-neighbour) graph $G$ over FREE cells. Edge exists if neighbours share a side or corner.

**Step 5 — A* Search:**
Use A* with the octile heuristic to find the shortest cell sequence from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$.

**Step 6 — Complexity Table:**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Build time | $O(N)$ |
| Memory | $O(N)$ |
| Search time (A*) | $O(N \log N)$ |
| Path length | Sub-optimal, bounded by $\sqrt{2} \cdot d_{Manhattan}$ |

**Step 7 — Advantages:**
- Simple to implement.
- Predictable memory footprint.
- Parallelisable across cells.

**Step 8 — Limitations:**
- **Resolution-dependent:** small obstacles $< \delta$ may be missed.
- **Inefficient** for sparse, large workspaces (wastes cells in empty regions).
- **Sub-optimal** paths (detours around diagonal obstacles).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Quadtree Idea:**
A quadtree recursively subdivides a 2D square region into **4 equal quadrants** (NW, NE, SW, SE). The subdivision stops only when a quadrant is **entirely FREE** or **entirely OBS**.

**Step 2 — Recursion Rule:**

$$
\text{Square } S \to \begin{cases} \text{STOP}, & \text{if } S \subseteq C_{free} \text{ or } S \subseteq C_{obs} \\ \text{Subdivide into 4 sub-squares}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Cell Count Derivation:**

At depth $k$, there are $4^{k}$ cells. The total number of cells up to depth $d$ is the geometric series:

$$
n_Q = \sum_{k=0}^{d} 4^{k} = \frac{4^{d+1} - 1}{3}
$$

The relation between $d$ and minimum cell size $\delta$ for a workspace of side $L$ is:

$$
d = \left\lceil \log_2 \left( \frac{L}{\delta} \right) \right\rceil
$$

Hence:

$$
n_Q = O\!\left( \frac{L^{2}}{\delta^{2}} \right) = O\!\left( \frac{A}{\delta^{2}} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Benefit Over Uniform Grid:**

| Property | Uniform Grid | Quadtree |
|---|---|---|
| Cell count in empty space | High (many FREE cells) | Low (one large FREE cell) |
| Cell count in obstacle area | High | Low (one OBS cell) |
| Resolution near obstacles | Same everywhere | Higher (recursive splitting) |
| Memory | $O(A/\delta^{2})$ fixed | $O(A/\delta^{2})$ worst case, often much less in practice |
| Implementation | Trivial | Recursive, slightly more complex |

**Step 5 — Application:**
Used in **mixed-detail maps** like urban environments (large free roads, cluttered intersections) and **medical imaging** (large uniform tissue + small critical structures).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Common mistakes students make on Part B (b):
> 1. Forgetting the **$-1/3$** correction in the geometric series sum — full marks depend on the correct formula $n_Q = (4^{d+1} - 1)/3$.
> 2. Confusing the quadtree's 2D quadrant count (4) with the octree's 3D octant count (8). Always state the dimension explicitly.
> 3. Failing to explain *why* quadtree beats uniform grid: **adaptive resolution** = fewer wasted cells in homogeneous regions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Decomposition Strategies**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ **Configuration Space ($C$-space):** $C = C_{free} \cup C_{obs}$, $C_{free} \cap C_{obs} = \emptyset$.
- ☐ **Cell decomposition:** $C_{free} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} F_i$ with $F_i$ pairwise disjoint.
- ☐ **Exact decomposition:** Cell boundaries = obstacle boundaries; no approximation.
- ☐ **Approximate decomposition:** Cell boundaries = fixed grid; obstacle boundaries approximated.
- ☐ **Cell adjacency graph $G(V, E)$:** Vertices = cells, edges = shared non-zero-length boundaries.
- ☐ **Resolution-completeness:** Algorithm returns a path iff one exists *at the given resolution $\delta$*.

### Key Algorithms
- ☐ **Trapezoidal:** Vertical sweep, cells = vertical slabs / trapezoids, count $O(n + m)$.
- ☐ **Boustrophedon:** Vertical sweep with cell merging at reflex vertices, count $\leq n_T$.
- ☐ **Uniform grid:** Fixed $\delta \times \delta$ cells, count $N = \lceil X/\delta \rceil \times \lceil Y/\delta \rceil$.
- ☐ **Quadtree:** Recursive 4-way subdivision, count $n_Q = (4^{d+1} - 1)/3$.

### Critical Formulas
- ☐ Trapezoidal bound: $n_T \leq n + m - 1$.
- ☐ Quadtree bound: $n_Q = O(A / \delta^{2})$.
- ☐ Quadtree depth: $d = \lceil \log_2(L / \delta) \rceil$.
- ☐ A* time: $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) = O(N \log N)$ for grid.
- ☐ Octile heuristic: $h = (\Delta x + \Delta y) + (\sqrt{2} - 2)\min(\Delta x, \Delta y)$.

### Examiner's Favourite Comparisons
- ☐ **Exact vs Approximate** — must state 3 contrasts (completeness, cost, accuracy).
- ☐ **Trapezoidal vs Boustrophedon** — must mention cell-merging and $n_B \leq n_T$.
- ☐ **Uniform grid vs Quadtree** — must mention adaptive resolution and $O(A/\delta^{2})$ bound.
- ☐ **Completeness types:** *Exact* (path exists $\Leftrightarrow$ algorithm finds it) vs *Resolution-complete* (path exists at scale $\delta$ $\Leftrightarrow$ algorithm finds it at that scale).

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ☐ Do **not** confuse *cell adjacency* with *cell intersection* — adjacency is via a shared boundary segment of positive measure.
- ☐ Do **not** forget to convert from cell sequence to a **continuous trajectory** before sending to the robot controller.
- ☐ Do **not** mix up quadtree (4 children) with octree (8 children) — they apply to 2D and 3D respectively.
- ☐ Always specify **$MIXED$-cell handling rule** (treat as OBS for safety) when describing approximate decomposition.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Map Representation** in robot motion planning refers to the formal mathematical encoding of an environment's spatial structure into a data structure that a robot can use for localization, perception, and path planning. The representation acts as the robot's internal "world model."

**Current Challenges in Map Representation** refer to the open research and engineering problems that arise when a robot must build, maintain, and reason about such an internal world model under realistic operating constraints — including sensor noise, dynamic obstacles, limited memory, real-time processing deadlines, and semantic ambiguity.

The most widely used representations fall into three broad families:

| Representation Family | Core Idea | Typical Data Structure |
|---|---|---|
| **Metric (Grid-based)** | Discretize space into cells/voxels and store occupancy | Occupancy Grid, Voxel Grid, Point Cloud |
| **Feature-based** | Extract geometric primitives (lines, planes, corners) | Landmark Maps, EKF Maps |
| **Topological** | Encode connectivity between distinct places | Graph of nodes & edges |
| **Hybrid / Semantic** | Combine metric precision with semantic labels | HD Maps, Octomap with labels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of *Robot Motion Planning* explicitly requires students to compare these representations and discuss why none of them is a "universal solution." Examiners frequently frame Part-B questions around trade-offs.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Robot as a Blind Architect"

Imagine a **blind architect** who is asked to draw a perfect floor plan of a building while walking through it, but:

- They can only "feel" the walls with a **white cane** of fixed length (limited sensor range).
- People and furniture are **moving around** them (dynamic obstacles).
- The building has **hundreds of floors** and thousands of rooms (scalability).
- The cane sometimes **bounces off glass** that they cannot detect (sensor noise / false negatives).
- They have only a **small notebook** to record everything (memory constraint).
- The architect must give directions to a visitor **within seconds** (real-time planning).

This blind architect is, metaphorically, the robot. Every challenge discussed below is a literal manifestation of one of these constraints in robotics.

## 1.3 Why These Challenges Matter in KTU Context

In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education framework, this topic directly maps to:

- **CO2:** *Analyze map representation techniques and their limitations for motion planning.*
- **CO3:** *Evaluate path planning algorithms under real-world uncertainties.*

The topic is a **high-weightage, integrative module** because it bridges perception (Module 1) and planning algorithms (Modules 3–4). KTU examiners expect students to articulate **why** a chosen representation fails in a given scenario, not just list its features.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Pillars of Map Representation Challenges

Below is a structured breakdown of the active research and engineering challenges, written in the KTU 14-mark answer style.

### Pillar 1 — Scale and Memory Complexity

- **Occupancy grids** scale as $O(n^2)$ in 2D and $O(n^3)$ in 3D, where $n$ is the resolution per axis.
- A high-resolution 3D voxel grid covering a $100\,\text{m} \times 100\,\text{m} \times 10\,\text{m}$ warehouse at $0.05\,\text{m}$ resolution would require:

$$N_{\text{voxels}} = \left(\frac{100}{0.05}\right) \times \left(\frac{100}{0.05}\right) \times \left(\frac{10}{0.05}\right) = 2000 \times 2000 \times 200 = 8 \times 10^{8}$$

- Storing each voxel as a 1-byte log-odd value gives **≈ 800 MB** of raw memory — impractical for embedded robots.

### Pillar 2 — Dynamic and Non-Static Environments

- Classical occupancy grids assume a **static world** (the *static world assumption*).
- Moving pedestrians, doors opening/closing, and movable furniture violate this assumption.
- Solutions like **decay-based occupancy** or **velocity-obstacle layers** are only partial fixes.

### Pillar 3 — Sensor Uncertainty and Noise

- Range finders (LiDAR, sonar, depth cameras) return measurements corrupted by:
  - Gaussian range noise $\sigma_r$
  - Angular quantization error
  - Specular reflections (false negatives on glass/mirrors)
  - Mixed-pixel effects at object boundaries
- The inverse sensor model $p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$ must propagate these uncertainties into the map via Bayesian filtering.

### Pillar 4 — Perceptual Aliasing and the Data Association Problem

- When two distinct locations look identical (corridors, symmetric rooms), the robot cannot tell if it is revisiting Place A or Place B.
- This causes **loop-closure failures** in SLAM, leading to map corruption.

### Pillar 5 — Computational Cost of Real-Time Updates

- Updating a grid map with every LiDAR sweep (e.g., 10 Hz, 100k points/s) demands efficient data structures — naive updates are $O(P)$ per scan, where $P$ is the number of points.

### Pillar 6 — Semantic Gap

- Geometric maps answer *"Where can I go?"* but not *"Where SHOULD I go?"* — they do not encode that a road is for cars and a sidewalk is for pedestrians.
- Semantic SLAM attempts to close this gap using CNN-based segmentation, but introduces new latency and training-data demands.

### Pillar 7 — Lifelong and Long-Term Autonomy

- Over weeks of operation, the map must remain consistent even as the world changes (seasonal vegetation, construction, furniture rearrangement).
- This is the **lifelong SLAM** problem — currently an active research frontier.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Grid memory | $M = \left(\frac{L_x}{r}\right)\left(\frac{L_y}{r}\right)\left(\frac{L_z}{r}\right) \cdot b$ | $b$ = bytes/cell, $r$ = resolution |
| Occupancy log-odds update | $l_{t}(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + l_{\text{inv}}(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) - l_0$ | $l = \log\frac{p(m_i)}{1-p(m_i)}$ |
| Inverse sensor model (probabilistic) | $p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)\,p(m_i)}{p(z_t \mid x_t)}$ | Bayes' theorem |
| Time complexity (Bresenham ray update) | $O(r_{\max}/\Delta\theta)$ per beam | $\Delta\theta$ = angular resolution |
| Octree node count | $O(N \log(N/k))$ for $N$ occupied voxels | $k$ = points per node |
| Voronoi graph edges | $E \le 3V - 6$ for planar graph | $V$ = vertices |
| Probabilistic roadmap samples | $E \approx kN$ for $k$-nearest neighbors | $N$ = nodes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** In the log-odds update, students often forget the **$-l_0$** term (prior log-odds). This is a guaranteed 1-mark loss in KTU valuations.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These challenges directly determine whether autonomous systems succeed in:

- **Autonomous vehicles** (handling dynamic traffic, HD-map maintenance).
- **Warehouse robots** (AMRs in Amazon-style fulfillment centers — scale problem).
- **Search-and-rescue UAVs** (GPS-denied, dynamic rubble environments).
- **Domestic service robots** (semantic understanding of rooms, lifelong mapping).
- **Planetary rovers** (NASA Mars rovers — extreme memory and bandwidth constraints).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Memory Footprint of a 3D Voxel Grid

This derivation is a classic KTU Part-B sub-question. We will write out every algebraic step explicitly.

**Problem:** Compute the memory required to store a 3D voxel grid covering a $50\,\text{m} \times 50\,\text{m} \times 5\,\text{m}$ room at a resolution of $r = 0.1\,\text{m}$, using 1 byte per voxel.

**Step 1 — Compute the number of voxels along each axis.**

$$\begin{aligned}
n_x &= \frac{L_x}{r} = \frac{50\,\text{m}}{0.1\,\text{m}} = 500 \text{ voxels} \\[4pt]
n_y &= \frac{L_y}{r} = \frac{50\,\text{m}}{0.1\,\text{m}} = 500 \text{ voxels} \\[4pt]
n_z &= \frac{L_z}{r} = \frac{5\,\text{m}}{0.1\,\text{m}} = 50 \text{ voxels}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total number of voxels.**

$$N = n_x \cdot n_y \cdot n_z$$

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= 500 \times 500 \times 50 \\[4pt]
  &= 250{,}000 \times 50 \\[4pt]
  &= 12{,}500{,}000 \text{ voxels} \\[4pt]
  &= 1.25 \times 10^{7}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by bytes per voxel.**

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= N \cdot b \\[4pt]
  &= 1.25 \times 10^{7} \times 1\,\text{byte} \\[4pt]
  &= 12.5\,\text{MB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to MB / GB for context.**

$$M = \frac{12.5}{1024} \approx 0.0122\,\text{GB} \approx 12.2\,\text{MB}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A single room at 10 cm resolution is tractable. However, if we **double the resolution to 5 cm** in all three dimensions, the memory grows by a factor of $2^3 = 8$, yielding $\approx 97.6\,\text{MB}$. For a full building or city block, this is why **octrees** or **point clouds** are preferred over dense voxel grids.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Log-Odds Occupancy Update

**Problem:** Show that the standard occupancy-grid update can be written in additive log-odds form.

**Step 1 — Start with the recursive Bayesian update.**

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the static-world Markov assumption.**

Under the assumption that the map is static, the prior $p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})$ already encodes all past evidence.

**Step 3 — Introduce the log-odds notation.**

$$l(x) = \log \frac{p(x)}{1 - p(x)} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad p(x) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{l(x)}}$$

**Step 4 — Take the log of both sides of the update.**

$$\begin{aligned}
l_t(m_i) &= \log\frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})} \\[4pt]
         &= \log\frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)}{1 - p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)} + \log\frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})} \\[4pt]
         &= l_{\text{inv}}(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) + l_{t-1}(m_i)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Add the prior correction term $l_0$.**

$$l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + l_{\text{inv}}(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) - l_0$$

> **Why the $-l_0$ term matters:** Without it, the very first measurement's contribution is double-counted because the initial prior $p(m_i) = 0.5$ contributes a non-zero $l_0$.

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Challenge | Affected Representation | Mitigation Strategy | Residual Issue |
|---|---|---|---|
| Memory blow-up | Dense voxel grids | Octree, OGM, hashing | Pointer overhead, slower access |
| Dynamic obstacles | Static occupancy grids | Velocity layers, decay | Cannot predict motion models |
| Sensor noise | All probabilistic maps | Bayesian filtering, Kalman | Computational cost |
| Perceptual aliasing | Topological, feature maps | Visual bag-of-words, place recognition | Computationally heavy descriptors |
| Real-time updates | 3D point clouds | Voxel hashing, incremental | Still limited at high rates |
| Semantic gap | Pure geometric maps | Semantic SLAM (CNN) | Requires labeled training data |
| Lifelong mapping | All current methods | Sub-mapping, change detection | Open research problem |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Map Representation Challenges Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Map Representation Challenges]:::root --> B[Data Structure Issues]:::cat
    A --> C[Environmental Issues]:::cat
```
