```mermaid
    A --> F[Cognitive Issues]:::cat

    B --> B1[Memory Blowup]
    B --> B2[Scalability Limit]
    B --> B3[Update Latency]

    C --> C1[Dynamic Obstacles]
    C --> C2[Static World Assumption]
    C --> C3[Long Term Drift]

    D --> D1[Range Noise]
    D --> D2[Specular Reflection]
    D --> D3[Aliasing]

    E --> E1[Real Time Deadline]
    E --> E2[Multi Robot Fusion]
    E --> E3[Bandwidth Limit]

    F --> F1[Semantic Gap]
    F --> F2[Place Recognition]
    F --> F3[Lifelong Learning]

    classDef root fill:#1f2937,stroke:#111827,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cat fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0369a1,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px
    classDef leaf fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#111827,stroke-width:1px
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each colored cluster represents a *category* of challenge. In a 14-mark answer, a student who can map each leaf-node to a concrete robotic scenario (e.g., *"specular reflection on glass doors in malls"*) earns full methodology marks.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Sensor-to-Map Update Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Raw Sensor Data]:::io --> P[Preprocessing]:::proc
    P --> R[Registration and Pose Estimate]:::proc
    R --> IS[Inverse Sensor Model]:::proc
    IS --> B[Bayesian Fusion]:::proc
    B --> M[Updated Map Representation]:::out
    M --> PL[Planner Module]:::out
    PL --> AC[Robot Action]:::out

    NO[Noise Sources]:::noise --> S
    UN[Pose Uncertainty]:::noise --> R
    DY[Dynamic Objects]:::noise --> M

    classDef io fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#111827
    classDef proc fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#111827
    classDef out fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#111827
    classDef noise fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,color:#111827
```

> **Engineering note:** The **red boxes** are the principal *sources of error* that make map representation hard. A strong KTU answer should connect them to the blue/green processing stages and explain the failure modes.

## 4.3 Comparison Block — Representation vs. Challenge Suitability

| Representation | Memory | Dynamics | Noise Robust | Real-Time | Semantic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Occupancy Grid | Low-Med | Poor | Strong | Excellent | Weak |
| Point Cloud | High | Poor | Strong | Good | Weak |
| Octomap | Medium | Poor | Strong | Good | Weak |
| Topological | Very Low | Good | Medium | Excellent | Medium |
| Feature Map | Low | Medium | Strong | Good | Medium |
| HD Semantic Map | High | Medium | Strong | Medium | Excellent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** This kind of table is the most efficient way to earn the 7-mark "compare" sub-question in Part B. Always include a column for **computational cost / real-time feasibility** — examiners look for it explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** What is the *static world assumption* in occupancy grid mapping, and why does it fail in real-world deployments?

**Model Answer:**
The *static world assumption* states that the environment being mapped does not change over time — only the robot moves. This lets the map be treated as a fixed probabilistic field updated by accumulating sensor evidence. It fails in real-world deployments because real environments contain moving entities (pedestrians, vehicles, opening doors, shifting furniture). When a moving obstacle is observed, its cells are updated as if the obstacle were *permanently* present, producing **ghost maps** (faded trails) and corrupting future localization. Modern systems relax this assumption by using velocity layers, decay-based occupancy, or short-term "foreground" maps separate from the long-term "background" map. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** Distinguish between *metric* and *topological* map representations, and give one scenario where each is most suitable.

**Model Answer:**
A **metric map** stores the environment in geometrically accurate coordinates — distances, angles, and shapes are preserved. Examples include occupancy grids and point clouds. It is most suitable for **local obstacle avoidance and trajectory optimization**, where millimeter-scale precision matters (e.g., robotic arm navigation in a cluttered cell).

A **topological map** stores the environment as a graph of *places* (nodes) and *connections* (edges) without geometric coordinates. It is most suitable for **large-scale long-range navigation**, where symbolic reasoning dominates (e.g., a delivery robot deciding "go from Room A to Room B via Corridor C"). Topological maps are memory-efficient but lack the precision for local maneuvering. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q3]**
>
> **Question A (14 marks):**
> *(a)* [7 marks] Discuss the major challenges in representing a large-scale 3D environment using traditional occupancy grids. Support your answer with quantitative memory analysis.
> *(b)* [7 marks] Explain how hierarchical data structures like **Octomaps** address these challenges. Derive the memory advantage for a sparse environment.

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Challenges in Large-Scale 3D Occupancy Grids [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Resolution vs. Memory Trade-off.** [2 marks]
A 3D grid scales as $O(n^3)$. For a $100\,\text{m}^3$ warehouse at $0.05\,\text{m}$ resolution, we derived $N = 8 \times 10^8$ voxels in Section 3.1. At 1 byte/voxel, this is $\approx 800\,\text{MB}$ — infeasible for embedded platforms (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson has only 8–32 GB shared with the OS).

**Step 2 — Uniform Resolution Waste.** [2 marks]
Most of the volume is empty space. Uniform grids waste memory on *free* cells, which are usually the vast majority. Realistic indoor scenes have occupancy ratios below 1%.

**Step 3 — Update Cost and I/O Bottleneck.** [1 mark]
Updating millions of cells per LiDAR sweep (10 Hz) creates severe memory bandwidth pressure, leading to missed real-time deadlines.

**Step 4 — Lack of Semantic Information.** [1 mark]
The grid only stores occupancy probability, not the *type* of object. A chair and a wall both become "occupied," preventing semantic reasoning.

**Step 5 — Susceptibility to Dynamic Changes.** [1 mark]
A static grid cannot model moving objects; integrating dynamic information requires auxiliary layers.

#### Part (b) — Octomap Solution and Sparse-Environment Memory Advantage [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Octree Concept.** [2 marks]
An **Octomap** recursively subdivides 3D space into **eight octants**. A cube containing only free space is replaced by a single pointer (or `NULL`), and subdivision stops — this is the **pruning** property.

**Step 2 — Memory Analysis for Sparse Environment.** [3 marks]
Suppose the actual occupied volume is $V_{\text{occ}} = 0.1\%$ of the total volume $V$. For a cube of side $L = 100\,\text{m}$:

$$V = L^3 = 10^6\,\text{m}^3, \quad V_{\text{occ}} = 10^3\,\text{m}^3$$

A naive grid at $r = 0.1\,\text{m}$ uses $M_{\text{grid}} = (L/r)^3 = 10^9$ cells $\approx 1\,\text{GB}$. An octree, however, needs only enough nodes to represent the *occupied* sub-volumes. The node count grows as:

$$N_{\text{octree}} = O(N_{\text{occupied}} \log(L/r))$$

For $N_{\text{occupied}} = 10^6$ occupied cells (since $0.1\% \times 10^9 = 10^6$), the octree needs roughly $10^6 \times \log_2(1000) \approx 10^6 \times 10 = 10^7$ node entries. At 16 bytes/node, this is $\approx 160\,\text{MB}$ — a **6× reduction**, and the savings grow dramatically as environments get larger or sparser.

**Step 3 — Additional Benefits.** [2 marks]
- **Multi-resolution queries** — coarse nodes for global planning, fine nodes for local avoidance.
- **Probabilistic updates preserved** — log-odds occupancy still maintained per node.
- **Incremental updates** — only changed sub-trees need modification.

---

> **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice:**
> *(a)* [7 marks] Explain the *data association* and *perceptual aliasing* challenges in feature-based map representations. How do modern place-recognition systems address them?
> *(b)* [7 marks] Discuss the **lifelong SLAM** problem. Why is long-term map maintenance fundamentally harder than one-time mapping?

### Model Solution — Question B (Outline Key Points)

#### Part (a) — Data Association & Perceptual Aliasing [7 marks]

- **Definition** [1 mark]: Data association is the problem of determining whether two sensor observations correspond to the *same physical feature* or to *different features*.
- **Perceptual aliasing** [2 marks]: Distinct locations that produce identical sensor signatures (e.g., symmetric corridors, repeated pillars) cause false correspondences.
- **Failure mode** [1 mark]: Wrong association → wrong loop-closure → catastrophic map divergence.
- **Solutions** [3 marks]: (i) Bag-of-Visual-Words with TF-IDF weighting; (ii) NetVLAD deep features; (iii) Geometric verification (RANSAC + PnP); (iv) Sequence-based place recognition (SeqSLAM).

#### Part (b) — Lifelong SLAM [7 marks]

- **Definition** [1 mark]: Operation of a mapping system over weeks/months in an environment that itself changes.
- **Why harder than one-shot mapping** [3 marks]: (i) Accumulated drift despite loop closures; (ii) stale map features; (iii) seasonal/structural changes; (iv) memory growth unbounded.
- **Active approaches** [3 marks]: (i) Sub-map based architectures (e.g., SLAM++); (ii) Change detection with re-observation scoring; (iii) Map compression via key-frame sparsification; (iv) Semantic anchoring of persistent landmarks.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing *map representation* with *SLAM algorithm*.** The map is the *output*; SLAM is the *process*. Examiners deduct 2 marks if the answer does not distinguish them.
> 2. **Omitting the $-l_0$ prior term** in the log-odds derivation. Always show it.
> 3. **Forgetting units** in memory calculations — write "MB" or "GB" explicitly, not just the number.
> 4. **Vague qualitative answers** — "it is slow" earns 0; "it is $O(n^3)$ and infeasible beyond $n=1000$" earns full marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the dynamic-environment challenge** — it appears in almost every KTU question paper since 2022.
> 6. **Using single-resolution analysis only** — examiners expect a *multi-resolution* or *hierarchical* discussion for full marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Map representations** are of three families: metric, topological, and hybrid/semantic.
- **Static world assumption** is the single most violated assumption in practice.
- **Log-odds form** of occupancy update: $l_t = l_{t-1} + l_{\text{inv}} - l_0$ — never forget the prior term.
- **Memory scales** as $O(n^3)$ in 3D grids; **Octomaps** reduce this to $O(N_{\text{occ}} \log(L/r))$.
- **Perceptual aliasing** causes loop-closure failures in feature/SLAM systems.
- **Dynamic obstacles** require either layered maps, decay models, or short-term velocity fields.
- **Real-time updates** depend on efficient ray-casting (e.g., Bresenham's, AMCL-style incremental updates).
- **Semantic gap** is closed by CNN-based segmentation, at the cost of GPU memory and training data.
- **Lifelong SLAM** is an open research problem; sub-mapping and change detection are the main paradigms.
- **Inverse sensor model** $p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$ is the bridge between noisy sensor data and the probabilistic map.
- **KTU 2024 focus:** Trade-off analysis between representations, not memorization of one specific algorithm.
- **Common exam phrasing:** *"Discuss the challenges of representing a dynamic environment"* — always answer with at least: scale, noise, dynamics, semantics, and real-time.
- **Unit discipline:** Always report memory in MB/GB, time in ms/s, and resolution in meters.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Probabilistic Map-Based Localization - Markov Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Probabilistic Map-Based Localization - Markov Localization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Markov Localization** is a recursive, probabilistic algorithm that estimates a robot's pose (position and orientation) within a known, static environment map by maintaining a probability distribution—called a *belief*—over all possible poses in the state space. It is rooted in the **Bayes Filter** framework and the **Markov Assumption**, which states that the robot's current state is a sufficient statistic for predicting the next state and the next measurement, given the control input and the observation.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the belief at time $t$ is defined as:

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

where:
- $x_t$ is the robot pose at time $t$ (typically $x_t = (x, y, \theta)$)
- $z_{1:t} = \{z_1, z_2, \dots, z_t\}$ is the sequence of measurements
- $u_{1:t} = \{u_1, u_2, \dots, u_t\}$ is the sequence of control actions (odometry)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Markov Localization is the *gold standard* for global localization. Unlike Kalman Filter-based localization, it can represent **multi-modal** (ambiguous) beliefs and recover from **global localization failures** (the "kidnapped robot problem"). It is, therefore, a compulsory topic in Module 2 of OEBRT833.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Markov Localization is **environment-model-based** (map-based), as opposed to the map-less approaches of dead reckoning. It is also **discrete-state-capable**, which makes it far more general than the Kalman Filter that mandates Gaussian (unimodal) posteriors.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you wake up in an unknown room in a large hotel. You have a **floor plan** of the hotel (the *map*). You don't know which room you are in, but you have three clues:
- The layout of doors and corridors you can see (*sensor measurement $z_t$*)
- The direction of your footsteps and how far you've walked (*control input $u_t$*)
- A rough guess of where you might be (*prior belief*)

You start with a **list of probabilities** for every room in the hotel. With every step you take, you **predict** where you could be by using the room layout. Then, when you open a door and see a corridor with a painting on the left wall, you **update** your belief—you are more likely to be in a room whose door opens to such a corridor. After several steps, your belief "peaks" at the room you are most likely in. This recursive *predict–update* cycle is exactly **Markov Localization**.

---

### 1.3 The Three Flavors of Markov Localization

| Flavor | State Space | Granularity | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Grid-Based (Metric)** | Discrete grid cells | Fine (e.g., 10 cm × 10 cm) | **High** |
| **Topological** | Discrete places (nodes) | Coarse (rooms, junctions) | **Low** |
| **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** | Set of weighted samples | Variable | **Medium** |

All three share the **same recursive Bayes filter mathematics**; they differ only in how the belief is represented internally.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU university exams, when a question says "explain Markov localization", you **must** mention that the algorithm is independent of the representation (grid, topological, or particle filter), and the *predict–update* cycle is the unifying principle.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Step Recursive Cycle

Markov Localization operates as a **two-step recursive estimator** for every time step $t$:

**Step 1 — Prediction (Motion Update):** The belief from the previous time step is propagated through the motion model.

**Step 2 — Measurement Update (Correction):** The predicted belief is multiplied by the likelihood of the current sensor observation, then normalized.

### 2.2 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. **Initialization:** Set the initial belief $bel(x_0)$ to a uniform distribution over the entire state space (for global localization), or to a tight Gaussian around the known initial pose (for position tracking).
2. **Perception Sampling:** Acquire a control input $u_t$ (from wheel encoders / IMU) and a measurement $z_t$ (from LiDAR / camera / sonar).
3. **Prediction:** Compute $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ by integrating the previous belief over all possible prior poses, weighted by the **motion model** $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$.
4. **Update:** Multiply the predicted belief by the **measurement model** $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ for every pose $x_t$.
5. **Normalization:** Divide by $\eta$, the normalizer, so that the belief sums to unity.
6. **Output:** Publish $bel(x_t)$ and repeat for $t+1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two steps?** Pure odometry drifts without bound; pure sensor readings are noisy and aliased. The Bayes filter mathematically *fuses* both, with odometry providing the *prediction* and sensors providing the *correction*. The Markov assumption ensures no older information needs to be retained.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Belief definition | $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Probability of being at $x_t$ given all history. |
| 2 | Markov Assumption | $p(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_{t-1})$ | Current state is sufficient summary of past. |
| 3 | Prediction (continuous) | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, bel(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$ | Total probability of $x_t$ under motion model. |
| 4 | Prediction (discrete grid) | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \sum_{x_{t-1}} p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, bel(x_{t-1})$ | Sum over all grid cells. |
| 5 | Measurement update | $bel(x_t) = \eta\, p(z_t \mid x_t)\, \overline{bel}(x_t)$ | Bayesian correction by sensor likelihood. |
| 6 | Normalizer | $\eta = \dfrac{1}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{x_t} p(z_t \mid x_t)\, \overline{bel}(x_t)}$ | Forces belief to integrate to $1$. |
| 7 | Bayes rule | $p(A \mid B) = \dfrac{p(B \mid A)\, p(A)}{p(B)}$ | Foundation of the update step. |
| 8 | Total probability | $p(B) = \sum_i p(B \mid A_i)\, p(A_i)$ | Foundation of the prediction step. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All probabilities are in **dimensionless units** [unitless]. State variables $x_t$ have units of [m] for position and [rad] for orientation. Control inputs $u_t$ from wheel odometry have units of [m/s] or [rad/s].

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs) in Warehouses:** Amazon's Kiva robots and Fetch Robotics use grid-based Markov localization to navigate aisles with centimetre accuracy.
- **Self-Driving Cars:** Multi-modal MCL handles the ambiguity of returning to a previously visited tunnel.
- **Domestic Vacuum Robots:** iRobot Roomba uses topological Markov localization to dock at the charging station.
- **Underwater ROVs (AUVs):** Particle-filter MCL dominates, since GPS is unavailable and acoustic beacons are sparse.
- **Planetary Rovers:** NASA's Mars rovers combine MCL with visual landmarks because terrain is partially unknown.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Recursive Bayes Filter

We start from the posterior we wish to estimate:

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

We condition the right-hand side on the latest control $u_t$ and the latest measurement $z_t$:

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_t, u_{1:t-1})$$

Apply **Bayes' Rule** (the measurement $z_t$ is conditioned on $x_t$):

$$bel(x_t) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})\, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

Apply the **Markov Assumption** ($z_t$ depends only on $x_t$ if the world is Markovian in the state):

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$$

Drop $u_{1:t-1}$ from the second term since the past controls are encoded in $x_{t-1}$ (Markov):

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{t}, u_{1:t-1})$$

Introduce the previous state $x_{t-1}$ via the law of total probability (marginalization):

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_t) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$$

Recognize that $p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) = bel(x_{t-1})$, and define the **predicted belief**:

$$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, bel(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$$

Putting it all together:

$$bel(x_t) = \eta\, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, bel(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$$

This is the **complete recursive Bayes filter** for continuous state. For discrete grid localization, the integral becomes a sum:

$$bel(x_t) = \eta\, p(z_t \mid x_t) \sum_{x_{t-1}} p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, bel(x_{t-1})$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — 1-D Corridor with Three Doors

Consider a robot in a 1-D corridor with **3 door cells** (state space $X = \{D_1, D_2, D_3\}$). The robot's only sensor reports "door present" or "no door" with a known likelihood model.

**Sensor Model:**

| | $p(z=\text{door} \mid x)$ | $p(z=\text{nodoor} \mid x)$ |
|---|---|---|
| $x = D_i$ | $0.7$ | $0.3$ |
| $x \neq D_i$ | $0.1$ | $0.9$ |

**Motion Model:** The robot moves one cell to the right with probability $0.9$, stays with probability $0.1$ (no wrap-around past $D_3$).

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 0 — Initialization (uniform belief):**

$$bel_0(D_1) = bel_0(D_2) = bel_0(D_3) = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 1 — Prediction (Robot moves right, then takes a measurement $z_1 =$ "door"):**

The prediction for $D_i$ is the total probability of arriving at $D_i$ from $D_{i-1}$ or $D_i$:

$$\overline{bel}_1(D_1) = 0.1 \cdot bel_0(D_1) + 0.9 \cdot bel_0(D_0)$$
$$\overline{bel}_1(D_1) = 0.1 \cdot \frac{1}{3} + 0 = 0.0333$$

$$\overline{bel}_1(D_2) = 0.1 \cdot bel_0(D_2) + 0.9 \cdot bel_0(D_1)$$
$$\overline{bel}_1(D_2) = 0.1 \cdot \frac{1}{3} + 0.9 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 0.3333$$

$$\overline{bel}_1(D_3) = 0.1 \cdot bel_0(D_3) + 0.9 \cdot bel_0(D_2)$$
$$\overline{bel}_1(D_3) = 0.1 \cdot \frac{1}{3} + 0.9 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 0.3333$$

Wait — the corridor has only 3 door cells. If the robot is at $D_3$ and tries to move right, it stays at $D_3$ (boundary condition). So we need:

$$\overline{bel}_1(D_3) = 0.1 \cdot bel_0(D_3) + 0.9 \cdot bel_0(D_2) + 0.9 \cdot bel_0(D_3)$$
$$\overline{bel}_1(D_3) = 0.1 \cdot \frac{1}{3} + 0.9 \cdot \frac{1}{3} + 0.9 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 0.6333$$

Predicted belief vector before normalization check (sums to $0.0333 + 0.3333 + 0.6333 = 1.0$, consistent).

**Step 2 — Update with $z_1 =$ "door":**

$$bel_1(D_i) = \eta\, p(z_1 = \text{door} \mid x = D_i)\, \overline{bel}_1(D_i)$$

We need $p(z_1 = \text{door} \mid x = D_i) = 0.7$ for door cells, but here the state space is the *door cell itself*, so we treat every cell as a door. Use $p(z_1 = \text{door} \mid D_1) = 0.7$, etc.

$$bel_1(D_1) = \eta \cdot 0.7 \cdot 0.0333 = 0.0233\,\eta$$
$$bel_1(D_2) = \eta \cdot 0.7 \cdot 0.3333 = 0.2333\,\eta$$
$$bel_1(D_3) = \eta \cdot 0.7 \cdot 0.6333 = 0.4433\,\eta$$

Sum $= (0.0233 + 0.2333 + 0.4433)\,\eta = 0.7000\,\eta$.

Therefore $\eta = 1/0.7000 \approx 1.4286$.

$$bel_1(D_1) \approx 0.0333, \quad bel_1(D_2) \approx 0.3333, \quad bel_1(D_3) \approx 0.6333$$

> [!NOTE]
> The result matches the predicted belief because, in this simplified model, every cell is a door, so the likelihood is uniform. The benefit of Markov localization becomes dramatic when the **sensor model is non-uniform** (e.g., a corridor that is wider in one region) — that is when the update step sharpens the belief.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix of Algorithm Variants

| Algorithm | Belief Representation | Multi-modal? | Continuous? | Global Localization? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Histogram / Grid Markov** | Discrete grid histogram | **Yes** | No (discretized) | **Yes** |
| **Kalman Filter** | Single Gaussian | No | Yes | No (position tracking only) |
| **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** | Single Gaussian (linearized) | No | Yes (approx.) | Limited |
| **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** | Set of weighted particles | **Yes** | Yes (empirically) | **Yes** |
| **Multi-Hypothesis Tracking** | Multiple Gaussians | **Yes** | Yes | Yes (high cost) |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU 2024 Part B answers, when you compare Markov localization with EKF, always emphasize: **MCL/Grid Markov handles global localization and multi-modal beliefs, EKF only handles position tracking with unimodal Gaussian posteriors.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithmic Flow of Markov Localization

The following block-level diagram captures the **functional architecture** of the Markov Localization algorithm. The control and sensor inputs are fused through the Bayes filter, producing a refined belief at every time step.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start t equals 0]) --> initB[Initialize belief bel at t0]
    initB --> acqC[Acquire control input ut from odometry]
    acqC --> acqD[Acquire sensor measurement zt]
    acqD --> predE[Apply motion model p of xt given ut and xt-1]
    predE --> predF[Compute predicted belief bar bel of xt via total probability]
    predF --> updG[Apply measurement model p of zt given xt]
    updG --> updH[Compute unnormalized posterior bel of xt]
    updH --> normI[Compute normalizer eta to enforce unity sum]
    normI --> outJ[Publish normalized belief bel of xt]
    outJ --> incK[Increment t by 1]
    incK --> acqC
```

### 4.2 Two-Step Functional Architecture (Prediction + Update)

The diagram below maps the **two-stage filter cycle** with its input and output flows. The "predict" stage uses the motion model; the "correct" stage uses the measurement model. The normalizer closes the loop with a feedback signal that scales the corrected belief.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pastBel[Previous belief bel of xt-1] --> predBlk[Prediction Block]
    ctrl[Control ut] --> predBlk
    predBlk --> predOut[Predicted belief bar bel of xt]
    predOut --> mult[Multiplication by likelihood]
    sensor[Measurement zt] --> measModel[Measurement Model p of zt given xt]
    measModel --> mult
    mult --> rawPost[Raw posterior]
    rawPost --> normBlk[Normalizer eta]
    normBlk --> newBel[New belief bel of xt]
    newBel --> pastBel
```

### 4.3 Failure-Recovery (Kidnapped Robot) State Machine

Markov Localization has a unique property: it can **re-localize a robot that has been teleported to an unknown pose**. The state machine below shows how the algorithm transitions from a confident (unimodal) belief to a confused (multi-modal) belief and back.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ConfidentTracking
    ConfidentTracking --> MultiModalAmbiguity : Sensor aliasing or symmetry
    MultiModalAmbiguity --> GlobalLost : Kidnapped robot event
    GlobalLost --> MultiModalAmbiguity : Particles re-spread uniformly
    MultiModalAmbiguity --> ConfidentTracking : Belief converges to true pose
    ConfidentTracking --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Variant Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Markov Localization Family] --> b1[Grid Based]
    rootA --> b2[Topological]
    rootA --> b3[Monte Carlo MCL]
    b1 --> c1[Histogram over cells]
    b2 --> c2[Belief over distinct places]
    b3 --> c3[Weighted particle set]
    c1 --> d1[High memory, fine metric]
    c2 --> d2[Low memory, coarse symbolic]
    c3 --> d3[Variable accuracy, robust]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define **Markov Localization** and state the **Markov Assumption** on which it is based. Why is this assumption crucial for recursive estimation?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Markov Localization is a probabilistic algorithm that estimates a robot's pose $x_t$ within a known map by maintaining a belief $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ over the entire state space and updating it recursively using the Bayes filter.
- **Markov Assumption (1 mark):** $p(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_{t-1})$. The current state $x_t$ is a sufficient summary of the entire history; the past carries no additional predictive information beyond $x_{t-1}$ and $u_{t-1}$.
- **Cruciality (1 mark):** Without this assumption, the belief update would require storing and conditioning on the **entire history** of measurements and controls, which is computationally intractable. The Markov assumption collapses the history to a single state, making the recursive Bayes filter feasible in real time.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **Markov Localization** and **Kalman Filter-based localization** in terms of belief representation and ability to handle global localization.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Belief Representation (1.5 marks):** Markov Localization represents the belief as a *general probability distribution* — discrete histogram, topological list, or set of particles. It can be **multi-modal**. Kalman Filter represents the belief as a **single Gaussian** (mean + covariance), which is strictly **unimodal**.
- **Global Localization (1.5 marks):** Markov Localization can perform **global localization** from an unknown initial pose, because a uniform prior over the map is a valid starting belief. It can also recover from the *kidnapped robot problem* (pose jumps). Kalman Filter cannot — it can only perform **local position tracking** once the initial pose is approximately known, because a single Gaussian cannot capture global ambiguity.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **recursive Bayes filter update equation** for Markov Localization, starting from the belief definition. Clearly state the Markov assumption and the role of the normalizer $\eta$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot operates in a 1-D corridor with **four cells** $\{C_1, C_2, C_3, C_4\}$. Cells $C_1$ and $C_3$ contain a "landmark"; cells $C_2$ and $C_4$ do not. The initial belief is uniform. The robot moves one cell to the right (with stay-probability $0.1$, move-probability $0.9$, with wrap-around to the leftmost cell if it moves past $C_4$). It then senses "landmark present" with sensor model $p(z = \text{landmark} \mid x \in \{C_1, C_3\}) = 0.8$ and $p(z = \text{landmark} \mid x \in \{C_2, C_4\}) = 0.2$. Compute the belief $bel_1$ after the update. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Start with the belief definition: $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ **(0.5 mark)**

Step 2 — Apply Bayes' rule, conditioning on $z_t$ and $u_t$: **(1 mark)**

$$bel(x_t) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})\, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

Step 3 — Apply the **Markov Assumption** to the likelihood $p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$ **(1 mark)**

Step 4 — Marginalize over the previous state $x_{t-1}$ using the law of total probability in the second term: **(1.5 marks)**

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\, bel(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$$

Step 5 — Define the predicted belief $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, bel(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$ **(1 mark)**

Step 6 — Combine and identify the normalizer $\eta = 1 / p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$: **(1 mark)**

$$bel(x_t) = \eta\, p(z_t \mid x_t)\, \overline{bel}(x_t)$$

Step 7 — State the role of $\eta$ (0.5 mark): it ensures the posterior integrates to $1$ and is computed as $\eta^{-1} = \sum_{x_t} p(z_t \mid x_t)\, \overline{bel}(x_t)$ in the discrete case.

**(b) Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Initial belief (1 mark):** $bel_0(C_i) = 0.25$ for $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$.

**Step 2 — Prediction (3 marks):** Apply motion model with wrap-around boundary:

$$\overline{bel}_1(C_1) = 0.1 \cdot bel_0(C_1) + 0.9 \cdot bel_0(C_4) = 0.1(0.25) + 0.9(0.25) = 0.25$$

$$\overline{bel}_1(C_2) = 0.1 \cdot bel_0(C_2) + 0.9 \cdot bel_0(C_1) = 0.1(0.25) + 0.9(0.25) = 0.25$$

$$\overline{bel}_1(C_3) = 0.1 \cdot bel_0(C_3) + 0.9 \cdot bel_0(C_2) = 0.25$$

$$\overline{bel}_1(C_4) = 0.1 \cdot bel_0(C_4) + 0.9 \cdot bel_0(C_3) = 0.25$$

(Uniform prior yields uniform prediction, as expected.)

**Step 3 — Update (3 marks):** Multiply by sensor likelihood:

| Cell | $\overline{bel}_1$ | $p(z_1 = \text{landmark} \mid x)$ | Product | Normalized $bel_1$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $C_1$ | $0.25$ | $0.8$ | $0.20$ | $0.40$ |
| $C_2$ | $0.25$ | $0.2$ | $0.05$ | $0.10$ |
| $C_3$ | $0.25$ | $0.8$ | $0.20$ | $0.40$ |
| $C_4$ | $0.25$ | $0.2$ | $0.05$ | $0.10$ |

Sum of products $= 0.20 + 0.05 + 0.20 + 0.05 = 0.50$, so $\eta = 1/0.50 = 2.0$.

**Final belief:** $bel_1(C_1) = 0.40$, $bel_1(C_2) = 0.10$, $bel_1(C_3) = 0.40$, $bel_1(C_4) = 0.10$.

The belief correctly concentrates on the two landmark-bearing cells, perfectly illustrating the **disambiguation power** of Markov Localization.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **three variants of Markov Localization** — grid-based, topological, and Monte Carlo — with diagrams. Compare them in terms of memory, accuracy, and computational cost. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **sensor model** $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ and the **motion model** $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ used in Markov Localization. How does the **kidnapped robot problem** test the limits of these models, and how does MCL recover? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Variants (7 marks):**

1. **Grid-Based Markov Localization (2 marks):** The state space is discretized into a regular 2-D grid of cells (typical resolution 5–50 cm). The belief is a histogram over the cells, where each cell stores the probability of the robot being at that pose. Memory grows as $O(N_x \cdot N_y \cdot N_\theta)$ and is the main drawback; however, it is the most accurate in metric terms.

2. **Topological Markov Localization (2 marks):** The map is abstracted into a graph of *distinct places* (e.g., rooms, doorways, T-junctions). The belief is a discrete distribution over graph nodes. Memory is $O(\vert V \vert)$ where $V$ is the vertex set. It is highly efficient but suffers from perceptual aliasing if two places look similar.

3. **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) (2 marks):** The belief is represented by a set of $M$ weighted samples (particles) $\{x_t^{(i)}, w_t^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{M}$. The posterior is approximated as $\sum_i w_t^{(i)} \delta(x_t - x_t^{(i)})$. Memory is $O(M)$ and accuracy scales with $M$. It is the most flexible and is dominant in modern research.

4. **Comparison (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Grid-Based | Topological | MCL |
|---|---|---|---|
| Memory | **High** | Low | Medium |
| Accuracy | High (metric) | Low (symbolic) | Tunable via $M$ |
| Multi-modal? | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Global localization? | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Real-time? | No (large maps) | Yes | Yes (with $M \approx 500$–$2000$) |

**(b) Sensor and Motion Models (7 marks):**

1. **Sensor Model (2 marks):** $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ is the probability of observing $z_t$ from pose $x_t$ given the known map $m$. For a range-finder (LiDAR/sonar), the model is a *beam model*: for each emitted ray, the expected range is computed by ray-casting the map from $x_t$, and a Gaussian is placed around this expected range to model measurement noise. The full likelihood is the product over all beams.

2. **Motion Model (2 marks):** $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ is the probability of ending at pose $x_t$ given the control $u_t$ (e.g., odometry) was applied from $x_{t-1}$. In the *odometry motion model*, $u_t = (\bar{x}_{t-1}, \bar{x}_t)$ is the relative pose change; Gaussian noise is added to translation and rotation. In the *velocity motion model*, $u_t = (v, \omega)$ and motion noise is added to the velocities.

3. **The Kidnapped Robot Problem (1.5 marks):** This is a stress test where the robot is suddenly teleported to an unknown pose. A unimodal estimator (EKF) will fail permanently because its Gaussian cannot re-converge from the wrong mean. A grid-based Markov localizer *can* recover if a small probability mass is preserved on far-away cells, but if a hard threshold is used, it can also fail.

4. **How MCL Recovers (1.5 marks):** MCL maintains a *fixed number of particles* $M$. Random particles are re-injected uniformly into the state space at every cycle with a small probability. After a kidnapping, these uniformly-distributed "rogue" particles will happen to land near the true pose, receive high sensor likelihood, gain weight, and through resampling, the particle cloud will rapidly re-converge on the true pose. This makes MCL the **most robust** variant for the kidnapped robot problem.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the normalizer $\eta$ (loses 2 marks):** Always normalize the belief at the end of the update step. A non-normalized belief is technically not a probability.
> 2. **Confusing the motion model and measurement model (loses 1–2 marks):** The motion model $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ belongs in the *prediction* step, NOT the update step. The measurement model $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ belongs in the *update* step, NOT the prediction.
> 3. **Skipping the Markov Assumption (loses 1 mark):** Even if your derivation is correct, omitting the explicit statement of $p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$ loses full credit in Part A.
> 4. **Confusing global localization with position tracking (loses 1 mark):** Markov Localization supports both, but EKF supports *only* position tracking. Examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Markov Localization** is a **probabilistic, recursive, map-based** localization algorithm that maintains a belief over the entire state space.
- The belief $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ is updated in two steps: **prediction** (motion model) and **update** (measurement model + Bayes' rule).
- The **Markov Assumption** is the engine that makes recursion tractable: the past is summarized entirely by the current state.
- The **normalizer $\eta$** enforces that the belief integrates to $1$ and is computed as $\eta^{-1} = \sum_{x_t} p(z_t \mid x_t)\, \overline{bel}(x_t)$.
- **Three variants:** Grid-based (histogram), Topological (graph), Monte Carlo (particles). All share the same Bayes filter math; they differ only in representation.
- **Multi-modal beliefs** are natively supported — the algorithm can track several plausible poses simultaneously.
- The **kidnapped robot problem** is the canonical stress test; MCL solves it via random particle re-injection.
- **Comparison with EKF:** EKF assumes Gaussian/unimodal belief → cannot do global localization. Markov Localization makes no such assumption.
- **Required formulas for the exam:** Bayes' rule, total probability, the recursive Bayes filter equation, the normalizer, the three variant names, and the structure of the sensor and motion models.
- **Key unit conventions:** Pose in [m, m, rad]; velocities in [m/s, rad/s]; probabilities dimensionless in $[0, 1]$.
