topics.title: Kalman Filter Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Kalman Filter Localization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kalman Filter Localization** is a recursive, optimal state-estimation algorithm that fuses **noisy sensor measurements** with a **dynamical motion model** to produce a statistically optimal estimate of a robot's pose (position and orientation) and its associated uncertainty, in real time.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (OEBRT833 – Robot Motion Planning), the Kalman Filter (KF) is treated as the **workhorse probabilistic estimator** for localizing a mobile robot from a stream of odometry readings and exteroceptive observations. For non-linear robot systems, the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** and the **Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF)** are used as the linearized variants of the same principle.

Formally, the Kalman Filter assumes that the **posterior distribution** over the robot state $x_t$ at every time step is **Gaussian**, represented entirely by a mean vector $\hat{x}_{t \vert t}$ and a covariance matrix $P_{t \vert t}$.

### 1.1.1 System State Definition

The robot's state vector for a 2D planar mobile robot is conventionally written as:

$$
x_t \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}_t
$$

where $x$ and $y$ are Cartesian coordinates in meters, and $\theta$ is the heading angle in radians. For velocity-based state estimation, the state is augmented with linear and angular velocities:

$$
x_t \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix}_t
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The Kalman Filter does *not* store past measurements or past states. It maintains only the *current* mean and covariance. This makes it **O(1) in memory** and **O(k²·⁵) in time** for a k-dimensional state — a critical property for embedded robot platforms.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Foggy Mountain Rescue

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine you are blindfolded on a foggy mountain and want to descend safely.** You have two sources of information:
> 1. **A noisy altimeter** (analogous to a *sensor measurement* $z_t$) — it tells you your altitude, but it jitters by ±5 m every second.
> 2. **Your sense of motion** (analogous to the *process/odometry model*) — you know roughly how far your legs have moved, but you may have slipped.
>
> If you trusted the altimeter alone, the noise would make you stumble. If you trusted your legs alone, accumulated slip would throw you off the cliff. The **Kalman Filter** is the "smart brain" that **weights each source by its current uncertainty** — when the altimeter is steady, it is trusted more; when the altimeter jitters wildly, the brain leans on the motion model. The two beliefs are fused into a *single best estimate* whose uncertainty is provably minimized at every step.

This real-time fusion is exactly what a robot does while navigating a hallway using wheel odometry and a laser range-finder.

---

## 1.3 Core Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are the **canonical noise and uncertainty parameters** that govern a Kalman Filter and are central to KTU 2024 numerical problems.

- **Process Noise Covariance $Q$** — captures uncertainty in the motion model. For a discrete-time constant-velocity model with sampling period $\Delta t$, the standard parameterization is $Q = \sigma_a^2 \begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{\Delta t^4}{4} & \tfrac{\Delta t^3}{2} \\ \tfrac{\Delta t^3}{2} & \Delta t^2 \end{bmatrix}$, where **$\sigma_a$ = 0.5 to 2.0 m/s²** is typical for indoor wheeled robots.
- **Measurement Noise Covariance $R$** — captures sensor uncertainty. For a wheel encoder with **$N$ = 500 pulses/rev**, $R \approx (\pi d / N)^2$ where $d$ is wheel diameter.
- **Initial Covariance $P_0$** — usually set to a **large value (e.g., 10⁴ · I)** to indicate high prior uncertainty before the first measurement.
- **Convergence Threshold** — a practical stopping metric where the trace of $P_{t \vert t}$ is monitored; values of $\text{tr}(P) < 0.01 \text{ m}^2$ indicate lock-on.

---

## 1.4 Syllabus Mapping & Course Outcome Alignment

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Mapping:**
> | Course Outcome | Cognitive Level | Topic Contribution |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | **CO2** | Apply (L3) | Apply KF to linear localization problems |
> | **CO3** | Analyze (L4) | Analyze EKF linearization for non-linear SLAM |
> | **CO4** | Evaluate (L5) | Evaluate filter performance via RMSE/NEES metrics |

The topic carries an expected weightage of **12–15 marks** in the End Semester Examination (ESE), typically combined with Monte Carlo Localization under Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Step Recursive Architecture

The Kalman Filter operates in a strict **predict–update** cycle at every time step $t = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$. The two phases have a beautifully symmetric mathematical structure that students must internalize.

### 2.1.1 Step A — Initialization (at $t = 0$)

- Set the **prior mean** $\hat{x}_{0 \vert 0}$ to the best available initial guess (often $\mathbf{0}$).
- Set the **prior covariance** $P_{0 \vert 0}$ to a large diagonal matrix representing *maximum ignorance*.

### 2.1.2 Step B — Prediction (Time Update) — *No measurement required*

The robot's motion model is propagated forward in time:

$$
\hat{x}_{t \vert t-1} \;=\; A \, \hat{x}_{t-1 \vert t-1} \;+\; B \, u_t
$$

$$
P_{t \vert t-1} \;=\; A \, P_{t-1 \vert t-1} \, A^{T} \;+\; Q
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is $Q$ *added* and not multiplied?** Because process noise $w_t$ is assumed to be **additive zero-mean Gaussian white noise** with covariance $Q$. The covariance of the sum of two independent Gaussians is the *sum* of their covariances.

- $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$: state transition matrix (kinematics).
- $B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$: control input matrix.
- $u_t \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$: control vector (e.g., commanded wheel velocities).
- $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$: process noise covariance.

### 2.1.3 Step C — Update (Measurement Update) — *Incorporates a sensor reading*

The predicted belief is corrected by the new measurement $z_t$:

$$
K_t \;=\; P_{t \vert t-1} \, H^{T} \, \bigl( H \, P_{t \vert t-1} \, H^{T} + R \bigr)^{-1}
$$

$$
\hat{x}_{t \vert t} \;=\; \hat{x}_{t \vert t-1} \;+\; K_t \, \bigl( z_t - H \, \hat{x}_{t \vert t-1} \bigr)
$$

$$
P_{t \vert t} \;=\; \bigl( I - K_t \, H \bigr) \, P_{t \vert t-1}
$$

- $H \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$: measurement matrix (sensor model).
- $R \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times p}$: measurement noise covariance.
- $K_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$: **Kalman Gain** — the optimal weighting factor.
- $z_t - H \hat{x}_{t \vert t-1}$: **innovation** or **measurement residual** $\nu_t$.

### 2.1.4 The "Why" Behind the Kalman Gain

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive meaning of $K_t$:**
> - If $R \to 0$ (perfect sensor), then $K_t \to H^{-1}$ — the filter trusts the measurement fully.
> - If $P_{t \vert t-1} \to 0$ (perfect model), then $K_t \to 0$ — the filter ignores the measurement.
> - $K_t$ is the **provably optimal** gain that minimizes the posterior covariance $\text{tr}(P_{t \vert t})$.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for Module 2 Kalman Filter derivations. Memorize these and the binding numerical values.

| Symbol | Meaning | Dimension / Units | Standard Form / Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $x_t$ | State vector at time $t$ | $\mathbb{R}^n$ | Position, velocity, orientation |
| $u_t$ | Control input | $\mathbb{R}^m$ | Wheel velocities, IMU |
| $z_t$ | Measurement vector | $\mathbb{R}^p$ | GPS, LiDAR, encoder |
| $A$ | State transition matrix | $n \times n$ | $A = I + F \Delta t$ for linear systems |
| $B$ | Input matrix | $n \times m$ | Identity for direct-drive kinematics |
| $H$ | Measurement matrix | $p \times n$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ for position only |
| $Q$ | Process noise covariance | $n \times n$, $\text{m}^2$ | $\sigma_a^2 \cdot G \cdot G^{T}$ |
| $R$ | Measurement noise covariance | $p \times p$, $\text{m}^2$ | $\sigma_z^2 \cdot I$ |
| $P$ | State covariance | $n \times n$ | Symmetric, positive semi-definite |
| $K_t$ | Kalman Gain | $n \times p$ | $K_t \leq H^{-1}$ in norm |
| $S_t$ | Innovation covariance | $p \times p$ | $S_t = H P_{t \vert t-1} H^{T} + R$ |
| $\nu_t$ | Innovation / residual | $\mathbb{R}^p$ | $\nu_t = z_t - H \hat{x}_{t \vert t-1}$ |
| $\text{NEES}$ | Normalized Estimation Error Squared | scalar | $\nu^{T} P^{-1} \nu$, ideal = 1.0 |
| $\text{RMSE}$ | Root Mean Square Error | meters | $\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{N} \sum (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2}$ |

---

## 2.3 Process & Measurement Models — Standard Forms

### 2.3.1 Constant-Velocity (CV) Motion Model

For a robot moving with nearly constant velocity over sampling period $\Delta t$:

$$
x_t \;=\; \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & \Delta t & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & \Delta t \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{A}
\, x_{t-1} \;+\; w_t
$$

with the position components $x$ and $y$ expressed in **meters**, velocity in **m/s**, and $\Delta t$ in **seconds**.

### 2.3.2 Constant-Turn-Rate-and-Velocity (CTRV) Model

Used when the robot's steering angle changes:

$$
A \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & \frac{\sin(\omega \Delta t)}{\omega} & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & -\frac{\cos(\omega \Delta t) - 1}{\omega} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the foundation of the **EKF for autonomous vehicles** and is a high-yield KTU topic.

### 2.3.3 Range-Bearing Measurement Model

When the robot observes a known landmark $l$ at position $(l_x, l_y)$:

$$
z_t \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(l_x - x)^2 + (l_y - y)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(l_y - y, \, l_x - x) \end{bmatrix} \;+\; v_t
$$

This is **non-linear** and requires EKF linearization (Jacobian $H = \partial z / \partial x$).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where is Kalman Filter Localization deployed in industry?**
> - **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla, Mobileye):** EKF/UKF for pose estimation at 100 Hz.
> - **GNSS-INS Integration (Aircraft, Drones):** Loosely-coupled KF fuses GPS with inertial navigation.
> - **Robotic Surgery (Da Vinci):** Sub-millimeter tool tracking with adaptive KF.
> - **Mars Rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance):** Visual-inertial odometry with UKF.
> - **Smartphone AR (Apple ARKit, Google ARCore):** Visual-inertial SLAM with extended Kalman predictor.

The filter's **O(1) memory** and **provable optimality under Gaussian-linear assumptions** make it the de facto standard in real-time embedded robotics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Kalman Gain — Minimizing the Posterior Covariance

This is the **most frequently asked 14-mark derivation** in the KTU ESE. We derive the optimal $K_t$ from first principles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Choose $K_t$ that minimizes the **posterior covariance trace** $\text{tr}(P_{t \vert t})$.

### Step 1 — Write the Posterior State Estimate

The updated state is a linear combination of the prediction and the measurement:

$$
\hat{x}_{t \vert t} \;=\; \hat{x}_{t \vert t-1} \;+\; K_t \, \bigl( z_t - H \, \hat{x}_{t \vert t-1} \bigr)
$$

### Step 2 — Define the Estimation Error

Let $e_t = x_t - \hat{x}_{t \vert t}$ be the posterior estimation error. Substituting $z_t = H x_t + v_t$:

$$
e_t \;=\; x_t - \hat{x}_{t \vert t} \;=\; (I - K_t H)(x_t - \hat{x}_{t \vert t-1}) - K_t v_t
$$

### Step 3 — Form the Posterior Covariance

By definition, $P_{t \vert t} = \mathbb{E}[e_t e_t^{T}]$. Since $\mathbb{E}[(x_t - \hat{x}_{t \vert t-1}) v_t^{T}] = 0$ (zero-mean independence):

$$
P_{t \vert t} \;=\; (I - K_t H) \, P_{t \vert t-1} \, (I - K_t H)^{T} \;+\; K_t \, R \, K_t^{T}
$$

### Step 4 — Expand the Quadratic in $K_t$

Expanding $(I - K_t H) P (I - K_t H)^{T}$:

$$
P_{t \vert t} \;=\; P_{t \vert t-1} \;-\; K_t H P_{t \vert t-1} \;-\; P_{t \vert t-1} H^{T} K_t^{T} \;+\; K_t (H P_{t \vert t-1} H^{T} + R) K_t^{T}
$$

### Step 5 — Take the Derivative with Respect to $K_t$ and Set to Zero

The optimal $K_t$ satisfies $\partial P_{t \vert t} / \partial K_t = 0$. Using the matrix derivative identity $\partial(\text{tr}(K A K^{T} B)) / \partial K = 2 B K A$:

$$
\frac{\partial \, \text{tr}(P_{t \vert t})}{\partial K_t} \;=\; -2 (H P_{t \vert t-1})^{T} \;+\; 2 K_t (H P_{t \vert t-1} H^{T} + R) \;=\; 0
$$

### Step 6 — Solve for the Kalman Gain

$$
K_t \, (H P_{t \vert t-1} H^{T} + R) \;=\; P_{t \vert t-1} H^{T}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,K_t \;=\; P_{t \vert t-1} \, H^{T} \, \bigl( H \, P_{t \vert t-1} \, H^{T} + R \bigr)^{-1}\,}
$$

### Step 7 — Simplify the Posterior Covariance (Joseph Form)

Substituting the optimal $K_t$ back yields the **simplified** and the **numerically stable Joseph form**:

$$
\boxed{\,P_{t \vert t} \;=\; (I - K_t H) \, P_{t \vert t-1} \;=\; (I - K_t H) \, P_{t \vert t-1} \, (I - K_t H)^{T} + K_t R K_t^{T}\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The Joseph form (right-hand side) is the *only* form that is **guaranteed symmetric positive-definite** in finite-precision arithmetic. The simplified form (left) can lose symmetry and cause filter divergence. Always cite both forms in long-answer questions.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — 2D Constant-Velocity Robot Tracker

This is a **fully solved KTU-style numerical problem** worth **7 marks**.

### Problem Statement

> A wheeled robot moves in 1D along the X-axis with nearly constant velocity. It is tracked by a 1D position sensor (e.g., ultrasonic rangefinder). The system is modeled as a 2-state constant-velocity KF with $\Delta t = 1$ s.
> **Given:**
> - Initial state: $\hat{x}_{0 \vert 0} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$, $P_{0 \vert 0} = \begin{bmatrix} 500 & 0 \\ 0 & 500 \end{bmatrix}$
> - $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $H = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$
> - $Q = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ (ideal motion), $R = 1$
> - **First measurement:** $z_1 = 1$ m
>
> **Compute** the posterior state $\hat{x}_{1 \vert 1}$ and posterior covariance $P_{1 \vert 1}$.

### Solution

#### Phase 1 — Prediction

The predicted state at $t = 1$ given $t = 0$:

$$
\hat{x}_{1 \vert 0} \;=\; A \, \hat{x}_{0 \vert 0} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The predicted covariance (with $Q = 0$):

$$
P_{1 \vert 0} \;=\; A \, P_{0 \vert 0} \, A^{T} + Q
$$

Computing the matrix product step by step:

$$
A \, P_{0 \vert 0} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 500 & 0 \\ 0 & 500 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 500 & 500 \\ 0 & 500 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
(A \, P_{0 \vert 0}) \, A^{T} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 500 & 500 \\ 0 & 500 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1000 & 500 \\ 500 & 500 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{1 \vert 0} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1000 & 500 \\ 500 & 500 \end{bmatrix}\,}
$$

#### Phase 2 — Innovation

The innovation covariance:

$$
S_1 \;=\; H \, P_{1 \vert 0} \, H^{T} + R \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1000 & 500 \\ 500 & 500 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + 1
$$

$$
S_1 \;=\; 1000 + 1 \;=\; 1001
$$

The innovation (measurement residual):

$$
\nu_1 \;=\; z_1 - H \, \hat{x}_{1 \vert 0} \;=\; 1 - \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; 1
$$

#### Phase 3 — Kalman Gain

$$
K_1 \;=\; P_{1 \vert 0} \, H^{T} \, S_1^{-1} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1000 & 500 \\ 500 & 500 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{1001}
$$

$$
K_1 \;=\; \frac{1}{1001} \begin{bmatrix} 1000 \\ 500 \end{bmatrix} \;\approx\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.9990 \\ 0.4995 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Phase 4 — State Update

$$
\hat{x}_{1 \vert 1} \;=\; \hat{x}_{1 \vert 0} + K_1 \, \nu_1 \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.9990 \\ 0.4995 \end{bmatrix} (1) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.9990 \\ 0.4995 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\hat{x}_{1 \vert 1} \;\approx\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.999 \text{ m} \\ 0.500 \text{ m/s} \end{bmatrix}\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** The robot's estimated position is **0.999 m** and its estimated velocity has been inferred as **0.500 m/s** purely from a single position measurement! The velocity estimate is a *byproduct* of the CV-model coupling.

#### Phase 5 — Covariance Update (Joseph Form)

$$
P_{1 \vert 1} \;=\; (I - K_1 H) \, P_{1 \vert 0} \, (I - K_1 H)^{T} + K_1 R K_1^{T}
$$

First, compute $I - K_1 H$:

$$
I - K_1 H \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0.9990 \\ 0.4995 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.0010 & 0 \\ -0.4995 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Next, multiply:

$$
(I - K_1 H) \, P_{1 \vert 0} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.0010 & 0 \\ -0.4995 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1000 & 500 \\ 500 & 500 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1.0000 & 0.5000 \\ 0.5000 & 250.25 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Multiplying by its transpose:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1.0000 & 0.5000 \\ 0.5000 & 250.25 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.0010 & -0.4995 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.0010 & 0.0005 \\ 0.0005 & 250.00 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Adding the Joseph correction $K_1 R K_1^{T}$:

$$
K_1 R K_1^{T} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.9990 \\ 0.4995 \end{bmatrix} (1) \begin{bmatrix} 0.9990 & 0.4995 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.9980 & 0.4990 \\ 0.4990 & 0.2495 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Summing both terms:

$$
\boxed{\,P_{1 \vert 1} \;\approx\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.9990 & 0.4995 \\ 0.4995 & 249.50 \end{bmatrix}\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Observations for the examiner:**
> - Position uncertainty collapsed from $P_{0,0} = 500$ to $P_{0,0} \approx 1$ — a **500× reduction** in just one update step.
> - Velocity uncertainty is still high ($P_{1,1} \approx 250$) because a single position measurement carries no direct velocity information.
> - The off-diagonal $P_{0,1} = 0.4995$ is the *cross-covariance* indicating strong position-velocity coupling — a hallmark of the CV model.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Filter Behavior in Limiting Regimes

| Regime | $R$ vs. $P_{t \vert t-1}$ | $K_t$ Behavior | Practical Consequence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **High Sensor Noise** | $R \gg P_{t \vert t-1}$ | $K_t \to 0$ | Filter ignores $z_t$; relies on prediction |
| **Low Sensor Noise** | $R \ll P_{t \vert t-1}$ | $K_t \to H^{-1}$ | Filter trusts $z_t$ completely |
| **Steady-State** | $P_{t \vert t} \to P_{\infty}$ | $K_t \to K_{\infty}$ | Computed by solving the **Discrete Algebraic Riccati Equation (DARE):** $P = A P A^{T} - A P H^{T} (H P H^{T} + R)^{-1} H P A^{T} + Q$ |
| **Divergence** | $Q$ underestimated | $K_t$ collapses | Filter becomes overconfident; risk of failure — see *Examiner's Warning* below |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning — Filter Divergence Pitfall:**
> If $Q$ is set too small and $R$ is set too large, the filter assigns near-zero gain to all measurements. The state estimate "freezes" while true motion continues, and the actual error grows unbounded while $P$ shrinks — a paradox called **smug filter divergence**. The KTU board explicitly tests this: a numerical problem with mismatched $Q$ and $R$ is a common trap.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Kalman Filter Predict–Update Cycle

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the complete operational architecture of a single iteration of the Kalman Filter for robot localization. The loop continues for every new sensor measurement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1([START]) --> A2[Initialize State Mean x0 and Covariance P0]
    A2 --> B1[Receive Control Input ut from Odometry]
    B1 --> B2[Compute State Prediction x_pred = A x_prev + B ut]
    B2 --> B3[Compute Covariance Prediction P_pred = A P_prev AT plus Q]
    B3 --> C1{Is New Measurement zt Available?}
    C1 -->|Yes| C2[Compute Innovation y = zt minus H x_pred]
    C1 -->|No| E1[Output x_pred and P_pred as Best Estimate]
    C2 --> C3[Compute Innovation Covariance S = H P_pred HT plus R]
    C3 --> C4[Compute Kalman Gain K = P_pred HT S inv]
    C4 --> D1[Update State x_new = x_pred plus K y]
    D1 --> D2[Update Covariance P_new = I minus K H P_pred]
    D2 --> D3[Store x_new and P_new as New Posterior]
    D3 --> E1
    E1 --> F1{Is Mission Complete?}
    F1 -->|No| B1
    F1 -->|Yes| G1([END])
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Mobile Robot Localization Stack

The following diagram maps how the Kalman Filter interfaces with the broader robot perception and control stack. This is the **block-level fallback** that the examiner expects students to sketch in long-answer derivations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Wheel Encoders] --> B1[Odometry Node]
    A2[IMU Gyroscope] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Control Vector ut]
    C1 --> D1[KALMAN FILTER PREDICT BLOCK]
    A3[LiDAR Scan Matcher] --> E1[Measurement Vector zt]
    A4[Camera Feature Tracker] --> E1
    A5[GPS Receiver] --> E1
    E1 --> F1[KALMAN FILTER UPDATE BLOCK]
    D1 --> G1[Robot Pose Mean x_hat]
    F1 --> G1
    D1 --> H1[Robot Pose Covariance P]
    F1 --> H1
    G1 --> I1[Motion Planner Module 3]
    H1 --> I1
    G1 --> J1[Path Tracker and Controller]
    H1 --> J1
    I1 --> J1
    J1 --> A1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:**
> - The **left column** lists the *exteroceptive sensors* (encoders, IMU, LiDAR, camera, GPS) — physical hardware.
> - The **central column** is the *Kalman Filter itself*, decomposed into the predict block (motion-driven) and the update block (measurement-driven).
> - The **right column** is the *consumer* of the localization output — the motion planner and the controller.
> - The **feedback loop** at the bottom represents the closed-loop nature of robot navigation: the controller's output closes back into the actuators, whose response becomes the next $u_t$.

## 4.3 Innovation Sequence — Concept Diagram

To build intuition for the **innovation** (the most important diagnostic quantity in the filter), here is a signal-flow representation:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[True Robot Pose xt] --> A2[True Measurement zt_true]
    A2 --> A3[Add Sensor Noise vt]
    A3 --> A4[Measured Reading zt]
    B1[Predicted State x_pred] --> B2[Predicted Measurement H x_pred]
    A4 --> C1[Subtract Node]
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Innovation nu_t]
    D1 --> E1[Innovation Covariance S_t]
    E1 --> F1[Kalman Gain K_t]
    D1 --> G1[Weighted Innovation K_t nu_t]
    F1 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[State Correction]
    B1 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Posterior State x_new]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in the exam:** A common KTU problem asks *"If the innovation sequence has non-zero mean, what does it indicate?"* The answer: **a bias in the model or sensor** (e.g., wheel slip not captured in $A$, or an uncalibrated range bias). The **Normalized Innovation Squared (NIS)** test, $\text{NIS} = \nu^{T} S^{-1} \nu$, is the $\chi^2$ consistency check that KTU examiners explicitly test.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
*Define the Kalman Filter. List the two fundamental assumptions that make the Kalman Filter the optimal linear estimator.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** The Kalman Filter is a recursive, linear, Gaussian state-estimation algorithm that produces the optimal minimum-mean-square-error (MMSE) estimate of a system's state by fusing a dynamical motion model with noisy sensor measurements.
>
> **Assumptions (1.5 marks):**
> 1. The **process and measurement models are linear** (i.e., expressible as $x_t = A x_{t-1} + B u_t + w_t$ and $z_t = H x_t + v_t$).
> 2. Both **process noise $w_t$ and measurement noise $v_t$ are zero-mean, white, uncorrelated Gaussian random processes** with known covariances $Q$ and $R$ respectively.
>
> Under these two assumptions, the Kalman Filter is **provably optimal** — no other linear estimator can achieve lower mean-square error.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Retest]**
*What is the "innovation" in a Kalman Filter? What does the Normalized Innovation Squared (NIS) test detect, and what is its expected value under a consistent filter?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Innovation (1 mark):** The innovation $\nu_t$ is the difference between the actual sensor reading $z_t$ and the predicted measurement $H \hat{x}_{t \vert t-1}$:
> $$\nu_t = z_t - H \hat{x}_{t \vert t-1}$$
> It represents the *new information* brought by the measurement that was not predictable from the motion model.
>
> **NIS Test (1.5 marks):** The **Normalized Innovation Squared** statistic is defined as
> $$\text{NIS} = \nu_t^{T} S_t^{-1} \nu_t, \quad \text{where } S_t = H P_{t \vert t-1} H^{T} + R$$
> It follows a $\chi^2$ distribution with $p$ degrees of freedom (where $p$ is the measurement dimension). Under a **consistent filter**, the expected value is $\mathbb{E}[\text{NIS}] = p$. If the empirical NIS significantly exceeds $p$ (typically outside the 95% bounds of the $\chi^2$ distribution), the filter is **inconsistent** — indicating a mismatch in $Q$, $R$, or model linearization.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question 3 — Option A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** *Derive the Kalman Filter update equations, clearly showing the derivation of the optimal Kalman Gain by minimizing the trace of the posterior covariance matrix. State the Joseph form of the covariance update and explain why it is numerically preferred.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A differential-drive robot navigates a 2D environment. Its motion in 1D along the X-axis is modeled as a constant-velocity process with sampling period $\Delta t = 1$ s. The state vector is $x_t = [p_t, v_t]^{T}$. The system matrices are:*
$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad H = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad Q = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad R = 2
$$
*Initial conditions: $\hat{x}_{0 \vert 0} = [0, 0]^{T}$ and $P_{0 \vert 0} = \begin{bmatrix} 1000 & 0 \\ 0 & 1000 \end{bmatrix}$. First measurement: $z_1 = 2.5$ m. Compute the posterior state estimate $\hat{x}_{1 \vert 1}$ and the posterior covariance $P_{1 \vert 1}$.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

> **Step 1 — Posterior state representation (1 mark):**
> $$\hat{x}_{t \vert t} = \hat{x}_{t \vert t-1} + K_t(z_t - H \hat{x}_{t \vert t-1})$$
>
> **Step 2 — Posterior error definition (1 mark):**
> $$e_t = (I - K_t H)(x_t - \hat{x}_{t \vert t-1}) - K_t v_t$$
>
> **Step 3 — Posterior covariance expansion (1 mark):**
> $$P_{t \vert t} = (I - K_t H) P_{t \vert t-1} (I - K_t H)^{T} + K_t R K_t^{T}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Setting derivative of $\text{tr}(P_{t \vert t})$ w.r.t. $K_t$ to zero (2 marks):**
> $$\frac{\partial \text{tr}(P_{t \vert t})}{\partial K_t} = -2 P_{t \vert t-1} H^{T} + 2 K_t (H P_{t \vert t-1} H^{T} + R) = 0$$
>
> **Step 5 — Solving for $K_t$ and stating the Joseph form (1 mark):**
> $$K_t = P_{t \vert t-1} H^{T}(H P_{t \vert t-1} H^{T} + R)^{-1}$$
> $$P_{t \vert t} = (I - K_t H) P_{t \vert t-1} (I - K_t H)^{T} + K_t R K_t^{T}$$
>
> **Step 6 — Numerical preference (1 mark):** The Joseph form maintains **symmetric positive-definiteness** in finite-precision arithmetic, whereas the simplified form $(I - K_t H) P_{t \vert t-1}$ can lose symmetry due to round-off errors, leading to filter divergence.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Prediction Step:**

$$
\hat{x}_{1 \vert 0} = A \hat{x}_{0 \vert 0} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
A P_{0 \vert 0} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1000 & 0 \\ 0 & 1000 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1000 & 1000 \\ 0 & 1000 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
A P_{0 \vert 0} A^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 1000 & 1000 \\ 0 & 1000 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2000 & 1000 \\ 1000 & 1000 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
P_{1 \vert 0} = A P_{0 \vert 0} A^{T} + Q = \begin{bmatrix} 2000.1 & 1000 \\ 1000 & 1000.1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Innovation:**

$$
\nu_1 = z_1 - H \hat{x}_{1 \vert 0} = 2.5 - \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = 2.5
$$

$$
S_1 = H P_{1 \vert 0} H^{T} + R = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2000.1 & 1000 \\ 1000 & 1000.1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + 2 = 2002.1
$$

**Kalman Gain:**

$$
K_1 = P_{1 \vert 0} H^{T} S_1^{-1} = \frac{1}{2002.1} \begin{bmatrix} 2000.1 \\ 1000 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.9990 \\ 0.4995 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**State Update:**

$$
\hat{x}_{1 \vert 1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.9990 \\ 0.4995 \end{bmatrix} (2.5) = \begin{bmatrix} 2.4975 \\ 1.2488 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Covariance Update (Simplified Form):**

$$
P_{1 \vert 1} = (I - K_1 H) P_{1 \vert 0} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0010 & 0 \\ -0.4995 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2000.1 & 1000 \\ 1000 & 1000.1 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 2.00 & 1.00 \\ 0.50 & 500.05 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\,\hat{x}_{1 \vert 1} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 2.498 \text{ m} \\ 1.249 \text{ m/s} \end{bmatrix}, \quad P_{1 \vert 1} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 2.00 & 1.00 \\ 1.00 & 500.05 \end{bmatrix}\,}
$$

---

### Question 3 — Option B (14 Marks, Internal Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** *Explain the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) for non-linear robot localization. State the non-linear motion and measurement models and derive the linearized form using Jacobian matrices. Clearly justify when EKF must replace the standard Kalman Filter.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A mobile robot uses an onboard range sensor to localize with respect to two known landmarks at positions $L_1 = (3, 0)$ and $L_2 = (0, 4)$ m. The robot's state is $x = [x_r, y_r]^{T}$. The range measurement model to landmark $L_i$ is $z_i = \sqrt{(x_{L_i} - x_r)^2 + (y_{L_i} - y_r)^2} + v_i$. Linearize this model around the current estimate $\hat{x} = [1, 1]^{T}$ and compute the measurement Jacobian $H$.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

> **Non-linear models (2 marks):** The standard KF requires linear models. For non-linear robot systems:
> $$x_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t, \quad z_t = h(x_t) + v_t$$
> Examples include the range-bearing landmark model and the omni-directional motion model with non-holonomic constraints.
>
> **EKF Idea (2 marks):** The EKF linearizes the non-linear functions at every time step using the **first-order Taylor expansion** around the current estimate:
> $$f(x_{t-1}, u_t) \approx f(\hat{x}_{t-1 \vert t-1}, u_t) + F_t (x_{t-1} - \hat{x}_{t-1 \vert t-1})$$
> $$h(x_t) \approx h(\hat{x}_{t \vert t-1}) + H_t (x_t - \hat{x}_{t \vert t-1})$$
> where $F_t = \partial f / \partial x \big\vert_{\hat{x}_{t-1 \vert t-1}, u_t}$ and $H_t = \partial h / \partial x \big\vert_{\hat{x}_{t \vert t-1}}$ are the **Jacobian matrices**.
>
> **EKF Equations (2 marks):** The prediction and update equations become:
> $$\hat{x}_{t \vert t-1} = f(\hat{x}_{t-1 \vert t-1}, u_t), \quad P_{t \vert t-1} = F_t P_{t-1 \vert t-1} F_t^{T} + Q$$
> $$K_t = P_{t \vert t-1} H_t^{T} (H_t P_{t \vert t-1} H_t^{T} + R)^{-1}$$
> $$\hat{x}_{t \vert t} = \hat{x}_{t \vert t-1} + K_t (z_t - h(\hat{x}_{t \vert t-1})), \quad P_{t \vert t} = (I - K_t H_t) P_{t \vert t-1}$$
>
> **Justification for using EKF (1 mark):** EKF must replace KF whenever **either the motion model $f$ or the measurement model $h$ is non-linear** and the linearization error is non-negligible (e.g., when the angular uncertainty $\sigma_{\theta} > 0.1$ rad, or when the robot operates near the field-of-view boundary of a sensor).

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

For landmark $L_1 = (3, 0)$ and estimate $\hat{x} = (1, 1)$:

$$
\hat{r}_1 = \sqrt{(3 - 1)^2 + (0 - 1)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 1} = \sqrt{5}
$$

The measurement Jacobian is:

$$
H_1 = \frac{\partial z_1}{\partial x_r}, \; \frac{\partial z_1}{\partial y_r} = \left[ \frac{x_{L_1} - x_r}{r_1}, \;\; \frac{y_{L_1} - y_r}{r_1} \right]
$$

Evaluating at the estimate:

$$
H_1 = \left[ \frac{3 - 1}{\sqrt{5}}, \;\; \frac{0 - 1}{\sqrt{5}} \right] = \left[ \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}, \;\; -\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \right] \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.8944 & -0.4472 \end{bmatrix}
$$

For landmark $L_2 = (0, 4)$ and estimate $\hat{x} = (1, 1)$:

$$
\hat{r}_2 = \sqrt{(0 - 1)^2 + (4 - 1)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 9} = \sqrt{10}
$$

$$
H_2 = \left[ \frac{0 - 1}{\sqrt{10}}, \;\; \frac{4 - 1}{\sqrt{10}} \right] = \left[ -\frac{1}{\sqrt{10}}, \;\; \frac{3}{\sqrt{10}} \right] \approx \begin{bmatrix} -0.3162 & 0.9487 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Stacked Jacobian for both landmarks:

$$
\boxed{\,H = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8944 & -0.4472 \\ -0.3162 & 0.9487 \end{bmatrix}\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation for the examiner:** The rows of $H$ are unit vectors pointing *from the robot to each landmark*. The determinant $|H| \approx 0.848 \neq 0$ confirms the landmarks are *not collinear* with the robot, so the localization is **observable** — both $[x_r, y_r]$ can be uniquely estimated from the two range measurements.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Innovation (–2 marks):** The innovation is $\nu = z - H \hat{x}$, *not* $H \hat{x} - z$. Reversing the sign flips the state update direction.
> 2. **Forgetting the $Q$ in Prediction (–1 mark):** Even if $Q = 0$ in the problem, the **general form** must include $Q$ symbolically in the prediction step; a numerical problem with $Q = 0$ is a trap.
> 3. **Transpose Confusion in $A P A^{T}$ (–2 marks):** The order is $A P A^{T}$, not $A^{T} P A$. The transpose of $A$ goes on the *right*.
> 4. **Skipping Units (–0.5 mark):** Always state units (m, m/s, m²). KTU 2024 has a dedicated "Engineering Sense" rubric.
> 5. **Covariance Update via Simplified Form without Symmetrization (–1 mark):** Mention $(P + P^{T})/2$ to enforce symmetry if using the simplified form.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 2 — Kalman Filter Localization: Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> - **Core Cycle (1 sentence):** Predict with $A, B, Q$ → Update with $H, R$ → Output $\hat{x}_{t \vert t}, P_{t \vert t}$.
> - **Five Canonical Equations:** Prediction: $\hat{x}_{t \vert t-1} = A \hat{x}_{t-1 \vert t-1} + B u_t$, $P_{t \vert t-1} = A P_{t-1 \vert t-1} A^{T} + Q$. Update: $K_t = P_{t \vert t-1} H^{T} (H P_{t \vert t-1} H^{T} + R)^{-1}$, $\hat{x}_{t \vert t} = \hat{x}_{t \vert t-1} + K_t (z_t - H \hat{x}_{t \vert t-1})$, $P_{t \vert t} = (I - K_t H) P_{t \vert t-1}$.
> - **Innovation $\nu_t$** is the *new information* per measurement; its mean should be $\approx 0$ for a consistent filter.
> - **Kalman Gain $K_t$** is the *optimal blending weight* between the model and the sensor.
> - **Joseph form** is the **numerically stable** covariance update — use it whenever the simplified form may lose symmetry.
> - **Initial $P_0$** must be **large** (e.g., $10^4 \cdot I$) to allow rapid learning from the first measurement.
> - **EKF** is required when **either model is non-linear**; linearize via Jacobians $F = \partial f / \partial x$ and $H = \partial h / \partial x$ at the current estimate.
> - **UKF** uses the *unscented transform* to capture second-order non-linearity without explicit Jacobians — preferred for highly non-linear systems (e.g., GPS-INS with 50+ states).
> - **Performance metrics:** NEES $\approx 1$, NIS $\approx p$, RMSE decreasing monotonically with steps.
> - **Failure modes to remember:** Smug filter divergence (underestimated $Q$), numerical asymmetry in $P$, and linearization breakdown in EKF.
> - **Range-bearing Jacobian template:** $H = \left[ \tfrac{x_l - x_r}{r}, \; \tfrac{y_l - y_r}{r}, \; \tfrac{-(y_l - y_r)}{r^2}, \; \tfrac{x_l - x_r}{r^2} \right]$ — memorize this.
> - **Sampling period $\Delta t$** enters $A$ linearly for CV model and through $\sin / \omega$ in CTRV model.
> - **Convergence test:** $\text{tr}(P_{t \vert t}) < 0.01 \text{ m}^2$ indicates filter lock-on for indoor robots.
> - **KTU 2024 weightage:** Expect 1 Part-A (3 marks) and 1 Part-B sub-question (7 marks) on this topic in the ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application to Mobile Robots: Kalman Filter Localization - Schematic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Application to Mobile Robots: Kalman Filter Localization - Schematic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – OEBRT833 (Robot Motion Planning)**, *Kalman Filter Localization* is defined as a recursive, optimal, Bayesian state-estimation algorithm used by a mobile robot to continuously fuse **noisy motion predictions** (from odometry/wheel encoders) with **noisy sensor measurements** (from range finders, cameras, GPS, beacons) in order to maintain an accurate estimate of the robot's pose $\mathbf{x}_k = [x, y, \theta]^T$ and its associated uncertainty covariance $\mathbf{P}_k$.

The "Schematic" of this filter refers to the canonical **Prediction → Update loop** block diagram that visually captures how the *a priori* (predicted) belief is corrected by the *a posteriori* (measured) belief to yield the optimal *Minimum Mean Square Error (MMSE)* estimate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Kalman Filter is the *de-facto* benchmark for linear-Gaussian localization problems. It assumes (i) a **linear system model**, (ii) **Gaussian zero-mean white noise** for both process and measurement, and (iii) **uncorrelated** process and measurement noise. The schematic you must know comprises exactly **two stages** — *Time Update (Predict)* and *Measurement Update (Correct)*.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Walking Blind in a Foggy Forest"

Imagine you are blindfolded and walking through a dense, foggy forest. You have:
1. **A pedometer (motion model)** — counts your steps, but *drifts* over time.
2. **A handful of trail markers with buzzers (sensor model)** — tell you "you are near marker #3", but the buzzer is *noisy*.

The **Kalman Filter** is your "smart brain" that does the following every second:
- **Predicts** where you *should* be based on your last known position + step count (this guess is *uncertain*).
- **Compares** that prediction with what the buzzers actually *say*.
- **Blends** both intelligently — if the pedometer is super accurate, it trusts it more; if the buzzers are very precise, it trusts them more.

The **Schematic** is simply the flowchart of this thinking process: `Predict → Measure → Compare → Blend → Repeat`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The Kalman Filter is *optimal* only under the linear-Gaussian assumption. For highly non-linear systems (e.g., a robot rotating sharply), the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** linearizes via Jacobians — a frequent follow-up question in KTU exams.

## 1.3 Standard Physical / Mathematical Constants in Localization

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{x}_k$ | Robot pose (state) at step $k$ | $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$, $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$ |
| $\mathbf{P}_k$ | State estimation error covariance | $3 \times 3$ symmetric positive definite matrix |
| $\mathbf{Q}_k$ | Process (motion) noise covariance | $3 \times 3$ matrix, tunable |
| $\mathbf{R}_k$ | Measurement noise covariance | $m \times m$ matrix, sensor-dependent |
| $\mathbf{K}_k$ | Kalman Gain | $3 \times m$ weighting matrix |
| $n$ | State dimension | Typically **3** for 2D ground robots |
| $m$ | Measurement dimension | Depends on sensor (2 for range-bearing) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Two-Stage Operational Logic

The Kalman Filter schematic is built on **five equations** distributed across **two stages**. Understanding *why* each equation exists is critical for the 14-mark KTU questions.

### Stage 1 — Time Update (Predict) — "Where do I expect to be?"

This stage uses the **motion model** $\mathbf{F}, \mathbf{B}$ and process noise $\mathbf{Q}$ to propagate the state forward in time, *before* any new sensor reading arrives.

- **Step 1 — State Prediction:** $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1\vert k-1} + \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{u}_k$
  - *Why:* Projects the previous best estimate through the kinematic/motion model.
  - *How:* $\mathbf{F}_k$ is the state-transition matrix; $\mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{u}_k$ is the control input (e.g., commanded velocity, wheel-encoder odometry).

- **Step 2 — Covariance Prediction:** $\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{P}_{k-1\vert k-1} \mathbf{F}_k^T + \mathbf{Q}_k$
  - *Why:* Uncertainty **always grows** during prediction because the motion is imperfect.
  - *How:* $\mathbf{Q}_k$ injects the *added* uncertainty from the motion noise.

### Stage 2 — Measurement Update (Correct) — "Where do I actually think I am?"

This stage uses the **sensor model** $\mathbf{H}$ and measurement noise $\mathbf{R}$ to refine the prediction using the *innovation* (residual) $\boldsymbol{\nu}_k = \mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1}$.

- **Step 3 — Kalman Gain:** $\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T \left( \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T + \mathbf{R}_k \right)^{-1}$
  - *Why:* Determines the **optimal blending weight** between prediction and measurement.
  - *Intuition:* If $\mathbf{R}_k$ is small (sensor very accurate) → $\mathbf{K}_k$ is large → trust measurement more. If $\mathbf{Q}_k$ is small (motion very accurate) → $\mathbf{K}_k$ is small → trust prediction more.

- **Step 4 — State Update:** $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k \boldsymbol{\nu}_k$
  - *Why:* Corrects the prediction using the weighted innovation.

- **Step 5 — Covariance Update (Joseph form for stability):** $\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k} = \left( \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}_k \right) \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1}$
  - *Why:* Uncertainty **shrinks** after incorporating a measurement.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** Absolute-value / determinant bars are written using `\vert` so the markdown table is not broken.

| # | Equation Name | LaTeX Formula | Function in Schematic |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | State Prediction | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1\vert k-1} + \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{u}_k$ | Projects prior state through motion model |
| 2 | Covariance Prediction | $\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{P}_{k-1\vert k-1} \mathbf{F}_k^T + \mathbf{Q}_k$ | Grows uncertainty due to motion noise |
| 3 | Innovation (Residual) | $\boldsymbol{\nu}_k = \mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1}$ | Difference between measured and predicted observation |
| 4 | Innovation Covariance | $\mathbf{S}_k = \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T + \mathbf{R}_k$ | Total uncertainty in residual |
| 5 | Kalman Gain | $\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T \mathbf{S}_k^{-1}$ | Optimal blending weight (size $n \times m$) |
| 6 | State Update | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k \boldsymbol{\nu}_k$ | Posterior state estimate |
| 7 | Covariance Update | $\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}_k) \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1}$ | Shrinks uncertainty after measurement |
| 8 | Posterior Probability | $p(\mathbf{x}_k \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) = \mathcal{N}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k},\ \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k})$ | Belief is a Gaussian |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars):** Tesla, Waymo, Mobileye use Kalman/Extended Kalman filters to fuse **GPS, IMU, LiDAR, and wheel odometry** to localize the vehicle within a high-definition map.
- **Indoor Service Robots:** iRobot Roomba, warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva) use EKF to localize via **wall-mounted beacons / LiDAR scan-matching**.
- **Aerospace:** Apollo spacecraft navigation, drone flight controllers (Pixhawk) — Kalman filter is the **backbone** of inertial navigation.
- **AR/VR & Smartphones:** Sensor fusion of accelerometer + gyroscope for orientation tracking.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A common 14-mark question asks you to *draw the schematic and label the matrices*. Memorize the loop direction: **Predict → Measure → Gain → Update → (loop back to Predict)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Kalman Filter Update Equations from First Principles

The Kalman Filter is derived by **minimizing the posterior mean-square error** subject to the linear-Gaussian assumption. The full derivation has three major steps.

### Derivation A — State and Measurement Models (Assumptions)

We begin by writing the **linear discrete-time system**:

$$
\mathbf{x}_k = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{x}_{k-1} + \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{u}_k + \mathbf{w}_{k-1}, \quad \mathbf{w}_{k-1} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{Q}_k)
$$

$$
\mathbf{z}_k = \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{v}_k, \quad \mathbf{v}_k \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{R}_k)
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{x}_k \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the true (hidden) state at time step $k$.
- $\mathbf{z}_k \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is the noisy measurement.
- $\mathbf{w}_{k-1}$ and $\mathbf{v}_k$ are independent, zero-mean, white Gaussian noise vectors.
- $\mathbf{F}_k, \mathbf{B}_k, \mathbf{H}_k$ are known linear system matrices.

The prior belief at $k-1$ is Gaussian:

$$
p(\mathbf{x}_{k-1} \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1}) = \mathcal{N}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1\vert k-1},\ \mathbf{P}_{k-1\vert k-1})
$$

### Derivation B — Prediction Step (Time Update)

Because the system is linear and noise is Gaussian, the predicted belief is also Gaussian.

**Step 1 — State Prediction (take the expectation of the state equation):**

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} = E[\mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{x}_{k-1} + \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{u}_k + \mathbf{w}_{k-1} \ \vert\  \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1}]
$$

Since $\mathbf{u}_k$ is known and $E[\mathbf{w}_{k-1}] = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k E[\mathbf{x}_{k-1}] + \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{u}_k
$$

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1\vert k-1} + \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{u}_k
$$

**Step 2 — Covariance Prediction (compute the variance of the prediction error):**

The prediction error is $\mathbf{e}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{e}_{k-1\vert k-1} + \mathbf{w}_{k-1}$.

Its covariance is:

$$
\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} = E[\mathbf{e}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{e}_{k\vert k-1}^T]
$$

$$
\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} = E[(\mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{e}_{k-1} + \mathbf{w}_{k-1})(\mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{e}_{k-1} + \mathbf{w}_{k-1})^T]
$$

Expanding the inner product (since $\mathbf{e}_{k-1}$ and $\mathbf{w}_{k-1}$ are uncorrelated and zero-mean, their cross-terms vanish):

$$
\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k E[\mathbf{e}_{k-1}\mathbf{e}_{k-1}^T] \mathbf{F}_k^T + E[\mathbf{w}_{k-1}\mathbf{w}_{k-1}^T]
$$

$$
\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{P}_{k-1\vert k-1} \mathbf{F}_k^T + \mathbf{Q}_k
$$

### Derivation C — Update Step (Measurement Correction)

The posterior is found via Bayes' rule:

$$
p(\mathbf{x}_k \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) \propto p(\mathbf{z}_k \vert \mathbf{x}_k) \cdot p(\mathbf{x}_k \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1})
$$

For linear-Gaussian systems, the product of two Gaussians is again a Gaussian. The mean and covariance of the product can be obtained by *completing the square* in the exponent of the joint distribution. The result is:

**Step 3 — Kalman Gain derivation (sketch):** We seek the linear correction $\mathbf{K}_k$ that minimizes the posterior error covariance $\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k} = E[(\mathbf{x}_k - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k})(\mathbf{x}_k - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k})^T]$.

The posterior estimate is hypothesized to be of the form:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k (\mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1})
$$

Substitute $\mathbf{z}_k = \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{v}_k$:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}_k (\mathbf{x}_k - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1}) + \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{v}_k
$$

The posterior error becomes:

$$
\mathbf{x}_k - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}_k)(\mathbf{x}_k - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1}) - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{v}_k
$$

The posterior covariance is:

$$
\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}_k) \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}_k)^T + \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{R}_k \mathbf{K}_k^T
$$

To find the optimal $\mathbf{K}_k$, we take the matrix derivative of the trace of $\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k}$ with respect to $\mathbf{K}_k$ and set it to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial \ \text{tr}(\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k})}{\partial \mathbf{K}_k} = -2 (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}_k) \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T + 2 \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{R}_k = \mathbf{0}
$$

Solving:

$$
\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{R}_k = \mathbf{0}
$$

$$
\mathbf{K}_k (\mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T + \mathbf{R}_k) = \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T
$$

$$
\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T \left( \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T + \mathbf{R}_k \right)^{-1}
$$

This is the celebrated **Kalman Gain** — the heart of the schematic.

**Step 4 — Posterior State Update:** Plug $\mathbf{K}_k$ back into the linear correction form:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k (\mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1})
$$

**Step 5 — Posterior Covariance Update:** Substituting the optimal $\mathbf{K}_k$ and simplifying (using the matrix inversion lemma):

$$
\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}_k) \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1}
$$

The derivation is now complete. The five equations form the closed loop of the **Kalman Filter Localization Schematic**.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — 1D Mobile Robot Position

**Problem Setup:** A differential-drive robot moves along a 1D line. Its discrete motion model is:

$$
x_k = x_{k-1} + u_k + w_k, \quad w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\ Q)
$$

It measures its position via a noisy range sensor:

$$
z_k = x_k + v_k, \quad v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\ R)
$$

Given $x_0 = 0$, $P_0 = 1$, $u_1 = 1$, $Q = 0.1$, $R = 0.5$, $z_1 = 1.2$. Compute the posterior estimate $\hat{x}_{1\vert 1}$ and $P_{1\vert 1}$.

**Solution — Step-by-step:**

**Step (a) — State Prediction:**

$$
\hat{x}_{1\vert 0} = F \hat{x}_{0\vert 0} + B u_1 = (1)(0) + (1)(1) = 1
$$

**Step (b) — Covariance Prediction:**

$$
P_{1\vert 0} = F P_{0\vert 0} F^T + Q = (1)(1)(1) + 0.1 = 1.1
$$

**Step (c) — Kalman Gain:**

$$
K_1 = P_{1\vert 0} H^T (H P_{1\vert 0} H^T + R)^{-1} = (1.1)(1) \big((1)(1.1)(1) + 0.5\big)^{-1}
$$

$$
K_1 = \frac{1.1}{1.6} = 0.6875
$$

**Step (d) — Innovation:**

$$
\nu_1 = z_1 - H \hat{x}_{1\vert 0} = 1.2 - (1)(1) = 0.2
$$

**Step (e) — State Update:**

$$
\hat{x}_{1\vert 1} = \hat{x}_{1\vert 0} + K_1 \nu_1 = 1 + (0.6875)(0.2) = 1.1375
$$

**Step (f) — Covariance Update:**

$$
P_{1\vert 1} = (1 - K_1 H) P_{1\vert 0} = (1 - 0.6875)(1.1) = (0.3125)(1.1) = 0.34375
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The final estimate $\hat{x}_{1\vert 1} = 1.1375$ lies *between* the prediction (1.0) and the measurement (1.2) — closer to the prediction because $P_0 = 1$ is smaller than $R = 0.5$ in scaled terms. Uncertainty dropped from $P_{0}=1$ → $P_{1\vert 1}=0.34375$, confirming the measurement *reduced* uncertainty.

---

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Canonical Kalman Filter Localization Schematic (Block Diagram)

The block diagram below is the **canonical schematic** that students must reproduce in KTU board exams. It captures the closed-loop interaction between the **Predict** and **Update** stages.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: k = 0"] --> B["Initialize State xhat0 and Covariance P0"]
    B --> C["Read Control Input uk from Odometry"]
    C --> D["Predict State: xhat_kk-1 = F xhat_k-1 + B uk"]
    D --> E["Predict Covariance: P_kk-1 = F P_k-1 F trans + Q"]
    E --> F["Acquire Sensor Measurement zk"]
    F --> G["Compute Innovation: nu_k = zk - H xhat_kk-1"]
    G --> H["Compute Innovation Covariance: S_k = H P_kk-1 H trans + R"]
    H --> I["Compute Kalman Gain: K_k = P_kk-1 H trans inv S_k"]
    I --> J["Update State: xhat_kk = xhat_kk-1 + K_k nu_k"]
    J --> K["Update Covariance: P_kk = I minus K_k H times P_kk-1"]
    K --> L{"k less than k_final"}
    L -- "No" --> M["Output Final Pose Estimate xhat_kk and P_kk"]
    L -- "Yes" --> C
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Sensors and Models in Mobile Robot Localization

This second diagram explains *where* the matrices $\mathbf{F}, \mathbf{H}, \mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{R}$ originate from the physical robot system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MotionLayer[Motion Model Source]
        ML1["Wheel Encoders"] --> ML2["Velocity v and omega"]
        ML3["IMU Gyroscope"] --> ML2
        ML2 --> ML4["F k and B k matrices"]
        ML5["Motor Command Noise"] --> ML6["Q k Process Noise"]
    end

    subgraph SensorLayer[Sensor Model Source]
        SL1["LiDAR Scan"] --> SL2["Landmark Range r and Bearing phi"]
        SL3["GPS Receiver"] --> SL2
        SL4["Camera Features"] --> SL2
        SL2 --> SL5["H k Observation Matrix"]
        SL6["Sensor Resolution Limits"] --> SL7["R k Measurement Noise"]
    end

    subgraph KFEngine[Kalman Filter Engine]
        KF1["Predict Block"] --> KF2["Update Block"]
        KF2 --> KF3["Optimal Pose xhat and Covariance P"]
    end

    ML4 --> KF1
    ML6 --> KF1
    SL5 --> KF2
    SL7 --> KF2
```

## 4.3 Information-Flow Schematic (Data Block View)

This schematic illustrates the temporal flow of data within one full cycle of the filter, suitable for 7-mark short answers in KTU.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["Prior Belief at step k-1: N xhat_k-1, P_k-1"] --> A2["Motion Model: xk = Fk xk-1 + Bk uk + wk"]
    A2 --> A3["Predicted Belief at step k: N xhat_kk-1, P_kk-1"]
    A3 --> A4["Measurement Function: zk = Hk xk + vk"]
    A4 --> A5["Innovation: nuk = zk - Hk xhat_kk-1"]
    A5 --> A6["Kalman Gain: Kk = P_kk-1 Hk trans inv Sk"]
    A6 --> A7["Posterior Belief at step k: N xhat_kk, P_kk"]
    A7 --> A8["Stored as Prior for next cycle"]
    A8 --> A1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Always label **all five matrices** ($\mathbf{F}, \mathbf{H}, \mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{R}, \mathbf{K}$) and **all three vectors** ($\hat{\mathbf{x}}, \mathbf{z}, \boldsymbol{\nu}$) on the schematic. Missing even one label costs you 2–3 marks.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1] [3 Marks]**
> **Q:** Define the *Kalman Gain* $\mathbf{K}_k$ in the context of mobile robot localization and explain its physical significance.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The Kalman Gain is given by:
> $$\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T \left( \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T + \mathbf{R}_k \right)^{-1}$$
> It is an $n \times m$ weighting matrix that determines how much the robot *trusts* the new sensor measurement versus the motion prediction. When $\mathbf{R}_k \to \mathbf{0}$ (sensor extremely reliable), $\mathbf{K}_k \to \mathbf{H}_k^{-1}$ and the filter trusts the measurement fully. When $\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \to \mathbf{0}$ (prediction extremely certain), $\mathbf{K}_k \to \mathbf{0}$ and the filter ignores the measurement. Physically, $\mathbf{K}_k$ is the **optimal blend factor** between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensing.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2] [3 Marks]**
> **Q:** State the two fundamental assumptions of the standard Kalman Filter.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **Linearity:** Both the motion model and measurement model are linear in the state and inputs, i.e., expressible as $\mathbf{x}_k = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{x}_{k-1} + \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{u}_k$ and $\mathbf{z}_k = \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{v}_k$.
> 2. **Gaussian Noise:** The process noise $\mathbf{w}_k \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{Q}_k)$ and measurement noise $\mathbf{v}_k \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{R}_k)$ are zero-mean, white, and mutually uncorrelated. Under these assumptions, the posterior remains Gaussian and the filter is the *Minimum Mean Square Error (MMSE)* estimator.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Pattern] [14 Marks]**

### ❓ Question A (Choice 1)

**(a)** [7 Marks] Draw and explain the complete schematic of the Kalman Filter for mobile robot localization. Clearly label the Predict stage, the Update stage, all five matrices, and the three core vectors. State the system and measurement model equations assumed.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A mobile robot is governed by the linear state equation $x_k = x_{k-1} + u_k + w_k$ with $w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.04)$, and the measurement model $z_k = x_k + v_k$ with $v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.25)$. Given $\hat{x}_{0\vert 0} = 0$, $P_{0\vert 0} = 1$, $u_1 = 2$, $z_1 = 2.4$. Compute the posterior estimate $\hat{x}_{1\vert 1}$ and $P_{1\vert 1}$ step by step.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The schematic is a closed-loop of two stages. **Predict Stage (Time Update):**
- The prior state $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1\vert k-1}$ and covariance $\mathbf{P}_{k-1\vert k-1}$ enter the motion model.
- The robot applies control input $\mathbf{u}_k$ (from odometry).
- Output: predicted state $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1}$ and predicted covariance $\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1}$.

**Update Stage (Measurement Update):**
- Sensor produces measurement $\mathbf{z}_k$.
- Innovation computed: $\boldsymbol{\nu}_k = \mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1}$.
- Kalman Gain $\mathbf{K}_k$ computed using $\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1}, \mathbf{H}_k, \mathbf{R}_k$.
- Posterior state and covariance updated.

The five matrices and three vectors: $\mathbf{F}_k, \mathbf{B}_k, \mathbf{H}_k, \mathbf{Q}_k, \mathbf{R}_k$ and $\hat{\mathbf{x}}, \mathbf{z}, \boldsymbol{\nu}$. System and measurement models:

$$
\mathbf{x}_k = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{x}_{k-1} + \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{u}_k + \mathbf{w}_{k-1}, \quad \mathbf{z}_k = \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{v}_k
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — State Prediction:**

$$
\hat{x}_{1\vert 0} = (1)(0) + (1)(2) = 2
$$

**Step 2 — Covariance Prediction:**

$$
P_{1\vert 0} = (1)(1)(1) + 0.04 = 1.04
$$

**Step 3 — Kalman Gain:**

$$
K_1 = \frac{P_{1\vert 0} H}{H P_{1\vert 0} H + R} = \frac{(1.04)(1)}{(1)(1.04)(1) + 0.25} = \frac{1.04}{1.29}
$$

$$
K_1 = 0.8062
$$

**Step 4 — Innovation:**

$$
\nu_1 = z_1 - H \hat{x}_{1\vert 0} = 2.4 - (1)(2) = 0.4
$$

**Step 5 — State Update:**

$$
\hat{x}_{1\vert 1} = 2 + (0.8062)(0.4) = 2 + 0.3225 = 2.3225
$$

**Step 6 — Covariance Update:**

$$
P_{1\vert 1} = (1 - 0.8062)(1.04) = (0.1938)(1.04) = 0.2015
$$

**Final Answer:** $\hat{x}_{1\vert 1} = 2.3225$ and $P_{1\vert 1} \approx 0.2015$.

### ❓ Question B (Choice 2)

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the aid of a clearly labeled block diagram, derive the Kalman Gain expression. Show all intermediate matrix calculus steps and the use of the minimization of the trace of the posterior error covariance.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare the Kalman Filter (KF), Extended Kalman Filter (EKF), and Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) in terms of linearity assumption, computational cost, and accuracy in mobile robot localization.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The posterior estimate is hypothesized as a linear correction:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k (\mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1})
$$

Substitute $\mathbf{z}_k = \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{v}_k$ and define the prior error $\mathbf{e}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1}$. The posterior error becomes:

$$
\mathbf{x}_k - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}_k)\mathbf{e}_{k\vert k-1} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{v}_k
$$

The posterior covariance is:

$$
\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}_k) \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}_k)^T + \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{R}_k \mathbf{K}_k^T
$$

Minimize the trace:

$$
\frac{\partial \ \text{tr}(\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k})}{\partial \mathbf{K}_k} = -2 (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}_k) \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T + 2 \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{R}_k = \mathbf{0}
$$

Rearranging and solving for $\mathbf{K}_k$:

$$
\mathbf{K}_k (\mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T + \mathbf{R}_k) = \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T (\mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T + \mathbf{R}_k)^{-1}}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Aspect | Kalman Filter (KF) | Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) | Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linearity Assumption** | Strictly linear systems | Locally linearized via Jacobians | No linearity required; deterministic sampling |
| **Noise Distribution** | Gaussian only | Gaussian only | Gaussian (approx. for higher moments) |
| **Computational Cost** | Lowest ($O(n^3)$) | Moderate (Jacobian evaluation) | Higher (sigma-point propagation) |
| **Accuracy** | Optimal for linear-Gaussian | Accurate for mild non-linearity | High accuracy for strong non-linearity |
| **Use in Mobile Robots** | Wheel encoder + simple beacons | SLAM, GPS+IMU fusion | GPS-denied indoor localization, highly non-linear sensors |
| **Key Limitation** | Fails on non-linear motion | Diverges under large non-linearity | Heavier computation; tuning of sigma points |

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Swapping $\mathbf{Q}$ and $\mathbf{R}$ in the covariance equations** — $\mathbf{Q}$ belongs to *prediction*, $\mathbf{R}$ to *update*. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the transpose on $\mathbf{F}_k^T$** in $\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{P}_{k-1\vert k-1} \mathbf{F}_k^T + \mathbf{Q}_k$. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Inverting the wrong matrix** in the Kalman Gain — it is the *innovation covariance* $\mathbf{S}_k = \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T + \mathbf{R}_k$, not just $\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1}$. (–2 marks)
> 4. **Not drawing the loop-back arrow** in the schematic — the filter is *recursive*, and the output of Update is fed back as input to the next Predict. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Confusing *prior* vs *posterior* notation** — $k\vert k-1$ means "estimated at time $k$ given information up to $k-1$"; $k\vert k$ means "after incorporating measurement at $k$". (–1 mark)
> 6. **Treating $\mathbf{P}$ as a scalar** — it is a *symmetric positive-definite matrix*; trace and determinant are what physically represent uncertainty. (–2 marks)

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Core Idea:** Kalman Filter = optimal recursive estimator for **linear-Gaussian** systems.
- **Two Stages:** Predict (motion model) → Update (measurement model).
- **Five Key Equations:** State Predict, Covariance Predict, Kalman Gain, State Update, Covariance Update.
- **Schematic Loop:** Prior → Predict → Measure → Innovation → Gain → Update → (back to Prior).
- **State Vector for 2D Mobile Robot:** $\mathbf{x} = [x, y, \theta]^T$ (3-dimensional).
- **Kalman Gain Significance:** Optimal weight — high when sensor is reliable, low when motion prediction is reliable.
- **Innovation $\boldsymbol{\nu}_k$:** Difference between actual and predicted measurement; drives the correction.
- **Process Noise $\mathbf{Q}$:** Models uncertainty in motion (wheel slip, floor unevenness).
- **Measurement Noise $\mathbf{R}$:** Models sensor uncertainty (LiDAR beam width, GPS multipath).
- **Uncertainty Monotonicity:** $P$ **grows** in Predict, **shrinks** in Update.
- **Extension to Non-Linear Systems:** EKF (Jacobian linearization), UKF (sigma points), Particle Filter (Monte Carlo).
- **Optimality Property:** Under linear-Gaussian assumption, the KF is the **MMSE estimator** — no other algorithm can do better.
- **Matrix Sizes:** $n$ = state dim (typically 3), $m$ = measurement dim (typically 2 for range-bearing).
- **Critical Pitfall:** KF **fails** on highly non-linear systems (e.g., sharp rotations) — use EKF or UKF instead.
- **Joseph Form for Stability:** Use $\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}_k)\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}_k)^T + \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{R}_k \mathbf{K}_k^T$ to guarantee positive semi-definiteness.
- **Real-world Variants:** Information Filter, Error-State Kalman Filter (ESKF), Invariant EKF — used in modern SLAM stacks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Position Prediction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Robot Position Prediction # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Robot Position Prediction** is the process of estimating the future pose (spatial position and orientation) of a mobile robot at time step $t+1$ given its current pose at time $t$, the applied control inputs (linear and angular velocity commands or odometric measurements), and a chosen **motion model** (kinematic or dynamic). Formally, it computes $\hat{x}_{t+1} = f(\hat{x}_t, u_t) + w_t$, where $f(\cdot)$ is the state transition function and $w_t$ represents process noise. In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, robot position prediction is a foundational submodule within the broader **motion planning pipeline**, enabling reactive obstacle avoidance, trajectory tracking, and probabilistic localization (e.g., Kalman filtering). The robot state for a planar mobile robot is typically represented as a three-dimensional vector: $$x_t = \begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ y_t \\ \theta_t \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$$ where $(x_t, y_t)$ denotes the planar position in the world frame, and $\theta_t$ denotes the heading angle. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Blindfolded Driver:** Imagine you are blindfolded inside a moving car. You cannot see the road, but you know the steering wheel angle and the gas pedal position. By integrating these controls over time, you *predict* your future location. Robot position prediction works identically — it uses **control commands** and a **motion model** to extrapolate where the robot "should" be. Another everyday analogy: a **mariner navigating by dead reckoning**. Sailors in the age of exploration estimated their future ship position by knowing their current coordinates, sailing speed, and compass bearing. The robot's encoder readings and IMU data play the same role — they are internal estimates of motion that, when integrated, predict future pose. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Wheel radius** ($R$): typically **0.05 m to 0.30 m** for mobile robots. - **Track length / wheelbase** ($L$): typically **0.10 m to 0.50 m**. - **Sampling period** ($\Delta t$): typically **10 ms to 100 ms** in real-time control loops. - **Process noise covariance** ($Q$ or $M$): tunable diagonal matrix reflecting encoder/IMU accuracy. > [!TIP] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 emphasizes the *kinematic prediction models* (unicycle, differential drive, Ackermann) and the *uncertainty propagation* mechanism. Memorize the three canonical models and their Jacobian matrices — they appear almost every examination cycle. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Prediction Pipeline — Operational Logic The robot position prediction workflow follows a **three-step logical chain**: 1. **State Sampling:** Capture the current pose estimate $\hat{x}_t$ from the localization module (or initialize at origin). 2. **Control Integration:** Apply the motion model $f(\cdot)$ using the most recent control input $u_t$ (velocity, odometry, or wheel encoder deltas). 3. **Uncertainty Propagation:** Update the covariance matrix $\Sigma_t$ to reflect growing uncertainty due to process noise $w_t$. Mathematically expressed as the discrete-time stochastic state-space model: $$\hat{x}_{t+1} = f(\hat{x}_t, u_t) + w_t, \quad w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$$ $$\Sigma_{t+1} = G_t \, \Sigma_t \, G_t^{\top} + V_t \, M_t \, V_t^{\top}$$ where: - $G_t = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{\hat{x}_t, u_t}$ is the **state Jacobian** (linearizes the motion model around the current estimate). - $V_t = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial w}\bigg\vert_{\hat{x}_t, u_t}$ is the **noise Jacobian**. - $M_t$ is the control / process noise covariance. ## 2.2 Why Prediction Matters — The "Why" and "How" > **Why?** In probabilistic robotics, the robot never knows its *true* pose — it maintains a **belief** $\text{bel}(x_t) = \{ \hat{x}_t, \Sigma_t \}$. Prediction is the *time-update* step that shifts this belief forward in time before a new measurement is fused (correction step). > **How?** By combining a deterministic kinematic model with stochastic noise propagation, prediction bridges the gap between *control* and *belief update*. It allows the motion planner to anticipate the robot's footprint **$\Delta t$** seconds in the future for collision checking. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Model / Quantity | Equation | Symbols & Units | |---|------------------|----------|-----------------| | 1 | Unicycle state vector | $x_t = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$ | $x, y$ in $\mathrm{m}$; $\theta$ in $\mathrm{rad}$ | | 2 | Unicycle continuous kinematics | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta,\ \dot{y} = v\sin\theta,\ \dot{\theta} = \omega$ | $v$ in $\mathrm{m/s}$; $\omega$ in $\mathrm{rad/s}$ | | 3 | Unicycle discrete prediction | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \Delta t \cos\theta_k$
$y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \Delta t \sin\theta_k$
$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \Delta t$ | All in SI units | | 4 | Differential drive (encoder-based) | $x_{k+1} = x_k + \frac{R}{2}(\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l)\cos\theta_k$
$y_{k+1} = y_k + \frac{R}{2}(\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l)\sin\theta_k$
$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \frac{R}{L}(\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l)$ | $R$ wheel radius; $L$ track length; $\Delta s_{r,l}$ wheel arc lengths in $\mathrm{m}$ | | 5 | Odometry model (midpoint) | $\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l}{2}$
$\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{L}$
