$y_{k+1} = y_k + \Delta s \sin(\theta_k + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2})$ | $\Delta s$ in $\mathrm{m}$; $\Delta \theta$ in $\mathrm{rad}$ | | 6 | Unicycle state Jacobian $G_t$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -v\Delta t\sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & v\Delta t\cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $3 \times 3$ matrix | | 7 | Unicycle noise Jacobian $V_t$ | $\begin{bmatrix} \Delta t \cos\theta & 0 \\ \Delta t \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & \Delta t \end{bmatrix}$ | $3 \times 2$ matrix (control $u = [v,\ \omega]^{\top}$) | | 8 | Covariance update | $\Sigma_{t+1} = G_t \Sigma_t G_t^{\top} + V_t M_t V_t^{\top}$ | $\Sigma, M$ symmetric positive definite | | 9 | Kalman filter prediction | $\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} = F_k \hat{x}_{k-1\mid k-1} + B_k u_k$
$P_{k\mid k-1} = F_k P_{k-1\mid k-1} F_k^{\top} + Q_k$ | $F_k = G_t$, $Q_k = V_t M_t V_t^{\top}$ | | 10 | Process noise $M_t$ (typical) | $\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_v^2 & 0 \\ 0 & \sigma_\omega^2 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\sigma_v$ in $\mathrm{m/s}$; $\sigma_\omega$ in $\mathrm{rad/s}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on absolute values:** When writing $\vert x \vert$ in any KTU answer, always use the LaTeX form `\vert x \vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table structure. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Robot position prediction is the **backbone of every autonomous mobile platform**: - **Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva, Locus Robotics):** use dead-reckoning + wheel odometry for sub-meter local accuracy. - **Autonomous vehicles (Waymo, Mobileye):** use unicycle/Ackermann prediction fused with GPS/IMU/LiDAR via EKF. - **Mars Rovers (NASA Perseverance, Curiosity):** rely on visual odometry + slip-aware kinematic prediction because wheel slip on Martian regolith is severe. - **Drones (quadrotors):** prediction uses the **dynamic model** $\ddot{x} = g - (u/m) \cdot R \cdot e_3$ rather than the kinematic unicycle. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Unicycle Discrete Prediction from Continuous Model **Step 1.** Start with the continuous-time unicycle kinematic model: $$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega$$ **Step 2.** Apply **forward Euler integration** with sampling period $\Delta t$. Euler's method approximates the derivative as $\dot{x} \approx (x_{k+1} - x_k)/\Delta t$, i.e., the change in state equals the rate of change times the interval. $$\frac{x_{k+1} - x_k}{\Delta t} = v_k \cos\theta_k$$ **Step 3.** Solve algebraically for $x_{k+1}$ by multiplying both sides by $\Delta t$ and adding $x_k$: $$x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cos\theta_k \cdot \Delta t$$ **Step 4.** Apply the same forward Euler logic independently to the $y$ and $\theta$ channels: $$y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \sin\theta_k \cdot \Delta t$$ $$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \cdot \Delta t$$ **Step 5.** Express the complete prediction in compact matrix-free form: $$x_{k+1} = \begin{bmatrix} x_k + v_k \Delta t \cos\theta_k \\ y_k + v_k \Delta t \sin\theta_k \\ \theta_k + \omega_k \Delta t \end{bmatrix}$$ > **Intuition behind Euler integration:** The robot's position changes linearly over the small interval $\Delta t$, with velocity held constant. For larger $\Delta t$, error accumulates — this is why real-world systems often use **Runge–Kutta 4th order (RK4)** for better fidelity. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Differential Drive Prediction from Wheel Encoder Deltas **Step 1.** Define wheel encoder increments. Each wheel of radius $R$ traverses an arc length $\Delta s_l$ (left) and $\Delta s_r$ (right) over time $\Delta t$: $$\Delta s_l = R \cdot \Delta \phi_l, \quad \Delta s_r = R \cdot \Delta \phi_r$$ where $\Delta \phi_{l,r}$ are wheel angular displacements in radians measured by encoders. **Step 2.** Compute the **average linear displacement** of the robot's center: $$\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l}{2} = \frac{R}{2}(\Delta \phi_r + \Delta \phi_l)$$ **Step 3.** Compute the **change in heading**. The differential drive rotates about the Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC), located perpendicular to the wheelbase axis at distance $R_{ICC}$ from the robot center. Geometrically: $$\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{L}$$ where $L$ is the wheelbase (track length). *Logic:* if both wheels move equally, $\Delta\theta = 0$ (pure translation); if they move oppositely, $\Delta\theta$ is large (in-place rotation). **Step 4.** Use the **midpoint odometry formula** to update position. The robot's heading is evaluated at the *midpoint* of the arc to reduce integration error: $$x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta s \cdot \cos\!\left(\theta_k + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right)$$ $$y_{k+1} = y_k + \Delta s \cdot \sin\!\left(\theta_k + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right)$$ $$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta \theta$$ **Step 5.** Substitute $\Delta s$ and $\Delta \theta$ from Steps 2 and 3 to get the final form: $$x_{k+1} = x_k + \frac{R}{2}(\Delta \phi_r + \Delta \phi_l)\cos\!\left(\theta_k + \frac{R(\Delta\phi_r - \Delta\phi_l)}{2L}\right)$$ $$y_{k+1} = y_k + \frac{R}{2}(\Delta \phi_r + \Delta \phi_l)\sin\!\left(\theta_k + \frac{R(\Delta\phi_r - \Delta\phi_l)}{2L}\right)$$ $$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \frac{R}{L}(\Delta \phi_r - \Delta \phi_l)$$ > **Why midpoint?** The exact solution for constant curvature motion requires evaluating $\theta$ at $t + \Delta t/2$. Using $\theta_k$ (the start) over-rotates the path; using $\theta_{k+1}$ (the end) under-rotates. The midpoint is the geometrically correct average for circular arcs. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Covariance Propagation (Linearized EKF Prediction) **Step 1.** State the nonlinear prediction with additive noise: $$x_{t+1} = f(x_t, u_t) + w_t, \quad w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, M_t)$$ **Step 2.** Linearize $f(\cdot)$ around the current estimate $\hat{x}_t$ using a first-order **Taylor expansion**. The Jacobian $G_t$ captures how the next state changes with the current state: $$G_t = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{(\hat{x}_t, u_t)} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial \theta} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial \theta} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial f_3}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_3}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial f_3}{\partial \theta} \end{bmatrix}_{\hat{x}_t, u_t}$$ **Step 3.** For the unicycle model $f = (x + v\Delta t\cos\theta,\ y + v\Delta t\sin\theta,\ \theta + \omega\Delta t)$, compute each partial derivative: $$\frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} = 1, \quad \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial y} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial \theta} = -v\Delta t\sin\theta$$ $$\frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial y} = 1, \quad \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial \theta} = v\Delta t\cos\theta$$ $$\frac{\partial f_3}{\partial x} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial f_3}{\partial y} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial f_3}{\partial \theta} = 1$$ **Step 4.** Assemble the Jacobian: $$G_t = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -v\Delta t\sin\theta_t \\ 0 & 1 & v\Delta t\cos\theta_t \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 5.** Compute the noise Jacobian $V_t = \partial f / \partial w$ (or equivalently $\partial f / \partial u$ when $u$ is the noisy control): $$V_t = \begin{bmatrix} \Delta t \cos\theta_t & 0 \\ \Delta t \sin\theta_t & 0 \\ 0 & \Delta t \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 6.** Propagate covariance using the standard EKF equation. *Logic:* uncertainty in the current state is mapped forward by $G_t$, and additional uncertainty from process noise is added via $V_t M_t V_t^{\top}$: $$\Sigma_{t+1} = G_t \, \Sigma_t \, G_t^{\top} + V_t \, M_t \, V_t^{\top}$$ > **Geometric intuition:** The $G_t \Sigma_t G_t^{\top}$ term **stretches and rotates** the current uncertainty ellipse along the robot's motion direction. The $V_t M_t V_t^{\top}$ term **adds a new ellipse** representing fresh process noise. The sum is the new prediction ellipse. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A differential-drive robot with $R = 0.08\,\mathrm{m}$ and $L = 0.30\,\mathrm{m}$ is at pose $\hat{x}_0 = [0,\ 0,\ 0]^{\top}$ (in meters/rad). Encoder deltas over $\Delta t = 0.1\,\mathrm{s}$: $\Delta \phi_r = 1.2\,\mathrm{rad}$, $\Delta \phi_l = 0.8\,\mathrm{rad}$. Predict the next pose. **Solution:** Step 1 — Compute wheel arc lengths: $$\Delta s_r = R \cdot \Delta \phi_r = 0.08 \times 1.2 = 0.096\,\mathrm{m}$$ $$\Delta s_l = R \cdot \Delta \phi_l = 0.08 \times 0.8 = 0.064\,\mathrm{m}$$ Step 2 — Compute average translation and rotation: $$\Delta s = \frac{0.096 + 0.064}{2} = 0.080\,\mathrm{m}$$ $$\Delta \theta = \frac{0.096 - 0.064}{0.30} = 0.1067\,\mathrm{rad}$$ Step 3 — Apply midpoint odometry update: $$x_1 = 0 + 0.080 \cdot \cos\!\left(0 + \tfrac{0.1067}{2}\right) = 0.080 \cdot \cos(0.0533) = 0.080 \times 0.9986 = 0.0799\,\mathrm{m}$$ $$y_1 = 0 + 0.080 \cdot \sin(0.0533) = 0.080 \times 0.0533 = 0.00426\,\mathrm{m}$$ $$\theta_1 = 0 + 0.1067 = 0.1067\,\mathrm{rad} \quad (\approx 6.11^{\circ})$$ **Predicted pose:** $\hat{x}_1 \approx [0.0799,\ 0.00426,\ 0.1067]^{\top}$. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Robot Position Prediction Pipeline The following Mermaid diagram captures the end-to-end functional flow of robot position prediction, including its interfaces with sensing, planning, and localization modules. ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["Current Belief State bel_x_t"] --> node2["Pose Estimator x_hat_t Sigma_t"] node2 --> node3["State Transition Function f x_t u_t"] nodeA["Wheel Encoders"] --> node4["Control Input u_t vel omega"] nodeB["IMU Sensor"] --> node4 node4 --> node3 node3 --> node5["Predicted Pose x_hat_t_plus_1"] node2 --> node6["Compute Jacobian G_t"] node3 --> node6 node4 --> node7["Compute Noise Jacobian V_t"] node6 --> node8["Covariance Propagation"] node7 --> node8 nodeC["Process Noise Covariance M_t"] --> node8 node8 --> node9["Updated Sigma_t_plus_1"] node5 --> node10["Motion Planner Trajectory Check"] node9 --> node10 node10 --> node11["Collision Check and Reactive Avoidance"] node5 --> node12["Localization Module EKF Correction"] node9 --> node12 nodeD["Landmark / LiDAR Measurement z_t"] --> node12 node12 --> node2 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The pipeline is a closed feedback loop. The **prediction branch** (left side) computes the new pose and covariance from the current belief and control input. The **planning branch** (right side) consumes the predicted belief for short-horizon collision checking. The **correction branch** (bottom) feeds measurements back to the pose estimator, closing the loop. This is the canonical EKF-based mobile robot architecture taught in KTU's OEBRT833 syllabus. ## 4.2 State Evolution Flow (Mermaid Sequence Diagram) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Enc as Wheel Encoders participant IMU as IMU participant KF as Prediction Module participant Loc as Localization participant Plan as Motion Planner Enc->>KF: Delta phi_r, Delta phi_l IMU->>KF: omega, acceleration KF->>KF: Compute x_hat_t_plus_1 via f KF->>KF: Update Sigma_t_plus_1 KF->>Loc: Provide predicted belief Loc->>KF: Return corrected belief bel_x_t_plus_1 KF->>Plan: Forward predicted pose Plan->>Plan: Check for collisions in Delta t Plan-->>KF: Updated velocity commands u_t_plus_1 ``` ## 4.3 Concept Hierarchy Map ```mermaid mindmap root(("Robot Position Prediction")) Models Unicycle Differential Drive Ackermann Steering Bicycle Model Methods Dead Reckoning Odometry Integration EKF Prediction Particle Filter Prediction Uncertainty Process Noise Q State Jacobian G Noise Jacobian V Covariance Propagation Applications Path Tracking Collision Avoidance SLAM Front End Autonomous Navigation ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting $\Delta t$ in unicycle Euler integration** — students often write $x_{k+1} = x_k + v\cos\theta$ without the $\Delta t$ factor, losing 2–3 marks instantly. > 2. **Confusing odometry vs. velocity models** — the odometry model uses $\Delta s$ and $\Delta \theta$ (incremental), while the velocity model uses $v$ and $\omega$ (instantaneous). Examiners test this distinction. > 3. **Skipping the Jacobian derivation** — when asked for the EKF prediction Jacobian, students must compute all 9 partial derivatives explicitly. A matrix with even one wrong entry loses 50% of the marks allotted. > 4. **Midpoint angle** — in differential-drive odometry, the heading used in $\cos/\sin$ is $\theta_k + \Delta\theta/2$, not $\theta_k$. This is a frequently tested trap. > 5. **Units inconsistency** — mixing degrees and radians in $\theta$ and $\omega$ is a silent error that propagates throughout the calculation. --- ## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define robot position prediction. State the general form of the discrete-time state transition model used in motion prediction. **Model Answer:** Robot position prediction is the process of estimating the robot's future pose $\hat{x}_{t+1}$ from the current pose $\hat{x}_t$ and the applied control input $u_t$, using a kinematic or dynamic motion model. The general discrete-time state transition model is: $$x_{t+1} = f(x_t, u_t) + w_t$$ where $f(\cdot)$ is the (possibly nonlinear) state transition function, $u_t$ is the control input (e.g., $u_t = [v_t, \omega_t]^{\top}$), and $w_t$ is the additive process noise typically assumed Gaussian, $w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$. **Mark split:** *Definition — 1 mark; state transition equation — 1 mark; noise model — 1 mark.* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Differentiate between the **velocity motion model** and the **odometry motion model** for robot position prediction. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Velocity Motion Model | Odometry Motion Model | |--------|------------------------|------------------------| | Input | Instantaneous $v_t, \omega_t$ | Incremental $\Delta s_r, \Delta s_l$ | | Noise | Added to $v, \omega$ | Added to $\Delta s, \Delta \theta$ | | Source | Desired/commanded velocities | Wheel encoder measurements | | Error type | Systematic (slip, drift) | Local (per step) | | Preferred use | Simulation, high-level planning | Real robot state estimation | **Mark split:** *Any 3 valid differentiating points — 3 marks.* --- ## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > Choose **ONE** of the following two questions. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** Derive the discrete-time prediction equations for a **unicycle mobile robot** using forward Euler integration of the continuous-time kinematic model. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Starting from the state transition $x_{t+1} = f(x_t, u_t) + w_t$, derive the **state Jacobian $G_t$** and the **noise Jacobian $V_t$** for the unicycle model, and write the **covariance propagation equation**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Assumption 1:** The control inputs $v_k$ and $\omega_k$ are held constant during the sampling interval $\Delta t$ (zero-order hold). **Assumption 2:** The sampling period $\Delta t$ is small enough that forward Euler integration is accurate. **Assumption 3:** The robot moves on a flat, planar surface (no elevation change). **Step 1 — Start with continuous kinematics:** $$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega$$ **Step 2 — Apply forward Euler discretization** ($\dot{x} \approx (x_{k+1} - x_k)/\Delta t$): $$\frac{x_{k+1} - x_k}{\Delta t} = v_k \cos\theta_k \implies x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \Delta t \cos\theta_k$$ **Step 3 — Repeat for $y$ and $\theta$ channels:** $$y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \Delta t \sin\theta_k$$ $$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \Delta t$$ **Step 4 — Compact matrix form:** $$x_{k+1} = \begin{bmatrix} x_k \\ y_k \\ \theta_k \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} v_k \Delta t \cos\theta_k \\ v_k \Delta t \sin\theta_k \\ \omega_k \Delta t \end{bmatrix}$$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Define the state transition function explicitly:** $$f(x_t, u_t) = \begin{bmatrix} x_t + v_t \Delta t \cos\theta_t \\ y_t + v_t \Delta t \sin\theta_t \\ \theta_t + \omega_t \Delta t \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the partial derivatives with respect to $x_t$:** $$\frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} = 1, \quad \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial f_3}{\partial x} = 0$$ **Step 3 — Compute the partial derivatives with respect to $y_t$:** $$\frac{\partial f_1}{\partial y} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial y} = 1, \quad \frac{\partial f_3}{\partial y} = 0$$ **Step 4 — Compute the partial derivatives with respect to $\theta_t$:** $$\frac{\partial f_1}{\partial \theta} = -v_t \Delta t \sin\theta_t, \quad \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial \theta} = v_t \Delta t \cos\theta_t, \quad \frac{\partial f_3}{\partial \theta} = 1$$ **Step 5 — Assemble the state Jacobian:** $$G_t = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -v_t \Delta t \sin\theta_t \\ 0 & 1 & v_t \Delta t \cos\theta_t \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 6 — Compute the noise Jacobian** (with respect to $u = [v, \omega]^{\top}$): $$V_t = \begin{bmatrix} \Delta t \cos\theta_t & 0 \\ \Delta t \sin\theta_t & 0 \\ 0 & \Delta t \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 7 — Write the covariance propagation:** $$\Sigma_{t+1} = G_t \, \Sigma_t \, G_t^{\top} + V_t \, M_t \, V_t^{\top}$$ where $M_t = \mathrm{diag}(\sigma_v^2, \sigma_\omega^2)$ is the process noise covariance. **Mark split — (a):** *Continuous model statement — 1 mark; Euler integration step — 2 marks; three final equations — 3 marks; assumptions — 1 mark.* **Mark split — (b):** *State function explicit — 1 mark; partial derivative computation — 3 marks; Jacobian assembly — 1 mark; noise Jacobian — 1 mark; final covariance equation — 1 mark.* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **(a)** A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $R = 0.05\,\mathrm{m}$ and wheelbase $L = 0.40\,\mathrm{m}$. It is currently at pose $\hat{x}_t = [1.0\,\mathrm{m},\ 2.0\,\mathrm{m},\ \pi/4\,\mathrm{rad}]^{\top}$. Over the next interval $\Delta t = 0.2\,\mathrm{s}$, the wheel encoders report $\Delta \phi_r = 2.5\,\mathrm{rad}$ and $\Delta \phi_l = 1.5\,\mathrm{rad}$. Using the **midpoint odometry model**, predict the next pose. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the concept of **uncertainty propagation** in robot position prediction. Discuss the role of the Jacobian matrices $G_t$ and $V_t$ in this process, and explain why uncertainty grows with time in the absence of measurement corrections. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Compute wheel arc lengths:** $$\Delta s_r = R \cdot \Delta \phi_r = 0.05 \times 2.5 = 0.125\,\mathrm{m}$$ $$\Delta s_l = R \cdot \Delta \phi_l = 0.05 \times 1.5 = 0.075\,\mathrm{m}$$ **Step 2 — Compute average translation $\Delta s$ and rotation $\Delta\theta$:** $$\Delta s = \frac{0.125 + 0.075}{2} = 0.100\,\mathrm{m}$$ $$\Delta \theta = \frac{0.125 - 0.075}{0.40} = 0.125\,\mathrm{rad}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the midpoint heading angle:** $$\theta_{\text{mid}} = \theta_t + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{0.125}{2} = 0.7854 + 0.0625 = 0.8479\,\mathrm{rad}$$ **Step 4 — Update position using midpoint formula:** $$x_{t+1} = 1.0 + 0.100 \cdot \cos(0.8479) = 1.0 + 0.100 \times 0.6626 = 1.0663\,\mathrm{m}$$ $$y_{t+1} = 2.0 + 0.100 \cdot \sin(0.8479) = 2.0 + 0.100 \times 0.7490 = 2.0749\,\mathrm{m}$$ **Step 5 — Update heading:** $$\theta_{t+1} = \frac{\pi}{4} + 0.125 = 0.7854 + 0.125 = 0.9104\,\mathrm{rad} \quad (\approx 52.16^{\circ})$$ **Final predicted pose:** $$\hat{x}_{t+1} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1.0663 \\ 2.0749 \\ 0.9104 \end{bmatrix}$$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Concept of Uncertainty Propagation:** In real robots, the true state is never known exactly. The robot maintains a **belief** about its pose characterized by a mean $\hat{x}_t$ and a covariance matrix $\Sigma_t$. The covariance matrix is symmetric and positive-definite, with diagonal elements representing variance in each state dimension and off-diagonal elements representing cross-correlation. **Role of $G_t$ (State Jacobian):** The state Jacobian $G_t$ captures the *sensitivity* of the predicted state to small changes in the current state. When propagating the covariance forward in time, $G_t$ *transforms* the current uncertainty ellipse into the new state space — rotating and stretching it according to the local motion direction. For the unicycle model: $$G_t \Sigma_t G_t^{\top}$$ This term alone *rotates* the covariance but cannot create new uncertainty. **Role of $V_t$ (Noise Jacobian):** The noise Jacobian $V_t$ maps process noise from the control space into the state space. The term $V_t M_t V_t^{\top}$ is **additive** — it injects new uncertainty into the belief at every time step, regardless of the current covariance. Physically, this represents the *unmodeled disturbances* (wheel slip, sensor quantization, terrain unevenness) that accumulate with every motion command. **Why Uncertainty Grows with Time:** In the absence of measurement corrections (the EKF *update* step), only the *predict* step runs. Since $V_t M_t V_t^{\top} > 0$ at every step, the covariance grows monotonically. Formally, after $N$ steps without correction: $$\Sigma_N \approx \Sigma_0 + N \cdot V M V^{\top} \quad \text{(approximately, for constant } V \text{)}$$ This **open-loop drift** is the fundamental reason why robots need periodic sensor-based corrections (e.g., from GPS, LiDAR, or landmarks) to bound localization error. **Mark split — (a):** *Arc lengths — 1.5 marks; $\Delta s, \Delta\theta$ — 1.5 marks; midpoint angle — 1 mark; position update — 2 marks; heading update — 1 mark.* **Mark split — (b):** *Belief explanation — 1 mark; $G_t$ role — 2 marks; $V_t$ role — 2 marks; growth argument — 2 marks.* --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Robot Position Prediction** - **State vector** is 3-D: $x_t = [x,\ y,\ \theta]^{\top}$ for planar mobile robots. - **Unicycle continuous model** is the foundation: $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$. - **Forward Euler integration** introduces $\Delta t$ explicitly — never omit it. - **Differential-drive odometry** uses $\Delta s = R(\Delta\phi_r + \Delta\phi_l)/2$ and $\Delta\theta = R(\Delta\phi_r - \Delta\phi_l)/L$. - **Midpoint formula** is the geometrically correct odometry update for constant-curvature arcs. - **State Jacobian $G_t$** has $-\!v\Delta t\sin\theta$ and $+\!v\Delta t\cos\theta$ in its top-right 2×1 block. - **Noise Jacobian $V_t$** has $\Delta t \cos\theta$ and $\Delta t \sin\theta$ entries. - **Covariance update** is $\Sigma_{t+1} = G_t \Sigma_t G_t^{\top} + V_t M_t V_t^{\top}$ — both terms are mandatory. - **Process noise** $M_t$ is typically diagonal: $\mathrm{diag}(\sigma_v^2, \sigma_\omega^2)$. - **Uncertainty grows monotonically** in open-loop (predict-only) mode. - **Kalman filter prediction** is the same equation, viewed through the lens of $F_k = G_t$ and $Q_k = V_t M_t V_t^{\top}$. - **Real-world applications:** AGVs, autonomous cars, Mars rovers, warehouse robots, drones. - **Common trap:** mixing degrees and radians in $\theta$, $\omega$, and $\Delta\theta$ — always verify units before any calculation. - **Higher-order integrators** (RK4) improve accuracy for large $\Delta t$ but Euler is sufficient when $\Delta t \le 0.1\,\mathrm{s}$ for typical mobile robot speeds. ---

topics.title: Observation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Observation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the formal framework of **Robot Motion Planning**, an **Observation** is defined as the sensory data $o \in \mathcal{O}$ acquired by a robot at a given time step, which provides *partial, probabilistic information* about the true state of the world $s \in \mathcal{S}$. Observations are mathematically modeled through an **observation model** (also called a sensor model), expressed as a conditional probability distribution:

$$P(o \mid s, a)$$

which denotes the probability of receiving observation $o$ when the robot executes action $a$ and lands in state $s$. In **Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs)**, the observation function is a fundamental component of the tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{O}, T, R, Z, \gamma)$, where $Z(o, s', a) = P(o \mid s', a)$ is the observation function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definition (KTU Board-Ready):** An observation is the stochastic outcome of a robot's sensor measurement, characterized by a probability distribution conditioned on the true world state. The set of all possible observations forms the **observation space** $\mathcal{O}$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Foggy Mountain Hiker"**
>
> Imagine a hiker navigating a foggy mountain at night. The hiker **cannot see the full terrain** (this is the *true state* $s$). Instead, the hiker uses a flashlight that reveals only a small, noisy circular patch of the ground (this is the *observation* $o$). Each sweep of the flashlight gives a *blurry, probabilistic glimpse* of the environment.
>
> - The **true terrain map** = the world state $s$ (hidden).
> - The **flashlight patch** = the observation $o$ (partial, noisy).
> - The **conditional probability** $P(o \mid s)$ = the physics of how the flashlight beam interacts with the actual terrain.
> - The **belief state** = the hiker's internal mental estimate of where they are, continuously updated as new observations arrive.
>
> This is *exactly* how a robot in motion planning perceives the world — through stochastic, partial observations that must be fused over time to maintain a coherent internal representation.

---

## 1.3 The Role of Observations in Motion Planning

Observations are pivotal because they enable three critical capabilities:

1. **State Estimation** — Maintaining a belief $b(s)$ about the true state when direct state access is impossible.
2. **Information-Gathering Planning** — Selecting actions that *maximize information gain* (e.g., exploration, active perception).
3. **Localization & Mapping** — Simultaneously estimating the robot's pose and the environment structure (SLAM).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Highlight):**
> - **Sensor noise** is typically modeled as **Gaussian** with standard deviation $\sigma$ (e.g., range finder $\sigma \approx 0.01$–$0.05$ m).
> - **Information gain** is measured in **bits** (using Shannon entropy $H$) or **nats** (using natural log).
> - **Observation frequency** is measured in **Hertz (Hz)**.

---

## 1.4 Observation Space — Formal Structure

The observation space $\mathcal{O}$ can take several forms:

| Observation Type | Space Structure | Example Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Discrete (Categorical) | Finite set $\mathcal{O} = \{o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_n\}$ | Door detector (open/closed/unknown) |
| Continuous Vector | $\mathcal{O} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ | LiDAR range scan, Camera image |
| Hybrid | Mixed discrete/continuous | Object detection (class + bounding box) |
| Topological | Graph node / landmark ID | WiFi fingerprint, RFID tag |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** Examiners frequently test the distinction between **fully observable** MDPs (where $o = s$ deterministically) and **partially observable** POMDPs (where $o$ is a stochastic function of $s$). Always clarify which framework a given observation model belongs to.

---

## 1.5 Categories of Observation in Motion Planning

Observations in robot motion planning are classified along several axes:

- **Active vs. Passive:** Active observations are taken explicitly to gather information; passive observations are a byproduct of normal operation.
- **Exteroceptive vs. Proprioceptive:** Exteroceptive sensors perceive the *external world* (cameras, LiDAR); proprioceptive sensors measure *internal robot state* (encoders, IMU).
- **Range vs. Bearing:** Range sensors measure distance; bearing sensors measure angular direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 2):** The observation topic bridges the gap between **deterministic motion planning** (where the state is fully known) and **stochastic planning under uncertainty** (where the state must be inferred from observations). This transition is a core learning outcome of Module 2.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Observation Model — Mathematical Foundation

An **observation model** specifies how sensor readings are generated from the true world state. Formally, for a robot in state $s'$ after executing action $a$, the observation is drawn from:

$$o \sim Z(\cdot \mid s', a) = P(o \mid s', a)$$

This must be a valid probability distribution:

$$\sum_{o \in \mathcal{O}} P(o \mid s', a) = 1 \quad \text{(discrete case)}$$

$$\int_{\mathcal{O}} P(o \mid s', a) \, do = 1 \quad \text{(continuous case)}$$

---

## 2.2 Structured Breakdown of the Observation Process

The complete observation pipeline in motion planning involves the following logical stages:

1. **State Realization** — The world assumes true state $s' \in \mathcal{S}$ (after the transition $T(s' \mid s, a)$).
2. **Sensor Interaction** — The robot's sensor physically interacts with the environment at state $s'$.
3. **Observation Generation** — A sample $o$ is drawn from $Z(\cdot \mid s', a)$ according to the sensor's noise characteristics.
4. **Belief Update** — The robot's internal belief distribution $b(s)$ is updated via **Bayes' rule** to incorporate the new observation.
5. **Action Selection** — The next action is chosen based on the updated belief, often optimizing a value function $V(b)$ or information-theoretic objective.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Stage:**
> - *Why probabilistic?* Real sensors are noisy; deterministic models are unrealistic.
> - *Why Bayesian update?* It is the mathematically optimal way to fuse prior belief with new evidence.
> - *Why belief-based?* In POMDPs, the optimal policy depends on the entire belief, not just the most recent observation.

---

## 2.3 The Belief State and Bayesian Update

Since the true state is hidden, the robot maintains a **belief state** $b(s) = P(s \mid o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_t, a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_t)$, a probability distribution over all possible states. When a new observation $o_{t+1}$ arrives after action $a_{t+1}$, the belief is updated:

$$b'(s') = \eta \cdot Z(o_{t+1} \mid s', a_{t+1}) \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} T(s' \mid s, a_{t+1}) \, b(s)$$

where $\eta$ is a normalization constant:

$$\eta = \left[ \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} Z(o_{t+1} \mid s', a_{t+1}) \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} T(s' \mid s, a_{t+1}) \, b(s) \right]^{-1}$$

This two-step update — **predict** (via $T$) then **correct** (via $Z$) — is the **Bayes filter** recursion, the cornerstone of probabilistic robotics.

---

## 2.4 Common Observation Models in Practice

### 2.4.1 Gaussian Range Sensor Model

For a range finder measuring distance $z$ to a landmark at true distance $d$:

$$P(z \mid d) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(z - d)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

### 2.4.2 Beam-Based Sensor Model (More Realistic)

$$P(z \mid d) = \begin{cases} \eta_{\text{beam}} \cdot p_{\text{hit}}(z \mid d) & \text{if } 0 \leq z \leq z_{\max} \\ p_{\max} & \text{if } z = z_{\max} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $p_{\text{hit}}(z \mid d)$ is a Gaussian centered at $d$, and $p_{\max}$ accounts for maximum-range returns.

### 2.4.3 Landmark Observation Model

For a robot at pose $(x, y, \theta)$ observing a landmark at known position $(l_x, l_y)$:

$$z = \begin{bmatrix} r \\ \phi \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(l_x - x)^2 + (l_y - y)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(l_y - y,\, l_x - x) - \theta \end{bmatrix} + \epsilon$$

where $r$ is range, $\phi$ is bearing, and $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$ is Gaussian noise with covariance $R$.

---

## 2.5 Information-Theoretic Measures of Observation Quality

To quantify the value of an observation, information theory provides key metrics:

| Measure | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Shannon Entropy | $H(b) = -\sum_s b(s) \log b(s)$ | Uncertainty in the belief |
| Conditional Entropy | $H(\mathcal{S} \mid \mathcal{O}) = -\sum_{s,o} P(s,o) \log P(s \mid o)$ | Residual uncertainty after observation |
| Mutual Information | $I(\mathcal{S};\mathcal{O}) = H(\mathcal{S}) - H(\mathcal{S} \mid \mathcal{O})$ | Information gained about state from observation |
| KL Divergence | $D_{\text{KL}}(b' \Vert b) = \sum_s b'(s) \log \frac{b'(s)}{b(s)}$ | Belief change magnitude |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked about "information gain," always reference the reduction in entropy $\Delta H = H(b) - H(b')$.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2 (Observation)**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Observation likelihood | $P(o \mid s, a)$ | Dimensionless probability |
| 2 | Bayes filter predict | $\bar{b}(s') = \sum_s T(s' \mid s, a) b(s)$ | Belief (dimensionless) |
| 3 | Bayes filter update | $b'(s') = \eta \, Z(o \mid s', a) \bar{b}(s')$ | Belief (dimensionless) |
| 4 | Normalizer | $\eta = P(o \mid b, a)^{-1}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Gaussian observation | $P(z \mid d) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(z-d)^2/(2\sigma^2)}$ | $\sigma$ in meters |
| 6 | Landmark range | $r = \sqrt{(l_x - x)^2 + (l_y - y)^2}$ | meters |
| 7 | Landmark bearing | $\phi = \text{atan2}(l_y - y,\ l_x - x) - \theta$ | radians |
| 8 | Shannon entropy | $H(b) = -\sum_s b(s) \log_2 b(s)$ | bits |
| 9 | Mutual information | $I(\mathcal{S};\mathcal{O}) = H(\mathcal{S}) - H(\mathcal{S} \mid \mathcal{O})$ | bits |
| 10 | Observation space size | $\vert \mathcal{O} \vert$ (discrete) or $\dim(\mathcal{O})$ (continuous) | count / dimension |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The observation model is the **bedrock of every deployed robotic system**:

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla):** Camera and LiDAR observations are fused via Bayesian filters to estimate the positions of pedestrians, vehicles, and lane markings.
- **Warehouse Robots (Amazon Kiva):** QR-code observations on the floor provide absolute localization with centimeter accuracy.
- **Mars Rovers (Perseverance, Curiosity):** Stereo camera observations are matched against orbital maps for visual terrain relative navigation.
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci):** Force/torque observations enable haptic feedback for tissue interaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A poorly calibrated observation model (underestimated noise $\sigma$) leads to **overconfident localization** — a dangerous failure mode where the robot "believes" it knows where it is but is actually severely misplaced. KTU examiners test awareness of this pitfall.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Bayes Filter Observation Update (Exhaustive)

We derive the belief update equation from first principles using **Bayes' theorem** and the **law of total probability**.

**Given:**
- Prior belief $b(s) = P(s \mid \text{history})$
- Action executed: $a_{t+1}$
- New observation: $o_{t+1}$
- Transition model $T(s' \mid s, a)$
- Observation model $Z(o \mid s', a)$

**Goal:** Derive $b'(s') = P(s' \mid o_{t+1}, a_{t+1}, b)$.

---

**Step 1: State the goal using conditional probability.**

By definition of the belief state:

$$b'(s') = P(s' \mid o_{t+1}, a_{t+1}, b)$$

**Step 2: Apply Bayes' theorem.**

Bayes' theorem inverts the conditioning:

$$P(s' \mid o, a, b) = \frac{P(o \mid s', a, b) \cdot P(s' \mid a, b)}{P(o \mid a, b)}$$

**Step 3: Simplify the observation likelihood using the Markov property.**

The Markov property states that the observation $o$ depends only on the current state $s'$ and action $a$, not on the full history:

$$P(o \mid s', a, b) = P(o \mid s', a) = Z(o \mid s', a)$$

**Step 4: Expand the prior $P(s' \mid a, b)$ using total probability over the previous state $s$.**

$$P(s' \mid a, b) = \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a, b) \cdot P(s \mid a, b)$$

**Step 5: Apply the Markov property to the transition term.**

Since $s'$ depends on the previous state $s$ and action $a$ only:

$$P(s' \mid s, a, b) = T(s' \mid s, a)$$

**Step 6: Recognize the second factor as the prior belief.**

$$P(s \mid a, b) = b(s)$$

since $b(s)$ already encodes all information in the history.

**Step 7: Combine Steps 4–6 to form the prediction step.**

$$P(s' \mid a, b) = \sum_{s} T(s' \mid s, a) \, b(s) \equiv \bar{b}(s')$$

**Step 8: Substitute back into Step 2.**

$$b'(s') = \frac{Z(o \mid s', a) \cdot \bar{b}(s')}{P(o \mid a, b)}$$

**Step 9: Identify the denominator as the normalizer $\eta^{-1}$.**

$$P(o \mid a, b) = \sum_{s'} P(o \mid s', a) P(s' \mid a, b) = \sum_{s'} Z(o \mid s', a) \, \bar{b}(s')$$

**Step 10: Final form of the Bayes filter update.**

$$\boxed{b'(s') = \eta \cdot Z(o_{t+1} \mid s', a_{t+1}) \cdot \bar{b}(s')}$$

where:

$$\eta = \frac{1}{\sum_{s'} Z(o_{t+1} \mid s', a_{t+1}) \cdot \bar{b}(s')}$$

This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mutual Information for Observation Selection

We derive how to compute the **expected information gain** of an observation, a key quantity in **active perception** and **informative path planning**.

**Given:**
- Current belief $b(s)$
- Candidate observation $o$
- Observation model $Z(o \mid s)$

**Goal:** Derive $I(\mathcal{S};\mathcal{O})$, the mutual information between the random state $S$ and observation $O$.

---

**Step 1: Start with the definition of mutual information.**

$$I(\mathcal{S};\mathcal{O}) = H(\mathcal{S}) - H(\mathcal{S} \mid \mathcal{O})$$

**Step 2: Expand the entropy of the state.**

The state distribution is given by the belief:

$$H(\mathcal{S}) = -\sum_{s} b(s) \log b(s)$$

**Step 3: Expand the conditional entropy $H(\mathcal{S} \mid \mathcal{O})$.**

By definition:

$$H(\mathcal{S} \mid \mathcal{O}) = -\sum_{o} P(o) \sum_{s} P(s \mid o) \log P(s \mid o)$$

**Step 4: Compute the joint probability $P(s, o)$.**

$$P(s, o) = P(s) \cdot P(o \mid s) = b(s) \cdot Z(o \mid s)$$

**Step 5: Compute the marginal $P(o)$.**

$$P(o) = \sum_{s} P(s, o) = \sum_{s} b(s) \cdot Z(o \mid s)$$

**Step 6: Compute the posterior $P(s \mid o)$ using Bayes' theorem.**

$$P(s \mid o) = \frac{P(s, o)}{P(o)} = \frac{b(s) \cdot Z(o \mid s)}{\sum_{s'} b(s') Z(o \mid s')}$$

**Step 7: Substitute Steps 4–6 back into Step 3.**

$$H(\mathcal{S} \mid \mathcal{O}) = -\sum_{o} \left( \sum_{s} b(s) Z(o \mid s) \right) \sum_{s'} \frac{b(s') Z(o \mid s')}{\sum_{s''} b(s'') Z(o \mid s'')} \log \left( \frac{b(s') Z(o \mid s')}{\sum_{s''} b(s'') Z(o \mid s'')} \right)$$

**Step 8: Subtract from $H(\mathcal{S})$ to obtain the closed-form mutual information.**

$$\boxed{I(\mathcal{S};\mathcal{O}) = \sum_{o} \sum_{s} b(s) Z(o \mid s) \log \frac{Z(o \mid s)}{\sum_{s'} b(s') Z(o \mid s')}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This quantity answers the question: *"On average, how much does observing $o$ reduce my uncertainty about the state $s$?"* The robot should choose actions that **maximize** $I(\mathcal{S};\mathcal{O})$ when its objective is pure information gathering. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Bayes Filter Update (KTU Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A robot operates in a 1D world with two possible states: $\mathcal{S} = \{s_1, s_2\}$. The current belief is $b(s_1) = 0.7$, $b(s_2) = 0.3$. The robot executes action $a$ (no state change) and receives observation $o_1$. The observation model is:
>
> $Z(o_1 \mid s_1) = 0.8$, $\quad Z(o_1 \mid s_2) = 0.3$
>
> **Compute the updated belief $b'(s)$.**

---

**Step 1: Identify the predict step.**

Since action $a$ does not change the state, $T(s' \mid s, a) = \mathbb{1}[s' = s]$, so:

$$\bar{b}(s_1) = b(s_1) = 0.7, \quad \bar{b}(s_2) = b(s_2) = 0.3$$

**Step 2: Apply the unnormalized update.**

$$b'_{\text{unnorm}}(s_1) = Z(o_1 \mid s_1) \cdot \bar{b}(s_1) = 0.8 \times 0.7 = 0.56$$

$$b'_{\text{unnorm}}(s_2) = Z(o_1 \mid s_2) \cdot \bar{b}(s_2) = 0.3 \times 0.3 = 0.09$$

**Step 3: Compute the normalizer.**

$$\eta = \frac{1}{0.56 + 0.09} = \frac{1}{0.65} \approx 1.5385$$

**Step 4: Normalize.**

$$b'(s_1) = 0.56 \times 1.5385 \approx 0.8615$$

$$b'(s_2) = 0.09 \times 1.5385 \approx 0.1385$$

**Step 5: Verify normalization.**

$$b'(s_1) + b'(s_2) = 0.8615 + 0.1385 = 1.0000 \checkmark$$

**Step 6: Interpret the result.**

The probability of being in $s_1$ has increased from **0.7** to **0.8615** — a **23% relative increase** in confidence — because the observation $o_1$ is more likely under $s_1$ (0.8) than under $s_2$ (0.3). $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Deterministic Observation (MDP) | Stochastic Observation (POMDP) |
|---|---|---|
| Mapping | $o = f(s)$ exactly | $o \sim P(o \mid s)$ |
| Information | Complete (no uncertainty) | Partial (noisy/aliased) |
| State access | Full observability | Partial observability |
| Policy type | $\pi : \mathcal{S} \to \mathcal{A}$ | $\pi : \mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{A}$ |
| Computational cost | Polynomial | PSPACE-complete (intractable) |
| Planning algorithm | Value iteration, A* | POMDP solvers (SARSOP, DESPOT) |
| Example | Chess (full info) | Robot localization (noisy GPS) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Pattern:** Direct comparison questions between MDP and POMDP observation models appear frequently in Part A. Memorize the key differences in this table.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Observation Processing Pipeline in Robot Motion Planning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[World State s] --> B[Sensor Interaction]
    B --> C[Raw Sensor Signal]
    C --> D[Noise Injection]
    D --> E[Observation o]
    E --> F[Bayesian Update]
    G[Prior Belief b] --> F
    F --> H[Posterior Belief b prime]
    H --> I[Action Selection Policy]
    I --> J[Action a]
    J --> K[State Transition T]
    K --> A
    H --> L[Information Gain Metric]
    L --> M[Logging and Analysis]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The cycle begins with the world state $s$ (hidden from the robot). The sensor interacts with the world, producing a noisy signal that becomes observation $o$. This observation, combined with the prior belief $b$, is fed into the Bayesian update block, producing a refined posterior belief $b'$. The policy then selects action $a$, which causes a state transition via $T$, and the cycle repeats. The information gain metric is logged for analysis.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Hierarchical Decomposition of the Observation Function $Z(o \mid s, a)$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z[Observation Function Z] --> Z1[Sensor Type]
    Z --> Z2[Noise Model]
    Z --> Z3[Occlusion Model]
    Z --> Z4[Field of View]
    Z1 --> Z1A[Camera]
    Z1 --> Z1B[LiDAR]
    Z1 --> Z1C[Sonar]
    Z1 --> Z1D[IMU]
    Z2 --> Z2A[Gaussian]
    Z2 --> Z2B[Uniform Mixture]
    Z2 --> Z2C[Non-parametric]
    Z3 --> Z3A[Visible]
    Z3 --> Z3B[Occluded]
    Z4 --> Z4A[Within FOV]
    Z4 --> Z4B[Outside FOV]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Belief Update State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PriorBelief
    PriorBelief --> Prediction: Execute Action a
    Prediction --> PredictedBelief: bar b
    PredictedBelief --> Correction: Receive Observation o
    Correction --> PosteriorBelief: b prime
    PosteriorBelief --> PolicyEvaluation: Compute V b
    PolicyEvaluation --> ActionSelection: argmax V
    ActionSelection --> Prediction: Next timestep
    PosteriorBelief --> [*]: Mission Complete
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Information-Gathering Planning Architecture (Active Perception)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Planning Module] --> Q1{Is Goal Reached?}
    Q1 -- No --> R[Compute Expected Information Gain]
    R --> S[Evaluate Candidate Actions]
    S --> T[Select Max Info Action]
    T --> U[Execute Action]
    U --> V[Acquire Observation]
    V --> W[Update Belief]
    W --> P
    Q1 -- Yes --> X[Mission Complete]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation:** This diagram represents the **active perception** loop, a special case of motion planning where the robot's objective is to *reduce uncertainty* rather than reach a physical goal. The information-gain computation (Step R) typically uses the mutual information formula derived in Section 3.2.

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Observability Spectrum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Full Observability] --> B[Partial Observability]
    B --> C[Sparse Observability]
    C --> D[No Observability]
    A --> A1[o equals s exactly]
    B --> B1[o is noisy subset of s]
    C --> C1[o available only at landmarks]
    D --> D1[Robot is blind]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** Most real robots operate in the **partial observability** regime. Full observability is the idealized MDP case; no observability means planning must be purely open-loop (no feedback), which is rare in practice.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Observation" in the context of robot motion planning. Differentiate between exteroceptive and proprioceptive observations with one example each.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

In robot motion planning, an **observation** $o \in \mathcal{O}$ is the sensory data acquired by the robot at a given time, providing partial, probabilistic information about the true world state $s \in \mathcal{S}$. It is formally modeled by the observation function $Z(o \mid s, a) = P(o \mid s, a)$, which gives the probability of receiving observation $o$ given the robot is in state $s$ after executing action $a$.

**Differentiation:**

| Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Exteroceptive** | Sensors that perceive the *external environment* | Camera, LiDAR, ultrasonic rangefinder |
| **Proprioceptive** | Sensors that measure the robot's *internal state* | Wheel encoders, IMU, joint position sensors |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Note (3-mark scheme):** Definition (1.5 marks) + clean differentiation table (1.5 marks).

---

### Question 2: What is a belief state? Why is it necessary in Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs)?

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **belief state** $b(s)$ is a probability distribution over all possible world states, representing the robot's internal estimate of where it might be, given its entire observation and action history:

$$b(s) = P(s \mid o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_t, a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_t)$$

**Necessity in POMDPs:**

In a POMDP, the true state $s$ is **not directly observable** — the robot only receives noisy observations $o$. Since the optimal action depends on what the robot *believes* about its state (not the true state, which is unknown), the belief state serves as a **sufficient statistic** for decision-making. The Markov property holds in the belief space: the next belief depends only on the current belief and the most recent action-observation pair, not on the full history.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Note (3-mark scheme):** Formal definition (1 mark) + POMDP justification (2 marks).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Derive the Bayes filter observation update equation starting from first principles. Clearly state the Markov property assumption used in your derivation.**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Prior belief $b(s)$
- Action $a_{t+1}$
- New observation $o_{t+1}$
- Transition model $T(s' \mid s, a)$
- Observation model $Z(o \mid s', a)$

**Step 1:** State the objective:

$$b'(s') = P(s' \mid o_{t+1}, a_{t+1}, b)$$

**Step 2:** Apply Bayes' theorem:

$$b'(s') = \frac{P(o_{t+1} \mid s', a_{t+1}, b) \cdot P(s' \mid a_{t+1}, b)}{P(o_{t+1} \mid a_{t+1}, b)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **Markov property** to simplify the observation likelihood:

$$P(o_{t+1} \mid s', a_{t+1}, b) = Z(o_{t+1} \mid s', a_{t+1})$$

**Step 4:** Expand the prior using total probability:

$$P(s' \mid a_{t+1}, b) = \sum_{s} T(s' \mid s, a_{t+1}) \, b(s) = \bar{b}(s')$$

**Step 5:** Compute the normalizer:

$$\eta^{-1} = P(o_{t+1} \mid a_{t+1}, b) = \sum_{s'} Z(o_{t+1} \mid s', a_{t+1}) \, \bar{b}(s')$$

**Step 6:** Combine to obtain the final form:

$$b'(s') = \eta \cdot Z(o_{t+1} \mid s', a_{t+1}) \cdot \bar{b}(s')$$

This two-step process — **predict** via $T$ then **correct** via $Z$ — is the **Bayes filter recursion**. The Markov property (used in Step 3) is the critical assumption that makes the recursion tractable by ensuring the current belief is a sufficient summary of history. $\blacksquare$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Consider a robot in a 1D corridor with three possible states: $\mathcal{S} = \{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$. The prior belief is $b = (0.5, 0.3, 0.2)$. The robot executes action $a$ with transition model $T$ given by the matrix (rows = current state, columns = next state):**

$$T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0.2 & 0.0 \\ 0.1 & 0.7 & 0.2 \\ 0.0 & 0.3 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix}$$

**The observation model is $Z(o_1 \mid s) = (0.7, 0.5, 0.2)$ for $s = s_1, s_2, s_3$ respectively. Compute the posterior belief after observing $o_1$.**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the predicted belief $\bar{b}(s') = \sum_s T(s' \mid s, a) \, b(s)$.**

For $s' = s_1$:

$$\bar{b}(s_1) = (0.8)(0.5) + (0.1)(0.3) + (0.0)(0.2) = 0.40 + 0.03 + 0.00 = 0.43$$

For $s' = s_2$:

$$\bar{b}(s_2) = (0.2)(0.5) + (0.7)(0.3) + (0.3)(0.2) = 0.10 + 0.21 + 0.06 = 0.37$$

For $s' = s_3$:

$$\bar{b}(s_3) = (0.0)(0.5) + (0.2)(0.3) + (0.7)(0.2) = 0.00 + 0.06 + 0.14 = 0.20$$

**Step 2: Apply the observation correction (unnormalized).**

$$b'_{\text{un}}(s_1) = Z(o_1 \mid s_1) \cdot \bar{b}(s_1) = 0.7 \times 0.43 = 0.301$$

$$b'_{\text{un}}(s_2) = Z(o_1 \mid s_2) \cdot \bar{b}(s_2) = 0.5 \times 0.37 = 0.185$$

$$b'_{\text{un}}(s_3) = Z(o_1 \mid s_3) \cdot \bar{b}(s_3) = 0.2 \times 0.20 = 0.040$$

**Step 3: Compute the normalizer.**

$$\eta = \frac{1}{0.301 + 0.185 + 0.040} = \frac{1}{0.526} \approx 1.9011$$

**Step 4: Normalize to obtain the posterior belief.**

$$b'(s_1) = 0.301 \times 1.9011 \approx 0.572$$

$$b'(s_2) = 0.185 \times 1.9011 \approx 0.352$$

$$b'(s_3) = 0.040 \times 1.9011 \approx 0.076$$

**Step 5: Verify.**

$$0.572 + 0.352 + 0.076 = 1.000 \checkmark$$

The posterior belief $(0.572, 0.352, 0.076)$ shows that the observation $o_1$ significantly increased confidence in $s_1$ (from 0.5 to 0.572) and decreased confidence in $s_3$ (from 0.2 to 0.076), consistent with $o_1$ being most likely under $s_1$. $\blacksquare$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain the concept of the observation model in robot motion planning. Discuss the Gaussian observation model for a range sensor, deriving the likelihood function and explaining its parameters.**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Conceptual Foundation:**

The **observation model** $Z(o \mid s, a) = P(o \mid s, a)$ describes the probability of receiving a particular sensor reading $o$ given that the robot is in state $s$ and has executed action $a$. It encapsulates the physics of the sensor, including noise, occlusions, and field-of-view limitations. The model is a critical input to the Bayes filter and to any POMDP-based planner.

**Gaussian Range Sensor Model Derivation:**

For a range sensor (e.g., LiDAR, sonar) measuring the distance $z$ to an obstacle at true distance $d$, the sensor reading is corrupted by additive Gaussian noise $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$:

$$z = d + \epsilon$$

The likelihood function is therefore:

$$P(z \mid d) = P(\epsilon = z - d) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(z - d)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Parameter Interpretation:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| True distance | $d$ | Ground-truth range to obstacle | 0.1 m to 30 m |
| Measured distance | $z$ | Noisy sensor reading | Same range as $d$ |
| Standard deviation | $\sigma$ | Sensor noise magnitude | 0.01–0.05 m (LiDAR) |
| Mean | $\mu = d$ | Unbiased measurement center | Same as $d$ |

**Key Properties:**

1. **Symmetry:** $P(z \mid d) = P(d \mid z)$ only if $\sigma$ is identical in both directions.
2. **Maximum likelihood:** The most likely measurement is $z = d$.
3. **Tail behavior:** The probability decays exponentially with the squared error $(z - d)^2$.

**Engineering Implication:** In practice, the Gaussian model is augmented with a uniform mixture component and a "max-range" return to handle unexpected readings (e.g., specular reflections, transparent surfaces). The pure Gaussian model is an *idealization* that works well for high-quality sensors in controlled environments. $\blacksquare$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**What is mutual information in the context of observation-based planning? Derive the expression for expected information gain and explain its role in active perception.**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**

**Mutual information** $I(\mathcal{S};\mathcal{O})$ quantifies the amount of information that an observation $O$ provides about the hidden state $S$:

$$I(\mathcal{S};\mathcal{O}) = H(\mathcal{S}) - H(\mathcal{S} \mid \mathcal{O})$$

where $H(\mathcal{S})$ is the Shannon entropy of the state distribution (prior uncertainty) and $H(\mathcal{S} \mid \mathcal{O})$ is the conditional entropy (residual uncertainty after observation).

**Derivation of Expected Information Gain:**

Starting from the definition and expanding the entropies:

$$H(\mathcal{S}) = -\sum_{s} P(s) \log P(s)$$

$$H(\mathcal{S} \mid \mathcal{O}) = -\sum_{o} P(o) \sum_{s} P(s \mid o) \log P(s \mid o)$$

Substituting $P(s) = b(s)$ (the belief) and $P(o \mid s) = Z(o \mid s)$ (the observation model):

$$I(\mathcal{S};\mathcal{O}) = \sum_{o} \sum_{s} b(s) Z(o \mid s) \log \frac{Z(o \mid s)}{\sum_{s'} b(s') Z(o \mid s')}$$

**Role in Active Perception:**

In **active perception**, the robot's objective is to *reduce uncertainty* about the world, rather than to reach a physical goal. The expected information gain $I(\mathcal{S};\mathcal{O})$ serves as the **reward signal** in this setting:

$$\pi^* = \arg\max_{a} \, \mathbb{E}_{o}\bigl[I(\mathcal{S};\mathcal{O} \mid b, a)\bigr]$$

The robot selects the action $a$ that maximizes the expected reduction in entropy of its belief. This is the foundation of:

- **Informative path planning** (choosing trajectories that maximize information)
- **Active SLAM** (deciding where to look next to improve the map)
- **Exploration vs. exploitation trade-off** in reinforcement learning

**Practical Example:** A search-and-rescue drone in a collapsed building uses active perception: it plans flight paths that maximize the expected information about survivor locations, even if those paths are not the shortest route to a goal. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE — Observation Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Normalizer $\eta$** — Students frequently write $b'(s') = Z(o \mid s', a) \bar{b}(s')$ without the normalizer $\eta$. This produces an *invalid probability distribution* (does not sum to 1). **Penalty: −1 to −2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Confusing Prior and Posterior** — Mixing up $b(s)$ (prior) and $b'(s')$ (posterior) in the update equation is a classic error. The prior comes from the *predict step*; the posterior comes from the *correct step*.
>
> 3. **Not Stating the Markov Property** — Any derivation involving the Bayes filter *must* explicitly invoke the Markov property. Examiners look for this phrase. **Penalty: −1 mark if omitted.**
>
> 4. **Wrong Units in Entropy** — Using $H$ in nats when the question asks for bits (or vice versa) costs partial credit. Always state the log base: $\log_2$ for bits, $\ln$ for nats.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the Normalization Constraint in Numerical Problems** — In numerical Bayes filter problems, students often skip the verification step $\sum b'(s') = 1$. Examiners reward this check.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Observation in Robot Motion Planning (Module 2):**
>
> - **Observation** = stochastic sensor reading $o \in \mathcal{O}$, modeled as $P(o \mid s, a)$.
> - **Observation space** $\mathcal{O}$ can be **discrete**, **continuous**, **hybrid**, or **topological**.
> - **MDPs** have deterministic full observations ($o = s$); **POMDPs** have stochastic partial observations.
> - **Belief state** $b(s)$ is the robot's internal probability distribution over states — a **sufficient statistic** for POMDP decision-making.
> - **Bayes filter** = **predict** ($\bar{b}(s') = \sum_s T b(s)$) then **correct** ($b'(s') = \eta Z(o \mid s', a) \bar{b}(s')$).
> - **Markov property** is the critical assumption that makes the Bayes filter tractable.
> - **Gaussian observation model**: $P(z \mid d) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(z-d)^2/(2\sigma^2)}$.
> - **Landmark observation**: range $r = \sqrt{(l_x - x)^2 + (l_y - y)^2}$, bearing $\phi = \text{atan2}(l_y - y, l_x - x) - \theta$.
> - **Entropy** $H(b) = -\sum_s b(s) \log_2 b(s)$ measures belief uncertainty in bits.
> - **Mutual information** $I(\mathcal{S};\mathcal{O}) = H(\mathcal{S}) - H(\mathcal{S} \mid \mathcal{O})$ measures the value of an observation.
> - **Active perception** uses expected information gain as the reward signal for planning.
> - **Normalizer** $\eta$ ensures the posterior belief is a valid probability distribution.
> - **Overconfident localization** is a critical failure mode caused by underestimated sensor noise $\sigma$.
> - **Exteroceptive** sensors perceive the external world; **proprioceptive** sensors measure internal robot state.
> - The Bayes filter derivation must explicitly state the **Markov property** for full marks.
> - Numerical problems must end with a **normalization verification** step ($b'$ sums to 1).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement Prediction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement Prediction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Robot Motion Planning** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, **Measurement Prediction** is formally defined as the computational process of forecasting the expected sensor reading (measurement) that a robot should observe, given its current belief about its state and its sensor model. Mathematically, it is the deterministic component of the measurement function evaluated at the predicted state estimate.

For a robot operating in discrete time steps, if $x_{k+1 \vert k}$ is the predicted state at time $k+1$ given all measurements up to time $k$, then the corresponding **measurement prediction** is:

$$\hat{z}_{k+1 \vert k} = h(\hat{x}_{k+1 \vert k})$$

where $h(\cdot)$ is the **measurement model function** (also called the observation model).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Lock:** Measurement prediction is a *prior estimate* of what sensors *should* read, computed *before* the actual sensor measurement arrives. It is the cornerstone of every Bayesian filter, Kalman Filter, Extended Kalman Filter (EKF), and Particle Filter used in robot localization and SLAM.

The associated **innovation** (or measurement residual) is then:

$$\nu_{k+1} = z_{k+1} - \hat{z}_{k+1 \vert k}$$

This residual quantifies the discrepancy between the predicted and actual measurement, which is the driver of every correction/ update step in recursive state estimation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Blindfolded Archer" Analogy**
> 
> Imagine you are an archer aiming at a target *while blindfolded*. You cannot see where the arrow lands, but you have a mental model of how the bow behaves, and someone shouts back the *approximate* distance of the arrow's landing. 
> 
> In robotics terms:
> - **Your brain's guess of arrow trajectory** = *State Prediction* ($\hat{x}_{k+1 \vert k}$)
> - **The "approximate distance shouted back"** = *Actual Sensor Measurement* ($z_{k+1}$)
> - **Your brain's forecast of what distance should be shouted** = *Measurement Prediction* ($\hat{z}_{k+1 \vert k}$)
> - **The difference between forecast and actual shout** = *Innovation* ($\nu_{k+1}$)
> 
> The brain (filter) then corrects the aim (state estimate) by trusting the shouted feedback (measurement) in proportion to how reliable the brain and the shouter are (covariances).

A simpler real-world analogy: **Weather Forecasting**. A meteorologist *predicts* tomorrow's temperature (measurement prediction). When the actual thermometer reading arrives, the gap between predicted and actual is the "innovation," which the forecaster uses to *update* their climate model.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical and Statistical Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\hat{z}_{k+1 \vert k}$ | Predicted measurement | Same as $z$ (m, rad, etc.) |
| $h(\cdot)$ | Measurement model | Non-linear vector function |
| $H_k$ | Jacobian of $h$ wrt $x$ | Dimensionless (or scaling matrix) |
| $S_{k+1}$ | Innovation covariance | Units of $z^2$ |
| $R_{k+1}$ | Measurement noise covariance | Units of $z^2$ |
| $P_{k+1 \vert k}$ | Predicted state covariance | Units of $x^2$ |
| $Q_k$ | Process noise covariance | Units of $x^2$ |

> [!TIP]
> Always keep track of **units**. KTU examiners frequently deduct marks when students confuse the **state covariance** $P$ with the **innovation covariance** $S$ or the **measurement noise** $R$.

---

## 1.4 The Bayesian Foundation

Measurement prediction is a *mandatory sub-step* of the **Bayesian recursive filter**, which alternates between two stages:

1. **Prediction (Time Update):** Propagate the state forward using the motion model.
2. **Correction (Measurement Update):** Compare the actual measurement with the predicted measurement and correct the state.

Measurement prediction lies at the **bridge** between these two stages — it is the prediction of *what the sensor will see* based on the *state prediction*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Stage Recursive Filter (Logical Breakdown)

### Stage 1: State Prediction (Motion Update)
Before we can predict a measurement, we must first predict the state. The robot's motion model is:

$$\hat{x}_{k+1 \vert k} = f(\hat{x}_{k \vert k}, u_k)$$

where:
- $\hat{x}_{k \vert k}$ is the posterior state estimate at time $k$
- $u_k$ is the control input (odometry, velocity command)
- $f(\cdot)$ is the non-linear motion model
- The associated uncertainty propagation is:

$$P_{k+1 \vert k} = A_k P_{k \vert k} A_k^T + Q_k$$

where $A_k = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\Big\vert_{\hat{x}_{k \vert k}}$ is the **Jacobian** of the motion model, and $Q_k$ is the **process noise covariance**.

### Stage 2: Measurement Prediction (Observation Forecast)
Once the state is predicted, the *expected* sensor reading is computed via the measurement model:

$$\hat{z}_{k+1 \vert k} = h(\hat{x}_{k+1 \vert k})$$

and the **innovation covariance** (uncertainty in the predicted measurement) is:

$$S_{k+1} = H_{k+1} \, P_{k+1 \vert k} \, H_{k+1}^T + R_{k+1}$$

where $H_{k+1} = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\Big\vert_{\hat{x}_{k+1 \vert k}}$ is the **Jacobian of the measurement model**, and $R_{k+1}$ is the **measurement noise covariance**.

### Stage 3: Innovation Computation
The actual measurement is compared with the prediction:

$$\nu_{k+1} = z_{k+1} - \hat{z}_{k+1 \vert k}$$

### Stage 4: Kalman Gain
The **Kalman Gain** weights the innovation against the predicted uncertainty:

$$K_{k+1} = P_{k+1 \vert k} \, H_{k+1}^T \, S_{k+1}^{-1}$$

### Stage 5: State Update
$$\hat{x}_{k+1 \vert k+1} = \hat{x}_{k+1 \vert k} + K_{k+1} \, \nu_{k+1}$$

### Stage 6: Covariance Update
$$P_{k+1 \vert k+1} = (I - K_{k+1} H_{k+1}) P_{k+1 \vert k}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** Measurement Prediction is **not** the entire Kalman filter. It is specifically the forecast of $z$ (Stages 2 and partially Stage 3) given the predicted state. The full cycle requires *all six* stages.

---

## 2.2 Why Measurement Prediction Matters — The "Why" and "How"

### The "Why":
- **Anomaly Detection:** If the innovation $\nu_k$ is too large (Mahalanobis distance $\nu^T S^{-1} \nu$ exceeds a threshold), the measurement is flagged as an outlier (e.g., a spurious GPS reading).
- **Data Association:** In SLAM, predicted measurements of landmarks are used to match the correct landmark in the sensor's field of view.
- **Sensor Fusion:** Multiple sensors are weighted optimally by comparing each sensor's measurement with its own prediction.

### The "How":
- **Linear systems** (e.g., constant-velocity robot with linear range sensors): The Jacobians $A_k$ and $H_k$ are *constant*, simplifying the recursion drastically — this is the standard **Kalman Filter (KF)**.
- **Non-linear systems** (e.g., a car with non-holonomic constraints and range-bearing sensors): We must linearize at every step using first-order Taylor expansion — this is the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Symbolic Form | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | State Prediction | $\hat{x}_{k+1 \vert k} = f(\hat{x}_{k \vert k}, u_k)$ | Motion model application |
| 2 | Covariance Prediction | $P_{k+1 \vert k} = A_k P_{k \vert k} A_k^T + Q_k$ | Uncertainty propagation |
| 3 | **Measurement Prediction** | $\hat{z}_{k+1 \vert k} = h(\hat{x}_{k+1 \vert k})$ | **Core topic equation** |
| 4 | **Innovation Covariance** | $S_{k+1} = H_{k+1} P_{k+1 \vert k} H_{k+1}^T + R_{k+1}$ | **Uncertainty of prediction** |
| 5 | Innovation | $\nu_{k+1} = z_{k+1} - \hat{z}_{k+1 \vert k}$ | Residual |
| 6 | Kalman Gain | $K_{k+1} = P_{k+1 \vert k} H_{k+1}^T S_{k+1}^{-1}$ | Optimal weighting |
| 7 | State Update | $\hat{x}_{k+1 \vert k+1} = \hat{x}_{k+1 \vert k} + K_{k+1} \nu_{k+1}$ | Posterior estimate |
| 8 | Covariance Update | $P_{k+1 \vert k+1} = (I - K_{k+1} H_{k+1}) P_{k+1 \vert k}$ | Posterior uncertainty |
| 9 | Linear KF (state) | $x_k = A x_{k-1} + B u_{k-1} + w_{k-1}$ | Linear motion |
| 10 | Linear KF (meas) | $z_k = H x_k + v_k$ | Linear sensor |
| 11 | EKF Linearization (f) | $A_k \approx \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\Big\vert_{\hat{x}_{k \vert k}}$ | First-order Taylor |
| 12 | EKF Linearization (h) | $H_k \approx \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\Big\vert_{\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}}$ | First-order Taylor |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla):** Predict where the camera/LiDAR *should* detect lane markings or pedestrians given the predicted ego-position.
- **Aerospace Drones (DJI):** Predict IMU-derived altitude vs. barometric measurement to detect sensor drift.
- **Mobile Robotics (Warehouse AMRs):** Predict wheel-encoder odometry vs. LiDAR scan-matching to detect wheel slip.
- **Robotic Surgery (Da Vinci):** Predict endoscopic camera position vs. EM-tracker feedback for sub-millimeter accuracy.
- **Underwater ROVs:** Predict sonar range vs. DVL-based dead-reckoning because GPS is unavailable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Linear Kalman Filter Measurement Prediction

We start from the **linear** measurement model:

$$z_k = H x_k + v_k$$

where $v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_k)$ is zero-mean Gaussian measurement noise with covariance $R_k$.

**Step 1:** Take the conditional expectation of $z_k$ given measurements up to time $k-1$ (this is the definition of measurement prediction):

$$\hat{z}_{k \vert k-1} = \mathbb{E}[z_k \mid z_{1:k-1}]$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the linear measurement model:

$$\hat{z}_{k \vert k-1} = \mathbb{E}[H x_k + v_k \mid z_{1:k-1}]$$

**Step 3:** Use the linearity of expectation $\mathbb{E}[aX + bY] = a\mathbb{E}[X] + b\mathbb{E}[Y]$:

$$\hat{z}_{k \vert k-1} = H \, \mathbb{E}[x_k \mid z_{1:k-1}] + \mathbb{E}[v_k \mid z_{1:k-1}]$$

**Step 4:** Recognize $\mathbb{E}[x_k \mid z_{1:k-1}] = \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}$ (the state prediction) and $\mathbb{E}[v_k \mid z_{1:k-1}] = 0$ (zero-mean noise):

$$\hat{z}_{k \vert k-1} = H \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}$$

This confirms the measurement prediction is simply the linear measurement matrix applied to the predicted state.

**Step 5:** Derive the innovation covariance. The innovation is:

$$\nu_k = z_k - \hat{z}_{k \vert k-1} = H x_k + v_k - H \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} = H \tilde{x}_{k \vert k-1} + v_k$$

where $\tilde{x}_{k \vert k-1} = x_k - \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}$ is the state prediction error.

**Step 6:** The covariance of the innovation is:

$$S_k = \mathbb{E}[\nu_k \nu_k^T] = \mathbb{E}[(H \tilde{x}_{k \vert k-1} + v_k)(H \tilde{x}_{k \vert k-1} + v_k)^T]$$

**Step 7:** Expand the outer product:

$$S_k = H \mathbb{E}[\tilde{x}_{k \vert k-1} \tilde{x}_{k \vert k-1}^T] H^T + H \mathbb{E}[\tilde{x}_{k \vert k-1} v_k^T] + \mathbb{E}[v_k \tilde{x}_{k \vert k-1}^T] H^T + \mathbb{E}[v_k v_k^T]$$

**Step 8:** Use the assumptions:
- $\mathbb{E}[\tilde{x}_{k \vert k-1} \tilde{x}_{k \vert k-1}^T] = P_{k \vert k-1}$
- $\mathbb{E}[\tilde{x}_{k \vert k-1} v_k^T] = 0$ (state and measurement noise are independent)
- $\mathbb{E}[v_k v_k^T] = R_k$

Substituting:

$$S_k = H P_{k \vert k-1} H^T + R_k$$

This is the innovation covariance.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Extended Kalman Filter Measurement Prediction

For a **non-linear** measurement model $z_k = h(x_k) + v_k$, we linearize using the first-order Taylor expansion around the predicted state $\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}$:

**Step 1:** Taylor series of $h(x_k)$ around $\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}$:

$$h(x_k) = h(\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}) + \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\Big\vert_{\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}} (x_k - \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}) + \text{higher order terms}$$

**Step 2:** Truncate the higher-order terms (first-order EKF approximation):

$$h(x_k) \approx h(\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}) + H_k (x_k - \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1})$$

where $H_k = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\Big\vert_{\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}}$ is the **Jacobian matrix**.

**Step 3:** Take the conditional expectation of the measurement:

$$\hat{z}_{k \vert k-1} = \mathbb{E}[h(x_k) + v_k \mid z_{1:k-1}] \approx h(\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1})$$

The linearization point is the predicted state, so $h$ is simply evaluated there.

**Step 4:** Form the innovation:

$$\nu_k = z_k - \hat{z}_{k \vert k-1} = h(x_k) - h(\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}) + v_k$$

**Step 5:** Apply the linearization to the innovation:

$$\nu_k \approx H_k (x_k - \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}) + v_k = H_k \tilde{x}_{k \vert k-1} + v_k$$

**Step 6:** The innovation covariance becomes:

$$S_k \approx H_k P_{k \vert k-1} H_k^T + R_k$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Examination Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Example 1: 1D Linear Robot with Range Sensor**
> 
> A robot moves along a 1D line. The state is position $x$. The motion model is $x_{k+1} = x_k + u_k + w_k$ with $u_k = 1.0$ m, $Q = 0.1 \, \text{m}^2$. The measurement model is $z_k = x_k + v_k$ with $R = 0.2 \, \text{m}^2$. The current estimate is $\hat{x}_{k \vert k} = 5.0$ m, $P_{k \vert k} = 0.5 \, \text{m}^2$. The actual measurement received is $z_{k+1} = 6.3$ m. Perform the **measurement prediction** step and compute the innovation covariance.

**Step 1: State Prediction**

$$\hat{x}_{k+1 \vert k} = \hat{x}_{k \vert k} + u_k = 5.0 + 1.0 = 6.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Covariance Prediction**

For a 1D linear motion with $A = 1$:

$$P_{k+1 \vert k} = A^2 P_{k \vert k} + Q = (1)(0.5) + 0.1 = 0.6 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3: Measurement Prediction**

The measurement model is $h(x) = x$, and the Jacobian is $H = 1$:

$$\hat{z}_{k+1 \vert k} = h(\hat{x}_{k+1 \vert k}) = 1 \cdot 6.0 = 6.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Innovation Covariance**

$$S_{k+1} = H^2 P_{k+1 \vert k} + R = (1)(0.6) + 0.2 = 0.8 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 5: Innovation**

$$\nu_{k+1} = z_{k+1} - \hat{z}_{k+1 \vert k} = 6.3 - 6.0 = 0.3 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Kalman Gain**

$$K_{k+1} = P_{k+1 \vert k} H^T S_{k+1}^{-1} = (0.6)(1) / (0.8) = 0.75$$

**Step 7: State Update**

$$\hat{x}_{k+1 \vert k+1} = 6.0 + (0.75)(0.3) = 6.0 + 0.225 = 6.225 \text{ m}$$

**Step 8: Covariance Update**

$$P_{k+1 \vert k+1} = (1 - 0.75)(0.6) = 0.15 \, \text{m}^2$$

The uncertainty reduced from $0.6$ to $0.15 \, \text{m}^2$ after incorporating the measurement.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: 2D EKF with Range-Bearing Sensor

> [!NOTE]
> **Example 2: 2D Robot with Range-Bearing Landmark Observation**
> 
> Robot state: $x = [p_x, p_y, \theta]^T$. Predicted state from previous step: $\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} = [2.0, 1.0, 30^\circ]^T$. A single landmark is at $l = [5.0, 2.0]^T$. The range-bearing sensor measures $z = [r, \phi]^T$ where:
> - $r = \sqrt{(l_x - p_x)^2 + (l_y - p_y)^2}$
> - $\phi = \text{atan2}(l_y - p_y, l_x - p_x) - \theta$
> 
> Compute the measurement prediction $\hat{z}_{k \vert k-1}$ and the Jacobian $H_k$.

**Step 1: Compute the relative position**

$$\Delta x = l_x - p_x = 5.0 - 2.0 = 3.0$$

$$\Delta y = l_y - p_y = 2.0 - 1.0 = 1.0$$

$$q = \Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2 = 9.0 + 1.0 = 10.0$$

$$\sqrt{q} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.1623$$

**Step 2: Compute measurement prediction**

$$\hat{r}_{k \vert k-1} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.1623 \text{ m}$$

$$\hat{\phi}_{k \vert k-1} = \text{atan2}(1.0, 3.0) - 30^\circ = 18.43^\circ - 30^\circ = -11.57^\circ$$

**Step 3: Compute the Jacobian $H_k$**

The Jacobian of $h(x) = [r, \phi]^T$ with respect to $x = [p_x, p_y, \theta]^T$ is:

$$H_k = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial r}{\partial p_x} & \frac{\partial r}{\partial p_y} & \frac{\partial r}{\partial \theta} \\[6pt] \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial p_x} & \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial p_y} & \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta} \end{bmatrix}$$

Computing the partial derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial r}{\partial p_x} = -\frac{\Delta x}{\sqrt{q}} = -\frac{3.0}{3.1623} \approx -0.9487$$

$$\frac{\partial r}{\partial p_y} = -\frac{\Delta y}{\sqrt{q}} = -\frac{1.0}{3.1623} \approx -0.3162$$

$$\frac{\partial r}{\partial \theta} = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial p_x} = \frac{\Delta y}{q} = \frac{1.0}{10.0} = 0.1$$

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial p_y} = -\frac{\Delta x}{q} = -\frac{3.0}{10.0} = -0.3$$

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta} = -1$$

**Step 4: Assemble the Jacobian**

$$H_k = \begin{bmatrix} -0.9487 & -0.3162 & 0 \\[3pt] 0.1 & -0.3 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

This Jacobian is then used in the innovation covariance calculation $S_k = H_k P_{k \vert k-1} H_k^T + R_k$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Measurement Prediction Subsystem

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *interactions* between the state prediction module, the measurement model, the Jacobian linearization, and the innovation computation. This is the canonical architecture used in every Bayesian filter (KF, EKF, UKF, Particle Filter).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Prior State Estimate x_k_k] --> B1[Motion Model f]
    A2[Control Input u_k] --> B1
    A3[Process Noise Covariance Q_k] --> B2[Covariance Propagator]
    A1 --> B2
    B1 --> C1[Predicted State x_k1_k]
    B2 --> C2[Predicted Covariance P_k1_k]
    C1 --> D1[Measurement Model h]
    D1 --> E1[Predicted Measurement z_hat_k1_k]
    C1 --> F1[Compute Jacobian H_k]
    F1 --> G1[Innovation Covariance S_k]
    C2 --> G1
    A4[Measurement Noise Covariance R_k] --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Kalman Gain K_k1]
    C2 --> H1
    F1 --> H1
    I1[Actual Sensor Reading z_k1] --> J1[Innovation Calculator]
    E1 --> J1
    J1 --> K1[Innovation nu_k1]
    H1 --> L1[State Update x_k1_k1]
    K1 --> L1
    H1 --> M1[Covariance Update P_k1_k1]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The top branch performs **state prediction** using the motion model $f$.
- The middle branch performs **covariance propagation**.
- The **predicted state** is then fed into the **measurement model** $h$ to produce $\hat{z}$.
- Simultaneously, the **Jacobian $H_k$** is computed at the predicted state.
- The actual sensor reading is subtracted from $\hat{z}$ to yield the **innovation** $\nu$.
- The innovation covariance $S_k$ depends on $H_k$, $P_{k+1 \vert k}$, and $R_k$.
- Finally, the Kalman Gain drives the state and covariance updates.

---

## 4.2 Measurement Prediction Cycle (Sequential Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: State Prediction x_hat_k1_k] --> S2[Step 2: Covariance Prediction P_k1_k]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Measurement Prediction z_hat_k1_k]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Innovation Covariance S_k]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Innovation nu_k]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Kalman Gain K_k]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: State Update]
    S6 --> S8[Step 8: Covariance Update]
```

---

## 4.3 Linear vs. Extended Kalman Filter Comparison (Architectural View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[State x_k] --> B{System Type}
    B -->|Linear| C[Linear Kalman Filter]
    B -->|Non-Linear| D[Extended Kalman Filter EKF]
    C --> E[Measurement Prediction: H x_hat]
    D --> F[Linearize h around x_hat: Compute Jacobian H_k]
    F --> G[Measurement Prediction: h x_hat]
    E --> H[Innovation Covariance: H P H^T + R]
    G --> I[Innovation Covariance: H_k P H_k^T + R]
    H --> J[Kalman Gain: P H^T S_inverse]
    I --> J
```

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Mapping: State Space to Measurement Space

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State Space X] -->|Motion Model f| B[Predicted State in X]
    B -->|Measurement Model h| C[Measurement Space Z]
    C --> D[Predicted Measurement z_hat]
    E[Actual Sensor z] --> F{Difference}
    D --> F
    F --> G[Innovation nu]
    G --> H[Update Belief in X]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagrammatic Interpretation for KTU Answers:** A measurement prediction is essentially a *projection* of the predicted state from state-space $\mathcal{X}$ into measurement-space $\mathcal{Z}$ via the observation function $h(\cdot)$. The closer $\hat{z}$ is to the true $z$, the smaller the innovation and the smaller the correction applied.
