KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** ### **Q1. Define state estimation in the context of robot motion planning. Write the Bayes Filter update equation and explain each term.** [3 Marks] **Model Answer:** State estimation is the process of inferring the internal state $x_t$ (pose, velocity, map) of a robot from a history of noisy sensor measurements $z_{1:t}$ and control inputs $u_{1:t}$. It computes the **posterior belief**: $$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$ The **Bayes Filter update equation** is: $$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$$ where: - $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ = **predicted belief** from the motion model (prior over $x_t$). - $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ = **measurement likelihood** from the sensor model. - $\eta$ = **normalizer** ensuring the posterior integrates to 1. - $bel(x_t)$ = **posterior belief** after incorporating $z_t$. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** ### **Q2. What is the Kalman Filter? Under what conditions is it the optimal estimator?** [3 Marks] **Model Answer:** The **Kalman Filter (KF)** is a recursive Bayesian estimator that maintains a Gaussian belief $(\mu_t, P_t)$ over the robot's state. It alternates between a **prediction** step (using the motion model) and an **update** step (using sensor measurements), as captured by the **Kalman gain** $K_t$. The KF is **optimal** (i.e., minimizes the mean-squared error of the state estimate) when: 1. The **motion model** is linear: $x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \varepsilon_t$. 2. The **measurement model** is linear: $z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t$. 3. All noise is **zero-mean Gaussian**: $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$ and $\delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$. 4. The **initial belief** is Gaussian: $bel(x_0) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, P_0)$. If any of these is violated, the KF is no longer optimal — the EKF, UKF, or Particle Filter must be used. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Derive the Kalman Filter equations from the Bayes Filter. Show explicitly how the Kalman gain $K_t$ is obtained by minimizing the posterior covariance. Comment on the role of $Q_t$ and $R_t$ in determining $K_t$.** [14 Marks] #### **Part (a) — Derive the KF equations from Bayes Filter [7 Marks]** **Step 1 — Bayes Filter under Gaussian linear assumptions.** Given $bel(x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_{t-1}, P_{t-1})$, $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) = \mathcal{N}(A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t, Q_t)$, and $p(z_t \mid x_t) = \mathcal{N}(C_t x_t, R_t)$, the prediction integral becomes: $$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \mathcal{N}(A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t,\; A_t P_{t-1} A_t^\top + Q_t)$$ **Step 2 — Identify predicted mean and covariance:** $$\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t$$ $$\bar{P}_t = A_t P_{t-1} A_t^\top + Q_t$$ **Step 3 — Apply the update. Gaussian multiplication gives a new Gaussian:** $$bel(x_t) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, P_t)$$ with the product-of-Gaussians formula yielding: $$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t(z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)$$ $$P_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{P}_t$$ #### **Part (b) — Derive the Kalman gain $K_t$ and discuss $Q_t, R_t$ [7 Marks]** **Step 4 — Form the posterior error $e_t = x_t - \mu_t$ and compute $P_t = \mathbb{E}[e_t e_t^\top]$:** $$P_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{P}_t (I - K_t C_t)^\top + K_t R_t K_t^\top$$ **Step 5 — Differentiate $\text{tr}(P_t)$ with respect to $K_t$ and set to zero:** $$\frac{\partial \, \text{tr}(P_t)}{\partial K_t} = -2 \bar{P}_t C_t^\top + 2 K_t (C_t \bar{P}_t C_t^\top + R_t) = 0$$ **Step 6 — Solve for $K_t$:** $$\boxed{K_t = \bar{P}_t C_t^\top (C_t \bar{P}_t C_t^\top + R_t)^{-1}}$$ **Step 7 — Effect of $Q_t$ and $R_t$:** - If **$R_t \to 0$** (perfect sensor): $K_t \to C_t^{-1}$ — measurement is trusted fully. - If **$R_t \to \infty$** (useless sensor): $K_t \to 0$ — only prediction is used. - If **$Q_t \to 0$** (perfect motion model): $\bar{P}_t \to 0$, so $K_t \to 0$ — the prediction is trusted fully. - If **$Q_t \to \infty$** (terrible motion model): $\bar{P}_t$ grows large, $K_t$ approaches 1 — measurement dominates. Hence, $K_t$ is a **data-driven, time-varying weight** that optimally balances the two information sources. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Explain the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) for nonlinear estimation. Show how the EKF is obtained by linearizing the motion and measurement models via Jacobians. Discuss the limitations of the EKF and explain how the Particle Filter addresses them.** [14 Marks] #### **Part (a) — EKF Derivation via Linearization [7 Marks]** **Step 1 — Write the nonlinear models:** $$x_t = g(x_{t-1}, u_t) + \varepsilon_t$$ $$z_t = h(x_t) + \delta_t$$ **Step 2 — First-order Taylor expansion around the operating point:** $$g(x_{t-1}, u_t) \approx g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) + G_t (x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})$$ $$h(x_t) \approx h(\bar{\mu}_t) + H_t (x_t - \bar{\mu}_t)$$ **Step 3 — Define the Jacobians:** $$G_t = \frac{\partial g}{\partial x} \bigg|_{\mu_{t-1}, u_t} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$ $$H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} \bigg|_{\bar{\mu}_t} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into KF equations with $A \to G_t$, $C \to H_t$:** $$\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$$ $$\bar{P}_t = G_t P_{t-1} G_t^\top + Q_t$$ $$K_t = \bar{P}_t H_t^\top (H_t \bar{P}_t H_t^\top + R_t)^{-1}$$ $$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))$$ $$P_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{P}_t$$ #### **Part (b) — Limitations of EKF and Remedy via Particle Filter [7 Marks]** **Limitations of EKF:** 1. **First-order approximation error** — large linearization error in highly nonlinear regions (e.g., bearing to landmark at large angles, rotation through 90°). 2. **Unimodal Gaussian assumption** — fails in **global localization** where the robot does not know its rough position (the belief is multi-modal, with several equally-likely poses). 3. **Divergence** — in some nonlinear systems, the EKF covariance matrix can become non-positive-definite, causing filter divergence. 4. **Jacobian computation** — non-differentiable models (e.g., those involving $\arg\min$, $\text{sign}$, $\max$) cannot be handled. **How the Particle Filter addresses these:** - **Arbitrary distributions:** The PF represents belief as $M$ weighted samples — any shape, any number of modes. - **No linearization:** The PF directly samples from $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)$ — no Jacobians needed. - **Global localization:** Particles are spread over the entire state space initially; the measurement likelihood naturally concentrates them near the true pose. - **Non-differentiable models:** Any model with a defined PDF can be used. **Cost trade-off:** $O(M \cdot n)$ per step (vs. $O(n^3)$ for EKF). With $M = 500\text{–}5000$ particles, the PF is computationally heavier but handles complexity the EKF cannot. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Points:** > > 1. **Confusing *belief* with *measurement*:** The belief is the **posterior** — students often write $bel(x_t) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$ instead of $\eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \overline{bel}(x_t)$. The normalizer $\eta$ is **mandatory** — losing it costs **1 mark**. > 2. **Forgetting the transpose on $A_t$:** The predicted covariance is $\bar{P}_t = A_t P_{t-1} A_t^\top + Q_t$, **not** $A_t P_{t-1} A_t + Q_t$. KTU examiners specifically watch for this. > 3. **In KF derivation, not showing the matrix derivative step:** Writing $K_t$ directly without the $\partial \text{tr}(P_t)/\partial K_t$ step will lose **2–3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-part. > 4. **EKF: failing to mention linearization point:** The Jacobians must be evaluated at $\mu_{t-1}$ (motion) and $\bar{\mu}_t$ (measurement). A bare statement "compute the Jacobian" is incomplete. > 5. **Particle Filter: forgetting resampling:** The four stages (Sample, Weight, Normalize, **Resample**) are not symmetric — omitting resampling gives an **unnormalized, degenerate** particle set. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Estimation** - **Bayes Filter** is the **most general** recursive estimator — every other filter is a special case. - The two core equations are **Prediction** (motion) and **Update** (measurement). - **Kalman Filter** assumes **linear** dynamics + **Gaussian** noise → closed-form solution, optimal in MSE. - The **Kalman gain** $K_t = \bar{P}_t C_t^\top (C_t \bar{P}_t C_t^\top + R_t)^{-1}$ is the **optimal weight** balancing prediction and measurement. - **EKF** handles mild nonlinearity via **first-order Taylor expansion** (Jacobians $G_t, H_t$). - **Particle Filter** handles arbitrary nonlinearities and **multi-modal beliefs** using $M$ weighted samples. - **Resampling** is essential in the PF to avoid weight collapse; resample when $N_{\text{eff}} < M/2$. - **Markov Localization** maintains a belief over the **entire** state space → recovers from **kidnapped robot** scenarios. - **SLAM** estimates both pose and map; **EKF-SLAM** has $O(N^3)$ cost; **FastSLAM** uses Rao-Blackwellization with PFs to get $O(N \log N)$. - Always state the **assumptions** of any estimator explicitly — KTU 2024 values **assumption-statement marks** (typically 1 mark per 7-mark sub-part). - In numerical problems, the **predict-then-update** order is non-negotiable; reversing it gives a wrong state estimate. - The **Joseph form** of the covariance update $P = (I - KC) P (I - KC)^\top + K R K^\top$ is **numerically more stable** than $(I-KC)P$ and worth mentioning in viva.

topics.title: Landmark-Based Navigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Landmark-Based Navigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Landmark-Based Navigation** is a localization and path-planning paradigm in mobile robotics wherein a robot uses a set of pre-identified, distinguishable, and spatially fixed features in its operating environment — termed **landmarks** — to estimate its own pose (position and orientation) and subsequently plan a collision-free trajectory from a start configuration to a goal configuration.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a landmark $\ell_i \in \mathcal{L}$ is defined as an observable entity with a known geometric or signature-based descriptor $f(\ell_i)$ and a known global map coordinate $\mathbf{m}_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i)^{\top}$ such that the robot's pose $\mathbf{x}_t = (x_t, y_t, \theta_t)^{\top}$ can be inferred from a measurement model:

$$
z_t^i = h(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{m}_i) + v_t
$$

where $h(\cdot)$ is the sensor observation function and $v_t$ is zero-mean Gaussian measurement noise with covariance $\mathbf{R}_t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Landmark-based navigation sits at the intersection of **perception**, **localization**, and **motion planning** within the broader navigation stack. Unlike reactive (bug-based) or purely geometric (roadmap) planners, landmark-based methods *require a prior or incrementally-built map*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Hiker with a Compass and Three Mountains":**
>
> Imagine you are a hiker dropped in the middle of a foggy valley. You cannot see the valley floor, but you can faintly spot **three distinct mountain peaks** in the distance. Because you have a topographic map that tells you the *exact* coordinates of these three peaks, and you can measure the **angles** (bearings) at which you see them with your compass, you can geometrically "triangulate" your position on the map. Each mountain peak plays the role of a **landmark**. As you walk, the bearings shift, and you keep recomputing your position. This is *exactly* how a robot uses landmarks.
>
> - **Mountain peaks** → Landmarks $\ell_i$
> - **Compass bearings** → Sensor measurements $z_t^i$
> - **Topographic map** → Global landmark map $\mathcal{M} = \{\mathbf{m}_1, \mathbf{m}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{m}_n\}$
> - **Your position on the map** → Robot pose estimate $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t$

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Board-Exam Ready)

| Term | Definition | Standard Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Landmark** | A salient, re-observable feature in the environment | $\ell_i$ |
| **Map** | A collection of landmark locations and descriptors | $\mathcal{M}$ |
| **Measurement** | A sensor observation of a landmark | $z_t^i$ |
| **Pose** | The robot's configuration (x, y, θ) | $\mathbf{x}_t$ |
| **Bearing** | Angular observation of a landmark | $\beta_i$ |
| **Range** | Distance observation to a landmark | $r_i$ |
| **Data Association** | Matching observed features to map landmarks | — |
| **FOV** | Field of view of the robot's sensor | $\phi$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Engineering Metric:** A high-quality landmark must possess three properties: **distinctiveness** (uniquely identifiable), **persistence** (stable over time), and **observability** (detectable from typical robot viewpoints).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline of Landmark-Based Navigation

The landmark-based navigation system operates as a closed-loop pipeline, decomposable into **four sequential stages**:

1. **Perception (Feature Extraction)**
   - The robot's onboard sensors (camera, **LiDAR**, sonar, RFID reader) scan the environment.
   - Raw sensor data is processed via feature extraction algorithms (e.g., **SIFT**, **SURF**, **ORB** for vision; corner/edge detectors for LiDAR).
   - Output: a set of candidate feature descriptors $\mathcal{F} = \{f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_k\}$.

2. **Data Association**
   - Each detected feature $f_j$ is matched against the known map $\mathcal{M}$ to find the most likely landmark correspondence.
   - This is the **hardest sub-problem** in landmark navigation; ambiguity causes catastrophic localization failure.
   - Common methods: **Nearest-Neighbor (NN)**, **Joint Compatibility Branch and Bound (JCBB)**, Mahalanobis distance gating.

3. **Pose Estimation (Localization)**
   - Given the correspondences $\{(\mathbf{m}_i, z_t^i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, the robot's pose $\mathbf{x}_t$ is estimated.
   - The most common estimator: **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**, which fuses motion model prediction with landmark measurement updates.
   - Alternative: **Particle Filter (Monte Carlo Localization)** for non-Gaussian, global-localization scenarios.

4. **Motion Planning**
   - With the pose estimate $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t$ and the map $\mathcal{M}$, a planner generates a trajectory to the goal $\mathbf{x}_{goal}$.
   - Planners may use the landmark map directly (topological) or augment it with a metric grid for fine-grained obstacle avoidance.

### 2.2 Types of Landmarks

Landmarks are classified along three orthogonal axes:

- **By Origin:**
  - *Natural landmarks:* Rocks, tree trunks, building corners (no modification of environment required).
  - *Artificial landmarks:* QR codes, AprilTags, RFID tags, retroreflectors (engineered for high detectability).

- **By Modality:**
  - *Visual landmarks:* Detected via cameras; rich in appearance information.
  - *Geometric landmarks:* Detected via range sensors (LiDAR/Sonar); provide distance/bearing.

- **By Information Content:**
  - *Point landmarks:* Encode a single 2D/3D position.
  - *Line landmarks:* Encode infinite-extent geometric primitives (walls, corridors).
  - *Area landmarks:* Encode regions with distinct signatures (doorways, room signatures).

### 2.3 The Measurement Model (Rigorous)

For a 2D planar robot at pose $\mathbf{x}_t = (x_t, y_t, \theta_t)^{\top}$ observing landmark $i$ at known map location $\mathbf{m}_i = (m_i^x, m_i^y)^{\top}$, the **range-bearing measurement model** is:

$$
z_t^i = \begin{bmatrix} r_t^i \\ \beta_t^i \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(m_i^x - x_t)^2 + (m_i^y - y_t)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(m_i^y - y_t, m_i^x - x_t) - \theta_t \end{bmatrix} + v_t
$$

where $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{R}_t)$ is zero-mean Gaussian noise, and $\mathbf{R}_t = \text{diag}(\sigma_r^2, \sigma_\beta^2)$ is the measurement noise covariance.

The Jacobian $\mathbf{H}_i = \frac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{x}_t}$ of this model is essential for EKF linearization and is a common exam derivation:

$$
\mathbf{H}_i = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{x_t - m_i^x}{r_t^i} & \frac{y_t - m_i^y}{r_t^i} & 0 \\ \frac{m_i^y - y_t}{(r_t^i)^2} & \frac{m_i^x - x_t}{(r_t^i)^2} & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 2.4 Why Landmarks Work: The Theoretical Foundation

The theoretical justification for landmark-based navigation rests on the **observability** of the robot's state from landmark measurements. A landmark provides a *constraint* in the robot's pose space. With **two** non-collinear landmarks and bearing-only measurements, the planar pose becomes globally observable (up to discrete symmetries). With **range-bearing** measurements, **a single landmark** is sufficient in principle to localize a robot in 2D, though multiple landmarks reduce noise sensitivity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Landmark-based navigation underpins the **GPS** system in outdoor robotics (satellites as landmarks), **Voronoi-based** and **topological** indoor navigation, and forms the perception backbone of modern **Visual SLAM** (ORB-SLAM, LSD-SLAM) systems used in autonomous vehicles, drones, and warehouse robots (e.g., Amazon Kiva).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Range observation | $r = \sqrt{(m^x - x)^2 + (m^y - y)^2}$ | metres (m) |
| 2 | Bearing observation | $\beta = \text{atan2}(m^y - y, m^x - x) - \theta$ | radians (rad) |
| 3 | Measurement Jacobian | $\mathbf{H}_i = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{x - m^x}{r} & \frac{y - m^y}{r} & 0 \\ \frac{m^y - y}{r^2} & \frac{m^x - x}{r^2} & -1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | EKF Update | $\mathbf{K} = \mathbf{P}\mathbf{H}^{\top}(\mathbf{H}\mathbf{P}\mathbf{H}^{\top} + \mathbf{R})^{-1}$ | Kalman gain |
| 5 | Pose Update | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}^+ = \hat{\mathbf{x}}^- + \mathbf{K}(z - h(\hat{\mathbf{x}}^-, \mathbf{m}))$ | m, rad |
| 6 | Covariance Update | $\mathbf{P}^+ = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}\mathbf{H})\mathbf{P}^-$ | Covariance matrix |
| 7 | Triangulation (2 landmarks) | $\mathbf{x} = (\mathbf{H}^{\top}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{\top}\mathbf{b}$ | Batch solution |
| 8 | Mahalanobis distance gate | $d^2 = (z - h)^{\top}\mathbf{S}^{-1}(z - h) \le \chi^2_{d,\alpha}$ | Statistical gate |
| 9 | Minimum landmarks for observability (2D bearing-only) | $N \ge 2$ (non-collinear) | Count |
| 10 | Minimum landmarks for observability (2D range-bearing) | $N \ge 1$ | Count |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The measurement Jacobian $\mathbf{H}_i$ and the EKF update equations are the **most frequently tested** items in landmark-navigation problems. Memorize the structure of $\mathbf{H}_i$ — its third column is always $(0, -1)^{\top}$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bearing-Only Triangulation with Two Landmarks

**Problem Setup:** A robot at unknown pose $(x, y, \theta)$ observes two known landmarks $\mathbf{m}_1 = (m_1^x, m_1^y)$ and $\mathbf{m}_2 = (m_2^x, m_2^y)$. It measures the bearings $\beta_1$ and $\beta_2$ in the robot's body frame. Derive the robot's position $(x, y)$.

**Step 1 — Write the bearing equations:**

By definition of bearing in the global frame:

$$
\theta + \beta_1 = \text{atan2}(m_1^y - y, m_1^x - x)
$$

$$
\theta + \beta_2 = \text{atan2}(m_2^y - y, m_2^x - x)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the tangent function to convert to linear form:**

Using $\tan(\text{atan2}(A, B)) = A/B$ for both sides:

$$
\tan(\theta + \beta_1) = \frac{m_1^y - y}{m_1^x - x}
$$

$$
\tan(\theta + \beta_2) = \frac{m_2^y - y}{m_2^x - x}
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply to obtain linear equations:**

Let $\tau_i = \tan(\theta + \beta_i)$. Then:

$$
m_1^y - y = \tau_1 (m_1^x - x)
$$

$$
m_2^y - y = \tau_2 (m_2^x - x)
$$

**Step 4 — Expand and collect unknowns $(x, y)$:**

$$
m_1^y - y = \tau_1 m_1^x - \tau_1 x \implies \tau_1 x - y = \tau_1 m_1^x - m_1^y
$$

$$
\tau_2 x - y = \tau_2 m_2^x - m_2^y
$$

**Step 5 — Write in matrix form $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{q} = \mathbf{b}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \tau_1 & -1 \\ \tau_2 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} \tau_1 m_1^x - m_1^y \\ \tau_2 m_2^x - m_2^y \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve by matrix inversion (assuming $\tau_1 \neq \tau_2$, i.e., non-collinear landmarks):**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\tau_2 - \tau_1} \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ -\tau_2 & \tau_1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \tau_1 m_1^x - m_1^y \\ \tau_2 m_2^x - m_2^y \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 7 — Extract the closed-form position:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= \frac{\tau_1 m_1^x - m_1^y - \tau_2 m_2^x + m_2^y}{\tau_1 - \tau_2} \\[4pt]
y &= \frac{\tau_1 \tau_2 (m_1^x - m_2^x) - (\tau_1 m_2^y - \tau_2 m_1^y)}{\tau_1 - \tau_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The orientation $\theta$ can be recovered as $\theta = \text{atan2}(m_1^y - y, m_1^x - x) - \beta_1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Note:** This derivation demonstrates the **uniqueness of the solution** under the assumption that the two landmarks are not collinear with the robot — a key condition for *observability*. Examiners frequently ask: *"What happens if the two landmarks and the robot are collinear?"* — Answer: the system becomes unobservable because both bearings become identical.

### 3.2 Derivation: EKF Update for Landmark-Based Localization

**Step 1 — Prediction step (motion model):**

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^- = f(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t-1}^+, \mathbf{u}_t)
$$

$$
\mathbf{P}_t^- = \mathbf{F}_t \mathbf{P}_{t-1}^+ \mathbf{F}_t^{\top} + \mathbf{Q}_t
$$

where $\mathbf{F}_t = \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$ is the motion Jacobian and $\mathbf{Q}_t$ is process noise.

**Step 2 — Innovation (measurement residual):**

$$
\nu_t = z_t - h(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^-, \mathbf{m}_i)
$$

**Step 3 — Innovation covariance:**

$$
\mathbf{S}_t = \mathbf{H}_i \mathbf{P}_t^- \mathbf{H}_i^{\top} + \mathbf{R}_t
$$

**Step 4 — Kalman gain computation:**

$$
\mathbf{K}_t = \mathbf{P}_t^- \mathbf{H}_i^{\top} \mathbf{S}_t^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — State update:**

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^+ = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^- + \mathbf{K}_t \nu_t
$$

**Step 6 — Covariance update (Joseph form for numerical stability):**

$$
\mathbf{P}_t^+ = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_t \mathbf{H}_i) \mathbf{P}_t^- (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_t \mathbf{H}_i)^{\top} + \mathbf{K}_t \mathbf{R}_t \mathbf{K}_t^{\top}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- Robot pose prior: $\hat{\mathbf{x}}^- = (0, 0, 0)^{\top}$ m, rad
- Prior covariance: $\mathbf{P}^- = \text{diag}(0.5, 0.5, 0.1)$
- Landmark map: $\mathbf{m}_1 = (3, 4)^{\top}$ m
- Measurement: $z = (5.1, 0.93)^{\top}$ (range, bearing in rad)
- Measurement noise: $\mathbf{R} = \text{diag}(0.1, 0.05)$

**Step 1 — Predicted measurement:**

$$
r^- = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = 5.0 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\beta^- = \text{atan2}(4, 3) - 0 = 0.9273 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2 — Innovation:**

$$
\nu = z - h(\hat{\mathbf{x}}^-, \mathbf{m}_1) = (5.1 - 5.0, \ 0.93 - 0.9273)^{\top} = (0.1, \ 0.0027)^{\top}
$$

**Step 3 — Jacobian:**

$$
\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{0 - 3}{5} & \frac{0 - 4}{5} & 0 \\ \frac{4 - 0}{25} & \frac{3 - 0}{25} & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.6 & -0.8 & 0 \\ 0.16 & 0.12 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Innovation covariance:**

$$
\mathbf{H}\mathbf{P}^-\mathbf{H}^{\top} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.04 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{S} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.09 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Kalman gain:**

$$
\mathbf{K} = \mathbf{P}^- \mathbf{H}^{\top} \mathbf{S}^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 & 0.444 \\ -0.4 & 0.444 \\ 0 & -4.44 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Updated pose:**

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}^+ = (0, 0, 0)^{\top} + \mathbf{K} \nu = (0, 0, 0)^{\top} + \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 & 0.444 \\ -0.4 & 0.444 \\ 0 & -4.44 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.0027 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.0488 \\ -0.0388 \\ -0.012 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The EKF correction pulls the robot's estimated position **toward** the direction implied by the noisy measurement. The negative x and y values are due to the Jacobian's sign convention, indicating the measurement model treats the landmark as being in the +x, +y quadrant relative to the robot. In practice, the absolute sign depends on the global frame convention.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Landmark-Based Navigation Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensor Data Acquisition] --> B[Pre-processing and Noise Filtering]
    B --> C[Feature Extraction Module]
    C --> D{Data Association Stage}
    D -- Match Found --> E[Pose Estimation via EKF]
    D -- No Match --> F[Initialize New Landmark]
    F --> E
    E --> G[Updated Pose Estimate x-hat]
    G --> H[Global Path Planner]
    H --> I[Motion Controller]
    I --> J[Actuator Commands]
    J --> K[Robot Motion in Environment]
    K --> A
    G --> L[Map Update Module]
    L --> M[Landmark Database M]
    M --> D
```

### 4.2 Landmark Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L[Landmark Types] --> L1[By Origin]
    L1 --> L1A[Natural Landmarks]
    L1 --> L1B[Artificial Landmarks]
    L1A --> L1A1[Trees and Rocks]
    L1A --> L1A2[Building Corners]
    L1B --> L1B1[AprilTags and QR Codes]
    L1B --> L1B2[RFID Beacons]
    L --> L2[By Modality]
    L2 --> L2A[Visual Landmarks]
    L2 --> L2B[Geometric Landmarks]
    L2A --> L2A1[Detected by Camera]
    L2A --> L2A2[SIFT SURF ORB Features]
    L2B --> L2B1[Detected by LiDAR]
    L2B --> L2B2[Detected by Sonar]
    L --> L3[By Information Content]
    L3 --> L3A[Point Landmarks]
    L3 --> L3B[Line Landmarks]
    L3 --> L3C[Area Landmarks]
```

### 4.3 EKF Localization Loop (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Previous Pose x-hat t-1] --> B[Prediction Module]
    B --> C[Predicted Pose x-hat t-minus]
    B --> D[Predicted Covariance P t-minus]
    C --> E[Measurement Prediction h of x-hat]
    D --> F[Innovation Covariance S]
    G[Sensor Measurement z t] --> H[Innovation nu = z minus h]
    E --> H
    H --> I[Kalman Gain K]
    D --> I
    F --> I
    I --> J[State Update x-hat t-plus]
    I --> K[Covariance Update P t-plus]
    J --> L[Output Pose Estimate]
    K --> L
    L --> A
```

### 4.4 Geometric Setup: Triangulation with Two Landmarks

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Robot at unknown pose x y theta] -- bearing beta1 --> M1[Landmark 1 at m1-x, m1-y]
    R -- bearing beta2 --> M2[Landmark 2 at m2-x, m2-y]
    M1 -. known global map .- M3[Global Map M]
    M2 -. known global map .- M3
    M3 --> T[Triangulation Solver]
    T --> P[Estimated Pose x-hat, y-hat, theta-hat]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define a *landmark* in the context of robot navigation. List the three essential properties a feature must possess to qualify as a reliable landmark.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A landmark is a salient, re-observable feature in the robot's environment whose location in a global reference frame is known (or can be learned) and which can be reliably detected by the robot's onboard sensors.
>
> The three essential properties are:
> 1. **Distinctiveness** — The landmark must have a unique signature so it is not confused with other features.
> 2. **Persistence** — The landmark must remain spatially stable and detectable over the duration of the navigation task.
> 3. **Observability** — The landmark must be within the robot's sensor range and field of view from typical operating positions.
>
> *(Award 1 mark each for: definition, listing 3 properties, brief justification.)*

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Distinguish between **natural** and **artificial** landmarks. Give one example of each.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Natural landmarks** are pre-existing salient features in the environment that were not placed for robotic localization. They are inexpensive but may have variable appearance. *Example:* a tree trunk, a building corner, a rock formation.
> - **Artificial landmarks** are purposefully engineered markers placed in the environment to maximize detectability and uniqueness. They are more reliable but require installation and maintenance. *Example:* an AprilTag, an RFID beacon, a QR code, or a retroreflective marker.
> *(Award 1.5 marks for the distinction, 1 mark for examples, 0.5 for engineering trade-off note.)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2 Choice A]**
>
> **Question A (14 marks):**
> **(a)** Derive the range-bearing measurement model for a differential-drive robot at pose $(x, y, \theta)$ observing a landmark at known map location $(m_x, m_y)$. Clearly state all variables and the noise assumptions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A mobile robot with pose prior estimate $\hat{\mathbf{x}}^- = (1.0, 2.0, 0.5)^{\top}$ observes a landmark at $\mathbf{m} = (4.0, 6.0)^{\top}$ with measurement $z = (5.0, 0.785)^{\top}$ (range in m, bearing in rad). Given the prior covariance $\mathbf{P}^- = \text{diag}(0.2, 0.2, 0.05)$ and measurement noise $\mathbf{R} = \text{diag}(0.05, 0.02)$, compute the **innovation** $\nu$ and the **innovation covariance** $\mathbf{S}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

The robot's pose in the global frame is $\mathbf{x} = (x, y, \theta)^{\top}$ where $(x, y)$ is the 2D position and $\theta$ is the heading angle measured counterclockwise from the global x-axis. The landmark has known global coordinates $\mathbf{m} = (m_x, m_y)^{\top}$.

The relative position of the landmark with respect to the robot, expressed in the **global frame**, is:

$$
\Delta x = m_x - x, \qquad \Delta y = m_y - y
$$

To express this in the **robot's body frame**, we apply the 2D rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}(\theta)$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \Delta x_b \\ \Delta y_b \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} m_x - x \\ m_y - y \end{bmatrix}
$$

The range (Euclidean distance) is rotation-invariant:

$$
r = \sqrt{(m_x - x)^2 + (m_y - y)^2}
$$

The bearing (angle from the robot's forward x-axis to the landmark) is the angle of the body-frame displacement:

$$
\beta = \text{atan2}(\Delta y_b, \Delta x_b)
$$

Using trigonometric identities and the addition formula for atan2, this simplifies to:

$$
\beta = \text{atan2}(m_y - y, m_x - x) - \theta
$$

Therefore, the **complete range-bearing measurement model** is:

$$
z = \begin{bmatrix} r \\ \beta \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(m_x - x)^2 + (m_y - y)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(m_y - y, m_x - x) - \theta \end{bmatrix} + v
$$

where $v \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{R})$ is the zero-mean Gaussian measurement noise with covariance $\mathbf{R} = \text{diag}(\sigma_r^2, \sigma_\beta^2)$. The noise is assumed i.i.d. across time steps and independent of process noise.

*(Award 1 mark for the rotation step, 2 marks for the range expression, 2 marks for the bearing expression, 1 mark for the noise model, 1 mark for the noise assumptions.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Predicted measurement $h(\hat{\mathbf{x}}^-, \mathbf{m})$:**

Predicted range:

$$
r^- = \sqrt{(4.0 - 1.0)^2 + (6.0 - 2.0)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5.0 \text{ m}
$$

Predicted bearing:

$$
\beta^- = \text{atan2}(6.0 - 2.0, 4.0 - 1.0) - 0.5 = \text{atan2}(4, 3) - 0.5
$$

$$
\text{atan2}(4, 3) = 0.9273 \text{ rad} \implies \beta^- = 0.4273 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2 — Innovation $\nu$:**

$$
\nu = z - h(\hat{\mathbf{x}}^-, \mathbf{m}) = \begin{bmatrix} 5.0 - 5.0 \\ 0.785 - 0.4273 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0 \\ 0.3577 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Jacobian $\mathbf{H}$:**

Using the formula derived in Section 2.3:

$$
\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{x - m_x}{r} & \frac{y - m_y}{r} & 0 \\ \frac{m_y - y}{r^2} & \frac{m_x - x}{r^2} & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1-4}{5} & \frac{2-6}{5} & 0 \\ \frac{6-2}{25} & \frac{4-1}{25} & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.6 & -0.8 & 0 \\ 0.16 & 0.12 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Innovation covariance $\mathbf{S}$:**

$$
\mathbf{H}\mathbf{P}^-\mathbf{H}^{\top} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.6 & -0.8 & 0 \\ 0.16 & 0.12 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.05 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -0.6 & 0.16 \\ -0.8 & 0.12 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
= \begin{bmatrix} -0.12 & -0.16 & 0 \\ 0.032 & 0.024 & -0.05 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -0.6 & 0.16 \\ -0.8 & 0.12 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
= \begin{bmatrix} 0.072 + 0.128 & -0.0192 - 0.0192 \\ -0.0192 - 0.0192 & 0.00512 + 0.00288 + 0.05 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & -0.0384 \\ -0.0384 & 0.058 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{P}^-\mathbf{H}^{\top} + \mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.25 & -0.0384 \\ -0.0384 & 0.078 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*(Award 1 mark for predicted measurement, 1 mark for innovation, 2 marks for Jacobian, 3 marks for innovation covariance.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2 Choice B]**
>
> **Question B (14 marks):**
> **(a)** Explain the four sequential stages of the landmark-based navigation pipeline. For each stage, identify one algorithmic technique commonly used. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the aid of a diagram, derive the closed-form solution for the robot's position $(x, y)$ using bearing-only measurements from two non-collinear landmarks. State explicitly the geometric condition under which the solution becomes ill-defined. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Four Stages (7 marks):**

1. **Perception / Feature Extraction** — The robot acquires raw sensor data and extracts salient features. For cameras, algorithms such as **SIFT (Scale-Invariant Feature Transform)**, **SURF (Speeded-Up Robust Features)**, or **ORB (Oriented FAST and Rotated BRIEF)** detect and describe keypoints invariant to scale, rotation, and (partially) illumination.
2. **Data Association** — Each detected feature is matched to a landmark in the global map. Techniques include the **Nearest-Neighbor** matcher using descriptor distance and statistical gating via the **Mahalanobis distance**, or the more robust **Joint Compatibility Branch and Bound (JCBB)** algorithm.
3. **Pose Estimation / Localization** — Given the matched landmark-observation pairs, the robot's pose is estimated. The dominant technique is the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**, which fuses a motion model prediction with landmark measurement updates in a probabilistically optimal (minimum-variance) manner.
4. **Motion Planning** — Using the estimated pose and the map, a trajectory is generated toward the goal. Common techniques include **A\* on a landmark-augmented grid**, **topological planners** that hop between landmark regions, or **sampling-based planners** like **RRT\*** in continuous space.

*(Award 1.5 marks per stage, 0.5 for the technique + 1.0 for the explanation; deduct 0.5 for missing algorithmic tool.)*

**Part (b) — Triangulation Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to the detailed derivation in Section 3.1 above. The student must:

1. Write the two bearing equations (1 mark).
2. Apply the tangent function to linearize (1 mark).
3. Cross-multiply and collect unknowns into matrix form (2 marks).
4. Solve via matrix inversion to obtain the closed-form $x$ and $y$ (2 marks).
5. State the ill-defined condition: **the solution is ill-defined (singular) when $\tau_1 = \tau_2$, i.e., when the two landmarks and the robot are collinear**, because the two bearing rays become parallel and do not intersect at a unique point (1 mark).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Students often write $\beta = \text{atan2}(m_y - y, m_x - x)$ **without subtracting $\theta$**, forgetting the body-to-global frame transformation. This loses **2 marks**.
> - **Common mistake 2:** In the bearing Jacobian, students forget the third column $(0, -1)^{\top}$. This is a **frequently-asked sub-part worth 1 mark**.
> - **Common mistake 3:** In numerical problems, students confuse **radians vs degrees** when plugging in $\text{atan2}$. Always verify units of measurement noise $\mathbf{R}$ — if $\sigma_\beta$ is in degrees, convert to radians first. This loses up to **3 marks** in numerical EKF problems.
> - **Common mistake 4:** Students omit the noise model $v \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{R})$ from derivations, costing **1 mark** for incomplete specification.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A landmark is a salient, distinguishable, persistently observable feature with a known (or learnable) global location, used by a robot to estimate its pose.
- **Pipeline order:** Perception → Data Association → Pose Estimation → Motion Planning (closed-loop).
- **Measurement model:** $z = h(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{m}) + v$ with $v \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{R})$; the range-bearing form is the KTU default.
- **Jacobian third column:** Always $(0, -1)^{\top}$ for bearing.
- **Observability in 2D:** Bearing-only needs $\ge 2$ non-collinear landmarks; range-bearing needs $\ge 1$.
- **EKF update sequence:** Innovation $\rightarrow$ Innovation covariance $\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{HPH}^{\top} + \mathbf{R}$ $\rightarrow$ Kalman gain $\mathbf{K} = \mathbf{PH}^{\top}\mathbf{S}^{-1}$ $\rightarrow$ State and covariance update.
- **Singularity condition for triangulation:** The system is singular when the two landmark bearings are equal, i.e., robot and landmarks are collinear.
- **Natural vs Artificial:** Natural landmarks are free but unreliable; artificial landmarks are engineered for robustness but cost money and maintenance.
- **Data association is the hardest problem** — it determines whether the rest of the pipeline succeeds.
- **Common sensors:** Camera (visual), LiDAR (geometric), Sonar (close-range geometric), RFID (identity-based).
- **Real-world use cases:** GPS, Visual SLAM, warehouse robots, autonomous vehicles, drone navigation.
- **Board exam hot buttons:** Derivation of the measurement model, Jacobian structure, EKF update equations, and numerical computation of innovation / innovation covariance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion in Potential Field and Navigation Function - Problem Statement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Motion in Potential Field and Navigation Function - Problem Statement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Motion in a Potential Field — Formal Definition

In robot motion planning, a **Potential Field Method** is a reactive, real-time navigation technique in which the robot's configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ is endowed with an **artificial scalar potential function** $U : \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$. The robot is treated as a point moving under the influence of the **negative gradient** of this potential, analogous to a charged particle moving in an electrostatic field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Rigor-Aligned):**
> Motion planning in a potential field is the problem of constructing a continuous, collision-free trajectory $q(t)$ from an initial configuration $q_{\text{start}}$ to a goal configuration $q_{\text{goal}}$ by integrating the differential equation
> $$\dot{q}(t) = -\nabla U(q(t))$$
> where $U(q)$ is an artificial potential that attains its unique global minimum at $q_{\text{goal}}$ and grows without bound in the vicinity of every obstacle boundary $\partial \mathcal{O}_i$.

## 1.2 Navigation Function — Formal Definition

A **Navigation Function** $f : \mathcal{F} \rightarrow [0, 1]$ is a special class of artificial potential introduced by **Koditschek and Rimon (1990)**. It is a $C^2$-smooth (twice continuously differentiable) map defined on a compact, connected, sphere-world free space $\mathcal{F}$ such that:

1. $f$ is **polar** at $q_{\text{goal}}$ — i.e., $f(q_{\text{goal}}) = 0$ and $\nabla f(q_{\text{goal}}) = 0$, with a Hessian that is positive definite.
2. $f$ is **admissible** — $f \rightarrow 1$ uniformly on the boundary $\partial \mathcal{F}$.
3. $f$ is a **Morse function** — all critical points are non-degenerate.
4. $\mathcal{F}$ is a **navigable sphere world** with respect to $q_{\text{goal}}$ — guaranteeing that the only critical point in $\mathcal{F}$ is the goal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Navigation Functions Matter (KTU High-Yield Point):**
> Standard potential fields suffer from **local minima** — trap configurations where $\nabla U = 0$ but $q \neq q_{\text{goal}}$. Navigation functions **provably eliminate** all local minima, yielding a *complete* reactive planner. This property makes them a board-favourite topic.

## 1.3 The Problem Statement (KTU Module 3 Focus)

The **Problem Statement** for Motion in a Potential Field / Navigation Function is formally stated as:

> Given:
> - A robot with configuration space $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ (often $\mathbb{R}^2$ or $\mathbb{R}^3$ for a point robot).
> - A bounded, simply connected, spherical free space $\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{W} \setminus \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} \text{int}(\mathcal{O}_i)$, where $\mathcal{W}$ is the workspace and $\mathcal{O}_i$ are disjoint spherical obstacles.
> - A start configuration $q_{\text{start}} \in \mathcal{F}$ and a goal configuration $q_{\text{goal}} \in \mathcal{F}$.
>
> **Find:** A potential function $U : \mathcal{F} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ and a feedback control law
> $$\dot{q} = -\nabla U(q)$$
> such that the resulting trajectory $q(t)$ is **collision-free**, **converges to $q_{\text{goal}}$**, and **admits no local minima** other than the goal.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Hilly Landscape

Imagine placing a marble on a carefully sculpted terrain inside a large bowl:

| Landscape Feature | Robotic Equivalent |
|-------------------|---------------------|
| The deepest valley of the bowl | The goal configuration $q_{\text{goal}}$ |
| Tall, steep pillars rising from the floor | Obstacles $\mathcal{O}_i$ (rejected as infinitely high) |
| The smooth, gently-sloping floor | Free space $\mathcal{F}$ |
| The rim of the bowl | Workspace boundary $\partial \mathcal{W}$ |
| Marble's natural rolling direction ($-\nabla U$) | Robot's velocity vector $\dot{q}$ |

The marble is released from $q_{\text{start}}$ and **always rolls downhill**. In a *well-designed* landscape (a **navigation function**), there is only one valley — the goal. In a *naively designed* landscape (classical **Khatib potential**), secondary dips and plateaus can trap the marble forever — these are the dreaded **local minima**.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Builder:** Think of the classical artificial potential as a child's playmat with random bumps. The navigation function is a *sculptor's masterwork* — mathematically guaranteed to channel the marble into exactly one basin.

## 1.5 Standard Performance Metrics

- **Convergence time** $t_f$ such that $\|q(t_f) - q_{\text{goal}}\| < \varepsilon$.
- **Path length** $\ell = \int_0^{t_f} \|\dot{q}(t)\| \, dt$.
- **Clearance** $\rho_{\min} = \min_{t} \min_i \rho(q(t), \mathcal{O}_i)$.
- **Completeness:** *Classical APF is NOT complete* (local minima problem); *Navigation functions ARE complete* on sphere worlds.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Standard Components of a Potential Field

The classical **Khatib Artificial Potential Field (APF)** decomposes the total potential additively:

$$U_{\text{total}}(q) = U_{\text{att}}(q) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} U_{\text{rep}_i}(q)$$

### 2.1.1 Attractive Potential (Goal Confinement)

The most common quadratic-cone attractive potential is:

$$U_{\text{att}}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \, k_a \, \Vert q - q_{\text{goal}} \Vert^{2}$$

This produces a **paraboloid of revolution** centred at $q_{\text{goal}}$, with force:

$$F_{\text{att}}(q) = -\nabla U_{\text{att}}(q) = -k_a (q - q_{\text{goal}})$$

- **Why it works:** It is a strictly convex function with a *unique* global minimum at $q_{\text{goal}}$.
- **Engineering trade-off:** A larger $k_a$ yields a steeper basin (faster convergence) but at the cost of larger actuator torques and possible oscillations near the goal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Behaviour:** As $\Vert q - q_{\text{goal}} \Vert \rightarrow \infty$, the quadratic $U_{\text{att}}$ grows without bound, which is undesirable in an unbounded workspace. A **conic** alternative $U_{\text{att}} = k_a \Vert q - q_{\text{goal}} \Vert$ is used when workspace is bounded.

### 2.1.2 Repulsive Potential (Obstacle Avoidance)

The standard FIKR (Far-Infra-Red / Fixed-Radius) repulsive potential is:

$$U_{\text{rep}_i}(q) = 
\begin{cases}
\tfrac{1}{2} \, k_r \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(q, \mathcal{O}_i)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2} & \text{if } \rho(q, \mathcal{O}_i) \leq \rho_0 \\[2mm]
0 & \text{if } \rho(q, \mathcal{O}_i) > \rho_0
\end{cases}$$

where $\rho(q, \mathcal{O}_i) = \min_{p \in \mathcal{O}_i} \Vert q - p \Vert$ is the *signed distance* from $q$ to obstacle $i$, and $\rho_0 > 0$ is the **influence radius** (zone of repulsion).

The corresponding repulsive force magnitude:

$$F_{\text{rep}_i}(q) = -k_r \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^{2}} \cdot \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial q}, \quad \rho \leq \rho_0$$

> [!CAUTION]
> **Local Minima Problem:** When the attractive basin "wins" against the repulsive hill too early, the robot stalls at a point $q^*$ where $F_{\text{att}}(q^*) + \sum F_{\text{rep}_i}(q^*) = 0$ but $q^* \neq q_{\text{goal}}$. This is the **fundamental flaw** of classical APF and the primary motivation for navigation functions.

## 2.2 The Koditschek–Rimon Navigation Function

For a workspace composed of $m$ disjoint **spherical obstacles** $\mathcal{O}_i$ of radius $r_i$ centred at $c_i$, and a goal $q_{\text{goal}}$ chosen as the origin of a "sphere world" (a ball $\mathcal{W}$ of radius $R$ centred at origin), the canonical navigation function is:

$$\varphi(q) = \frac{\Vert q - q_{\text{goal}} \Vert^{2}}{\left[ \Vert q - q_{\text{goal}} \Vert^{2k} + \beta(q) \right]^{1/k}}$$

with the **obstacle function**:

$$\beta(q) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \Vert q - c_i \Vert^{2} - \delta^{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Constraint (Exam-Critical):** The parameter $k$ must be chosen **large enough** so that $\varphi$ becomes a navigation function on the sphere world $\mathcal{F}$. Koditschek and Rimon proved that for a sphere world with $m$ obstacles, choosing
> $$k \geq \frac{m+1}{m}$$
> guarantees no spurious local minima.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|--------|---------|----------------------|
| $k_a$ | Attractive gain | $0.5$ – $5.0$ (N/m or N·m/rad) |
| $k_r$ | Repulsive gain | $1.0$ – $20.0$ |
| $\rho_0$ | Influence radius | $0.5$ – $3.0$ m |
| $\delta$ | Tunability parameter of $\beta(q)$ | $\in (0, r_{\min})$ |
| $k$ | Exponent in navigation function | $\geq (m+1)/m$ |
| $\mathcal{F}$ | Free configuration space | $C^2$-smooth manifold |
| $m$ | Number of obstacles | $1, 2, \ldots$ |
| $\nabla U$ | Generalized force / control input | $F = -\nabla U$ |
| $H(q)$ | Hessian of $\varphi$ at $q$ | $n \times n$ symmetric matrix |
| $\lambda_i(H)$ | Eigenvalues of Hessian | $\lambda_i \neq 0$ at every critical point (Morse) |

## 2.4 Why the Negative Gradient Works (Lyapunov Stability)

Let $V(q) = U(q) \geq 0$ be a continuously differentiable, positive-definite scalar function with $V(q_{\text{goal}}) = 0$. The robot dynamics under the gradient-descent law yield:

$$\dot{V}(q) = \nabla U(q)^{\top} \dot{q} = \nabla U(q)^{\top} (-\nabla U(q)) = - \Vert \nabla U(q) \Vert^{2} \leq 0$$

Thus $V$ is a **Lyapunov function**. By LaSalle's invariance principle, every trajectory converges to the largest invariant set where $\dot{V} = 0$, i.e., where $\nabla U = 0$ — the set of critical points. The success of the planner therefore **depends entirely on the topology of the critical-point set** of $U$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:**
> - **Mobile robots** (Roomba, AGVs in warehouses): APF is the workhorse of low-cost reactive navigation.
> - **Robotic manipulators**: Used in on-line collision avoidance during teleoperation.
> - **Autonomous vehicles**: Lane-keeping as attractive potential, pedestrians as repulsive sources.
> - **Drone swarms**: Inter-agent repulsion + target attraction = flocking.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Attractive Force from a Quadratic Potential

**Goal:** Derive $F_{\text{att}} = -k_a (q - q_{\text{goal}})$ from $U_{\text{att}}$.

**Step 1 — Define the potential.** The simplest admissible (i.e., $\nabla U = 0$ only at $q_{\text{goal}}$) attractive potential that vanishes at the goal is the squared Euclidean distance, scaled by $k_a > 0$:

$$U_{\text{att}}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_a \, (q - q_{\text{goal}})^{\top} (q - q_{\text{goal}}) = \frac{1}{2} k_a \sum_{j=1}^{n} (q_j - q_{j,\text{goal}})^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Take the gradient with respect to $q$.** Differentiating the quadratic form:

$$\frac{\partial U_{\text{att}}}{\partial q_j} = \frac{1}{2} k_a \cdot 2 (q_j - q_{j,\text{goal}}) = k_a (q_j - q_{j,\text{goal}})$$

**Step 3 — Assemble in vector form.**

$$\nabla_q U_{\text{att}} = k_a (q - q_{\text{goal}})$$

**Step 4 — Apply the gradient-descent law.** The force (or velocity, depending on the formulation) is the negative gradient:

$$F_{\text{att}}(q) = -\nabla U_{\text{att}}(q) = -k_a (q - q_{\text{goal}})$$

**Step 5 — Verify the minimum.** The Hessian is $H_{\text{att}} = k_a I_n$, which is **positive definite** for any $k_a > 0$. Hence $U_{\text{att}}$ is strictly convex and $q_{\text{goal}}$ is the unique global minimum. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Repulsive Force from a FIKR Potential

**Goal:** Derive the repulsive force for a circular obstacle of radius $r$ centred at $c$, given the FIKR potential.

**Step 1 — Express the distance.**

$$\rho(q) = \Vert q - c \Vert - r$$

Note: this is the **signed distance** from the robot (point) to the boundary of the obstacle.

**Step 2 — Define the potential (only active in the influence zone).**

$$U_{\text{rep}}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_r \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2}, \quad \rho(q) \leq \rho_0$$

**Step 3 — Apply the chain rule.** Let $g(\rho) = \frac{1}{2} k_r \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2}$. Then:

$$\nabla U_{\text{rep}} = \frac{\partial g}{\partial \rho} \cdot \nabla \rho$$

**Step 4 — Compute the inner derivative.**

$$\frac{\partial g}{\partial \rho} = \frac{1}{2} k_r \cdot 2 \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{\rho^{2}} \right) = -k_r \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the spatial gradient of the distance.**

$$\nabla \rho = \frac{\partial}{\partial q} \Vert q - c \Vert = \frac{q - c}{\Vert q - c \Vert} = \hat{n}$$

where $\hat{n}$ is the **unit outward normal** pointing from the obstacle centre to the robot.

**Step 6 — Assemble the repulsive force.**

$$F_{\text{rep}}(q) = -\nabla U_{\text{rep}} = k_r \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^{2}} \cdot \frac{q - c}{\Vert q - c \Vert}, \quad \rho \leq \rho_0$$

**Step 7 — Check limiting behaviour.**

- As $\rho \rightarrow 0^{+}$ (collision imminent): $\frac{1}{\rho} \to \infty$, hence $\Vert F_{\text{rep}} \Vert \to \infty$. **The robot is pushed away violently** — exactly the desired safety property.
- As $\rho \rightarrow \rho_0$ (boundary of influence): $F_{\text{rep}} \to 0$, $C^1$-continuously. The potential and force vanish smoothly, ensuring the function is differentiable throughout the influence region. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Koditschek–Rimon Navigation Function

**Goal:** Show that the choice of $\varphi(q) = \dfrac{\Vert q \Vert^{2}}{\left[ \Vert q \Vert^{2k} + \beta(q) \right]^{1/k}}$ (with $q_{\text{goal}} = 0$) yields the four required properties of a navigation function, given a sphere world.

**Step 1 — Set up the sphere world.** Let the workspace be the ball $\mathcal{W} = \{q : \Vert q \Vert \leq R\}$. Inside it, place $m$ disjoint open balls $\mathcal{O}_i = \{q : \Vert q - c_i \Vert \leq r_i\}$. The free space is $\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{W} \setminus \bigcup_i \mathcal{O}_i$.

**Step 2 — Construct the obstacle function.**

$$\beta(q) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \Vert q - c_i \Vert^{2} - \delta^{2}$$

The first term vanishes on the boundary $\partial \mathcal{O}_i$ and is positive outside each obstacle. The parameter $\delta > 0$ ensures $\beta(q) < 0$ inside every obstacle and $\beta(q) \geq 0$ outside, with the zero level set lying *just inside* the obstacle boundaries.

**Step 3 — Verify the polar property at the goal.** At $q = 0$ (the chosen goal):

- Numerator $\Vert 0 \Vert^{2} = 0$, so $\varphi(0) = 0$.
- The numerator's gradient $\nabla \Vert q \Vert^{2} = 2q$ vanishes at $q = 0$.
- The Hessian of $\varphi$ at $q = 0$ is **positive definite** (Koditschek–Rimon Theorem 4.1), confirming a strict local minimum. ✓

**Step 4 — Verify admissibility on the boundary.** On the workspace boundary $\partial \mathcal{W}$, $\Vert q \Vert = R$. With appropriate normalization, $\varphi(q) \to 1$ as $q \to \partial \mathcal{W}$. On the obstacle boundary $\partial \mathcal{O}_i$, $\beta(q) = 0$, reducing $\varphi$ to $\Vert q \Vert^{2 - 2/k}$, which is bounded and $C^{1}$ (for $k \geq 1$). ✓

**Step 5 — Verify the Morse property.** The function $\varphi$ is constructed as a ratio of $C^{\infty}$ functions whose denominator never vanishes on $\mathcal{F}$. Hence $\varphi$ is $C^{\infty}$ on $\mathcal{F}$, and all critical points are non-degenerate (the Hessian is invertible at every critical point). ✓

**Step 6 — Verify uniqueness of the critical point.** By the **Koditschek–Rimon Sphere World Theorem**, for $k \geq (m+1)/m$, the only critical point of $\varphi$ in $\mathcal{F}$ is the goal $q_{\text{goal}} = 0$. The intuitive reason: $\beta(q) \to 0$ near each obstacle, making $\varphi$ tend to a constant (or zero) on each $\partial \mathcal{O}_i$, eliminating spurious saddles/minima in the interior. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Total Field Properties at a Glance

| Property | Classical APF (Khatib) | Navigation Function (Koditschek–Rimon) |
|----------|------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| $C^2$ smoothness | Only at $\rho = \rho_0$ | Globally $C^{\infty}$ on $\mathcal{F}$ |
| Local minima other than goal | **Yes — major flaw** | **No — provably absent on sphere world** |
| Completeness | Not complete in general | Complete on star-shaped / sphere worlds |
| Computational cost | $O(m)$ per evaluation | $O(m)$ per evaluation |
| Obstacle shape restriction | Arbitrary polygons (with care) | **Spheres only** in canonical form |
| Convergence guarantee | Heuristic, not guaranteed | **Mathematically proven** |
| Real-time suitability | Excellent | Excellent (closed-form gradient) |
| Implementation complexity | Low | Moderate (requires tuning of $k$, $\delta$) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an APF Planner

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **closed-loop control architecture** of a potential-field-based motion planner, from sensor acquisition to actuator command.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Robot Pose q from Encoders and IMU] --> B1[Obstacle Map Builder]
    B1 --> C1[Compute Distances rho_i to each obstacle]
    C1 --> D1[Attractive Potential U_att]
    C1 --> D2[Repulsive Potentials U_rep_i for i = 1 to m]
    D1 --> E1[Total Potential U_total]
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Gradient Computation grad U_total]
    F1 --> G1[Control Law F equals minus grad U]
    G1 --> H1[Velocity Command to Motor Drivers]
    H1 --> A1
    style A1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B
    style E1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style G1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **outer loop** (left side) senses the robot's current pose and the obstacle geometry.
- The **middle column** computes the *two families* of potentials in parallel.
- The **right column** closes the loop by converting the gradient into a velocity command.

## 4.2 Decision Flow for a Navigation Function Planner

The following diagram captures the *algorithmic logic* (rendered as a flowchart rather than executable code, per KTU non-CS engineering constraints) of a Koditschek–Rimon navigation function controller.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Receive current configuration q_t] --> S2[Compute beta of q_t = product of distances squared minus delta squared]
    S2 --> S3{Is q_t on obstacle boundary or workspace boundary?}
    S3 -->|Yes| S4[Clamp or apply boundary condition phi = 1]
    S3 -->|No| S5[Compute phi of q_t = norm q to goal squared over bracket term to power 1 slash k]
    S5 --> S6[Compute gradient grad phi analytically or numerically]
    S6 --> S7[Set velocity v_t equals minus grad phi]
    S4 --> S8[Apply safety stop or boundary velocity]
    S7 --> S9{Has q_t converged to q_goal within epsilon?}
    S8 --> S9
    S9 -->|Yes| S10[Terminate: Goal Reached]
    S9 -->|No| S11[Step to q_t plus 1 = q_t plus delta t times v_t]
    S11 --> S1
    style S5 fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#006400
    style S7 fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#8B0000
    style S10 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Potential Landscape

The diagram below depicts the **qualitative shape** of the potential landscape: a deep central valley (goal), surrounded by a smooth plateau, with **conical repulsive peaks** rising over each obstacle.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Workspace Boundary: phi to 1]
    L2[Plateau Region: phi approximately 0.4 to 0.6]
    L3[Goal Basin: phi = 0 at q_goal]
    L4[Repulsive Peak over Obstacle 1: phi to 1]
    L5[Repulsive Peak over Obstacle 2: phi to 1]
    L6[Free Space: Smoothly decreasing phi toward goal]
    L3 --> L6
    L6 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
    L4 -. blocks .-> L6
    L5 -. blocks .-> L6
    style L3 fill:#00FA9A,stroke:#006400
    style L1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000
    style L4 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000
    style L5 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000
```

**Interpretation of arrows:**
- The *solid downward* arrows from L3 to L1 represent the **gradient flow** from goal to boundary.
- The *dotted blocking* arrows from L4 and L5 represent **forbidden trajectories** that the repulsion redirects around the obstacles.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper, Q1(a)]**

**Q1(a).** *Define an artificial potential field used in robot motion planning. State its two main components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An artificial potential field is a scalar function $U : \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ defined over the robot's free configuration space, whose negative gradient $-\nabla U$ is used as a control input to drive the robot. Its two main components are:
1. **Attractive potential** $U_{\text{att}}$ — minimum at the goal $q_{\text{goal}}$; pulls the robot towards it.
2. **Repulsive potential** $U_{\text{rep}}$ — grows large near obstacle boundaries; pushes the robot away to ensure collision avoidance.

The total potential is $U_{\text{total}} = U_{\text{att}} + \sum_{i=1}^{m} U_{\text{rep}_i}$. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper, Q1(b)]**

**Q1(b).** *What is a navigation function? Why is it superior to the classical artificial potential field?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **navigation function** is a $C^2$-smooth, polar, admissible Morse function $f : \mathcal{F} \rightarrow [0, 1]$ on a sphere-world free space, whose unique critical point is the goal $q_{\text{goal}}$. It is superior because:
- The classical APF can exhibit **spurious local minima** (trap configurations) where $\nabla U = 0$ but $q \neq q_{\text{goal}}$, causing the robot to stall.
- A navigation function is **provably free of local minima** (under the Koditschek–Rimon conditions), guaranteeing convergence to the goal from any initial configuration. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper, Q11 Module 3]**

### OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Formulate the problem statement for motion planning in a potential field. Clearly define the workspace, free space, obstacles, the potential function, and the control law. Mention the boundary conditions required for the planner to be well-posed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the repulsive force from a single circular obstacle of radius $r$ centred at $c$, using the FIKR (Fixed Influence Radius) potential
$$U_{\text{rep}}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_r \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2}, \quad \rho \leq \rho_0$$
and verify that the force magnitude tends to infinity as the robot approaches the obstacle boundary. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Problem Formulation [7 marks]:**

Let $\mathcal{W} \subset \mathbb{R}^{n}$ be a bounded, simply connected workspace. The free configuration space for a point robot is

$$\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{W} \setminus \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} \text{int}(\mathcal{O}_i)$$

where $\mathcal{O}_i$ are $m$ closed, pairwise-disjoint obstacle sets with $C^2$-smooth boundaries.

**Boundary conditions on the potential:**

1. $U(q) \to 0$ (or some finite constant) at the goal $q_{\text{goal}} \in \mathcal{F}$ — the potential attains its **global minimum** there.
2. $U(q) \to +\infty$ as $q \to \partial \mathcal{O}_i$ for any $i$ — the potential acts as an **infinite barrier** at obstacle boundaries.
3. $U(q) \to U_{\max}$ as $q \to \partial \mathcal{W}$ — finite and well-defined on the workspace boundary (admissibility).

**Control law:** Under the gradient-descent feedback, the robot's velocity (or force, depending on dynamics) is

$$\dot{q} = -K \nabla U(q)$$

where $K > 0$ is a diagonal gain matrix. The closed-loop dynamics give $\dot{U} = -\nabla U^{\top} K \nabla U \leq 0$, confirming Lyapunov stability. **The well-posedness of the planner therefore reduces to ensuring that $U$ has no spurious critical points** — precisely the property guaranteed by navigation functions on sphere worlds. **[7 marks — full credit breakdown: 2 for space definitions, 2 for boundary conditions, 3 for control law and Lyapunov argument]**

**Part (b) — Repulsive Force Derivation [7 marks]:**

**Step 1.** Define the signed distance from $q$ to obstacle boundary:

$$\rho(q) = \Vert q - c \Vert - r$$

**Step 2.** Apply the chain rule on $U_{\text{rep}}$:

$$\nabla U_{\text{rep}} = \frac{\partial U_{\text{rep}}}{\partial \rho} \cdot \nabla \rho$$

**Step 3.** Compute the inner derivative:

$$\frac{\partial U_{\text{rep}}}{\partial \rho} = \frac{1}{2} k_r \cdot 2 \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{\rho^{2}} \right) = -k_r \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^{2}}$$

**Step 4.** Compute $\nabla \rho$:

$$\nabla \rho = \nabla \Vert q - c \Vert = \frac{q - c}{\Vert q - c \Vert}$$

**Step 5.** Assemble the repulsive force:

$$F_{\text{rep}}(q) = -\nabla U_{\text{rep}} = k_r \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^{2}} \cdot \frac{q - c}{\Vert q - c \Vert}, \quad \rho \leq \rho_0$$

**Step 6 — Limiting behaviour verification.** As $\rho \to 0^{+}$, we have $\frac{1}{\rho} \to \infty$, so the multiplicative factor $\left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^{2}} \to \infty$. The direction $\frac{q - c}{\Vert q - c \Vert}$ is bounded (unit norm). Hence

$$\lim_{\rho \to 0^{+}} \Vert F_{\text{rep}}(q) \Vert = \infty$$

confirming that the robot experiences an unbounded repulsive push as it approaches the obstacle boundary — a critical safety property. **[7 marks — full credit breakdown: 1 for distance, 1 for chain rule setup, 2 for derivative, 1 for $\nabla \rho$, 1 for assembly, 1 for limit verification]**

---

### OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Define a **navigation function** rigorously. State the four properties that a scalar field $f : \mathcal{F} \rightarrow [0, 1]$ must satisfy to be called a navigation function. Explain the role of the tuning parameter $k$ in the Koditschek–Rimon construction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 2D sphere world with $m = 3$ circular obstacles and goal at the origin, write the Koditschek–Rimon navigation function and explain why choosing $k \geq \frac{m+1}{m} = \frac{4}{3}$ guarantees no local minima in the free space. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Definition and Properties [7 marks]:**

A **navigation function** $f : \mathcal{F} \to [0, 1]$ on a compact, connected free space $\mathcal{F} \subset \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is a $C^2$-smooth function with the following four properties:

1. **Polar at the goal** — $f(q_{\text{goal}}) = 0$ and $\nabla f(q_{\text{goal}}) = 0$, with positive-definite Hessian at $q_{\text{goal}}$. The goal is the unique global minimum.
2. **Admissibility** — $f(q) \to 1$ uniformly as $q$ approaches the boundary $\partial \mathcal{F} = \partial \mathcal{W} \cup \bigcup_i \partial \mathcal{O}_i$. No trajectory can "escape" the free space.
3. **Morse property** — All critical points of $f$ are non-degenerate (the Hessian is invertible at every critical point). This excludes flat regions.
4. **Navigability / Unique critical point** — On a *sphere world* (a ball with disjoint spherical holes), the goal is the **only** critical point in the interior of $\mathcal{F}$. All other critical points lie on the boundary (where the flow terminates safely).

**Role of $k$:** The exponent $k$ in the denominator of

$$f(q) = \frac{\Vert q \Vert^{2}}{[\Vert q \Vert^{2k} + \beta(q)]^{1/k}}$$

controls the *sharpness* of the navigation function near obstacle boundaries. As $k \to \infty$, the term $\Vert q \Vert^{2k} + \beta(q)$ is dominated by the larger of its summands, and $f$ approaches an idealized "bang-bang" potential: $f \to 0$ near the goal and $f \to 1$ elsewhere. Koditschek and Rimon proved that $k \geq (m+1)/m$ is **sufficient** to suppress spurious critical points. **[7 marks — full credit breakdown: 2 for definition, 1 each for four properties, 1 for role of $k$]**

**Part (b) — Construction and Sufficiency of $k$ [7 marks]:**

For $m = 3$ circular obstacles centred at $c_1, c_2, c_3 \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$ with the goal at the origin, the Koditschek–Rimon navigation function is:

$$\varphi(q) = \frac{\Vert q \Vert^{2}}{\left[ \Vert q \Vert^{2k} + \beta(q) \right]^{1/k}}, \quad q \in \mathcal{F} \subset \mathbb{R}^{2}$$

with the obstacle function:

$$\beta(q) = \Vert q - c_1 \Vert^{2} \cdot \Vert q - c_2 \Vert^{2} \cdot \Vert q - c_3 \Vert^{2} - \delta^{2}$$

and the threshold $k \geq \frac{m+1}{m} = \frac{4}{3}$.

**Why this guarantees no local minima:**

- The numerator $\Vert q \Vert^{2}$ is **strictly convex** and vanishes only at the goal.
- The denominator $\left[ \Vert q \Vert^{2k} + \beta(q) \right]^{1/k}$ is **strictly positive** throughout $\mathcal{F}$ (by construction, since $\beta(q) \geq 0$ outside the obstacles).
- Setting the gradient $\nabla \varphi = 0$ and clearing the positive denominator yields a polynomial critical-point equation whose **degree depends on $m$ and $k$**.
- Koditschek and Rimon showed that the *number of real roots* of this equation in $\mathcal{F}$ drops to **exactly one** (the goal) precisely when $k \geq (m+1)/m$. For $k < (m+1)/m$, additional roots appear as spurious local minima.
- Geometrically: a larger $k$ "flattens" the function near the workspace boundary, forcing the gradient field to be a *star-shaped* flow that channels every trajectory into the goal basin. **[7 marks — full credit breakdown: 2 for writing $\varphi$, 2 for writing $\beta$, 3 for sufficiency argument]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the signed distance definition** — Writing $\rho = \Vert q - c \Vert$ instead of $\rho = \Vert q - c \Vert - r$ is a common slip; the dimension of $\rho$ becomes inconsistent, and the **repulsive force direction is wrong** (no longer purely normal). Lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Not verifying the limit $\rho \to 0$** — The unbounded repulsive force is the *defining safety property* of the FIKR potential; omitting its verification costs at least 1 mark on Part B.
> 3. **Writing $k > 1$ instead of $k \geq (m+1)/m$** — The lower bound is **explicitly dependent on $m$**. Writing $k > 1$ generically is too weak; it is the threshold $k = (m+1)/m$ that matters. Lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the chain rule justification** — Many students jump from $U_{\text{rep}}$ to the final $F_{\text{rep}}$ without explicitly invoking $\nabla U = (\partial U / \partial \rho) \nabla \rho$. The examiner expects at least one line of chain rule. Lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing "admissible" with "polar"** — These are *two distinct* properties of a navigation function. Polar = behaviour at goal; Admissible = behaviour at boundary. Confusing them costs 1 mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 (Potential Field & Navigation Function):**

- **The problem statement** demands three ingredients: a smooth potential $U$, a control law $\dot{q} = -\nabla U$, and a guarantee of convergence to $q_{\text{goal}}$ from any $q_{\text{start}} \in \mathcal{F}$.
- **Classical APF** = attractive + repulsive potential, but **suffers from local minima** (e.g., equidistant from goal and obstacle, or narrow passages).
- **Navigation function** is a *carefully designed* potential that is $C^2$-smooth, polar at the goal, admissible on the boundary, and Morse.
- The **Koditschek–Rimon construction** $\varphi(q) = \Vert q \Vert^{2} / \left[ \Vert q \Vert^{2k} + \beta(q) \right]^{1/k}$ works on **sphere worlds** (workspace = ball, obstacles = disjoint spherical holes).
- The **obstacle function** is $\beta(q) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \Vert q - c_i \Vert^{2} - \delta^{2}$ with $\delta > 0$ ensuring $\beta < 0$ inside each obstacle.
- The **tuning parameter** $k \geq (m+1)/m$ is **necessary and sufficient** for the sphere world to be a navigable domain.
- **Attractive force** from quadratic $U_{\text{att}} = \frac{1}{2} k_a \Vert q - q_{\text{goal}} \Vert^{2}$ is $F_{\text{att}} = -k_a(q - q_{\text{goal}})$.
- **Repulsive force** from FIKR $U_{\text{rep}} = \frac{1}{2} k_r \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2}$ is $F_{\text{rep}} = k_r \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^{2}} \hat{n}$ for $\rho \leq \rho_0$.
- **Lyapunov argument:** $\dot{U} = -\Vert \nabla U \Vert^{2} \leq 0$ guarantees *non-increase* of the potential, but **convergence** requires the **absence of non-goal critical points** — the central engineering insight.
- **High-yield exam tip:** Whenever the question asks "prove that the planner converges," the answer must invoke BOTH the Lyapunov non-increase AND the Morse/topological property of the potential.
- **Real-world uses:** AGVs, manipulator obstacle avoidance, drone swarms, lane-keeping in autonomous vehicles.
- **Limitation to remember:** Navigation functions in their original form require **spherical obstacles**; extensions to star-shaped and convex obstacles exist but introduce more complex parameter tuning.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Configuration Space
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Configuration Space # 📘 CONFIGURATION SPACE (C-SPACE) — A Complete KTU Board-Ready Treatment ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Configuration Space (C-space)** is the **mathematical space of all possible configurations** that a robot can attain. Every single point in the C-space corresponds to one and only one unique pose (position + orientation) of the robot, and conversely, every configuration the robot can achieve is represented by exactly one point in this space. Let the robot $R$ be a rigid (or articulated) body moving in the **workspace** $W \subset \mathbb{R}^{2}$ (planar) or $\mathbb{R}^{3}$ (spatial). A **configuration** $q$ of $R$ is a minimal set of parameters that completely specifies the location of every point of $R$ in $W$. The C-space is then the set: $$ \mathcal{C} = \{\, q \mid q \text{ is a valid configuration of the robot } R \,\} $$ For a robot with $n$ **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**, the C-space is a smooth manifold of dimension $n$, locally homeomorphic to $\mathbb{R}^{n}$. ### 1.1.1 Decomposition of the C-space The C-space is partitioned into two disjoint regions: $$ \mathcal{C} \;=\; \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \;\cup\; \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} $$ where: - **$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$** — The set of all configurations where the robot does **not collide** with any obstacle. This is the region in which motion planning is permitted. - **$\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$** — The **Configuration-Space Obstacle**, defined formally as the set of configurations in which the robot intersects at least one workspace obstacle $O$: $$ \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \{\, q \in \mathcal{C} \mid R(q) \cap O \neq \emptyset \,\} $$ > [!NOTE] > The motion planning problem reduces to: **Find a continuous path $\tau : [0,1] \to \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ such that $\tau(0) = q_{\text{start}}$ and $\tau(1) = q_{\text{goal}}$.** ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🌍 Imagine you are navigating a **car in a crowded parking lot** (parallel parking problem). The car's state is fully described by: 1. Its $(x, y)$ position on the asphalt, 2. Its heading angle $\theta$ (the way the steering wheels point). Each parking spot you might aim for, with a particular steering angle, is **one single point** in a 3-D world called the *configuration space*. The cars already parked are not "obstacles" in this 3-D world — they have been **transformed into complex curved volumes** (a Honda sedan looks like a long banana-shaped forbidden region; a truck looks like a thick box-shaped forbidden region). Your task is no longer to "dodge cars on a 2-D map" but to **slide a single point** (your car) through a 3-D "maze" without entering the curved forbidden volumes. | Workspace Thinking | C-space Thinking | |---|---| | Robot is a complex shape, obstacles are fixed | Robot is a **single point**, obstacles are **grown/morphed** | | Geometry is hard to compute | Geometry is cleaner (often polygons/algebraic sets) | | Steering constraints are implicit | Steering constraints become non-holonomic in C-space | > [!TIP] > **The grand trick of motion planning:** Reduce a hard geometric problem (avoiding collisions with a complex robot) into a much simpler point-motion problem in a higher-dimensional space. The cost is increased dimension; the benefit is conceptual and algorithmic uniformity. ## 1.3 Key Physical & Mathematical Quantities (Bolded Standard Metrics) - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** — Number of independent parameters required to fully specify a configuration. Denoted $n$ or $d$. - **Generalized coordinates** $q = (q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n)$ — Independent variables parameterizing the C-space. - **Configuration** $q$ — A specific instantiation of generalized coordinates. - **Workspace dimension** $m$ — Typically $2$ (planar) or $3$ (spatial). - **C-space dimension** $n \geq m$ in general, $n = m$ for a free-flying rigid body in $m$-D, $n = 6$ for a free-flying rigid body in 3-D. # 📘 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Robot's Configuration A configuration $q$ encodes **two distinct kinds of information**: 1. **Position / Translation** — Where the robot is located in the workspace. 2. **Orientation / Rotation** — How the robot is rotated/aligned. Mathematically, for a free-flying rigid body in 3-D: $$ q \;=\; (p,\, R), \qquad p \in \mathbb{R}^{3}, \quad R \in SO(3) $$ The full configuration space is the **Special Euclidean group**: $$ SE(3) \;=\; \mathbb{R}^{3} \times SO(3) $$ where $SO(3)$ is the group of $3\times 3$ rotation matrices satisfying $R^{T}R = I$ and $\det R = +1$. ## 2.2 Constructing the C-space — Step-by-Step Logic The construction proceeds in well-defined logical stages: - **Step 1 — Identify Generalized Coordinates.** Choose a minimal set of independent parameters $(q_1, \ldots, q_n)$ that uniquely determine every point on the robot. The minimum number of such parameters is the **DOF**. - **Step 2 — Determine the Topology.** Identify the underlying manifold: - Revolute joint $\Rightarrow$ circle $S^{1}$. - Prismatic joint $\Rightarrow$ line $\mathbb{R}$. - $n$ revolute joints chained $\Rightarrow$ $n$-torus $T^{n} = S^{1} \times S^{1} \times \cdots \times S^{1}$. - Floating base in 3-D $\Rightarrow$ $SE(3) = \mathbb{R}^{3} \times SO(3)$. - **Step 3 — Map Workspace Obstacles into C-space.** For each workspace obstacle $O_i$, define its C-space image: $$ \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}, i} = \{\, q \in \mathcal{C} \mid R(q) \cap O_i \neq \emptyset \,\} $$ Then the total C-obstacle is the union: $$ \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}, i} $$ - **Step 4 — Isolate the Free Space.** $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$. Planning happens here. - **Step 5 — Apply Kinematic Constraints (if any).** For non-holonomic systems (e.g., a car), additional velocity-level constraints $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$ further restrict feasible motions, though $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ itself is unaltered. ## 2.3 Why and How — The C-space Approach Wins | Why use C-space? | How it helps | |---|---| | **Uniform representation** | A rigid polygonal robot, an articulated arm, a mobile base — all become a single "point" moving in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. | | **Topology-aware path search** | Sampling-based planners (RRT, PRM) only need to test for $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ membership. | | **Holonomic/navigation-function methods** | Potential fields can be designed directly on $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. | | **Decouples geometry from shape** | The robot's intrinsic shape is encoded once and "grown" into the obstacles. | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋 | # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Domain / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Configuration | $q = (q_1, \ldots, q_n)$ | Dimensionless angles / meters | | 2 | DOF of planar rigid body | $n = 3$ | — | | 3 | DOF of spatial rigid body | $n = 6$ | — | | 4 | Grübler's formula (planar) | $n = 3(L-1) -
