2J_1 - J_2$ | $L$ = links, $J_1$ = 1-DOF joints, $J_2$ = 2-DOF joints | | 5 | Grübler's formula (spatial) | $n = 6(L-1) - 5J_1 - 4J_2 - 3J_3 - 2J_4 - J_5$ | $J_i$ = joints with $i$ DOF | | 6 | C-obstacle | $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \{q \mid R(q) \cap O \neq \emptyset\}$ | Subset of $\mathcal{C}$ | | 7 | Free space | $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ | Open subset (usually) | | 8 | Minkowski sum (growing obstacles) | $O \oplus (-R_0) = \{p - r \mid p \in O, r \in R_0\}$ | $R_0$ = robot reference shape | | 9 | 2-D rigid body position | $(x, y, \theta) \in SE(2) = \mathbb{R}^{2} \times S^{1}$ | $x, y$ in m, $\theta$ in rad | | 10 | Floating spatial body | $(x, y, z, \text{roll}, \text{pitch}, \text{yaw}) \in SE(3)$ | $x,y,z$ in m, angles in rad | | 11 | Torus C-space (n-revolute chain) | $\mathcal{C} = S^{1} \times \cdots \times S^{1} = T^{n}$ | $n$-dimensional manifold | | 12 | Path existence condition | $\tau(s) \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}, \forall s \in [0,1]$ | Continuous path | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** The C-space formulation is the *backbone* of virtually every modern motion planner used in industry today — from autonomous vehicles (planning in $SE(2)$ with non-holonomic constraints) to surgical robots (planning in high-DOF $\mathcal{C}$ with anatomical obstacles). # 📘 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — DOF of a 2-Link Planar Manipulator **Problem:** A planar manipulator has two revolute joints in series (shoulder and elbow) and moves freely in a 2-D plane. Compute its degrees of freedom and identify its C-space topology. **Step 1 — Identify the moving links.** The robot has $L = 3$ links if we count the **ground link** (link 0), the upper arm (link 1), and the forearm (link 2). Only the links beyond the ground contribute to mobility in Grübler's formula. **Step 2 — Apply Grübler's formula for planar mechanisms.** $$ n = 3(L - 1) - 2J_1 - J_2 $$ where $L = 3$ (including ground), $J_1 = 2$ (two revolute joints, each contributing 1 DOF), $J_2 = 0$. **Step 3 — Substitute.** $$ \begin{aligned} n &= 3(3 - 1) - 2(2) - 0 \\ &= 3 \cdot 2 - 4 \\ &= 6 - 4 \\ &= 2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Interpret.** The 2-DOF planar arm has $n = 2$. Each joint angle $\theta_1, \theta_2 \in S^{1}$. **Step 5 — Write the C-space.** $$ \mathcal{C} = S^{1} \times S^{1} = T^{2} \quad \text{(the 2-torus)} $$ A configuration is $q = (\theta_1, \theta_2)$, and $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \subset T^{2}$ is a subset of the torus — often visualized as a unit square with opposite edges identified. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — C-obstacle for a Circular Robot and a Circular Obstacle **Problem:** A disk-shaped robot of radius $r$ moves in 2-D. There is a single circular workspace obstacle of radius $R$ centered at $O = (x_O, y_O)$. Compute the C-space obstacle $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$. > [!NOTE] > For a **circular** robot, orientation $\theta$ is irrelevant — the C-space is just $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ (the disk's center positions). For polygonal robots, orientation adds the extra $S^{1}$ dimension. **Step 1 — The robot in its reference pose.** In the reference (zero-orientation) configuration, the robot is a disk of radius $r$ centered at the origin: $R_0 = \{p \in \mathbb{R}^{2} \mid \vert p \vert \leq r\}$. **Step 2 — Define the Minkowski sum.** A configuration $q = (x, y)$ places the robot's center at $(x,y)$. The collision condition is: $$ R(q) \cap O \neq \emptyset \iff \exists\, p \in R_0,\; \exists\, o \in O : q + p = o $$ **Step 3 — Rearrange.** $\exists\, p, o : q = o - p$ where $p \in R_0$ and $o \in O$. Therefore: $$ q \in O \oplus (-R_0) $$ where $-R_0 = \{-p \mid p \in R_0\}$ (the robot reflected through the origin). **Step 4 — Specialize to circles.** The Minkowski sum of two disks of radii $R$ and $r$ (with $R_0$ reflected, still radius $r$) is a single disk centered at $O$ with radius $R + r$: $$ \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \{\, (x, y) \mid (x - x_O)^{2} + (y - y_O)^{2} \leq (R + r)^{2} \,\} $$ **Step 5 — Conclusion.** For a circular robot and circular obstacle, the C-obstacle is simply a **grown circle** of radius $R + r$. The robot's radius is "added" to the obstacle's radius. This is the geometric origin of the phrase **"growing the obstacles by the robot"**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — C-obstacle for a Point Robot and a Polygonal Obstacle **Problem:** A point robot (radius $r = 0$) moves in 2-D. There is a single convex polygon $O$ with vertices $v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k$. Compute $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$. **Step 1 — Specialize the Minkowski sum.** For a point robot $R_0 = \{(0,0)\}$, we have $O \oplus (-R_0) = O$. So: $$ \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = O $$ **Step 2 — Inward offset for a non-point robot.** If the robot has finite radius $r$, the C-obstacle is the **inward offset** of $O$ by $-r$ — the set of points at distance at least $r$ from the boundary of $O$ is $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$, and $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ is the **outward offset** (growth) of $O$ by $+r$: $$ \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \{\, p \in \mathbb{R}^{2} \mid \text{dist}(p, O) \leq r \,\} $$ For a convex polygon with edges $e_1, \ldots, e_k$ and vertices $v_1, \ldots, v_k$: - Each edge grows into a parallel edge at distance $r$ outward. - Each vertex generates a **circular arc** of radius $r$ spanning the external angle. **Step 3 — Explicit parametric form.** For an edge from $v_i$ to $v_{i+1}$ with outward unit normal $\hat{n}_i$: $$ e_i^{\text{grown}} = \{\, v + r\,\hat{n}_i \mid v \in [v_i, v_{i+1}] \,\} $$ For a vertex $v_i$ with external angle $\alpha_i$: $$ \text{arc}_i = \{\, v_i + r(\cos\phi, \sin\phi) \mid \phi \in [\theta_i, \theta_i + \alpha_i] \,\} $$ The full $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ is the union of grown edges and vertex arcs, with the **inward** boundary now defining $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$'s outer reach. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — C-space Topology for an n-DOF Revolute Chain **Problem:** An open kinematic chain has $n$ revolute joints. Find the dimension and topology of the C-space. **Step 1 — Each joint contributes one DOF.** By Grübler, with $L = n+1$ (including ground), $J_1 = n$, $J_2 = 0$, in planar case: $$ n_{\text{DOF}} = 3(n+1-1) - 2n = 3n - 2n = n $$ **Step 2 — Each joint angle is an element of $S^{1}$.** A revolute joint angle $\theta_i \in [0, 2\pi)$, with $0$ and $2\pi$ identified. **Step 3 — Form the Cartesian product.** Since the joints are independent: $$ \mathcal{C} = S^{1} \times S^{1} \times \cdots \times S^{1} = T^{n} \quad (n \text{ times}) $$ **Step 4 — The C-space is the $n$-torus.** For $n = 1$: circle. For $n = 2$: torus (donut surface). For $n = 3$: 3-torus. > [!NOTE] > **Wraparound is critical.** When planning on $T^{n}$, an angle crossing $2\pi$ does not represent a discontinuity — the planner must treat $0$ and $2\pi$ as the *same* point. This non-Euclidean topology is what makes many grid-based methods fail on revolute-joint robots unless wraparound is explicitly handled. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — C-space Properties vs Robot Type | Robot Type | DOF | $\mathcal{C}$ Topology | $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ Shape | Planning Difficulty | |---|---|---|---|---| | Point in 2-D | 2 | $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ | Polygon (offset by $r$) | Easy | | Disk in 2-D | 2 | $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ | Grown polygon | Easy | | Polygon in 2-D | 3 | $\mathbb{R}^{2} \times S^{1}$ | Cylindrical extruded shape | Moderate | | 2-R planar arm | 2 | $T^{2}$ | Curved 2-D patches on torus | Moderate | | 6-R spatial arm | 6 | $T^{6}$ (joint space) / $SE(3)$ (Cartesian) | Highly complex | Hard | | Car (Dubins) | 3 in $SE(2)$ | $\mathbb{R}^{2} \times S^{1}$ with non-holonomic constraint | Curved walls + forbidden regions | Moderate–Hard | | Free-flying satellite | 6 | $SE(3) = \mathbb{R}^{3} \times SO(3)$ | Twisted obstacle regions | Hard | # 📘 STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Concept Flow: From Workspace to C-space ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Workspace W] --> B[Robot R with obstacles O] B --> C[Identify DOF and generalized coordinates q] C --> D[Construct Configuration Space C] D --> E[Compute C-obstacles Cobs] E --> F[C-free = C minus Cobs] F --> G[Plan point path in C-free] G --> H[Map path back to workspace trajectory] H --> I[Execute motion commands to robot] classDef defStyle fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1E88E5,color:#0D47A1; classDef emphStyle fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00,color:#E65100; classDef goalStyle fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#43A047,color:#1B5E20; class A,B,C defStyle; class D,E,F emphStyle; class G,H,I goalStyle; ``` ## 4.2 Architecture: C-space as the Bridge Between Kinematics and Planning ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Input Layer] K[Robot Kinematics
DH Parameters] O[Workspace Obstacles] Q[Start and Goal Configurations] end subgraph CSPACE[Configuration Space Layer] C[Manifold C] COBS[Cobstacles] CFREE[Cfree = C minus Cobstacles] end subgraph PLANNER[Planning Algorithms] PRM[PRM] RRT[RRT and RRTstar] POT[Potential Fields] GRID[Graph Search A star] end subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer] PATH[Continuous Path tau s] TRAJ[Trajectory with timing] end K --> C O --> COBS Q --> CFREE C --> COBS COBS --> CFREE CFREE --> PRM CFREE --> RRT CFREE --> POT CFREE --> GRID PRM --> PATH RRT --> PATH POT --> PATH GRID --> PATH PATH --> TRAJ ``` ## 4.3 Mapping Schematic: Workspace Polygon Robot vs Point in C-space ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph WS[Workspace View] P1[Robot as polygon P] O1[Obstacles O1 and O2] end subgraph CS[Cspace View] P2[Robot as point p] O2[Grewn obstacles O1 plus P and O2 plus P] end P1 -. Grow by robot shape .-> O2 O1 -. Minkowski sum .-> O2 P1 -. Reduce to point .-> P2 classDef wsBox fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#0D47A1; classDef csBox fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57C00,color:#E65100; class P1,O1 wsBox; class P2,O2 csBox; ``` ## 4.4 C-space Partition Visualized ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Configuration Space C] --> B[Cobs Collision Configurations] A --> C[Cfree Free Configurations] B --> B1[Workspace obstacle O1 mapped] B --> B2[Workspace obstacle O2 mapped] B --> B3[Workspace obstacle O3 mapped] C --> D[Connected component 1 contains start] C --> E[Connected component 2 contains goal] C --> F[Disconnected free regions unreachable] classDef rootStyle fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,color:#01579B; classDef obsStyle fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#B71C1C; classDef freeStyle fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20; classDef warnStyle fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#EF6C00,color:#E65100; class A rootStyle; class B,B1,B2,B3 obsStyle; class C,D,E freeStyle; class F warnStyle; ``` > [!TIP] > **Reading the diagrams:** Notice that the *same* problem becomes progressively cleaner as we move from Workspace to C-space. The C-space is the **canonical representation** in which all motion-planning algorithms operate, regardless of the robot's actual shape. # 📘 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define Configuration Space. Why is it used in robot motion planning? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Pattern]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Configuration Space (C-space) is the set of all possible configurations that a robot can attain. Each point $q \in \mathcal{C}$ represents a unique pose of the robot, specified by its generalized coordinates $q = (q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n)$, where $n$ is the number of degrees of freedom. **Importance (1.5 marks):** It transforms a complex motion-planning problem — involving collision avoidance for a robot of arbitrary shape moving among obstacles — into a much simpler point-navigation problem in a higher-dimensional space. The robot becomes a single point in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$, and standard algorithms (RRT, PRM, A*, potential fields) can be applied uniformly. It also makes the topological structure of the planning problem explicit (e.g., $S^{1}$, $T^{n}$, $SE(3)$). --- ### Q2. Differentiate between $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ and $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Pattern]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ | $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ | |---|---|---| | Definition | Set of collision-free configurations | Set of configurations where robot intersects any obstacle | | Mathematical form | $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ | $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \{q \in \mathcal{C} \mid R(q) \cap O \neq \emptyset\}$ | | Role in planning | Region where path is searched | Region that must be **avoided** | | Construction | Complement of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ in $\mathcal{C}$ | Minkowski sum of workspace obstacles with reflected robot | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) > [!IMPORTANT] > Following the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students are given an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** within the same module. Both are presented below; in the actual exam, you answer **one** of them. --- ### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 3 Pattern]`** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the concept of Configuration Space. Define $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ and $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ and write the formal mathematical conditions for each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A circular mobile robot of radius $r = 0.5$ m must navigate in a 2-D workspace containing a single circular obstacle of radius $R = 1.0$ m, centered at the origin. Compute the C-space obstacle $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ and the C-free space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. Discuss what happens to the planning problem after this transformation. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q.A(a) **Step 1 — Diagram of C-space concept (2 marks):** A 2-D workspace with a polygonal robot $R$ and an obstacle $O$ is mapped to a C-space where $R$ is a point and $O$ becomes a "grown" region $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$. **Step 2 — Formal definition of C-space (2 marks):** The C-space $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of all configurations $q = (q_1, \ldots, q_n)$ of the robot, where $n$ is the number of degrees of freedom. **Step 3 — Definition and condition for $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ (1.5 marks):** $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \{q \in \mathcal{C} \mid R(q) \cap O \neq \emptyset\}$ — all configurations where the robot body intersects any obstacle. **Step 4 — Definition and condition for $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ (1.5 marks):** $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ — the set of all collision-free configurations. Path planning is constrained to lie entirely within $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. #### Model Solution for Q.A(b) **Step 1 — Identify the robot's reference shape (1 mark):** The robot is a disk of radius $r = 0.5$ m centered at the origin in its reference configuration: $R_0 = \{(x,y) \mid x^{2} + y^{2} \leq 0.25\}$. **Step 2 — Identify the obstacle (1 mark):** The workspace obstacle is a disk of radius $R = 1.0$ m centered at $(0,0)$: $O = \{(x,y) \mid x^{2} + y^{2} \leq 1.0\}$. **Step 3 — Apply the Minkowski sum principle (2 marks):** Since the robot is a circular disk, its C-space is $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ (orientation is irrelevant), and the C-obstacle is the Minkowski sum: $$ \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = O \oplus (-R_0) $$ For two circles, the Minkowski sum is a circle of radius equal to the **sum of radii** centered at the sum of the centers. **Step 4 — Compute the result (2 marks):** Effective radius $= R + r = 1.0 + 0.5 = 1.5$ m. Therefore: $$ \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \{\, (x, y) \mid x^{2} + y^{2} \leq 2.25 \,\} $$ **Step 5 — Compute the C-free space (1 mark):** $$ \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathbb{R}^{2} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \{\, (x, y) \mid x^{2} + y^{2} > 2.25 \,\} $$ --- ### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 3 Pattern]`** **(a)** Explain the concept of degrees of freedom (DOF) for a robot. Apply Grübler's formula to compute the DOF of (i) a 2-link planar revolute manipulator, and (ii) a 3-R spatial manipulator. State the corresponding C-space topology in each case. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Construct the C-space for a 2-R planar arm with link lengths $L_1 = 2$ m and $L_2 = 1.5$ m moving in a workspace containing a single point obstacle at $(1.5, 0)$. Show that the resulting C-obstacle is a curve in the joint space $T^{2}$, and derive its parametric equation. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q.B(a) **Step 1 — DOF definition (1 mark):** The number of independent parameters (generalized coordinates) required to fully specify a robot's configuration is its degrees of freedom (DOF). **Step 2 — Grübler's formula (1 mark):** For a planar mechanism: $n = 3(L-1) - 2J_1 - J_2$. **(i) 2-link planar arm (2 marks):** $L = 3$ (ground + 2 links), $J_1 = 2$ (two revolute joints), $J_2 = 0$. $$ n = 3(3-1) - 2(2) - 0 = 6 - 4 = 2 $$ C-space: $\mathcal{C} = S^{1} \times S^{1} = T^{2}$. **(ii) 3-R spatial arm (2 marks):** For a spatial mechanism: $n = 6(L-1) - 5J_1 - 4J_2 - 3J_3 - 2J_4 - J_5$. $L = 4$, $J_1 = 3$, others $= 0$. $$ n = 6(4-1) - 5(3) - 0 = 18 - 15 = 3 $$ C-space: $\mathcal{C} = S^{1} \times S^{1} \times S^{1} = T^{3}$. **Step 3 — Topology summary (1 mark):** Both are tori of differing dimensions; the 3-R spatial arm has an additional rotational DOF, making its C-space one dimension higher. #### Model Solution for Q.B(b) **Step 1 — Forward kinematics of the 2-R arm (1.5 marks):** End-effector position: $$ \begin{aligned} x &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ y &= L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Collision condition with the point obstacle (1.5 marks):** A collision occurs when the end-effector coincides with the obstacle, OR when the obstacle lies anywhere along either link's swept path. For a point obstacle, the simplest condition is when the obstacle is reached by the end-effector (ignoring self-collision along the links for this standard derivation): $$ L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) = 1.5, \quad L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) = 0 $$ **Step 3 — Solve the second equation (1 mark):** $\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \theta_1 + \theta_2 = 0$ or $\pi$ (mod $2\pi$). **Case A:** $\theta_1 + \theta_2 = 0 \Rightarrow \theta_2 = -\theta_1$. Then $x = (L_1 + L_2)\cos\theta_1 = 3.5\cos\theta_1 = 1.5 \Rightarrow \cos\theta_1 = 3/7$. Two solutions: $\theta_1 = \pm \arccos(3/7)$. **Case B:** $\theta_1 + \theta_2 = \pi \Rightarrow \theta_2 = \pi - \theta_1$. Then $x = L_1\cos\theta_1 - L_2\cos\theta_1 = 0.5\cos\theta_1 = 1.5 \Rightarrow \cos\theta_1 = 3$. No real solution (since $|\cos| \leq 1$). **Step 4 — Final C-obstacle (2 marks):** The C-obstacle is a finite set of isolated points in $T^{2}$: $\{(\theta_1, \theta_2) = (\arccos(3/7), -\arccos(3/7)),\, (-\arccos(3/7), \arccos(3/7))\}$. > [!NOTE] > **Generalization:** For a point obstacle in 2-D, the C-obstacle is a 1-D curve in $T^{2}$. For an extended obstacle, it becomes a 2-D region. The dimensionality of the C-obstacle is (workspace obstacle dim) – (point dimension fixed) = 1, embedded in a 2-D $T^{2}$ — hence a curve. **Step 5 — Parametric form (1 mark):** As the obstacle moves around the workspace, the C-obstacle sweeps out a region. For a fixed point obstacle, the parametric equation is: $$ \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \{\, (\theta_1, \theta_2) \mid L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) = 1.5,\; L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) = 0 \,\} $$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout ⚠️ > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost marks in this topic:** > > 1. **Forgetting orientation for non-circular robots.** A square robot has 3 DOF (x, y, θ), not 2. Many students write the C-space as $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ and lose 2–3 marks. > 2. **Minkowski sum direction error.** The robot is "grown" into the obstacle (or equivalently, the obstacle is grown by the robot's shape). Students often shrink the obstacle instead, producing an inflated $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ that is geometrically wrong. > 3. **Ignoring topology wraparound on $S^{1}$.** When $\theta_1 = 2\pi - 0.01$ and $\theta_1 = 0.01$ are *neighbors* on the circle, naïve grid-based planners that don't identify $0$ and $2\pi$ will give wrong connectivity answers. > 4. **Confusing DOF with number of joints.** A 6-joint robot can have fewer than 6 DOF if joints are dependent (e.g., closed loops, parallel mechanisms). Use Grübler's formula, not joint counting. > 5. **Mixing workspace and C-space coordinates in formulas.** All forward kinematics expressions use the *joint-space* $q$, not the *workspace* $(x,y)$. Substituting workspace variables into joint-space formulas is a frequent error. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝 - **C-space** is the space of all robot configurations; **one point = one pose**. - **$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$** = collision-free configurations; **$\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$** = collision configurations. - **Degrees of freedom (DOF)** = number of independent generalized coordinates = dimension of $\mathcal{C}$. - **Grübler's formula** (planar): $n = 3(L-1) - 2J_1 - J_2$. **Grübler's formula** (spatial): $n = 6(L-1) - 5J_1 - 4J_2 - 3J_3 - 2J_4 - J_5$. - **Minkowski sum** principle: C-obstacle = $O \oplus (-R_0)$. Geometrically, "grow the obstacle by the robot's shape". - **C-space topologies to remember:** - Point in 2-D $\Rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{2}$ - Disk in 2-D $\Rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{2}$ - Polygon in 2-D $\Rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{2} \times S^{1}$ - 2-D revolute chain $\Rightarrow T^{n}$ (n-torus) - Free-flying rigid body in 3-D $\Rightarrow SE(3) = \mathbb{R}^{3} \times SO(3)$ - **Path existence condition:** $\tau(s) \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ for all $s \in [0,1]$. - **C-space dimension > workspace dimension** in general, except for a free-flying point. - **Practical takeaway:** All modern motion planners (RRT, PRM, A*, potential fields) operate on $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$; the C-space is the universal language of motion planning. - **Real-world engineering applications:** Autonomous vehicles (planning in $SE(2)$ with non-holonomic constraints), industrial robotic arms (planning in joint-space $T^{n}$), mobile manipulators (hybrid $SE(2) \times T^{n}$), and surgical robots (planning in $SE(3)$ or joint space with anatomical obstacles).

topics.title: Free Configuration Space
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Free Configuration Space

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Free Configuration Space** (denoted $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$) is rigorously defined as the set of all configurations a robot can assume without any part of its body intersecting any obstacle in the workspace. Mathematically, it is the subset of the total configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ that excludes the *configuration space obstacle* region $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$.

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \{ q \in \mathcal{C} \mid \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} = \varnothing \}$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{C}$ is the full configuration space (a topological manifold parameterized by the robot's degrees of freedom)
- $\mathcal{A}(q)$ is the region occupied by the robot in the workspace when placed at configuration $q$
- $\mathcal{O}$ denotes the union of all workspace obstacles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The distinction between the *workspace* $\mathcal{W}$ (the physical 2D/3D environment) and the *configuration space* $\mathcal{C}$ (the abstract parameter space of robot poses) is the **single most critical conceptual leap** in motion planning. Every planning algorithm ultimately operates in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$, not in $\mathcal{W}$.

## 1.2 The Configuration Space Itself

The configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ is a smooth manifold whose dimension equals the number of **degrees of freedom (DOF)** $n$ of the robot:

| Robot Type | DOF ($n$) | Topology of $\mathcal{C}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Point in 2D | 2 | $\mathbb{R}^2$ |
| Rigid polygon in 2D | 3 | $\mathbb{R}^2 \times \mathbb{S}^1$ |
| Point in 3D | 3 | $\mathbb{R}^3$ |
| Free-flying rigid body in 3D | 6 | $\mathbb{R}^3 \times SO(3)$ |
| $n$-revolute-joint planar arm | $n$ | $\mathbb{T}^n$ (n-torus) |
| Car-like robot (Reeds-Shepp) | 3 | $\mathbb{R}^2 \times \mathbb{S}^1$ (with velocity limits) |

> [!NOTE]
> A configuration $q$ is a vector of generalized coordinates: $q = (x, y, \theta)$ for a planar rigid body, or $q = (\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_n)$ for a serial manipulator. Each unique $q$ corresponds to exactly one geometric placement of the robot in $\mathcal{W}$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lifted Point Robot" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The Pointer-and-Map Analogy:** Imagine you are navigating a complex **L-shaped sofa** through a cluttered living room. Instead of doing the hard mental work of rotating the sofa, sliding it, and re-checking clearance with every chair, you create a single **"shadow point"** that you move on a separate top-down *navigation map*. The navigation map is pre-built so that the shadow point never collides with anything **if and only if** the real sofa, when placed at the corresponding position, fits. The sofa's shape, size, and rotation rules are "baked into" the shape of forbidden zones on this map. That navigation map **IS** the free configuration space.

**Why this abstraction is powerful:**
- It transforms a *geometrically complex* problem (rotating polygons) into a *point-motion* problem.
- A point robot in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ becomes the canonical problem; complexity shifts from the robot to the *obstacle geometry in $\mathcal{C}$*.
- Path planning reduces to: **find a continuous curve $\tau : [0,1] \to \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$** connecting $q_{\text{start}}$ to $q_{\text{goal}}$.

## 1.4 Worked Pictorial Overview

For a square robot of side $a$ translating (no rotation) in a workspace containing one circular obstacle of radius $r$:

- **Workspace view:** Square + circle → need to compute clearance at every step.
- **Configuration space view:** A 2D plane where the *square is reduced to a point* and the *circle is grown* to a "configuration obstacle" of radius $r + \tfrac{a\sqrt{2}}{2}$ (the half-diagonal, since the square can rotate and reach the farthest diagonal point). Planning becomes a pure 2D point-avoidance task.

This "growing" is formalized by the **Minkowski sum** in Section 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of the Free Space

### 2.1.1 Topological Properties

$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ inherits from $\mathcal{C}$ several important topological characteristics:

1. **Openness:** Under standard definitions, $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \{q : \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \varnothing\}$ is a *closed* set (it includes the obstacle boundary), so $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ is an **open** subset of $\mathcal{C}$.

2. **Connectivity:** $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is generally **disconnected** in cluttered environments. Each *connected component* (or *region* in KTU textbook terminology) represents a topologically distinct set of robot placements.

3. **Reachability Criterion:** A motion plan exists from $q_s$ to $q_g$ if and only if **both lie in the same connected component of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$**.

### 2.1.2 Closure vs. Interior (KTU Favourite Nuance)

| Symbol | Set | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ | Interior of valid configurations | Robot strictly does not touch obstacles |
| $\mathrm{cl}(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}})$ | Closure | Robot may contact (touch) obstacles |
| $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ | Obstacle region (closed) | Robot penetrates the obstacle |

> [!NOTE]
> In some KTU references, the **closure** $\mathrm{cl}(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}})$ is used to permit "contact" motions, which is essential for assembly tasks. The default convention in motion planning literature is the **open** version.

## 2.2 Constructing $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ — The Minkowski Sum Machinery

For a **translating** robot (orientation held fixed or absent), the configuration obstacle is computed via the **Minkowski sum**:

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{R}) = \{o - r \mid o \in \mathcal{O},\; r \in \mathcal{R} \}$$

Where $\mathcal{R}$ denotes the robot's body in a reference configuration. Equivalently, this is the *configuration* where the robot becomes a point and the obstacle is "grown" by the shape of the robot.

**Geometric meaning of Minkowski sum $A \oplus B$:**
$$A \oplus B = \{a + b \mid a \in A,\; b \in B\}$$

It is the union of all translations of $B$ by every point in $A$ (or vice versa).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Definition | Condition / Domain | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \{q \in \mathcal{C} \mid \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} = \varnothing\}$ | All robots | Reachability condition |
| 2 | $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ | All robots | Obstacle region in C-space |
| 3 | $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{R})$ | Translating rigid body | Grows obstacles by robot shape |
| 4 | $A \oplus B = \{a + b \mid a \in A,\; b \in B\}$ | Any compact sets | Minkowski sum definition |
| 5 | $A \oplus B = \bigcup_{b \in B} (A + b) = \bigcup_{a \in A} (a + B)$ | Equivalent identities | Sweep-based computation |
| 6 | $r_{\text{disc}} \to r_{\text{obs}} + r_{\text{robot}}$ | Disc robot of radius $r_{\text{robot}}$ | Simple 2D planning |
| 7 | $\dim(\mathcal{C}) = n =$ DOF | $n$-DOF robot | Manifold dimension |
| 8 | $q : [0,1] \to \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ continuous | Path existence | Continuous path condition |
| 9 | $\tau(0) = q_s,\ \tau(1) = q_g$ | Path endpoints | Boundary values of plan |
| 10 | Homotopy class in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ | Topological classes | Distinct "qualitative" paths |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In any markdown table row that contains mathematical set notation, **always** use `\vert` or `\mid` in place of a literal `|` (pipe character) so the KTU rendering engine does not break the column structure. The examples above already comply.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ for a car includes a 2D position plus a steering angle; $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ encodes "no-go zones" in $(x, y, \theta)$ space (e.g., the car is too long to make that turn).
- **Surgical Robotics:** $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ of a snake-like endoscopic tool inside an anatomical lumen dictates safe insertion paths.
- **Warehouse AMR (Autonomous Mobile Robots):** $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ of a differential-drive robot in a fulfillment center governs all feasible trajectories.
- **Robotic Surgery & Assembly:** When $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ has multiple connected components, the task is *kinematically infeasible* without environmental modification.

## 2.5 The Configuration Space Obstacle Boundary

For a disc robot, the **boundary** $\partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ is at a constant distance from the workspace obstacle's boundary:

$$\partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \{ q \in \mathcal{C} \mid \mathrm{dist}(q, \mathcal{O}) = r \}$$

For a **polygonal** robot of $k$ vertices, the boundary is built from $k$ "swept" copies of $\partial \mathcal{O}$ — this is what gives the configuration obstacle its characteristic *star-shaped* silhouette.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Configuration Obstacle for a Translating Disc Robot

**Setup:** A disc-shaped robot of radius $\rho$ translates freely in a 2D workspace $\mathcal{W} = \mathbb{R}^2$ containing a single convex obstacle $\mathcal{O}$. We must derive $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$.

**Step 1 — Configuration space identification.**
For a translating disc without rotation, a configuration is fully specified by the disc's center coordinates:
$$q = (x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$$

**Step 2 — Robot body in workspace at configuration $q$.**
The disc occupies the set:
$$\mathcal{A}(q) = \{(x', y') \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid (x' - x)^2 + (y' - y)^2 \le \rho^2 \}$$

**Step 3 — Collision condition.**
A collision occurs if and only if the disc intersects the obstacle:
$$\mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \varnothing$$

**Step 4 — Translate to distance condition.**
For two compact sets, intersection is non-empty iff the distance between their centroids (here, the disc center and the obstacle set) is at most $\rho$:
$$\min_{(o_x, o_y) \in \mathcal{O}} \sqrt{(x - o_x)^2 + (y - o_y)^2} \le \rho$$

Squaring both sides (valid since both are non-negative):
$$(x - o_x)^2 + (y - o_y)^2 \le \rho^2 \;\;\text{for some}\;\;(o_x, o_y) \in \mathcal{O}$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the Minkowski sum structure.**
The above is precisely the condition for the point $q = (x,y)$ to lie in the *offset* of $\mathcal{O}$ by distance $\rho$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} &= \left\{ q \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid \mathrm{dist}(q, \mathcal{O}) \le \rho \right\} \\[4pt]
&= \mathcal{O} \oplus B_\rho(0) \quad \text{where} \;\; B_\rho(0) = \{b \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid \Vert b \Vert \le \rho\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Conclude free space.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} &= \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} \\[2pt]
&= \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \left( \mathcal{O} \oplus B_\rho(0) \right) \\[2pt]
&= \left\{ q \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid \mathrm{dist}(q, \mathcal{O}) > \rho \right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** The disc robot can be *shrunk* to a point, and simultaneously the obstacle is *grown* by a uniform offset of $\rho$. This transformation is what makes configuration-space planning tractable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Configuration Obstacle for a Translating Polygon Robot

**Setup:** A convex polygon robot $\mathcal{R}$ with vertices $\{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k\}$ translates (no rotation) in $\mathbb{R}^2$ among convex polygon obstacles $\mathcal{O}_1, \mathcal{O}_2, \ldots, \mathcal{O}_m$.

**Step 1 — Apply the general Minkowski sum formula.**
For a translating polygonal robot:
$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs},i} = \mathcal{O}_i \oplus (-\mathcal{R}), \quad i = 1, \ldots, m$$

**Step 2 — Compute $-\mathcal{R}$.**
Reflect the robot about the origin:
$$-\mathcal{R} = \{-v \mid v \in \mathcal{R}\}$$

So the vertices of $-\mathcal{R}$ are $\{-v_1, -v_2, \ldots, -v_k\}$.

**Step 3 — Express the Minkowski sum geometrically.**
For two convex polygons, the Minkowski sum is a convex polygon whose edges are obtained by **merging and sorting** the edges of both inputs by angle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\partial(\mathcal{O}_i \oplus (-\mathcal{R})) = \mathrm{conv}\!\left(\; \bigcup_{p \in \mathrm{vertices}(\mathcal{O}_i),\; v \in \mathrm{vertices}(-\mathcal{R})} (p + v) \;\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Construct the full configuration obstacle.**
The total $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ is the union across all workspace obstacles:
$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs},i} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} \left( \mathcal{O}_i \oplus (-\mathcal{R}) \right)$$

**Step 5 — Free space definition (final).**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} &= \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} \\[2pt]
&= \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} \left( \mathcal{O}_i \oplus (-\mathcal{R}) \right) \\[2pt]
&= \bigcap_{i=1}^{m} \left( \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \left( \mathcal{O}_i \oplus (-\mathcal{R}) \right) \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The De Morgan step in the final line is **exam-critical** — it shows that the free space is an *intersection* of "safe" half-planes / safe regions, one per obstacle.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Algebraic Verification of Minkowski Sum Identity

**Claim:** For any compact sets $A, B \subset \mathbb{R}^n$, we have
$$A \oplus B = \bigcap_{C : A \cap C = \varnothing,\; \text{translated}} C^c$$

Actually, a more useful identity for motion planning is:

$$A \oplus B = \{ x \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid \exists a \in A, b \in B \text{ s.t. } x = a + b \}$$

**Verification that the robot-as-point transformation is exact:**

Suppose the robot at configuration $q = (x, y)$ occupies the set $q + \mathcal{R} = \{q + r \mid r \in \mathcal{R}\}$. Collision occurs iff:
$$(q + \mathcal{R}) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \varnothing$$

This is equivalent to existence of $r \in \mathcal{R}$ and $o \in \mathcal{O}$ such that $q + r = o$, i.e., $q = o - r$. Collecting all such $q$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} &= \{ o - r \mid o \in \mathcal{O}, r \in \mathcal{R} \} \\[4pt]
&= \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{R})
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the foundational identity every C-space construction rests upon.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Workspace $\mathcal{W}$ | Configuration Space $\mathcal{C}$ | Free Space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Domain type | Physical 2D/3D | Abstract $n$-dimensional manifold | Open subset of $\mathcal{C}$ |
| What moves | The entire robot body | A *single* point (the "ghost") | The same point, constrained |
| What "grows" | Nothing (obstacles fixed) | Obstacles grow by Minkowski sum | — |
| Path output | Sequence of geometric placements | Continuous curve in $\mathcal{C}$ | Same curve, restricted to $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ |
| Typical shape of obstacles | Polygons, polyhedra | Swept (offset) shapes, sometimes non-convex | Concave, possibly multi-component |
| Algorithmic complexity | Hard (rotation, shape) | Hard (topology) but point-based | Same as $\mathcal{C}$, but lower-dim intersection queries |
| Real-world analogy | Driving a long truck | Driving a point on a custom map | Driving a point on a map avoiding red zones |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Workspace to Free C-Space Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the *data-flow architecture* by which workspace entities are transformed into the free configuration space. This block-level view sidesteps the difficulty of physically drawing 2D robot scenes while still conveying every conceptual interaction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workspace W with Obstacles O] --> B[Robot Body R Reference Model]
    B --> C[Compute Reflected Robot -R]
    A --> D[For Each Obstacle O_i]
    D --> E[Minkowski Sum O_i plus minus R]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Union of C_obs,i across all i]
    F --> G[Full C_obs Region]
    A --> H[Total Configuration Space C]
    H --> I[Set Difference C minus C_obs]
    G --> I
    I --> J[Free Configuration Space C_free]
    J --> K{Connectivity Test}
    K -- Same component --> L[Path Exists]
    K -- Different components --> M[No Feasible Path]
    L --> N[Output: Continuous Path q of t in C_free]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Follow the data flow from the *top-left* (workspace + robot model) to the *bottom-right* (existence of a continuous feasible path). The node `C_free` is the central artifact of the entire motion planning problem — every subsequent algorithm (PRM, RRT, cell decomposition) consumes it.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — C-Space Obstacle Formation Mechanics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Convex Obstacle O_i Polygon] --> Q1[Edge List sorted by angle]
    P2[Reflected Robot minus R Polygon] --> Q1
    Q1 --> R1[Merge and sort edges by orientation]
    R1 --> S1[Convex hull of summed vertex set]
    S1 --> T1[C_obs,i polygon]
    T1 --> U1[Union over i from 1 to m]
    U1 --> V1[Total C_obs]
    V1 --> W1[C_free is complement]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering takeaway:** This second diagram emphasizes that the Minkowski sum of two convex polygons is itself a convex polygon, computable in $O(k_1 + k_2)$ time using the angular sweep algorithm. For *non-convex* obstacles, the obstacle must be triangulated first, the sum taken piece-wise, and the union reconstructed.

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Disc Robot "Ghost Point" Reduction

Although Mermaid is best for flow logic, the following block schematic maps the conceptual equivalence between the workspace and the configuration-space viewpoints:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WS[Workspace View]
        WS1[Robot disc R] --> WS2[Obstacle circle O radius r]
        WS2 --> WS3[Compute clearance dist center to O]
    end
    subgraph CS[Configuration Space View]
        CS1[Shrink robot to point q] --> CS2[Grow obstacle to radius r plus rho]
        CS2 --> CS3[Test point is in grown obstacle]
    end
    WS3 -- Equivalent to --> CS3
    WS -- If clearance greater than rho --> FREE1[Collision Free in Workspace]
    CS -- If point not in grown obstacle --> FREE1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in the KTU exam:** A common examiner trick is to give a workspace diagram and ask for the *configuration-space diagram*. The two are equivalent **if** the robot is treated as a point and the obstacle is grown by the Minkowski offset. Memorize this reduction.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Q: Define the *free configuration space* of a robot. How is it related to the configuration space obstacle?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The free configuration space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is the set of all configurations $q$ of the robot such that the robot body $\mathcal{A}(q)$ does not intersect any obstacle in the workspace. It is defined as:

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \{ q \in \mathcal{C} \mid \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} = \varnothing \}$$

It is the *complement* of the configuration space obstacle $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ within the total configuration space $\mathcal{C}$:
$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$$

A motion plan from $q_s$ to $q_g$ exists if and only if both configurations belong to the **same connected component** of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.

*(Examiner allocates: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for set-relation, 1 mark for connectivity/interpretation.)*

---

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]`
**Q: What is a Minkowski sum? State its role in computing the configuration space obstacle for a translating robot.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Minkowski sum of two sets $A, B \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ is defined as:
$$A \oplus B = \{ a + b \mid a \in A,\; b \in B \}$$

In motion planning, the configuration space obstacle for a translating rigid robot $\mathcal{R}$ among obstacles $\mathcal{O}$ is computed as:
$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{R})$$

**Role:** It allows the robot to be reduced to a point while the obstacles are "grown" by the reflected robot's shape, transforming the planning problem into a point-avoidance task in configuration space.

*(Examiner allocates: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for C-space formula, 1 mark for the role/interpretation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B.A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Part B Module 3]`

**(a) Explain the concept of configuration space with suitable examples. Discuss its topological structure for a planar 2R robot arm.** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition.** The configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ of a robot is the set of all possible configurations (placements) the robot can assume. Each configuration $q$ is a vector of generalized coordinates whose dimension equals the robot's degrees of freedom (DOF).

**Example 1 — Point in 2D.** For a point robot moving in $\mathbb{R}^2$, the configuration is $q = (x, y)$, so $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$.

**Example 2 — Translating polygon.** A square translating in 2D has $q = (x, y)$, and $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$.

**Example 3 — Planar 2R arm.** A 2-revolute-joint planar manipulator with link lengths $L_1, L_2$ has configuration $q = (\theta_1, \theta_2)$, where $\theta_1, \theta_2 \in [0, 2\pi)$. The configuration space is therefore the 2-torus $\mathbb{T}^2$.

**Topological structure of $\mathbb{T}^2$:** It is a compact, orientable 2-manifold. Topologically, $\mathbb{T}^2 = \mathbb{S}^1 \times \mathbb{S}^1$. The fundamental group $\pi_1(\mathbb{T}^2) = \mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$ — meaning there are infinitely many topologically distinct path classes the arm can traverse without re-entering the same homotopy class. A configuration obstacle in $\mathcal{C}$ may *partition* $\mathbb{T}^2$ into multiple disconnected components, and the arm's reachability between configurations depends on whether the path can avoid these obstacles.

**Practical significance.** For the 2R arm, $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \subset \mathbb{T}^2$ is non-trivial: forward-kinematics singularities and self-collision regions create additional "holes" in free space beyond workspace obstacles.

*(Examiner allocates: 2 marks for definition, 2 marks for examples, 3 marks for topological discussion of $\mathbb{T}^2$.)*

---

**(b) Derive the configuration space obstacle for a circular robot of radius $\rho$ translating in a 2D workspace containing a single convex obstacle $\mathcal{O}$.** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the configuration space.** The robot's configuration is the center $q = (x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$, so $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$.

**Step 2 — Express the robot body in workspace.** At configuration $q$, the disc occupies:
$$\mathcal{A}(q) = \{(x', y') \mid (x' - x)^2 + (y' - y)^2 \le \rho^2 \}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the collision condition.** A collision occurs iff:
$$\mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \varnothing$$

**Step 4 — Transform via Minkowski sum.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} &= \{ q \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid (q + B_\rho(0)) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \varnothing \} \\[4pt]
&= \{ q \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid q \in \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{R}) \} \\[4pt]
&= \mathcal{O} \oplus B_\rho(0)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Distance formulation.**
$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \{ q \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid \mathrm{dist}(q, \mathcal{O}) \le \rho \}$$

**Step 6 — Free space.**
$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \{ q \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid \mathrm{dist}(q, \mathcal{O}) > \rho \}$$

**Interpretation:** Obstacles are *grown* by a uniform offset of $\rho$; the robot is reduced to a point. The grown obstacle has a boundary that is everywhere at distance $\rho$ from the original boundary.

*(Examiner allocates: 2 marks for setup, 2 marks for collision condition, 2 marks for Minkowski sum, 1 mark for free-space final form.)*

---

### Question B.B `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Part B Module 3]`

**(a) Discuss the topological properties of the free configuration space. Why is connectivity of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ crucial for motion planning?** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Topological Properties of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$:**

1. **Open Set:** $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ is closed (since collision is a closed condition), so $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is an open subset of $\mathcal{C}$.

2. **Manifold Structure:** For robots with no joint limits, $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is an open subset of a smooth manifold (e.g., $\mathbb{R}^n$, $\mathbb{T}^n$, or $\mathbb{R}^2 \times \mathbb{S}^1$).

3. **Disconnectedness:** $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is generally not connected. Each connected component is called a *region* or *trapped region* depending on whether the robot can reach the goal.

4. **Holes and Cavities:** Even when connected, $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ may have non-trivial topology (e.g., fundamental group $\pi_1 \neq 0$), corresponding to *narrow passages* the robot must thread through.

5. **Boundedness:** For confined workspaces, $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ may be bounded; for unconstrained robots, it is unbounded.

**Why Connectivity Matters:**

A continuous path $\tau : [0,1] \to \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ from $q_s$ to $q_g$ exists **if and only if** both $q_s$ and $q_g$ lie in the same connected component of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. Therefore:

- **No path** is possible if they lie in different components — the task is kinematically infeasible.
- **At least one path** is possible if they are in the same component — but finding the *optimal* one is the central algorithmic challenge.

Algorithms such as PRM, RRT, and cell decomposition all exploit connectivity of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ in different ways (probabilistic, sampling-based, or exact).

*(Examiner allocates: 3 marks for properties, 2 marks for reachability theorem, 2 marks for algorithmic implications.)*

---

**(b) For a polygon robot translating among polygon obstacles, explain with a step-by-step procedure how the configuration space obstacle is computed using the Minkowski sum.** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify robot and obstacle polygons.** Let $\mathcal{R}$ be a convex polygon robot with vertices $\{r_1, \ldots, r_k\}$ and edges $\{e_1, \ldots, e_k\}$. Let the workspace have $m$ convex polygon obstacles $\mathcal{O}_1, \ldots, \mathcal{O}_m$.

**Step 2 — Reflect the robot about the origin.**
$$-\mathcal{R} = \{-r \mid r \in \mathcal{R}\}$$
This polygon has vertices $\{-r_1, \ldots, -r_k\}$.

**Step 3 — Apply Minkowski sum per obstacle.**
For each obstacle $\mathcal{O}_i$:
$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs},i} = \mathcal{O}_i \oplus (-\mathcal{R})$$

**Step 4 — Use the angular sweep algorithm for convex polygons.**
- Extract edge directions of $\mathcal{O}_i$ and $-\mathcal{R}$ (as unit vectors).
- Sort both edge lists by angle.
- Merge the sorted edge lists into a single circular list.
- Walk the merged list, summing edges cumulatively from a starting vertex — the result is the boundary of the Minkowski sum polygon. Complexity: $O(k + n_i)$ where $n_i$ is the vertex count of $\mathcal{O}_i$.

**Step 5 — Take the union over all obstacles.**
$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs},i}$$

For non-convex obstacles, triangulate first, take the Minkowski sum for each triangle, and take the union of the resulting polygons.

**Step 6 — Compute free space.**
$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$$

**Step 7 — Path search in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.** Apply any 2D path planner (visibility graph, cell decomposition, A*) on $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ with the point robot.

*(Examiner allocates: 1 mark for setup, 1 mark for reflection, 2 marks for angular sweep, 1 mark for union, 1 mark for free space, 1 mark for planner choice.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the reflection ($-1$ factor):** Writing $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{O} \oplus \mathcal{R}$ instead of $\mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{R})$. The reflection matters because the configuration is the robot's *reference point*, not the robot's body.
> 2. **Confusing workspace and configuration space:** Drawing a Minkowski sum in the workspace rather than in $\mathcal{C}$. The growth is computed in the configuration space.
> 3. **Missing the closed vs. open distinction:** Forgetting to specify whether $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ includes or excludes the boundary (default: closed).
> 4. **Ignoring topology:** Citing the formula but not mentioning that $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ may be disconnected — losing 2–3 marks on the "interpretation" portion.
> 5. **Forgetting DOF count:** Stating $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$ for a *rotating* planar robot (it is $\mathbb{R}^2 \times \mathbb{S}^1$ — **3-DOF**, not 2).
> 6. **Skipping the De Morgan step:** Writing $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ as a complement-of-union rather than as a clean intersection — examiners value the algebraic elegance.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU Exam Hall:**

- $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is the set of **collision-free** configurations, an **open** subset of $\mathcal{C}$.
- $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$.
- **Reachability** is equivalent to **same connected component** of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.
- For a **translating** robot: $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{R})$.
- For a **translating disc** of radius $\rho$: $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ is the **offset** of $\mathcal{O}$ by distance $\rho$.
- **Configuration space dimension = DOF** of the robot.
- $\mathcal{C}$ for a free-flying 2D rigid body is $\mathbb{R}^2 \times \mathbb{S}^1$.
- $\mathcal{C}$ for a free-flying 3D rigid body is $\mathbb{R}^3 \times SO(3)$.
- $\mathcal{C}$ for an $n$-revolute-joint arm is $\mathbb{T}^n$ (an $n$-torus).
- The Minkowski sum of two **convex polygons** is computable in $O(k_1 + k_2)$ time using the **angular sweep** method.
- The **reflected robot** $-\mathcal{R}$ is obtained by negating all vertex coordinates.
- The **de Morgan form** $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \bigcap_i (\mathbb{R}^n \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs},i})$ is the exam-friendly rewrite.
- **Topological properties** to state: openness, connectivity, holes (fundamental group), boundedness.
- **Practical lesson:** Planning a complex-shape robot among complex obstacles reduces to planning a *point* in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ — this is the unifying abstraction of the entire course.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gradient Descent Method of Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gradient Descent Method of Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Gradient Descent (GD)** is a first-order iterative optimization algorithm used to find the **local minimum** of a differentiable cost (objective) function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$. The method updates the parameters in the direction of the **steepest descent**, i.e., opposite to the gradient of the function at the current point, scaled by a positive **learning rate** $\alpha > 0$.

The canonical parameter update rule is given by:

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} - \alpha \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})$$

where:
- $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ — parameter (decision) vector at iteration $k$
- $\nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})$ — gradient vector of $J$ evaluated at $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k}$
- $\alpha$ — **learning rate** (step size), a small positive scalar

In **robot motion planning**, gradient descent is the backbone of trajectory optimization, potential-field navigation, inverse kinematics resolution, and reinforcement-learning policy updates.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Blindfolded Hiker**
> Imagine a hiker trapped on a foggy mountain who wants to reach the lowest valley. The hiker cannot see the terrain but can feel the slope beneath their boots. At every step, the hiker checks the **steepest downward direction** (negative gradient) and takes a small step in that direction. If the step is too small, descent is painstakingly slow. If the step is too large, the hiker overshoots the valley and may climb the opposite slope. The hiker repeats this until the slope flattens (gradient ≈ 0), indicating the valley floor.

**Mapping to Gradient Descent:**

| Hiker Element | Gradient Descent Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Mountain terrain | Cost function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ |
| Slope direction | Gradient $\nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ |
| Step length | Learning rate $\alpha$ |
| Hiker position | Parameter vector $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ |
| Valley floor | Local (or global) minimum $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{*}$ |

For a robotic manipulator, the "hiker" is the algorithm searching the **configuration space (C-space)** to find joint angles $\boldsymbol{q}$ that minimize a cost such as end-effector error, joint torque, or path length.

### 1.3 Physical / Mathematical Constants and Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Key Quantities to Remember (Bolded for Emphasis):**
> - **Learning rate** $\alpha$: typically in $[10^{-4}, 10^{-1}]$ depending on the application.
> - **Convergence tolerance** $\varepsilon$: typically $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$.
> - **Momentum coefficient** $\beta$: typically $0.9$ (or $0.99$ for Adam).
> - **Decay rates** $\beta_1, \beta_2$ in Adam: defaults $0.9$ and $0.999$.
> - **Gradient norm** $\Vert \nabla J \Vert_2$: the Euclidean norm used as a stopping criterion.

### 1.4 Why Gradient Descent Matters in Robot Motion Planning

- **Trajectory Optimization**: Smooth joint-space trajectories minimizing time, energy, or jerk.
- **Inverse Kinematics**: Solving $\boldsymbol{x} = f(\boldsymbol{q})$ by minimizing $\Vert f(\boldsymbol{q}) - \boldsymbol{x}_{d}\Vert^{2}$.
- **Potential Field Methods**: Treating obstacles as repulsive potentials and goals as attractive potentials.
- **Policy Optimization in RL**: Updating robot control policies using Policy Gradient methods.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

> [!NOTE]
> The Gradient Descent algorithm operates through an **iterative refinement** procedure. Below is the structured algorithmic logic every KTU examiner expects a student to articulate.

**Step 1 — Initialization**
- Initialize parameter vector $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{0}$ (e.g., zero vector, random initialization, or warm-start from a previous solution).
- Choose learning rate $\alpha$, maximum iterations $K_{\max}$, and tolerance $\varepsilon$.

**Step 2 — Gradient Computation**
- Compute $\nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})$, the partial derivatives of $J$ with respect to each parameter.
- For a function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2}\Vert \boldsymbol{r}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \Vert^{2}$ (residual form), the gradient is:
  $$\nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \left(\frac{\partial \boldsymbol{r}}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}}\right)^{T} \boldsymbol{r}(\boldsymbol{\theta})$$

**Step 3 — Direction Selection**
- The negative gradient $-\nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})$ is mathematically proven (by the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality) to be the **direction of steepest descent** in the local neighborhood.

**Step 4 — Update Step**
- Update parameters: $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} - \alpha_{k} \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})$.
- The new parameters move toward the local minimum.

**Step 5 — Convergence Check**
- Stop if any of the following hold:
  - $\Vert \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k}) \Vert_{2} \leq \varepsilon$ (gradient norm small).
  - $\Vert \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} - \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} \Vert_{2} \leq \varepsilon$ (parameter change small).
  - $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1}) - J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k}) \vert \leq \varepsilon$ (cost change small).
  - $k \geq K_{\max}$ (iteration limit reached).

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' — Mathematical Justification

**Why the negative gradient?**
By Taylor's theorem (first-order expansion):

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1}) \approx J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k}) + \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})^{T} \Delta \boldsymbol{\theta}$$

For the cost to decrease, we need $\nabla J^{T} \Delta \boldsymbol{\theta} < 0$. Choosing $\Delta \boldsymbol{\theta} = -\alpha \nabla J$ guarantees this for small $\alpha$.

**How does step size affect convergence?**
- **Too small $\alpha$**: Slow convergence, may stall in flat regions.
- **Too large $\alpha$**: Oscillation or divergence.
- **Optimal $\alpha$**: Depends on the curvature (Hessian eigenvalues) of $J$ at $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k}$.

### 2.3 Variants of Gradient Descent (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiners frequently test the differences between Batch, Stochastic, and Mini-Batch Gradient Descent.**

| Variant | Data Used per Update | Update Rule | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Batch GD (BGD)** | Entire dataset | $\boldsymbol{\theta} = \boldsymbol{\theta} - \alpha \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ | Stable, deterministic convergence | Slow on large datasets, memory-heavy |
| **Stochastic GD (SGD)** | Single sample $i$ | $\boldsymbol{\theta} = \boldsymbol{\theta} - \alpha \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J^{(i)}(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ | Fast, escapes shallow local minima | Noisy updates |
| **Mini-Batch GD** | Batch of size $m$ | $\boldsymbol{\theta} = \boldsymbol{\theta} - \alpha \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J_{\text{batch}}(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ | Balance of speed and stability | Requires tuning batch size |

### 2.4 Enhanced Variants — Momentum, Nesterov, Adam

> [!NOTE]
> These variants introduce memory of past gradients to accelerate convergence in ravines and saddle points.

**Momentum Update:**
$$\boldsymbol{v}_{k+1} = \beta \boldsymbol{v}_{k} + \alpha \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})$$

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} - \boldsymbol{v}_{k+1}$$

**Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (NAG):**
$$\boldsymbol{v}_{k+1} = \beta \boldsymbol{v}_{k} + \alpha \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} - \beta \boldsymbol{v}_{k})$$

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} - \boldsymbol{v}_{k+1}$$

**Adam (Adaptive Moment Estimation):**
$$m_{k+1} = \beta_1 m_{k} + (1 - \beta_1) \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})$$

$$v_{k+1} = \beta_2 v_{k} + (1 - \beta_2) \left[\nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})\right]^{2}$$

$$\hat{m}_{k+1} = \frac{m_{k+1}}{1 - \beta_1^{k+1}}, \quad \hat{v}_{k+1} = \frac{v_{k+1}}{1 - \beta_2^{k+1}}$$

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} - \alpha \frac{\hat{m}_{k+1}}{\sqrt{\hat{v}_{k+1}} + \delta}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All critical equations in one place. Memorize these for the KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} - \alpha \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})$ | Canonical GD update | All gradient methods |
| 2 | $\nabla J = \left(\frac{\partial \boldsymbol{r}}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}}\right)^{T} \boldsymbol{r}$ | Gradient via Jacobian | Least-squares cost |
| 3 | $\boldsymbol{v}_{k+1} = \beta \boldsymbol{v}_{k} + \alpha \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})$ | Momentum velocity | Momentum GD |
| 4 | $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1}) \approx J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k}) - \alpha \Vert \nabla J \Vert^{2}$ | Cost decrease estimate | Convergence proof |
| 5 | $\alpha_{\text{opt}} \approx \frac{\Vert \nabla J \Vert^{2}}{\nabla J^{T} \mathbf{H} \nabla J}$ | Optimal step size (Newton-like) | Adaptive step sizing |
| 6 | $0 < \alpha < \frac{2}{\lambda_{\max}}$ | Convergence bound | Convex $J$ |
| 7 | $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{*} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{0} - \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \alpha \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})$ | Infinite-horizon form | Theoretical analysis |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Self-Driving Cars**: Path smoothing using gradient-based trajectory optimization.
- **Robotic Manipulators**: Real-time inverse kinematics via Jacobian-transpose pseudoinverse.
- **Drone Swarm Control**: Distributed policy gradient for cooperative behavior.
- **Computer Vision**: Camera calibration, image alignment, SLAM pose refinement.
- **Industrial Automation**: Cost minimization in pick-and-place trajectories.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Gradient Descent Update Rule from Taylor Expansion

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal**: Show rigorously *why* $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} - \alpha \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})$ is the natural update law.

**Step 1 — Start with a First-Order Taylor Expansion**

We expand $J$ around the current point $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k}$ using a small perturbation $\Delta \boldsymbol{\theta}$:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} + \Delta \boldsymbol{\theta}) \approx J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k}) + \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})^{T} \Delta \boldsymbol{\theta}$$

*Explanation:* The Taylor series truncates at the first-order term because higher-order terms are $O(\Vert \Delta \boldsymbol{\theta} \Vert^{2})$, negligible for small steps.

**Step 2 — Impose the Descent Condition**

We require the cost to strictly decrease:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} + \Delta \boldsymbol{\theta}) < J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})$$

This means:

$$\nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})^{T} \Delta \boldsymbol{\theta} < 0$$

*Explanation:* The dot product of the gradient with the perturbation must be negative for descent.

**Step 3 — Optimize the Perturbation Direction (Cauchy Argument)**

By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, for any vectors $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$:

$$\mathbf{a}^{T} \mathbf{b} \geq - \Vert \mathbf{a} \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{b} \Vert$$

Equality (most negative) is achieved when $\mathbf{b} = -\mathbf{a}$. Therefore, the most negative value of $\nabla J^{T} \Delta \boldsymbol{\theta}$ occurs when:

$$\Delta \boldsymbol{\theta} = -\eta \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})$$

for some scalar $\eta > 0$.

*Explanation:* This is the formal proof that the **negative gradient direction** is the direction of steepest descent.

**Step 4 — Introduce the Learning Rate**

Setting $\eta = \alpha$ (the learning rate), we obtain:

$$\Delta \boldsymbol{\theta} = -\alpha \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})$$

Therefore:

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} + \Delta \boldsymbol{\theta} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} - \alpha \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})$$

*Explanation:* The new parameter equals the old parameter plus the optimal descent step.

### 3.2 Convergence Condition for a Quadratic Cost

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **high-yield derivation** for KTU exams when a quadratic cost $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2} \boldsymbol{\theta}^{T} \mathbf{A} \boldsymbol{\theta}$ is given.

**Step 1 — Compute the Gradient**

$$\nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbf{A} \boldsymbol{\theta}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the Update Rule**

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} - \alpha \mathbf{A} \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} = (\mathbf{I} - \alpha \mathbf{A}) \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k}$$

**Step 3 — Analyze the Eigenstructure**

Let $\mathbf{A}$ have eigendecomposition $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{Q} \boldsymbol{\Lambda} \mathbf{Q}^{T}$. Then:

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} = \mathbf{Q} \boldsymbol{\Lambda}^{k} (\mathbf{I} - \alpha \boldsymbol{\Lambda})^{k} \mathbf{Q}^{T} \boldsymbol{\theta}_{0}$$

**Step 4 — Convergence Condition**

For $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} \to \boldsymbol{\theta}^{*} = \mathbf{0}$ as $k \to \infty$, we need all eigenvalues of $(\mathbf{I} - \alpha \mathbf{A})$ to satisfy:

$$\vert 1 - \alpha \lambda_{i} \vert < 1 \quad \forall i$$

This gives:

$$0 < \alpha < \frac{2}{\lambda_{\max}(\mathbf{A})}$$

*Explanation:* If $\alpha$ lies in this range, the algorithm converges for any positive-definite $\mathbf{A}$.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem**: Minimize $J(\theta) = (\theta - 3)^{2}$ using gradient descent with $\theta_0 = 0$ and $\alpha = 0.1$.

**Step 1 — Compute the Gradient**

$$\nabla J(\theta) = 2(\theta - 3)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Update Rule**

$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_{k} - 0.1 \cdot 2(\theta_{k} - 3) = \theta_{k} - 0.2(\theta_{k} - 3)$$

**Step 3 — Iterate**

$$\theta_{1} = 0 - 0.2(0 - 3) = 0.6$$

$$\theta_{2} = 0.6 - 0.2(0.6 - 3) = 1.08$$

$$\theta_{3} = 1.08 - 0.2(1.08 - 3) = 1.464$$

$$\theta_{4} = 1.464 - 0.2(1.464 - 3) = 1.7712$$

$$\theta_{5} = 1.7712 - 0.2(1.7712 - 3) = 2.01696$$

**Step 4 — Tabulate Convergence**

| Iteration $k$ | $\theta_{k}$ | $J(\theta_{k}) = (\theta_{k} - 3)^{2}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0000 | 9.0000 |
| 1 | 0.6000 | 5.7600 |
| 2 | 1.0800 | 3.6864 |
| 3 | 1.4640 | 2.3593 |
| 4 | 1.7712 | 1.5100 |
| 5 | 2.0170 | 0.9664 |
| $\infty$ | 3.0000 | 0.0000 |

*Explanation:* The sequence converges geometrically toward $\theta^{*} = 3$, the global minimum.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Descent Strategies

| Aspect | Vanilla GD | SGD | Momentum GD | Adam |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Convergence speed | Slow | Fast (noisy) | Fast | Very fast |
| Memory | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(2n)$ | $O(3n)$ |
| Tunable hyperparams | $\alpha$ | $\alpha$ | $\alpha, \beta$ | $\alpha, \beta_1, \beta_2, \delta$ |
| Robust to saddle pts | No | Somewhat | Yes | Yes |
| Use in robotics IK | Rare | Sometimes | Common | Common (deep RL) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Gradient Descent Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START]) --> initB[Initialize theta0, alpha, eps]
    initB --> iterC{k = 0}
    iterC --> gradD[Compute gradient of J at theta_k]
    gradD --> updE[Update theta via theta minus alpha times grad]
    updE --> convF{Convergence criteria met?}
    convF -- No --> incrG[k = k + 1]
    incrG --> gradD
    convF -- Yes --> outH[Output theta star as minimum]
    outH --> endI([END])
```

*Explanation:* This is the canonical loop structure every KTU student must memorize. The decision diamond `convF` encapsulates all stopping criteria (gradient norm, parameter change, cost change, or iteration limit).

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Robotic Manipulator IK via Gradient Descent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    desX[Desired End-Effector Pose Xd] --> errA[Compute Pose Error e = Xd minus X]
    errA --> costB[Formulate Cost J equals 0.5 e transpose e]
    costB --> jacC[Compute Jacobian J of Forward Kinematics]
    jacC --> gradD[Compute Gradient grad J equals J transpose e]
    gradD --> updE[Update Joint Angles q new equals q old minus alpha grad J]
    updE --> fkF[Forward Kinematics to compute new X]
    fkF --> convG{Error below tolerance?}
    convG -- No --> errA
    convG -- Yes --> outH[Output Joint Configuration q star]
```

*Explanation:* This block diagram captures the iterative essence of inverse kinematics solved by gradient descent — a standard KTU module-level question on motion planning.

### 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Variants of Gradient Descent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Gradient Descent Family] --> b1[Batch GD]
    rootA --> b2[Stochastic GD]
    rootA --> b3[Mini-Batch GD]
    b1 --> enh1[Enhanced Variants]
    b2 --> enh1
    b3 --> enh1
    enh1 --> v1[Momentum GD]
    enh1 --> v2[Nesterov Accelerated]
    enh1 --> v3[AdaGrad]
    enh1 --> v4[RMSProp]
    enh1 --> v5[Adam Optimizer]
    v5 --> app1[Used in Robot Deep RL]
    v3 --> app2[Used in Sparse-Feature Tasks]
    v1 --> app3[Used in IK Solvers]
```

*Explanation:* This shows the taxonomy KTU examiners love to draw on the board.

### 4.4 Mermaid Convergence Behavior Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    initA[theta0 Initial] --> s1[Step 1]
    s1 --> s2[Step 2]
    s2 --> s3[Step 3]
    s3 --> s4[Step 4]
    s4 --> sN[Step N near minimum]
    sN --> minB[theta star Local Minimum]
    initA -. Negative Gradient Direction .-> s1
    s1 -. Negative Gradient Direction .-> s2
    s2 -. Negative Gradient Direction .-> s3
```

*Explanation:* Each arrow represents the descent step; dashed lines represent the negative-gradient vector at each point.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Style]**

#### **Question 1** (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the Gradient Descent optimization method. State its parameter update rule.**

**Model Answer:**

Gradient Descent is a first-order iterative optimization algorithm used to find the minimum of a differentiable function. The parameter update rule is:

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} - \alpha \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})$$

where $\alpha > 0$ is the learning rate and $\nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})$ is the gradient of the cost function at iteration $k$. The algorithm terminates when $\Vert \nabla J \Vert_{2} \leq \varepsilon$.

> *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for update rule + 1 for termination criterion)*

---

#### **Question 2** (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Differentiate between Batch Gradient Descent and Stochastic Gradient Descent.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Batch GD | Stochastic GD |
|---|---|---|
| Data used per update | Entire dataset | Single random sample |
| Update rule | $\boldsymbol{\theta} = \boldsymbol{\theta} - \alpha \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ | $\boldsymbol{\theta} = \boldsymbol{\theta} - \alpha \nabla J^{(i)}(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ |
| Convergence | Smooth, stable | Noisy but faster |
| Computational cost per iteration | High | Low |
| Use in robotics | Trajectory optimization | Online policy learning |

> *(3 marks: 2 for tabular differentiation + 1 for application in robotics)*

---

### PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **Answer ANY ONE full question from the choices below. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Style]`

**(a)** Derive the gradient descent update rule starting from the first-order Taylor expansion of a cost function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$. Show clearly why the negative-gradient direction yields maximum local decrease.

**(b)** For the quadratic cost $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2} \boldsymbol{\theta}^{T} \mathbf{A} \boldsymbol{\theta}$ with $\mathbf{A}$ symmetric positive-definite having eigenvalues $\lambda_i$, derive the condition on the learning rate $\alpha$ for guaranteed convergence. Apply it to the case $\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 8 \end{bmatrix}$ and find the largest stable $\alpha$.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A:**

**(a) Derivation of Update Rule (7 marks)**

We start with the first-order Taylor expansion of $J$ around $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k}$:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} + \Delta \boldsymbol{\theta}) \approx J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k}) + \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})^{T} \Delta \boldsymbol{\theta} \tag{1}$$

For descent, we impose:

$$\nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})^{T} \Delta \boldsymbol{\theta} < 0 \tag{2}$$

We wish to minimize the dot product subject to $\Vert \Delta \boldsymbol{\theta} \Vert = c$ (fixed step length). By Cauchy–Schwarz:

$$\nabla J^{T} \Delta \boldsymbol{\theta} \geq - \Vert \nabla J \Vert \cdot \Vert \Delta \boldsymbol{\theta} \Vert = -c \Vert \nabla J \Vert \tag{3}$$

Equality holds when $\Delta \boldsymbol{\theta} = -c \frac{\nabla J}{\Vert \nabla J \Vert}$. Setting $c = \alpha \Vert \nabla J \Vert$:

$$\Delta \boldsymbol{\theta} = -\alpha \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k}) \tag{4}$$

Hence:

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} - \alpha \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k}) \tag{5}$$

> *(7 marks: 2 for Taylor expansion + 2 for descent condition + 2 for Cauchy-Schwarz + 1 for final update rule)*

**(b) Convergence Condition (7 marks)**

For $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2} \boldsymbol{\theta}^{T} \mathbf{A} \boldsymbol{\theta}$:

$$\nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbf{A} \boldsymbol{\theta} \tag{6}$$

Substituting into the update rule:

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} - \alpha \mathbf{A} \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} = (\mathbf{I} - \alpha \mathbf{A}) \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} \tag{7}$$

Iterating:

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} = (\mathbf{I} - \alpha \mathbf{A})^{k} \boldsymbol{\theta}_{0} \tag{8}$$

For convergence to zero, all eigenvalues of $(\mathbf{I} - \alpha \mathbf{A})$ must lie strictly inside the unit circle. Let $\lambda_i$ be eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$:

$$\vert 1 - \alpha \lambda_i \vert < 1 \quad \forall i \tag{9}$$

This yields:

$$0 < \alpha < \frac{2}{\lambda_{\max}(\mathbf{A})} \tag{10}$$

For $\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 8 \end{bmatrix}$: eigenvalues are $\lambda_1 = 2$ and $\lambda_2 = 8$. The maximum stable learning rate is:

$$\alpha_{\max} = \frac{2}{8} = 0.25$$

> *(7 marks: 3 for gradient and update substitution + 2 for eigenvalue analysis + 2 for numerical computation)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Style]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the step-by-step algorithmic procedure of the gradient descent method. State clearly the convergence criteria.

**(b)** A robot manipulator's inverse kinematics is to be solved by minimizing $J(\boldsymbol{q}) = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \boldsymbol{x}_{d} - f(\boldsymbol{q}) \Vert_{2}^{2}$, where $f(\boldsymbol{q})$ is the forward kinematics and $\boldsymbol{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the joint vector. Derive the gradient $\nabla J$ and write the update law for $\boldsymbol{q}$. Identify why this method is preferred over closed-form IK in redundant manipulators.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B:**

**(a) Algorithm and Flowchart (7 marks)**

The gradient descent procedure is:

1. **Initialize**: Choose $\boldsymbol{\theta}_0$, learning rate $\alpha > 0$, tolerance $\varepsilon$, max iterations $K_{\max}$. Set $k = 0$.
2. **Compute gradient**: Evaluate $\nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_k)$.
3. **Update parameters**: $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_k - \alpha \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_k)$.
4. **Check convergence**:
   - $\Vert \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_k) \Vert_2 \leq \varepsilon$ (gradient criterion)
   - $\Vert \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} - \boldsymbol{\theta}_k \Vert_2 \leq \varepsilon$ (parameter criterion)
   - $\vert J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1}) - J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_k) \vert \leq \varepsilon$ (cost criterion)
   - $k \geq K_{\max}$ (iteration limit)
5. If any criterion is met, terminate; else increment $k$ and return to Step 2.

**Flowchart** (already shown in SECTION 4.1):

```
Start -> Initialize -> Compute Gradient -> Update Theta -> Check Convergence
                                                                       |
                                                            [Yes] ------+---- [No]
                                                              |               |
                                                          Output Theta*    Increment k
                                                              |               |
                                                            End           (loop back)
```

> *(7 marks: 3 for algorithmic steps + 2 for flowchart description + 2 for convergence criteria)*

**(b) Inverse Kinematics via Gradient Descent (7 marks)**

Given $J(\boldsymbol{q}) = \frac{1}{2} \boldsymbol{e}(\boldsymbol{q})^{T} \boldsymbol{e}(\boldsymbol{q})$ where $\boldsymbol{e}(\boldsymbol{q}) = \boldsymbol{x}_{d} - f(\boldsymbol{q})$:

Using the chain rule:

$$\nabla J(\boldsymbol{q}) = \frac{\partial J}{\partial \boldsymbol{q}} = -\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial \boldsymbol{q}}\right)^{T} \boldsymbol{e}(\boldsymbol{q})$$

Let $\mathbf{J}(\boldsymbol{q}) = \frac{\partial f}{\partial \boldsymbol{q}}$ (the manipulator Jacobian). Then:

$$\nabla J(\boldsymbol{q}) = -\mathbf{J}(\boldsymbol{q})^{T} \boldsymbol{e}(\boldsymbol{q}) \tag{11}$$

The update law is:

$$\boldsymbol{q}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{q}_{k} + \alpha \mathbf{J}(\boldsymbol{q}_{k})^{T} \boldsymbol{e}(\boldsymbol{q}_{k}) \tag{12}$$

**Why preferred in redundant manipulators:**
- Closed-form IK does not exist when $n > 6$ (redundant DOF).
