- Gradient descent can incorporate secondary objectives (obstacle avoidance, joint limits) by adding penalty terms.
- The Jacobian-transpose method naturally handles singularities through regularization.

> *(7 marks: 2 for gradient derivation + 2 for update law + 2 for comparison with closed-form IK + 1 for redundant manipulator benefit)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Forgetting the negative sign**: Many students write $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} + \alpha \nabla J$, which causes **divergence**. Always subtract the gradient.
> - **Conflating convergence criteria**: Examiners award marks separately for **gradient norm**, **parameter change**, and **cost change**. Mention all three.
> - **Missing the role of $\alpha$**: A derivation that does not discuss the learning-rate bound $0 < \alpha < 2/\lambda_{\max}$ loses **2 marks minimum** in Part B.
> - **Sign error in IK gradient**: The gradient of the IK cost involves $-\mathbf{J}^T \boldsymbol{e}$, not $+\mathbf{J}^T \boldsymbol{e}$. The update **adds** $\alpha \mathbf{J}^T \boldsymbol{e}$ to drive the error toward zero.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Gradient Descent (KTU Module 3)**

- **Core Update Rule**: $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} - \alpha \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})$.
- **Direction**: Negative gradient = direction of steepest local descent (proved via Cauchy–Schwarz).
- **Learning Rate**: $0 < \alpha < \frac{2}{\lambda_{\max}(\mathbf{H})}$ ensures convergence for convex quadratic costs.
- **Stopping Criteria**: Gradient norm, parameter change, cost change, or iteration limit.
- **Variants**:
  - **Batch GD** → uses full dataset, stable.
  - **SGD** → uses single sample, noisy but fast.
  - **Mini-Batch** → compromise.
  - **Momentum** → adds velocity term $\beta \boldsymbol{v}_{k}$.
  - **Nesterov** → looks ahead using $\nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_k - \beta \boldsymbol{v}_{k})$.
  - **Adam** → adaptive learning rates using first and second moment estimates.
- **Robotics Applications**:
  - **Inverse Kinematics**: $\boldsymbol{q}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{q}_k + \alpha \mathbf{J}^T \boldsymbol{e}$.
  - **Trajectory Optimization**: Joint-space cost minimization.
  - **Potential Field Navigation**: Repulsive + attractive gradient fields.
- **Taylor Expansion Anchor**: Always derive the update rule starting from $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_k + \Delta \boldsymbol{\theta}) \approx J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_k) + \nabla J^{T} \Delta \boldsymbol{\theta}$.
- **Common Pitfall**: Sign errors in update rules, especially for IK gradient formulation.
- **Hyperparameter Memory**: $\alpha \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-1}]$, $\beta = 0.9$, $\beta_1 = 0.9$, $\beta_2 = 0.999$, $\varepsilon \in [10^{-6}, 10^{-3}]$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gradient Descent without and with Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gradient Descent without and with Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Gradient Descent (GD)** is a first-order, iterative optimization algorithm used in robot motion planning to find the **local minimum** of a differentiable scalar-valued objective (cost) function $f(\mathbf{q})$ defined over the robot's configuration space $\mathcal{C} \subset \mathbb{R}^n$. At each iteration, the configuration $\mathbf{q}$ is updated by moving in the direction of the **negative gradient** $-\nabla f(\mathbf{q})$, which is the direction of steepest descent of the cost function.

Mathematically, the unconstrained update rule is expressed as:

$$\mathbf{q}_{k+1} = \mathbf{q}_k - \alpha_k \, \nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)$$

where:
- $\mathbf{q}_k \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the configuration vector at iteration $k$ (e.g., joint angles of a manipulator).
- $\nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the gradient (column vector of partial derivatives) of the cost function.
- $\alpha_k > 0$ is the **learning rate** (also called *step size*), which may be constant or follow a schedule.

**Constrained Gradient Descent** extends this formulation to optimization problems where the configuration $\mathbf{q}$ must satisfy additional constraints $g_i(\mathbf{q}) \leq 0$ and $h_j(\mathbf{q}) = 0$ (e.g., joint limits, collision avoidance, end-effector pose targets). The most rigorous formulation is the **Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT)** framework, which generalizes the classical **method of Lagrange multipliers** to inequality-constrained problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** In motion planning, $f(\mathbf{q})$ is typically a *potential function* or *navigation function* whose minimum corresponds to the goal configuration. The gradient $-\nabla f$ thus defines a vector field that pushes the robot toward the goal while avoiding local minima where possible.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a blindfolded hiker standing on a foggy, hilly landscape who wants to reach the lowest valley (the goal). The hiker cannot see the valley, but can feel the slope of the ground beneath their feet.

- **The slope they feel is the gradient** $\nabla f(\mathbf{q})$.
- **To descend, they take a step in the opposite direction** (negative gradient).
- **The length of the step is the learning rate** $\alpha_k$. Too small a step → painfully slow descent. Too large a step → the hiker might overshoot the valley and land on the opposite slope.

In the **constrained** version, picture the hiker restricted to a marked trail (constraints) — they cannot walk straight down the slope but must instead follow the trail while still trying to descend. The constrained gradient descent computes a **projected descent direction** that respects these boundaries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Convention:** Throughout this module, bold lowercase letters ($\mathbf{q}$, $\mathbf{x}$) denote column vectors, $\|\cdot\|$ denotes the Euclidean **L2 norm**, and $\nabla f(\mathbf{q}) = \left[ \frac{\partial f}{\partial q_1}, \frac{\partial f}{\partial q_2}, \dots, \frac{\partial f}{\partial q_n} \right]^T$ is treated as a column vector. The learning rate $\alpha$ is dimensionless when $f$ is normalized, but generally has units inverse to those of $f$ to keep $\alpha \nabla f$ dimensionally consistent with $\mathbf{q}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithmic Logic — Unconstrained Gradient Descent

The unconstrained variant follows a strict, repeatable loop:

1. **Initialize** the configuration $\mathbf{q}_0 \in \mathcal{C}$ (could be the robot's current pose or a random feasible start).
2. **Evaluate the gradient** $\nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)$ at the current configuration.
3. **Compute the descent direction** $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)$ (steepest descent direction).
4. **Update the configuration** by stepping along the descent direction: $\mathbf{q}_{k+1} = \mathbf{q}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k$.
5. **Check convergence** using one of:
   - Gradient norm: $\|\nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)\| < \epsilon_1$
   - Step size: $\|\mathbf{q}_{k+1} - \mathbf{q}_k\| < \epsilon_2$
   - Objective change: $|f(\mathbf{q}_{k+1}) - f(\mathbf{q}_k)| < \epsilon_3$
6. If converged, terminate; else, return to step 2.

**Why it works:** For a smooth function, the first-order Taylor expansion gives:

$$f(\mathbf{q}_{k+1}) \approx f(\mathbf{q}_k) + \nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)^T (\mathbf{q}_{k+1} - \mathbf{q}_k) = f(\mathbf{q}_k) - \alpha_k \|\nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)\|^2$$

So as long as $\alpha_k > 0$ and the gradient is non-zero, the objective strictly **decreases** locally.

### 2.2 Learning Rate Strategies

- **Fixed step:** $\alpha_k = \alpha$ (constant). Simple, but risks divergence.
- **Diminishing step (Robbins–Monro):** $\alpha_k = \frac{\alpha_0}{1 + \beta k}$ with $\beta > 0$. Guarantees convergence under mild conditions.
- **Line search (Wolfe / Armijo):** Solve $\min_{\alpha > 0} f(\mathbf{q}_k - \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k))$ at each iteration. More expensive per step but converges in fewer iterations.
- **Adaptive methods (Adam, RMSProp):** Use running estimates of gradient moments to scale step size per coordinate.

### 2.3 Constrained Gradient Descent — Three Principal Variants

#### Variant A: Projected Gradient Descent
Used when the feasible set $\mathcal{Q} = \{ \mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid \mathbf{A}\mathbf{q} \leq \mathbf{b} \}$ is **convex**. After each unconstrained step, project the result back onto $\mathcal{Q}$ using the Euclidean projector $P_{\mathcal{Q}}(\cdot)$:

$$\mathbf{q}_{k+1} = P_{\mathcal{Q}}\!\left( \mathbf{q}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k) \right)$$

#### Variant B: Lagrangian Gradient Descent (Equality Constraints)
Minimize $f(\mathbf{q})$ subject to $h_j(\mathbf{q}) = 0$. Define the **Lagrangian**:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{q}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{q}) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j \, h_j(\mathbf{q})$$

Update both primal and dual variables:

$$\mathbf{q}_{k+1} = \mathbf{q}_k - \alpha_k \nabla_{\mathbf{q}} \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{q}_k, \boldsymbol{\lambda}_k), \quad \boldsymbol{\lambda}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{\lambda}_k + \beta_k \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\lambda}} \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{q}_k, \boldsymbol{\lambda}_k)$$

#### Variant C: KKT Conditions (Full Inequality Treatment)
At a constrained local optimum $(\mathbf{q}^\star, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^\star, \boldsymbol{\mu}^\star)$:

1. **Stationarity:** $\nabla f(\mathbf{q}^\star) + \sum_j \lambda_j^\star \nabla h_j(\mathbf{q}^\star) + \sum_i \mu_i^\star \nabla g_i(\mathbf{q}^\star) = \mathbf{0}$
2. **Primal feasibility:** $g_i(\mathbf{q}^\star) \leq 0$, $\,h_j(\mathbf{q}^\star) = 0$
3. **Dual feasibility:** $\mu_i^\star \geq 0$
4. **Complementary slackness:** $\mu_i^\star \, g_i(\mathbf{q}^\star) = 0$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Quantity** | **Formula / Definition** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Unconstrained update | $\mathbf{q}_{k+1} = \mathbf{q}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)$ | $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| Descent direction | $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)$ | Direction of steepest descent |
| Gradient (column vec) | $\nabla f = [\partial f / \partial q_1, \dots, \partial f / \partial q_n]^T$ | First partial derivatives |
| Armijo condition | $f(\mathbf{q}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k) \leq f(\mathbf{q}_k) + c_1 \alpha \nabla f^T \mathbf{d}_k$ | $0 < c_1 < 1$ |
| Wolfe curvature | $\vert \nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k)^T \mathbf{d}_k \vert \leq c_2 \vert \nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)^T \mathbf{d}_k \vert$ | $c_1 < c_2 < 1$ |
| Lipschitz constant | $\vert \vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}) - \nabla f(\mathbf{y}) \vert \vert \leq L \vert \vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \vert \vert$ | $L > 0$ smoothness bound |
| Stable step bound | $\alpha \in (0,\, 2 / L)$ | Guarantees descent |
| Lagrangian | $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{q}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = f + \sum \lambda_j h_j + \sum \mu_i g_i$ | $\lambda_j$ unconstrained, $\mu_i \geq 0$ |
| Projected GD | $\mathbf{q}_{k+1} = P_{\mathcal{Q}}(\mathbf{q}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k))$ | $\mathcal{Q}$ convex |
| KKT stationarity | $\nabla_{\mathbf{q}} \mathcal{L} = \mathbf{0}$ | Necessary for optimum |
| Complementary slackness | $\mu_i g_i = 0,\ \forall i$ | Active vs. inactive |
| Convergence rate (convex, $L$-smooth) | $f(\mathbf{q}_k) - f^\star = \mathcal{O}(1/k)$ | Sublinear |
| Convergence rate (strongly convex, $\mu$) | Linear / geometric: $\mathcal{O}\!\left( (1 - \mu/L)^k \right)$ | Exponential |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real robot motion planning, projected gradient descent is used to enforce **box constraints** like joint angle limits $q_i^{\min} \leq q_i \leq q_i^{\max}$, and Lagrangian/KKT methods are used to enforce **end-effector pose constraints** or to keep a manipulator on a planned trajectory. The **Lipschitz constant $L$** of the cost function is critical — for a quadratic cost $f(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{q}^T \mathbf{H} \mathbf{q}$, the largest eigenvalue $\lambda_{\max}(\mathbf{H})$ equals $L$, and choosing $\alpha \in (0, 2/\lambda_{\max})$ ensures monotonic descent.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why the Negative Gradient is the Steepest Descent Direction

**Goal:** Show that $-\nabla f(\mathbf{q})$ locally minimizes $f$ the fastest for a given infinitesimal step size.

We wish to choose the unit direction $\mathbf{d}$ (i.e., $\|\mathbf{d}\| = 1$) that minimizes the first-order change in $f$:

$$\Delta f \approx \nabla f(\mathbf{q})^T (\alpha \mathbf{d}) = \alpha \, \nabla f(\mathbf{q})^T \mathbf{d}$$

The direction $\mathbf{d}$ that minimizes the inner product $\nabla f^T \mathbf{d}$ is the one **opposite** to $\nabla f$.

**Formal Cauchy–Schwarz argument:**

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{q})^T \mathbf{d} \geq -\|\nabla f(\mathbf{q})\| \cdot \|\mathbf{d}\| = -\|\nabla f(\mathbf{q})\|$$

Equality holds **if and only if** $\mathbf{d} = -\nabla f(\mathbf{q}) / \|\nabla f(\mathbf{q})\|$.

Therefore the unit direction of steepest descent is $\mathbf{d}^\star = -\nabla f / \|\nabla f\|$, and the unconstrained update follows.

### 3.2 Derivation: Convergence of Unconstrained GD for $L$-Smooth Convex Functions

**Setup:** Assume $f$ is convex and $L$-smooth. Choose a fixed step size $\alpha = 1/L$.

**Step 1 — Smoothness inequality.** By $L$-smoothness:

$$f(\mathbf{q}_{k+1}) \leq f(\mathbf{q}_k) + \nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)^T (\mathbf{q}_{k+1} - \mathbf{q}_k) + \frac{L}{2}\|\mathbf{q}_{k+1} - \mathbf{q}_k\|^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the GD update** $\mathbf{q}_{k+1} - \mathbf{q}_k = -\alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)$:

$$f(\mathbf{q}_{k+1}) \leq f(\mathbf{q}_k) - \alpha \|\nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)\|^2 + \frac{L \alpha^2}{2} \|\nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)\|^2$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the right-hand side:**

$$f(\mathbf{q}_{k+1}) \leq f(\mathbf{q}_k) - \alpha \left(1 - \frac{L\alpha}{2}\right) \|\nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)\|^2$$

**Step 4 — Choose $\alpha = 1/L$:**

$$f(\mathbf{q}_{k+1}) \leq f(\mathbf{q}_k) - \frac{1}{2L} \|\nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)\|^2$$

This shows $f$ **strictly decreases** by an amount proportional to the squared gradient norm.

**Step 5 — Sum telescoping series from $k=0$ to $K-1$:**

$$\sum_{k=0}^{K-1} \left[ f(\mathbf{q}_k) - f(\mathbf{q}_{k+1}) \right] \geq \frac{1}{2L} \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} \|\nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)\|^2$$

$$f(\mathbf{q}_0) - f(\mathbf{q}_K) \geq \frac{1}{2L} \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} \|\nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)\|^2$$

**Step 6 — Bound the minimum gradient norm.** By convexity, $f(\mathbf{q}_K) \geq f^\star$, so:

$$\frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} \|\nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)\|^2 \leq \frac{2L \left[ f(\mathbf{q}_0) - f^\star \right]}{K}$$

$$\min_{0 \leq k \leq K-1} \|\nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)\| \leq \sqrt{\frac{2L[f(\mathbf{q}_0) - f^\star]}{K}}$$

This is the celebrated **$\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{K})$** sublinear rate for convex $L$-smooth functions.

### 3.3 Derivation: Constrained Minimum via the Method of Lagrange Multipliers

**Problem:** Minimize $f(\mathbf{q})$ subject to $h(\mathbf{q}) = 0$.

**Step 1 — Form the Lagrangian** with multiplier $\lambda$:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{q}, \lambda) = f(\mathbf{q}) + \lambda \, h(\mathbf{q})$$

**Step 2 — Set partial derivatives to zero** (first-order necessary conditions):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{q}} = \nabla f(\mathbf{q}) + \lambda \, \nabla h(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{0}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \lambda} = h(\mathbf{q}) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Geometric interpretation:** The gradient $\nabla f$ at the constrained optimum must be a **linear combination** of the constraint gradient $\nabla h$, i.e., the level set of $f$ is *tangent* to the constraint surface.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** Use gradient descent to minimize $f(q) = (q - 4)^2$ starting from $q_0 = 0$ with $\alpha = 0.3$. Run for 4 iterations and check convergence.

**Step 1 — Compute the gradient:**

$$\nabla f(q) = 2(q - 4)$$

**Step 2 — Update rule:**

$$q_{k+1} = q_k - \alpha \cdot 2(q_k - 4) = q_k - 0.6 (q_k - 4) = 0.4 q_k + 2.4$$

**Step 3 — Iterations:**

$$\begin{aligned}
q_0 &= 0 \\
q_1 &= 0.4(0) + 2.4 = 2.4 \\
q_2 &= 0.4(2.4) + 2.4 = 0.96 + 2.4 = 3.36 \\
q_3 &= 0.4(3.36) + 2.4 = 1.344 + 2.4 = 3.744 \\
q_4 &= 0.4(3.744) + 2.4 = 1.4976 + 2.4 = 3.8976
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convergence check:** The true minimum is $q^\star = 4$ with $f^\star = 0$. After 4 iterations $q_4 \approx 3.898$, error $\approx 0.102$, confirming convergence.

> [!NOTE]
> **General closed-form:** For this quadratic with step $\alpha$, the iterates obey $q_k = 4 - 4(1 - 2\alpha)^k = 4 - 4(0.4)^k$, which matches the numerical values above.

### 3.5 Constrained Example via Lagrange Multipliers

**Problem:** Minimize $f(q_1, q_2) = q_1^2 + q_2^2$ subject to $q_1 + q_2 = 2$.

**Step 1 — Lagrangian:**

$$\mathcal{L} = q_1^2 + q_2^2 + \lambda (q_1 + q_2 - 2)$$

**Step 2 — Stationarity:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_1} = 2q_1 + \lambda = 0 \implies q_1 = -\lambda/2$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_2} = 2q_2 + \lambda = 0 \implies q_2 = -\lambda/2$$

**Step 3 — Primal feasibility:**

$$q_1 + q_2 = 2 \implies -\lambda = 2 \implies \lambda = -2$$

**Step 4 — Optimum:**

$$q_1^\star = q_2^\star = 1,\quad f^\star = 1^2 + 1^2 = 2$$

The gradient of $f$ at the optimum is $\nabla f = [2, 2]^T$, and the gradient of the constraint is $\nabla h = [1, 1]^T$. We verify $\nabla f = -\lambda \nabla h = 2 [1,1]^T$. ✓

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithm Flow: Unconstrained Gradient Descent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Initialize q0 and alpha]
    B --> C{k = 0, 1, 2, ...}
    C --> D[Compute gradient nabla f at qk]
    D --> E[Compute descent direction dk = -nabla f]
    E --> F[Update qk+1 = qk + alpha dk]
    F --> G{Convergence check}
    G -- Not converged --> C
    G -- Converged --> H[Output qstar]
    H --> I([End])

    style A fill:#1f6feb,color:#fff
    style I fill:#1f6feb,color:#fff
    style H fill:#2da44e,color:#fff
    style G fill:#bf8700,color:#fff
    style C fill:#bf8700,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Algorithm Flow: Constrained Gradient Descent (KKT / Lagrangian)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Define f, g_i, h_j, and feasible set Q]
    B --> C[Initialize q0, lambda0, mu0]
    C --> D[k = 0, 1, 2, ...]
    D --> E[Compute gradients of f, g_i, h_j at qk]
    E --> F[Form Lagrangian L]
    F --> G[Compute gradient w.r.t. q: nabla_q L]
    G --> H[Update dual variables lambda, mu]
    H --> I[Update primal qk+1 = qk - alpha nabla_q L]
    I --> J{Project onto feasible set}
    J -- If outside --> K[Project qk+1 onto Q]
    J -- If inside --> L[Keep qk+1]
    K --> M{KKT residuals < epsilon}
    L --> M
    M -- No --> D
    M -- Yes --> N[Output qstar, lambda, mu]
    N --> O([End])

    style A fill:#1f6feb,color:#fff
    style O fill:#1f6feb,color:#fff
    style N fill:#2da44e,color:#fff
    style M fill:#bf8700,color:#fff
    style D fill:#bf8700,color:#fff
    style J fill:#cf222e,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Gradient Descent in a Motion Planning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Perception_Module
        A1[Robot State Estimator] --> A2[Configuration Vector q]
        A2 --> A3[Obstacle / Map Data]
    end

    subgraph Cost_Module
        B1[Goal Attraction Potential] --> B3[Composite Cost f q]
        B2[Obstacle Repulsion Potential] --> B3
        A2 --> B1
        A3 --> B2
    end

    subgraph Optimizer_Module
        B3 --> C1[Gradient Evaluator nabla f]
        C1 --> C2[Constraint Checker g q, h q]
        C2 -- Feasible --> C3[Update q via GD]
        C2 -- Infeasible --> C4[Lagrangian / KKT Corrector]
        C4 --> C3
        C3 --> C5{Converged}
    end

    subgraph Control_Module
        C5 -- Yes --> D1[Send qstar to Controller]
        C5 -- No --> B3
        D1 --> D2[Actuator Commands]
    end

    style B3 fill:#9e6a03,color:#fff
    style C3 fill:#2da44e,color:#fff
    style C4 fill:#cf222e,color:#fff
    style D2 fill:#1f6feb,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Diagram: Effect of Step Size on Convergence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step alpha small] --> T1[Slow convergence\nOvershoot risk low]
    S2[Step alpha = 1 slash L] --> T2[Optimal monotonic descent\nFastest stable rate]
    S3[Step alpha > 2 slash L] --> T3[Divergence or oscillation\nCost increases]

    style S1 fill:#0969da,color:#fff
    style S2 fill:#2da44e,color:#fff
    style S3 fill:#cf222e,color:#fff
    style T1 fill:#eaeef2,color:#000
    style T2 fill:#eaeef2,color:#000
    style T3 fill:#eaeef2,color:#000
```
