## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define gradient descent. State the unconstrained update rule and explain the role of the learning rate $\alpha$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Gradient descent is a first-order iterative optimization method that minimizes a differentiable scalar function $f(\mathbf{q})$ by repeatedly moving the variable $\mathbf{q}$ in the direction opposite to the gradient. The update rule is:

$$\mathbf{q}_{k+1} = \mathbf{q}_k - \alpha_k \, \nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)$$

The **learning rate** $\alpha_k > 0$ scales the step size. A small $\alpha_k$ gives slow but stable convergence; a large $\alpha_k$ may overshoot minima or cause divergence. For $L$-smooth functions, choosing $\alpha_k \in (0, 2/L)$ guarantees monotonic descent.

*(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — update rule, 1 mark — role of learning rate.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*State the Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) conditions for the problem: minimize $f(\mathbf{q})$ subject to $g_i(\mathbf{q}) \leq 0$, $h_j(\mathbf{q}) = 0$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

At a local optimum $(\mathbf{q}^\star, \boldsymbol{\mu}^\star, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^\star)$:

1. **Stationarity:** $\nabla f(\mathbf{q}^\star) + \sum_i \mu_i^\star \nabla g_i(\mathbf{q}^\star) + \sum_j \lambda_j^\star \nabla h_j(\mathbf{q}^\star) = \mathbf{0}$
2. **Primal feasibility:** $g_i(\mathbf{q}^\star) \leq 0$ and $h_j(\mathbf{q}^\star) = 0$
3. **Dual feasibility:** $\mu_i^\star \geq 0$ for all $i$
4. **Complementary slackness:** $\mu_i^\star \, g_i(\mathbf{q}^\star) = 0$ for all $i$

*(1 mark per condition; 4 conditions × 3/4 ≈ 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the unconstrained gradient descent update rule from the first-order Taylor expansion of $f(\mathbf{q})$. Show that the steepest descent direction is $-\nabla f(\mathbf{q})$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply gradient descent with learning rate $\alpha = 0.25$ to minimize $f(q) = (q - 5)^2$ starting from $q_0 = 0$. Compute $q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4$ and the corresponding function values. Comment on convergence. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The first-order Taylor expansion of $f$ around $\mathbf{q}_k$ is:

$$f(\mathbf{q}_k + \Delta \mathbf{q}) \approx f(\mathbf{q}_k) + \nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)^T \Delta \mathbf{q}$$

We wish to choose $\Delta \mathbf{q}$ with $\|\Delta \mathbf{q}\| = \alpha$ to minimize the right-hand side. The most negative inner product $\nabla f^T \Delta \mathbf{q}$ is achieved when $\Delta \mathbf{q}$ is anti-parallel to $\nabla f$:

$$\Delta \mathbf{q}^\star = -\alpha \, \frac{\nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)}{\|\nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)\|}$$

For computational simplicity, drop the normalization (absorbed into $\alpha$):

$$\Delta \mathbf{q} = -\alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)$$

Hence the update rule:

$$\mathbf{q}_{k+1} = \mathbf{q}_k - \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)$$

By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, no other unit direction $\mathbf{d}$ gives a larger decrease than $-\nabla f/\|\nabla f\|$, confirming it is the steepest descent direction. **(7 marks: 3 for Taylor setup, 2 for minimization, 2 for C-S bound.)**

**(b) Numerical iteration (7 marks):**

Gradient: $\nabla f(q) = 2(q - 5)$. Update: $q_{k+1} = q_k - 0.25 \cdot 2(q_k - 5) = 0.5 q_k + 2.5$.

$$\begin{aligned}
q_0 &= 0, \quad f(q_0) = 25 \\
q_1 &= 0.5(0) + 2.5 = 2.5, \quad f(q_1) = (2.5 - 5)^2 = 6.25 \\
q_2 &= 0.5(2.5) + 2.5 = 3.75, \quad f(q_2) = (3.75 - 5)^2 = 1.5625 \\
q_3 &= 0.5(3.75) + 2.5 = 4.375, \quad f(q_3) = (4.375 - 5)^2 = 0.390625 \\
q_4 &= 0.5(4.375) + 2.5 = 4.6875, \quad f(q_4) = (4.6875 - 5)^2 \approx 0.0977
\end{aligned}$$

**Convergence comment:** True minimum is $q^\star = 5$ with $f^\star = 0$. The sequence converges geometrically with ratio $0.5$ per iteration, i.e., $q_k = 5 - 5(0.5)^k$. The algorithm is **monotonically convergent** because $\alpha = 0.25 \in (0, 2/L)$ where $L = 2$ (from $f'' = 2$).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** State the general constrained optimization problem. With the Lagrangian $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{q}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = f(\mathbf{q}) + \sum_j \lambda_j h_j(\mathbf{q}) + \sum_i \mu_i g_i(\mathbf{q})$, write down the four KKT conditions and explain the meaning of complementary slackness. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Use the method of Lagrange multipliers to minimize $f(q_1, q_2) = q_1^2 + 2q_2^2$ subject to $q_1 + q_2 = 1$. Verify your answer is a constrained minimum using the bordered Hessian. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) KKT derivation (7 marks):**

General constrained problem: minimize $f(\mathbf{q})$ subject to $g_i(\mathbf{q}) \leq 0$ and $h_j(\mathbf{q}) = 0$, $i = 1, \dots, p$, $j = 1, \dots, m$.

The four KKT conditions at $(\mathbf{q}^\star, \boldsymbol{\mu}^\star, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^\star)$ are:

1. **Stationarity:** $\nabla_{\mathbf{q}} \mathcal{L} = \nabla f(\mathbf{q}^\star) + \sum_i \mu_i^\star \nabla g_i(\mathbf{q}^\star) + \sum_j \lambda_j^\star \nabla h_j(\mathbf{q}^\star) = \mathbf{0}$
2. **Primal feasibility:** $g_i(\mathbf{q}^\star) \leq 0$, $h_j(\mathbf{q}^\star) = 0$
3. **Dual feasibility:** $\mu_i^\star \geq 0$
4. **Complementary slackness:** $\mu_i^\star \, g_i(\mathbf{q}^\star) = 0$

**Complementary slackness meaning:** For each inequality constraint, either the multiplier is zero (constraint **inactive**, the optimum lies strictly inside the feasible region with respect to this constraint) or the constraint is active (multiplier may be positive). This enforces the idea that a multiplier is "paying" for a binding constraint.

**(b) Lagrange multiplier solution (7 marks):**

Lagrangian:

$$\mathcal{L} = q_1^2 + 2 q_2^2 + \lambda (q_1 + q_2 - 1)$$

Stationarity:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_1} = 2q_1 + \lambda = 0 \implies q_1 = -\lambda/2$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_2} = 4q_2 + \lambda = 0 \implies q_2 = -\lambda/4$$

Primal feasibility: $q_1 + q_2 = 1 \implies -\lambda/2 - \lambda/4 = 1 \implies -3\lambda/4 = 1 \implies \lambda = -4/3$.

Hence:

$$q_1^\star = 2/3,\quad q_2^\star = 1/3,\quad f^\star = (2/3)^2 + 2(1/3)^2 = 4/9 + 2/9 = 6/9 = 2/3$$

**Bordered Hessian check:** Hessian of $f$ is $\mathbf{H} = \text{diag}(2, 4)$. Bordered Hessian:

$$\mathbf{H}_b = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \nabla h^T \\ \nabla h & \mathbf{H} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

For one equality constraint in 2 variables, the sign of the leading principal minor of order 2 must be checked: $\det \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = -1 < 0$ (since $m = 1$ is odd, we need this sign to indicate a **minimum**). Indeed $-1 < 0$ confirms a constrained minimum. ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to check dual feasibility** ($\mu_i \geq 0$) in KKT problems — this costs 1 full mark in 7-mark questions.
> 2. **Confusing stationary point type** (min vs. max vs. saddle) — the bordered Hessian test is mandatory for constrained second-order conditions; missing it loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Choosing $\alpha$ outside $(0, 2/L)$** — leads to divergence, and the student is expected to verify the bound numerically. A single mark is deducted for not stating the step-size bound.
> 4. **Skipping the convergence comment** in numerical GD problems — examiners require an explicit statement of the rate (linear/geometric) and the closed-form trajectory.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Unconstrained GD update:** $\mathbf{q}_{k+1} = \mathbf{q}_k - \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k)$.
- **Steepest descent direction** is $-\nabla f(\mathbf{q})$ (proved via Cauchy–Schwarz).
- **Stable step size** for $L$-smooth $f$: $\alpha \in (0, 2/L)$.
- **Convex $L$-smooth:** sublinear rate $\mathcal{O}(1/k)$ in function value.
- **Strongly convex (modulus $\mu$):** linear rate $\mathcal{O}((1 - \mu/L)^k)$.
- **Armijo (sufficient decrease)** and **Wolfe (curvature)** conditions are used in line search.
- **Three constrained variants:** projected GD, Lagrangian GD, full KKT.
- **KKT has 4 parts:** stationarity, primal feasibility, dual feasibility, complementary slackness.
- **Lagrangian:** $\mathcal{L} = f + \sum \lambda_j h_j + \sum \mu_i g_i$, with $\mu_i \geq 0$.
- **Bordered Hessian sign test** determines whether a constrained stationary point is a min, max, or saddle.
- **Applications in robot motion planning:** potential-field navigation, trajectory optimization, inverse kinematics, and compliant control all rely on (constrained) gradient descent.
- **Common pitfalls:** local minima, ill-conditioning, slow convergence near saddle points, step-size tuning.
- **Higher-order alternatives (for reference):** Newton's method uses $\mathbf{H}^{-1} \nabla f$ and gives quadratic convergence near the optimum but is expensive per step.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minkowski Sum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Minkowski Sum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Minkowski Sum — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robot Motion Planning**, the **Minkowski Sum** is a fundamental set-theoretic binary operation used to construct **Configuration Space Obstacles (C-obstacles)**. Given two sets $A$ and $B$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$, the Minkowski sum is formally defined as:

$$A \oplus B = \{a + b \;\mid\; a \in A, \; b \in B\}$$

Equivalently, it can be expressed as the **union of translated copies** of one set over every point of the other:

$$A \oplus B = \bigcup_{b \in B} (A + b) = \bigcup_{a \in A} (a + B)$$

For motion planning, the *reflected* (or *dilation*) operation is more critical:

$$A \oplus (-B) = \{a - b \;\mid\; a \in A, \; b \in B\}$$

where $-B = \{-b \mid b \in B\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The C-obstacle for a translating robot $R$ and a workspace obstacle $W_i$ is given by:
> $$C_{obs} = W_i \oplus (-R)$$
> This is the *single most important equation* in Module 3 and is a guaranteed KTU question.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Paintbrush Sweep"

Imagine you are holding a **paintbrush shaped like your robot** $R$, and you want to paint every spot the brush *touches* as you slide it around a stationary obstacle $W$ so that the brush never penetrates $W$. The painted region is exactly the Minkowski sum $W \oplus R$.

Alternatively, picture a **circular sweeping lawnmower blade** rotating about a fixed pivot point. As the blade sweeps, every point on the blade traces a circular arc. The total region covered by the blade's sweep is the Minkowski sum of the blade's shape and the circle traced by each of its points. The "center" of the blade must avoid this swept region to prevent collision.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important for robots?** Without Minkowski sums, you must check whether *every* point of the robot collides with *every* obstacle at *every* timestep. With C-space + Minkowski sum, you **collapse the entire robot into a single point** and grow the obstacles instead. This turns a hard problem into a tractable one.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Parameters

- **Dimensionality $n$:** Typically $n = 2$ (planar) or $n = 3$ (spatial) for workspace; C-space may be higher when rotations are included.
- **Computational complexity for convex polygons:** $O(m + n)$ where $m, n$ are the number of edges.
- **Configuration space dimension for a free-flying rigid body in 2D:** $SE(2) = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ (**3 DOF**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 3: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

The Minkowski sum is built upon the following structural properties, each of which has direct algorithmic consequences in motion planners:

- **Commutativity:** $A \oplus B = B \oplus A$. Order of operands does not matter.
- **Associativity:** $(A \oplus B) \oplus C = A \oplus (B \oplus C)$. Allows incremental obstacle growth.
- **Identity Element:** $A \oplus \{0\} = A$. The singleton at the origin leaves a set invariant.
- **Convexity Preservation:** If $A$ and $B$ are both **convex**, then $A \oplus B$ is also convex.
- **Scaling Property:** For scalar $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$, $(\lambda A) \oplus (\lambda B) = \lambda(A \oplus B)$.
- **Translation Property:** $(A \oplus B) + t = (A + t) \oplus B = A \oplus (B + t)$.
- **Boundary Operation:** The boundary $\partial(A \oplus B)$ is the envelope traced by translated copies of $\partial B$ as the translation point ranges over $\partial A$ (and vice versa).

## 2.2 Role in Configuration Space Construction

The operational logic for transforming a *workspace* motion planning problem into a *configuration space* problem is:

1. **Define** the robot $R$ (as a subset of the workspace) and the obstacle $W$.
2. **Anchor** the robot at a reference point $q_0$ (typically the origin of the robot's body frame).
3. **Grow** the obstacle by the *reflected* robot shape $-R$ (reflect through origin).
4. The new "grown" obstacle $C_{obs} = W \oplus (-R)$ represents all configurations $q$ of the reference point $q_0$ that would cause the robot $R$ to intersect $W$.
5. The **free configuration space** becomes: $C_{free} = C_{space} \setminus C_{obs}$.
6. The motion planning problem reduces to finding a curve for a *point* through $C_{free}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** The operation $W \oplus (-R)$ is equivalent to sweeping the *reflected* robot body around the boundary of $W$. The result is a "fattened" obstacle whose boundary is offset outward from $W$ by the shape of $R$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Meaning / Use |
|---|--------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $A \oplus B = \{a + b \mid a \in A,\; b \in B\}$ | Definitional form |
| 2 | $A \oplus B = \bigcup_{b \in B} (A + b)$ | Sweep representation |
| 3 | $-B = \{-b \mid b \in B\}$ | Reflection about origin |
| 4 | $C_{obs} = W_i \oplus (-R)$ | **C-obstacle construction** |
| 5 | $C_{free} = C_{space} \setminus C_{obs}$ | Free configuration space |
| 6 | $A \oplus B = B \oplus A$ | Commutativity |
| 7 | $(A \oplus B) \oplus C = A \oplus (B \oplus C)$ | Associativity |
| 8 | $\partial(A \oplus B)$ = envelope of $\partial A + \partial B$ | Boundary representation |
| 9 | $\text{vol}(A \oplus B) \geq \text{vol}(A) + \text{vol}(B)$ | Volume monotonicity |
| 10 | $A \oplus \{0\} = A$ | Identity |
| 11 | For a translating disc of radius $r$: $W \oplus D_r$ = $W$ "grown" by $r$ | Offset / dilation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When the robot is a *translating disc of radius $r$*, the C-obstacle is simply the **Minkowski sum of the workspace obstacle with a disc of radius $r$**, which geometrically equals the obstacle "grown" by $r$ in all directions. This is a very common 7-mark sub-question.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Inflating lane boundaries and static obstacles by the vehicle footprint to plan a safe centerline trajectory.
- **CNC Machining:** Computing the *swept volume* of a cutting tool so the operator can verify the gouge-free region.
- **Robotic Surgery:** Defining the no-go zone for a surgical tool tip by dilating anatomical structures by the tool radius.
- **Computational Geometry:** Used in **GJK (Gilbert-Johnson-Keerthi)** collision detection algorithms and **Voronoi diagram** construction.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — C-Obstacle for a Translating Polygonal Robot

**Problem Setup (typical KTU problem):** A translating triangular robot has vertices at $R = \{(0,0), (2,0), (1,1.5)\}$. A square obstacle has vertices at $W = \{(3,3), (5,3), (5,5), (3,5)\}$. Compute the C-obstacle $C_{obs} = W \oplus (-R)$.

### Step 1 — Compute the Reflected Robot $-R$

Reflect every vertex of $R$ through the origin by negating both coordinates:

$$-R = \{(0,0), (-2,0), (-1,-1.5)\}$$

### Step 2 — Minkowski Sum as Sweep

The Minkowski sum $W \oplus (-R)$ is the union of all translated copies of $W$ by each point $p \in -R$:

$$C_{obs} = W \oplus (-R) = \bigcup_{p \in -R} (W + p)$$

Substituting the three reflected vertices $p_1 = (0,0)$, $p_2 = (-2,0)$, $p_3 = (-1,-1.5)$:

$$C_{obs} = (W + (0,0)) \cup (W + (-2,0)) \cup (W + (-1,-1.5))$$

### Step 3 — Vertices of the C-Obstacle

For two convex polygons with $m$ and $n$ vertices, the Minkowski sum has at most $m + n$ vertices. We compute vertex-wise:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{C_{obs}} &= \{ w + p \;\mid\; w \in V_W,\; p \in V_{-R} \} \\
&= \{(3,3) + (0,0),\; (5,3) + (0,0),\; (5,5) + (0,0),\; (3,5) + (0,0), \\
&\quad (3,3) + (-2,0),\; (5,3) + (-2,0),\; (5,5) + (-2,0),\; (3,5) + (-2,0), \\
&\quad (3,3) + (-1,-1.5),\; (5,3) + (-1,-1.5),\; (5,5) + (-1,-1.5),\; (3,5) + (-1,-1.5) \}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Convex Hull to Obtain the Final C-Obstacle

After computing all 12 candidate vertices, the **convex hull** of these points yields the boundary of $C_{obs}$. The extreme points (those not interior to the hull) form the vertices of the grown obstacle, which is a convex polygon that "wraps" the original square.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Evaluation Note:** Most students lose marks here by listing *all* 12 candidate points as vertices. Examiners expect you to identify the **convex hull boundary** and discard interior points. Always conclude with: *"The C-obstacle is the convex hull of $\{w + p \mid w \in V_W, p \in V_{-R}\}$."*

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Disc Robot & Polygon Obstacle (Offset Curve)

**Problem Setup:** Robot is a *disc* of radius $r = 2$ centered at origin. Workspace obstacle is a triangle with vertices $A = (0,0)$, $B = (4,0)$, $C = (0,3)$. Compute $C_{obs} = W \oplus D_2$.

### Step 1 — Discretize the Disc Approximation

The disc $D_2$ has the boundary:

$$D_2 = \{(x,y) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid x^2 + y^2 \leq 4\}$$

### Step 2 — Minkowski Sum Equivalent to Inward/Outward Offset

For a polygon $W$ and a disc $D_r$, the Minkowski sum is geometrically equivalent to **offsetting the polygon's edges outward by $r$** and **rounding the corners with circular arcs of radius $r$**.

Mathematically:

$$W \oplus D_r = \{ p \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid \text{dist}(p, W) \leq r \}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Outward Normal Offsets

For each edge of triangle $ABC$, the outward normal is computed, and the edge is shifted outward by $r = 2$.

Edge $\overline{AB}$: from $A(0,0)$ to $B(4,0)$, outward normal is $\hat{n} = (0,-1)$. Offset line: $y = -2$.

Edge $\overline{BC}$: from $B(4,0)$ to $C(0,3)$, edge vector is $(-4, 3)$, length $= 5$, unit normal $\hat{n} = (3/5, 4/5)$. Line equation: $3x + 4y = 0$. Offset by $r = 2$ along normal: $3x + 4y = 2 \cdot 5 = 10$.

Edge $\overline{CA}$: from $C(0,3)$ to $A(0,0)$, edge vector is $(0, -3)$, unit normal $\hat{n} = (1, 0)$. Line equation: $x = 0$. Offset line: $x = -2$.

### Step 4 — Find the Offset Vertices

Solve the pairwise intersection of the three offset lines:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{V}_1 &: y = -2 \;\cap\; 3x + 4y = 10 \;\Rightarrow\; 3x - 8 = 10 \;\Rightarrow\; x = 6,\; y = -2 \\
\text{V}_2 &: 3x + 4y = 10 \;\cap\; x = -2 \;\Rightarrow\; -6 + 4y = 10 \;\Rightarrow\; y = 4,\; x = -2 \\
\text{V}_3 &: x = -2 \;\cap\; y = -2 \;\Rightarrow\; (-2, -2)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Add the Rounded Corners

At each offset vertex, attach a **circular arc of radius $r = 2$**. The center of each arc is located at the *original* triangle vertex. The three arc centers are $A = (0,0)$, $B = (4,0)$, $C = (0,3)$, each with sweep angle equal to the *external angle* of the original vertex.

### Final Answer

$$C_{obs} = \triangle V_1 V_2 V_3 \text{ with three quarter arcs of radius 2 at each corner}$$

## 3.3 Analogy Matrix — Minkowski Sum vs. Related Operations

| Operation | Formula | Geometric Meaning | KTU Use |
|-----------|---------|-------------------|---------|
| **Minkowski Sum** | $A \oplus B = \{a+b\}$ | Sweep $A$ along $B$ | C-obstacle construction |
| **Minkowski Difference** | $A \ominus B = A \oplus (-B)$ | Erode $A$ by $B$ | Inverse / collision test |
| **Cartesian Product** | $A \times B$ | Pair elements | Higher-dim spaces |
| **Convex Hull** | $\text{conv}(A)$ | Smallest convex wrapper | Hull of $A \oplus B$ |

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Algebraic Verification of Associativity

**Problem Setup:** Prove $(A \oplus B) \oplus C = A \oplus (B \oplus C)$ for $A = \{0,1\}$, $B = \{2,3\}$, $C = \{5\}$.

### Step 1 — Compute $A \oplus B$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \oplus B &= \{a + b \mid a \in A, b \in B\} \\
&= \{0+2,\; 0+3,\; 1+2,\; 1+3\} \\
&= \{2, 3, 4, 5\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute $(A \oplus B) \oplus C$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(A \oplus B) \oplus C &= \{x + c \mid x \in \{2,3,4,5\},\; c \in \{5\}\} \\
&= \{7, 8, 9, 10\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute $B \oplus C$

$$
\begin{aligned}
B \oplus C &= \{b + c \mid b \in B,\; c \in C\} \\
&= \{2+5,\; 3+5\} \\
&= \{7, 8\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute $A \oplus (B \oplus C)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \oplus (B \oplus C) &= \{a + y \mid a \in \{0,1\},\; y \in \{7,8\}\} \\
&= \{7, 8, 8, 9\} \\
&= \{7, 8, 9\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Re-examination

The two results *appear* different, but this is because $A \oplus B = \{2,3,4,5\}$ contained $\{4,5\}$ and the second computation lost the 10. The correct LHS is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(A \oplus B) \oplus C &= \{2+5,\; 3+5,\; 4+5,\; 5+5\} = \{7,8,9,10\}
\end{aligned}
$$

And $A \oplus (B \oplus C) = \{0+7,\; 0+8,\; 1+7,\; 1+8\} = \{7, 8, 8, 9\} = \{7,8,9\}$.

**Correction:** $A \oplus B$ should be reconsidered — actually both sides must equal. Let us recount:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \oplus B &= \{0+2, 0+3, 1+2, 1+3\} = \{2, 3, 3, 4\} = \{2,3,4\}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Correcting the set notation — duplicates collapse.) Then:

$$
(A \oplus B) \oplus C = \{2+5, 3+5, 4+5\} = \{7, 8, 9\}
$$

And $A \oplus (B \oplus C) = \{0+7, 1+7, 0+8, 1+8\} = \{7, 8, 8, 9\} = \{7, 8, 9\}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Both sides equal $\{7, 8, 9\}$**, confirming associativity. The earlier discrepancy was a set-deduplication oversight — a common student error.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Module 3: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — C-Obstacle Construction Pipeline

The following mermaid diagram depicts the **computational pipeline** for converting a workspace motion planning problem into a configuration space problem using the Minkowski sum:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Workspace Motion Planning Problem] --> A1[Input: Robot Body R in Workspace Coordinates]
    A1 --> A2[Input: Workspace Obstacles W1 W2 ... Wn]
    A2 --> A3[Step 1: Select Reference Point q0 on Robot Body]
    A3 --> A4[Step 2: Compute Reflected Robot -R by Negating All Vertices]
    A4 --> A5[Step 3: For Each Obstacle Wi, Compute Ci = Wi plus -R]
    A5 --> A6[Step 4: Union All Ci to Form Cobs]
    A6 --> A7[Step 5: Compute Cfree = Cspace minus Cobs]
    A7 --> A8[Step 6: Apply Point Path Planner on Cfree]
    A8 --> A9[Output: Collision-Free Trajectory in C-Space]
    A9 --> A10[Step 7: Map Back to Workspace to Get Robot Trajectory]
    A10 --> A11[End]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Minkowski Sum Computation for Convex Polygons

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Input: Polygon P with m edges] --> B1[Input: Polygon Q with n edges]
    B1 --> B2[Compute Edge Vectors of P and Q]
    B2 --> B3[Sort All Edges by Angle theta]
    B3 --> B4[Initialize Sum Polygon S at p + q]
    B4 --> B5[Traverse Edges Using Two-Pointer Merge]
    B5 --> B6[Append Vertices to Sum Polygon S]
    B6 --> B7{All Edges Processed?}
    B7 -->|No| B5
    B7 -->|Yes| B8[Return Convex Polygon S = P plus Q]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Offset / Dilation Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Workspace Obstacle W: Triangle] --> C1[Apply Minkowski Sum with Disc of Radius r]
    C1 --> C2[Edges Offset Outward by r]
    C2 --> C3[Vertices Replaced by Arcs of Radius r]
    C3 --> C4[Final C-Obstacle: Rounded Triangle]
    C4 --> C5[Reference Point Path Must Avoid C-Obstacle]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** In the first diagram, node A0 (start), A1 through A11 follow the canonical Lozano-Pérez configuration space construction algorithm. The crucial transform happens at A5 (Minkowski sum step). In the second diagram, the **two-pointer edge merge** is the standard $O(m+n)$ algorithm for convex polygon Minkowski sum.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Module 3: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Define Minkowski Sum. State and justify the formula for constructing a configuration space obstacle.**

### Model Answer (3 marks):

The Minkowski sum of two sets $A$ and $B$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is defined as:

$$A \oplus B = \{a + b \mid a \in A,\; b \in B\}$$

For a translating robot $R$ and workspace obstacle $W$, the configuration space obstacle is constructed as:

$$C_{obs} = W \oplus (-R)$$

**Justification:** $-R$ is the reflection of $R$ through its reference point. The Minkowski sum $W \oplus (-R)$ represents the locus of all reference point positions for which *some* point of the robot lies inside the obstacle. The reference point must therefore avoid $C_{obs}$ to guarantee a collision-free configuration. **(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — formula, 1 mark — justification)**

---

> **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **State any four properties of the Minkowski sum.**

### Model Answer (3 marks):

1. **Commutativity:** $A \oplus B = B \oplus A$ — order of operands does not affect the result. **(0.75 mark)**
2. **Associativity:** $(A \oplus B) \oplus C = A \oplus (B \oplus C)$ — permits incremental growth. **(0.75 mark)**
3. **Convexity Preservation:** If $A$ and $B$ are convex, then $A \oplus B$ is convex. **(0.75 mark)**
4. **Identity:** $A \oplus \{0\} = A$ — the origin singleton acts as identity. **(0.75 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
> **(a)** Define the Minkowski sum. Using a clear diagram, explain how the Minkowski sum is used to construct the configuration space obstacle for a translating polygonal robot in a workspace containing a single polygonal obstacle. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A translating robot is a triangle with vertices at $R = \{(1,1), (3,1), (2,3)\}$. A workspace obstacle is a square with vertices at $W = \{(4,4), (7,4), (7,7), (4,7)\}$. Compute the C-obstacle $C_{obs} = W \oplus (-R)$ and list its vertices. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Definition and Construction (7 marks)

The **Minkowski sum** of two sets $A, B \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ is the set:

$$A \oplus B = \{a + b \mid a \in A,\; b \in B\}$$

**Construction of C-obstacle for translating robot:** Consider a translating (non-rotating) rigid robot $R$ that must navigate a workspace containing a polygonal obstacle $W$. Choose a reference point $q_0$ on the robot (e.g., the centroid). The robot at configuration $q$ occupies the set $R(q) = R + q$, where $R$ is the robot's shape at the reference configuration. The robot collides with $W$ iff:

$$(R + q) \cap W \neq \emptyset \;\Longleftrightarrow\; q \in W \oplus (-R)$$

Therefore the **C-obstacle** is:

$$C_{obs} = W \oplus (-R)$$

Geometrically, $C_{obs}$ is obtained by sweeping the *reflected* robot shape $-R$ around the boundary of $W$. For convex polygons with $m$ and $n$ vertices, the resulting C-obstacle is a convex polygon with at most $m + n$ vertices, computable in $O(m+n)$ time using the two-pointer edge-merge algorithm. **(3.5 marks for definition + interpretation, 3.5 marks for geometric construction + complexity)**

#### Part (b) — Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Compute the reflected robot by negating all vertex coordinates:

$$-R = \{(-1,-1),\; (-3,-1),\; (-2,-3)\}$$

**Step 2:** Form candidate vertex set $V_{C_{obs}} = \{w + p \mid w \in V_W, p \in V_{-R}\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{C_{obs}} = \{&(4,4)+(-1,-1)=(3,3),\\
&(4,4)+(-3,-1)=(1,3),\\
&(4,4)+(-2,-3)=(2,1),\\
&(7,4)+(-1,-1)=(6,3),\\
&(7,4)+(-3,-1)=(4,3),\\
&(7,4)+(-2,-3)=(5,1),\\
&(7,7)+(-1,-1)=(6,6),\\
&(7,7)+(-3,-1)=(4,6),\\
&(7,7)+(-2,-3)=(5,4),\\
&(4,7)+(-1,-1)=(3,6),\\
&(4,7)+(-3,-1)=(1,6),\\
&(4,7)+(-2,-3)=(2,4)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Identify the convex hull boundary. Scanning the points, the extreme (lowest) $y$-coordinate is $y=1$ at $(2,1)$ and $(5,1)$. The highest is $y=6$ at $(4,6)$ and $(3,6)$. The leftmost is $x=1$ at $(1,3)$ and $(1,6)$. The rightmost is $x=6$ at $(6,3)$ and $(6,6)$.

**Step 4:** The vertices of $C_{obs}$ in counter-clockwise order are:

$$V_{C_{obs}} = \{(2,1),\; (5,1),\; (6,3),\; (6,6),\; (4,6),\; (3,6),\; (1,6),\; (1,3)\}$$

This is an **octagon** (8 vertices), which matches the upper bound $m + n = 4 + 3 = 7$, with one extra vertex due to the geometric configuration. The C-obstacle is the convex hull of these points. **(2 marks for $-R$, 3 marks for vertex computation, 1 mark for hull identification, 1 mark for final octagon listing)**

---

> **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]`
> **(a)** Explain the concept of configuration space. Derive the formula for the C-obstacle using the Minkowski sum for a translating disc-shaped robot of radius $r$ in a workspace with a polygonal obstacle. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A disc-shaped robot of radius $r = 1$ navigates a triangular workspace obstacle with vertices $A = (2,0)$, $B = (6,0)$, $C = (4,4)$. Compute the C-obstacle and identify the type of geometric shape obtained. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Configuration Space & Disc Robot (7 marks)

**Configuration Space (C-space):** The configuration space $C$ of a robot is the set of all possible configurations the robot can attain. For a translating disc robot in 2D workspace, $C = \mathbb{R}^2$ since the configuration is fully specified by the position $(x,y)$ of the disc's center.

**C-obstacle derivation:** Let the workspace obstacle be $W \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ and the disc robot be $D_r = \{(x,y) \mid x^2 + y^2 \leq r^2\}$. The robot at configuration $q = (q_x, q_y)$ occupies $D_r + q = \{(x + q_x, y + q_y) \mid x^2 + y^2 \leq r^2\}$. Collision occurs when:

$$(D_r + q) \cap W \neq \emptyset$$

This is equivalent to the existence of a point $p \in W$ that is within distance $r$ of $q$, i.e., $\text{dist}(q, W) \leq r$. The set of all such $q$ is:

$$C_{obs} = W \oplus D_r = \{q \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid \text{dist}(q, W) \leq r\}$$

This is the **$r$-offset** (or **dilation**) of the obstacle $W$. Geometrically, it is the region swept by translating the disc center along the boundary of $W$. **(2 marks for C-space definition, 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for geometric interpretation)**

#### Part (b) — Offset of a Triangle (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Identify the three edges of triangle $ABC$:
- Edge $\overline{AB}$: $A(2,0)$ to $B(6,0)$, outward normal $\hat{n}_1 = (0,-1)$, line equation $y = 0$.
- Edge $\overline{BC}$: $B(6,0)$ to $C(4,4)$, edge vector $(-2,4)$, length $\sqrt{20} = 2\sqrt{5}$, unit outward normal $\hat{n}_2 = (4/(2\sqrt{5}), 2/(2\sqrt{5})) = (2/\sqrt{5}, 1/\sqrt{5})$.
- Edge $\overline{CA}$: $C(4,4)$ to $A(2,0)$, edge vector $(-2,-4)$, unit outward normal $\hat{n}_3 = (-1/\sqrt{5}, 2/\sqrt{5})$ (outward).

**Step 2:** Offset each edge outward by $r = 1$:
- Offset of $\overline{AB}$: $y = -1$.
- Offset of $\overline{BC}$: line parallel to $\overline{BC}$ shifted by $1$ in direction $\hat{n}_2$.
- Offset of $\overline{CA}$: line parallel to $\overline{CA}$ shifted by $1$ in direction $\hat{n}_3$.

**Step 3:** Compute offset vertices by intersecting the three offset lines:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &: y = -1 \;\cap\; \text{offset of } \overline{BC} \\
V_2 &: \text{offset of } \overline{BC} \;\cap\; \text{offset of } \overline{CA} \\
V_3 &: \text{offset of } \overline{CA} \;\cap\; y = -1
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving (after algebraic manipulation):

$$V_1 \approx (2.894,\; -1), \quad V_2 \approx (4.553,\; 5.106), \quad V_3 \approx (1.447,\; 5.106)$$

**Step 4:** The C-obstacle consists of a **triangle formed by $V_1, V_2, V_3$** with **three circular arcs of radius 1** centered at the original triangle vertices $A, B, C$. The arc at each vertex spans the *external angle* of the original triangle.

**Conclusion:** The C-obstacle is a **rounded triangle** (a triangle with its corners replaced by circular arcs of radius 1). **(2 marks edge equations, 2 marks offsets, 2 marks vertex computation, 1 mark arc + final identification)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to negate the robot:** Many students write $C_{obs} = W \oplus R$ instead of $W \oplus (-R)$. This is the *most common* error and costs full marks on the C-obstacle sub-question.
> 2. **Including interior points in the vertex list:** Always take the **convex hull**; do not list all $m \cdot n$ candidate sums as vertices.
> 3. **Forgetting the corner arcs in the disc case:** For a disc robot and polygon obstacle, the C-obstacle is *not* just an offset triangle — it has rounded corners. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks for omitting the arcs.
> 4. **Mixing workspace and configuration space coordinates:** When asked for the *workspace* trajectory, you must map the C-space path back through the inverse of the transformation $q \mapsto R + q$.
> 5. **Ignoring the reference point choice:** The shape of $C_{obs}$ depends on where $q_0$ is anchored on the robot. State this assumption explicitly.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** $A \oplus B = \{a + b \mid a \in A, b \in B\}$ — the set of all pairwise sums.
- **C-obstacle formula:** $C_{obs} = W \oplus (-R)$ — **memorize verbatim**; this is a guaranteed question.
- **C-free formula:** $C_{free} = C_{space} \setminus C_{obs}$.
- **Reflection:** $-R$ is obtained by negating every coordinate of every point in $R$.
- **Properties:** Commutative, Associative, Convexity-preserving, Identity element $\{0\}$.
- **Convex polygon algorithm:** Two-pointer edge merge sorted by angle — $O(m+n)$ time.
- **Disc + Polygon:** Result = polygon offset outward by $r$ **+** circular arcs of radius $r$ at every corner.
- **Reference point choice matters:** The C-obstacle shape depends on the chosen $q_0$; always state your choice.
- **Volume monotonicity:** $\text{vol}(A \oplus B) \geq \text{vol}(A) + \text{vol}(B)$.
- **Engineering applications:** Autonomous vehicle path planning, CNC swept volumes, surgical robot safety zones, GJK collision detection.
- **Translation vs. Rotation:** The formula $C_{obs} = W \oplus (-R)$ is valid only for *translating* robots. For rotating robots, the C-space is $SE(2) = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ and the C-obstacle computation is more complex (handled in higher modules).
- **Mapping back to workspace:** A C-space trajectory $q(t)$ corresponds to a workspace trajectory $R(q(t)) = R + q(t)$.
- **Set deduplication:** When computing $A \oplus B$ by hand, always remove duplicate sums before identifying the convex hull.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Potential Field
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Potential Field

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Artificial Potential Field (APF)** method is a reactive, real-time robot motion planning technique introduced by **Oussama Khatib (1986)**. In this approach, the robot is treated as a charged particle moving through a scalar potential field $U(q)$ constructed in the robot's configuration space (or Cartesian workspace). The goal position acts as a region of **low potential (gravitational well)**, while obstacles act as regions of **high potential (repulsive hills)**. The robot experiences a virtual force equal to the negative gradient of this potential, causing it to glide smoothly toward the goal while simultaneously being pushed away from obstacles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Potential Field method falls under *Reactive / Local Motion Planning* in Module 3. It is a *closed-form, gradient-descent-based planner* — not a graph search. Memorize the distinction: **Potential Field = Continuous Field + Gradient Descent**, whereas **A* / Dijkstra = Discrete Graph + Optimal Search**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Mountain Hiker

Imagine a hiker standing on a mountainous terrain at night who wants to reach a valley camp (the **goal**):

- The valley floor exerts a **gravitational pull** on the hiker — this is the **attractive potential**. The further the hiker is from the camp, the steeper the slope.
- Rocky peaks and cliffs (the **obstacles**) push the hiker away — this is the **repulsive potential**. The closer the hiker gets to a rock, the stronger the pushback.
- The hiker's actual walking direction at every step is dictated by the **steepest local descent** — mathematically, the **negative gradient** $\mathbf{F} = -\nabla U$.

The hiker never needs a complete global map of the mountains; he just feels the local slope and steps accordingly. This is precisely how an APF-guided robot behaves.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **$k_{att}$** — Attractive gain constant (controls the pull toward the goal). Units: N/m or V/m.
- **$k_{rep}$** — Repulsive gain constant (controls the push from obstacles). Units: N or V.
- **$\rho_0$** — Distance of influence / threshold radius of an obstacle. Units: **meters (m)**. Beyond this distance, the obstacle has no effect.
- **$\rho(q)$** — Current distance from robot to obstacle center. Units: **meters (m)**.
- **$q$** — Robot configuration (position vector) in workspace.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Exam Note:** The potential $U(q)$ has units of **Joules (J)** in physics analogy, but in robotics it is treated as a **dimensionless scalar field** whose negative gradient gives force.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of the Total Potential Field

The total potential $U(q)$ is the **superposition** of two independent scalar fields:

$$U(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)$$

where:
- $U_{att}(q)$ — Attractive potential (function of distance to goal)
- $U_{rep}(q)$ — Repulsive potential (function of distance to nearest obstacle)

The robot's total induced force is:

$$\mathbf{F}(q) = -\nabla U(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) - \nabla U_{rep}(q)$$

### 2.2 Attractive Potential — The "Pull"

The attractive potential is typically modeled as a **parabolic well** (quadratic form) anchored at the goal configuration $q_{goal}$.

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \, k_{att} \, \rho_{goal}^{\,2}(q)$$

where $\rho_{goal}(q) = \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert$ is the Euclidean distance from current configuration to the goal.

**Attractive Force:**

$$\mathbf{F}_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -k_{att}(q - q_{goal})$$

**Why parabolic?** It is the simplest smooth function that vanishes at the goal and grows with distance. Its gradient (force) is **linear and bounded**, ensuring no infinite acceleration near the goal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative form (Conic Attractor):** Some authors use a **conic** potential $U_{att} = k_{att}\rho_{goal}$ for far-field (to avoid overshoot in narrow corridors) and a **quadratic** form for the near-field. This is the basis of the **Khatib's hybrid potential** and is frequently asked in KTU 14-mark questions.

### 2.3 Repulsive Potential — The "Push"

The repulsive potential becomes active only when the robot is within the influence radius $\rho_0$ of an obstacle. It is defined as:

$$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2} \, k_{rep} \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2} & \text{if } \rho(q) \le \rho_0 \\[6pt] 0 & \text{if } \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

where:
- $\rho(q)$ — shortest distance from robot to the obstacle boundary
- $\rho_0$ — maximum influence distance (threshold)
- $k_{rep}$ — repulsive gain

**Repulsive Force:**

$$\mathbf{F}_{rep}(q) = -\nabla U_{rep}(q) = k_{rep}\left(\frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^{2}(q)} \cdot \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial q}$$

For a point obstacle, $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial q} = \dfrac{q - q_{obs}}{\rho(q)}$, so:

$$\mathbf{F}_{rep}(q) = k_{rep}\left(\frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^{2}(q)} \cdot \frac{q - q_{obs}}{\rho(q)}$$

This force points **away from the obstacle** along the line connecting the robot to the obstacle center.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the $\left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)$ term?** It guarantees **smooth continuity** — at $\rho = \rho_0$, both $U_{rep}$ and $\mathbf{F}_{rep}$ are exactly **zero**, eliminating discontinuities that would cause jerky robot motion.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Total Potential | $U(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)$ | Scalar field |
| Attractive Potential | $U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{att} \rho_{goal}^{\,2}$ | Quadratic, smooth |
| Conic Attractive (far-field) | $U_{att}(q) = k_{att} \rho_{goal}$ | Linear, for $d > d^{\ast}$ |
| Repulsive Potential | $U_{rep}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{rep}\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}$ | Active if $\rho \le \rho_0$ |
| Total Force | $\mathbf{F}(q) = -\nabla U(q)$ | Negative gradient |
| Attractive Force | $\mathbf{F}_{att}(q) = -k_{att}(q - q_{goal})$ | Linear, points to goal |
| Repulsive Force Magnitude | $\vert \mathbf{F}_{rep} \vert = k_{rep}\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \tfrac{1}{\rho^{2}}$ | Falls off as $1/\rho^{3}$ |
| Influence Threshold | $\rho_0$ | Pre-defined constant |
| Attractive Gain | $k_{att}$ | Tunable, larger $\Rightarrow$ faster pull |
| Repulsive Gain | $k_{rep}$ | Tunable, larger $\Rightarrow$ wider safety margin |
| Goal Distance | $\rho_{goal} = \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert$ | Euclidean norm |
| Gradient in 2D | $\nabla U = \left[\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial x}, \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial y}\right]^{T}$ | Vector field |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial manipulators** — smooth collision-free trajectories in pick-and-place cells.
- **Autonomous mobile robots (AMRs)** in warehouses (Amazon Kiva-style robots).
- **Drone swarm navigation** — each drone is a particle in a shared potential field.
- **Driverless cars** — APF is the **local planner** layered on top of a global A* / RRT route.
- **Surgical robotics** — da Vinci systems use APF for instrument guidance around organs.
- **Spacecraft proximity operations** — autonomous docking uses APF for safe rendezvous.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Attractive Force from Gradient

We start with the attractive potential:

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \, \rho_{goal}^{\,2}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \left[(x - x_{g})^{2} + (y - y_{g})^{2}\right]$$

**Step 1:** Write out the Euclidean distance squared explicitly in 2D.

$$\rho_{goal}^{\,2} = (x - x_{g})^{2} + (y - y_{g})^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute back into $U_{att}$.

$$U_{att} = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \left[(x - x_{g})^{2} + (y - y_{g})^{2}\right]$$

**Step 3:** Compute the partial derivative with respect to $x$ (chain rule for scalar field).

$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \cdot 2(x - x_{g}) = k_{att}(x - x_{g})$$

**Step 4:** Compute the partial derivative with respect to $y$.

$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial y} = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \cdot 2(y - y_{g}) = k_{att}(y - y_{g})$$

**Step 5:** Assemble the gradient vector.

$$\nabla U_{att} = \begin{bmatrix} k_{att}(x - x_{g}) \\ k_{att}(y - y_{g}) \end{bmatrix} = k_{att}\begin{bmatrix} x - x_{g} \\ y - y_{g} \end{bmatrix} = k_{att}(q - q_{goal})$$

**Step 6:** Apply the negative sign to obtain the attractive force.

$$\mathbf{F}_{att} = -\nabla U_{att} = -k_{att}(q - q_{goal}) = k_{att}(q_{goal} - q)$$

**Physical interpretation:** The force is a **linear spring** pulling the robot toward $q_{goal}$ with stiffness $k_{att}$. Near the goal, the force vanishes — the robot naturally stops.

### 3.2 Derivation: Repulsive Force from Gradient

We begin with the repulsive potential (active region, $\rho \le \rho_0$):

$$U_{rep}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2}$$

Let us define the auxiliary variable:

$$u = \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}$$

So $U_{rep} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{rep} u^{2}$.

**Step 1:** Apply chain rule for $\dfrac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial x}$.

$$\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial x} = k_{rep} \cdot u \cdot \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}$. Since $\rho_0$ is constant, only the first term contributes.

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\rho^{-1}\right) = -\rho^{-2} \cdot \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial x}$. Recall $\rho = \sqrt{(x - x_{o})^{2} + (y - y_{o})^{2}}$.

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} = \frac{x - x_{o}}{\sqrt{(x - x_{o})^{2} + (y - y_{o})^{2}}} = \frac{x - x_{o}}{\rho}$$

**Step 4:** Combine Steps 2 and 3.

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = -\rho^{-2} \cdot \frac{x - x_{o}}{\rho} = -\frac{x - x_{o}}{\rho^{3}}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back into Step 1.

$$\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial x} = k_{rep} \left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \left(-\frac{x - x_{o}}{\rho^{3}}\right) = -k_{rep}\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \frac{x - x_{o}}{\rho^{3}}$$

**Step 6:** Symmetrically, for the $y$-component:

$$\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial y} = -k_{rep}\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \frac{y - y_{o}}{\rho^{3}}$$

**Step 7:** Assemble gradient and apply negative sign for force.

$$\mathbf{F}_{rep} = -\nabla U_{rep} = k_{rep}\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^{3}} \begin{bmatrix} x - x_{o} \\ y - y_{o} \end{bmatrix} = k_{rep}\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^{3}}(q - q_{obs})$$

**Key observation:** The force magnitude scales as $1/\rho^{3}$ — extremely strong near obstacles, vanishing rapidly as the robot moves away. This guarantees a **safety bubble** of radius $\rho_0$ around every obstacle.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example — 2D Workspace:** A robot at $q = (2, 3)$ must reach $q_{goal} = (10, 8)$. An obstacle is at $q_{obs} = (4, 4)$ with $\rho_0 = 3$ m. Given $k_{att} = 1$ N/m and $k_{rep} = 50$ N, compute the net force on the robot.

**Step 1:** Compute $\rho_{goal}$.

$$\rho_{goal} = \sqrt{(10-2)^{2} + (8-3)^{2}} = \sqrt{64 + 25} = \sqrt{89} \approx 9.43 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Compute attractive force vector.

$$\mathbf{F}_{att} = -k_{att}(q - q_{goal}) = -(1)\begin{bmatrix} 2-10 \\ 3-8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\rho$ (distance to obstacle).

$$\rho = \sqrt{(4-2)^{2} + (4-3)^{2}} = \sqrt{4 + 1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Check influence condition. Since $2.236 < 3.0$, the obstacle is active.

**Step 5:** Compute the scalar coefficient.

$$\eta = k_{rep}\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\frac{1}{\rho^{3}} = 50 \times \left(\frac{1}{2.236} - \frac{1}{3}\right) \times \frac{1}{(2.236)^{3}}$$

$$\eta = 50 \times (0.4472 - 0.3333) \times \frac{1}{11.18} = 50 \times 0.1139 \times 0.0894 \approx 0.509 \text{ N/m}$$

**Step 6:** Compute repulsive force vector.

$$\mathbf{F}_{rep} = 0.509 \begin{bmatrix} 2 - 4 \\ 3 - 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.018 \\ -0.509 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N}$$

**Step 7:** Net force (vector sum).

$$\mathbf{F}_{net} = \mathbf{F}_{att} + \mathbf{F}_{rep} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 - 1.018 \\ 5 - 0.509 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6.982 \\ 4.491 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N}$$

**Step 8:** Magnitude and direction.

$$\vert \mathbf{F}_{net} \vert = \sqrt{6.982^{2} + 4.491^{2}} \approx 8.31 \text{ N}$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{4.491}{6.982}\right) \approx 32.74^{\circ} \text{ (above positive x-axis)}$$

The robot will accelerate at $8.31/m_{robot}$ N/kg in a direction roughly toward the goal, but **veering slightly south** to avoid the obstacle.

### 3.4 The Local Minima Problem — A Critical Limitation

The gradient-descent nature of APF means the robot can get trapped at a configuration where $\mathbf{F}_{net} = 0$ but $q \neq q_{goal}$. These are **local minima**.

**Conditions for local minima:**
- An attractive force is exactly cancelled by repulsive forces from one or more obstacles, or
- The robot enters a narrow concave region (e.g., U-shaped obstacle), or
- The robot faces two equal obstacles in symmetric configuration.

**Common remedies (frequently asked in KTU):**
- **Random walk / noise injection** — perturb the robot out of the minimum.
- **Virtual obstacles / tangent field** — add a sideways force near concavities.
- **Harmonic potential fields** (Connolly & Grupen) — use Laplace's equation $\nabla^{2} U = 0$ to guarantee a single minimum at the goal.
- **Navigation functions** (Koditschek & Rimon) — provably local-minima-free for spherical-world star-shaped workspaces.
- **Hybrid A* / APF fusion** — use APF for local guidance, A* for global escape from traps.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of the APF Planner

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Current State q] --> B[Compute Goal Distance]
    A --> C[Compute Obstacle Distances]
    B --> D[Attractive Potential Generator]
    C --> E[Repulsive Potential Generator]
    D --> F[Gradient Calculator]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Total Force Vector Fnet]
    G --> H{Force Magnitude and Direction}
    H --> I[Velocity Controller]
    I --> J[Robot Actuators]
    J --> K[New Robot State q_new]
    K --> A
    H --> L{Goal Reached?}
    L -- Yes --> M[STOP]
    L -- No --> A
```

### 4.2 Potential Field Visualization (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WORKSPACE[Robot Workspace]
        GOAL[Goal Position q_goal - Low Potential Well]
        OB1[Obstacle 1 - Repulsive Hill]
        OB2[Obstacle 2 - Repulsive Hill]
        OB3[Obstacle 3 - Repulsive Hill]
    end
    
    subgraph FIELDS[Potential Field Construction]
        UATT[Attractive Field U_att quadratic]
        UREP[Repulsive Field U_rep inverse]
        UTOT[Total Field U = U_att + U_rep]
    end
    
    subgraph FORCES[Virtual Forces on Robot]
        FATT[Attractive Force toward goal]
        FREP[Repulsive Force away from obstacles]
        FNET[Net Force = F_att + F_rep]
    end
    
    GOAL --> UATT
    OB1 --> UREP
    OB2 --> UREP
    OB3 --> UREP
    UATT --> UTOT
    UREP --> UTOT
    UTOT --> FATT
    UTOT --> FREP
    FATT --> FNET
    FREP --> FNET
    FNET --> PATH[Smooth Trajectory to Goal]
```

### 4.3 Algorithm Flow — Local Minima Detection and Recovery

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Initialize q at start] --> LOOP[Loop Each Timestep]
    LOOP --> CALC[Compute F_att and F_rep]
    CALC --> CHECK{F_net magnitude and below threshold}
    CHECK -- Above threshold --> MOVE[Move robot by delta F_net]
    MOVE --> LOOP
    CHECK -- Below threshold --> SUSPECT{Local Minima Suspected}
    SUSPECT -- Yes --> RECOVER[Activate Recovery: Random Walk or Tangent Escape]
    RECOVER --> LOOP
    SUSPECT -- No --> GOALCHECK{Goal reached}
    GOALCHECK -- Yes --> ENDNODE[Terminate Successfully]
    GOALCHECK -- No --> LOOP
```

### 4.4 Schematic Mapping of Force Components in 2D

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot at q equals x, y] --> B[Vector to Goal: q_goal minus q]
    A --> C[Vector to Obstacle: q_obs minus q]
    B --> D[F_att = k_att times q_goal minus q]
    C --> E[Distance rho = norm of q_obs minus q]
    E --> F{rho less than rho_0}
    F -- Yes --> G[F_rep = k_rep times 1/rho minus 1/rho_0 times q minus q_obs over rho^3]
    F -- No --> H[F_rep = 0]
    D --> SUM[Vector Sum]
    G --> SUM
    H --> SUM
    SUM --> I[Net Force F_net and Velocity v = F_net / damping]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define Artificial Potential Field method. State any two advantages of this approach.

**Model Answer:**
The **Artificial Potential Field (APF)** method is a real-time, gradient-based local motion planning technique in which the robot navigates through a scalar potential field $U(q)$ constructed over the workspace. The goal acts as a low-potential attractor and obstacles act as high-potential repellers; the robot moves along the negative gradient of the total field.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Computationally cheap and real-time** — only local gradient evaluation is needed per timestep, making it suitable for fast dynamic environments.
2. **Mathematically smooth trajectories** — the gradient-descent nature yields naturally smooth, jerk-minimized paths without explicit path-smoothing post-processing.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
What is the local minima problem in potential field planning? Suggest one remedy.

**Model Answer:**
The **local minima problem** occurs when the robot reaches a configuration where the net virtual force is zero (i.e., $\mathbf{F}_{att} + \mathbf{F}_{rep} = 0$) but the goal has not yet been reached. The robot becomes permanently trapped.

**Remedy — Random Walk / Best-of-Two (BT) Escape:** When a local minimum is detected (force magnitude below a threshold for several timesteps), the planner injects a random walk or rotates the robot by a small angle and resumes gradient descent. This perturbs the configuration out of the trap.

*(Alternative acceptable remedies: Navigation functions, harmonic fields, virtual obstacle placement, hybrid A* layer.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Derive the expressions for attractive and repulsive potentials and the corresponding force vectors used in the Artificial Potential Field method. Clearly state the role of the parameter $\rho_0$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-DOF planar robot at configuration $q = (1, 2)$ must reach $q_{goal} = (8, 6)$. An obstacle is centered at $q_{obs} = (3, 3)$ with influence radius $\rho_0 = 2.5$ m. Given $k_{att} = 1$ N/m and $k_{rep} = 30$ N, compute the net virtual force vector. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Attractive Potential:** Let $\rho_{goal} = \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert$. The attractive potential is defined as:

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \rho_{goal}^{\,2} = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \left[(x-x_g)^2 + (y-y_g)^2\right]$$

**Step 2 — Attractive Force:** Taking the gradient and negating:

$$\mathbf{F}_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -k_{att}(q - q_{goal})$$

**Step 3 — Repulsive Potential:** Active only if $\rho(q) \le \rho_0$:

$$U_{rep}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{rep}\left(\frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Repulsive Force:**

$$\mathbf{F}_{rep}(q) = k_{rep}\left(\frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^{3}(q)} (q - q_{obs})$$

**Role of $\rho_0$:** It is the **influence threshold** that defines the maximum distance at which an obstacle can affect the robot. Beyond $\rho_0$, $U_{rep} = 0$ and $\mathbf{F}_{rep} = 0$ — guaranteeing smooth continuity of the force field and limiting computational load to nearby obstacles.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Goal distance:**

$$\rho_{goal} = \sqrt{(8-1)^2 + (6-2)^2} = \sqrt{49 + 16} = \sqrt{65} \approx 8.062 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Attractive force:**

$$\mathbf{F}_{att} = -k_{att}(q - q_{goal}) = -(1)\begin{bmatrix} 1-8 \\ 2-6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Obstacle distance:**

$$\rho = \sqrt{(3-1)^2 + (3-2)^2} = \sqrt{4+1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Check influence condition:** $2.236 < 2.5$ — **obstacle is active**.

**Step 5 — Compute scalar coefficient:**

$$\eta = k_{rep}\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\frac{1}{\rho^{3}} = 30 \times \left(\frac{1}{2.236} - \frac{1}{2.5}\right) \times \frac{1}{11.18}$$

$$\eta = 30 \times (0.4472 - 0.4000) \times 0.0894 = 30 \times 0.0472 \times 0.0894 \approx 0.1266$$

**Step 6 — Repulsive force vector:**

$$\mathbf{F}_{rep} = 0.1266 \begin{bmatrix} 1-3 \\ 2-3 \end{bmatrix} = 0.1266 \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.253 \\ -0.127 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N}$$

**Step 7 — Net force:**

$$\mathbf{F}_{net} = \begin{bmatrix} 7 - 0.253 \\ 4 - 0.127 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6.747 \\ 3.873 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N}$$

**Step 8 — Magnitude and direction:**

$$\vert \mathbf{F}_{net} \vert = \sqrt{6.747^2 + 3.873^2} \approx 7.78 \text{ N}, \quad \theta \approx 29.86^{\circ}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the local minima problem in the Artificial Potential Field method. Discuss any three techniques to overcome it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare Artificial Potential Field method with the A* graph search algorithm for robot motion planning on the parameters: optimality, completeness, computational cost, and real-time reactivity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Local Minima Problem:**
In the APF method, the robot follows the negative gradient of the total potential $U(q)$. A **local minimum** is a configuration $q^{\ast} \neq q_{goal}$ where $\nabla U(q^{\ast}) = 0$. At such points, the attractive and repulsive forces cancel each other exactly, so $\mathbf{F}_{net} = 0$ and the robot halts prematurely. Common scenarios include:
- Single obstacle directly between the robot and goal.
- U-shaped (concave) obstacle traps.
- Symmetrically placed obstacles producing balanced repulsion.

**Three Techniques to Overcome Local Minima:**

1. **Random Walk / Stochastic Perturbation:** When the planner detects the robot has stagnated (force below threshold for $N$ consecutive steps), it injects a small random velocity vector. Once un-stuck, gradient descent resumes. Simple but not deterministic.

2. **Navigation Functions (Koditschek & Rimon, 1992):** A specially constructed potential $U(q)$ based on the **workspace sphere-world model** that is provably free of local minima outside the goal. The function is bounded, smooth, and has the goal as its unique minimum. Mathematically:

$$\varphi(q) = \frac{\Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^{2}}{\left[\Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^{2k} + \beta(q)\right]^{1/k}}$$

where $\beta(q)$ is a function encoding obstacle geometry.

3. **Harmonic Potential Fields (Connolly, Grupen):** Solutions to **Laplace's equation** $\nabla^{2} U = 0$ with boundary conditions $U = 0$ on goal and $U = 1$ on obstacle boundaries. By the **maximum principle**, harmonic functions have no local minima — only the prescribed boundary minimum survives.

#### Model Solution for (b):

| Parameter | APF Method | A* Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| **Optimality** | Locally optimal (gradient descent), not globally optimal | Provably globally optimal (with admissible heuristic) |
| **Completeness** | Not complete — may fail in local minima | Complete on finite/discretized graphs |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(1)$ per timestep (only local gradient) | $O(E \log V)$ worst case (Dijkstra-like) |
| **Real-time Reactivity** | Excellent — naturally reactive to dynamic obstacles | Poor — full re-planning required for dynamic changes |
| **Map Requirement** | Local sensing sufficient | Full known map required |
| **Path Smoothness** | Naturally smooth trajectories | Piecewise linear, requires smoothing |
| **Memory** | Minimal (stores only $k_{att}, k_{rep}, \rho_0$) | Large (stores open and closed lists) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Loss of 2 to 3 Marks Common:**
> 1. **Sign error in repulsive force:** Students frequently write $\mathbf{F}_{rep} = -\nabla U_{rep}$ but forget that the gradient itself is *negative* (since $U_{rep}$ decreases with $\rho$); hence $\mathbf{F}_{rep}$ should point **away** from the obstacle — the algebra must show $(q - q_{obs})$, not $(q_{obs} - q)$.
> 2. **Forgetting the influence condition $\rho \le \rho_0$:** Many students write $U_{rep}$ as if it were always active. KTU expects you to explicitly mention the **piecewise definition** with $\rho > \rho_0 \Rightarrow U_{rep} = 0$.
> 3. **Mixing up gradient with directional derivative:** The gradient is a **vector**; never write $\nabla U = \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial x}$ alone in 2D. Always show both components.
> 4. **No units mentioned:** $U$ in Joules (analogy) or dimensionless; $k_{att}$ in N/m; $k_{rep}$ in N. KTU awards a half-mark for correct units.

---

### Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **APF = Khatib (1986)**, and it is a **reactive / local planner**, not a global planner.
- Total potential is a **scalar sum**: $U(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)$.
- The robot's motion is governed by the **negative gradient**: $\mathbf{F} = -\nabla U$.
- **Attractive potential** is **quadratic** (parabolic well) at the goal: $U_{att} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{att} \rho_{goal}^{\,2}$.
- **Repulsive potential** is **active only within $\rho_0$** and uses an **inverse-distance-squared kernel**: $U_{rep} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{rep}\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}$.
- Repulsive force magnitude **falls off as $1/\rho^{3}$**, giving a strong local safety bubble.
- The **negative gradient** ensures forces **always point downhill** (toward the goal and away from obstacles).
- **Local minima** are the chief limitation — caused by force cancellation in symmetric or concave geometries.
- **Remedies** to remember for KTU: random walk, navigation functions (Koditschek-Rimon), harmonic potentials (Laplace's equation), hybrid A* + APF layering.
- **APF is fast and reactive** but **not complete or globally optimal**; A* is the **opposite trade-off**.
- Conic-vs-quadratic **hybrid attractive** potential is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question** — memorize the switching distance $d^{\ast}$.
- Always state the **piecewise nature** of $U_{rep}$ explicitly and the **smooth continuity** at $\rho = \rho_0$.
- For numerical problems, remember the **vector order**: $\mathbf{F}_{rep} \propto (q - q_{obs})$ — pushes away from obstacle.
- The force $\mathbf{F}_{att}$ is a **linear virtual spring** anchored at $q_{goal}$ — natural equilibrium at the goal.
- Tuning $k_{att}$ vs $k_{rep}$ is critical: too large $k_{rep}$ causes oscillation; too small causes collisions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navigation Function - in Static Deterministic Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Navigation Function - in Static Deterministic Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Navigation Function (NF)** is a real-valued analytic map $\varphi: \mathcal{F} \rightarrow [0,1]$ defined on a connected, compact, analytic manifold $\mathcal{F}$ with boundary, such that the following **Rimon–Koditschek axioms** (1990) hold:

1. **Smoothness:** $\varphi$ is at least $C^{2}$ (twice continuously differentiable) on the interior of $\mathcal{F}$.
2. **Admissibility (Boundary Behaviour):** $\varphi(q) \rightarrow 1$ as $q$ approaches the obstacle boundary $\partial \mathcal{F}$.
3. **Polar Property:** $\varphi$ possesses a single, isolated critical point in the interior of $\mathcal{F}$, which is a global minimum.
4. **Goal Convergence:** This unique minimum coincides with the goal configuration $q_{d}$.

In a **Static Deterministic Environment**, the obstacle set $\mathcal{O} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{O}_{i}$ is **time-invariant** (static) and **fully known a priori** to the planner (deterministic). The robot is therefore operating with a complete, exact model of its workspace, and only the *initial configuration* $q_{0}$ is uncertain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Definition Box**
> A Navigation Function is a *strictly proper Morse function* on the configuration space that vanishes uniquely at the goal and tends to unity on the workspace boundary. It is the **first complete solution** to the point-robot navigation problem in a known cluttered environment, resolving the *local-minima problem* that plagued classical Artificial Potential Fields (APF).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hilly Rubber Sheet"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners**
> Picture a stretchy rubber sheet stretched horizontally in a frame. Now imagine:
> - **Stretch the centre down** to form a deep funnel — this is the **goal** $q_{d}$.
> - **Push the sheet upward** at the obstacle locations to form steep cones — these are the **obstacles** $\mathcal{O}_{i}$.
> - Place a **marble** anywhere on the sheet — this is the **robot**.
>
> When released, the marble **always rolls into the funnel**, never getting stuck on a plateau or a secondary dip. The "height" of the sheet at any point is precisely the value of the Navigation Function $\varphi(q)$. Because the topology of the sheet is engineered so that there is **only one valley** (the goal), the marble's trajectory is guaranteed to terminate at the goal while avoiding the cones.

### 1.3 The Static Deterministic Workspace — Sphere World

The canonical setting in which navigation functions are constructed is the **Sphere World** $\mathcal{W}_{N}$, introduced by Koditschek & Rimon:

$$
\mathcal{W}_{N} = \mathbb{R}^{n} \setminus \bigcup_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{B}(c_{i}, r_{i})
$$

where $\mathcal{B}(c_{i}, r_{i})$ is a closed ball of radius $r_{i}$ centred at $c_{i}$. The obstacles are pairwise disjoint, and the goal $q_{d} \in \mathcal{W}_{N}$ lies outside every ball.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Deterministic" and "Static"?**
> - **Deterministic:** The planner knows $\{(c_{i}, r_{i})\}_{i=1}^{N}$ and $q_{d}$ exactly. There is no stochastic uncertainty in obstacle location.
> - **Static:** No $c_{i}$ or $r_{i}$ changes with time. The set $\mathcal{W}_{N}$ is invariant.
> These two properties together permit *offline* construction of $\varphi$ and *online* execution by pure gradient descent — a clean separation of planning and control.

### 1.4 Key Symbols & Convention

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units (KTU convention) |
|---|---|---|
| $q \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ | Robot configuration | metres (m) or radians (rad) |
| $q_{d}$ | Goal configuration | m or rad |
| $c_{i}$ | Centre of $i$-th obstacle | m |
| $r_{i}$ | Radius of $i$-th obstacle | m |
| $N$ | Number of obstacles | dimensionless |
| $k$ | Tuning gain | dimensionless, $k > 0$ |
| $\gamma_{d}(q)$ | Distance (goal) function | m$^{2}$ |
| $\beta_{i}(q)$ | $i$-th obstacle function | m$^{2}$ |
| $\beta(q)$ | Composite obstacle function | m$^{2N}$ |
| $\varphi(q)$ | Navigation function | dimensionless, $\in [0,1]$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Decomposition — The Three-Stage Architecture

A navigation function is built from **three cooperative scalar fields** layered on top of each other. Understanding the role of each layer is essential for KTU long-answer questions.

#### Layer 1 — The Distance (Goal) Function $\gamma_{d}(q)$

A **polar function** that vanishes uniquely at $q_{d}$ and grows monotonically with distance from the goal. The standard choice is the *squared Euclidean distance*:

$$
\gamma_{d}(q) = \lVert q - q_{d} \rVert^{2} = (q - q_{d})^{\top}(q - q_{d})
$$

Properties:
- $\gamma_{d}(q_{d}) = 0$ (only zero is at the goal).
- $\nabla \gamma_{d}(q) = 2(q - q_{d})$ — points radially outward from $q_{d}$.
- Hessian: $H_{\gamma_{d}} = 2I$ (positive definite everywhere).

#### Layer 2 — The Obstacle Function $\beta(q)$

Encodes *repulsion* from obstacles. For each spherical obstacle $\mathcal{B}(c_{i}, r_{i})$:

$$
\beta_{i}(q) = \lVert q - c_{i} \rVert^{2} - r_{i}^{2}
$$

- $\beta_{i}(q) < 0$ inside the obstacle (infeasible).
- $\beta_{i}(q) = 0$ on the obstacle boundary.
- $\beta_{i}(q) > 0$ in free space.

The **composite (unnormalised) obstacle function** is the product of all individual functions:

$$
\beta(q) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \beta_{i}(q)
$$

To make the navigation function attain a finite value at the goal, $\beta$ is **normalised**:

$$
\beta(q) = \frac{\prod_{i=1}^{N} \beta_{i}(q)}{\prod_{i=1}^{N} \beta_{i}(q_{d})} \quad \text{so that } \beta(q_{d}) = 1.
$$

This is the **Rimon–Koditschek normalisation** and is mandatory for the construction to be a proper NF.

#### Layer 3 — The Navigation Function $\varphi(q)$

The two layers are combined through a **Tuned Quotient Construction**:

$$
\boxed{\;\varphi(q) = \frac{\gamma_{d}(q)}{\bigl(\gamma_{d}(q)\bigr)^{1/k} + \beta(q)}\;}
$$

where $k > 0$ is the **tuning parameter** (a dimensionless real number). The exponent $1/k$ on $\gamma_{d}$ is the *k-tuning trick* introduced by Koditschek & Rimon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Quotient Form?**
> The numerator $\gamma_{d}$ attracts the robot to the goal; the denominator term $\beta$ repels it from obstacles. The exponent $1/k$ on the distance function in the denominator ensures that the resulting field has a **uniform gradient behaviour** in the deep interior, while the $\beta$ term dominates near obstacles. This dual behaviour is the mathematical heart of the NF construction.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorise for KTU ESE**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\gamma_{d}(q) = \lVert q - q_{d} \rVert^{2}$ | Squared distance to goal (polar function) |
| 2 | $\beta_{i}(q) = \lVert q - c_{i} \rVert^{2} - r_{i}^{2}$ | $i$-th spherical obstacle function |
| 3 | $\beta(q) = \dfrac{\prod_{i=1}^{N} \beta_{i}(q)}{\prod_{i=1}^{N} \beta_{i}(q_{d})}$ | Normalised composite obstacle function |
| 4 | $\varphi(q) = \dfrac{\gamma_{d}(q)}{\bigl(\gamma_{d}(q)\bigr)^{1/k} + \beta(q)}$ | Rimon–Koditschek Navigation Function |
| 5 | $\nabla \gamma_{d}(q) = 2(q - q_{d})$ | Gradient of squared distance |
| 6 | $\nabla \beta_{i}(q) = 2(q - c_{i})$ | Gradient of single obstacle function |
| 7 | $\dot{q} = -K \cdot \nabla \varphi(q)$ | Gradient-descent control law ($K > 0$ gain) |
| 8 | $k \geq N + 1$ (in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$) | Sufficient condition on tuning parameter |
| 9 | $\varphi(q_{d}) = 0$ | NF vanishes at goal |
| 10 | $\varphi(q) \to 1$ as $q \to \partial \mathcal{F}$ | NF tends to unity on obstacle boundary |
| 11 | $\beta_{i}(q) \geq 0 \; \forall q \in \mathcal{W}_{N}$ | Free-space admissibility of $\beta$ |
| 12 | $\varphi \in (0,1)$ on $\mathrm{int}(\mathcal{W}_{N})$ | Range of NF in free space |

> **Units Convention:** Configuration space coordinates in m; $\gamma_{d}$ and individual $\beta_{i}$ in m$^{2}$; composite $\beta$ in m$^{2N}$; $\varphi$ is **dimensionless**.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Navigation functions underpin several real-world systems:

- **Warehouse AMR (Autonomous Mobile Robot) Path Planning** — Kiva/Amazon robots in static, mapped warehouses navigate to shelf pick-up points using NF-derived potentials.
- **Surgical Robot Assistants** — In a static, pre-operative CT-mapped anatomy, NF guides the manipulator tip from entry port to lesion.
- **Satellite Cluster Reconfiguration** — In a deterministic orbit, NF drives inter-satellite docking.
- **Drone Cinematography** — A static obstacle map (trees, buildings) combined with NF gradient descent gives smooth, collision-free shots.

The principal engineering advantage is **decoupling**: the NF is built offline once; online execution is a single line — a gradient projection — making real-time deployment cheap.
