# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define a Navigation Function. List the four axioms it must satisfy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Navigation Function $\phi: \mathcal{F}_{\text{free}} \to [0,1]$ is a real-valued, analytic function on the free workspace that maps every configuration to a value between 0 and 1, encoding goal-reaching and obstacle-avoidance behavior.

The four axioms (Koditschek & Rimon, 1992) are:

1. **Smoothness / Analyticity:** $\phi$ is at least $C^2$ smooth (continuously twice-differentiable) on the interior of $\mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$.
2. **Polar Property:** $\phi$ attains its unique global minimum at $q_d$ (the goal) and is uniformly maximal ($\phi \to 1$) on the obstacle boundary $\partial \mathcal{F}$.
3. **Morse / Admissibility:** All critical points are non-degenerate (the Hessian is non-singular).
4. **Properness:** $\nabla \phi$ vanishes only at $q_d$, guaranteeing no spurious local minima.

> [!NOTE]
> **Awarding Marks:** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for correctly listing the four axioms (0.5 each).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**What is the difference between a static and dynamic artificial potential field? Why is the latter more complex?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Static APF:** The obstacle set $\mathcal{O}$ and goal $q_d$ are fixed in time. The potential $U(q) = U_{\text{att}}(q) + U_{\text{rep}}(q)$ is purely spatial. The robot's velocity is simply $v = -\nabla U(q)$.

**Dynamic APF:** Both obstacles and the goal may move, so the potential is spatio-temporal: $U(q, t) = U_{\text{att}}(q,t) + \sum_i U_{\text{rep},i}(q,t)$. The robot velocity command depends on time explicitly:
$$\dot{q}(t) = -\nabla_q U(q,t)$$

**Complexity arises because:**
1. The potential is **time-varying**, requiring the robot to re-plan continuously (not just once).
2. **Relative-velocity terms** ($\dot{\rho}$) must be incorporated to anticipate future collisions.
3. The robot requires a **predictive estimator** (EKF or PF) to forecast obstacle motion, which itself introduces sensor noise and latency.
4. The gradient $\nabla U$ is no longer a conservative field in the strict sense — it may not be curl-free.

> [!NOTE]
> **Awarding Marks:** 1 mark each for the two definitions and 1 mark for the complexity reasoning.

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** For Navigation Function problems, students commonly (a) forget to mention the **Morse / admissibility** axiom, losing 2 marks; (b) confuse the obstacle function $\beta_i(q)$ with the potential $U$, losing 3 marks; (c) omit the **tuning parameter $k$**, losing 1 mark. For Kalman Filter problems, students often (a) invert $S_k$ incorrectly (scalar case only — for matrix $S_k$ you need cofactor method, losing 4 marks); (b) forget to update the covariance $P_{k\mid k}$, losing 2 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat mathematical formulation, explain the construction of a Navigation Function in a sphere-world with $m$ spherical obstacles. Show how the Koditschek–Rimon function $\phi$ satisfies the four NF axioms.**

**Model Answer:**

**Sphere-World Setup:** The free workspace is $\mathcal{F} = \mathbb{R}^n \setminus \bigcup_{i=0}^{m} \mathcal{O}_i$, where each obstacle $\mathcal{O}_i$ is a sphere centered at $q_i$ with radius $r_i$. The goal is at $q_d$.

**Obstacle Function:**
$$\beta_i(q) = \vert\vert q - q_i \vert\vert^2 - r_i^2$$
$$\beta(q) = \prod_{i=0}^{m} \beta_i(q)$$

By construction, $\beta_i(q) > 0$ if $q \notin \mathcal{O}_i$ and $\beta_i(q) = 0$ on the boundary $\partial \mathcal{O}_i$. Therefore $\beta(q) > 0$ everywhere in $\mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$.

**Koditschek–Rimon NF:**
$$\phi(q) = \frac{\vert\vert q - q_d \vert\vert^2}{\Big(\vert\vert q - q_d \vert\vert^{2k} + \beta(q)\Big)^{1/k}}$$

**Axiom Verification:**

1. **Smoothness (1.5 marks):** $\phi$ is a composition of polynomials, products, sums, and the smooth $(.)^{1/k}$ function (which is $C^{\infty}$ for arguments $> 0$). Since $\beta(q) > 0$ on $\mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$, the denominator never vanishes inside the free space. Hence $\phi \in C^{\infty}$ on $\mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$.

2. **Polar (2 marks):**
   - As $q \to q_d$, numerator $\to 0$ and denominator $\to \beta(q_d)^{1/k} > 0$, so $\phi \to 0$.
   - As $q \to \partial \mathcal{O}_i$, $\beta_i \to 0 \Rightarrow \beta \to 0$, so denominator $\to (\vert\vert q - q_d\vert^{2k})^{1/k} = \vert\vert q - q_d\vert\vert^2$, giving $\phi \to \vert\vert q - q_d\vert\vert^2 / \vert\vert q - q_d\vert\vert^2 = 1$.
   - Therefore $\phi$ is maximal on $\partial \mathcal{F}$ and minimal at $q_d$ — the **polar property** holds.

3. **Morse / Admissibility (1.5 marks):** For sufficiently large $k$ (specifically $k > 0$ chosen such that the Hessian determinant is non-zero at every critical point), all critical points are non-degenerate. Koditschek proved that for $k \geq 3$ in typical sphere-worlds, admissibility is guaranteed.

4. **Properness (2 marks):** Because $\phi \in [0,1]$ and is bounded on the compact closure of $\mathcal{F}$, $\nabla \phi$ is bounded. By the construction, the only place $\nabla \phi = 0$ in the interior is at $q_d$. Hence there are no spurious local minima.

> [!NOTE]
> **Awarding Marks:** 1.5 marks for setup, 1.5 for axiom 1, 2 for axiom 2, 1.5 for axiom 3, 2 for axiom 4, plus 0.5 for proper conclusion.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Derive the gradient (control law) of the attractive potential $U_{\text{att}}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\xi \vert\vert q - q_d \vert\vert^2$ and explain how it produces a goal-seeking behavior.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Expand the Squared Norm**
$$\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{att}}(q) &= \frac{1}{2}\xi\,\vert\vert q - q_d \vert\vert^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\xi\,(q - q_d)^T (q - q_d)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Gradient Operator**
$$\begin{aligned}
\nabla U_{\text{att}}(q) &= \frac{1}{2}\xi\,\nabla\big[(q - q_d)^T (q - q_d)\big] \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\xi\,\big[2(q - q_d)\big] \\
&= \xi\,(q - q_d)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute the Attractive Force**
$$F_{\text{att}}(q) = -\nabla U_{\text{att}}(q) = -\xi\,(q - q_d)$$

**Step 4: Control Law**
The robot velocity is set proportional to the force:
$$\dot{q}(t) = -\nabla U_{\text{att}} = -\xi\,(q - q_d)$$

**Step 5: Solve the ODE to Confirm Goal-Seeking**
This is a linear ODE: $\dot{q} = -\xi(q - q_d)$. The solution is:
$$q(t) = q_d + (q_0 - q_d)\,e^{-\xi t}$$
As $t \to \infty$, $q(t) \to q_d$. The exponential convergence rate is governed by $\xi$; a larger $\xi$ gives faster (but more aggressive) convergence.

**Goal-Seeking Behavior Explanation (2 marks):**
- The force vector $F_{\text{att}}$ always points from the robot's current position $q$ toward the goal $q_d$.
- The magnitude $\vert F_{\text{att}} \vert = \xi \vert\vert q - q_d \vert\vert$ is proportional to distance — large when far, small when close (smooth deceleration).
- Since the field is **conservative** (gradient of a scalar), it has zero curl: $\nabla \times F_{\text{att}} = 0$, guaranteeing no oscillatory or divergent trajectories.

> [!NOTE]
> **Awarding Marks:** 1 mark each for steps 1–4, 2 marks for ODE solution, 2 marks for behavioral explanation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Consider a mobile robot with state $x = [p_x, p_y, v_x, v_y]^T$ moving with constant-velocity model. The process noise covariance is $Q = \text{diag}(0.1, 0.1, 0.05, 0.05)$. Predict the state and covariance given $\hat{x}_{k-1} = [0, 0, 1, 0]^T$, $P_{k-1} = I_4$, $\Delta t = 1\,\text{s}$, and $u_k = [0, 0]^T$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Define the Constant-Velocity Model**
$$\begin{aligned}
p_{x,k} &= p_{x,k-1} + v_{x,k-1}\,\Delta t + w_1 \\
p_{y,k} &= p_{y,k-1} + v_{y,k-1}\,\Delta t + w_2 \\
v_{x,k} &= v_{x,k-1} + w_3 \\
v_{y,k} &= v_{y,k-1} + w_4
\end{aligned}$$

In matrix form:
$$x_k = F\,x_{k-1} + w_k$$
where:
$$F = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & \Delta t & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & \Delta t \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: State Prediction (2 marks)**
$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} &= F\,\hat{x}_{k-1\mid k-1} + B\,u_k \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Covariance Prediction (3 marks)**
$$\begin{aligned}
P_{k\mid k-1} &= F\,P_{k-1}\,F^T + Q \\
&= F\,I\,F^T + Q \\
&= F\,F^T + Q
\end{aligned}$$

Compute $F\,F^T$:
$$F\,F^T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Add $Q$:
$$P_{k\mid k-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2.1 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 1.05 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 1.05 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Interpretation (2 marks)**
- The position has moved from $(0,0)$ to $(1,0)$ — consistent with velocity $(1,0)$ and $\Delta t = 1$.
- The position uncertainty has grown from $1$ to $2.1$ (diagonal element), reflecting accumulated process noise.
- The velocity uncertainty grew from $1$ to $1.05$, reflecting the noise model $Q_{33} = 0.05$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Awarding Marks:** 1 mark for state equation setup, 2 marks for predicted state, 3 marks for covariance, 1 mark for interpretation.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) and contrast it with the EKF for nonlinear estimation in a dynamic obstacle tracking scenario.**

**Model Answer:**

**UKF Overview (3 marks):**
The UKF (Julier & Uhlmann, 1997) addresses a fundamental weakness of the EKF: linearization errors. Instead of approximating $f(\cdot)$ and $h(\cdot)$ by their Jacobians, the UKF deterministically samples a small set of $2n+1$ **sigma points** that capture the mean and covariance of the prior distribution exactly to second order:

$$\mathcal{X}_0 = \hat{x}, \quad \mathcal{X}_i = \hat{x} + (\sqrt{(n+\lambda)P})_i,\quad \mathcal{X}_{i+n} = \hat{x} - (\sqrt{(n+\lambda)P})_i$$

These sigma points are propagated through the *true nonlinear* function:
$$\mathcal{Y}_i = f(\mathcal{X}_i)$$

The posterior mean and covariance are recovered via weighted averages:
$$\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} = \sum_{i=0}^{2n} W_i^{(m)} \mathcal{Y}_i, \quad P_{k\mid k-1} = \sum_{i=0}^{2n} W_i^{(c)} (\mathcal{Y}_i - \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1})(\mathcal{Y}_i - \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1})^T + Q$$

**EKF vs UKF Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Criterion | EKF | UKF |
|---|---|---|
| Linearization | Jacobian $\partial f/\partial x$ | Sigma point sampling |
| Order of accuracy | First order (linear) | Second order (true mean & covariance) |
| Computational cost | $O(n^3)$ for Jacobian, fast | $O(n^3)$ for Cholesky, similar |
| Derivative required | Yes (analytical or numerical) | No (only function evaluations) |
| Performance in high nonlinearity | Degrades (large linearization error) | Robust |
| Application to obstacle tracking | Works for mild turns | Better for aggressive maneuvering |

**Dynamic Obstacle Tracking Justification:**
Moving obstacles (pedestrians, vehicles) follow *nonlinear* motion models (e.g., constant-turn-rate, bicycle model). The EKF accumulates linearization errors during sharp turns, causing the predicted trajectory to drift away from the true path. The UKF's higher-order accuracy makes it the preferred choice in modern autonomous driving stacks (Apollo, Autoware).

> [!NOTE]
> **Awarding Marks:** 3 marks for UKF derivation, 2 marks for sigma point equations, 2 marks for the comparison table or verbal contrast.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 3 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Navigation Function & Potential Fields in Dynamic Environment**

### Core Conceptual Points
- **Navigation Function (Koditschek & Rimon):** Analytic $\phi: \mathcal{F}_{\text{free}} \to [0,1]$ with unique minimum at goal, uniform maximum on $\partial \mathcal{F}$, all critical points Morse.
- **Construction Formula:** $\phi = \vert\vert q - q_d\vert\vert^2 \,/\, (\vert\vert q - q_d\vert\vert^{2k} + \beta(q))^{1/k}$ where $\beta = \prod_i (\vert\vert q - q_i\vert\vert^2 - r_i^2)$.
- **Parameter $k$:** Higher $k$ ⇒ more admissible (eliminates spurious minima); typically $k \geq 3$.
- **APF Components:** Attractive $\tfrac{1}{2}\xi \vert\vert q - q_d\vert\vert^2$ and Repulsive $\tfrac{1}{2}\eta(1/\rho - 1/\rho_0)^2$.
- **Dynamic APF:** Includes $\dot{\rho}$ term using relative velocity between robot and obstacle.
- **Gradient Flow Control:** $\dot{q} = -\nabla U$ for APF; $\dot{q} = -\kappa \nabla \phi$ for NF.

### Estimation Layer
- **Kalman Filter:** Two-step (Predict / Update); optimal for *linear Gaussian* systems.
- **EKF:** Uses Jacobian linearization; suitable for *mildly nonlinear* systems.
- **UKF:** Uses sigma points; *second-order accurate*; preferred for highly nonlinear dynamic obstacle tracking.
- **Particle Filter:** Monte Carlo sampling for *non-Gaussian / multi-modal* posteriors; requires resampling when $N_{\text{eff}} < N/2$.

### Key Equations to Memorize
- **Kalman Gain:** $K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} H_k^T (H_k P_{k\mid k-1} H_k^T + R_k)^{-1}$
- **NF Uniform Bound:** $0 \leq \phi \leq 1$
- **NF Obstacle Function:** $\beta_i = \vert\vert q - q_i\vert\vert^2 - r_i^2$
- **Dynamic Closing Rate:** $\dot{\rho} = (q - q_o)^T (v_{\text{robot}} - v_{\text{obs}}) \,/\, \vert\vert q - q_o \vert\vert$

### Exam-Writing Tips
- Always draw a **labeled diagram** of the workspace with goal, obstacles, and gradient vectors.
- Always state the **four axioms** of Navigation Function explicitly — partial answers lose marks.
- For Kalman Filter problems, **always compute the updated covariance** $P_{k\mid k}$, not just the state.
- For dynamic APF problems, mention the **closing velocity** $\dot{\rho}$ explicitly.
- Show all matrix dimensions; **scalar confusion** is a common mark-loser.

### Common Pitfalls
- ❌ Confusing $\beta_i$ (obstacle function) with $U_{\text{rep}}$ (potential).
- ❌ Forgetting to take absolute value of $\dot{\rho}$ in dynamic repulsion.
- ❌ Inverting $S_k$ incorrectly for non-scalar cases.
- ❌ Using EKF when system is highly nonlinear — UKF or PF is better.
- ❌ Ignoring process noise $Q_k$ in covariance update.
- ❌ Claiming NF has no local minima without justifying $k \geq 3$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prediction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Prediction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robot Motion Planning**, **Prediction** refers to the computational process of estimating a robot's (or an agent's) future state, configuration, or trajectory based on its current state, dynamic model, and applied control inputs, over a finite time horizon. Prediction forms the foundational block of **model-based planning**, **receding-horizon control (RHC)**, **trajectory tracking**, and **dynamic obstacle avoidance**.

Mathematically, prediction is the act of propagating a motion model $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = f(\mathbf{x}_k, \mathbf{u}_k, \mathbf{w}_k)$ forward in time, where $\mathbf{x}_k$ is the current state, $\mathbf{u}_k$ is the control input, and $\mathbf{w}_k$ represents process noise/disturbance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Verbatim)**
> *Prediction is the act of forecasting the future state or configuration of a robot over a defined time horizon by applying the robot's motion model (kinematic or dynamic) iteratively, beginning from the present measured or estimated state.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway. Your brain continuously performs **prediction**:

- You see a car ahead → your brain **predicts** where it will be in 3 seconds.
- You know your own speed → your brain **predicts** where *your* car will be in 5 seconds.
- You see a pedestrian stepping off the curb → your brain **predicts** whether they will cross your lane in time.

A robot does exactly the same thing — but with **mathematical precision**. The robot's "brain" (its motion planner + controller) uses equations of motion to **simulate the future** before committing to an action. This is *prediction*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Prediction Matters in Motion Planning**
> - Enables **look-ahead trajectory optimization** (the planner sees into the future).
> - Critical for **collision avoidance with moving obstacles** (dynamic environments).
> - Forms the **inner loop** of Model Predictive Control (MPC).
> - Used in **replanning** when the actual trajectory deviates from the predicted one.

## 1.3 Physical & Algorithmic Constants

| Quantity | Standard Symbol | Typical Unit | Notes |
|----------|-----------------|--------------|-------|
| Prediction horizon | $H_p$ or $N$ | seconds / steps | Finite look-ahead window |
| Sampling time | $\Delta t$ | **seconds (s)** | Discrete prediction step |
| Process noise covariance | $\mathbf{Q}$ | unit² | Uncertainty in motion model |
| Robot state dimension | $n$ | — | e.g., $n=3$ for 2D planar |
| State vector | $\mathbf{x}_k \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | — | Position, velocity, etc. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Prediction in Motion Planning

Prediction in KTU Module 3 is structured around three hierarchical pillars:

### Pillar 1: **Self-Prediction (Robot's Own Future)**
The robot uses its **kinematic** or **dynamic** model to predict where it *itself* will be in $H_p$ steps.

- **Kinematic Self-Prediction**: Uses geometric/velocity relations. Cheaper, ignores forces.
- **Dynamic Self-Prediction**: Uses Newton-Euler / Lagrangian equations. Accounts for forces, torques, inertia.

### Pillar 2: **Environment Prediction (Other Agents / Obstacles)**
The robot predicts the **future motion of dynamic obstacles** so it can plan a collision-free path.

- **Constant Velocity Model**: Simplest. Assumes obstacle moves at constant $v$.
- **Learning-Based Prediction**: Uses neural networks / Gaussian Processes trained on observed trajectories.
- **Intent Prediction**: High-level reasoning (e.g., "pedestrian intends to cross").

### Pillar 3: **Trajectory-Level Prediction (Path over Time)**
Instead of predicting a single future state, predict the **entire trajectory** (sequence of states over $H_p$).

- Used in **trajectory optimization**, **MPC**, and **dynamic replanning**.

## 2.2 Why and How of Each Prediction Step

| Step | "Why?" (Purpose) | "How?" (Mechanism) |
|------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1. State Estimation | Need current state to start | Sensors, encoders, Kalman filter |
| 2. Model Selection | Pick correct physics | Kinematic vs. dynamic decision |
| 3. State Propagation | Roll state forward | Apply $f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u})$ iteratively |
| 4. Uncertainty Growth | Real systems are noisy | Add covariance $\mathbf{P}_{k+1} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{P}_k\mathbf{A}^T + \mathbf{Q}$ |
| 5. Horizon Termination | Limit compute | Stop at $H_p$ or when state meets goal |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The Following Table is High-Yield for KTU University Exam Questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description | When to Use |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | Discrete State Propagation | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = f(\mathbf{x}_k, \mathbf{u}_k)$ | Generic non-linear motion model | Base of all prediction |
| 2 | Linear Prediction | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}_k$ | LTI system prediction | When model is linearized |
| 3 | Constant Velocity (1D) | $x(t+\Delta t) = x(t) + v(t)\cdot \Delta t$ | Simplest kinematic prediction | Obstacle tracking |
| 4 | Constant Acceleration (1D) | $x(t+\Delta t) = x(t) + v(t)\Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2}a(t)\Delta t^2$ | With acceleration term | Vehicles, drones |
| 5 | 2D Constant Velocity (Vector) | $\mathbf{p}_{k+1} = \mathbf{p}_k + \mathbf{v}_k \Delta t$ | Position update | Mobile robots |
| 6 | Odometry-based Prediction | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k+1} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_k \oplus \Delta \mathbf{x}_{odom}$ | Composition of odometry | Wheeled robots |
| 7 | Covariance Propagation | $\mathbf{P}_{k+1} = \mathbf{A}_k \mathbf{P}_k \mathbf{A}_k^T + \mathbf{Q}_k$ | Uncertainty growth | EKF, MPC |
| 8 | Polynomial Trajectory Prediction | $p(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ | Cubic prediction | Trajectory planning |
| 9 | Quintic (5th order) Prediction | $p(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{5} a_i t^i$ | Smooth jerk-continuous | High-fidelity motion |
| 10 | Kalman Filter Prediction Step | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{A}\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1\vert k-1} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}_{k-1}$ | Two-step filter predict-update | State estimation |
| 11 | Prediction Horizon Cost (MPC) | $J = \sum_{i=0}^{H_p-1} \ell(\mathbf{x}_{k+i}, \mathbf{u}_{k+i})$ | Sum of stage costs | Model Predictive Control |
| 12 | Time-to-Collision (TTC) | $\tau_{TTC} = \frac{\vert \mathbf{d} \vert}{\vert \mathbf{v}_{rel} \vert}$ | Collision time estimate | Dynamic avoidance |

> Use `\vert` or `\mid` (not `|`) when writing absolute value inline in any answer sheet to avoid markdown table corruption.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Prediction is the **backbone** of:

- **Autonomous Vehicles (AVs)**: Predicting pedestrian & vehicle trajectories 3–5 seconds ahead (used in Tesla FSD, Waymo).
- **Industrial Robotic Arms**: Predicting grasp stability and object motion post-pick.
- **Aerial Swarms (Drones)**: Predicting neighbor drone positions to avoid mid-air collision.
- **Human-Robot Collaboration (HRC)**: Predicting human intent for safe co-working.
- **Space Rovers (NASA)**: Predicting terrain traversability before committing to a path.
- **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva)**: Predicting shelf position and human worker motion.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Below are exhaustive derivations — every algebraic step is explicitly shown. No shortcuts, no truncation.**

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Constant Velocity Prediction Model (1D → Vector Generalization)

### Starting Point (Physical Assumption)
A point mass moves with **constant velocity** $v$ along a straight line. We know its position at time $t$ and want to predict its position at $t + \Delta t$.

**Step 1** — Write the elementary kinematic relation (definition of velocity):

$$v = \frac{dx}{dt}$$

**Step 2** — Rearrange to isolate the differential of position:

$$dx = v \, dt$$

**Step 3** — Integrate both sides from time $t$ to $t + \Delta t$:

$$\int_{x(t)}^{x(t+\Delta t)} dx = \int_{t}^{t+\Delta t} v \, dt$$

**Step 4** — Since $v$ is constant, it comes out of the integral on the RHS:

$$x(t+\Delta t) - x(t) = v \int_{t}^{t+\Delta t} dt$$

**Step 5** — Evaluate the integral:

$$\int_{t}^{t+\Delta t} dt = \Delta t$$

**Step 6** — Substitute back:

$$x(t+\Delta t) - x(t) = v \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 7** — Solve for the predicted position:

$$\boxed{x(t+\Delta t) = x(t) + v \cdot \Delta t}$$

### Vector Generalization (2D)
Define the state as $\mathbf{x}_k = \begin{bmatrix} x_k \\ y_k \\ v_{x,k} \\ v_{y,k} \end{bmatrix}$ with the linear model matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A} &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & \Delta t & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & \Delta t \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \mathbf{0}_{4\times 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the predicted state evolves as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{x}_{k+1} &= \mathbf{A} \, \mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{B} \, \mathbf{u}_k \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & \Delta t & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & \Delta t \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_k \\ y_k \\ v_{x,k} \\ v_{y,k} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_k + v_{x,k}\Delta t \\ y_k + v_{y,k}\Delta t \\ v_{x,k} \\ v_{y,k} \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is **exactly** the constant-velocity prediction used in KTU problems on dynamic obstacle tracking.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Constant Acceleration Prediction (2D, 3-Step Horizon)

### Setup
A robot has position $\mathbf{p}$, velocity $\mathbf{v}$, and acceleration $\mathbf{a}$, all 2D vectors. We want to predict the state over the next **3 time steps** ($H_p = 3$).

### Step 1: Write the kinematic equations of motion

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{p}_{k+1} &= \mathbf{p}_k + \mathbf{v}_k \Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2} \mathbf{a}_k \Delta t^2 \\
\mathbf{v}_{k+1} &= \mathbf{v}_k + \mathbf{a}_k \Delta t \\
\mathbf{a}_{k+1} &= \mathbf{a}_k \quad \text{(assumed constant)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Build the augmented state vector

Let $\mathbf{x}_k = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{p}_k \\ \mathbf{v}_k \\ \mathbf{a}_k \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^6$ (concatenating 2D vectors).

The state transition matrix becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A} &= \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{I}_2 & \Delta t \, \mathbf{I}_2 & \tfrac{1}{2}\Delta t^2 \mathbf{I}_2 \\ \mathbf{0}_2 & \mathbf{I}_2 & \Delta t \, \mathbf{I}_2 \\ \mathbf{0}_2 & \mathbf{0}_2 & \mathbf{I}_2 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Predict one step ahead (k → k+1)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{x}_{k+1} &= \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x}_k \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{p}_k + \mathbf{v}_k \Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2} \mathbf{a}_k \Delta t^2 \\ \mathbf{v}_k + \mathbf{a}_k \Delta t \\ \mathbf{a}_k \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Predict two steps ahead (k+1 → k+2)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{p}_{k+2} &= \mathbf{p}_{k+1} + \mathbf{v}_{k+1} \Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2} \mathbf{a}_{k+1} \Delta t^2 \\
&= (\mathbf{p}_k + \mathbf{v}_k \Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2} \mathbf{a}_k \Delta t^2) + (\mathbf{v}_k + \mathbf{a}_k \Delta t)\Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2} \mathbf{a}_k \Delta t^2 \\
&= \mathbf{p}_k + 2\mathbf{v}_k \Delta t + 2\mathbf{a}_k \Delta t^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Predict three steps ahead (k+2 → k+3)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{p}_{k+3} &= \mathbf{p}_{k+2} + \mathbf{v}_{k+2} \Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2} \mathbf{a}_{k+2} \Delta t^2 \\
\mathbf{v}_{k+2} &= \mathbf{v}_k + 2\mathbf{a}_k \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting and simplifying:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{p}_{k+3} &= (\mathbf{p}_k + 2\mathbf{v}_k \Delta t + 2\mathbf{a}_k \Delta t^2) + (\mathbf{v}_k + 2\mathbf{a}_k \Delta t)\Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{a}_k \Delta t^2 \\
&= \mathbf{p}_k + 3\mathbf{v}_k \Delta t + \tfrac{9}{2}\mathbf{a}_k \Delta t^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result — General Closed-Form for Constant Acceleration

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{p}_{k+n} = \mathbf{p}_k + n\,\mathbf{v}_k\,\Delta t + \tfrac{n^2}{2}\,\mathbf{a}_k\,\Delta t^2}
$$

where $n$ is the prediction step count.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **kinematic cornerstone** used in KTU 2024 ESE problems on "predict the obstacle position after $N$ steps" — memorize it.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cubic Polynomial Trajectory Prediction

### Step 1: Assume a cubic polynomial form for position as a function of time

$$p(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

### Step 2: Derive velocity (first derivative) and acceleration (second derivative)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{p}(t) &= a_1 + 2a_2 t + 3a_3 t^2 \\
\ddot{p}(t) &= 2a_2 + 6a_3 t
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Apply 4 boundary conditions (start position, end position, start velocity, end velocity)

Let $t_0 = 0$ and $t_f = T$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(0) = p_0 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad a_0 = p_0 \\
p(T) = p_f \quad &\Rightarrow \quad a_0 + a_1 T + a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 = p_f \\
\dot{p}(0) = v_0 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad a_1 = v_0 \\
\dot{p}(T) = v_f \quad &\Rightarrow \quad a_1 + 2a_2 T + 3a_3 T^2 = v_f
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Solve the linear system for the coefficients

From the second and fourth equations, substitute $a_0 = p_0$ and $a_1 = v_0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_f - p_0 - v_0 T &= a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 \\
v_f - v_0 &= 2a_2 T + 3a_3 T^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Write in matrix form $\mathbf{M}\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{b}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} T^2 & T^3 \\ 2T & 3T^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_2 \\ a_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} p_f - p_0 - v_0 T \\ v_f - v_0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Solve via Cramer's rule

Determinant: $D = T^2 \cdot 3T^2 - T^3 \cdot 2T = 3T^4 - 2T^4 = T^4$

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_2 &= \frac{(p_f - p_0 - v_0 T)(3T^2) - T^3(v_f - v_0)}{T^4} \\
&= \frac{3(p_f - p_0 - v_0 T) - T(v_f - v_0)}{T^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_3 &= \frac{T^2(v_f - v_0) - 2T(p_f - p_0 - v_0 T)}{T^4} \\
&= \frac{(v_f - v_0) - \frac{2}{T}(p_f - p_0 - v_0 T)}{T^2} \\
&= \frac{-2(p_f - p_0) + (2v_0 + v_f)T}{T^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Final compact form (the famous KTU cubic coefficients)

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
a_0 &= p_0 \\
a_1 &= v_0 \\
a_2 &= \frac{3(p_f - p_0)}{T^2} - \frac{2v_0 + v_f}{T} \\
a_3 &= \frac{-2(p_f - p_0)}{T^3} + \frac{v_0 + v_f}{T^2}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

These coefficients allow **prediction of position, velocity, and acceleration at any time** $0 \le t \le T$, which is the foundation of smooth trajectory prediction in motion planning.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Covariance (Uncertainty) Growth in Prediction

### Setup
In the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** prediction step, the uncertainty in the state estimate grows because the model is imperfect. We quantify this using the **covariance matrix** $\mathbf{P}_k$.

### Step 1: Recall the linearized state prediction

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k+1\vert k} = f(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k}, \mathbf{u}_k) \approx \mathbf{A}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k} + \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{u}_k$$

### Step 2: The true state evolves as

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{A}_k \mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{u}_k + \mathbf{w}_k$$

where $\mathbf{w}_k \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{Q}_k)$ is the process noise.

### Step 3: Define the prediction error

$$\mathbf{e}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k+1\vert k} = \mathbf{A}_k (\mathbf{x}_k - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k}) + \mathbf{w}_k = \mathbf{A}_k \mathbf{e}_k + \mathbf{w}_k$$

### Step 4: Compute the covariance of the error

$$\mathbf{P}_{k+1\vert k} = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{e}_{k+1} \mathbf{e}_{k+1}^T]$$

### Step 5: Substitute and expand

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{P}_{k+1\vert k} &= \mathbb{E}[(\mathbf{A}_k \mathbf{e}_k + \mathbf{w}_k)(\mathbf{A}_k \mathbf{e}_k + \mathbf{w}_k)^T] \\
&= \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{A}_k \mathbf{e}_k \mathbf{e}_k^T \mathbf{A}_k^T] + \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{A}_k \mathbf{e}_k \mathbf{w}_k^T] + \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{w}_k \mathbf{e}_k^T \mathbf{A}_k^T] + \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{w}_k \mathbf{w}_k^T]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Apply zero-mean independence assumptions

$\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{e}_k \mathbf{w}_k^T] = \mathbf{0}$ and $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{w}_k \mathbf{e}_k^T] = \mathbf{0}$ because prediction error and process noise are uncorrelated. Also, $\mathbf{A}_k$ is deterministic.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{P}_{k+1\vert k} &= \mathbf{A}_k \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{e}_k \mathbf{e}_k^T] \mathbf{A}_k^T + \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{w}_k \mathbf{w}_k^T] \\
&= \mathbf{A}_k \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k} \mathbf{A}_k^T + \mathbf{Q}_k
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result — Covariance Prediction Equation

$$\boxed{\mathbf{P}_{k+1\vert k} = \mathbf{A}_k \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k} \mathbf{A}_k^T + \mathbf{Q}_k}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** This equation is the **#1 most-asked** prediction equation in KTU 2024 ESE. It is the dual of $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}_k$ — *state predicts linearly, uncertainty predicts quadratically.*

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Prediction Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Current State x_k from Sensors] --> B[State Estimation Filter]
    B --> C[Estimated State x_hat_k]
    C --> D{Motion Model Selection}
    D -->|Low Speed| E[Kinematic Model]
    D -->|High Speed or Contact| F[Dynamic Model]
    E --> G[Iterative State Propagation]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Predicted Trajectory over H_p]
    H --> I[Collision Check with Obstacles]
    I -->|Collision Predicted| J[Replan or Brake]
    I -->|Safe| K[Execute Control u_k]
    K --> L[New Sensor Measurement]
    L --> B
    H --> M[Uncertainty Covariance P_k]
    M --> N[Update Belief for Next Step]
    N --> B
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Prediction Types Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Prediction in Motion Planning] --> SP[Self Prediction]
    P --> EP[Environment Prediction]
    P --> TP[Trajectory Level Prediction]
    SP --> SK[Kinematic Self Prediction]
    SP --> SD[Dynamic Self Prediction]
    EP --> ECV[Constant Velocity Model]
    EP --> ECA[Constant Acceleration Model]
    EP --> ELB[Learning Based Prediction]
    EP --> EI[Intent Prediction]
    TP --> TPOL[Polynomial Trajectory]
    TP --> TSPL[Spline Trajectory]
    TP --> TMPC[MPC Rollout]
    SK --> KF[Kalman Filter Based]
    SD --> NMPC[Nonlinear MPC]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Prediction in Receding Horizon Loop

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant R as Robot/Sensor
    participant E as Estimator (EKF)
    participant P as Predictor
    participant O as Optimizer
    participant C as Controller
    R->>E: Provide measurement y_k
    E->>E: Update state x_hat_k
    E->>P: Send x_hat_k
    P->>P: Roll forward H_p steps
    P->>O: Send predicted trajectory
    O->>O: Solve min cost J over H_p
    O->>C: Return optimal u_k
    C->>R: Apply control u_k
    R-->>E: Next measurement y_{k+1}
    Note over P,O: This loop repeats every Delta t seconds
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Uncertainty Growth Over Horizon

```mermaid
graph LR
    S0[State at k: Tight Ellipse] --> S1[State at k+1: Larger Ellipse]
    S1 --> S2[State at k+2: Even Larger]
    S2 --> S3[State at k+Hp: Highly Uncertain]
    S0 -.->|P_k| P1((Small))
    S3 -.->|P_{k+Hp}| P2((Large))
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** As the prediction horizon $H_p$ grows, the **uncertainty ellipse inflates** — this is why prediction is limited to a finite horizon. KTU 2024 expects students to explain this *visually* and *quantitatively* using $\mathbf{P}_{k+1} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{P}_k\mathbf{A}^T + \mathbf{Q}$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define prediction in the context of robot motion planning. Mention any two models used for prediction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Prediction is the process of estimating the future state or configuration of a robot over a finite time horizon $H_p$ by propagating the current state $\mathbf{x}_k$ forward in time using a motion model. It is essential for model-based planning, MPC, and dynamic obstacle avoidance.

**Two models used for prediction (1.5 marks each):**
1. **Kinematic Model** — based on velocity and geometry; e.g., $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}_k$ with constant velocity.
2. **Dynamic Model** — based on Newton-Euler equations; accounts for forces, torques, and inertia.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a prediction horizon? Why is it kept finite in MPC-based motion planning?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **prediction horizon** $H_p$ is the number of future time steps over which the robot's trajectory is predicted during one planning cycle. It defines how far the planner "looks into the future" before deciding on a control action.

It is kept **finite** (1.5 marks) because:
- Computational cost grows with horizon length (real-time constraint).
- Model uncertainty grows with horizon (covariance inflation — $\mathbf{P}_{k+H_p}$ becomes large).
- Beyond a certain point, predictions become unreliable and planning becomes futile.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Answer **ONE** of the two question choices below. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`**

**A robotic mobile platform moves with constant velocity in 2D. At time $k$, the measured state is:**
$$\mathbf{x}_k = \begin{bmatrix} x_k \\ y_k \\ \dot{x}_k \\ \dot{y}_k \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.0 \\ 1.0 \\ 0.5 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$
**The sampling time is $\Delta t = 0.5$ s.**

**(a)** Derive the linear state prediction model for a constant-velocity mobile robot and predict the state at $k+1$ and $k+2$. Show all matrix operations explicitly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the robot has an on-board covariance $\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k} = \text{diag}(0.01, 0.01, 0.005, 0.005)$ and process noise covariance $\mathbf{Q} = \text{diag}(0.001, 0.001, 0.0005, 0.0005)$, compute the predicted covariance $\mathbf{P}_{k+1\vert k}$ and comment on uncertainty growth. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the general discrete kinematic model:
$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}_k$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Construct the constant-velocity transition matrix:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A} &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & \Delta t & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & \Delta t \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 0.5 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute $\mathbf{x}_{k+1}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{x}_{k+1} &= \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}_k = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 0.5 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2.0 \\ 1.0 \\ 0.5 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 2.0 + 0.5(0.5) \\ 1.0 + 0.5(0.2) \\ 0.5 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.25 \\ 1.10 \\ 0.50 \\ 0.20 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute $\mathbf{x}_{k+2} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}_{k+1}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{x}_{k+2} &= \begin{bmatrix} 2.25 + 0.5(0.50) \\ 1.10 + 0.5(0.20) \\ 0.50 \\ 0.20 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.50 \\ 1.20 \\ 0.50 \\ 0.20 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer for (a):**
$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.25 \\ 1.10 \\ 0.50 \\ 0.20 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{x}_{k+2} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.50 \\ 1.20 \\ 0.50 \\ 0.20 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Apply covariance prediction formula:
$$\mathbf{P}_{k+1\vert k} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k}\mathbf{A}^T + \mathbf{Q}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A}\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k} &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 0.5 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.01 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.005 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0.005 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 & 0 & 0.0025 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.01 & 0 & 0.0025 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.005 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0.005 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute $(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k})\mathbf{A}^T$ and add $\mathbf{Q}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A}\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k}\mathbf{A}^T &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 & 0 & 0.0025 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.01 & 0 & 0.0025 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.005 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0.005 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.5 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.010+0.00125 & 0 & 0.0025 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.010+0.00125 & 0 & 0.0025 \\ 0.0025 & 0 & 0.005 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.0025 & 0 & 0.005 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.01125 & 0 & 0.0025 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.01125 & 0 & 0.0025 \\ 0.0025 & 0 & 0.005 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.0025 & 0 & 0.005 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Add $\mathbf{Q}$:
$$
\boxed{\mathbf{P}_{k+1\vert k} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.01225 & 0 & 0.0025 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.01225 & 0 & 0.0025 \\ 0.0025 & 0 & 0.0055 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.0025 & 0 & 0.0055 \end{bmatrix}}
$$

**Comment (full 7 marks requires the comment):** The diagonal entries (variances) have increased from $[0.01, 0.01, 0.005, 0.005]$ to $[0.01225, 0.01225, 0.0055, 0.0055]$ — confirming that **uncertainty grows with each prediction step**. The off-diagonal coupling (e.g., $0.0025$) shows that position uncertainty becomes correlated with velocity uncertainty, a hallmark of kinematic prediction.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`**

**(a)** With reference to a robotic manipulator trajectory, derive the cubic polynomial prediction equation and the closed-form coefficients for a segment with start position $p_0$, end position $p_f$, start velocity $v_0$, end velocity $v_f$, and segment time $T$. Show every step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1-DOF robot must move from $p_0 = 0$ m to $p_f = 1$ m in $T = 2$ s with $v_0 = 0$ m/s and $v_f = 0$ m/s. Compute the cubic coefficients and predict the position at $t = 0.5$ s, $t = 1.0$ s, and $t = 1.5$ s. Discuss the smoothness property. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Assume a cubic polynomial:
$$p(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Differentiate to get velocity:
$$\dot{p}(t) = a_1 + 2a_2 t + 3a_3 t^2$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply 4 boundary conditions at $t=0$ and $t=T$:
- $p(0) = p_0 \Rightarrow a_0 = p_0$
- $\dot{p}(0) = v_0 \Rightarrow a_1 = v_0$
- $p(T) = p_f \Rightarrow p_0 + v_0 T + a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 = p_f$
- $\dot{p}(T) = v_f \Rightarrow v_0 + 2a_2 T + 3a_3 T^2 = v_f$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Solve the resulting $2\times 2$ linear system for $a_2, a_3$ via Cramer's rule (determinant $= T^4$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_2 &= \frac{3(p_f - p_0 - v_0 T) - T(v_f - v_0)}{T^2} \\
&= \frac{3(p_f - p_0)}{T^2} - \frac{2v_0 + v_f}{T}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_3 &= \frac{T^2(v_f - v_0) - 2T(p_f - p_0 - v_0 T)}{T^4} \\
&= \frac{-2(p_f - p_0)}{T^3} + \frac{v_0 + v_f}{T^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Box the final result:
$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
a_0 &= p_0, \quad a_1 = v_0 \\
a_2 &= \frac{3(p_f - p_0)}{T^2} - \frac{2v_0 + v_f}{T} \\
a_3 &= \frac{-2(p_f - p_0)}{T^3} + \frac{v_0 + v_f}{T^2}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Substitute the given values: $p_0 = 0$, $p_f = 1$, $v_0 = 0$, $v_f = 0$, $T = 2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_0 &= 0 \\
a_1 &= 0 \\
a_2 &= \frac{3(1 - 0)}{4} - \frac{0 + 0}{2} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75 \\
a_3 &= \frac{-2(1 - 0)}{8} + \frac{0 + 0}{4} = -\frac{1}{4} = -0.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The trajectory equation:
$$p(t) = 0 + 0\cdot t + 0.75\,t^2 - 0.25\,t^3 = 0.75t^2 - 0.25t^3$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Evaluate at the three time points:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(0.5) &= 0.75(0.25) - 0.25(0.125) = 0.1875 - 0.03125 = 0.15625 \text{ m} \\
p(1.0) &= 0.75(1) - 0.25(1) = 0.75 - 0.25 = 0.50 \text{ m} \\
p(1.5) &= 0.75(2.25) - 0.25(3.375) = 1.6875 - 0.84375 = 0.84375 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Smoothness discussion:
- **Position** is continuous and smooth (polynomial of degree 3).
- **Velocity** $\dot{p}(t) = 1.5t - 0.75t^2$ is continuous (zero jerk discontinuities at endpoints if $v_0 = v_f = 0$).
- **Acceleration** $\ddot{p}(t) = 1.5 - 1.5t$ varies linearly — start accel $= 1.5$ m/s², end accel $= -1.5$ m/s².
- **Cubic gives $C^2$ continuity** (position and velocity continuous, acceleration linear) — suitable for most industrial manipulators. For jerk-continuous motion, use **quintic (5th order)** polynomials.

**Final Tabulated Result (1 mark):**

| Time $t$ (s) | Predicted Position $p(t)$ (m) |
|--------------|-------------------------------|
| 0.0 | 0.000 |
| 0.5 | 0.156 |
| 1.0 | 0.500 |
| 1.5 | 0.844 |
| 2.0 | 1.000 |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes — Lose 2 to 4 Marks Each!**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\mathbf{Q}$ term** in $\mathbf{P}_{k+1} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{P}_k\mathbf{A}^T + \mathbf{Q}$ — costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing prediction step with update step** in Kalman filter. The **prediction** uses only the model; the **update** uses measurement. KTU asks specifically about prediction.
> 3. **Using $\Delta t = 1$ s by default** — always read the question for $\Delta t$.
> 4. **Writing $p(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ without showing the differentiation** for velocity and acceleration — the boundary conditions on $\dot{p}$ and $\ddot{p}$ are the *whole point* and earn 3-4 marks.
> 5. **Not commenting on uncertainty growth** in covariance questions — verbal explanation is mandatory for full marks.
> 6. **Mixing up vector and scalar notation** in 2D constant velocity problems — write everything in vector form $\mathbf{p}_{k+1} = \mathbf{p}_k + \mathbf{v}_k \Delta t$.
> 7. **Skipping units** in final answers — always write m, m/s, s, etc.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- **Core Idea**: Prediction = rolling the motion model forward in time from the current state.
- **Three Types**: Self-prediction, Environment prediction, Trajectory-level prediction.
- **Key Equation (State)**: $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}_k$
- **Key Equation (Covariance)**: $\mathbf{P}_{k+1} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{P}_k\mathbf{A}^T + \mathbf{Q}$
- **Constant Velocity (1D)**: $x(t+\Delta t) = x(t) + v\,\Delta t$
- **Constant Acceleration (1D)**: $x(t+\Delta t) = x(t) + v\,\Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2}a\,\Delta t^2$
- **General Closed-Form (Const Acc)**: $\mathbf{p}_{k+n} = \mathbf{p}_k + n\mathbf{v}_k\Delta t + \tfrac{n^2}{2}\mathbf{a}_k\Delta t^2$
- **Cubic Polynomial Coefficients** (KTU 2024 *most-asked*):
  - $a_2 = \frac{3(p_f - p_0)}{T^2} - \frac{2v_0 + v_f}{T}$
  - $a_3 = \frac{-2(p_f - p_0)}{T^3} + \frac{v_0 + v_f}{T^2}$
- **Prediction Horizon** $H_p$: finite because of compute cost + uncertainty inflation.
- **MPC Stage Cost**: $J = \sum_{i=0}^{H_p - 1} \ell(\mathbf{x}_{k+i}, \mathbf{u}_{k+i})$
- **Time-to-Collision**: $\tau_{TTC} = \frac{\vert \mathbf{d} \vert}{\vert \mathbf{v}_{rel} \vert}$
- **Uncertainty Grows** with each prediction step (covariance diagonal entries increase).
- **Polynomial Choice**: Cubic for $C^2$ continuity, Quintic for $C^3$ (jerk-continuous).
- **Linear vs Nonlinear Models**: Linear → use $\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B}$; Nonlinear → linearize via Jacobian $\mathbf{A}_k = \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\big\vert_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k}$.
- **Always include units** in the final answer.
- **Always comment on** the meaning of the result (uncertainty growth, smoothness, etc.) for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimization in Robot Motion Planning** is the mathematical process of determining the *best* trajectory, path, or control input from a set of available alternatives, subject to a defined set of **objective (cost) functions** and **kinodynamic constraints**. Formally, it seeks a control sequence $\mathbf{u}(t)$ and resulting state $\mathbf{x}(t)$ that minimizes a performance index $J(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}, t)$ while satisfying the robot dynamics $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u})$ and boundary conditions.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, optimization is treated as the **bridge between geometric path planning and executable, real-world motion**. A path that is geometrically valid (collision-free) but infeasible for the actuators (too fast, too much torque) is useless — optimization refines the path to be physically realizable.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Robot Motion Optimization

> [!NOTE]
> Every optimization problem in robotics is defined by exactly three components:
> 1. **Decision Variables** — the quantities we can choose (joint angles $\mathbf{q}$, velocities $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$, accelerations $\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$, control torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}$, or waypoint times $t_i$).
> 2. **Objective Function (Cost)** — a scalar $J$ that quantifies "goodness" (minimum time, minimum energy, minimum jerk, minimum path length).
> 3. **Constraints** — equality constraints (dynamics) and inequality constraints (joint limits, obstacle avoidance, torque limits).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Hiking Trip Planner

Imagine you are planning a mountain hike from base camp (start) to the summit (goal). You have:
- **Decision variables** = which trails to take and how long to rest at each viewpoint.
- **Objective** = either shortest distance, least energy (calories burned), or fastest time — these are three different optimal solutions.
- **Constraints** = the trails must not pass through a swamp (obstacles), your knees cannot exceed a certain slope (joint limits), and you must rest at least 10 minutes per hour (dynamic constraints).

A naive plan might send you straight up the steepest face — *geometrically shortest* but *energy-expensive* and *joint-destroying*. **Optimization** is the smart planner that finds the trail balancing *all* your goals. In robotics, the "hiker" is the robot's end-effector, the "trails" are candidate trajectories, and the "rest stops" are feasible joint configurations.

## 1.4 Classification of Optimization Problems in Robotics

| Classification Axis | Types | Robotics Example |
|---|---|---|
| **By Variables** | Continuous vs. Discrete | Continuous: joint angles; Discrete: visiting waypoint sequence (TSP) |
| **By Constraints** | Constrained vs. Unconstrained | Constrained: joint limits; Unconstrained: ideal joint space |
| **By Linearity** | Linear vs. Nonlinear | Linear: LQR; Nonlinear: minimum-time problems |
| **By Optimality** | Local vs. Global | Local: gradient descent; Global: simulated annealing, GA |
| **By Dynamics** | Kinematic vs. Dynamic | Kinematic: shortest path; Dynamic: minimum-torque |
| **By Time** | Open-loop vs. Closed-loop (Receding Horizon) | MPC uses receding horizon |

## 1.5 Standard Performance Indices Used in KTU Syllabus

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate:** Students must recognize the four canonical cost functionals:
> $$J = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} L(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}, t)\, dt$$
> where $L$ takes the form $1$ (time-optimal), $\mathbf{u}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{u}$ (energy-optimal), $1 + \lambda \ddot{\mathbf{q}}^T\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ (jerk-penalized), or $\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{u}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{u}$ (quadratic / LQR).

## 1.6 Why Optimization is Indispensable in Motion Planning

- **Reduces wear and tear** on actuators (energy-optimal trajectories).
- **Improves cycle time** in manufacturing cells (time-optimal trajectories).
- **Enhances passenger comfort** in autonomous vehicles (jerk-minimized).
- **Guarantees feasibility** under physical actuator limits.
- **Enables predictive behavior** via Model Predictive Control (MPC).
- **Enables learning** when combined with neural networks (differentiable optimization layers).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Formulation of the Optimal Control Problem

The **Bolza problem** (general form encountered in KTU Module 3) is stated as:

$$\min_{\mathbf{u}(t)} J = \Phi(\mathbf{x}(t_f), t_f) + \int_{t_0}^{t_f} L(\mathbf{x}(t), \mathbf{u}(t), t)\, dt$$

subject to:
- System dynamics: $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}, t)$ (equality constraint).
- Boundary conditions: $\mathbf{x}(t_0) = \mathbf{x}_0$, $\mathbf{x}(t_f) = \mathbf{x}_f$ (or $\mathbf{x}_f$ free).
- Path constraints: $g(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}, t) \le 0$ (e.g., joint limits, torque bounds).
- Control bounds: $\mathbf{u}_{\min} \le \mathbf{u}(t) \le \mathbf{u}_{\max}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Bolza"?** In 1913, mathematician Oskar Bolza unified three classical variational problems (Lagrange, Mayer, and a mixed form) into one general functional. KTU examiners use this terminology to test whether students can recognize which *form* is being asked.

## 2.2 Lagrange, Mayer, and Bolza Equivalence

| Problem | Form | Special Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Lagrange** | $J = \int L\, dt$ | $\Phi = 0$, integrated cost only |
| **Mayer** | $J = \Phi(\mathbf{x}(t_f), t_f)$ | $L = 0$, terminal cost only |
| **Bolza** | $J = \Phi + \int L\, dt$ | General form (most common) |

## 2.3 Calculus of Variations — The Heart of Continuous Optimization

The **fundamental theorem of the calculus of variations** states that a function $\mathbf{x}^*(t)$ minimizes a functional $J$ if and only if the **Euler-Lagrange equation** is satisfied:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{x}} - \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\mathbf{x}}}\right) = \mathbf{0}$$

For the general optimal control case, the augmented cost includes system dynamics via a **costate (Lagrange multiplier) vector** $\boldsymbol{\lambda}(t)$:

$$\mathcal{H} = L(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}, t) + \boldsymbol{\lambda}^T f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}, t)$$

This is the **Hamiltonian**, and the necessary conditions for optimality are the **Euler-Lagrange / Hamilton equations**:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{H}}{\partial \boldsymbol{\lambda}} = f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}, t)$$

$$\dot{\boldsymbol{\lambda}} = -\frac{\partial \mathcal{H}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{H}}{\partial \mathbf{u}} = \mathbf{0} \quad \text{(stationarity condition)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These three equations form the backbone of KTU Module 3 Part B (14-mark) questions. Memorize them as the **"Three Pillars of PMP"**: state equation, costate equation, stationarity.

## 2.4 Pontryagin's Minimum Principle (PMP)

When the control $\mathbf{u}(t)$ is **bounded** (e.g., $|\tau_i| \le \tau_{\max}$), the stationarity condition is **replaced** by the global minimum:

$$\mathbf{u}^*(t) = \arg\min_{\mathbf{u} \in \mathcal{U}} \mathcal{H}(\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{u}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*, t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Difference from Euler-Lagrange:** Euler-Lagrange finds a *stationary* point; PMP guarantees the *global minimum* over a bounded admissible set $\mathcal{U}$. This is why PMP handles time-optimal bang-bang control elegantly.

## 2.5 Direct vs. Indirect Optimization Methods

| Aspect | Indirect Methods | Direct Methods |
|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Derive optimality conditions, then solve | Transcribe to NLP, then solve |
| **Key Tool** | Euler-Lagrange, PMP, Two-Point BVP | Collocation, multiple shooting, pseudospectral |
| **Difficulty** | High (analytical, sensitive to initial guess) | Lower (numerical, robust) |
| **Accuracy** | Very high | High (mesh refinement) |
| **KTU Frequency** | Theory questions (Module 3, Part A) | Numerical problems (Part B) |
| **Examples** | LQR, minimum-jerk trajectory | Direct collocation, SNOPT, IPOPT |

## 2.6 Numerical Optimization Algorithms (KTU High-Yield)

### 2.6.1 Gradient Descent
$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \eta_k \nabla J(\mathbf{x}_k)$$
where $\eta_k > 0$ is the step size (learning rate).

### 2.6.2 Newton's Method
$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{H}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}_k) \nabla J(\mathbf{x}_k)$$
where $\mathbf{H}$ is the Hessian — converges quadratically near optimum.

### 2.6.3 Sequential Quadratic Programming (SQP)
Solves a sequence of quadratic programs approximating the original NLP. Industry standard for trajectory optimization.

### 2.6.4 Quadratic Penalty and Augmented Lagrangian
Convert constrained to unconstrained:
$$J_{\text{pen}} = J(\mathbf{x}) + \mu \sum_i [\max(0, g_i(\mathbf{x}))]^2$$
$$\mathcal{L}_A = J + \mu \sum g_i^2 + \sum \lambda_i g_i$$

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J = \Phi + \int L\, dt$ | General Bolza performance index | scalar |
| 2 | $\mathcal{H} = L + \boldsymbol{\lambda}^T f$ | Hamiltonian (costate trick) | scalar |
| 3 | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{H}}{\partial \boldsymbol{\lambda}}$ | State equation (PMP #1) | state/s |
| 4 | $\dot{\boldsymbol{\lambda}} = -\frac{\partial \mathcal{H}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$ | Costate equation (PMP #2) | costate/s |
| 5 | $\frac{\partial \mathcal{H}}{\partial \mathbf{u}} = \mathbf{0}$ | Stationarity (unconstrained) | scalar eq. |
| 6 | $\mathbf{u}^* = \arg\min_{\mathbf{u} \in \mathcal{U}} \mathcal{H}$ | PMP minimum (bounded) | optimal $\mathbf{u}$ |
| 7 | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} - \frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}} = 0$ | Euler-Lagrange (basic) | scalar |
| 8 | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \eta \nabla J$ | Gradient descent update | state |
| 9 | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{H}^{-1} \nabla J$ | Newton update | state |
| 10 | $J_{\text{LQR}} = \int (\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{u}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{u})\, dt$ | Linear Quadratic Regulator cost | scalar |
| 11 | $\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P}\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{P}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{B}^T\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{0}$ | Continuous Algebraic Riccati Eqn. (CARE) | matrix |
| 12 | $\mathbf{u}^* = -\mathbf{K}\mathbf{x} = -\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{B}^T\mathbf{P}\mathbf{x}$ | LQR optimal feedback gain | control |
| 13 | $J_{pen} = J + \mu \sum [\max(0, g_i)]^2$ | Quadratic penalty | scalar |
| 14 | $J_{\text{jerk}} = \int_0^{t_f} \dddot{\mathbf{q}}^T\dddot{\mathbf{q}}\, dt$ | Jerk-minimized trajectory | scalar |
| 15 | $\mathbf{x}(t) = \mathbf{x}_0 + (t-t_0)\dot{\mathbf{x}}_0 + \tfrac{1}{2}(t-t_0)^2 \ddot{\mathbf{x}}_0$ | Cubic polynomial trajectory (boundary) | state |

## 2.8 Real-World Utility of Each Formula Category

- **PMP (Eqs 3–6):** Designing **time-optimal trajectories** for industrial robots (bang-bang torque profiles) and **energy-efficient satellite attitude control**.
- **LQR (Eqs 10–12):** Stabilizing **quadrotors**, **autonomous cars** (lane keeping), and **inverted pendulums** in real time.
- **Penalty / Augmented Lagrangian (Eq 13):** **Motion planning with obstacle avoidance** in ROS MoveIt and **trajectory optimization in Drake**.
- **Gradient/Newton (Eqs 8–9):** **Neural network training** and **inverse kinematics** solving.
- **Jerk minimization (Eq 14):** **CNC machine tools** and **surgical robots** where smooth motion prevents damage.

## 2.9 Worked Concept — LQR End-to-End

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU-favorite LQR problem** for a linear system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}$:
> 1. Choose $\mathbf{Q} \succeq 0$ (state weight) and $\mathbf{R} \succ 0$ (control weight).
> 2. Solve the **Continuous Algebraic Riccati Equation (CARE)**:
> $$\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P}\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{P}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{B}^T\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{0}$$
> 3. Compute feedback gain: $\mathbf{K} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{B}^T\mathbf{P}$.
> 4. Apply control: $\mathbf{u}(t) = -\mathbf{K}\mathbf{x}(t)$.
> 5. Closed-loop dynamics: $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = (\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{B}\mathbf{K})\mathbf{x}$, which is **asymptotically stable** if $(\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B})$ is stabilizable and $(\mathbf{A}, \sqrt{\mathbf{Q}})$ is detectable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Euler-Lagrange Equation from First Principles

**Problem Statement:** Find the curve $x(t)$ connecting two fixed points $(t_0, x_0)$ and $(t_f, x_f)$ that minimizes:

$$J = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} L(x, \dot{x}, t)\, dt$$

### Step 1 — Perturb the Optimal Curve
Let $x^*(t)$ be the optimal solution. Introduce a small smooth variation $\epsilon \eta(t)$ where $\eta(t_0) = \eta(t_f) = 0$ (to keep endpoints fixed):

$$x(t, \epsilon) = x^*(t) + \epsilon \eta(t)$$

### Step 2 — Define the Varied Cost
Substitute into $J$:

$$J(\epsilon) = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} L(x^* + \epsilon\eta, \dot{x}^* + \epsilon\dot{\eta}, t)\, dt$$

### Step 3 — Necessary Condition for a Minimum
Since $x^*$ minimizes $J$, we must have $\left.\dfrac{dJ}{d\epsilon}\right|_{\epsilon=0} = 0$.

$$\frac{dJ}{d\epsilon} = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} \left[ \frac{\partial L}{\partial x}\eta + \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}}\dot{\eta} \right] dt = 0$$

### Step 4 — Integration by Parts on the $\dot{\eta}$ Term

$$\int_{t_0}^{t_f} \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}}\dot{\eta}\, dt = \left[ \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}}\eta \right]_{t_0}^{t_f} - \int_{t_0}^{t_f} \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}}\right) \eta\, dt$$

The boundary term vanishes because $\eta(t_0) = \eta(t_f) = 0$. Therefore:

$$\int_{t_0}^{t_f} \left[ \frac{\partial L}{\partial x} - \frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}} \right] \eta(t)\, dt = 0$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Fundamental Lemma of Calculus of Variations
Since $\eta(t)$ is arbitrary, the bracketed term must be zero everywhere on $[t_0, t_f]$:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} - \frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}} = 0}$$

> This is the **Euler-Lagrange equation**. Any optimal path must satisfy it.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hamilton's Equations for Optimal Control

**Problem Statement:** Derive the three necessary conditions for the Bolza problem using the Hamiltonian.

### Step 1 — Form the Augmented Functional
Augment the cost with the dynamics constraint, weighted by the costate $\boldsymbol{\lambda}(t)$:

$$\tilde{J} = \Phi(\mathbf{x}(t_f), t_f) + \int_{t_0}^{t_f} \left[ L(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}, t) + \boldsymbol{\lambda}^T(f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}, t) - \dot{\mathbf{x}}) \right] dt$$

Define the Hamiltonian $\mathcal{H} \equiv L + \boldsymbol{\lambda}^T f$:

$$\tilde{J} = \Phi(\mathbf{x}(t_f), t_f) + \int_{t_0}^{t_f} \left[ \mathcal{H}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, t) - \boldsymbol{\lambda}^T \dot{\mathbf{x}} \right] dt$$

### Step 2 — First Variation
Take $\delta \tilde{J} = 0$ and integrate by parts the $\boldsymbol{\lambda}^T \dot{\mathbf{x}}$ term:

$$\delta \tilde{J} = \left[ \left(\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial \mathbf{x}} - \boldsymbol{\lambda}(t_f)\right)^T \delta\mathbf{x}(t_f) \right] + \int_{t_0}^{t_f} \left[ \left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{H}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} + \dot{\boldsymbol{\lambda}}\right)^T \delta\mathbf{x} + \left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{H}}{\partial \mathbf{u}}\right)^T \delta\mathbf{u} - \dot{\mathbf{x}}^T\delta\boldsymbol{\lambda} \right] dt = 0$$

### Step 3 — Collect Coefficients
Since $\delta\mathbf{x}$, $\delta\mathbf{u}$, and $\delta\boldsymbol{\lambda}$ are independent and arbitrary:

$$\boxed{\dot{\mathbf{x}}^* = \frac{\partial \mathcal{H}}{\partial \boldsymbol{\lambda}} = f(\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{u}^*, t)}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{\boldsymbol{\lambda}}^* = -\frac{\partial \mathcal{H}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \mathcal{H}}{\partial \mathbf{u}} = \mathbf{0} \quad \text{(for unconstrained } \mathbf{u}\text{)}}$$

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\lambda}(t_f) = \frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\bigg|_{t_f} \quad \text{(transversality condition)}}$$

> These four equations constitute the **complete PMP framework**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: LQR Steady-State Solution via Riccati Equation

**System:** $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}$, $\mathbf{x}(0) = \mathbf{x}_0$.

**Cost:** $J = \int_0^\infty (\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{u}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{u})\, dt$.

### Step 1 — Guess a Quadratic Value Function
Assume the optimal cost-to-go has the form $V(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{P}\mathbf{x}$ for some symmetric $\mathbf{P} \succ 0$.

### Step 2 — Apply the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) Equation
For infinite-horizon LQR, $V$ must satisfy:

$$0 = \min_{\mathbf{u}} \left[ \mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{u}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{u} + \frac{\partial V}{\partial \mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}) \right]$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Minimizer
Substitute $V = \mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{P}\mathbf{x}$, so $\frac{\partial V}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = 2\mathbf{P}\mathbf{x}$. The HJB inner bracket is:

$$\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{u}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{u} + 2\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{P}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u})$$

Take $\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{u}} = 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{u} + 2\mathbf{B}^T\mathbf{P}\mathbf{x} = 0$:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{u}^* = -\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{B}^T\mathbf{P}\mathbf{x} = -\mathbf{K}\mathbf{x}}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Back to Get the Riccati Equation
Plug $\mathbf{u}^*$ into the HJB:

$$0 = \mathbf{x}^T(\mathbf{Q} + \mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P}\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{P}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{B}^T\mathbf{P})\mathbf{x}$$

Since this must hold for all $\mathbf{x}$:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P}\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{P}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{B}^T\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{0}}$$

This is the **Continuous Algebraic Riccati Equation (CARE)**. Solving it gives $\mathbf{P}$, then $\mathbf{K}$, and the closed-loop system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = (\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{B}\mathbf{K})\mathbf{x}$ is guaranteed stable.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Minimum-Jerk Trajectory for a Point-to-Point Move

**Setup:** A robot joint must move from rest at $q(0) = q_0$ to rest at $q(t_f) = q_f$ in time $T$. Minimize:

$$J = \int_0^T \dddot{q}^2\, dt$$

### Step 1 — Write $L$ in Euler-Lagrange Form
Since $L = \dddot{q}^2$, treat $\dddot{q}$ as $\dot{y}$ where $y = \ddot{q}$. We need the higher-order Euler-Lagrange:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial q} - \frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}} + \frac{d^2}{dt^2}\frac{\partial L}{\partial \ddot{q}} - \frac{d^3}{dt^3}\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dddot{q}} = 0$$

### Step 2 — Substitute and Simplify
With $L = \dddot{q}^2$ and $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dddot{q}} = 2\dddot{q}$:

$$0 - 0 + 0 - \frac{d^3}{dt^3}(2\dddot{q}) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \frac{d^3 \dddot{q}}{dt^3} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; q^{(6)} = 0$$

### Step 3 — Integrate Six Times
$$q(t) = c_0 + c_1 t + c_2 t^2 + c_3 t^3 + c_4 t^4 + c_5 t^5$$

### Step 4 — Apply Six Boundary Conditions
- $q(0) = q_0 \Rightarrow c_0 = q_0$
- $\dot{q}(0) = 0 \Rightarrow c_1 = 0$
- $\ddot{q}(0) = 0 \Rightarrow c_2 = 0$
- $q(T) = q_f$
- $\dot{q}(T) = 0$
- $\ddot{q}(T) = 0$

Solving the resulting linear system gives the **quintic polynomial trajectory** widely used in industrial robots.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Optimizer

| Problem Trait | Recommended Method | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Linear system, quadratic cost | **LQR** | Closed-form via Riccati |
| Linear system, bounded input | **MPC with QP** | Convex, real-time |
| Nonlinear, single minimum | **Newton / SQP** | Quadratic convergence |
| Nonlinear, many local minima | **GA / PSO** | Global search |
| Obstacle avoidance, no analytical gradient | **Penalty / Augmented Lagrangian** | Robust constraint handling |
| High-dimensional, real-time | **iLQR / DDP** | Iterative LQR scales to $n \gg 10$ |
| Discrete decisions (e.g., waypoint order) | **Dynamic Programming, Branch & Bound** | Combinatorial |
| Path in known grid | **A\*, Dijkstra (graph opt.)** | Discrete shortest path |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Optimization Pipeline in a Robotic System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task Specification: Start, Goal, Cost] --> B[Path Planner: Geometric Path]
    B --> C[Parameter Selection: Waypoints, Time Intervals]
    C --> D[Cost Function Definition: Time, Energy, Jerk]
    D --> E[Constraint Encoding: Joint Limits, Obstacles, Torques]
    E --> F{Solver Selection}
    F -->|Linear| G1[LQR / Riccati]
    F -->|Nonlinear Smooth| G2[Newton / SQP / iLQR]
    F -->|Non-convex| G3[GA / PSO / Simulated Annealing]
    F -->|Real-time| G4[MPC / Receding Horizon]
    G1 --> H[Optimal Trajectory q t, u t]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I[Controller: Feedback Tracking]
    I --> J[Robot Actuators]
    J --> K[Sensor Feedback]
    K --> I
```

## 4.2 Pontryagin's Minimum Principle (PMP) Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    State[State Equation: x dot equals f of x, u, t] -->|integrate| StateTraj[x of t]
    Costate[Costate Equation: lambda dot equals negative partial H over partial x] -->|integrate| CostateTraj[lambda of t]
    Hamiltonian[Hamiltonian: H equals L plus lambda transpose f] --> Station[Stationarity: partial H over partial u equals 0 OR argmin over U]
    StateTraj --> Hamiltonian
    CostateTraj --> Hamiltonian
    Hamiltonian --> State
    Hamiltonian --> Costate
    Station -->|optimal u of t| Control[u star of t]
    Control --> State
```

## 4.3 Classification Tree of Robot Motion Optimization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Robot Motion Optimization] --> K[Kinematic Only]
    Root --> D[Dynamic - Includes Inertia, Torque]
    K --> K1[Shortest Path: Geometric]
    K --> K2[Smoothest Path: Min Curvature]
    K --> K3[Minimum Time: Velocity-limited]
    D --> D1[Energy Optimal: u transpose R u]
    D --> D2[Time Optimal: Bang-Bang via PMP]
    D --> D3[Jerk Optimal: Smooth for wear]
    D --> D4[Hybrid: Weighted sum]
    D1 --> D4
    D2 --> D4
    D3 --> D4
```

## 4.4 Iterative LQR (iLQR) Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Init[Initial Guess: nominal x-bar, u-bar] --> Lin[Linearize dynamics around nominal]
    Lin --> QP[Backward pass: Solve Riccati for K, k]
    QP --> Fwd[Forward pass: Roll out new x, u with line search]
    Fwd --> Cost{J of new trajectory}
    Init --> Cost
    Cost -->|decrease| Accept[Accept new trajectory as nominal]
    Accept --> Lin
    Cost -->|no decrease| Shrink[Reduce step size, retry]
    Shrink --> Fwd
```

## 4.5 Constrained Optimization via Augmented Lagrangian

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Constrained Problem] --> B[Form Augmented Lagrangian L_A]
    B --> C[Solve unconstrained subproblem]
    C --> D{Constraints Satisfied?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Optimal Solution Found]
    D -->|No| F[Update Lagrange multipliers lambda]
    F --> G[Increase penalty mu]
    G --> C
```

## 4.6 Convergence Behavior Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Iter1[Iteration History]
        direction LR
        S0[Start: x0] --> S1[Step 1: x1]
        S1 --> S2[Step 2: x2]
        S2 --> S3[Step k: x star]
    end
    S0 -.->|large step gradient| Note1[Linear convergence]
    S1 -.->|smaller step Newton| Note2[Quadratic convergence]
    S2 -.->|oscillation possible| Note3[GA / PSO: stochastic, slower but global]
```
