The MPF uses $N_s = 1000$ particles and after one update step the normalised weights are $\{0.42,\, 0.31,\, 0.15,\, 0.08,\, 0.04\}$ (rest negligible). Compute $N_{eff}$ and comment on whether resampling is required. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 (1 mark)** Draw the block diagram (see SECTION 4.1 above) showing INS prediction → proposal construction → weight update → resampling.

**Step 2 (2 marks)** Write the general importance sampling identity:
$$ \mathbb{E}_{p(\cdot)}[g(\mathbf{x})] \approx \frac{1}{N_s} \sum_i w^{(i)} g(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) $$

**Step 3 (2 marks)** Expand the recursive posterior:
$$ p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k}\mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{z}_k\mid\mathbf{x}_k)\,p(\mathbf{x}_k\mid\mathbf{x}_{k-1})}{p(\mathbf{z}_k\mid\mathbf{z}_{1:k-1})} \, p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k-1}\mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1}) $$

**Step 4 (2 marks)** Form the ratio with proposal $q(\mathbf{x}_k\mid\mathbf{x}_{k-1},\mathbf{z}_k)$ to obtain:
$$ w_k^{(i)} = w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot \frac{p(\mathbf{z}_k\mid\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}) \, p(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}\mid\mathbf{x}_{k-1}^{(i)})}{q(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}\mid\mathbf{x}_{k-1}^{(i)},\mathbf{z}_k)} $$

This is the **Modified Importance Weight** — the heart of MPF. **(7 marks total)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Given weights $\{0.42,\, 0.31,\, 0.15,\, 0.08,\, 0.04\}$ and $N_s=1000$.

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Sum of squared normalised weights:

$$ \sum_i (\tilde{w}^{(i)})^2 = (0.42)^2 + (0.31)^2 + (0.15)^2 + (0.08)^2 + (0.04)^2 $$

$$
= 0.1764 + 0.0961 + 0.0225 + 0.0064 + 0.0016 = 0.3030
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute $N_{eff}$:

$$ N_{eff} = \frac{1}{0.3030} \approx 3.30 $$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Threshold: $N_{thr} = N_s/2 = 500$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Since $N_{eff} = 3.30 \ll 500$, **resampling is mandatory**; in fact, the degeneracy is catastrophic (only ~3 effective particles). The MPF will perform systematic resampling and regularisation to restore diversity.

**(7 marks total)**

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the Rao-Blackwellised Particle Filter (RBPF) variant of MPF and justify why it is particularly suitable for GNSS/INS fusion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a 30-second GNSS outage in an urban canyon, an autonomous car uses an MPF with 2000 particles to bridge the gap. The INS-only drift after 30 s has RMSE of 18 m. The MPF reduces this to 6.5 m. Calculate the **(i)** percentage reduction in RMSE and **(ii)** the ratio of effective sample size if the pre-outage and post-outage $N_{eff}$ values are $1850$ and $470$ respectively. Comment on the post-outage ESS. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** In RBPF, the state $\mathbf{x}$ is partitioned as $\mathbf{x}=[\mathbf{x}^l,\ \mathbf{x}^n]^T$ where $\mathbf{x}^l$ is *linear-Gaussian* conditioned on $\mathbf{x}^n$. The linear part is marginalised analytically using a Kalman filter per particle; the non-linear part is sampled.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** For GNSS/INS, the position/velocity sub-state given an attitude is conditionally linear-Gaussian; hence it is RBPF-eligible, while attitude (non-linear) is sampled.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Benefit: the dimensionality of sampling is reduced from $n$ to $n-n_l$, drastically lowering the number of particles needed and reducing variance.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Hence RBPF is the **de-facto modified PF** for tightly-coupled GNSS/INS in production systems (e.g., NovAtel SPAN, OxTS RT3000). **(7 marks total)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Percentage reduction in RMSE (3 marks):**

$$ \%\text{ reduction} = \frac{18 - 6.5}{18} \times 100 = \frac{11.5}{18}\times 100 \approx 63.89\% $$

**(ii) Ratio and comment (4 marks):**

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{470}{1850} = 0.254
$$

i.e., $N_{eff}$ drops to **25.4 %** of its pre-outage value. Given threshold $N_{thr} = 1000$, the post-outage ESS ($470$) is **below threshold** → resampling will fire on the next GNSS reacquisition, restoring diversity. The MPF robustness is evident because, despite the long 30-s outage, the RMSE improvement of **63.89 %** confirms the proposal's INS-based forecast kept particles in the true region. **(7 marks total)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Losers):**
> 1. **Writing $|x|$** in the formula sheet — the vertical pipe breaks the markdown table parser and *costs 0.5 mark* on the answer sheet equivalent. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\lvert x \rvert`.
> 2. Forgetting to **normalise** weights before computing $N_{eff}$ — a frequent 2-mark loss.
> 3. Confusing *proposal density* with *likelihood* in the importance-weight derivation.
> 4. Skipping the **Markov assumption** step in derivation questions — examiners deduct 1 mark if it's omitted.
> 5. In numericals, *not stating the resampling threshold explicitly* (always write $N_{thr} = N_s/2$).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MPF ≠ BPF:** MPF modifies the *proposal density* using the current GNSS measurement (typically via a local EKF), whereas BPF uses only the prior.
- **Importance weight (MPF)** carries the ratio $\dfrac{p(\mathbf{z}_k|\mathbf{x}_k)\,p(\mathbf{x}_k|\mathbf{x}_{k-1})}{q(\mathbf{x}_k|\mathbf{x}_{k-1},\mathbf{z}_k)}$.
- **Optimal proposal** is the *true posterior conditional* $p(\mathbf{x}_k|\mathbf{x}_{k-1},\mathbf{z}_k)$ — approximated by EKF in practice.
- **Resampling rule:** trigger when $N_{eff} < N_s/2$; use **systematic resampling** for low-variance O($N_s$) operation.
- **Regularisation** (kernel jitter) prevents sample impoverishment after resampling.
- **Rao-Blackwellisation** analytically integrates linear-Gaussian sub-states (e.g., position given attitude), saving particles.
- **Pseudo-range model:** $\rho^{(s)} = \Vert \mathbf{r}_r - \mathbf{r}^{(s)}\Vert + c\,\delta t_r + I + T + \varepsilon$; the **3 × 10⁸ m/s** speed of light must appear in any derivation.
- **Strapdown Coriolis term** $-(2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n)\times \mathbf{v}^n$ is essential during prediction.
- **GNSS outage resilience** is the chief KTU-motivating scenario — MPF outperforms EKF/UKF because it tolerates non-Gaussian multipath and intermittent measurements.
- **Computational rule of thumb:** MPF needs **3–5× fewer particles** than BPF for equal accuracy.
- **Always** normalise weights, **always** state $N_{thr}$, **never** confuse proposal and likelihood.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimating Velocity by Combining GNSS and INS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Estimating Velocity by Combining GNSS and INS # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **GNSS/INS Integration for Velocity Estimation** is a multi-sensor data fusion technique that combines the absolute, long-term stable position and velocity measurements from a **Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)** receiver with the high-bandwidth, self-contained, short-term precise motion measurements of an **Inertial Navigation System (INS)**. The fusion is performed recursively using a **Kalman Filter (KF)** or **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**, which optimally estimates the vehicle's **Position, Velocity, and Attitude (PVA)** — collectively called the *navigation state* — by exploiting the complementary error characteristics of both subsystems. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, this topic falls under *Sensor Fusion in Mobile Robotics and Autonomous Systems*, where the robot must estimate its own velocity reliably despite GNSS signal loss (urban canyons, tunnels, indoor) and INS drift (bias, scale factor, random walk). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The integration architecture (loosely, tightly, ultra-tightly coupled), the Kalman Filter state vector, the INS mechanization equations, and the measurement update step are the *high-yield* concepts that fetch marks in Module 4. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a blindfolded passenger in a car trying to estimate the car's speed: - **GNSS is like asking a stranger on the road every 5 seconds, "Where am I?"** — The answers are fairly accurate (within ~3 m), but the stranger only replies occasionally, and he goes silent inside tunnels. - **INS is like feeling the acceleration with your inner ear (the vestibular system) and timing it with a stopwatch.** — You can compute velocity every millisecond, but tiny errors compound: a 0.1 % bias in acceleration integrated over 60 seconds yields a 3 m/s velocity error! > [!NOTE] > **The Core Idea:** Use INS for *smooth, high-rate* velocity and GNSS for *drift correction*. The Kalman Filter acts like a "judge" that weighs both opinions, giving more credit to whichever sensor is currently more reliable. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Metric | Typical Value | |---|---| | **GNSS Position Accuracy (Single Point)** | **2.5 m CEP** (Circular Error Probable) | | **GNSS Velocity Accuracy** | **0.05 m/s RMS** | | **GNSS Update Rate** | **1–10 Hz** (typical: 1 Hz) | | **INS Update Rate** | **50–1000 Hz** (MEMS-grade) | | **Accelerometer Bias (MEMS)** | **10⁻³ to 10⁻¹ m/s²** | | **Gyroscope Bias (MEMS)** | **1 to 100 °/h** | | **Speed of Light (c)** | **2.998 × 10⁸ m/s** | | **WGS-84 Semi-major Axis (a)** | **6,378,137 m** | | **WGS-84 Earth Rotation Rate (ωₑ)** | **7.292115 × 10⁻⁵ rad/s** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Combine GNSS and INS? Each sensor alone fails in a unique way: - **GNSS limitations:** Signal blockage (tunnels, urban canyons, forests), multipath errors, intentional jamming/spoofing, low update rate (1 Hz), and poor attitude observability. - **INS limitations:** Accelerometer and gyroscope biases cause **drift** — velocity error grows linearly with time, and position error grows quadratically ($\propto t^2$). A tightly integrated system leverages: 1. **Complementary spectra:** INS errors are *low-frequency*, GNSS errors are *high-frequency* (white noise). 2. **Different failure modes:** They are statistically independent, so fusion dramatically reduces total variance. 3. **Bridging GNSS gaps:** INS "coasts" through outages while the Kalman Filter propagates the error covariance. ## 2.2 INS Mechanization — The Heart of Velocity Estimation The INS computes velocity by **integrating the specific force** $\mathbf{f}^b$ measured in the body frame, transformed to the navigation frame using the **Direction Cosine Matrix (DCM)** $\mathbf{C}_b^n$, and corrected for gravity and the Coriolis effect: $$ \dot{\mathbf{v}}^n = \mathbf{C}_b^n \, \mathbf{f}^b - (2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \mathbf{v}^n + \mathbf{g}^n $$ Where each term has a precise meaning: - $\mathbf{v}^n$ — Velocity vector in the navigation (local-level) frame: $\mathbf{v}^n = [v_N, v_E, v_D]^T$. - $\mathbf{C}_b^n$ — Direction Cosine Matrix mapping body-to-navigation frame (computed from roll, pitch, yaw). - $\mathbf{f}^b$ — Specific force vector measured by the accelerometers: $\mathbf{f}^b = [f_x, f_y, f_z]^T$. - $2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n \times \mathbf{v}^n$ — **Coriolis acceleration** due to Earth rotation ($\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}$ = Earth rotation rate vector). - $\boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n \times \mathbf{v}^n$ — Transport rate (motion of the navigation frame over the curved Earth). - $\mathbf{g}^n$ — Local gravity vector including the centripetal correction. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The accelerometer does **not** measure true acceleration — it measures *specific force* (non-gravitational acceleration). Gravity must be removed analytically, which is why the mechanization equation above is so critical. ## 2.3 The Kalman Filter State Vector The full error-state Kalman Filter tracks **15 to 17 states**: $$ \mathbf{x} = [\delta \mathbf{r}^n, \; \delta \mathbf{v}^n, \; \boldsymbol{\psi}^n, \; \mathbf{b}_a, \; \mathbf{b}_g]^T $$ | Symbol | Meaning | Dimension | |---|---|---| | $\delta \mathbf{r}^n$ | Position error | 3 | | $\delta \mathbf{v}^n$ | Velocity error | 3 | | $\boldsymbol{\psi}^n$ | Attitude (tilt) error | 3 | | $\mathbf{b}_a$ | Accelerometer bias | 3 | | $\mathbf{b}_g$ | Gyroscope bias | 3 | ## 2.4 Process Model (Prediction Step) The linearized error dynamics matrix $\mathbf{F}$ governs state propagation: $$ \dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{G} \mathbf{w} $$ Where $\mathbf{w} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{Q})$ is the process noise (IMU sensor noise), and $\mathbf{Q}$ is built from the **Power Spectral Densities (PSDs)** of the accelerometers and gyroscopes. The covariance update is: $$ \mathbf{P}_{k|k-1} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}_{k-1} \, \mathbf{P}_{k-1|k-1} \, \boldsymbol{\Phi}_{k-1}^T + \mathbf{Q}_{k-1} $$ ## 2.5 Measurement Model (Update Step) GNSS provides a position/velocity measurement $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{H} \mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\nu}$ with $\boldsymbol{\nu} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{R})$. The Kalman gain is: $$ \mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k|k-1} \mathbf{H}^T (\mathbf{H} \mathbf{P}_{k|k-1} \mathbf{H}^T + \mathbf{R})^{-1} $$ And the state is corrected as: $$ \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k (\mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H} \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1}) $$ ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\dot{\mathbf{v}}^n = \mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{f}^b - (2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \mathbf{v}^n + \mathbf{g}^n$ | INS velocity mechanization | | 2 | $\mathbf{P}_{k \vert k-1} = \boldsymbol{\Phi} \mathbf{P}_{k-1 \vert k-1} \boldsymbol{\Phi}^T + \mathbf{Q}_{k-1}$ | Covariance prediction | | 3 | $\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k \vert k-1} \mathbf{H}^T (\mathbf{H} \mathbf{P}_{k \vert k-1} \mathbf{H}^T + \mathbf{R})^{-1}$ | Kalman gain | | 4 | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k (\mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H} \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k-1})$ | State update | | 5 | $\mathbf{P}_{k \vert k} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}) \mathbf{P}_{k \vert k-1}$ | Covariance update (Joseph form preferred) | | 6 | $\dot{\boldsymbol{\phi}} = \boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n$ | Wander-azimuth / Schuler rate | | 7 | $\boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n = \left[ \frac{v_E}{R_E + h}, \; \frac{-v_N}{R_N + h}, \; \frac{-v_E \tan\phi}{R_E + h} \right]^T$ | Transport rate components | | 8 | $\mathbf{Q}_d \approx \mathbf{G} \, \mathbf{Q}_c \, \mathbf{G}^T \, \Delta t$ | Discrete process noise | | 9 | $\text{DRMS} = \sqrt{\sigma_E^2 + \sigma_N^2}$ | Distance RMS position error | | 10 | $\mathbf{f}^b_{\text{meas}} = \mathbf{a}^b - \mathbf{g}^b$ | Accelerometer output relationship | > [!TIP] > **Real-world Utility:** Autonomous vehicles (Waymo, Mobileye), UAVs (DJI drones), maritime navigation, guided missiles, and planetary rovers (NASA Mars Helicopter *Ingenuity*) all rely on GNSS/INS fusion. The estimator runs at 100–1000 Hz, providing *robust* velocity even at 100 km/h through tunnels. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: INS Velocity Update in Discrete Time The continuous mechanization equation is integrated using a simple Euler step (for clarity) or higher-order Runge–Kutta. Starting from: $$ \dot{\mathbf{v}}^n = \mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{f}^b - (2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \mathbf{v}^n + \mathbf{g}^n $$ **Step 1 — Define the total effective rotation rate vector:** $$ \boldsymbol{\omega}_{in}^n = \boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n $$ The term $(2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \mathbf{v}^n$ represents the Coriolis and transport-rate correction. Substituting: $$ \dot{\mathbf{v}}^n = \mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{f}^b - \boldsymbol{\omega}_{in}^n \times \mathbf{v}^n - \boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n \times \mathbf{v}^n + \mathbf{g}^n $$ **Step 2 — Discretize for digital implementation:** Let $\Delta t$ be the IMU sampling period. The discrete update is: $$ \mathbf{v}^n_{k+1} = \mathbf{v}^n_k + \left[ \mathbf{C}_{b,k}^n \mathbf{f}^b_k - (2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en,k}^n) \times \mathbf{v}^n_k + \mathbf{g}^n \right] \Delta t $$ **Step 3 — Introduce the skew-symmetric matrix for compactness:** For any vector $\mathbf{a} = [a_1, a_2, a_3]^T$, define: $$ [\mathbf{a} \times] = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -a_3 & a_2 \\ a_3 & 0 & -a_1 \\ -a_2 & a_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ So the update becomes: $$ \mathbf{v}^n_{k+1} = \mathbf{v}^n_k + \left[ \mathbf{C}_{b,k}^n \mathbf{f}^b_k - [2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en,k}^n]_\times \mathbf{v}^n_k + \mathbf{g}^n \right] \Delta t $$ **Step 4 — Apply in the n-frame with explicit components:** $$ \begin{aligned} v_{N,k+1} &= v_{N,k} + \left[ f_N - (2\omega_e \sin\phi + \tfrac{v_E \tan\phi}{R_E+h})\, v_E + (2\omega_e \cos\phi + \tfrac{v_E}{R_E+h})\, v_D \right] \Delta t \\ v_{E,k+1} &= v_{E,k} + \left[ f_E + (2\omega_e \sin\phi + \tfrac{v_E \tan\phi}{R_E+h})\, v_N - \tfrac{v_N v_D}{R_N+h} \right] \Delta t \\ v_{D,k+1} &= v_{D,k} + \left[ f_D - (2\omega_e \cos\phi + \tfrac{v_E}{R_E+h})\, v_N + \tfrac{v_N^2}{R_N+h} + \tfrac{v_E^2}{R_E+h} - g \right] \Delta t \end{aligned} $$ This is the **fundamental velocity update loop** of any INS algorithm. The $\phi$ term is the geodetic latitude, $R_N$ and $R_E$ are the meridian and prime-vertical radii of curvature. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Linearized Velocity Error Dynamics Define velocity error $\delta \mathbf{v}^n = \hat{\mathbf{v}}^n - \mathbf{v}^n$ (estimated minus true). Linearize the mechanization equation around the navigation state. The dominant error terms are: 1. **Tilt-induced specific-force error:** $-\boldsymbol{\psi}^n \times \mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{f}^b$ (a 1° tilt error projects into a $\sim 0.17 \text{ m/s}^2$ acceleration error). 2. **Coriolis error:** $-(2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \delta \mathbf{v}^n$. 3. **Accelerometer bias:** $+\mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{b}_a$. Combining these: $$ \delta \dot{\mathbf{v}}^n = -\boldsymbol{\psi}^n \times \hat{\mathbf{f}}^n - (2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \delta \mathbf{v}^n + \mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{b}_a $$ In matrix form, the velocity error row of $\mathbf{F}$ is: $$ \mathbf{F}_{vv} = -[2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n]_\times, \quad \mathbf{F}_{v\psi} = -[\hat{\mathbf{f}}^n]_\times, \quad \mathbf{F}_{vb_a} = \mathbf{C}_b^n $$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3: GNSS Velocity from Doppler Shift GNSS receivers compute velocity from the **Doppler shift** of the carrier frequency. The pseudorange rate is: $$ \dot{\rho}^{(i)} = (\mathbf{u}^{(i)})^T (\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{v}^{(i)}) + c \, \dot{b} + \dot{\epsilon} $$ Where $\mathbf{u}^{(i)}$ is the line-of-sight unit vector to satellite $i$, $\mathbf{v}^{(i)}$ is the satellite's velocity, $\dot{b}$ is the receiver clock drift, and $\dot{\epsilon}$ is noise. Stacking $N \geq 4$ such equations: $$ \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\rho}^{(1)} \\ \dot{\rho}^{(2)} \\ \vdots \\ \dot{\rho}^{(N)} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (\mathbf{u}^{(1)})^T & 1 \\ (\mathbf{u}^{(2)})^T & 1 \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ (\mathbf{u}^{(N)})^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{v} \\ c\dot{b} \end{bmatrix} $$ Solving via weighted least squares (since $\mathbf{R}$ is the carrier-phase Doppler variance) yields the **GNSS velocity measurement** $\mathbf{z}_{v,GNSS}$, which is then used as $\mathbf{z}$ in the Kalman update. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Doppler-based velocity?** Unlike position (trilateration), Doppler velocity is **instantaneous** — it does not require differentiating two consecutive positions, which would amplify noise. This is why high-grade GNSS receivers report velocity with $\sigma_v \approx 0.05 \text{ m/s}$. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Coupled vs. Decoupled Coupling | Architecture | Measurement Input to KF | Pros | Cons | |---|---|---|---| | **Loosely Coupled** | GNSS position & velocity (N ≥ 4 sats required) | Simple, robust, easy to retrofit | Loses sats < 4, no raw-data benefit | | **Tightly Coupled** | Raw pseudorange & pseudorange-rate | Works with N = 1 sat, more accurate | More complex, requires GNSS board access | | **Ultra-Tightly / Deep** | INS aids GNSS tracking loops | Best jamming resistance, urban robustness | Requires firmware-level integration | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Loosely Coupled GNSS/INS Integration ```mermaid flowchart LR A[IMU Accel and Gyro at 200 Hz] --> B[INS Mechanization Module] B --> C[Predicted Nav State
pos, vel, attitude] C --> D[Error State Kalman Filter
15 state vector] E[GNSS Receiver at 1 Hz
pos and vel from Doppler] --> F[Measurement Vector z] F --> D D --> G[Corrected Nav State
pos, vel, attitude] G --> H[Feedback to INS
bias correction delta b a, delta b g] H --> B D --> I[Output to Robot Controller
velocity and heading] subgraph ProcessNoise J[Q matrix from IMU PSDs] --> D end subgraph MeasNoise K[R matrix from GNSS variance] --> D end ``` ## 4.2 Architecture Comparison Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph LooselyCoupled[Loosely Coupled] L1[GNSS Receiver] --> L2[GNSS Solver
pos vel] L2 --> L3[Kalman Filter] I1[IMU] --> I2[INS Mechanization] I2 --> L3 L3 --> O1[Nav Solution] end subgraph TightlyCoupled[Tightly Coupled] T1[GNSS Raw Pseudorange] --> T2[Vectorized Meas] I3[IMU] --> I3a[INS Mechanization] I3a --> T2 T2 --> T3[Kalman Filter] T3 --> O2[Nav Solution] end ``` ## 4.3 Velocity Error Budget — Drift vs. Correction ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Time t equals 0] --> B[INS only error grows quadratically] A --> C[GNSS error is bounded white noise] B --> D[Fused error grows slowly then saturates] C --> D D --> E[Velocity RMSE at 60 s
INS only 50 m/s vs Fused 0.3 m/s] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Schematic Interpretation:** This block diagram illustrates the *closed-loop feedback* nature of the loosely coupled architecture — the Kalman Filter's velocity-error estimate $\delta \mathbf{v}^n$ is fed back to remove residual drift in the INS state, ensuring bounded long-term error. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] List three advantages of integrating GNSS with INS over using either system alone. **Model Answer:** 1. **Complementary error spectra:** INS errors are low-frequency (biases, drift), while GNSS errors are high-frequency (white noise, multipath). Fusion cancels both. 2. **Bridging GNSS outages:** When fewer than 4 satellites are visible (tunnels, urban canyons), INS "coasts" the solution, while the Kalman Filter propagates the covariance. 3. **High update rate with bounded error:** INS provides 100–1000 Hz motion data, while GNSS prevents long-term drift — yielding smooth, accurate velocity for control loops. 4. (Bonus) **Improved attitude observability:** Even single-antenna GNSS benefits from INS-derived lever-arm compensation. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] What is the role of the Kalman Filter in GNSS/INS integration? **Model Answer:** The Kalman Filter is the **optimal recursive estimator** that fuses INS predictions with GNSS measurements by: - **Prediction step:** Propagating state $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ and covariance $\mathbf{P}$ using the INS mechanization and IMU noise model ($\mathbf{Q}$ matrix). - **Update step:** Correcting the predicted state using the GNSS measurement $\mathbf{z}$ weighted by the Kalman gain $\mathbf{K}$, which balances the relative trust (covariance) in prediction vs. measurement ($\mathbf{R}$). - The minimum-variance estimate it produces is *unbiased* and has *minimum mean-square error* under Gaussian assumptions. ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024] #### (a) Derive the INS velocity mechanization equation in the navigation frame. State the meaning of every term. (7 marks) **Step 1 — Start with Newton's second law in a rotating frame:** The true acceleration of the vehicle in the Earth-Centered Earth-Fixed (ECEF) frame is: $$ \mathbf{a}_i = \mathbf{C}_n^e \dot{\mathbf{v}}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en} \times \mathbf{C}_n^e \mathbf{v}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie} \times \mathbf{C}_n^e \mathbf{v}^n $$ **Step 2 — Account for the specific-force measurement:** The accelerometer measures specific force, which excludes gravity: $$ \mathbf{f}^n = \mathbf{a}_i - \mathbf{g}^n $$ **Step 3 — Substitute and solve for $\dot{\mathbf{v}}^n$:** Multiplying out and isolating: $$ \dot{\mathbf{v}}^n = \mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{f}^b - (2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \mathbf{v}^n + \mathbf{g}^n $$ **Term meanings:** - $\mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{f}^b$: Specific force resolved into the navigation frame. - $2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n \times \mathbf{v}^n$: Coriolis acceleration. - $\boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n \times \mathbf{v}^n$: Transport rate (curvature correction). - $\mathbf{g}^n$: Local gravity vector. #### (b) Explain the loosely coupled GNSS/INS integration architecture with a block diagram. Discuss its limitations. (7 marks) **Architecture Description:** In loosely coupled integration, the GNSS receiver operates as a **black box** that outputs position and velocity at 1 Hz, which the central Kalman Filter fuses with INS predictions at 100–200 Hz. The filter maintains a 15-state error vector: position error (3), velocity error (3), attitude error (3), accelerometer bias (3), and gyroscope bias (3). **Process Model:** INS propagates the state; IMU noise drives the $\mathbf{Q}$ matrix. **Measurement Model:** The difference $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{z}_{GNSS} - \mathbf{z}_{INS}$ (position/velocity innovation) is the input, with $\mathbf{H} = [\mathbf{I}_6 \; \mathbf{0}]$ mapping errors to measurements. **Feedback:** The estimated biases $\mathbf{b}_a, \mathbf{b}_g$ are fed back to the INS to correct its mechanization. **Limitations:** 1. **Requires ≥ 4 satellites:** When fewer are visible, GNSS solution becomes invalid; the system must fall back to pure INS (drift accumulates). 2. **Information loss:** Raw pseudorange/Doppler data is discarded, eliminating the ability to detect and reject individual satellite faults. 3. **Correlated measurement noise:** GNSS position/velocity errors are temporally correlated (multipath, SA residual), violating KF white-noise assumptions. ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] *Alternative choice* #### (a) With necessary derivations, show how the Kalman Filter predicts and updates the velocity error state during GNSS/INS fusion. (7 marks) **Prediction Step (between GNSS updates):** Given state estimate $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1\vert k-1}$ and covariance $\mathbf{P}_{k-1\vert k-1}$, integrate the IMU: $$ \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k-1} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}_{k-1} \, \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1 \vert k-1} $$ $$ \mathbf{P}_{k \vert k-1} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}_{k-1} \, \mathbf{P}_{k-1 \vert k-1} \, \boldsymbol{\Phi}_{k-1}^T + \mathbf{Q}_{k-1} $$ The velocity error row of $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ (to first order) is: $$ \boldsymbol{\Phi}_{vv}(\Delta t) \approx \mathbf{I} - [2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n]_\times \Delta t $$ **Update Step (when GNSS arrives):** Innovation: $\boldsymbol{\nu}_k = \mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H} \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k-1}$ Kalman gain: $\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k \vert k-1} \mathbf{H}^T (\mathbf{H} \mathbf{P}_{k \vert k-1} \mathbf{H}^T + \mathbf{R})^{-1}$ State update: $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k \boldsymbol{\nu}_k$ Covariance update: $\mathbf{P}_{k \vert k} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}) \mathbf{P}_{k \vert k-1}$ The velocity error estimate $\delta \hat{\mathbf{v}}^n$ is the most critical component — it directly removes accumulated drift from the INS velocity channel. #### (b) Compare tightly coupled and loosely coupled integration. When would you prefer a tightly coupled system for a robot navigating an urban environment? (7 marks) **Comparison:** | Aspect | Loosely Coupled | Tightly Coupled | |---|---|---| | Input to KF | Position, velocity | Raw pseudorange, pseudorange rate | | Minimum sats | 4 (GNSS solution) | 1 (still updates KF) | | Outage robustness | Pure INS drift | Degraded but functional | | Complexity | Low | High | | Fault detection | Limited | Per-satellite possible | **Preference in urban environments:** Tightly coupled integration is strongly preferred because: 1. **Urban canyons cause frequent N < 4 satellite visibility.** Tightly coupled systems can still update the KF using even 1–2 pseudoranges, whereas loosely coupled systems fall back to pure INS. 2. **Multipath-induced outliers** in individual satellites can be identified and excluded through innovation monitoring at the pseudorange level. 3. **Tighter covariance tracking** yields smaller position/velocity errors during partial outages (typical of city driving). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - Students often forget to include the **Coriolis term** $2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n \times \mathbf{v}^n$ in the mechanization equation — losing up to **2 marks**. > - Confusing **specific force** $\mathbf{f}^b$ (what the accelerometer measures) with true acceleration is a *classic* KTU mistake. The accelerometer does **not** measure gravity. > - In the Kalman Filter, the **innovation sequence** $\boldsymbol{\nu}_k$ must be **zero-mean and white**; if not, your $\mathbf{Q}$ or $\mathbf{R}$ matrices are mismodeled. Examiners check for this awareness. > - The state vector order is critical: keep the **same ordering** in $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{F}, \mathbf{H}$ or matrix dimensions will be wrong. Most KTU answer scripts lose marks here. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **GNSS gives bounded but noisy, low-rate (1 Hz) position/velocity via Doppler.** - ✅ **INS gives smooth, high-rate (100–1000 Hz) velocity by integrating specific force, but drifts.** - ✅ **The Kalman Filter is the optimal fusion engine** — it minimizes the trace of the error covariance matrix. - ✅ **The INS mechanization equation** must include the Coriolis term, transport rate, and gravity removal. - ✅ **The 15-state error-state KF** is industry standard: 3 position, 3 velocity, 3 attitude, 3 accel bias, 3 gyro bias. - ✅ **Loosely coupled** is simple but needs N ≥ 4 sats; **Tightly coupled** is robust in urban environments. - ✅ **GNSS velocity comes from Doppler shift**, not from differentiating positions (which would amplify noise). - ✅ **Process noise Q** is built from IMU PSDs (accel & gyro ARW / bias instability). - ✅ **Measurement noise R** comes from satellite elevation, CN0, and Dilution of Precision (DOP). - ✅ **Feedback loop**: estimated biases are fed back to INS to keep its mechanization error-free. - ✅ **Velocity error in pure INS** grows $\propto t$ (linear drift); **position error** grows $\propto t^2$ (quadratic). - ✅ **GNSS-only velocity accuracy is ~0.05 m/s RMS**; combined with INS, effective accuracy improves by an order of magnitude under good geometry. - ✅ **In ECEF vs. ECI vs. navigation frame**, ensure all vectors are in the *same frame* before subtraction. - ✅ **Schuler period of 84.4 minutes** is the fundamental oscillation period of an undamped INS — a healthy KF keeps the error bounded at this timescale.

topics.title: A-GPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** A-GPS # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of A-GPS ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Assisted GPS (A-GPS)** is a positioning enhancement technique used in robot motion planning and mobile robotics that augments the standard Global Positioning System (GPS) by incorporating external assistance from a network server (cellular, Wi-Fi, or internet-based). The primary objective of A-GPS is to **drastically reduce the Time-To-First-Fix (TTFF)** and **improve sensitivity**, especially in challenging signal conditions such as urban canyons, indoor environments, or forested areas where robotic navigation is commonly required. In the context of **robot motion planning**, A-GPS serves as a localization layer that provides coarse-to-medium accuracy global pose estimates to the robot, which are then fused (often via Kalman or Particle Filters) with other sensor modalities like LiDAR, IMU, and wheel odometry to construct a reliable belief state for path planning algorithms such as A*, RRT*, or Dynamic Window Approach (DWA). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A-GPS is treated under *Module 4: Advanced Localization and Sensing Techniques* as a hybrid localization strategy that bridges the gap between absolute (satellite-based) and relative (dead-reckoning) positioning, forming a critical input to the motion planning pipeline. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a tourist arriving in an unfamiliar city for the first time: - **Standard GPS** is like a person who has a map but has never opened it. They must slowly unfold it, orient it to North, find their location, and then plan a route. This is slow, especially when the sky is "cloudy" (poor signal). - **A-GPS** is like a tourist who has a local guide friend call them on the phone *before* they start walking. The friend immediately tells them: *"You are near the 3rd Avenue Subway, here is the current traffic on each road, and here is your destination's coordinates."* The tourist's device boots up with everything pre-loaded. For a **robot**, this translates to: - The robot does not need to download the slow satellite **almanac** (a coarse orbital database valid for several months) and **ephemeris** (precise orbital parameters valid for ~4 hours) directly from weak satellite signals. - Instead, a network reference server (assistance server) supplies these parameters over a high-bandwidth wireless link. - The receiver only needs to measure **pseudoranges** to compute the fix. ## 1.3 Key Terminology | Term | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | | **TTFF** | Time To First Fix — duration from cold start to a valid position estimate. | | **Almanac** | Coarse orbital data for *all* GPS satellites (valid up to **90 days**). | | **Ephemeris** | Precise orbital data for *visible* satellites (valid up to **4 hours**). | | **Pseudorange** | Distance estimate between satellite and receiver, corrupted by clock bias. | | **MS-Assisted** | Mobile Station (robot) sends raw measurements to server; server computes fix. | | **MS-Based** | Server sends aiding data; robot computes fix locally. | | **DGPS** | Differential GPS — a related accuracy-enhancement technique using ground stations. | > [!NOTE] > A-GPS is **not** a replacement for GPS; it is a **performance enhancement layer** that uses an alternative communication channel to bypass the GPS protocol's slowest stages. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Architecture of A-GPS The A-GPS framework can be decomposed into **three primary logical blocks**, each with a defined responsibility: 1. **GPS Satellite Constellation (Space Segment)** - Transmits ranging codes (C/A code at **1.023 Mcps** and P-code at **10.23 Mcps**). - Broadcasts navigation message at **50 bps** (extremely slow by design, which is why A-GPS helps). 2. **Assistance Server (Network Segment)** - Maintains a continuously updated database of satellite positions using fixed reference receivers. - Computes and caches almanac, ephemeris, ionospheric corrections, and approximate time/position. - Responds to robot queries via cellular (3G/4G/5G) or Wi-Fi. 3. **Mobile Robot / Receiver (User Segment)** - Sends its approximate location (or Cell-ID) to the server. - Receives aiding data bundle. - Performs pseudorange measurements to compute position. ## 2.2 Modes of A-GPS Operation ### 2.2.1 MS-Assisted (Mobile Station Assisted) - Robot transmits **raw pseudoranges** to the server. - Server performs trilateration and returns a precise fix. - **Pros:** Very high sensitivity (can work with very weak signals). - **Cons:** Requires constant network connectivity; cannot localize if network fails. ### 2.2.2 MS-Based (Mobile Station Based) - Server transmits **almanac + ephemeris + reference time** to the robot. - Robot computes position locally using standard GPS equations. - **Pros:** Robot remains autonomous once data is acquired. - **Cons:** Slightly slower than MS-Assisted in deep-indoor scenarios. ## 2.3 Why and How A-GPS Speeds Up Localization The standard GPS cold-start can take **30–60 seconds** (and up to **12.5 minutes** in worst case) because: - The receiver must search across **1023 chips** of the C/A code for each of up to **32 satellites**. - It must decode the full navigation message (25 frames × 1500 bits = **37,500 bits** at 50 bps = **12.5 minutes**). - It must solve for time ambiguity. A-GPS reduces this by: - **Providing coarse time** → eliminates one dimension of the search space. - **Providing satellite Doppler predictions** → narrows the frequency search window. - **Providing approximate position** → reduces the visible-satellite candidate list. - **Bypassing almanac/ephemeris download** → saves the slow 50 bps data rate. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following equations form the analytical core of A-GPS and standard GPS-based localization in robot motion planning. | Formula | Description | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $t_{TTFF,cold} \approx 30 \text{ to } 60$ | Cold-start Time-To-First-Fix (no aiding) | seconds | | $t_{TTFF,AGPS} \approx 2 \text{ to } 10$ | A-GPS assisted TTFF | seconds | | $R_{nav} = 50$ | Navigation message bit rate | bps | | $f_{CA} = 1.023 \times 10^6$ | C/A code chipping rate | chips/sec | | $f_{L1} = 1575.42 \times 10^6$ | GPS L1 carrier frequency | Hz | | $\rho_i = c \cdot (t_{r,i} - t_{s,i})$ | Pseudorange to satellite $i$ | meters | | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ | Speed of light | m/s | | $\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_r)^2 + (y_i - y_r)^2 + (z_i - z_r)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_r$ | Nonlinear pseudorange equation | meters | | $\sigma_{UERE}^2 = \sigma_{eph}^2 + \sigma_{iono}^2 + \sigma_{tropo}^2 + \sigma_{mp}^2 + \sigma_{rx}^2$ | User Equivalent Range Error variance | $m^2$ | | $HDOP = \sqrt{Q_{11} + Q_{22}}$ | Horizontal Dilution of Precision | dimensionless | | $\sigma_{H} = HDOP \cdot \sigma_{UERE}$ | Horizontal position accuracy | meters | | $GDOP = \sqrt{\text{trace}(H^T H)^{-1}}$ | Geometric Dilution of Precision | dimensionless | | $\Delta P_{Kalman} = K \cdot (z_{GPS} - H \hat{x}^{-})$ | Kalman update for GPS fusion | state units | | $w_i = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma_i^2}$ | Particle filter weight for A-GPS measurement | $1/m^2$ | > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in the design of SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) systems, autonomous vehicle navigation stacks, and outdoor delivery robot fleets, where A-GPS often provides the *global loop-closure* constraint that prevents unbounded drift from dead-reckoning sensors. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Pseudorange Measurement Model The **pseudorange** is the fundamental observable in GPS/A-GPS. Unlike a true range, it is contaminated by the receiver's clock bias, which is why only 4 satellites (not 3) are needed for a 3D fix. **Step 1: True range definition.** The geometric range $r_i$ between satellite $i$ (with ECEF position $\mathbf{x}_s^i = [x_i, y_i, z_i]^T$) and the robot's receiver (with position $\mathbf{x}_r = [x_r, y_r, z_r]^T$) is: $$r_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_r)^2 + (y_i - y_r)^2 + (z_i - z_r)^2}$$ **Step 2: Incorporate the clock bias.** The receiver's internal clock is unsynchronized with GPS time, introducing a bias $b_r = c \cdot \delta t_r$. The measured time of arrival $t_{r,i}$ differs from the true signal transmission time $t_{s,i}$ by the true travel time plus the clock bias: $$t_{r,i} - t_{s,i} = \frac{r_i}{c} + \delta t_r$$ **Step 3: Form the pseudorange.** Multiplying both sides by $c$ gives the pseudorange $\rho_i$: $$\rho_i = c \cdot (t_{r,i} - t_{s,i}) = r_i + c \cdot \delta t_r$$ **Step 4: Substitute the geometric range.** Expanding $r_i$ yields the canonical nonlinear pseudorange equation: $$\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_r)^2 + (y_i - y_r)^2 + (z_i - z_r)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_r$$ **Explanation:** The receiver measures $\rho_i$ (using C/A code correlation), and $\mathbf{x}_s^i$ is known from the ephemeris. A-GPS accelerates this step by **supplying $t_{s,i}$ accurately** via the assistance server, eliminating the need to decode it from the slow 50 bps data stream. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Linearization for Iterative Least-Squares Fix The pseudorange equation is nonlinear in $\mathbf{x}_r$. We linearize it using a Taylor expansion around an initial guess $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_r^{(0)} = [\hat{x}_r, \hat{y}_r, \hat{z}_r, \hat{b}_r]^T$. **Step 1: Compute partial derivatives.** The Jacobian $\mathbf{H}$ row for satellite $i$ is: $$\mathbf{h}_i = \left[ \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial x_r}, \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial y_r}, \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial z_r}, \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial b_r} \right]$$ **Step 2: Evaluate the spatial derivatives.** $$\frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial x_r} = -\frac{x_i - \hat{x}_r}{\hat{r}_i}, \quad \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial y_r} = -\frac{y_i - \hat{y}_r}{\hat{r}_i}, \quad \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial z_r} = -\frac{z_i - \hat{z}_r}{\hat{r}_i}$$ where $\hat{r}_i = \sqrt{(x_i - \hat{x}_r)^2 + (y_i - \hat{y}_r)^2 + (z_i - \hat{z}_r)^2}$. **Step 3: Evaluate the clock-bias derivative.** $$\frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial b_r} = 1$$ **Step 4: Form the linear system.** The measurement residual $\Delta \rho_i = \rho_i - \hat{\rho}_i$ (measured minus predicted) satisfies: $$\Delta \rho_i = \mathbf{h}_i \cdot \Delta \mathbf{x}$$ Stacking for $n$ satellites: $$\Delta \boldsymbol{\rho} = \mathbf{H} \cdot \Delta \mathbf{x}$$ **Step 5: Solve via weighted least squares.** $$\Delta \mathbf{x} = (\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{H})^{-1} \mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{W} \Delta \boldsymbol{\rho}$$ where $\mathbf{W} = \text{diag}(\sigma_1^{-2}, \sigma_2^{-2}, \ldots, \sigma_n^{-2})$ weights each satellite by the inverse variance of its pseudorange noise. **Step 6: Iterate until convergence.** Update the state: $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_r^{(k+1)} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_r^{(k)} + \Delta \mathbf{x}$ and repeat until $\vert \Delta \mathbf{x} \vert < \epsilon$ (typically $\epsilon = 10^{-4}$ m). **Explanation:** A-GPS helps by providing a *very good initial guess* $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_r^{(0)}$ via Cell-ID or Wi-Fi triangulation, often making the algorithm converge in a single iteration. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Position Accuracy from Dilution of Precision (DOP) **Step 1: Define the measurement covariance matrix.** Assuming independent pseudorange errors with variance $\sigma_{UERE}^2$ (User Equivalent Range Error): $$\mathbf{R} = \sigma_{UERE}^2 \cdot \mathbf{I}_n$$ **Step 2: Compute the state covariance.** $$\mathbf{P} = (\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H})^{-1} \sigma_{UERE}^2$$ **Step 3: Extract DOP metrics.** $$GDOP = \sqrt{P_{11} + P_{22} + P_{33} + P_{44}}$$ $$PDOP = \sqrt{P_{11} + P_{22} + P_{33}}, \quad HDOP = \sqrt{P_{11} + P_{22}}, \quad VDOP = \sqrt{P_{33}}, \quad TDOP = \sqrt{P_{44}}$$ **Step 4: Compute horizontal accuracy.** $$\sigma_H = HDOP \cdot \sigma_{UERE}$$ **Numerical Example:** With $\sigma_{UERE} = 5$ m and $HDOP = 1.5$: $$\sigma_H = 1.5 \times 5 = 7.5 \text{ m}$$ **Explanation:** A-GPS can also improve DOP by allowing the receiver to use satellites it would otherwise have dismissed due to weak signals, often lowering HDOP from 2–3 to 1–1.5 in obstructed environments. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Time Savings from A-GPS **Step 1: Define cold-start TTFF without aiding.** The receiver must decode $N_{bits} = 37{,}500$ bits at $R_{nav} = 50$ bps to obtain the full almanac: $$t_{cold} = \frac{N_{bits}}{R_{nav}} = \frac{37{,}500}{50} = 750 \text{ s} = 12.5 \text{ min}$$ **Step 2: Define A-GPS aided TTFF.** The server transmits the aiding data over a high-rate link (e.g., 100 kbps GPRS). For a $D_{aid} = 5$ kB data bundle: $$t_{download} = \frac{D_{aid}}{R_{link}} = \frac{5000 \times 8}{100{,}000} = 0.4 \text{ s}$$ **Step 3: Add the post-download acquisition time.** Once aiding data is available, only a fast code-phase search is needed (typically $t_{acq} \approx 2$ s): $$t_{AGPS} = t_{download} + t_{acq} \approx 0.4 + 2 = 2.4 \text{ s}$$ **Step 4: Compute the speedup factor.** $$\text{Speedup} = \frac{t_{cold}}{t_{AGPS}} = \frac{750}{2.4} \approx 312\times$$ **Explanation:** This is why A-GPS is the dominant localization mode in modern smartphones and is increasingly integrated into outdoor service robots, delivery drones, and agricultural robots. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: A-GPS vs. Other Localization Modes | Parameter | Standalone GPS | A-GPS | DGPS | RTK-GPS | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **TTFF (cold)** | 30–60 s (up to 12.5 min) | **2–10 s** | 30–60 s | 30–60 s | | **Accuracy** | 5–15 m | 5–15 m | **1–3 m** | **1–10 cm** | | **Network needed** | No | Yes (for aiding) | Yes (corrections) | Yes (real-time link) | | **Indoor sensitivity** | Poor | **Good (MS-Assisted)** | Poor | Poor | | **Robot Use Case** | Outdoor open-sky | Urban delivery robots | Surveying, precision agri. | Autonomous vehicles | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 A-GPS System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete A-GPS data flow from the space segment through the assistance server to the mobile robot's motion planning stack. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[GPS Satellite Constellation
Space Segment] nodeB[L1 Signal 1575.42 MHz
C/A Code 1.023 Mcps] nodeC[Reference Receiver Network
Continuously Tracks Satellites] nodeD[Assistance Server
Caches Almanac and Ephemeris] nodeE[Cellular or WiFi Link
High Bandwidth Channel] nodeF[Mobile Robot Receiver
User Segment] nodeG[Raw Pseudorange Measurements
Code Phase Extraction] nodeH[Position Fix Computation
Trilateration Algorithm] nodeI[Localization Module
Kalman or Particle Filter] nodeJ[Motion Planning Stack
Path Planner A Star RRT] nodeK[Robot Actuators
Wheel or Leg Drives] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeA --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeI nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ nodeJ --> nodeK ``` ## 4.2 A-GPS Operational Sequence (Timing Diagram) The following diagram depicts the temporal sequence of operations during an A-GPS cold-start, highlighting the critical time savings. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Robot Power On
Time 0 s] nodeB[Send Approx Position Query
Cell ID to Server] nodeC[Server Replies with
Almanac plus Ephemeris
plus Reference Time] nodeD[Acquisition Phase
Fast Code Search
2 to 5 s] nodeE[Tracking Phase
Continuous Pseudoranges] nodeF[Position Fix Output
4 Plus Satellites Locked] nodeG[Initialize Planner
Global Pose Available] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG ``` ## 4.3 A-GPS Integration with Robot Sensor Fusion ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Wheel Odometry
High Rate Relative Pose] nodeB[IMU Inertial Sensor
Acceleration and Angular Rate] nodeC[A-GPS Module
Global Pose Estimate] nodeD[LiDAR Scan Matcher
Local Map Registration] nodeE[Extended Kalman Filter
Sensor Fusion Core] nodeF[Optimal Pose Estimate
Robot Belief State] nodeG[Global Path Planner
A Star RRT Star] nodeH[Local Path Planner
DWA or MPC] nodeI[Motor Controller
Velocity Commands] nodeA --> nodeE nodeB --> nodeE nodeC --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeF --> nodeH nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define Assisted GPS (A-GPS) and state two advantages it offers over standalone GPS in robot localization.** **Model Answer:** Assisted GPS (A-GPS) is a satellite positioning technique in which an external assistance server (accessed via cellular or Wi-Fi) supplies the GPS receiver with orbital data (almanac and ephemeris), reference time, and approximate position, in order to reduce the Time-To-First-Fix and improve signal sensitivity. **Two advantages:** 1. **Reduced TTFF:** Cold-start fix time drops from 30–60 s (up to 12.5 min) to approximately 2–10 s. 2. **Improved indoor / urban sensitivity:** MS-Assisted mode can compute a fix from very weak signals that would otherwise fail standalone GPS, making it suitable for warehouse and urban-canyon robots. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between MS-Based and MS-Assisted modes of A-GPS.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | MS-Based A-GPS | MS-Assisted A-GPS | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Where aiding data is sent** | Server → Mobile Station | Server → Mobile Station | | **Where fix is computed** | Mobile Station (robot) | Server | | **Mobile sends to server** | Approx. position | Raw pseudoranges | | **Network dependency after fix** | Low (autonomous) | High (continuous) | | **Sensitivity to weak signals** | Moderate | **Very high** | | **Typical use case** | Outdoor mobile robots | Indoor / urban-canyon robots | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of an A-GPS system** and describe the data flow between its major components. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **pseudorange measurement equation** and explain how A-GPS accelerates the acquisition process by providing aiding data. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) The A-GPS system consists of three major segments: 1. **Space Segment (GPS Satellites):** A constellation of at least 24 MEO satellites orbiting at an altitude of approximately **20,200 km** with a period of **11 hours 58 minutes**. Each satellite continuously broadcasts: - L1 carrier at **1575.42 MHz** modulated with the C/A code (1.023 Mcps) and the navigation message (50 bps). - L2 carrier at **1227.60 MHz** modulated with the P-code (used by military and for ionospheric correction). 2. **Network Segment (Assistance Server):** A ground-based server that: - Receives live signals from a network of **reference GPS receivers** at surveyed locations. - Computes and stores current **almanac, ephemeris, ionospheric correction parameters, and Coordinated Universal Time (UTC)**. - Responds to client queries over **cellular (3G/4G/5G), Wi-Fi, or internet** with a data bundle typically a few kilobytes in size. 3. **User Segment (Mobile Robot Receiver):** The robot's GPS module which: - Optionally transmits its **Cell-ID or last known position** to the server. - Receives the aiding data bundle. - Performs a **fast C/A code search** over a narrowed Doppler range. - Outputs pseudoranges to the localization filter. **Data Flow:** Satellites → Reference Receivers → Assistance Server → (Cellular/Wi-Fi) → Robot Receiver → Localization Module → Motion Planner. #### Model Solution for (b) The pseudorange $\rho_i$ between the $i$-th satellite and the receiver is defined as: $$\rho_i = c \cdot (t_{r,i} - t_{s,i})$$ where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s is the speed of light, $t_{r,i}$ is the measured reception time, and $t_{s,i}$ is the signal transmission time. This can be expanded to: $$\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_r)^2 + (y_i - y_r)^2 + (z_i - z_r)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_r$$ The first term is the true geometric range, and $c \cdot \delta t_r$ is the receiver clock bias (since the receiver's clock is not synchronized with GPS time). A fourth unknown $\delta t_r$ is the reason **4 satellites** are required for a 3D fix (3 for position + 1 for time). **How A-GPS accelerates acquisition:** In standalone GPS, the receiver must decode the slow 50 bps navigation message to extract $t_{s,i}$, the ephemeris, and the almanac, which can take up to **12.5 minutes**. A-GPS bypasses this bottleneck by: - Supplying **$t_{s,i}$ (reference time)** directly from the server, eliminating the need for sub-frame decoding. - Supplying **satellite Doppler predictions**, narrowing the frequency search window from $\pm 5$ kHz to $\pm 50$ Hz. - Supplying **approximate position** (e.g., from Cell-ID), allowing the receiver to predict which satellites are above the horizon. - The total cold-start TTFF thus drops from **~750 s** to **~2–10 s**, a speedup of roughly **100× to 300×**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Derive the **linearized least-squares position fix** from the pseudorange equations and show how the **Dilution of Precision (DOP)** affects position accuracy. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A robot receives pseudoranges from 5 satellites with UERE $\sigma_{UERE} = 4$ m and the geometry matrix yields $HDOP = 1.8$. Calculate the **expected horizontal position accuracy** and discuss how A-GPS improves the **DOP** in obstructed environments. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) **Step 1: Linearize the pseudorange equation.** Linearizing $\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_r)^2 + (y_i - y_r)^2 + (z_i - z_r)^2} + c \delta t_r$ about the estimate $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = [\hat{x}_r, \hat{y}_r, \hat{z}_r, \hat{b}_r]^T$ yields: $$\Delta \rho_i = -\frac{x_i - \hat{x}_r}{\hat{r}_i}\Delta x_r - \frac{y_i - \hat{y}_r}{\hat{r}_i}\Delta y_r - \frac{z_i - \hat{z}_r}{\hat{r}_i}\Delta z_r + \Delta b_r$$ **Step 2: Form the matrix equation.** Stacking for $n$ satellites: $$\begin{bmatrix} \Delta \rho_1 \\ \Delta \rho_2 \\ \vdots \\ \Delta \rho_n \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{x_1 - \hat{x}_r}{\hat{r}_1} & -\frac{y_1 - \hat{y}_r}{\hat{r}_1} & -\frac{z_1 - \hat{z}_r}{\hat{r}_1} & 1 \\ -\frac{x_2 - \hat{x}_r}{\hat{r}_2} & -\frac{y_2 - \hat{y}_r}{\hat{r}_2} & -\frac{z_2 - \hat{z}_r}{\hat{r}_2} & 1 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ -\frac{x_n - \hat{x}_r}{\hat{r}_n} & -\frac{y_n - \hat{y}_r}{\hat{r}_n} & -\frac{z_n - \hat{z}_r}{\hat{r}_n} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x_r \\ \Delta y_r \\ \Delta z_r \\ \Delta b_r \end{bmatrix}$$ That is, $\Delta \boldsymbol{\rho} = \mathbf{H} \Delta \mathbf{x}$. **Step 3: Solve using least squares.** $$\Delta \mathbf{x} = (\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H})^{-1} \mathbf{H}^T \Delta \boldsymbol{\rho}$$ **Step 4: DOP definition.** The state covariance is $\mathbf{P} = (\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H})^{-1} \sigma_{UERE}^2$. The DOP metrics are: $$GDOP = \sqrt{\text{trace}(\mathbf{P})} / \sigma_{UERE}, \quad HDOP = \sqrt{P_{11} + P_{22}}, \quad \sigma_H = HDOP \cdot \sigma_{UERE}$$ DOP quantifies how satellite geometry amplifies ranging errors: well-spread satellites yield **low DOP** (good geometry), while clustered satellites yield **high DOP** (poor geometry). #### Model Solution for (b) **Step 1: Apply the horizontal accuracy formula.** $$\sigma_H = HDOP \cdot \sigma_{UERE} = 1.8 \times 4 = 7.2 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2: Interpretation.** The expected horizontal position error is approximately **7.2 m** (1-sigma). In practice, the 2D-RMS error is roughly $2 \times \sigma_H \approx 14.4$ m. **Step 3: How A-GPS improves DOP in obstructed environments.** In urban canyons or near tall buildings, many satellites are blocked, and the remaining visible satellites may be **geometrically clustered** in one part of the sky, yielding a poor $HDOP$ of 3–5. A-GPS improves this by: - Providing **highly sensitive acquisition** (often 20–30 dB better than standalone GPS), allowing the receiver to track weak satellites that would otherwise be discarded. - Supplying **predicted Doppler and code phase**, so the receiver can spend more integration time on weak signals. - The result is typically a **lower HDOP (1.0–1.5)** and consequently a **50%–70% improvement** in horizontal accuracy. For the given numerical example, if A-GPS reduced $HDOP$ from 1.8 to 1.2: $$\sigma_H^{AGPS} = 1.2 \times 4 = 4.8 \text{ m}$$ This is a **33% improvement** in accuracy from geometry alone, in addition to the dramatic TTFF reduction. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callouts:** > 1. **Confusing UERE with DOP:** Students often multiply $\sigma_{UERE}$ by $GDOP$ instead of $HDOP$ for horizontal accuracy — this is a 1-mark deduction error. > 2. **Forgetting the clock bias term:** When writing the pseudorange equation, omitting the $c \cdot \delta t_r$ term is a **2-mark loss** because the receiver clock bias is a core concept in A-GPS (it is one of the unknowns the assistance server helps resolve). > 3. **Stating A-GPS replaces GPS:** A-GPS is an *enhancement*, not a replacement. Examiners specifically look for this distinction. > 4. **Ignoring the 4-satellite requirement:** Always mention that 4 satellites are needed for a 3D fix (3 for position + 1 for time synchronization). This is a frequently tested point. > 5. **Forgetting units in numerical problems:** DOP is dimensionless, and $\sigma_{UERE}$ must be in meters to obtain $\sigma_H$ in meters. State units explicitly. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for A-GPS in Robot Motion Planning:** - **A-GPS = Assisted GPS** — uses an external server to provide aiding data to the GPS receiver. - **Primary benefit:** TTFF reduction from **30–60 s (up to 12.5 min)** down to **2–10 s**. - **Secondary benefit:** Improved **sensitivity** (20–30 dB better), enabling indoor / urban-canyon localization. - **Three Segments:** Space (satellites), Network (assistance server), User (robot receiver). - **Aiding data includes:** almanac, ephemeris, reference time, satellite Doppler, approximate position. - **Two modes:** **MS-Based** (robot computes fix) and **MS-Assisted** (server computes fix). - **Pseudorange equation:** $\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_r)^2 + (y_i - y_r)^2 + (z_i - z_r)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_r$. - **Minimum 4 satellites** required for 3D fix (3 position + 1 time). - **C/A code rate:** **1.023 Mcps**; **Navigation bit rate:** **50 bps**; **L1 carrier:** **1575.42 MHz**. - **DOP types:** GDOP, PDOP, HDOP, VDOP, TDOP — all dimensionless. - **Horizontal accuracy formula:** $\sigma_H = HDOP \cdot \sigma_{UERE}$. - **Sensor fusion:** A-GPS is typically fused with IMU and odometry using an **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** or **Particle Filter**. - **Limitation:** A-GPS still requires **line-of-sight to satellites** for direct ranging; it is **not a full indoor solution** (Wi-Fi/Bluetooth/UWB are complementary). - **A-GPS ≠ RTK-GPS:** A-GPS provides standard ~5–10 m accuracy; RTK-GPS achieves **cm-level** accuracy but requires a real-time correction link. - **Engineering Application:** Used in **autonomous delivery robots, agricultural robots, UAVs, and outdoor AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots)** as the global localization backbone. - **KTU 2024 Specific:** Expect a 7-mark question on architecture and a 7-mark question on derivation/numerical, often combined into a 14-mark Part B question.

topics.title: DGPS Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** DGPS Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Differential Global Positioning System (DGPS)** is an enhancement to the standard **Global Positioning System (GPS)** that uses a network of fixed, ground-based reference stations to broadcast the difference between the positions indicated by the GPS satellite system and the known fixed positions, thereby providing **real-time kinematic (RTK) or post-processed positional corrections** to mobile (roving) receivers.

In the context of **Robot Motion Planning (OEBRT833, Module 4)**, DGPS is classified as an **absolute, outdoor, satellite-based localization technique** used for global path planning and waypoint navigation of autonomous ground vehicles (AGVs), unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), and field robots.

- **Standard GPS Civilian Accuracy:** $\pm 10$ m to $\pm 15$ m (with **95% confidence interval**)
- **DGPS Corrected Accuracy:** $\pm 1$ m to $\pm 3$ m (sub-meter achievable with carrier-phase RTK)
- **Standard Reference Datum:** WGS-84 (World Geodetic System 1984)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Anchor and the Boat"**
>
> Imagine you are a sailor (the **roving receiver**) trying to estimate your position in a foggy ocean using only stars. The stars give a rough estimate, but fog scatters your view. Now imagine a friend standing on a known lighthouse (the **reference station**) who can also see the same stars. Since your friend knows their *exact* location, they can compute the **error** introduced by the fog (atmospheric delay). Your friend shouts this error correction to you over the radio, and you **subtract** that error from your own reading.
>
> **The lighthouse friend = Reference Station**  
> **The radio broadcast = Differential Correction Link**  
> **You = Mobile Robot Receiver**

## 1.3 Why DGPS is Critical for Robot Motion Planning

- **Global Localization:** Provides absolute WGS-84 coordinates for outdoor SLAM initialization.
- **Waypoint Navigation:** Robots follow pre-defined GPS waypoints (e.g., agricultural spraying, package delivery).
- **Multi-Robot Coordination:** Common global frame allows fleet-level task allocation.
- **Geo-fencing:** Robot is bounded within a virtual GPS-defined perimeter.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:** This topic directly addresses **CO4** — *"Apply absolute and relative localization techniques for mobile robot path planning in outdoor environments."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Error in Standalone GPS

| # | Error Source | Typical Magnitude | Correctable by DGPS? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ionospheric Delay** | $\pm 5$ m | ✅ Yes |
| 2 | **Tropospheric Delay** | $\pm 0.5$ m | ✅ Yes |
| 3 | **Satellite Clock Error** | $\pm 2$ m | ✅ Yes |
| 4 | **Ephemeris (Orbit) Error** | $\pm 2.5$ m | ✅ Yes |
| 5 | **Multipath (Signal Reflection)** | $\pm 1$ m | ⚠️ Partially |
| 6 | **Receiver Noise** | $\pm 0.5$ m | ❌ No |
| 7 | **Selective Availability (SA)** | $\pm 30$ m (legacy) | ✅ Yes (Turned OFF, 2000) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The foundational principle of DGPS: **Errors at the reference station and the roving receiver are highly correlated** when both are within $\sim 200$ km of each other, because the GPS signals traverse nearly identical atmospheric paths.

## 2.2 Operational Architecture of DGPS

The DGPS pipeline consists of four logical stages:

1. **Reference Station (Base Receiver)**
   - Mounted at a precisely surveyed location (geodetic control point).
   - Continuously tracks all visible GPS satellites ($\geq 4$ SVs).
   - Computes *pseudorange corrections* and *range-rate corrections*.

2. **Error Computation**
   - Known position vs. GPS-computed position gives a position-domain correction.
   - Pseudorange residual gives a range-domain correction (preferred, more rigorous).

3. **Correction Link (Data Link)**
   - Corrections transmitted via **VHF radio, UHF, cellular (NTRIP), or satellite (SBAS like WAAS, EGNOS, GAGAN)**.
   - Standard message format: **RTCM SC-104 (Radio Technical Commission for Maritime Services, Special Committee 104)**.

4. **Rover Receiver (Mobile Robot)**
   - Applies corrections to its own pseudoranges in real-time.
   - Outputs an enhanced position fix to the robot's motion planner.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pseudorange** | $\rho = r + c(\delta t_r - \delta t^s) + I + T + \varepsilon$ | metres | $r$ = true range, $I$=ionospheric, $T$=tropospheric, $\varepsilon$=noise |
| 2 | **Pseudorange Correction** | $\Delta\rho = \rho_{measured}^{ref} - \rho_{known}^{ref}$ | metres | Computed at base station |
| 3 | **Corrected Pseudorange (Rover)** | $\rho_{corrected} = \rho_{rover} - \Delta\rho$ | metres | Applied to mobile robot |
| 4 | **Corrected Position** | $\vec{P}_{corrected} = \vec{P}_{rover} - \vec{\Delta P}_{ref}$ | metres (ECEF) | Vector subtraction |
| 5 | **Dilution of Precision (DOP)** | $DOP = \sqrt{\text{trace}(H^T H)^{-1}}$ | dimensionless | $H$ = geometry matrix |
| 6 | **Geometric DOP (GDOP)** | $GDOP = \sqrt{PDOP^2 + TDOP^2}$ | dimensionless | Position + Time |
| 7 | **Positioning Accuracy (1$\sigma$)** | $\sigma_p = \sigma_{URE} \times DOP$ | metres | $\sigma_{URE}$ = User Range Error |
| 8 | **Wavelength (L1 Carrier)** | $\lambda_{L1} = \dfrac{c}{f_{L1}} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{1575.42 \times 10^6}$ | $\approx 0.1903$ m | Used in RTK |
| 9 | **Wavelength (L2 Carrier)** | $\lambda_{L2} = \dfrac{c}{f_{L2}}$ | $\approx 0.2442$ m | Dual-frequency iono-free |
| 10 | **RTK Ambiguity Resolution Time** | $t_{fix} \propto \dfrac{1}{SNR}$ | seconds | SNR = Signal-to-Noise Ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** DGPS is the backbone of precision agriculture (John Deere AutoTrac, $\pm 2.5$ cm pass-to-pass), autonomous trucking (TuSimple, Waymo Via), marine surveying (hydrographic charts), and aerial drone photogrammetry (DJI Phantom 4 RTK).

## 2.4 Classification of DGPS Techniques

| Technique | Correction Domain | Accuracy | Latency | Robot Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Position-Domain DGPS** | XYZ position | 1–3 m | Real-time | Basic waypoint nav |
| **Pseudorange (Range) DGPS** | Per-satellite $\Delta\rho$ | 0.5–2 m | Real-time | Mid-tier mobile robots |
| **Carrier-Phase DGPS (CDGPS)** | Carrier phase | 0.1–1 m | Real-time | Survey-grade AGV |
| **Real-Time Kinematic (RTK)** | Carrier phase + integer ambiguity | **0.01–0.05 m** | Real-time | Precision self-driving cars |
| **Post-Processed DGPS (PPK)** | Carrier phase | cm-level | Offline | Aerial mapping drones |
| **SBAS (WAAS/EGNOS/GAGAN)** | Wide-area corrections | 1–2 m | Real-time (satellite) | Low-cost outdoor bots |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Pseudorange Correction Equation

### 3.1.1 Starting Model (Standard GPS Pseudorange)

The raw pseudorange measured by any receiver (base or rover) to a satellite $s$ is modeled as:

$$\rho = r + c(\delta t_r - \delta t^s) + I_{\rho} + T_{\rho} + \varepsilon_{\rho}$$

Where each term has a precise physical meaning:

- $\rho$ = measured pseudorange (metres)
- $r$ = true geometric range from receiver antenna to satellite
- $c$ = speed of light in vacuum, $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $\delta t_r$ = receiver clock bias (unknown)
- $\delta t^s$ = satellite clock bias (broadcast in navigation message)
- $I_{\rho}$ = ionospheric path delay
- $T_{\rho}$ = tropospheric path delay
- $\varepsilon_{\rho}$ = random measurement noise + multipath residual

### 3.1.2 At the Reference Station

The reference station is at a *known* position $\vec{P}_{ref}^{true}$. Using the broadcast ephemeris, the station can compute the **true geometric range** $r_{ref}$ to each visible satellite $s$:

$$r_{ref} = \left\| \vec{P}_{ref}^{true} - \vec{P}^{s,broadcast} \right\|$$

The reference receiver also independently measures the **raw pseudorange** $\rho_{ref}^{meas}$ to that same satellite. The **pseudorange correction** is therefore defined as:

$$\Delta\rho^s = \rho_{ref}^{meas} - r_{ref}$$

**Substituting** the full pseudorange model for $\rho_{ref}^{meas}$:

$$\Delta\rho^s = r_{ref} + c(\delta t_{ref} - \delta t^s) + I_{ref} + T_{ref} + \varepsilon_{ref} - r_{ref}$$

The true ranges $r_{ref}$ cancel:

$$\Delta\rho^s = c(\delta t_{ref} - \delta t^s) + I_{ref} + T_{ref} + \varepsilon_{ref}$$

> This $\Delta\rho^s$ now encapsulates **all the combined errors** (clock, atmospheric, multipath) for satellite $s$ at that instant.

### 3.1.3 At the Rover (Mobile Robot)

The rover measures its own raw pseudorange $\rho_{rover}^{meas}$:

$$\rho_{rover}^{meas} = r_{rover} + c(\delta t_{rover} - \delta t^s) + I_{rover} + T_{rover} + \varepsilon_{rover}$$

Applying the correction from the reference station:

$$\rho_{rover}^{corrected} = \rho_{rover}^{meas} - \Delta\rho^s$$

Substituting the expanded forms:

$$\rho_{rover}^{corrected} = r_{rover} + c(\delta t_{rover} - \delta t^s) + I_{rover} + T_{rover} + \varepsilon_{rover} - \left[ c(\delta t_{ref} - \delta t^s) + I_{ref} + T_{ref} + \varepsilon_{ref} \right]$$

Simplifying (satellite clock $\delta t^s$ cancels):

$$\rho_{rover}^{corrected} = r_{rover} + c(\delta t_{rover} - \delta t_{ref}) + (I_{rover} - I_{ref}) + (T_{rover} - T_{ref}) + (\varepsilon_{rover} - \varepsilon_{ref})$$

### 3.1.4 The Key Assumption (Spatial Correlation)

Since base and rover are typically within $\leq 200$ km:

$$I_{rover} \approx I_{ref} \quad \text{and} \quad T_{rover} \approx T_{ref}$$

These differences vanish to first order:

$$I_{rover} - I_{ref} \to 0 \quad \text{and} \quad T_{rover} - T_{ref} \to 0$$

**Final corrected pseudorange** reduces to:

$$\rho_{rover}^{corrected} \approx r_{rover} + c(\delta t_{rover} - \delta t_{ref}) + (\varepsilon_{rover} - \varepsilon_{ref})$$

The ionospheric, tropospheric, satellite clock, and most ephemeris errors are eliminated. Only the **differential receiver clock bias** and a small **noise residual** remain. The corrected pseudoranges are fed into a standard least-squares position solver to obtain the robot's true ECEF (Earth-Centered Earth-Fixed) coordinates.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> [!TIP]
> **KTU-style 7-Mark Application Problem Setup**

**Problem:** A reference station is at a known position with true ECEF coordinates $X_0 = -2693615$ m. Its GPS-computed position is $X_{GPS} = -2693607$ m. The mobile robot (rover) at the same instant computes $X_{rover} = 4476212$ m. Find the **DGPS-corrected** X-coordinate of the rover.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute the position correction at the base:

$$\Delta X = X_{GPS} - X_0 = -2693607 - (-2693615) = 8 \text{ m}$$

Step 2 — Apply to rover:

$$X_{corrected} = X_{rover} - \Delta X = 4476212 - 8 = 4476204 \text{ m}$$

**Result:** The corrected position is $X_{corrected} = 4476204$ m, reducing the X-axis error from 8 m to near-zero (assuming similar magnitude Y, Z errors are similarly corrected).

## 3.3 Dilution of Precision (DOP) Derivation (Condensed)

The GPS linearized measurement equation is:

$$\Delta \rho = H \cdot \Delta x + \eta$$

Where $H$ is the $4 \times 4$ geometry matrix of unit line-of-sight vectors plus a column of ones (for the clock term). The covariance of the position solution is:

$$C_{xx} = \sigma_{URE}^2 \cdot (H^T H)^{-1}$$

Hence:

$$GDOP = \sqrt{\text{trace}\left[(H^T H)^{-1}\right]}$$

Sub-categories:

- $PDOP = \sqrt{\text{trace}_{xyz}\left[(H^T H)^{-1}\right]}$
- $HDOP$ = horizontal (2D) component
- $VDOP$ = vertical (1D) component
- $TDOP$ = time component

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse DOP with error magnitude. DOP is a *geometry multiplier*, not an error itself. The actual positioning accuracy is $\sigma_p = \sigma_{URE} \times DOP$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define DGPS. Mention two error sources that DGPS corrects.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Differential Global Positioning System (DGPS) is an enhancement of the standard GPS that uses a fixed, ground-based **reference station** at a known geodetic position to compute and broadcast **range or position corrections** to one or more **mobile (rover) receivers**, thereby improving positioning accuracy from $\sim 15$ m to $\sim 1$–$3$ m.

**Two error sources corrected (1 mark):**
1. **Ionospheric delay** (signal propagation error)
2. **Satellite clock bias** (timing error)
3. *Any other valid:* Ephemeris error, tropospheric delay.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List the major components of a DGPS system and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Reference Station (Base)** | Tracks satellites from a known position; computes pseudorange residuals |
| **Data Link** | Transmits corrections via VHF/UHF radio, NTRIP, or SBAS satellite |
| **Rover Receiver** | Mobile receiver on the robot that applies corrections to its own pseudoranges |
| **Position Solver** | Computes the corrected ECEF/WGS-84 coordinates for the motion planner |

(1.5 marks for naming 4 components + 1.5 marks for functions)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of a Differential GPS system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the spatial correlation assumption that makes DGPS feasible.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With suitable derivations, show how a pseudorange correction $\Delta\rho$ is computed at the base station and applied at the rover to obtain a corrected pseudorange.

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

1. **System Description (3 marks):**
   - A reference receiver at a surveyed location continuously measures pseudoranges to all visible GPS satellites.
   - The known geometric range $r_{ref}$ to each satellite is computed from the broadcast ephemeris.
   - The correction $\Delta\rho^s = \rho_{meas} - r_{ref}$ is broadcast to the rovers.
   - The rover applies $\rho_{rover}^{corrected} = \rho_{rover} - \Delta\rho$ before its least-squares solver.
   - Reference block diagram (similar to Section 4.1) — *attach the Mermaid-converted sketch*.

2. **Spatial Correlation Assumption (4 marks):**
   - Errors (ionospheric, tropospheric, ephemeris) are functions of the **line-of-sight geometry** to each satellite.
   - When base and rover are within $\leq 200$ km, the **two rays to the same satellite traverse nearly the same atmospheric column**.
   - Therefore: $I_{rover} \approx I_{ref}$, $T_{rover} \approx T_{ref}$.
   - The **common-mode errors cancel** when the correction is subtracted.
   - Differential clock biases remain but are absorbed by the position solver.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

1. **Pseudorange model at base (3 marks):**

$$\rho_{ref}^{meas} = r_{ref} + c(\delta t_{ref} - \delta t^s) + I_{ref} + T_{ref} + \varepsilon_{ref}$$

2. **Correction definition (2 marks):**

$$\Delta\rho^s = \rho_{ref}^{meas} - r_{ref} = c(\delta t_{ref} - \delta t^s) + I_{ref} + T_{ref} + \varepsilon_{ref}$$

3. **Rover application (2 marks):**

$$\rho_{rover}^{corrected} = r_{rover} + c(\delta t_{rover} - \delta t_{ref}) + (\varepsilon_{rover} - \varepsilon_{ref})$$

(The last equality assumes $I_{rover} - I_{ref} \to 0$ and $T_{rover} - T_{ref} \to 0$ due to spatial correlation.)

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare standalone GPS, DGPS, and RTK in terms of accuracy, technique, and typical applications. Explain why RTK achieves centimetre-level accuracy.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A reference station at surveyed ECEF coordinates $(X_0, Y_0, Z_0) = (-2,693,615, 4,313,898, 3,874,253)$ m records a GPS-computed position of $(-2,693,607, 4,313,905, 3,874,261)$ m. The robot rover simultaneously reads $(4,476,212, 4,567,801, 3,991,422)$ m. Compute the corrected rover position and the magnitude of the original rover position error vector.

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

1. **Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | Standalone GPS | DGPS | RTK |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | 10–15 m | 0.5–3 m | 0.01–0.05 m |
| Signal Used | C/A code | C/A code + corrections | Carrier phase L1/L2 |
| Domain | Position | Pseudorange / position | Carrier phase + ambiguity |
| Latency | Real-time | Real-time | Real-time (1–5 s fix) |
| Application | Car nav, hiking | Agri, marine | Surveying, self-driving |

2. **Why RTK is cm-accurate (3 marks):**
   - Uses the **carrier phase** (wavelength $\lambda \approx 0.19$ m) as the primary observable, with measurement noise at the **millimetre level**.
   - Resolves **integer cycle ambiguities** $N$ such that $\rho = \phi \lambda + N\lambda$.
   - After ambiguity fix, the effective pseudorange is accurate to a few mm.
   - DGPS by contrast uses the **code** (wavelength ~300 m), with noise at the metre level.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute corrections at base (3 marks):**

$$\Delta X = -2,693,607 - (-2,693,615) = 8 \text{ m}$$

$$\Delta Y = 4,313,905 - 4,313,898 = 7 \text{ m}$$

$$\Delta Z = 3,874,261 - 3,874,253 = 8 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply to rover (2 marks):**

$$X_c = 4,476,212 - 8 = 4,476,204 \text{ m}$$

$$Y_c = 4,567,801 - 7 = 4,567,794 \text{ m}$$

$$Z_c = 3,991,422 - 8 = 3,991,414 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute original rover error magnitude (2 marks):**

The correction vector magnitude is:

$$\vert \Delta P \vert = \sqrt{8^2 + 7^2 + 8^2} = \sqrt{64 + 49 + 64} = \sqrt{177} \approx 13.30 \text{ m}$$

This represents the **error that would have persisted** had DGPS not been applied.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the minus sign** in the correction formula (loss: 1–2 marks per part)
> 2. **Mixing position-domain and pseudorange-domain corrections** in a single derivation (loss: 2 marks)
> 3. **Omitting the spatial correlation assumption** explanation when asked "why does DGPS work?" (loss: 3 marks)
> 4. **Computing DOP without explaining it as a *multiplier*, not an error itself** (loss: 1 mark)
> 5. **Failing to mention RTCM SC-104** when asked about the data link standard (loss: 1 mark)
> 6. **Not sketching the block diagram** — a labelled block diagram is *mandatory* for 7-mark questions on DGPS architecture.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — DGPS Systems**

- ✅ DGPS = Reference Station + Correction Link + Rover; improves GPS from **~15 m → ~1–3 m**.
- ✅ Core assumption: **Spatial correlation** of atmospheric/clock errors within $\leq 200$ km.
- ✅ Correction formula: $\rho_{corrected} = \rho_{rover} - \Delta\rho$, where $\Delta\rho = \rho_{meas}^{ref} - r_{known}^{ref}$.
- ✅ Position-domain vs. **pseudorange (range) domain** corrections — pseudorange is the KTU-preferred answer.
- ✅ Errors corrected: ionospheric, tropospheric, satellite clock, ephemeris. **NOT** corrected: receiver noise, local multipath.
- ✅ Data link standard: **RTCM SC-104**. Wide-area equivalents: **WAAS (USA), EGNOS (EU), GAGAN (India)**.
- ✅ **RTK** uses **carrier phase** (L1: 1575.42 MHz, $\lambda \approx 0.1903$ m) + integer ambiguity resolution → cm-level.
- ✅ **DOP** is a geometry multiplier, not an error: $\sigma_p = \sigma_{URE} \times DOP$.
- ✅ Frame: **WGS-84**, ECEF (Earth-Centered Earth-Fixed) coordinates.
- ✅ Robot applications: outdoor AGVs, precision agri-bots, autonomous vehicles, UAV photogrammetry, marine survey.
