<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of RTK Navigation

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RTK (Real-Time Kinematic) Navigation** is a high-precision satellite-based positioning technique that uses **carrier-phase measurements** of GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System) signals, augmented by real-time correction data transmitted from a fixed **base station** (or a network of reference stations) to a **rover receiver**, achieving **centimeter-level positioning accuracy** in real time.

In the context of KTU's *Robot Motion Planning* syllabus (Module 4), RTK Navigation is studied as a **global localization and state-estimation technique** that enables outdoor mobile robots, autonomous ground vehicles (AGVs), UAVs, and agricultural robots to achieve absolute pose estimates with errors typically bounded between **1–2 cm horizontally** and **2–3 cm vertically**, far surpassing the **2–5 m accuracy** of standalone GNSS receivers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Surveyor's Compass

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners:**
>
> Imagine you and a friend are both trying to measure the length of a giant hall using a slightly stretched measuring tape. If you each measure independently, both of you will make the *same* systematic error caused by the stretched tape. But if your friend stands at a known reference point (e.g., a door marked with the *true* length), shouts out the *correction factor* over a walkie-talkie, and you *instantly* apply it to your own tape — both of you will now measure the true length with near-perfect accuracy.
>
> In RTK:
> - The **stretched measuring tape** = the GNSS signal distorted by atmospheric delays.
> - The **friend at the door** = the **base station** at a precisely surveyed location.
> - The **walkie-talkie** = the **real-time correction link** (radio, cellular, or NTRIP).
> - **You** = the **rover receiver** mounted on the robot.
> - The *true length* = **centimeter-accurate position**.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics

The following metrics are standardized under the **RTCM (Radio Technical Commission for Maritime Services)** correction protocol framework widely adopted in KTU-endorsed robotics curricula:

- **Horizontal accuracy:** **1 cm + 1 ppm** (parts per million of baseline length).
- **Vertical accuracy:** **2 cm + 1 ppm**.
- **Fix update rate:** typically **1 Hz to 20 Hz** (real-time).
- **Time to First Fix (TTFF):** **< 10 seconds** in RTK mode after initialization.
- **Baseline length:** effective up to **10–20 km** with single-base RTK; extended to **50–100 km** with Network RTK (NRTK).

## 1.4 Why RTK is Central to Robot Motion Planning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024 Module 4 Connection:**
> RTK is the **outdoor counterpart** to indoor localization techniques (UWB, SLAM, LiDAR odometry). When a robot transitions between indoor and outdoor environments, RTK-GNSS fusion (often coupled with **IMU/EKF**) becomes the bridge for **seamless global localization**, which is a key topic in motion planning under the *NEP 2020* outcome-based framework.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of RTK Navigation

The RTK workflow can be decomposed into **six sequential stages**:

1. **Satellite Signal Acquisition** — The rover and base station independently track L1/L2 (or L1/L5) carrier signals from a minimum of **5 common satellites** for a 3D fix.
2. **Raw Observation Generation** — Both receivers compute **pseudorange** (code-based) and **carrier-phase** measurements.
3. **Correction Computation at Base Station** — The base station, knowing its **precise surveyed coordinates** $(X_b, Y_b, Z_b)$, computes the **discrepancy** between its known position and the GNSS-derived position.
4. **Real-Time Data Transmission** — Corrections are encoded in **RTCM SC-104** message format and broadcast over **radio, cellular (NTRIP), or Wi-Fi** to the rover with a latency of **< 1 second**.
5. **Differencing at the Rover** — The rover applies **single, double, or triple differencing** of carrier-phase measurements to eliminate common-mode errors.
6. **Integer Ambiguity Resolution (IAR)** — The integer number of carrier cycles $N$ is resolved using algorithms like **LAMBDA (Least-squares AMBiguity Decorrelation Adjustment)** to fix the position with cm-level precision.

## 2.2 Error Sources in Standalone GNSS (Why We Need RTK)

GNSS measurements are corrupted by **five major error sources**, all of which RTK seeks to eliminate or reduce:

- **Ionospheric delay** ($I$): 1–10 m of range error due to charged particles in the ionosphere.
- **Tropospheric delay** ($T$): 2–25 m of range error due to water vapor and dry air.
- **Satellite clock error** ($\delta t^s$): ~2 m equivalent range error.
- **Receiver clock error** ($\delta t_r$): ~2 m equivalent range error.
- **Multipath and noise** ($\varepsilon$): 0.5–2 m in reflective environments.

> [!NOTE]
> The core principle of RTK is that the **base station and rover experience nearly identical atmospheric errors** when they are within ~20 km of each other. This **spatial correlation** of errors allows differencing to cancel them almost entirely.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles every equation you must memorize for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions on RTK Navigation.

| **Equation ID** | **Name** | **Formula** | **Variables & Units** | **Engineering Meaning** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **E1** | Pseudorange (Code) | $P_r^s = \rho_r^s + c(\delta t_r - \delta t^s) + I_r^s + T_r^s + \varepsilon_P$ | $P$: pseudorange [m]; $\rho$: geometric range [m]; $c$: speed of light $\mathbf{3 \times 10^8 \ m/s}$; $\delta t$: clock bias [s] | Basic code-based range observation |
| **E2** | Carrier Phase | $\Phi_r^s = \rho_r^s + c(\delta t_r - \delta t^s) - I_r^s + T_r^s + \lambda N + \varepsilon_\Phi$ | $\Phi$: carrier-phase [cycles]; $\lambda$: wavelength (~**0.19 m** for L1); $N$: integer ambiguity | High-precision phase observation |
| **E3** | Single Difference (Between Receivers) | $\Delta \Phi_{rb}^s = \Phi_r^s - \Phi_b^s$ | Subscripts $r,b$: rover, base; superscript $s$: satellite | Eliminates satellite clock error |
| **E4** | Double Difference (Between Satellites) | $\nabla \Delta \Phi_{rb}^{sj} = \Delta \Phi_{rb}^s - \Delta \Phi_{rb}^j$ | $s, j$: two satellites | Eliminates receiver clock error |
| **E5** | Triple Difference (Between Epochs) | $\delta \nabla \Delta \Phi_{rb}^{sj}(t_{k}, t_{k+1}) = \nabla \Delta \Phi(t_{k+1}) - \nabla \Delta \Phi(t_k)$ | $t_k$: epoch time | Eliminates integer ambiguity; used for cycle-slip detection |
| **E6** | Carrier Wavelength | $\lambda = c / f$ | $f$: carrier frequency [Hz] | For L1: $f = 1575.42 \ \mathrm{MHz} \Rightarrow \lambda \approx 0.190 \ \mathrm{m}$ |
| **E7** | Dilution of Precision | $\mathrm{PDOP}, \mathrm{HDOP}, \mathrm{VDOP}, \mathrm{GDOP}$ | Geometric factor from satellite-receiver geometry | Lower DOP $\Rightarrow$ better accuracy |
| **E8** | Baseline Error Scaling | $\sigma = \sigma_0 + k \cdot b$ | $\sigma_0$: baseline-accuracy floor [cm]; $k$: **1 ppm** coefficient; $b$: baseline [km] | E.g., 1 ppm means +1 cm error per 10 km |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Sign Conventions:** The ionospheric term has **opposite signs** in pseudorange ($+I$) vs carrier-phase ($-I$) equations because ionospheric delay *advances* the carrier phase but *delays* the code. This **dispersive nature** is exploited in **dual-frequency RTK** to estimate $I$ directly.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Robotics

RTK navigation underpins several KTU-relevant robotic applications:

- **Autonomous Tractors & Agricultural Robots** — Row-following at sub-decimeter accuracy (e.g., John Deere AutoTrac™).
- **UAV Surveying & Mapping** — Photogrammetry and LiDAR georeferencing for 3D city models.
- **Autonomous Vehicles** — Lane-level positioning in HD-map-relative localization stacks.
- **Construction Robotics** — Automated earth-moving and machine control with dozer blades and excavators.
- **Search & Rescue Drones** — Precise return-to-home in GPS-denied recovery scenarios.
- **Maritime Robotics** — USV (Unmanned Surface Vehicle) harbor navigation.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Pseudorange to Carrier-Phase Observation

### Step 1: Define the True Geometric Range
The true geometric range $\rho_r^s$ between satellite $s$ and receiver $r$ in the **ECEF (Earth-Centered Earth-Fixed)** frame is given by the Euclidean distance:

$$\rho_r^s = \sqrt{(X^s - X_r)^2 + (Y^s - Y_r)^2 + (Z^s - Z_r)^2}$$

**Explanation:** This is the direct line-of-sight distance. The satellite coordinates $(X^s, Y^s, Z^s)$ are broadcast in the navigation message; the receiver coordinates $(X_r, Y_r, Z_r)$ are unknowns we wish to estimate.

### Step 2: Introduce Receiver and Satellite Clock Biases
The receiver's local clock is not synchronized with GNSS time, introducing a bias $\delta t_r$. Similarly, the satellite's onboard atomic clock drifts by $\delta t^s$. These biases propagate into the range measurement at the speed of light $c$:

$$P_{\mathrm{measured}} = \rho + c \cdot \delta t_r - c \cdot \delta t^s + \text{other errors}$$

**Explanation:** The receiver's clock error **adds** delay to the measurement (positive sign), while the satellite's clock error **subtracts** because the satellite timestamps its transmission earlier than true GNSS time.

### Step 3: Add Atmospheric Delays
The signal traverses the **ionosphere** (60–1000 km altitude, charged particles) and the **troposphere** (0–60 km, neutral atmosphere). Both delay the signal:

$$P_r^s = \rho_r^s + c(\delta t_r - \delta t^s) + I_r^s + T_r^s + \varepsilon_P$$

**Explanation:** The ionospheric delay $I$ is **frequency-dependent (dispersive)**, while the tropospheric delay $T$ is approximately **non-dispersive** at GNSS frequencies. $\varepsilon_P$ lumps together multipath and thermal noise.

### Step 4: Derive the Carrier-Phase Equation
Unlike code pseudorange, the carrier-phase measurement $\Phi$ is in units of **cycles**, not meters. The receiver measures the **fractional phase** at lock-on plus an unknown **integer number of cycles** $N$ (the ambiguity):

$$\Phi_r^s = \frac{\rho_r^s}{\lambda} + f(\delta t_r - \delta t^s) - \frac{I_r^s}{\lambda} + \frac{T_r^s}{\lambda} + N + \varepsilon_\Phi$$

Multiplying through by $\lambda$ to express everything in meters:

$$\lambda \Phi_r^s = \rho_r^s + c(\delta t_r - \delta t^s) - I_r^s + T_r^s + \lambda N + \varepsilon_\Phi$$

**Explanation:** The ionospheric sign **flips** because the carrier wave experiences **phase advance** (negative delay) while the code experiences **group delay** (positive delay) — a fundamental property of dispersive media.

### Step 5: Combine into the Standard Carrier-Phase Form
Replacing $\lambda \Phi$ with the phase range in meters, we obtain the canonical **carrier-phase observation equation**:

$$\Phi_r^s = \rho_r^s + c(\delta t_r - \delta t^s) - I_r^s + T_r^s + \lambda N + \varepsilon_\Phi$$

**Explanation:** This is **Equation E2** from the formula sheet. The integer ambiguity $N$ is the key unknown that RTK must resolve to achieve cm-level accuracy.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Double Differencing (The Heart of RTK)

### Step 1: Single Difference Between Base and Rover
Take the carrier-phase equation at the **rover** $r$ and **base** $b$ tracking the **same satellite** $s$ at the **same epoch** $t$. Subtract:

$$\Delta \Phi_{rb}^s = \Phi_r^s - \Phi_b^s = (\rho_r^s - \rho_b^s) + c(\delta t_r - \delta t_b) - (I_r^s - I_b^s) + (T_r^s - T_b^s) + \lambda(N_r - N_b) + \Delta \varepsilon$$

**Explanation:** The satellite clock error $\delta t^s$ is **identically present** in both equations and therefore **cancels out** — this is the first major benefit of single differencing.

### Step 2: Apply the Short-Baseline Approximation
If the baseline (rover–base distance) is short ($\leq 20$ km), the ionospheric and tropospheric delays from satellite $s$ are nearly identical at both receivers:

$$I_r^s \approx I_b^s, \quad T_r^s \approx T_b^s$$

This gives:

$$\Delta \Phi_{rb}^s \approx (\rho_r^s - \rho_b^s) + c(\delta t_r - \delta t_b) + \lambda(N_r - N_b) + \Delta \varepsilon$$

**Explanation:** The **spatial correlation** of atmospheric errors is the fundamental assumption that makes RTK possible. Over long baselines, this approximation breaks down and **Network RTK (NRTK)** with multiple reference stations is required.

### Step 3: Double Difference Between Two Satellites
Now introduce a second satellite $j$ tracked by both receivers. Form a single difference for $j$ and subtract from the single difference for $s$:

$$\nabla \Delta \Phi_{rb}^{sj} = \Delta \Phi_{rb}^s - \Delta \Phi_{rb}^j$$

Expanding:

$$\nabla \Delta \Phi_{rb}^{sj} = (\rho_r^s - \rho_b^s - \rho_r^j + \rho_b^j) + c(\delta t_r - \delta t_b - \delta t_r + \delta t_b) + \lambda(N_r^s - N_b^s - N_r^j + N_b^j) + \nabla \Delta \varepsilon$$

**Explanation:** The **receiver clock bias** $c \delta t_r$ and $c \delta t_b$ appear with opposite signs and **cancel out** completely. We are left with the **double-difference integer ambiguity** $\nabla \Delta N = (N_r^s - N_b^s) - (N_r^j - N_b^j)$, which is **also an integer** — a critical property that enables efficient resolution.

### Step 4: Final Compact Form
Defining $\Delta \rho^{sj} = (\rho_r^s - \rho_b^s) - (\rho_r^j - \rho_b^j)$, we obtain:

$$\nabla \Delta \Phi_{rb}^{sj} = \Delta \rho^{sj} + \lambda \nabla \Delta N + \nabla \Delta \varepsilon$$

**Explanation:** This is **Equation E4**. The unknowns are now only the **rover coordinates** (hidden inside $\Delta \rho^{sj}$) and the **integer ambiguities** $\nabla \Delta N$. With enough satellites and epochs, the system is solvable.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Integer Ambiguity Resolution (Conceptual)

The double-difference equation contains an **integer** unknown $\nabla \Delta N$. The standard approach is:

### Step 1: Float Solution
Ignore the integer constraint and solve the system using **least-squares** to obtain a real-valued (float) estimate $\hat{\nabla \Delta N}_{\mathrm{float}}$ and its covariance $\mathbf{Q}_{\hat{N}}$.

### Step 2: Integer Search Using LAMBDA
The **LAMBDA method** decorrelates the ambiguities via a **Z-transformation**, performs an integer least-squares search within an ellipsoidal region defined by the covariance, and returns the integer vector $\hat{\nabla \Delta N}_{\mathrm{fixed}}$ that minimizes:

$$\min_{\nabla \Delta N \in \mathbb{Z}^n} \Vert \hat{\nabla \Delta N}_{\mathrm{float}} - \nabla \Delta N \Vert_{\mathbf{Q}_{\hat{N}}}^2$$

### Step 3: Ratio Test for Validation
The **fixed** solution is accepted only if the ratio of the second-best to the best candidate residuals exceeds a threshold (typically **3.0**):

$$\frac{\text{Residual}_{\mathrm{second}}}{\text{Residual}_{\mathrm{best}}} > 3.0$$

**Explanation:** The LAMBDA method, proposed by **Teunissen (1995)**, is the de-facto industry standard. Dual-frequency GNSS enables faster and more reliable ambiguity resolution (often **< 1 second** in motion) compared to **30–60 seconds** for single-frequency RTK.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Positioning Techniques

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compares RTK with related positioning paradigms, frequently asked in KTU comparisons:

| **Parameter** | **Standalone GNSS** | **DGPS (Code-Based)** | **RTK (Carrier-Phase)** | **PPK (Post-Processed Kinematic)** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Accuracy (Horizontal)** | 2–5 m | 0.5–2 m | **1–2 cm** | **1–2 cm** |
| **Real-Time?** | Yes | Yes | **Yes** | No (post-processed) |
| **Base Station Required?** | No | Yes | **Yes** | Yes |
| **Integer Ambiguity Resolution?** | N/A | N/A | **Yes (in real time)** | Yes (post-process) |
| **Latency** | None | < 1 s | **< 1 s** | Minutes to hours |
| **Typical Use Case** | Consumer GPS | Maritime, aviation | **Robotics, surveying** | Aerial mapping, GIS |
| **Cost of Infrastructure** | Low | Medium | **Medium** | Low (no radio needed) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RTK System Architecture (Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **end-to-end functional architecture** of an RTK-enabled robotic system, mapping the data flow from satellites to the robot's motion planner.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sat["GNSS Satellite Constellation"]
        sat1["Satellite S1"]
        sat2["Satellite S2"]
        sat3["Satellite S3"]
        sat4["Satellite S4"]
        sat5["Satellite S5"]
    end

    subgraph Base["Base Station Reference Site"]
        antB["GNSS Antenna Base"]
        rxB["Base Receiver L1 L2"]
        surv["Surveyed Coordinates Known"]
        corr["RTCM Correction Generator"]
    end

    subgraph Comm["Correction Communication Link"]
        radio["Radio Link or NTRIP Cellular"]
    end

    subgraph Rover["Rover Mounted on Robot"]
        antR["GNSS Antenna Rover"]
        rxR["Rover Receiver L1 L2"]
        diff["Differencing Engine"]
        lamb["LAMBDA Ambiguity Resolver"]
        pose["RTK Fixed Position Output X Y Z"]
    end

    subgraph Robot["Robot Motion Planner"]
        ekf["EKF Fusion with IMU"]
        planner["Trajectory Planner"]
        ctrl["Motor Controller"]
    end

    sat1 --> antB
    sat2 --> antB
    sat3 --> antB
    sat4 --> antB
    sat5 --> antB
    antB --> rxB
    surv --> corr
    rxB --> corr
    corr --> radio
    radio --> diff

    sat1 --> antR
    sat2 --> antR
    sat3 --> antR
    sat4 --> antR
    sat5 --> antR
    antR --> rxR
    rxR --> diff
    diff --> lamb
    lamb --> pose
    pose --> ekf
    ekf --> planner
    planner --> ctrl
```

**Explanation of Diagram Flow:**

- The **GNSS satellite constellation** broadcasts ranging signals to **both** the base and rover simultaneously.
- The **base station** uses its pre-surveyed coordinates to compute the difference between its true and GNSS-derived position — this difference is the correction data.
- The **communication link** (radio or NTRIP over cellular) streams the correction data to the rover in RTCM format.
- The **rover's differencing engine** applies the corrections, then the **LAMBDA module** fixes integer ambiguities to produce a cm-accurate pose.
- This pose is **fused with IMU data** in an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) for high-rate localization, then fed to the **trajectory planner** and **motor controller**.

## 4.2 RTK Operational State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NoFix
    NoFix --> SinglePoint: Less than 4 satellites
    SinglePoint --> DGPSFix: Code corrections received
    DGPSFix --> FloatRTK: Carrier phase tracked, ambiguities float
    FloatRTK --> FixedRTK: LAMBDA resolves integers, ratio test passes
    FixedRTK --> FloatRTK: Cycle slip or satellite lost
    FixedRTK --> NoFix: Less than 4 satellites in view
    FixedRTK --> [*]
```

**Explanation of States:**

- **NoFix** — Fewer than 4 satellites visible; no position available.
- **SinglePoint** — Standalone pseudorange solution (~5 m accuracy).
- **DGPSFix** — Code-based differential corrections applied (~1 m accuracy).
- **FloatRTK** — Carrier phase tracked but ambiguities unresolved (~10–30 cm).
- **FixedRTK** — Integer ambiguities successfully resolved (**1–2 cm**).

## 4.3 Pseudorange vs Carrier-Phase Conceptual Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Code["Pseudorange Code Measurement"]
        c1["Satellite broadcasts PRN code"]
        c2["Receiver correlates with replica"]
        c3["Time shift gives range"]
        c4["Accuracy ~ 1 to 3 meters"]
        c1 --> c2 --> c3 --> c4
    end

    subgraph Phase["Carrier Phase Measurement"]
        p1["Satellite broadcasts L1 carrier wave"]
        p2["Receiver measures beat phase"]
        p3["Add unknown integer N"]
        p4["Accuracy ~ 0.1 to 1 millimeter per measurement"]
        p1 --> p2 --> p3 --> p4
    end
```

**Explanation:** Code measurements give an unambiguous but coarse range (1–3 m). Carrier-phase measurements are extremely precise (~mm-level) but ambiguous by an unknown integer number of cycles. RTK's central task is to **resolve the integer ambiguity** to convert mm-precision into cm-absolute accuracy.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between code-based pseudorange and carrier-phase measurements in GNSS. Why is carrier-phase preferred in RTK?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Feature** | **Pseudorange (Code)** | **Carrier-Phase** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Measurement principle | Time shift of PRN code replica | Beat phase of L1/L2 carrier |
| Precision per measurement | ~1–3 m | ~0.1–1 mm |
| Ambiguity | Unambiguous | Integer ambiguity $N$ unknown |
| Suitability for RTK | Limited to DGPS (~1 m) | **Essential for cm-level RTK** |

> **Key Point (1 mark):** Carrier-phase is preferred in RTK because its measurement precision is **~1000× finer** than code, enabling centimeter-level positioning once the integer ambiguity is resolved.
>
> **Trade-off (1 mark):** The integer ambiguity $N$ must be resolved, which is the central computational challenge of RTK.
>
> **Conclusion (1 mark):** RTK exploits the **high precision of carrier-phase** combined with **LAMBDA-based integer resolution** to achieve 1–2 cm accuracy in real time.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define integer ambiguity in RTK. Mention any one algorithm used to resolve it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The integer ambiguity $N$ is the **unknown whole number of carrier cycles** between a satellite and the receiver at the moment of signal lock-on. Since the carrier-phase measurement only provides the fractional part, the total range requires adding $\lambda N$. Resolving $N$ to its true integer value is called **Integer Ambiguity Resolution (IAR)**, and it is the critical step that converts mm-precise phase measurements into cm-absolute positions.

**Algorithm (1 mark):** The **LAMBDA (Least-squares AMBiguity Decorrelation Adjustment)** method, proposed by Teunissen (1995), is the most widely used. It performs a **Z-transformation** to decorrelate ambiguities, then applies integer least-squares search to find the optimal integer vector, validated by the **ratio test**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Question A (a) [7 marks]:** With neat mathematical formulation, derive the **carrier-phase observation equation** for GNSS, clearly identifying each term. Explain the significance of the integer ambiguity term in the context of RTK.

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Geometric Range (1 mark):**
The true line-of-sight range from satellite $s$ to receiver $r$ in ECEF coordinates:

$$\rho_r^s = \sqrt{(X^s - X_r)^2 + (Y^s - Y_r)^2 + (Z^s - Z_r)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Clock Biases (1 mark):**
The receiver clock offset $\delta t_r$ and satellite clock offset $\delta t^s$ contribute a range-equivalent error of $c(\delta t_r - \delta t^s)$, where $c = 3 \times 10^8 \ \mathrm{m/s}$.

**Step 3 — Atmospheric Delays (1.5 marks):**
The signal traverses the ionosphere (delay $I$, dispersive) and troposphere (delay $T$, non-dispersive). The ionosphere **advances the carrier phase** but **delays the code**, so it appears with **opposite signs** in the two equations.

**Step 4 — Carrier-Phase Observation (2 marks):**
Combining all effects and expressing the carrier-phase range in meters:

$$\lambda \Phi_r^s = \rho_r^s + c(\delta t_r - \delta t^s) - I_r^s + T_r^s + \lambda N + \varepsilon_\Phi$$

**Step 5 — Significance of Integer Ambiguity (1.5 marks):**
- $N$ is an **unknown integer** representing whole carrier cycles between lock-on and the current epoch.
- Carrier-phase precision is **~0.1–1 mm** per measurement, but the ambiguity makes the absolute position uncertain by **many wavelengths** (e.g., ~19 cm for L1).
- Resolving $N$ to its true integer is the **central task of RTK**, enabling the leap from mm-precision to cm-accuracy.
- Modern algorithms like **LAMBDA** solve this in **< 1 second** with dual-frequency receivers.

---

**Question A (b) [7 marks]:** Explain the concept of **double differencing** in RTK. Show mathematically how it eliminates satellite and receiver clock errors. What assumption about atmospheric errors is implicit in single-baseline RTK?

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Single Difference Definition (1 mark):**
For two receivers $r$ (rover) and $b$ (base) tracking the same satellite $s$ at the same epoch:

$$\Delta \Phi_{rb}^s = \Phi_r^s - \Phi_b^s$$

**Step 2 — Expand Both Phases (1 mark):**

$$\Delta \Phi_{rb}^s = (\rho_r^s - \rho_b^s) + c(\delta t_r - \delta t_b) - (I_r^s - I_b^s) + (T_r^s - T_b^s) + \lambda(N_r^s - N_b^s) + \Delta \varepsilon$$

The satellite clock error $\delta t^s$ **cancels** because it is common to both terms.

**Step 3 — Double Difference Definition (1 mark):**
Introduce a second satellite $j$ and subtract the two single differences:

$$\nabla \Delta \Phi_{rb}^{sj} = \Delta \Phi_{rb}^s - \Delta \Phi_{rb}^j$$

**Step 4 — Expand Double Difference (2 marks):**

$$\nabla \Delta \Phi_{rb}^{sj} = (\rho_r^s - \rho_b^s - \rho_r^j + \rho_b^j) + \cancel{c(\delta t_r - \delta t_b - \delta t_r + \delta t_b)} + \lambda \nabla \Delta N + \nabla \Delta \varepsilon$$

The receiver clock terms $c \delta t_r$ and $c \delta t_b$ **cancel** completely.

**Step 5 — Final Compact Form (1 mark):**

$$\nabla \Delta \Phi_{rb}^{sj} = \Delta \rho^{sj} + \lambda \nabla \Delta N + \nabla \Delta \varepsilon$$

**Step 6 — Atmospheric Assumption (1 mark):**
Single-baseline RTK assumes **short baseline** ($\leq 20$ km), so that **$I_r^s \approx I_b^s$ and $T_r^s \approx T_b^s$** — i.e., the ionospheric and tropospheric delays are **spatially correlated** and thus eliminated by differencing. For longer baselines, **Network RTK (NRTK)** with atmospheric modeling is required.

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**Question B (a) [7 marks]:** Describe the **architecture of an RTK system** with reference to: (i) base station, (ii) rover, (iii) communication link, and (iv) correction message format. Include a labeled block diagram.

#### Model Solution:

**(i) Base Station (2 marks):**
- Located at a **precisely surveyed** point with known ECEF coordinates $(X_b, Y_b, Z_b)$.
- Equipped with a **geodetic-grade dual-frequency GNSS receiver** and a **choke-ring antenna** to minimize multipath.
- Computes real-time corrections by comparing its known position to the GNSS-derived position.
- Mounted on a **stable monument** (e.g., rooftop pillar) to prevent vibration-induced errors.

**(ii) Rover (2 marks):**
- Mounted on the **robot platform** (UGV, UAV, AGV).
- Uses a **dual-frequency GNSS receiver** sharing at least **5 common satellites** with the base.
- Runs the **differencing engine** and **LAMBDA** algorithm to fix integer ambiguities.
- Outputs position at **1–20 Hz** with cm-level accuracy.

**(iii) Communication Link (1.5 marks):**
- **Radio link** (UHF/VHF, 433 MHz or 900 MHz): low-latency, range up to ~10 km, license-free in many regions.
- **NTRIP (Networked Transport of RTCM via Internet Protocol)**: uses cellular 4G/5G, range effectively global, ideal for fleet robotics.
- **Wi-Fi/Bluetooth**: for short-range or tethered operation.

**(iv) Correction Message Format (1.5 marks):**
- **RTCM SC-104** is the international standard.
- Key message types: **RTCM 1004** (extended L1/L2 observations), **RTCM 1019** (GPS ephemeris), **RTCM 1033** (base station info), **RTCM 1074/1094** (MSM — Multiple Signal Messages for modern multi-constellation, multi-frequency RTK).
- Designed for **streaming** at typical rates of 1–10 Hz.

**Block Diagram (included in SECTION 4.1 above).**

---

**Question B (b) [7 marks]:** Compare **RTK** with **PPK (Post-Processed Kinematic)** positioning. Under what robotic scenarios would you prefer PPK over RTK, and why? Discuss the role of **dual-frequency GNSS** in accelerating ambiguity resolution.

#### Model Solution:

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **RTK** | **PPK** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Correction delivery | Real-time | Post-mission |
| Latency | < 1 s | Minutes to hours |
| Computational load | Onboard (rover) | Office PC (after survey) |
| Radio/Internet needed | **Yes** | No |
| Robustness to link loss | Poor — degrades to float | **High** — corrections applied later |
| Accuracy | 1–2 cm | 1–2 cm |

**Scenarios Preferring PPK (2 marks):**
- **Aerial mapping drones** — loss of radio link during flight is common; PPK processes corrections on the ground later.
- **Surveying in remote areas** without cellular coverage.
- **Maritime or polar robotics** where real-time communication is impractical.
- **High-precision GIS data collection** where real-time output is not required.

**Role of Dual-Frequency GNSS (2 marks):**
- The **ionospheric delay $I$** is dispersive: it depends on frequency as $I \propto 1/f^2$.
- By tracking **two frequencies** (L1 = 1575.42 MHz, L2 = 1227.60 MHz), the **ionospheric-free linear combination**:

$$\Phi_{\mathrm{IF}} = \frac{f_1^2 \Phi_1 - f_2^2 \Phi_2}{f_1^2 - f_2^2}$$

removes ~99.9% of the ionospheric error, enabling **On-The-Fly (OTF)** ambiguity resolution in **< 1 second**, even during motion. Single-frequency RTK may require **30–60 s** of static initialization.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the sign flip of ionospheric term** between code ($+I$) and carrier-phase ($-I$) equations. This costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing single, double, and triple differencing.** Remember:
>    - **Single difference** = between receivers (eliminates **satellite clock**).
>    - **Double difference** = between satellites (eliminates **receiver clock**).
>    - **Triple difference** = between epochs (eliminates **integer ambiguity**, used for cycle-slip detection).
> 3. **Stating RTK accuracy as "1 mm" or "sub-mm"** — the realistic range is **1–2 cm horizontal, 2–3 cm vertical** for single-base RTK.
> 4. **Omitting the "real-time" qualifier** in the definition of RTK; PPK is a related but distinct technique.
> 5. **Failing to mention LAMBDA** when asked about ambiguity resolution — it is the **industry-standard algorithm** expected in any KTU answer.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — RTK Navigation**

- ✅ **Definition:** RTK = carrier-phase GNSS + real-time corrections from a base station → **1–2 cm accuracy**.
- ✅ **Two key components:** (1) **Base station** at known coordinates; (2) **Rover** on the robot; (3) **Real-time link** (radio/NTRIP).
- ✅ **Carrier-phase precision** is ~0.1–1 mm per measurement, **~1000× finer than code pseudorange**.
- ✅ **Integer ambiguity $N$** is the unknown whole number of carrier cycles — RTK's central challenge.
- ✅ **LAMBDA algorithm** (Teunissen, 1995) is the de-facto standard for ambiguity resolution.
- ✅ **Single difference** eliminates **satellite clock error**.
- ✅ **Double difference** eliminates **receiver clock error** and reduces atmospheric errors.
- ✅ **Triple difference** eliminates **integer ambiguity** and is used for **cycle-slip detection**.
- ✅ **Short-baseline assumption** ($b \leq 20$ km) → spatial correlation of atmospheric errors.
- ✅ **Baseline error scaling:** **1 ppm** means **+1 cm error per 10 km** of baseline length.
- ✅ **Dual-frequency GNSS** enables ionospheric-free combination → faster OTF ambiguity resolution (**< 1 s**).
- ✅ **RTCM SC-104** is the standard correction message format.
- ✅ **HDOP, PDOP, GDOP** quantify satellite geometry; lower is better (HDOP < 2 is excellent).
- ✅ **RTK vs PPK:** RTK is real-time but link-dependent; PPK is post-processed but more robust to link loss.
- ✅ **Robotics applications:** AGVs, UAV surveying, agricultural robotics, autonomous vehicles, USVs.
- ✅ **Fusion with IMU** in an EKF provides high-rate, cm-accurate localization for motion planning.
- ✅ **Network RTK (NRTK)** extends baseline to 50–100 km using multiple reference stations and atmospheric models.
- ✅ **Carrier wavelength** for L1: $\lambda \approx 0.190 \ \mathrm{m}$; for L2: $\lambda \approx 0.244 \ \mathrm{m}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain RTK," structure your answer in **three layers** — (1) **why** standalone GNSS is insufficient (errors listed above), (2) **how** RTK solves it (base + differencing + ambiguity resolution), and (3) **what** accuracy/results it delivers. This layered approach consistently scores full marks under KTU's outcome-based evaluation rubric.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT833 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** GNSS Threats

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GNSS Threats** are defined as any intentional, unintentional, or environmental phenomena that degrade the **integrity**, **availability**, **continuity**, **accuracy**, or **authentication** of Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) signals used for Position, Navigation, and Timing (PNT) services in a robotic or autonomous platform.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context of *Robot Motion Planning*, GNSS threats are critical because outdoor mobile robots, autonomous vehicles, UAVs (drones), and agricultural robots rely on GNSS-derived pose estimates. A compromised GNSS signal can inject **catastrophic position errors** (from a few metres to kilometres), directly violating the safety and accuracy constraints of motion planners.

The four pillars of GNSS service quality that are vulnerable to threats are:

| Pillar | Definition | Threat Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | Closeness of estimated position to true position | Multipath, atmospheric bias |
| **Integrity** | Trustworthiness of the navigation solution; ability to alert the user | Spoofing, satellite clock anomaly |
| **Availability** | Fraction of time the service is usable | Jamming, signal obstruction |
| **Continuity** | Ability to complete an operation without interruption | Brief jamming, interference |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board examinations, students are expected to explicitly identify **which pillar** a given threat violates — a very common 3-mark question.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are **blindfolded in a large stadium** and you must reach the centre of the field. Five friends are standing at the corners of the stadium, each shouting the distance to you ("You are 30 m from me!", "You are 45 m from me!", etc.). By triangulating these shouts, you can pinpoint your position.

**GNSS works exactly this way**: satellites (your friends) continuously broadcast, and your robot's receiver uses the time-of-flight to compute the **pseudorange** to each satellite, then triangulates position via multilateration.

Now the **threats** are the things that mess up this game:

- **Jamming** → Someone across the stadium is playing loud music, drowning out your friends' voices. You cannot hear them at all.
- **Spoofing** → An impersonator pretends to be your friend, shouting fake distances ("You are 5 m from me") to lure you to the wrong spot. This is the most dangerous attack.
- **Multipath** → The stadium has shiny glass walls. Your friends' voices bounce, and you hear *both* the direct shout and the echo, getting confused about the true distance.
- **Atmospheric errors** → A smoky haze distorts the speed of sound, making every friend's voice arrive slightly late.
- **Interference** → Other unrelated noise (cameras flashing, crowd chatter) raises the noise floor, making it harder to extract the message.
- **Meaconing** → A malicious actor *records* your friends' voices and *replays* them with a delay, fooling the receiver about both distance and time.
- **Satellite/Receiver failure** → One of your friends suddenly lies, or their voice goes silent, breaking the geometry of your solution.

> [!TIP]
> A single real-world event can be a combination. For instance, solar storms cause **ionospheric scintillation** (atmospheric) that simultaneously degrades accuracy *and* availability — a compound threat.

---

## 1.3 Taxonomy of GNSS Threats (KTU High-Yield Classification)

For examination purposes, threats are categorised along **two axes**: *intentionality* and *origin*.

```
GNSS THREATS
├── 1. Intentional Threats (Hostile)
│   ├── 1.1 Jamming (Suppression)
│   │   ├── Narrowband / Wideband
│   │   ├── Continuous vs. Pulsed
│   │   └── Smart / Reactive Jamming
│   ├── 1.2 Spoofing (Deception)
│   │   ├── Simulated (signal-generator) spoofing
│   │   ├── Meaconing (record-and-replay)
│   │   └── Lift-off / Carry-off attacks
│   └── 1.3 Data-Injection / Cyber-attack
│       └── False ephemeris, almanac manipulation
│
├── 2. Unintentional Threats
│   ├── 2.1 RFI (Radio Frequency Interference)
│   │   ├── In-band (e.g., DME/TACAN, mobile comms harmonics)
│   │   └── Out-of-band (L1/L2/L5 adjacents)
│   ├── 2.2 Inter-system / Intra-system interference
│   └── 2.3 Receiver hardware faults
│
└── 3. Environmental / Natural Threats
    ├── 3.1 Ionospheric delays & scintillation
    ├── 3.2 Tropospheric delays
    ├── 3.3 Multipath (urban canyon, foliage, water)
    ├── 3.4 Signal blockage & shadowing
    └── 3.5 Space-weather events (solar flares, geomagnetic storms)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU board examiner expects the candidate to know that **Spoofing** is the *most insidious* threat because it can fool an integrity-monitoring system if the attacker is sophisticated, whereas **jamming** is loud, detectable, and often leads to loss-of-lock (which at least triggers a fail-safe).

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used Throughout the Chapter

- **C/N₀** — Carrier-to-Noise Density ratio (dB-Hz). Healthy GNSS C/N₀: **45–50 dB-Hz**. Degraded: **< 40 dB-Hz**.
- **J/S** — Jamming-to-Signal power ratio (dB).
- **P(Y)/C/A code** — Precision (encrypted) and Coarse/Acquisition codes of GPS.
- **NMA** — Navigation Message Authentication (Galileo OS-NMA, GPS CHIMERA).
- **DOP** — Dilution of Precision (geometric sensitivity multiplier).
- **RAIM** — Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring.
- **AOD** — Age of Data.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The GNSS Signal Model Under Threat

A clean received GNSS signal (in the baseband-equivalent model) is:

$$r(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} A_i \, D_i(t) \, C_i(t) \, \cos(2\pi f_i t + \phi_i) + n(t)$$

where:
- $A_i$ = amplitude of $i$-th satellite
- $D_i(t)$ = navigation data bits (50 bps)
- $C_i(t)$ = PRN spreading code (C/A: 1.023 Mcps; P(Y): 10.23 Mcps)
- $f_i$ = carrier frequency (L1 = 1575.42 MHz, L2 = 1227.60 MHz, L5 = 1176.45 MHz)
- $n(t)$ = AWGN with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$

**When a threat is introduced**, the model becomes:

$$r(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} A_i D_i C_i \cos(2\pi f_i t + \phi_i) + J(t) + S(t) + M(t) + n(t)$$

where $J(t)$ = jammer, $S(t)$ = spoofer, $M(t)$ = multipath, each corrupting a different property of the signal.

---

## 2.2 Jamming — Mechanism and Effect

**Definition**: Jamming is the deliberate emission of RF energy in the GNSS band to overwhelm the receiver's correlator, preventing signal acquisition or tracking.

**Mechanism in steps**:
1. Jammer radiates in L1, L2, or L5 band with high EIRP.
2. Receiver's front-end sees the jammer as a raised noise floor.
3. Effective $N_0$ becomes $N_0 + N_J$.
4. Effective C/N₀ drops below acquisition threshold (~37 dB-Hz for cold start).
5. Receiver loses lock, position solution unavailable.

> [!NOTE]
> A **consumer-grade jammer** with only **100 mW EIRP** can defeat a commercial GPS receiver at ranges of **10–15 km**, which is why jammers are illegal in most jurisdictions.

### 2.2.1 Jamming-to-Signal Ratio (J/S) and C/N₀ Degradation

$$J/S \, (\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_J}{P_S}\right)$$

When jamming is present, the effective C/N₀ becomes:

$$\left(\frac{C}{N_0}\right)_{\text{eff}} = \frac{C/N_0}{1 + 10^{J/S \, / \, 10}}$$

In decibels:

$$\left(\frac{C}{N_0}\right)_{\text{eff, dB}} = (C/N_0)_{\text{dB}} - 10 \log_{10}\!\left(1 + 10^{J/S \, / \, 10}\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> This is the **most-asked formula** in KTU module-4 derivations. Memorise it.

**Rule of thumb**: When $J/S \gg 0$ dB, the jammer dominates and $(C/N_0)_{\text{eff}} \approx (C/N_0)_{\text{dB}} - (J/S)$ dB.

### 2.2.2 Jammer Categories

| Jammer Type | Bandwidth | Threat Level | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Narrowband (NB)** | < 1 MHz | Low to Medium | Defeated by notch filters |
| **Wideband (WB)** | 1–20 MHz | Medium to High | Affects whole GNSS band |
| **Barrage** | > 20 MHz | High | Hard to filter |
| **Pulsed** | Time-domain | High | Causes intermittent loss of lock |
| **Smart / Reactive** | Adaptive | Critical | Listens for GNSS, transmits only when victim active |

---

## 2.3 Spoofing — Mechanism and Effect

**Definition**: Spoofing is the broadcast of **synthetic GNSS signals** structured to cause the target receiver to compute a *false but plausible* position, velocity, and time (PVT).

**The three sub-types**:

1. **Simulator Spoofing**: A SDR (Software Defined Radio) like a USRP generates fake signals aligned in code, carrier, and navigation message.
2. **Meaconing**: A receiver records genuine GNSS signals and replays them after a delay. The victim's receiver reports a *valid* signal but at the *wrong* position (translated by attacker).
3. **Lift-off / Carry-off Attack**: Spoofer gradually pulls the receiver's reported position away from the true one at a rate below the receiver's tracking-loop bandwidth.

> [!WARNING]
> A naive RAIM system will *not* detect a sophisticated spoofer because the false signals satisfy inter-channel consistency. Detection requires **multi-antenna**, **cryptographic (NMA)**, or **IMU-aided** cross-checks.

### 2.3.1 Spoofing Detection Metrics

The **effective C/N₀ anomaly** is a key indicator:

$$\Delta(C/N_0) = (C/N_0)_{\text{measured}} - (C/N_0)_{\text{expected}}$$

A sudden **increase** in C/N₀ or a sudden **change** in its distribution (Kullback–Leibler divergence from baseline) signals potential spoofing.

**Code–carrier coherence** discriminator:

$$\Delta \rho = \rho_{\text{code}} - \rho_{\text{carrier}}$$

Under clean conditions, $\Delta \rho$ is small. Under spoofing, the code and carrier branches of the spoofer may diverge briefly (during hand-over), producing a **spike** in $\Delta \rho$.

---

## 2.4 Multipath Error Envelope

Multipath occurs when the direct line-of-sight signal combines with one or more delayed echoes from reflectors.

The **pseudorange multipath error envelope** for early–late correlators with chip spacing $d$ (in chips) is bounded by:

$$|\epsilon_{\text{MP}}| \le \frac{c \, \tau}{2} \cdot \frac{1 - d}{\text{num\_corr}} \quad \text{[metres]}$$

A more rigorous bound (Ray, 2000) for the multipath error in code-phase:

$$\epsilon_{\text{MP}}(\tau) \le \frac{c \, \tau}{2} \cdot \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi d}{1}\right) \cdot \frac{\alpha}{1 + \alpha}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = multipath delay (seconds)
- $d$ = correlator spacing (chips)
- $\alpha$ = multipath-to-direct amplitude ratio

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The envelope shows that multipath error is **bounded** — it never grows indefinitely with delay. Maximum error occurs at $\tau \approx 0.5$ chip and equals roughly **150 m** for the C/A code (1 chip ≈ 293 m). For P(Y) code (1 chip ≈ 29.3 m), maximum is ~15 m.

---

## 2.5 Ionospheric Error Model

The ionosphere, a dispersive medium, delays GNSS signals by:

$$I = \frac{40.3 \cdot \text{TEC}}{f^2} \quad \text{[metres]}$$

where TEC = Total Electron Content (TECU), $1\,\text{TECU} = 10^{16}\,\text{el/m}^2$.

**Differential GNSS** cancels most of this because two receivers see the **same** ionospheric delay if the baseline is short. **Dual-frequency** receivers cancel it analytically using the *ionosphere-free combination*:

$$P_{\text{IF}} = \frac{f_1^2 P_1 - f_2^2 P_2}{f_1^2 - f_2^2}$$

**Scintillation** is rapid TEC fluctuation causing rapid phase/fade — characterised by the **S4 index** (amplitude) and **$\sigma_\phi$ index** (phase).

---

## 2.6 Interference and Adjacent-Band Threats

Adjacent-band interference is quantified via **ACLR (Adjacent Channel Leakage Ratio)** of the interferer and the receiver's **ACS (Adjacent Channel Selectivity)**. A non-GNSS signal near L1 can be problematic when:

$$\text{Interference Margin} = \text{ACLR}_{\text{tx}} - \text{ACS}_{\text{rx}} - \text{Safety Margin}$$

If this margin is positive, the interferer *will* degrade GNSS.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the **single most important revision block** for KTU module-4.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Carrier-to-noise density | $C/N_0$ | dB-Hz, healthy 45–50 |
| 2 | Jammer degradation | $(C/N_0)_{\text{eff}} = (C/N_0) - 10\log_{10}(1+10^{J/S/10})$ | dB |
| 3 | J/S ratio | $J/S = 10\log_{10}(P_J/P_S)$ | dB |
| 4 | Ionospheric delay | $I = 40.3 \cdot \text{TEC}/f^2$ | m |
| 5 | Ion-free pseudorange | $P_{\text{IF}} = (f_1^2 P_1 - f_2^2 P_2)/(f_1^2 - f_2^2)$ | m |
| 6 | Tropospheric delay (Saastamoinen) | $T_d = 0.002277 / \cos z (P + 1225/T + 0.05)\exp(-1.5 h_H)$ | m |
| 7 | Pseudorange measurement | $\rho = r + c(\delta t_u - \delta t_s) + I + T + \epsilon$ | m |
| 8 | Position error bound | $\sigma_P = \text{DOP} \cdot \sigma_{\text{UERE}}$ | m |
| 9 | UERE (User Equivalent Range Error) | $\sigma_{\text{UERE}}^2 = \sigma_{\text{eph}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{iono}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{tropo}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{mp}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{rcvr}}^2$ | m |
| 10 | GDOP | $\sqrt{\text{trace}(H^T H)^{-1}}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Scintillation S4 | $S_4 = \sqrt{\langle I^2 \rangle - \langle I \rangle^2}/\langle I \rangle$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Multipath error envelope | $\epsilon_{\text{MP}} \le (c \tau/2)\sin(\pi d)\alpha/(1+\alpha)$ | m |
| 13 | Pseudorange rate | $\dot{\rho} = -\mathbf{e} \cdot (\mathbf{v}_r - \mathbf{v}_s) + c(\dot{\delta t}_u - \dot{\delta t}_s)$ | m/s |
| 14 | Carrier-phase measurement | $\phi = r/\lambda + N + f(\delta t_u - \delta t_s) - I/\lambda + \epsilon$ | cycles |
| 15 | NMA signature verification | $\text{EC} = \text{SHA-256}(M \vert K_{\text{pub}})$ | bit field |
| 16 | RAIM protection level | $HPL = K \cdot \sigma_{\text{slope,max}}$ | m |
| 17 | INS-GNSS Kalman innovation | $r_k = z_k - H \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1}$ | innovation vector |
| 18 | CRLB on pseudorange | $\text{Var}(\hat{\tau}) \ge 1/(2 \beta^2 \cdot C/N_0 \cdot T)$ | s² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the markdown above, every absolute-value / magnitude use is rendered as plain text (e.g., $\sigma_{\text{UERE}}^2$) to avoid breaking the table syntax. The vertical pipe character is **never** used inside the table cells.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Why GNSS-threat knowledge matters |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Must detect lane-level spoofing; spoofing a Tesla with 50 m error could cause a fatal lane change. |
| **UAV / Drone Swarms** | Jamming a swarm's leader drops all followers; mitigation uses **CRPA antennas** and **vision/INS fusion**. |
| **Precision Agriculture** | Multipath from crops/cansopies causes row misalignment of tractor GPS-guided steering. |
| **Maritime Robotics** | Spoofing of a ship's GNSS can cause groundings; IMSO mandates spoofing alarms. |
| **Space Robotics / LEO** | Space-weather scintillation disrupts GNSS-based orbit determination. |
| **Defence Robotics** | Anti-jam receivers (SAASM, M-Code) are mandatory. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Jamming-Induced C/N₀ Degradation

**Starting point**: Total noise power spectral density seen by the receiver is the sum of thermal noise and jammer equivalent noise density:

$$N_{0,\text{total}} = N_0 + N_J$$

The jammer's effective noise density is the jammer power divided by its (one-sided) bandwidth:

$$N_J = \frac{P_J}{B_J}$$

**Step 1** — Express the J/S ratio in terms of $C/N_0$ and $N_J$:

$$\frac{J}{S} = \frac{P_J}{P_S} = \frac{N_J \, B_J}{C} = \frac{N_J \, B_J}{C/N_0 \cdot N_0} \cdot \frac{1}{N_0/N_0}$$

Rewriting:

$$\frac{J}{S} = \frac{N_J \cdot B_J}{C} = \frac{N_J}{N_0} \cdot \frac{N_0 \cdot B_J}{C}$$

**Step 2** — Use the fact that for a clean signal $C/N_0$ is measured in dB-Hz, and the equivalent $N_J$ in dBW/Hz:

$$10^{J/S/10} = \frac{N_J}{C} \cdot B_J$$

**Step 3** — Form the effective carrier-to-noise ratio:

$$\left(\frac{C}{N_{0,\text{total}}}\right)_{\text{eff}} = \frac{C}{N_0 + N_J} = \frac{C/N_0}{1 + N_J/N_0}$$

**Step 4** — Substitute $N_J/N_0 = (J/S) \cdot (C/(N_0 B_J)) \cdot (N_0/C) = (J/S) \cdot (1 / (C/N_0 \cdot B_J/N_0))$ which simplifies. For simplicity, if we *define* the effective J/S after the receiver's matched filter bandwidth $B_n$:

$$\left(\frac{C}{N_0}\right)_{\text{eff}} = \frac{C/N_0}{1 + \frac{B_n}{C/N_0} \cdot 10^{J/S/10}}$$

A more direct, commonly-quoted form in textbooks:

$$\left(\frac{C}{N_0}\right)_{\text{eff}} = \frac{C/N_0}{1 + 10^{J/S/10}}$$

**Step 5** — Convert to dB (this is the final form used in KTU problems):

$$\left(\frac{C}{N_0}\right)_{\text{eff,dB}} = (C/N_0)_{\text{dB}} - 10\log_{10}\!\left(1 + 10^{J/S/10}\right)$$

**Numerical sanity check** (KTU typical):
- $C/N_0 = 45$ dB-Hz, $J/S = 30$ dB.
- $10^{30/10} = 1000$.
- $1 + 1000 = 1001$.
- $10\log_{10}(1001) = 30.004$ dB.
- Effective C/N₀ = 45 − 30.004 = **14.996 dB-Hz** — far below the 37 dB-Hz acquisition threshold; receiver loses lock.

> [!TIP]
> This shows why even a *modest* jammer (J/S = 30 dB) is devastating: a 30 dB jammer is 1000× the signal power.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Multipath Error Envelope (Code-Phase)

**Setup**: A direct signal with amplitude $A$ and a single multipath echo with amplitude $\alpha A$ and relative code delay $\tau$ (chips). Correlator spacing $d$ chips (early–late).

**Step 1** — Autocorrelation of the C/A code, ideal triangular, peaks at $\pm 1$ chip. The early-late discriminator output (proportional to code-phase error) is:

$$D(\epsilon) = R(\epsilon - d/2) - R(\epsilon + d/2)$$

where $R(\cdot)$ is the autocorrelation function.

**Step 2** — In the presence of multipath, the composite correlator output is:

$$R_{\text{comp}}(\epsilon) = R(\epsilon) + \alpha R(\epsilon - \tau)\cos(\Delta\phi)$$

where $\Delta\phi$ is the relative RF phase between direct and multipath (worst case = $\pi$, in-phase = 0).

**Step 3** — The discriminator zero-crossing shift (the multipath error) satisfies:

$$R(\epsilon - d/2) + \alpha R(\epsilon - d/2 - \tau) = R(\epsilon + d/2) + \alpha R(\epsilon + d/2 - \tau)$$

**Step 4** — For small $\tau$ and small $d$, linearising $R$ around its peak (slope = 2 per chip for C/A), the error is:

$$\epsilon_{\text{MP}} \approx \frac{\alpha \tau \cos(\Delta\phi)}{1 + \alpha \cos(\Delta\phi)} \cdot \frac{1 - d}{2} \quad \text{[chips]}$$

**Step 5** — Converting to metres (1 chip of C/A = 293.05 m) and bounding by worst-case $\cos(\Delta\phi) = 1$:

$$|\epsilon_{\text{MP}}| \le \frac{\alpha \tau (1 - d)}{2(1 + \alpha)} \quad \text{[chips]}$$

In metres, with $\tau$ in seconds:

$$\boxed{\;|\epsilon_{\text{MP}}| \le \frac{c \, \tau \, \alpha \, (1-d)}{2(1+\alpha)} \quad \text{[metres]}\;}$$

For C/A code at chip rate 1.023 Mcps, the maximum is reached near $\tau = 0.5$ chip (~146.5 m range offset) and equals approximately **0.5 chip × (1−d) / 2** in chips, i.e. ~73 m for $d=0.1$ chip — explaining why **narrow-correlator** (small $d$) receivers outperform wide ones.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spoofing-Induced Position Error (Worst-Case Geometry)

**Step 1** — Linearised measurement equation in ECEF frame:

$$\delta \mathbf{z} = \mathbf{G} \delta \mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$$

where $\delta \mathbf{z}$ is $n \times 1$ measurement residual (typically $n=8$ satellites), $\mathbf{G}$ is the geometry matrix (lines-of-sight), and $\delta \mathbf{x}$ is the 4-D state correction (X, Y, Z, clock).

**Step 2** — Least-squares solution:

$$\delta \hat{\mathbf{x}} = (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{G})^{-1} \mathbf{G}^T \delta \mathbf{z}$$

**Step 3** — Suppose a spoofer injects a vector $\delta \mathbf{z}_s$ in the *same direction* as the first column of $\mathbf{G}$ (i.e. along the X-axis). Then:

$$\delta \hat{\mathbf{x}} = (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{G})^{-1} \mathbf{G}^T \delta \mathbf{z}_s = \frac{\delta z_{s,x}}{(\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{G})^{-1}_{11}} \, \mathbf{e}_x$$

**Step 4** — Covariance of position estimate under spoofing:

$$\mathbf{P}_{\text{spoof}} = (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{G})^{-1} \mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{R}_s \mathbf{G} (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{G})^{-1}$$

where $\mathbf{R}_s$ is the spoofing-bias covariance.

**Step 5** — Worst-case error magnitude:

$$\|\delta \mathbf{x}_{\text{worst}}\| = \sigma_{\text{PDOP}} \cdot \|\delta \mathbf{z}_s\|$$

This is the **engineering justification** for PDOP/HDOP monitoring — even a *small* spoofing bias multiplied by a large DOP yields a large position error.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU 14-mark question may ask to derive the position error under spoofing. The key insight is that the *bias in pseudorange* maps to *position bias* scaled by **DOP**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — CRPA Anti-Jam Array Gain

A **Controlled Reception Pattern Antenna (CRPA)** of $M$ elements combined with adaptive beamforming has array gain against a single jammer:

$$G_{\text{array}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{M}{1}\right) = 10 \log_{10} M \quad \text{dB}$$

For $M = 7$ elements (typical CRPA), $G_{\text{array}} = 10 \log_{10}(7) = 8.45$ dB — meaning the J/S is *reduced* by 8.45 dB, bringing the receiver back into healthy operation.

**Step-by-step**:
1. Each element receives the jammer with same amplitude.
2. Beamformer forms null toward jammer direction.
3. Signal is received from above with constructive interference (gain $\propto M$).
4. Jammer is suppressed by factor $1/(M-1)$ in the null.
5. Net array gain = $M/(M-1) \approx 1$ for $M \gg 1$ **per element** in jammer direction, but signal direction sees $M$-fold gain, leading to $\sim 10\log_{10}M$ overall improvement.

> [!TIP]
> This is why **CRPA + M-Code** receivers can operate even in contested RF environments.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Ionospheric Delay and Dual-Frequency Cancellation

**Step 1** — Group delay for L1 frequency $f_1$:

$$I_1 = \frac{40.3 \, \text{TEC}}{f_1^2}$$

**Step 2** — Similarly for $f_2$:

$$I_2 = \frac{40.3 \, \text{TEC}}{f_2^2}$$

**Step 3** — Pseudorange measurements (ignoring non-dispersive terms):

$$P_1 = \rho + I_1 + \epsilon_1, \quad P_2 = \rho + I_2 + \epsilon_2$$

**Step 4** — Solve for ionosphere-free pseudorange and ionospheric delay. Multiplying $P_1$ by $f_1^2$ and $P_2$ by $f_2^2$:

$$f_1^2 P_1 = f_1^2 \rho + 40.3 \, \text{TEC} + f_1^2 \epsilon_1$$
$$f_2^2 P_2 = f_2^2 \rho + 40.3 \, \text{TEC} + f_2^2 \epsilon_2$$

**Step 5** — Subtract:

$$f_1^2 P_1 - f_2^2 P_2 = (f_1^2 - f_2^2) \rho + f_1^2 \epsilon_1 - f_2^2 \epsilon_2$$

**Step 6** — Solve for $\rho$:

$$\rho = \frac{f_1^2 P_1 - f_2^2 P_2}{f_1^2 - f_2^2} + \text{noise correction}$$

This is the **ionosphere-free combination** (LC), used by PPP and SBAS reference stations.

> [!NOTE]
> The trade-off is **noise amplification** by a factor $f_1^2 / (f_1^2 - f_2^2)$ — for GPS L1/L2, this is **~2.6** — so the LC pseudorange is ~2.6× noisier than the raw L1 pseudorange.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — RAIM Protection Level

**Step 1** — Detection statistic under $H_0$ (no fault):

$$T = \mathbf{r}^T \mathbf{P}^{-1} \mathbf{r} \sim \chi^2_{n-4}$$

where $\mathbf{r}$ is the residual vector and $\mathbf{P}$ the covariance.

**Step 2** — Threshold for false alarm $P_{FA}$:

$$T_{\text{th}} = \chi^2_{n-4, \, 1 - P_{FA}}^{-1}$$

**Step 3** — Slope corresponding to the worst-case satellite in direction $\mathbf{u}_i$:

$$s_i = \sqrt{(\mathbf{A}_i^T (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{G})^{-1} \mathbf{A}_i)}$$

where $\mathbf{A}_i$ is the i-th row of $\mathbf{G}$.

**Step 4** — Horizontal Protection Level (HPL):

$$HPL = K_{\text{HPL}} \cdot \max_i(s_i) \cdot \sigma_{\text{UERE}}$$

where $K_{\text{HPL}}$ corresponds to the integrity budget (e.g. 6.18 for $10^{-7}$/hr).

> [!WARNING]
> Under **spoofing**, residuals can be *artificially small* (because the spoofer's signals are self-consistent), causing RAIM to *miss* the fault. This is the **fundamental limitation of single-antenna RAIM against spoofing**.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between jamming and spoofing in GNSS. Which is considered more dangerous for a robotic platform, and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — each bullet ≈ 1 mark):**
- **Jamming** is the *suppression* of GNSS signals by a high-power RF interferer in the GNSS band, leading to *loss of lock* and unavailability of position. It is loud, easy to detect via C/N₀ drop, and triggers fail-safes.
- **Spoofing** is the *deception* of a receiver by feeding it fabricated signals that mimic genuine satellites, leading to *false but plausible* PVT (position, velocity, time). The receiver continues to report a "valid" solution.
- Spoofing is **more dangerous** because it is *silent* — the receiver's integrity monitor may not detect it, so the robot accepts a wrong position and acts on it, with potential catastrophic consequences (e.g., lane departure, collision).

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners award full 3 marks only if the student mentions *silent failure* and *integrity* explicitly.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: List any three mitigation techniques against GNSS spoofing. Briefly explain one.**

**Model Answer:**
- **NMA / OS-NMA** (Navigation Message Authentication) — cryptographic signature embedded in the navigation message.
- **Multi-antenna / CRPA** — direction-of-arrival validation.
- **INS / IMU cross-check** — physics-based consistency.
- **Signal Quality Monitoring (SQM)** — detect distortion in correlation peak.
- **Carrier-phase / Code-phase consistency** check.

**Explanation of NMA (1 mark):** Galileo OS-NMA uses a TESLA-style chain: the public key authenticates past messages via hash chains, so the receiver can verify that the navigation data came from the satellite, not a spoofer.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a)** Derive the expression for the effective carrier-to-noise density ratio $(C/N_0)_{\text{eff}}$ in the presence of a jamming signal of J/S ratio. Plot the relationship qualitatively and identify the acquisition threshold. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a CRPA-based anti-jam GNSS receiver for a robotic platform. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the noise model. Total noise density seen by the receiver is the sum of thermal and jammer equivalent noise:
$$N_{0,\text{total}} = N_0 + N_J$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Define the J/S ratio. J/S is the ratio of jammer power in receiver bandwidth to signal power:
$$\frac{J}{S} = \frac{P_J}{P_S}, \quad J/S_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}(P_J/P_S)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Write the effective C/N₀ expression:
$$\left(\frac{C}{N_0}\right)_{\text{eff}} = \frac{C/N_0}{1 + 10^{J/S/10}}$$

In dB:
$$\left(\frac{C}{N_0}\right)_{\text{eff, dB}} = (C/N_0)_{\text{dB}} - 10\log_{10}\!\left(1 + 10^{J/S/10}\right)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Asymptotes:
- When $J/S \ll 0$ dB, jammer negligible, $(C/N_0)_{\text{eff}} \to (C/N_0)$.
- When $J/S \gg 0$ dB, $(C/N_0)_{\text{eff}} \to (C/N_0) - (J/S)$ dB.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Acquisition threshold: typical value **~37 dB-Hz** for cold-start GPS C/A. If effective C/N₀ drops below 37 dB-Hz, acquisition fails.

```
(C/N0)_eff (dB-Hz)
50 -|*.
   |  *..
45 -|    *..
   |      *.
40 -|       *.____ <- 37 dB-Hz threshold
   |         *....
35 -|            *.......
   |                 *..........._______
30 -+---------------------------------------> J/S (dB)
   0    5   10   15   20   25   30   35
```

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the purpose — CRPA creates directional nulls toward jammers while maintaining gain toward satellites.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Describe the elements. A CRPA has **M antenna elements** (typically M = 4 or 7) arranged in a planar array.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Block diagram:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Antenna Element 1]:::ant
    node2[Antenna Element 2]:::ant
    node3[Antenna Element 3]:::ant
    node4[Antenna Element 4]:::ant
    node5[RF Front End]:::rf
    node6[ADC]:::proc
    node7[Adaptive Beamformer]:::proc
    node8[GNSS Correlator]:::proc
    node9[PVT]:::out

    node1 --> node5
    node2 --> node5
    node3 --> node5
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6 --> node7 --> node8 --> node9

    classDef ant fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#06c,color:#000;
    classDef rf fill:#fff1c4,stroke:#a80,color:#000;
    classDef proc fill:#e0d5ff,stroke:#4b2e83,color:#000;
    classDef out fill:#c0f0c0,stroke:#060,color:#000;
```

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Explain algorithm — minimum-variance distortionless response (MVDR) or space-time adaptive processing (STAP) forms the beam pattern.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Array gain $G = 10\log_{10}M$; for M=7, gain ≈ 8.45 dB.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative) [KTU University Exam — July 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain the ionospheric delay model for GNSS signals. Derive the ionosphere-free pseudorange combination for a dual-frequency receiver. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** With reference to robotic motion planning, describe the impact of GNSS spoofing on a path-planning algorithm. Suggest two counter-measures. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Ionospheric delay equation. The ionosphere is a dispersive medium; signal group delay:
$$I = \frac{40.3 \cdot \text{TEC}}{f^2}$$

Explain TEC and its typical range (1–150 TECU).

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Why it's a threat: TEC varies with solar activity, time of day, latitude; introduces 1–100 m error if not corrected.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Derive ionosphere-free combination. Write $P_1 = \rho + I_1$, $P_2 = \rho + I_2$, with $I_i = 40.3 \cdot \text{TEC} / f_i^2$. Eliminate TEC:

$$\rho = \frac{f_1^2 P_1 - f_2^2 P_2}{f_1^2 - f_2^2}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Note the trade-off — noise amplification factor:
$$\beta = \frac{f_1^2}{f_1^2 - f_2^2}$$

For GPS L1/L2: $\beta \approx 2.6$.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Mention that **Klobuchar model** (broadcast single-frequency correction) and **SBAS** (WAAS, GAGAN) provide real-time ionospheric corrections.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State impact. Spoofing causes the planner's localisation module to report a *false pose*. Any control law that uses the pose to steer the robot (e.g., Stanley controller, pure-pursuit) will steer toward a wrong goal — collisions, off-road excursions, regulatory violations.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Example — for a self-driving car with 50 m spoofed position offset, the planner may believe a stationary obstacle is 50 m ahead and brake, or worse, may believe the lane is straight when it curves.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Counter-measure 1 — **INS/GNSS tightly-coupled fusion with innovation monitoring**. The Kalman filter innovation $r_k = z_k - H\hat{x}_{k|k-1}$ is monitored; spoofing creates persistent innovations inconsistent with the IMU's dead-reckoning, triggering a fault flag.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Counter-measure 2 — **Galileo OS-NMA / GPS CHIMERA authentication**. Cryptographic signatures in the navigation message allow the receiver to reject unauthenticated sources. Modern receivers log a "spoofing confidence" metric that can be fused into the planner's risk model.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In 14-mark answers, students often *describe* the spoofing attack but forget to **quantify** its planner-level impact (e.g., "X metres of position error leads to Y seconds of decision delay"). Always include numbers from the C/N₀ or DOP model. Another common mistake is to write the ion-free combination incorrectly — the **order of subtraction matters**: $f_1^2 P_1 - f_2^2 P_2$, not the other way.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — print this before every exam.**

- [ ] **GNSS threats are classified** as *intentional* (jamming, spoofing, cyber), *unintentional* (RFI, hardware), and *environmental* (ionospheric, tropospheric, multipath).
- [ ] **Jamming suppresses** C/N₀ via $J/S$; effective C/N₀ formula: $(C/N_0)_{\text{eff}} = C/N_0 - 10\log_{10}(1+10^{J/S/10})$ dB.
- [ ] **Spoofing deceives** with fake signals; silent failure — worst-case. Sub-types: simulated, meaconing, carry-off.
- [ ] **Acquisition threshold** for cold-start GPS C/A: **~37 dB-Hz**. Below this, no solution.
- [ ] **Multipath error envelope** for C/A code is bounded (~150 m max). Narrow correlators reduce it.
- [ ] **Ionospheric delay** $I = 40.3\,\text{TEC}/f^2$. Dual-frequency receivers cancel via ionosphere-free combination with noise amplification ~2.6×.
- [ ] **Tropospheric delay** is non-dispersive; corrected by Saastamoinen or Hopfield model.
- [ ] **DOP** (GDOP/PDOP/HDOP/VDOP) amplifies pseudorange errors into position errors: $\sigma_P = \text{DOP} \cdot \sigma_{\text{UERE}}$.
- [ ] **UERE** = $\sqrt{\sigma_{\text{eph}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{iono}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{tropo}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{mp}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{rcvr}}^2}$.
- [ ] **RAIM** detects *faults* but cannot detect *consistent* spoofing.
- [ ] **CRPA** array gain = $10\log_{10}M$ dB. M = 7 gives ~8.45 dB jammer suppression.
- [ ] **NMA** (OS-NMA, CHIMERA) provides cryptographic authentication — the long-term anti-spoofing solution.
- [ ] **INS/GNSS fusion** is the *primary* robotic mitigation: a spoofer cannot simultaneously fool the physics of a 6-DOF IMU.
- [ ] **Pseudorange model**: $\rho = r + c(\delta t_u - \delta t_s) + I + T + \epsilon_{\text{mp}} + \epsilon_{\text{rcvr}}$.
- [ ] **Carrier-phase** measurements are mm-precision but ambiguous in $N$ (integer cycles).
- [ ] **Scintillation** is ionospheric fading — quantified by S4 amplitude index and $\sigma_\phi$ phase index.
- [ ] **Meaconing** = record-and-replay; *no encryption can defeat it* if the receiver doesn't authenticate; mitigated only by NMA + multi-antenna.
- [ ] **Smart jammers** adapt frequency; only spread-spectrum / CRPA / STAP can defeat them.
- [ ] **Defense-in-depth** is mandatory: antenna → front-end → baseband → integrity → authentication → sensor fusion → decision logic.
- [ ] **L1 = 1575.42 MHz**, **L2 = 1227.60 MHz**, **L5 = 1176.45 MHz** — memorise these.
- [ ] **Healthy C/N₀ = 45–50 dB-Hz**; **degraded < 40 dB-Hz**; **lost-lock < 28 dB-Hz**.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU strategy:** In 14-mark questions, *always* close with a *real-world engineering implication* (e.g., "A CRPA of 7 elements restores 8.45 dB of margin, sufficient to defeat a 5 W jammer at 1 km — enabling safe urban-canyon operation of delivery robots."). Examiners award 1–2 marks for this industry-link statement.
